response_text: "The targeted outcomes of the Solid Waste Management Improvement Project are primarily focused on improving solid waste management (SWM) services and overall management in Tashkent city by 2019. To be considered an overall success, the activity needs to achieve the following: households served by the SWM system must be maintained at above 95% (approximately 645,500 households, up from a 2013 baseline of 604,500); the percentage of households segregating municipal solid waste (MSW) must increase significantly from about 25% in 2013 to about 90%; and 100% of MSW disposal must accord with international standards of environmental protection, handling approximately 1,950 tons per day (up from a 2013 baseline of zero tons)."

"The targeted outcomes of the Power Sector Reform: Development Policy Credit activity are to develop an efficient and consumer-oriented electric power system in Pakistan that is sustainable and affordable, ensuring it meets the country's needs. This involves focusing on policy and institutional reforms to improve the sector's financial viability and reduce its burden on public finances. Specific outcomes include: reducing subsidies and improving tariff policy; enhancing sector performance and facilitating private sector participation; and strengthening accountability and transparency within sector institutions. More detailed goals by the policy include: enhancing generation capacity sustainably; promoting a culture of conservation and responsibility; ensuring affordable electricity, preferably using indigenous fuels; minimizing technical, financial, and inefficiency losses; controlling pilferage and theft; and aligning activities and strengthening governance among federal and provincial authorities, regulators, and implementing entities. To be considered an overall success, the activity needs to achieve the following by FY15/16 and 2017: a reduction in federal budget subsidies from 1.8% to 0.4% of GDP; an increase in Disco bill collection rates from 86% to 94%; an increase in domestic gas supply from 3.8 to 5 billion standard cubic feet per day; the separation of market and transmission system operations through an independent Central Power Purchasing Agency; and an increase in household consumer awareness of electricity subsidies from zero to 25%. By 2017, the activity aims to: eliminate the demand/supply gap, reducing it from around 7000 MW at peak times to zero; decrease the weighted average generation cost from USc 12/kWh to below 10 USc per kWh; reduce transmission and distribution losses from 23-25% to about 16%; increase system-wide collection rates from 85% to above 95%; and reduce decision times for the Government, related departments, and regulators."

"The targeted outcome is to improve public transport performance and efficiency along a high-priority corridor in Ho Chi Minh City. To be considered an overall success, the activity needs to achieve: 28,300 daily BRT passengers; 50% overall, 60% women, and 50% low-income rider satisfaction; a travel time of 37 minutes from An Lac to Ky Con; 1,290,000 workers/students and 1,208,000 residents accessing Thu Thiem within 45 minutes; and a 23,000 tons/year reduction in GHG emissions. Additionally, careful management, strong institutional support, and sustained funding are crucial."

"The targeted outcomes of the Togo: Mining Governance and Development Project include: the streamlining of institutional structures of key organizations within the extractive industries sector to bolster their efficiency and accountability; improved management of the sector; an improved investment climate to attract private and foreign investors; and the establishment of the mining sector as a crucial pillar for sustainable economic and social development in Togo.Specific numerical and qualitative targets are detailed as follows:

Targeted Outcomes:

1.  **Mining Operations Under Production Control:** An increase in mining operations subject to production control mechanisms, targeting 15 operations annually by year five, from a baseline of zero.
2.  **Mining Cadastre Modernization:** The modernization and deployment of the mining cadastre, targeted for achievement by year three.
3.  **Direct Project Beneficiaries:** An increase from a baseline of 0 to 2200 direct project beneficiaries by the end of the project.
4.  **Female Beneficiaries:** An increase in the percentage of direct project beneficiaries who are female from a baseline of 0% to 35% by the end of the project.
5.  **Environmental Law Compliance:** An increase in the percentage of Extractive Industries (EI) companies complying with environmental law (submission of ESIA, RAP, and ESMP) from a baseline of 0% to 70% by the end of the project.
6.  **Project Monitoring and Accountability:** Preparation and public review of project monitoring reporting on an annual basis, leading to an "Accountability Forums" being established and functional under the EITI MSG or an appropriate multi-stakeholder arrangement by year two and maintained through the end of the project.
7.  **Nationals Trained in EI Skills:** An increase in the number of nationals trained in EI skills from a baseline of 0 to 500 by the end of the project.
8.  **Perception of Environmental Impacts:** An improvement in the perception of environmental impacts of mining by local populations, with 75% of respondents declaring an improvement by the end of the project.
9.  **Institutional Modernization:** Coherent application of recommendations from institutional evaluations to support modernization, leading to an improved institutional framework of the Ministry of Mines and Energy (MME) applying best practices of EI governance by the end of the project.
10. **Advisory Services on Transactions:** Provision of advisory services on transactions, increasing from a baseline of 0 to 2 by the end of the project.
11. **Access to Information Toolkit:** The development and availability of a toolkit for access to information by year four, and its sustained availability thereafter.

To consider the activity an overall success, all the aforementioned targeted outcomes must be achieved. This includes meeting the specific numerical targets for beneficiaries, compliance rates, trained individuals, operations under control, and advisory services, as well as the qualitative targets such as the establishment and functionality of accountability forums, the modernization of the mining cadastre, the improvement of institutional frameworks, and enhanced public perception of environmental impacts. The overarching goals of bolstering efficiency and accountability, improving the investment climate, and establishing the mining sector as a crucial pillar for sustainable economic and social development must also be realized."

"The activity targets establishing 6 comprehensive sustainable urban mobility frameworks, reducing peak-hour travel time for buses and cars by 15%, reducing road crash fatalities and serious injuries by 15%, and applying climate resilience practices in design. It also aims for 20% pedestrian satisfaction with walking environments and 15% of women walking due to safety improvements. Key infrastructure (40km road sections), ITS, and smart parking will be upgraded, with integrated bus management and on-demand transit launched. Overall success depends on achieving these targets and fulfilling critical assumptions like obtaining necessary approvals and renegotiating the bus management contract."

"The activity aims for equitable, sustainable economic/human development, modernizing public sector/revenue management, and reducing gender inequality. Success, primarily by Aug 2016, entails: 1.2% tax arrears revenue share; 60% producer groups with improved market access; 100% state area with improved hydro-geological disaster risk identification; 80% fundamental education survival rate; 50% high-risk pregnant women referred; 86% attending >7 ante-natal check-ups; 29% ICU bed increase; 210 business proposals & 350 micro-catchment plans; State DRM Policy Framework established; 90% teacher training participation with 7,830 teachers trained; 340 schools rehabilitated/expanded; automated health sector payroll implemented."

"Targeted outcomes: sustain critical infrastructure access for firms/households post-COVID-19 and establish policy for sustainable, resilient recovery. Success by Dec 2022 requires: COP 600B disbursed to utilities; 3 mass transit systems' deficits covered; 80% road PPPs financially stable; 1,374 MW NCRE awarded; 200 MW NCRE installed; 45 MW energy storage installed; 6.5M Ton/YR fluvial freight; 12.9% logistics costs; 125 hrs maritime processing; 3,360 electric vehicles; 2 new mass transit co-financing agreements; 19 PPPs under new FCCL; USD 1.0B CAPEX for 5G projects tendered. Also, shifting commuting to safe, green modes and improving public transport sustainability."

"The targeted outcomes of the Sava and Drina Rivers Corridors Integrated Development Program are to improve flood protection and enhance transboundary water cooperation in the Sava and Drina Rivers Corridors. For the activity to be considered an overall success, the following would need to be achieved: an increase in the number of people protected from a 1-in-100-year flood event in the Sava and Drina River Basin (with a specific count for females), an increase in the hectares of areas protected by flood risk mitigation measures under the project, and the updating and endorsement by countries of a river basin management plan that reflects integrated measures."

"The activity targets supporting the Solomon Islands Government (SIG) in maintaining its economic reform momentum and providing crucial financing for post-flood recovery. Specific outcomes include: improved management of public expenditure and debt (e.g., controlled scholarship spending, transparent on-lending to SOEs), strengthened management of extractive industries (e.g., increased transparency, predictable mining tax regime), and improved conditions for private sector growth (e.g., transparent land transactions)."

"[\"The Federal District Multisector Management activity targets public sector modernization, decentralization, and integration to improve quality of life, reduce inequalities, and enhance DF's competitiveness. Specific outcomes include compliance with the Annual Financing Plan and budget execution targets, establishing results-based management in public agencies, and improving services in education, health, and transport. Education aims for increased early childhood access (90%), higher completion rates (80-85%), and reduced overage distortion. Health targets include 50% Citizen Health Card coverage, 40% Family Health Program coverage, and improved maternal care. Transport seeks an approved Urban Master Plan, improved data control, remote fleet monitoring (10%), and 90% bus regularity. Overall success requires achieving these global and sector-specific development indicators and disbursement targets by 2011, ensuring sustained public sector reforms and improved service delivery.\",\"The Federal District Multisector Management activity targets public sector modernization, decentralization, and integration to improve quality of life, reduce inequalities, and enhance DF's competitiveness. Specific outcomes include compliance with the Annual Financing Plan and budget execution targets, establishing results-based management in public agencies, and improving services in education, health, and transport. Education aims for increased early childhood access (90%), higher completion rates (80-85%), and reduced overage distortion. Health targets include 50% Citizen Health Card coverage, 40% Family Health Program coverage, and improved maternal care. Transport seeks an approved Urban Master Plan, improved data control, remote fleet monitoring (10%), and 90% bus regularity. Overall success requires achieving these global and sector-specific development indicators and disbursement targets by 2011, ensuring sustained public sector reforms and improved service delivery.\"]"

"The targeted outcomes are: improved access to infrastructure and connectivity, reduced flood risk, reduced flood impact and environmental pollution, reduced travel time, enhanced climate-resilient urban management, and improved living conditions, economic/social opportunities, and health for urban residents. Success requires: 118,600 people (51% female) with new/improved drainage/wastewater access; 25% travel time reduction between key city areas; area protected by 1-in-100-year river flood polder; an integrated flood risk management system developed. Crucial assumptions for success include timely land acquisition, asset maintenance by local authorities/communities, affordable services, and sustained maintenance of flood/geospatial platforms."

"Targeted outcomes are to: 1) Reduce costs in agriculture/transport: Input Fund capitalized to at least CFAF 10B, 30,000 tons more fertilizer distributed, customs clearance time at Ouaga Inter reduced by 50%, certified trucking professionals/companies increased by 10%. 2) Improve transparency/accountability: Mining fiscal revenues increase to 4% of GDP, 6+ mining companies submit EITI data, Corruption Perceptions Index score to 40+, 50% increase in spot checks, 50% more CSM elected members, 20% reduction in court ruling/contract enforcement times. 3) Reduce vulnerability: Decentralized fiscal transfers to 5% of national budget, FAARF beneficiaries >80,000 (loan portfolio >CFAF 5B), national food/emergency stocks to 50,000/25,000 tons. Overall success means achieving all these targets by end-2015/2016."

"[\"The activity targets include significantly reducing open defecation; increasing the proportion of the population practicing good hygiene behaviors (including use of hygienic toilets and hand washing); increasing safe water coverage, reaching 80,000 households with sustainable water supply services and 255,000 new or rehabilitated water connections; ensuring 100% of schools and health clinics have hygienic latrines, adequate water, and hand washing facilities in 340 communes; and achieving commune-wide sanitation in 680 communes. It also aims to build capacity at provincial, commune, and village levels for managing sanitation and water services, delivering effective behavior change communication and sanitation marketing, and integrating new approaches into the successor program, while also addressing climate change resilience in water systems. An overall 4,900,000 direct program beneficiaries are targeted.\",\"Overall success requires achieving \\\"Commune-Wide Sanitation,\\\" defined as 100% of public kindergartens, primary, secondary schools, and health centers having hygienic water, hand washing, and MoH-standard sanitation facilities, plus 100% of households having improved latrines. Additionally, \\\"Sustainable Water Systems\\\" must be established, providing clean water, operating under a recognized management model, having a high percentage of functioning connections, and covering operational costs through revenues. Meeting PDO indicators, including 4,900,000 direct beneficiaries and 80,000 households with sustainable water supply, and hitting DLI targets, are crucial.\"]"

"The Forest Sustainability and Competitiveness Program targets the following outcomes: 1. Increase forested area with high-value species and good management practices for quality timber production (target: 1,200,000 managed hectares, 80,000 hectares with high-value species). 2. Improve MSME access to high-value supply chains (target: 30% difference in new MSMEs integrating into value chains). 3. Increase information availability for MSMEs (target: 14,000 MSMEs using information system). 4. Increase MSME formality (target: 750 MSMEs completing timber legality verification, 30% legal wood processed in financed centers). 5. Increase sustainability of MSME forest management and chain of custody (target: 80 MSMEs certified with CERFOAR). The activity is considered an overall success when these targets are met by the project's end date, relative to their 2012 baselines."

"The targeted outcomes of the Public Investment Capacity Building Project are primarily to improve the quality and efficiency of the preparation and implementation of investment projects in key priority sectors, particularly infrastructure, in Azerbaijan. To be considered an overall success, the project needs to achieve the following:1.  Improvement in the quality of preparation of infrastructure projects, specifically measured by an increase in the percentage of government-financed projects prepared based on rate of return criteria. By FY2013, 50% of projects should meet this criterion.2.  Improvement in the efficiency of implementation of infrastructure projects, measured by a reduction in the average project life for key projects based on disbursement rates. By FY2013, a 20%-30% reduction in average project life is targeted.Intermediate outcomes that contribute to this success include:1.  Implementation of new procedures for managing projects, with 40% of new projects utilizing these procedures by FY2013.2.  A significant number of people trained in project management and various disciplines, with 85% of the annual training program participants attending by FY2013.3.  Adoption of a revised curriculum for general project management courses.4.  Adoption and operationalization of certification and accreditation programs.5.  Training of 150 people in specialized technical areas, with 85% of annual training program participants attending by FY2013.6.  Adoption of a revised curriculum at the University of Architecture and Construction (UAC) for technical training.7.  Adoption of an action plan on policy changes in capacity development."

"The DJ-URBAN POVERTY REDUCTION PROJECT aims to improve access to basic social and economic infrastructure and community development opportunities in Quarter 7. Targeted outcomes include improved roads, sanitation, essential facilities (e.g., CDC), enhanced community development through small projects, and facilitated microcredit for income-generating activities, benefiting women especially. Success is defined by: a 35% increase in properties within 50 meters of paved roads, proper sanitation for all renovated road properties, 20% of youth using the CDC, 10% of residents (50% women) benefiting from economic sub-projects, and 8,000 person-days of short-term employment created."

"The targeted outcome of the Thimphu Road Improvement Project is the improved safety and efficiency of the road network in Thimphu Thromde. This outcome will be considered achieved by 2020 when the length of unsafe segments of urban roads decreases by 36.53 km from the total 243.00 km. To be considered an overall success, the activity must achieve two key outputs by 2019: (1) The repair, resurfacing, and improvement of roads in Thimphu Thromde must be completed, including 32.99 km of urban roads improved, 9.15 km of drainage constructed, 4.83 km of footpaths constructed, 4,239 square meters of parking space improved by 2017, and 3.54 km of urban roads widened. (2) The municipality's capacity for asset management and O&M must be strengthened by training 10-15 key staff (at least 30% women) in project planning, design, construction, asset management, O&M, road safety, and traffic management."

"The targeted outcomes of the Chonburi Natural Gas Power Project are to ensure power system stability and reliability in Thailand, with an outcome of increased delivery of stable and reliable power to the domestic grid. Specific targets include: the creation of at least 60 additional jobs during operation by 2023, of which 15% should be taken up by women; the commissioning of 2,500 megawatts of power capacity by 2022; the creation of 3,200 jobs during construction by 2023; supporting the growth of the local economy; and undertaking gender outreach campaigns for local communities, specifically by holding up to two health and education awareness-raising events per year, including targeted measures for girls to enter science, technology, engineering, and math training and girls' education in water sanitation and hygiene practices, HIV and AIDS, and safety. The project also aims to contribute to a CO2 reduction of 737,000 tons per annum.To consider the activity an overall success, the project would need to achieve the following: an increased delivery of stable and reliable power to the domestic grid as evidenced by the creation of at least 60 additional operational jobs by 2023, with 15% being held by women; the natural gas power plant must be commissioned, delivering 2,500 megawatts of power capacity by 2022; 3,200 jobs must be created during construction by 2023; the local economy must demonstrate supported growth; and the gender outreach campaigns must be successfully undertaken, with up to two health and education awareness-raising events per year implemented for local communities, including the specified targeted measures for girls. Additionally, overall success hinges on mitigating risks such as low plant availability, reduced efficiency, deficient operation and maintenance technology, the unavailability of appropriately skilled women for operational jobs, severe capital market or economy disruptions, noncompliance with contractual obligations, construction delays due to force majeure, and increases in raw material prices above budget. The project must also adhere to environmental, involuntary resettlement, and indigenous peoples safeguards, comply with national labor laws and international core labor standards, and implement anticorruption measures."

"The targeted outcomes of the Results-based Road Maintenance and Safety Project are to maintain the condition and improve the safety of the Borrower's Primary Road and Primary-Secondary Road networks, and to strengthen sustainable and efficient road asset management and safety practices for the benefit of road-users. Specifically, road-users are expected to benefit from reduced travel times, lower vehicle operating costs, and improved road safety, which will decrease social costs and economic losses from accidents. Secondary beneficiaries include ARA, which will gain improved road maintenance outcomes and capacity in performance-based maintenance contracting and road asset management; IMRSC, through advancements in road safety policy planning; and the local construction industry, via expanded private sector opportunities. The project also aims to contribute to ending extreme poverty and promoting shared prosperity by creating long-term employment, fostering economic growth in rural areas, improving market access, and enhancing access to health and education services, while also improving road safety, which disproportionately affects primary male breadwinners. Specifically, the project targets a 50% reduction in road fatality rates by 2020 as part of a national goal.

To consider the activity an overall success, the following would be needed:

*   **Road Condition:** The average International Roughness Index (IRI) of the Project network must not deteriorate.
*   **Safety:** Road fatalities across the national road network must be reduced on an annual basis.
*   **Sustainability:** Fully-costed multi-year maintenance plans must be prepared and implemented annually.
*   **Periodic Maintenance:** A pre-defined length of periodic maintenance must be completed, targeting 100% of the periodic maintenance on the estimated 554 km of project roads over four and a half years.
*   **Routine Maintenance:** Regular routine maintenance must be carried out under Performance-based Contracts on Project roads for five-year maintenance contracts. This is achieved if, on average across all maintenance contracts, at least 80% of the Contract Lump Sum is paid to the Contractor, indicating predefined service targets are met.
*   **Social Transparency System:** A system must be developed and made operational to monitor feedback and generate monthly trend and analysis reports, remaining operational for the entire year in subsequent years.
*   **IRAP Safety Surveys:** Periodic IRAP safety surveys must be completed, demonstrating an improvement in Safety Star rating across the Project roads compared to a baseline.
*   **Road Asset Management System:** Annual condition surveys must be completed, and multi-year maintenance programs prepared on an annual basis."

"The targeted outcomes of the CM-Urban and Water Development Support Project include: increasing access for the urban population, particularly those in low-income settlements, to basic infrastructure and services, including water supply; improving mobility, reducing flooding, enhancing access to water and sanitation, and decreasing waterborne diseases; enabling additional populations to gain year-round access to transportation, improved sanitation, and safe water; strengthening municipal capacity in Bamenda, Douala, Maroua, Mbalmayo, and Yaoundé for managing infrastructure and urban service delivery; enhancing Camwater's capacity to develop water supply services and reinforcing the sustainability and management capabilities of urban water supply services; strengthening the capacity and accountability of local administrations in target cities regarding programming, implementation, and maintenance of infrastructure and services; assisting urban development stakeholders in preparing and implementing instruments and investments to maximize service access; and supporting and facilitating the establishment of public-private partnerships for urban water service provision. To consider the activity an overall success, the following would be needed: an increase in the number of additional people benefiting from all-year access to transportation and improved sanitary conditions; an increase in the number of additional people with access to water services; an increase in the length of roadways passable by standard vehicles year-round; an increase in the length of working drainage networks; an increase in the number of social water connections built and new water standposts; an increase in the length of newly built or rehabilitated water supply networks; an increase in the number of city development contracts prepared; an increase in the share of municipal budget allocated to the maintenance of investments; achievement of financial equilibrium in the urban water supply sector; a reduction in the share of public customers in total water consumption; the project achieving a positive economic Net Present Value (9,987 FCFA M at 12%) and an Economic Internal Rate of Return of 12.2%, with net benefits turning positive from 2012 onwards; the establishment of the project's institutional framework; the creation and staffing of a Coordination Unit (CP) and Control Committee (CC); the implementation of an IDA-acceptable fiduciary management system within the CC; the establishment of administrative, financial, and accounting procedures manuals acceptable to the Bank; and the finalization of Camwater's installation, including the signing of affermage, concession, performance, and subsidiary financing agreements, for the disbursement of the urban hydraulic component."

"[\"Targeted outcomes include: MINAGRI's organizational reform (plan approved, HR upgraded) and policy competence (private sector strategy, subsidy reforms). Operational digital platforms (data warehouse, registrations, public dashboard). $11.15M private investment in PPP infrastructure. 30% small farmers using improved seeds; 14,000 households with private advisory services. 2,940 Ha new irrigation, 3,055 Ha new terracing via viable PPPs. RAB restructuring (plan approved, sound financials: +/-10% budget deviation, unqualified audit). Effective public-private dialogues and value chain platforms.\",\"Overall success requires: MINAGRI's completed institutional/policy reforms, enhancing capacity and private sector leverage. Operational, integrated digital platforms for agriculture data/performance. Mobilizing specified private investment in PPP infrastructure. Effective, results-yielding public-private dialogues/value chain platforms. Achieving farmer engagement targets in improved practices/advisory services. Developing specified new irrigation/terracing via viable PPPs. RAB's restructuring, sound financials, and unqualified audits. Ultimately, fostering a productive, green, market-led agricultural sector, aligned with national development/resilience.\"]"

"The Second Gujarat State Highway Project (GSHP II) targets improving the efficiency, capacity, quality, and safety of Gujarat's core state highway network, and strengthening institutional effectiveness for improved service delivery and financing. Key outcomes include: increasing roads in good/fair condition from 43% to 60% of the CRN; reducing average car travel time on project roads from 75 to 50 minutes/50 km and truck travel time from 85 to 65 minutes/50 km; reducing average Volume/Capacity ratios; and cutting annual fatalities on safe corridors. Success hinges on achieving these quantitative targets and establishing a robust road safety management system, enhancing institutional/financial capacities, increasing private sector participation, and improving asset management practices across approximately 625 km of the network."

"The CM-Sanitation Project targets increasing quality and access to sanitation. Outcomes include: 2,042,688 direct beneficiaries (50.6% women); 175,000 urban & 143,000 rural people with improved sanitation; 60% washing hands with soap; 120,000 school children with improved sanitation; 20 local authorities with community sanitation plans. All beneficiaries get hygiene education; 318,000 get partial household subsidies; urban areas get improved sludge management; municipalities receive capacity building. Success (for APL-2) requires: sanitation water surcharge, transparent local authority financing, Douala sludge plant institutional arrangements, and satisfactory advanced sanitation designs."

"The activity targets increasing the efficiency, environmental sustainability, and competitiveness of select Organized Industrial Zones (OIZs) and enhancing firm competitiveness. Outcomes include significant energy and water savings, CO2 emission reduction, increased OIZ attractiveness for new investments, more firms benefiting from green and basic infrastructure, renewable energy generation, improved wastewater treatment, developed Green OIZ regulations, certification of OIZs, increased firm understanding of Green OIZ benefits, facilitated private investment, and local job creation. This supports Turkey's sustainable industrial development and GHG emission reduction goals."

"[\"The CCE-Initiation Plan 2023-2024 targets include: finalizing GCF/GEF project proposals on biodiversity, marine spatial planning, net zero, and gender; developing long-term climate change programs; strengthening policy/regulatory frameworks for green energy in transport; enhancing capacity for E-mobility; creating 70 green jobs (10 E-truck, 60 EV drivers); achieving 560 direct beneficiaries for green energy access; reducing 45 tons/year CO2 from PV/E-trucks (plus 30 from other E-vehicles); establishing carbon emission tracking and designing Article 6.2 carbon credit projects, with 1 pilot and 10 trainees for ETS readiness.\", \"Overall success hinges on: mobilizing unfunded budget ($179,707); strengthening policies/regulations for green transport; implementing an EV plan for public transport, including replication in Quy Nhon/Binh Dinh; achieving targeted CO2 reductions (45+30 tons/year); creating 70 green jobs and benefiting 560 people; establishing effective carbon emissions tracking, designing Article 6.2 carbon credit projects, and successfully piloting ETS readiness; aligning energy targets in NDCs with net-zero pathways; and fostering partnerships with Japanese organizations.\"]"

"The activity aims to increase the number of highly skilled professionals in Africa in water, energy, environment, and infrastructure engineering by making 2iE a regional center of excellence. Success would be achieved by 2011 if the total number of students reaches 1000, maintaining a 90% employment rate for graduates in their qualified fields six months after graduation. Specific targets include increasing boarding capacity to 1000 and classroom/laboratory capacity to 2,500 seats, with 22,000 square meters built. The number of doctoral students is targeted to rise to 25, with 60% of professors holding PhDs and permanent teaching staff increasing to 45, and 9 Masters degrees achieving international certification. Non-francophone students should comprise 10% of enrollment, and 450 students are targeted for distance education. Financially, success entails zero non-targeted subsidies, with 2011 revenues expected from fees (42%), contracted services/distance education (29%), and donor-financed contracts (29%)."

"The targeted outcomes of the Landslide Risk Management Sector Project are to reduce vulnerability and exposure to landslide events. Specifically: 1. By 2029, landslide risk is decreased for 4,700 people (≥50% women), 650 ha of land, 65 km of linear infrastructure, and 14 community buildings. 2. By 2028, 3.0 million m³ of slope unloaded/reshaped (100 ha revegetated) and 40 km of drainage conduits installed. 3. By 2026, on-site monitoring systems in ~20 sites, one integrated IT/InSAR system operational, and 230 staff/community members (≥30% women) with increased monitoring capacity. 4. By 2025, gender-sensitive landslide risk management road map adopted by MES and consolidated risk database operational. By 2026, 500 staff/community members (≥30% women) and 1,000 community members (≥45% women) report improved knowledge/action on risk assessment, mitigation, resettlement, and planning. The activity is successful if these targets are met, demonstrating reduced risk and enhanced capabilities."

"The Road Rehabilitation and Maintenance Program targets sustaining at least 35% of the Uruguay National Road Network in good or very good condition (measured by IRI and IES \">= 85\") and improving road sector management through completing its Action Plan. It aims to rehabilitate 524 km of national roads to an IES rating of 85 and maintain an increasing number of kilometers annually (e.g., 3200-3300 km) via performance-based contracts. Broader outcomes include ensuring cost-effective land transport, supporting Uruguay's logistics platform ambition, bolstering industry competitiveness, improving access to essential services, and reducing traffic accident fatalities to enhance road safety. Economically, it projects a Net Present Value of US$144 million and an Internal Rate of Return of 29%, yielding savings in vehicle operating and road reconstruction costs, and reduced travel times."

"[\"The SN- First Governance and Growth Support Project targets improved economic governance, strengthened accountability, efficient public expenditure, and private sector growth. Specific outcomes by 2013 include: all ministers declaring assets, Audit Court reports published within <6 months, basic education wage budget credibility <110%, university budget credibility <125%, hospital efficiency at 76%, government energy subsidies at CFAF80 billion, and SENELEC's debt-to-equity ratio at 2. By 2015, outcomes extend to basic education wage budget at 100%, university budget to 100-105%, girls' scholarships at 40%, load shedding at 10 GWH, total energy subsidies at CFAF50 billion, and SENELEC's debt-to-equity at 1.\", \"To consider the SN- First Governance and Growth Support Project an overall success, several critical factors are needed. Primarily, achieving all specified key outcome indicators by their respective target dates is essential. Beyond quantitative metrics, success hinges on strong government ownership of the structural reforms, as emphasized in lessons from previous projects. Furthermore, sustained political commitment at the highest level is crucial to prevent weakening coordination and implementation. This commitment must also ensure the mobilization of the entire government and foster clear leadership and accountability throughout the reform process.\"]"

"The targeted outcomes of the Grenada - Caribbean Regional Air Transport Connectivity activity are: 1. To improve operational safety and navigation efficiency of air transportation, which entails achieving modernization of four air navigation systems and oversight mechanisms, and a regional change in airspace area covered by a globally standardized air traffic monitoring system by 100%. 2. To increase the climate and disaster resilience of Maurice Bishop International Airport (MBIA), which means the Air Cargo Facility must be certified as a trusted point of departure. 3. To strengthen Grenada's capacity in civil aviation and airport management, requiring a regional capacity building program on air traffic control and management to be prepared and endorsed. The activity will be considered an overall success when these end targets are met."

"The Kandy Multimodal Transport Terminal Development Project aims to enhance accessibility, efficiency, and safety for public transport users of the Kandy Multimodal Transport Terminal. Targeted outcomes include: improved accessibility to the terminal, measured by the availability of intermodal transfer passages and physical accommodation/facilities for people with disabilities and baggage; improved efficiency of bus operations, with a target of 85% of buses departing on time; improved safety inside the terminal, ensuring designated waiting areas for passengers are separated from bus bays and parking, and facilities for social distancing and sanitation are available; and improved personal security, measured by the percentage of passengers who feel safe to travel through the terminal. The project also aims to ensure the KMTT terminal is built and operational, a high percentage of public transport users are satisfied with services, and 90% of grievances are addressed within one month. Furthermore, it targets the development of a road safety action plan for Kandy city, a robust financial plan for terminal operations and maintenance, and an institutional setup for its operation and maintenance. Eighty percent of complaints related to terminal service provision through kiosks should be addressed within six months. To be considered an overall success, several crucial assumptions must hold true: the completion of Resettlement Action Plan (RAP) payments, the establishment of a new Kandy Project Management Unit (PMU), effective coordination among all stakeholders, no critical increase in private motorized vehicles, and the successful implementation of a Kandy Public Transport Strategy. Additionally, design gaps concerning bus and passenger flows, capacity, terminal user aspects, and pandemic resilience, identified during a World Bank review, must be addressed through a design gap analysis and the subsequent design-build contract. Success will also manifest as less city congestion, safer and faster commutes, greater accessibility to markets, employment, schools, and health facilities, benefiting public transport users, particularly women and people with disabilities. Increased business opportunities and tourism potential, along with lower operational costs and improved revenues for transport operators, are also indicators of success, contributing to the preservation of Kandy's cultural heritage and improved city branding."

"Enhanced access to potable water for participating communities and improved hygiene, sanitation, and water-related practices at individual, family, and institutional levels in rural areas. Specifically, the project targets by Year 4: 50-54% of people with network water access (>=4 days/week, 12 hrs/day); 69% of households within 500/1000m of an improved water source; 60% Operation and Maintenance cost recovery in targeted utilities; 80% of SES water quality tests meeting national standards; and a 60% reduction in annual cases of Water Supply & Sanitation-related illnesses."

"The targeted outcomes of the Dominica Geothermal Risk Mitigation Project are to diversify Dominica's domestic power generation mix by integrating clean, renewable geothermal energy and to demonstrate the potential for a larger development of the geothermal resource. This will lead to a more resilient and sustainable electricity supply for all existing and prospective customers, along with lower and more stable prices and a reduced carbon footprint. Dominica expects to gain new growth opportunities, regional electricity trade potential, and broader economic and social benefits. The project aims to construct a 5-7 MW Small Geothermal Power Plant (SGPP) and associated steam above-ground systems (SAGS) to displace costly diesel-based generation, confirm reservoir operating conditions, and transform the geothermal field into a less risky brown-field operation. It also includes a facility for additional drilling to mitigate residual resource risks and ensure SGPP operability. A comprehensive pre-feasibility study for a Large Geothermal Power Plant (LGPP) for export will be conducted to evaluate viability, identify a roadmap, and attract private sector investment, including gender-focused workshops to identify income-generating opportunities for women. The long-term outcomes target a more efficient, greener, and resilient energy mix, leading to lower electricity prices, increased energy security, and a reduced carbon footprint, potentially transforming Dominica's economy through sustained growth and macroeconomic consolidation if geothermal electricity can be exported regionally. Specific environmental targets include an estimated GHG emissions reduction of 38,223 tCO2/year. The project also targets increased income-generating opportunities for women, aiming for 60% share of women employed in tourism activities facilitated by the project and/or the Government in proximity of the power plant. In terms of energy generation, the project aims to enable 7 MW of geothermal electricity generation capacity and an annual electricity generation of 51.2 GWh from geothermal, avoiding 936,463 tCO2 over the project's 25-year lifetime, and bringing co-benefits such as more affordable energy access, improved energy security, environmental and health co-benefits, electricity export revenues, and employment opportunities. To consider the activity an overall success, the following would be needed: construction of a 7.00 MW geothermal energy generation capacity under the project, achieving a 51.00% share of renewable energy capacity in the domestic generation mix (from a baseline of 24.70%), and ascertaining the preliminary feasibility of larger development of the geothermal field. Specifically, one Small Geothermal Power Plant (SGPP) must be constructed, 100% of the Steam Above Ground System (SAGS) completed, insurance for contingent drilling established, a pre-feasibility study for the Large Geothermal Power Plant (LGPP) completed, and outreach to market to solicit private sector interest to invest in DGDC completed. Additionally, a Gender and Geothermal Energy Workshop must be conducted. Critical assumptions for project success include a robust Power Purchase Agreement (PPA) between DGDC and DOMLEC, full recovery and increased resilience of the power network, an agreement with DOMLEC on optimal geothermal supply dispatch, proper steamfield management, and DGDC's follow-up on gender empowerment measures identified in the Environmental and Social Impact Assessment."

"The Competitiveness DPL targets increased investments by companies in TIDZs to \n50 million by 2013, a 45% share of medium/high-tech exports, and 80 companies in export support programs. It aims for \n4.6 million in agricultural land lease payments, 15% of agricultural support for structural measures, 5% of beneficiaries adhering to Good Agricultural Practices, and 8% subject to on-spot inspections. Trade logistics targets a 5% reduction in border waiting times and 90% of CEMT licenses for EURO 4/5 vehicles. Labor market outcomes include 8% formal work among social assistance recipients, 38,000 online employment contracts, and visa/work permit processing time reduced to 35 days. Success would be achieving all these specific targets by the planned end date."

"The activity aims to ensure continued effective use of Cambodia's road network by improving MPWT's capacity for maintenance planning, budgeting, and operations, fostering a road asset management culture. Targeted outcomes include: no increase in vehicle operating costs or travel times; roads accommodating traffic increases; maintaining road asset value. Success requires: 65% (2100km) primary, 45% (2600km) secondary, 8% (6500km) provincial roads in good condition; US$40M annual domestic revenue for maintenance; 100% national and at least 30% maintainable secondary/provincial routine maintenance domestically funded; 5% national and 4% secondary/provincial periodic maintenance domestically funded; fully operational RMDS; and successful MBPI implementation."

"[\"The activity targets establishing and operationalizing a regional approach to the electricity sector in OECS Participating Countries, improving public confidence in sector governance, enhancing the investment climate, facilitating renewable energy projects and potential cross-border interconnections, and increasing operating efficiency. Key outcomes include the entry into force of the ECERA through treaty ratification by participating countries, adoption of new licensing recommendations, and the design and adoption of cost-reflective and performance-based tariffs. Intermediate objectives include creating a new regional institutional framework, harmonizing sector policies, drafting and ratifying the ECERA treaty, amending domestic legislation, hiring a full complement of staff, appointing commissioners, approving and operationalizing a self-financing mechanism, and designing licensing rules for new investments and tariff decisions.\", \"The activity would be considered an overall success upon the achievement of specific target values within defined years. This includes: the ECERA Treaty being drafted (YR2) and ratified by participating countries (YR3), leading to the ECERA's entry into force as regulator (YR3). Amendments to domestic legislation to operationalize ECERA must be prepared (YR2). A full complement of staff must be hired (YR3), and Commissioners appointed (YR3). A self-financing mechanism for ECERA must be approved and operational (YR3). New licensing recommendations must be adopted by the Council of Ministers (YR5). Cost-reflective and performance-based tariffs must be designed and adopted (YR5). Licensing rules for new investments in electricity generation must be designed (YR4), and the Authority's decision on tariffs designed and adopted (YR5). Overall success implies ECERA becoming an independent, transparent, and operational regional regulatory body.\"]"

"[\"The Benin Rural Water Supply Universal Access Program aims to increase access to water supply services and strengthen service delivery in rural areas, supporting the GoB's goal of universal access by 2021. It targets providing 5.5M beneficiaries with water access within a 30-minute round trip, including 2.5M people in rural/semi-urban areas. Specifically, the program expects to finance 270 new and rehabilitate/expand 360 existing water supply systems, leading to 34,500 new household connections and 3,300 standpipes, providing access to 1,660,000 people. It also aims to professionalize service delivery through 6 regional performance-based affermage contracts.\",\"Success hinges on key results indicators: the number of people provided with improved water access (including female), the percentage of piped rural water systems managed by regional operators, and the number of bi-annual service monitoring reports published by ANAEPMR. Specific targets for overall success include: 34,500 new household connections, 3,300 new standpipes, Framework Partnership Agreements (FPAs) signed by at least 50 municipalities, publication of 11 bi-annual service monitoring reports, and the signing of 6 performance-based contracts with regional operators and service orders issued.\"]"

"[\"The project aims to improve water supply services and strengthen urban governance and finances in Amritsar and Ludhiana. Key outcomes include establishing and operationalizing water utilities, increasing own source revenues for ULBs (property tax), enhancing operational efficiency through e-governance, and strengthening social accountability via social audits. It also targets improved water quality, increased water availability through new infrastructure (WTPs, storage), and reduced energy consumption in water systems. Gender inclusion is sought by increasing female representation in utility boards and staff training. Climate resilience is addressed by integrating climate risk into investment and asset plans.\",\"Overall success requires: 2 WSS utilities submitting annual reports; 30% female Board Directors in Amritsar/Ludhiana water utilities; 80% citizen grievance resolution; building permits issued within 20 days; dedicated IT cells established and staffed; contractor payment clearance reduced to 30 days; 50% increase in digitized/disclosed official documents; 50% MC staff trained; AMPs/CIPs prepared and in use in both MCs; 50% OSR increase in AMC and 30% in LMC; property tax return filing at 65% (AMC) and 85% (LMC); improved water quality in both cities; established/operational water utilities; 2 WTPs built/operational; 90/110 storage facilities built/rehabilitated in Amritsar/Ludhiana; 100% water supply reservoirs receiving treated water; reduced energy consumption to 0.02 MWh (Amritsar) and 0.03 MWh (Ludhiana).\"]"

"[\"Targeted outcomes include: improved economic opportunities for Jordanians and Syrian refugees, formal labor market access for Syrians, an enhanced investment climate, reduced regulatory burden on firms, and increased/retained domestic/foreign investments, particularly in Special Economic Zones (SEZs), leading to formal, decent job creation. Other outcomes include improved labor conditions in SEZs, greater mobility for Syrians, enhanced regulatory predictability and transparency, reduced red tape, increased household businesses, and improved Customs operations.\",\"Success is measured by: annual work permits issued to Syrian refugees (e.g., 90,000 by CY22); adopting a predictability process for business regulations by 2017; identifying and implementing a key business regulatory reform by 2018; 1000 officially established household enterprises (100 Syrian-owned, 100 female-owned) by 2019; 70% of mandatory business regulations issued via the predictability process by 2020; and a 30% decrease in regulatory burden on businesses following reform implementation.\"]"

"The GEF small grants 7th Phase targets strengthening community bio-enterprises in four ecosystems with improved market access and establishing operational multi-stakeholder bio-entrepreneurship networks for effective landscape governance. Success requires developing at least 16 innovative products and supporting 20 bio-enterprises with innovative financial mechanisms. Also, nine multi-stakeholder networks, nine value chain strategies (developed and implemented), and six market agreements must be established. The project aims for 8,533 direct beneficiaries (5,120 women), 191,468 hectares of land, and 2,637 hectares of marine habitat under improved management, and 20 functional community bio-enterprises (50% women-coordinated)."

"The activity targets increased water availability and access to improved water supply in Maputsoe, Hlotse, and surrounding areas, and improved WASCO's technical and financial performance. Success requires: 25,000 m³/day water production capacity; 98,000 urban & 17,000 rural people accessing improved sources; 100,000 existing connections benefiting from 12+ hours/day service; WASCO achieving 70% of LEWA-defined financial/technical targets; 267 km pipelines constructed; non-revenue water reduced to 25%; 100% technical and 95% financial data quality actions achieved; 70%+ WASCO performance composite score; and a 14% EIRR."

"Targeted outcomes: 50% reduction in water service interruptions (from 430 days), irrigation system efficiency increase from 65% to 80%, O&M cost recovery rate improvement from 43% to 80%, and share of production value marketed through contractual arrangements from 15% to 30%. Intermediate outcomes include a 25% irrigation tariff increase, 95% fee collection rate, 70% satisfied users, and 25,900 hectares with improved services. The project also targets creating/maintaining 2,700 jobs. Success means achieving these targets, improving market access and income for small producers, and preserving hydraulic resources."

"The activity targets strengthening biodiversity conservation in and around 67,861 hectares of High Value Conservation Forests (HCVFs), addressing climate mitigation, and securing forest ecosystem services. It aims to shift Guzara forest management to owner communities, strengthen community organizations, and support sustainable livelihoods. National SFM practices and regulations will be established, deforestation of HCVFs avoided, and forest/biodiversity resources improved. Community incomes will be enhanced through alternative livelihoods. A total carbon sequestration of 9,908,090 tCO2-eq over 30 years is expected. Success hinges on effective implementation, overcoming institutional weaknesses, and sustained community engagement to shift unsustainable practices."

"Targeted outcomes: improve Government's capacity to mitigate economic volatility by increasing available resources and reinforcing Integrated Water Resource Management (WRM). By 2015, key results include: Fiscal Management: 1. Tax expenditure measures evaluated: 0% to 25%. 2. Tax arrears recovered in 60 days: 20.8% to 27%. 3. Goods procured using e-invoice price info: 0% to 25%. 4. Managerial cost reports: 0 to 14. 5. Contingent liabilities policy: effective. WRM: 6. Farmers in 'More Water, More Income': 413 to 3,000. 7. Approved river basin plans: 1 to 12. 8. New hydro-meteorological stations: 0 to 80. Success means achieving all these targets by 2015."

"The Somalia Crisis Recovery Project targets recovery of livelihoods (1.2-1.7M people, 116k-166k female-headed households) and infrastructure (1.6-2.2M households, 804k-1.1M female-headed households), strengthening national disaster preparedness (525k-750k people benefiting from flood risk management, improved mechanisms). It aims for gender inclusion (56-80% GBV survivor support, 30% women in decision-making), benefiting 300k households/1.6M persons, 160k livestock, 40+ communities, with climate resilience. Success requires achieving these specific targets."

"[\"The AR Road Safety activity targets reducing road traffic injuries and fatalities in Argentina. Specific outcomes include: a 20% reduction in death rates and non-fatal injuries on selected corridors by YR5; creation of national driver license and traffic records/infractions registry systems (active in 8 provinces by YR5); 30 provinces/municipalities developing Strategic Road Safety Plans; an operational National Road Safety Observatory website; increased compliance with seat belts and speed limits (+60% by YR5 in Safe Corridors); 250 road safety education campaigns; improved emergency response (hospitals implementing protocols); enhanced enforcement via 2000 joint operations and training workshops; and an overall decrease in national death rates. Economic benefits include US$59M-141M in net present value benefits.\",\"Overall success for the AR Road Safety activity would require achieving the targeted outcomes, particularly a 20% reduction in death rates and non-fatal injuries on selected corridors by YR5. Key success triggers include the creation and operationalization of national driver license and traffic records/infractions registry systems (active in 8 provinces), a fully functional National Road Safety Observatory public website, substantial increases in seat belt usage and speed limit compliance in 'Safe Corridors' (+60% by YR5), and the development of 30 Strategic Road Safety Plans by provinces/municipalities. Furthermore, the disbursement of a significant portion of project funds and demonstrating high economic viability (US$59M-141M NPV) are crucial for overall success and potential for a second phase.\"]"

"The Power Sector Improvement and Recovery activity aims to restore adequate public utility service for electricity and water in urban Madagascar and lay the foundation for sustainable, commercially-oriented service. Targeted outcomes include rescuing JIRAMA from its financial/operational crisis, ensuring long-term private management, and improving operational efficiency (generation, loss reduction), revenue management, and financial performance (e.g., positive EBITDA margin). Overall success hinges on transforming JIRAMA into an efficient, profit-making, creditworthy enterprise providing quality services to an expanded customer base, able to self-finance investments, and securing a long-term private operator via a signed PPP agreement."

"[\"The activity targets strengthening institutional effectiveness by implementing an integrated monitoring system for road sector companies, adopting corporate governance plans, establishing a new institutional setup, and executing service performance agreements. It aims to enhance operational efficiency by reducing operating expense ratios (HAC+ARZ to <0.27, HC to <0.27) and operating expenditures per kilometer (HAC+ARZ to <0.60, HC to <0.07), increasing weighted average toll revenue (HAC/>0.67, ARZ/>0.67), implementing an automated tolling system, reducing operating costs (HAC+ARZ by 30%, HC by 515mn HRK annually), and improving staff efficiency (HAC+ARZ staff/km <2.2). Debt service capacity will increase by achieving DSCR and LLCR of 1.0x for all companies and reducing Debt to CFADS ratios (HAC/ARZ to 10.0x, HC to 7.3x).\",\"The activity will be an overall success when: MSTI has an integrated monitoring system, and road networks are managed under rolling investment plans, with all debt optimization transactions executed and the Planning Department fully operational. Operationally, HAC+ARZ and HC achieve operating expense ratios below 0.27 and operating expenditures per kilometer below 0.60 and 0.07, respectively. Weighted average toll revenue exceeds 0.67 for HAC and ARZ, a new automated tolling system is implemented, HAC+ARZ operating expenses are reduced by 30%, HC operating costs by 515mn HRK annually, and HAC+ARZ staff per km is below 2.2. All companies achieve DSCR and LLCR of 1.0x, and Debt to CFADS ratios are 10.0x for HAC/ARZ and 7.3x for HC, enabling self-sustained debt service.\"]"

"Targeted outcomes include providing 8,200 Attil residents with environmentally sound wastewater services by 2022 (including 29.9 km network and treating 600-700 cubic meters/day), implementing improved cross-border wastewater flow measurements (3 functional flowmeters, monthly data, PWA/IWA transboundary mechanism functional by 2022), and establishing a revised water tariff in Attil (1 modified tariff, 4 awareness workshops, 3 training sessions) for sustainable service. Success will be determined by high collection and willingness-to-pay rates, low Unaccounted-For-Water, reduced service breaks, and few customer complaints."

"The MZ - Spatial Development Planning Technical Assistance Project aims to improve national social and economic development planning by introducing, institutionalizing, and mainstreaming multi-sectoral spatial development planning methodologies and practices. Targeted outcomes include the elaboration of at least four climate-change resilient spatial development initiatives (SDIs) for transport and trade corridors at the pre-feasibility stage, and at least four SDIs with a high impact on youth employment and women also elaborated at the pre-feasibility stage. A total of 20 projects under SDIs are targeted to be adopted for feasibility and subsequent investment by the government, the private sector, and/or Public-Private Partnerships (PPPs). Other key outcomes include the full functionality of the Coordination Commission for Studies and Projects (COCEP) at the Ministry of Transport and Telecommunications (MTC), evidenced by 10 inter-ministerial committee meetings per year. Six interactive multi-sector GIS-based SDI planning database modules are to be made accessible to key planning stakeholders. The project also targets 330 work-days of technical exchange visits through South-South Cooperation and the training of 45 government staff in spatial development planning, social and environmental safeguards, impact analysis, and climate change resilience, supported by 200 training days from national universities. Additionally, the operationalization of specific cross-cutting programs for targeted SDIs, such as six HIV/AIDS prevention and mitigation programs, six traffic safety and standard improvement programs, six cargo load management studies, and ten rural mobility strategies and pilot programs, are targeted. Under the Spatial Development Initiative component, a total of four SDIs are to be elaborated at the pre-feasibility stage and two at the profile stage. Furthermore, 20 projects are to be prepared up to pre-feasibility in Maputo, Beira, Nacala, and Lubombo corridors for government, the private sector, and/or PPPs, with 20 potential projects identified as having high employment generation for youth and women per each spatial development initiative."

"The project aims to strengthen integrated water resources management and increase agricultural and water productivity. Targeted outcomes include completing 8 river basin assessments, creating/strengthening 900 minor-level Water User Associations (WUAs), increasing crop yields (e.g., Paddy to 4.3 T/ha), a 68% rise in agricultural output, and a 30% increase in system-wide water use productivity. Other targets involve restoring irrigation service to 93% of designed area (0.60 M ha), expanding irrigation coverage to 79%, increasing cropping intensity to 194%, saving 4.5 M liters diesel/3 M units power annually, converting 15,523 ha water-logged land to crop land, and saving Rs 176 M from flood/drought damage. Success is achieving these targets."

"[\"The activity targets supporting Botswana's recovery from the COVID-19 crisis, aiming for high-income status by 2036. Outcomes include strengthening pandemic response by modernizing social protection, accurately identifying beneficiaries, and improving social spending. It aims to create an enabling business environment for job creation, enhancing firm productivity, improving access to finance, and fostering credit industry confidence. For green recovery, targets include transitioning to solar power, strengthening environmental assessments, attracting private green investment, commissioning 100 MW of renewable energy by Dec 2022, and awarding 10 MW of rooftop solar permits by Dec 2022, with 33% of EMP/EIA processes integrating climate resilience by Dec 2022.\", \"Overall success for the DPF depends on the effective implementation of key policy reforms. These include the National Social Protection Framework, Single Social Registry, Credit Information Bill, Rooftop Solar Guidelines, and amendments to the Environmental Assessment Act. Crucially, success hinges on the government's capacity to enforce new regulations and its ability to attract and facilitate private sector investment, particularly in the green energy sector. Furthermore, expanding social protection coverage to all vulnerable populations, amidst ongoing climate vulnerabilities, is vital.\"]"

"The targeted outcome is to improve solid waste management in selected zones. Overall success would be achieved when: one Regional Waste Management Center (RWMC) is operational; 50% of total waste in selected zones is collected by Island Waste Management Centers (IWMCs); 50% of organic waste in selected zones is treated in IWMCs; 50% of inorganic waste in selected zones is stored and transported to RWMCs; 75% of target beneficiaries are satisfied with the solid waste management approach; 15 IWMCs are established; 50% of RWMC operational and maintenance expenditures are covered by user fees; 15 vehicles are provided to selected IWMCs; and 50% of organic waste treated in IWMCs is put to productive use (composting)."

"Targeted outcomes: Improved urban environments and greater access to improved urban services in Dong Hoi and Hoi An. Success requires by 2023: Dong Hoi developing a new Bao Ninh urban area serving 2,900 households, and increasing improved wastewater service to 7,000 households. Hoi An needs new Co Co River urban areas serving 2,000 households, a $2M annual reduction in coastal flooding damage for 15,600 households, and water supply cost-over-revenue ratio improved to 0.6. By 2022, also includes completing climate-resilient urban infrastructure (new urban development, roads, sewerage, water connections, integrated flood management, water supply improvements) and strengthening staff competencies (40 PPC and 20 PMU staff trained, public awareness programs)."

"Targeted outcomes include strengthening sustainable municipal solid waste management, operationalizing national/local planning, and rehabilitating dumpsites. A primary outcome is Tunisia's entry into the global carbon credits market. Success requires improved public-private partnerships reducing landfill financing shortfalls by at least 10%. The Government must sell Certified Emissions Reductions (CERs) generating total revenues of US$20 million or more. ANGed needs satisfactory institutional/technical capacity with an operational framework and an open procurement system. Additionally, hazardous healthcare waste threats must be reduced, and significant VERs/CERs purchased from solid waste operations."

"Targeted outcomes: 25% of over-exploited ATS fisheries to sustainable levels; 800,000 ha under improved biodiversity management, with two new MPAs (645,000 ha total) and strengthened existing MPAs. A functioning regional governance mechanism, national inter-ministerial committees, and 25% SAP finance secured. Local NAP regulations and resource beneficiary training are also key. Success demands achieving these, plus the regional governance mechanism's full endorsement by all four littoral countries, 125 km of coastline under integrated coastal management, improved ecosystem health via Ecosystem Approach to Fisheries Management, and substantial progress toward SAP goals like 15-20% IUU fishing reduction and 20% marine/coastal habitat protection."

"Targeted outcomes: Strengthen Tunisia's electricity transmission system (increasing South-North corridor capacity from 721 MW to 3,090 MW, adding 384 km lines, 3 substations). Improve STEG's commercial performance (100% smart metering for high/medium voltage customers, 100% large low-voltage customers in revenue protection, 99% private sector invoice collection, cash recovery index from 83% to 92%, STEG's ability to pay Carthage Power Company, enhanced consumption measurement, citizen engagement). Success requires achieving all these specific targets."

"[\"The activity targets improved efficiency and reliability of Montenegro's power system, enhanced supply security, and closer integration into regional electricity markets. Specific outcomes include increased consumers choosing suppliers, liberalized regional markets, and a functioning regional electricity market. For Montenegro, fewer blackouts at substations, increased hydropower generation at lower costs, and more efficient EPCG operations due to improved telecommunications are expected. Economic benefits include €250k annual savings from reduced leased telecom capacity and regional market savings of up to €6.7 billion.\", \"Overall success hinges on regional countries meeting ECSEE Treaty commitments, establishing functioning liberalized electricity markets integrated into the EU market, and ongoing restructuring. Specific criteria include satisfactory completion of all component sub-projects, operational communication links with two neighboring dispatch centers, a 50% reduction in unserved energy from forced outages in sub-project areas, a 75% reduction in Perucica HPP trash rack O&M costs, a 20% reduction in turbine runner maintenance costs, and EPCG's collection efficiency improving to 94% or above by end of 2008.\"]"

"Targeted outcomes include increasing access to improved sanitation for 250,000 people, improved water supply for 250,000 people, and strengthening environmental sanitation management in GAMA. Specifics: 400 tons/year BOD pollution removed, US$3.1M annual health/time saving benefits, 1,350 productive years avoided loss. Success requires achieving these targets, an ERR of 14% (NPV $26.1M), strong water supply viability, institutional strengthening, and implementing sustainability measures like O&M capacity building, social accountability, and carbon credit generation."

"The Kathmandu Valley Wastewater Management Project targets sustainable wastewater management, aiming for improved access to and efficiency of wastewater services, enhanced institutional development, and improved governance. Specific targeted outcomes and indicators by the end of the project (2018) include: an increase in sewage collection through the sewerage network from less than 5% in 2012 to more than 80%; an increase in wastewater treatment capacity from 16.4 MLD to 90.5 MLD; an increase in samples of effluent from wastewater treatment plants complying with national effluent standards from less than 5% in 2012 to almost 100%; and the provision of new sewer connections to 58,850 buildings (168,900 households), with specific targets for poor households (30% of new beneficiaries) and women-headed households (100% of those in the service area). The ultimate impact by 2020 is to increase the population connected to the wastewater network from 1.20 million in 2012 to 1.96 million, with 30% of new beneficiaries from poor households and 100% of women-headed households in the service area benefitting. Additionally, water quality in the Bagmati River and its tributaries in the urban zone of Kathmandu Valley should improve to national effluent discharge standards by 2020. Key outputs contributing to these outcomes include the rehabilitation and expansion of the sewerage network (87 km of interceptor sewers, 166 km of combined sewers, 151 km of new sewers, and 77 km sewers/33 km drains for separation of sanitary/storm water systems), modernization and expansion of five wastewater treatment plants to treat 90.5 MLD to acceptable effluent standards with septage and sludge treatment, generation of 910 kW of power from sludge, and 6.9 tons per day of ash for fertilizer/building material, along with the construction of decentralized wastewater treatment systems (DEWATS) in low-income areas. Capacities are to be strengthened through institutionalized operational and financial reforms, including an operational sewerage asset database, wastewater master plan, GIS, asset management plan, environmental monitoring, customer grievance redress system, improved financial management for KUKL, pro-poor and gender-inclusive sanitation services, and onsite sanitation solutions in low-income areas. To be considered an overall success, the project needs to achieve these specific performance targets by 2018/2020, demonstrating improved wastewater service access and efficiency, enhanced institutional development, and better governance. This also requires the sustained commitment of the government to improving wastewater services, the successful delivery of capacity building and public-private partnership outcomes, and the timely completion of the Melamchi tunnel to ensure adequate water supply for WWTPs. Financial sustainability is key, with the economic internal rate of return (EIRR) exceeding 12.0% (estimated at 14.6%) and the financial internal rate of return (FIRR) exceeding the weighted average cost of capital (estimated at 1.11%, with 3.22% achieved), ensuring that the project provides sufficient economic and financial returns and is affordable for low-income households."

"The targeted outcomes include collecting over BTN 5 billion in GST, achieving a \"Green\" score for debt transparency (full information on loans, <yearly periodicity, debt management strategy with targets), collecting 5% of the Sustainable Development Fee from non-MDPR leisure arrivals, reducing ambient PM10 in Pasakha to 120 µg/m³, incorporating 4 low-emission mitigation actions into NDCs, over 150 companies benefiting from the National Credit Guarantee Scheme, 90% of the eligible population receiving two COVID-19 vaccine doses, and 75% of GBV survivor service requests receiving support. Overall success requires meeting these targets, safeguarding social sector spending, fostering macroeconomic stability, offering diversified employment through sustainable tourism, job creation, health and economic normalization, and achieving positive environmental, poverty, social, and gender impacts in the medium to long run."

"The targeted outcome of the Hunan Dongjiang Lake Integrated Environmental Protection and Management Project is to achieve integrated environmental protection in the Dongjiang Lake basin by the end of 2021. For the activity to be considered an overall success, the following specific targets must be met: public satisfaction with environmental and municipal services in the project area must increase to 80% (from a 72% baseline); wastewater collection and treatment must increase to 80% (from 5%); pollution loads to Dongjiang Lake must be reduced by 1,389 tons of chemical oxygen demand, 100 tons of total phosphorus, 227 tons of total nitrogen, and 80,000 tons of garbage. Additionally, about 131,888 people need to be serviced with new or improved water supply, and approximately 24,600 mu of farmland protected by improved flood capacity. Success also hinges on the operationalization of six township wastewater treatment plants and 2,856 rural wastewater treatment facilities, an increase in solid waste collection and treatment, the application of green fertilizers and pest control in farmland, the establishment of urban-rural water supply systems (including two WSPs and extensive pipelines), river course rehabilitation (clearing/dredging, green embankments, increased flood control), integrated ecosystem rehabilitation (fish stocking, new wetlands, reforestation, improved bamboo forest efficiency, alternative livelihood training), and strengthened environmental and project management capacity (monitoring center, research center, staff training, M&E system)."

"The Rural Productivity Project targets several key outcomes, aiming to increase incomes and employment for small rural producers in Panama, while conserving globally important biodiversity and protecting associated forest, mountain, and marine-coastal ecosystems. Specific outcomes are expected to include the reduction of natural resource depletion, deforestation, rural poverty, over-exploitation, illegal harvesting, and lax enforcement through the consolidation and strengthening of existing management plans for globally significant biodiversity. By the end of the project, improved local and national institutional capacity is expected for the management of 14 protected areas (675,775 ha), with at least 50,000 ha of forests and other natural ecosystems under effective conservation. Furthermore, 60% of districts are expected to incorporate biodiversity aspects into their policies and plans, and community and rural producer associations are anticipated to implement at least 450 natural resource, sustainable agriculture, and conservation subprojects.Specific outcomes for individual protected areas include: Parque Internacional La Amistad (PILA) aims for land-use plan adoption and implementation, increased ecofriendly production, and a 40% reduction in hunting. Santa Fe targets a 50% reduction in forest clearing and hunting, along with increased ecofriendly production. Cerro Hoya expects new park management arrangements, reduced deforestation, and increased ecofriendly production. Omar Torrijos plans for land-use plan adoption, working management arrangements, and increased ecofriendly production. Isla Bastimento anticipates a pilot management arrangement, solid waste management, and a developed seashore resource plan. Volcán Barú also aims for a pilot PA management, a developed and adopted land-use plan, increased ecofriendly production, and a 40% reduction in hunting.The activity would be considered an overall success if, by the end of the project, the following are achieved: local and national institutional capacity to manage 14 protected areas (675,775 ha) is improved, with the capacity building criteria score reaching the highest level for all 14 PAs; at least 50,000 ha of forests and other natural ecosystems of global biodiversity significance in the buffer zones of protected areas and biological corridors are under effective conservation (protection and sustainable management); biodiversity of global significance is under effective conservation as measured by vegetation cover and indicator species of conservation interest; 60% of districts in the project area have incorporated biodiversity aspects into sector policies and plans, adapted appropriate regulations, and implemented these plans; community associations and rural producer organizations implement at least 450 natural resource, sustainable agriculture, and conservation subprojects; and protected area co-management agreements under implementation increase by at least 100%."

"The Shandong Groundwater Protection Project targets improved conservation and sustainable use of groundwater in Weifang-Zibo by 2023, aiming for average groundwater level recovery over 120,000 ha in the depression zone and an additional 60 million m³ annual surface water retained. By 2022, key outputs include creating 880 ha wetlands, increasing annual water supply to Mata/Judian Lake wetlands to 47 million m³, and an operational water monitoring system in Huantai. Also, 345 km of canals will be rehabilitated, Nanzhai/Juchenghe reservoirs will retain up to 8.3 million m³ rainwater, water diversion capacity will rise to 49 million m³/year, and 40 km of water supply pipes will be installed. Capacity building includes training 150 staff, publishing 3 knowledge products, and creating 1,680 construction and 170 operation jobs (with gender/poverty targets). Success requires achieving all these targets."

"Targeted outcomes: Improved capacity for basin water resource and irrigation management, increased irrigated agriculture productivity. Specifics include enhanced institutional effectiveness, strengthened participatory irrigation management, rehabilitation of infrastructure, and support for climate change adaptation. Quantifiable benefits: reliable water delivery, increased farmer output, income, and economic value. Success requires: 12 functional national/provincial basin agencies; 45 functional Irrigation Commissions; 390,000 Ha with over 15% agricultural production increase; establishment of functional management systems, WUAFs, and compliance with governance/safeguard policies."
