"The targeted outcomes are to reduce poverty, boost shared prosperity, establish long-term energy supply reliability, improve the energy sector's financial viability, enhance its governance and accountability, and mitigate power shortage impacts on poor regions. Overall success requires a financially viable energy sector enabling increased investments, reduced burden on public finances, and improved power supply reliability. It also necessitates strengthened governance, transparency, and accountability through reforms like establishing an economic regulator and equitable power shortage management."

"The activity targets assisting GoT in its reform agenda, providing fiscal support, and sustaining public/economic reforms. Outcomes include strengthened PFM (transparency), better fiscal policy (maintain public services), and an improved business environment (private sector development/job creation). It aims to alleviate poverty (basic needs poverty reached 22.5% by 2009), enhance budget execution, and benefit poor households via energy reforms (access, lower costs, price stability). Success means GoT's effective reform implementation, leading to sustained good governance, fiscal stability, private sector growth, poverty reduction, and improved energy access for the poor."

"[\"The project targets building on LIDP1 achievements in basic infrastructure, job creation, and SME/SMI capacity, while enhancing municipal capacity for urban development. It aims to reinforce participatory approaches for transparency. Key outcomes include increasing access to services and integrating underserviced neighborhoods via urban/neighborhood infrastructure (roads, water/sanitation, health centers, schools, markets). It also seeks to strengthen municipalities and stakeholders to plan/manage urban services transparently and inclusively, and ensure correct project implementation including 8.3 km of road rehabilitation and environmental sustainability.\",\"The activity will be an overall success if it achieves increased access to services in underserviced neighborhoods, their integration into the urban fabric, and completion of planned infrastructure (e.g., 8.3 km of road rehabilitation). Success hinges on investments being cost-effective, equitable, sustainable, operable/maintainable, generating employment, and benefiting vulnerable groups. Strengthened capacity of municipalities and stakeholders for transparent, inclusive urban service management is critical, alongside sustained participatory engagement and Mayor/citizen involvement. Effective mitigation of all environmental and social impacts, including no long-term adverse effects, and correct implementation, monitoring, and evaluation of all project activities are also essential.\"]"

"The targeted outcomes of the CO Integrated Mass Transit Systems activity are: (i) to develop high-quality and sustainable Bus Rapid Transit Systems (BRTS) in participating cities to enhance mobility along strategic mass transit corridors; (ii) to improve accessibility to public transportation for low-income populations; and (iii) to build greater institutional capacity within the Borrower's public transportation institutions for formulating integrated urban transport policies and improving urban transport planning and traffic management. To consider the activity an overall success, the following would need to be achieved: (i) for improved mobility and quality of public transport services in strategic mass transit corridors, there must be a reduction in generalized door-to-door travel cost (fare, time) to users, and a percentage of people rating the system as being better than the previous system; (ii) for improved accessibility to low-income populations, there must be increased use by the poor (two poorest quintiles) of public transport services along the area of influence of the planned corridors, both in absolute (number of passengers) and relative terms (as a percentage of total number of passengers), compared to the baseline without the mass transit system; and (iii) for enhanced institutional capacity for urban transport policy formulation and system development, at the local level, system occupation per square meter must be below a given threshold while maintaining no subsidies, and at the national level, at least three BRTSs schemes must be operating successfully in targeted participating cities."

"[\"Targeted outcomes include identifying and developing bankable climate adaptation/mitigation projects (water, environment, agriculture, forestry) using a landscape approach. These are intended to graduate to investment facilities, attract over \\u20ac500M private finance, improve vulnerable group wellbeing/livelihoods, enhance ecosystem health, and address climate change/SDGs through early-stage de-risking and inclusive development.\", \"Success requires projects to successfully graduate to investment facilities, become bankable through grant funding/technical assistance, contribute to attracting \\u20ac500M+ private capital, meet DFCD investment criteria (e.g., Rio Marker 2, commercial viability, vulnerable group impact, financial additionality, no excluded activities), and achieve tangible positive environmental/social impacts, protecting communities and biodiversity.\"]"

"The Alma Transmission Project targets providing southern Kazakhstan, especially the Almaty region, with improved access to reliable, cost-effective, high-quality electricity, addressing growing supply constraints and power deficits (e.g., 865 MW in 2007, projected to 2,300 MW by 2020). It supports broader societal objectives like improved employment, incomes, and standard of living by increasing regional power transmission network capacity. Success is when power deficits are resolved, and reliable, quality electricity supply contributes to regional economic and human development."

"The Rampur Hydropower Project aims to: 1) Improve reliability of India's Northern Grid by adding 412 MW (1,770 million units/year) low-carbon energy. 2) Enhance SJVN's effectiveness in sustainable hydropower development, promoting good practices and strengthening capacity. 3) Support a low-carbon growth strategy, contributing to India's 11th Plan hydropower capacity, and provide US$12M annual royalty to Himachal Pradesh. Success requires achieving targeted generation at competitive tariffs, effective implementation of SJVN reforms, successful social and environmental mitigation, institutional viability, and avoiding cost overruns."

"The targeted outcomes include improving energy efficiency in industrial enterprises by designing and establishing a financing mechanism for energy-saving investments. This aims to boost productivity, cut costs, reduce greenhouse gas emissions, enhance international competitiveness, and free up natural gas and electricity for export. Success would be measured by the effective operation of the financing mechanism, FIs developing expertise in EE lending, IEs adopting efficient technologies, demonstrating significant energy savings and cost reductions, and achieving reductions in GHG emissions, ultimately leading the Government of Uzbekistan to scale up the pilot project with more banks and a larger credit line."

"The Changzhi Sustainable Urban Transport Project targets improving urban mobility safely, sustainably, and efficiently. Outcomes include increased corridor capacity, enhanced public transport services/infrastructure, improved non-motorized transport quality/accessibility, boosted traffic safety, and MGC capacity building. Goals also cover reducing accidents/congestion, bus prioritization, better data collection, timely traveler information, improved public transit capacity/efficiency, and mitigating parking issues. Success requires effective plan implementation, diligent construction/operational impact management, and successful institutional capacity building to ensure sustained improvements and a people-oriented transport system."

"Targeted outcomes: Integrate Agrarian Reform Beneficiaries (ARBs), farmers, and landless rural workers into agricultural value chains. Improve smallholder farmers' market access and competitiveness in ARC clusters. Enhance farmer organization capabilities through training. Secure land titles for on-farm investment. Develop rural infrastructure for productivity and market access. Provide matching grants to boost competitiveness and market standing. Resolve supply chain inefficiencies. Enable producers to meet market demands for scale and quality. Overall success is the demonstrably achieved realization of all these objectives, confirmed by project evaluations."

"The targeted outcomes of this activity are to strengthen policy frameworks for: i) relief and recovery for vulnerable people and firms, and ii) recovery and resilience in infrastructure provision. Key results include: expanded bank credit to SMEs; increased mobile wallets (for men and women); more healthcare workers with personal protective equipment; increased schools with gender-based violence training; reduced public sector arrears to the energy utility; and increased safe water supplied by urban utilities. Success requires achieving these key results through effective implementation of supported policy actions, such as regulatory changes to increase SME finance, facilitating wider e-money use, issuing health facility guidelines for worker protection and waste management, approving a gender-based violence action plan for schools, revising the electricity utility Partnership Agreement to reduce arrears, and approving urban water utility Emergency Response Plans to maintain services and build resilience."

"The activity aims to sustain access to critical infrastructure services for firms and households post-COVID-19, establishing policy foundations for recovery through sustainable and resilient infrastructure. Targeted outcomes include a legal framework for low-carbon growth, accelerated digitalization of the power grid, closing energy access gaps, greening logistics/urban mobility, and strengthening private/long-term infrastructure financing. Success entails achieving these, promoting non-conventional renewable energy, increasing PPP viability, reducing logistics costs, integrating cleaner bus fleets, boosting infrastructure financing, and generating positive impacts on poverty, jobs, and environment with reduced emissions."

"The targeted outcomes of the Public Sector Efficiency and Green Recovery DPL are to enhance public sector efficiency and establish foundations for faster, private sector-led growth in Serbia. Specifically, under Pillar A (Public Sector Efficiency), reforms aim for better management of public resources, efficiency gains to ensure public finance sustainability, and improvements in public investment management, public procurement, public administration, and State-Owned Enterprise (SOE) management, including stricter environmental safeguards and green procurement. Under Pillar B (Growth Enhancing Reforms), outcomes include expanding the leasing market, facilitating access to foreign financing and international transactions, improving state-aid regulation, financial sector diversification, promoting internationalization of Serbian businesses, better control of state aid, and increasing private sector investment and growth. Poverty and social impacts are expected to include job creation in the private sector, easier access to finance for SMEs, entrepreneurs, and farmers, increased foreign investment, and poverty reduction. Environmental impacts are anticipated to be neutral or small positive, driven by stricter environmental safeguards and green procurement guidelines. To consider the activity an overall success, it would need to demonstrate a tangible increase in the efficiency of managing public resources, successful implementation of the targeted reforms, and the establishment of foundations for faster, private sector-led growth. Success would also be evidenced by job creation in the private sector, easier access to finance leading to business expansion, increased foreign investment, expansion of Serbian businesses internationally, and a reduction in poverty. Additionally, the adoption and enforcement of stricter environmental safeguards in public investment management and the successful integration of green procurement guidelines would contribute to overall success."

"Targeted outcomes: (i) Strengthen efficiency/financial sustainability of social protection/health services (e.g., improved pro-poor targeting, sustainable Universal Medical Coverage); (ii) Support climate change mitigation/adaptation (e.g., reduce emissions/deforestation from unsustainable cocoa, promote renewable energy); (iii) Foster digital economy (e.g., inclusive platforms, e-tax, secure land tenure, boost local revenue). Success: Achieving these PDOs, resulting in positive poverty/social/environmental effects, including sustainable cocoa production outcomes."

"The targeted outcomes for the TradeMark East Africa - Strategy 2 (Regional) activity include a reduction in trade transaction costs for imports and exports for selected value chains, a decrease in the time required to clear and move goods from origin to destination, an increase in product shelf life in export markets for selected value chains, and an increase in the number of MSMEs exporting produce and the volume of exports. The program also aims for improved regional harmonisation of trade policies, enhanced capacity for trade policy formulation and implementation in East Africa, and an independent evaluation to coherently articulate results from TMA's trade policy programme implemented in Strategy 2. The activity would be considered an overall success upon the achievement of these reductions and increases, along with improvements in policy harmonisation and capacity, and the successful articulation of results through the independent evaluation."

"The activity targets mitigating the immediate COVID-19 impact and advancing an inclusive, sustainable recovery in Croatia. Success means: Pillar 1 (Mitigation): Wage subsidy covers 50%+ tourism workers in 2020. Complementary health insurance coverage increases by 15%+. Non-financial firms' liquidity ratios stay above 1 (2016-2018 average). Incoming bankruptcy cases increase not exceeding 30% (vs. 2017-2019 average). Pillar 2 (Recovery): 19,000 elderly receive a new benefit. 90%+ emission unit auction revenues go to climate. Credit registry covers 80% of adults. 35%+ EU Competitiveness & Cohesion funds paid out to beneficiaries (from 26.2% in 2019)."

"[\"The CCEU Initiation Plan 2020 targets two main outcomes. First, to develop new project concept notes and finalized project proposals for various funding windows, including a GCF project on smallholder agriculture resilience, an Adaptation Fund proposal for agriculture in Northern Viet Nam, GEF7 PIFs on environment/eco-tourism, IKI program proposals (adaptation, green financing, SMEs, energy transition), a GCF mitigation project feasibility study, and an ocean spatial planning project for Norway. Second, to undertake strategic policy analysis, dialogues, and stakeholder engagement to foster innovative policies and ideas for long-term program development. This includes finalizing and launching a Climate Public Expenditure and Investment Review (CPEIR), the Provincial Environmental Performance Index (EPI), a report on the impacts of 1.5/2\b0C, the blue economy in Viet Nam, and a concept note and roadmap for circular economy, alongside an assessment of investment potentials for Viet Nam’s Power Development Master Plan (PDPVIII).\", \"The activity would be considered an overall success if all planned activities under both outputs are successfully completed. This entails the successful development and finalization of all specified project concept notes and proposals, leading to the mobilization of funding and preparation for project implementation. It also requires the successful finalization and launch of the strategic policy assessments, innovative tools, and reports (CPEIR, EPI, 1.5/2\b0C report, blue economy report, circular economy concept note/roadmap), and the completion of the investment potential assessment for PDPVIII. Success is measured by achieving these foundational results that enable future long-term program development in climate change and environment, as documented in the Annual Progress Report and Final Report against the work-plan.\"]"

"[\"The project aims to establish Bagre as a functional agribusiness growth pole model, generating spillover effects for future growth poles in Burkina Faso. Key outcomes include attracting private investments by expanding scope beyond agriculture to include backward/post-production linkages, improving technical/business management skills, enhancing regional/global economic linkages, fostering a conducive business environment, establishing a New Zone Authority with mandates for planning/management/land allocation, implementing a PPP framework, and developing critical infrastructure (irrigation, transport, GIS monitoring). It also targets supporting SMEs and smallholders via the FASBagré matching grant, creating new enterprises, and increasing irrigated hectares.\",\"The activity will be considered an overall success upon achieving an increase in both domestic and foreign private investment in Bagre, an increase in the total number of jobs created, and an increase in the overall value of production within the Bagre Growth Pole. Intermediate success indicators include the number of irrigated hectares developed, the creation of new enterprises, and the number of firms benefiting from the matching grant fund (FASBagré).\"]"

"The targeted outcomes are: 1) improved natural resource management; 2) transition towards greener physical capital; and 3) human capital development for green sectors. Success requires establishing ICZM frameworks, strengthening fishing laws, modernizing water management, implementing aquifer contracts; creating low-carbon growth and renewable energy regulatory frameworks, pollution control (green taxes, reduced subsidies); promoting green agriculture tech, aquaculture investment, and ecotourism training for rural populations."

"Targeted outcomes: Increase electricity export from newly constructed hydropower generation stations in the Ajara region and improve electricity supply to the city of Batumi by strengthening the south-western transmission grid. Key results: Increased total electricity exported from Ajara HPPs; enhanced transmission grid connection capacity (measured by short circuit capacity of Batumi substation); improved quality of power supply to Batumi (reduced outage duration/frequency, eliminated voltage drop); increased power exchange with Turkey. Success requires achieving these results, stimulating new hydropower investment, enhancing the electricity market (via updated metering, balancing, and trading systems), and developing hydropower sustainably (with SEIA and sector studies)."

"[\"The activity targets increased government capacity for disaster risk management (DRM), encompassing protection of Maldives' natural and built environment from disasters, guiding policy-making, and providing operational guidance for disaster response, ultimately leading to a more functional DRM system and improved infrastructure resilience.\",\"A key outcome is the government's effective implementation of adaptation activities under its National Determined Contribution (NDC) plan, aimed at reducing risks from weather, climate, and oceanic hazards to acceptable levels and addressing long-term climate change consequences and extreme events.\",\"Increased Government of Maldives (GoM) preparedness and response capacities for public health threats, achieving the required core International Health Regulations (IHR) capacities, is a primary targeted outcome.\",\"The activity also aims to further develop the country's regulatory, institutional, technical, and financial capacities to better respond to the impact of natural hazards and emergency situations, consolidating its ongoing DRM agenda.\",\"Targeted outcomes include enhancing the institutional and regulatory framework to manage climate and disaster risk (Pillar A), integrating climate change and disaster risk reduction in key sectors (Pillar B), and improving human health crisis preparedness and response capacity (Pillar C).\",\"For overall success, it is essential to mitigate the identified high macroeconomic and institutional capacity risks, including potential changes in key counterpart agencies and limited institutional capacity for coordination and enforcement of standards.\",\"The continued operational status and availability of sufficient resources for the Pandemic Emergency Financing Facility (PEF) are crucial for the activity's success.\",\"Effective multi-agency coordination among key stakeholders (MoF, NDMA, ME, MNPI, MoH) and diligent implementation of policy reforms, especially under the leadership of the NDMA, are vital for project success.\",\"Full adoption and implementation of the National Emergency Operations Plan (NEOP) is necessary to mitigate risks to Pillar A outcomes and overall project success.\",\"Consideration and integration of gender, age, and vulnerability sensitivities in crisis response, particularly through the Health Emergency Operations Plan (HEOP) and social protection programs, are critical for achieving positive social impacts and overall success.\",\"The macroeconomic framework must maintain adequacy by mitigating downside fiscal, external, and political risks to ensure the activity's overall success.\"]"

"The activity targets mitigating the economic and social impact of COVID-19, particularly for vulnerable populations and MSMEs, and laying foundations for strong, resilient economic recovery. Key outcomes include strengthening social safety nets, ensuring access to finance, safeguarding fiscal and debt sustainability, improving the business climate, enhancing disaster and environmental risk management, and transforming agriculture for climate resilience. Overall success hinges on effectively cushioning the pandemic's impact, achieving a sustained economic recovery with a return to positive growth, maintaining fiscal capacity, and implementing crucial institutional and policy reforms to enhance resilience against future shocks while also securing positive environmental outcomes."

"[\"Targeted outcomes for Pillar 1: Panama automatically exchanges financial information for tax purposes, enhances its role against tax evasion, and consolidates financial management under ISTMO and TSA for transparent, efficient public resource use. Pillar 2: Improved CCT targeting/coverage to benefit extreme poor/vulnerable, and long-term improvements in education/training to facilitate youth school-to-labor market transitions and enhance firm productivity via skill mismatch reduction. Pillar 3: Enhanced energy/water regulatory framework promoting energy efficiency, pollution prevention, emission reduction, and improved water service delivery.\",\"The activity would be an overall success if Panama successfully implements automatic exchange of financial information for tax purposes and efficiently manages public resources through ISTMO and TSA. Success also requires improved CCT targeting and expanded coverage for vulnerable groups, smoother youth transitions from school to the labor market, and increased firm productivity through addressed skill mismatches. Additionally, effective implementation of energy efficiency regulations resulting in pollution and emission reductions, along with strengthened water sector regulation and improved service delivery, are crucial. Overall, positive effects on poverty reduction and shared prosperity in the medium- to long-term, driven by stronger institutions and enhanced regulatory frameworks, signify success.\"]"

"The activity aims to support Uruguayan agricultural producers in adopting more appropriate, environmentally sustainable, and climate-smart practices. Targeted outcomes include: establishing an integrated information and decision-making support system for the agricultural sector; executing climate-smart subprojects to reduce vulnerability to extreme events, improve productivity, increase water availability, and foster energy efficiency; strengthening the capacity of producers, advisory service providers, and MGAP officials in natural resource management and climate change strategies; and ensuring efficient project management, monitoring, and communication of results. "

"The targeted outcome of the project is increased end-use energy efficiency in project areas. Overall success by 2026 requires: 266 GWh annual energy savings and 245,000 tons CO2e emissions avoided per year. By 2025, key outputs for success include: installation of 5M smart meters/intelligent energy management elements, 160 MW distributed solar PV systems, 10,000 e-vehicles/charging stations. Also, 200 women (poor/socially backward prioritized) trained as commercial drivers; 500+ end-users (40%+ women) in efficiency workshops; and 100+ agency staff trained in energy-efficient technologies/business models."

"The activity targets: improved lives for 100,000 lifeline customers and reliable electricity for 1.3 million total customers (200,000 new, 1.1 million existing) in urban Dhaka, via energy-efficient, climate- and disaster-resilient infrastructure. An annual reduction of 14,699.3 tons of CO2e GHG emissions is aimed for. DESCO's institutional capacity will be strengthened in O&M, emergency management, commercial borrowing (roadmap, action plan), and safeguard management (in-house unit, GESI strategy). PPP capacity of relevant authorities will be enhanced (program, pipeline, policy). Gender-specific targets include 90 female students aware of energy sector opportunities, 300 students (30% girls) informed on careers, and 40 DESCO staff (90% women) trained on O&M."

"Targeted outcomes include by 2020: 63% forest cover, 750M m³ forest stock, 150k tons carbon sequestration annually, and a carbon monitoring system. By 2018: 1% project area forest cover increase, higher yields for tea tree oil (525 kg/ha), orange (22.5k kg/ha), tea leaf (3k kg/ha), 6M person-days jobs, and CNY9M/year for 20k households from land leasing. Success requires sustained government policy, stakeholder commitment, sufficient funds, farmers' adoption of sustainable practices, effective training, and natural disaster mitigation."

"The Sarulla Geothermal Power Generation Project targets the construction and commissioning of three geothermal power generation units with a total capacity of 320 MW, expanding geothermal power generation in North Sumatra. Outcomes include displacing fossil fuel-generated power, reducing 1.3 million tons of CO2 emissions annually, creating local employment, increasing private sector investment in geothermal, boosting electrification, and demonstrating the viability of large-scale IPP geothermal projects. Overall success requires the successful implementation and operation of the 320 MW plant, deployment of concessional cofinancing, and achievement of these environmental and socio-economic benefits."

"Targeted outcomes include reducing air pollution, improving public health in BTH, transforming Hebei's economic structure (less coal, more clean energy), fostering public transport, mitigating biomass burning, and strengthening environmental governance and worker support. Specifically, goals include reductions in coal (12.4M tons), CO2 (18M tons), PM2.5 (19%), SO2 (33%), NOx (14%), and VOCs (5%) from 2012 baselines. Success requires completing 17 policy actions by March 2016, securing consistent financing, resolving monitoring and enforcement capacity gaps, and robust enforcement."

"Targeted outcomes include enhanced sustainable urbanization and urban population welfare via improved access to inclusive, resilient, and sustainable urban services in Djizzak, Sirdaryo, and Khorezm. This involves: 1) Providing inclusive municipal/tourist infrastructure (e.g., 6ha park/community center in Havast, digital museum/greenway in Khiva, area-based development in 3 Djizzak mahallas with universal/women-friendly designs). 2) Enhancing climate-resilient WSS with smart systems (universal coverage in 3 Djizzak mahallas for 4,598 households; improved bulk water supply, SCADA; environmental solid waste services with collection points). 3) Strengthening urban governance, institutional capacity (strategic planning, O&M, financial management), and livelihood support (tourism/SME training for women/youth)."

"[\"The targeted outcomes include the establishment and operation of an integrated multimodal logistics facility in Zamyn Uud, providing efficient and cost-effective transport services with nondiscriminatory access, improved capacity utilization, and international best practices for terminal management. The project aims to strengthen the Mongolia Railway Authority, enhance staff capacity, and facilitate private sector participation. It also seeks to significantly reduce logistics costs, promote international trade, increase Mongolia's trade competitiveness, generate substantial economic and social benefits (job creation, improved living standards, poverty reduction), develop the local economy, and improve family welfare, especially for women.\",\"To be considered an overall success, the activity requires the facility to be robustly viable and sustainable, achieving high economic and financial internal rates of return. A sufficient trial and testing period must ensure successful operation, supported by adequate maintenance spares and training for approximately 50 personnel. Key milestones include physical completion by December 2015, civil works by December 2014, and six months of trial operations starting July 2015, with loan account closure by June 2016. Effective implementation of environmental and public health mitigation plans, and a successfully executed management contract for facility operation are also crucial.\"]"

"Targeted outcomes include developing and operating a 60 MWac solar plant, installing 78 MW DC solar capacity, achieving a $0.03877/kWh tariff to lower electricity prices, reducing annual GHG emissions by 110,700 tons, and promoting gender equality through local employment, female internships, anti-harassment policy, and community engagement. Success requires achieving commercial operations by June 29, 2022, maintaining the low tariff for 20 years, meeting emissions reduction targets, successfully implementing gender mainstreaming measures, and demonstrating transparent, competitively-bid, low-cost clean energy at scale, thus providing an alternative to coal-fired generation."

"Targeted outcomes: Strengthen social stabilization for IDPs/returnees, addressing humanitarian/recovery needs, supporting reintegration, securing legal rights, and mitigating security threats. Includes sustainable livelihoods, economic recovery, social inclusion, restored basic services, enhanced protection, and increased justice access for vulnerable groups, reducing discrimination. Success: vulnerable groups reintegrated, legal rights secured, security threats addressed, livelihoods improved, services restored, protection ensured, justice accessible, and social harmony fostered."

"Targeted outcomes for EMRP 2 are: reconstruction/rehabilitation of critical infrastructure (roads, electricity, water); strengthened governance; prepared medium-term sector strategies; enhanced capacity for decentralization, local development, and environmental/social management; and robust program/project management. Overall success requires: achieved infrastructure reconstruction, demonstrable governance strengthening, successful strategy development, effective capacity building in all targeted areas, enhanced local development, strong program management, and full, timely implementation of all environmental/social safeguards ensuring sustainable activities per World Bank policies."

"The targeted outcomes of the Sustainable Cities activity are to improve the quality, efficiency, and sustainability of service delivery in participating municipalities. Specifically, Component A aims to enable municipalities to improve their environmental, social, and financial/economic dimensions of sustainability, moving them from a planned city to a smart city. Component B is intended to finance sustainable investments that support environmental, social, or financial sustainability in sectors like public transport, water and sanitation, solid waste management, and energy. For the activity to be considered an overall success, there would need to be demonstrable improvements in the quality, efficiency, and sustainability of service delivery across participating municipalities. Furthermore, successful implementation of this project, as the first in a series, would enable Iller Bank and the World Bank to expand this approach as more sustainable investments are identified and additional municipalities meet eligibility requirements for financing."

"The targeted outcomes of the activity, \"TA for Capacity Development in Hydropower and Mining Sector,\" are to strengthen Lao PDR's institutional capacity for the sustainable development and management of its natural resources and extractive industry. This involves:1. Improving project planning for natural resources.2. Enhancing transparent mobilization and management of revenues from hydropower.3. Implementing mining regulations effectively and managing mineral resources.4. Improving practices in alluvial and small-scale mining.5. Increasing transparency in overall revenue management.More specifically, expected outcomes include:1. Improved institutional capacity of the Government of Lao and its agencies, as measured by:   a. The adoption of river-basin planning.   b. The application of lessons learned from NT2 to other hydropower projects.   c. The adoption of mining regulation.   d. The initiation of the Extractive Industries Transparency Initiative (EITI).   e. The transparent award of future concessions.   f. The regulation of alluvial and small-scale mining.2. In the hydropower sector:   a. Increased use of river-basin planning to optimize hydropower potential, water/land use, and minimize cumulative impacts.   b. Optimization of revenue benefits for the government from export-oriented hydropower projects.   c. Establishment of a Lao Hydropower Learning Center to reinforce policy, analytical underpinning, and expand training/capacity building.   d. Adaptation of the EITI to the hydropower sector.   e. Strengthened planning and monitoring capacity within MEM departments to enhance analytical competencies in natural resource optimization and planning.   f. Designed, tested, and disseminated reporting tools for monitoring and evaluating operators' compliance.   g. Adapted concession agreement templates and drafted guidelines/reporting templates to incorporate National Policy on Sustainable Hydropower (NPSH) provisions, reviewed in light of ESIA and Compensation and Resettlement Decree revisions.3. In the mining sector:   a. Deepening legislative reform through the revised Mining Law and preparing detailed regulations and guidelines.   b. Strengthening supervisory institutions and enhancing clarity of roles/responsibilities.   c. Upgrading the government's mine title registry and cadastre systems for better monitoring and security of tenure.   d. Enhancing geological data processing and dissemination to strengthen the business environment.   e. Enhancing operational efficiency and health/safety conditions for artisanal and small-scale operators.   f. Improving investment appraisal practices.   g. Strengthening capacity for mine inspection, monitoring/evaluation, and EITI implementation.Overall success for this activity would be achieved when Lao PDR's institutional capacity for sustainable natural resource development and extractive industry management is demonstrably strengthened. This includes the widespread adoption of river-basin planning, the successful application of NT2 lessons, the effective adoption and implementation of new mining regulations, the full initiation of the EITI across both sectors, and a transparent process for awarding future concessions, alongside robust regulation of alluvial and small-scale mining. Success would also entail effective training and capacity building, particularly for the 120 expected beneficiaries, and the operationalization of improved monitoring and enforcement mechanisms across relevant government agencies."

"The activity targets restoring essential infrastructure and services, addressing urgent reconstruction needs, creating employment, and building Iraq's capacity for large-scale reconstruction. Key outcomes include rehabilitating water and sanitation systems in Baghdad and other urban areas, initiating master plans for water, sanitation, and urban development, and addressing urgent social and economic infrastructure needs. Capacity building and technical assistance for Iraqi civil servants are also targeted. Overall success hinges on overcoming substantial risks (security, political, capacity, financial, and procedural unfamiliarity) while ensuring sustained Iraqi ownership and institutional capacity."

"The primary targeted outcome of this one-year initiation plan is to establish minimal preparatory conditions for the harmonious deployment of the entire portfolio over the subsequent four years, with a specific focus on technological innovation, improving the business climate, and promoting the private sector for growth and development. Key expected outcomes include improved strategic planning with a defined development vision for 2050, enhanced institutional and sectoral coordination, a better business climate capable of creating jobs and contributing to national development, and a state budget structured around sectoral strategies to improve living conditions. Specific outputs targeted include the production of 2 reports on exploration and experimentation in Labs, 1 EDIC on trade, 1 mining map, 1 diagnostic impact study on the mining cadastre, and 1 project document for improving the business climate, alongside ensuring project management. To be considered an overall success, this initiation activity must receive a satisfactory evaluation of its one-year experimental phase. This evaluation will specifically assess the lessons learned and challenges identified concerning the implementation of the Strategic Planning Support and Development Policies Portfolio (APSPD). Success also entails the effective establishment of minimal preparatory conditions, which will then facilitate the harmonious and full deployment of the portfolio over the next four years. The successful elaboration of the full portfolio document, informed by this initiation phase's insights, is a critical indicator. The activity's success is further predicated on the concrete achievement of its specified outputs, including the production of all targeted reports, EDICs, mining maps, diagnostic impact studies, and project documents for business climate improvement, as outlined in the work plan."

"The targeted outcomes of the Muara Laboh Geothermal Power Project include expanding geothermal power generation in West Sumatra by 80 MW, demonstrating commercial viability, displacing fossil-fuel-generated power, creating local employment, and reducing carbon dioxide emissions by an estimated 471,240 tons per year. The project also aims to assist government efforts for sustainable growth via private investment and support climate commitments. Success would be achieved by the construction and commissioning of the 80 MW geothermal power unit, its commercial operation effectively displacing fossil fuels, generating local employment, and achieving the targeted CO2 emissions reduction, thereby contributing to Indonesia's renewable energy and climate targets."

"The activity aims to enhance Bolivia's road sector management and improve its primary paved road network. Outcomes include rehabilitating ~567 km of roads and strengthening ABC's capacity in financial management, procurement, socio-environmental practices, rehabilitation planning, contract management, road safety, and asset management. Success entails meeting KM rehabilitation targets, implementing an integrated financial management system, approving manuals, building capacity, improving access, and reducing transport costs."

"Targeted outcomes: By 2017, reduce households below poverty line by >=15%. By 2014, 0.6M poor in 600 villages get improved rural infrastructure/services (50% satisfied, half women). 0.5M urban dwellers in 34 cities get improved sanitation (20% increase), 50% urban residents increase hygiene awareness. Functioning CIOs (>=40% women) and participatory plans established. Infrastructure grants result in high quality works with O&M and employment. Success needs: Continued gov support for community-driven development, adequate/timely national/district funds, sustained urban pollution reduction priority, and community resources/commitment for works/maintenance."

"The project aims to contribute to inclusive growth and environmentally sustainable development in Ji'an by improving urban transport efficiency and sustainability. The impact is an efficient, inclusive, and sustainable urban transport system, with the outcome of efficient multimodal access to major activity centers. This includes linking the new high-speed railway station and development area to the existing city and improving the public transport network."

"The activity targets reversing watershed degradation, increasing forest cover by at least 10% by 2025, raising rural household incomes by 30% (P60,000-P90,000 per annum) by 2020, and reducing poverty incidence from 47% to 25%. It also aims to establish river basin/watershed management plans (1.13M hectares by 2015), rehabilitate 94,010 hectares of natural forestland (by 2018), establish 24,450 hectares of agroforestry, and implement 560 km rural roads, irrigation for 3,040 hectares, and 275 potable water units. Overall success requires achieving these outcomes, along with demonstrating economic viability (18-43% EIRR)."

"The targeted outcomes include a reduction in greenhouse gas emissions in Viet Nam by avoiding 12,000 tCO2e by 2025 and 820,000 tCO2e by 2037, and Selex mobilizing at least $5 million in additional equity/debt by 2026 and $50 million by 2037. Success also requires Selex to produce 17,000 electric two-wheelers by 2025 and 570,000 by 2037, and to significantly enhance gender equality: 90% employee awareness on gender sensitization, 60% female employees receiving clean tech training, two campaigns promoting women in technical fields, and one partnership with a women-in-tech organization."

"The targeted outcomes of the \"Togo First Fiscal Management and Energy Reform DPF\" are structured around three pillars: (i) increasing tax revenue mobilization and improving public investment and debt management; (ii) strengthening energy sector financial viability; and (iii) enhancing health sector financial viability and governance. Specifically, expected results include strengthened revenue mobilization, improved efficiency and transparency of public investment procedures, and strengthened financial viability and governance in the energy and health sectors. This means an anticipated increase in VAT revenues, property tax revenues, and the share of taxes from MSMEs, alongside improved public investment and debt management, lower energy provision costs, and increased financial sustainability in the energy sector, as well as strengthened financial viability and performance of the health sector. To be considered an overall success, the activity would need to achieve these objectives and expected results, demonstrating improved tax revenue mobilization, efficient public investment and debt management, and enhanced financial viability and governance within the energy and health sectors."

"Targeted outcomes include strengthening biodiversity conservation and protected area management, transitioning from donor financing to sustainable sources, and piloting a sustainable financing mechanism. Specific objectives are to continue managing protected areas, operationalize the Foundation for Biodiversity (FBG), raise US$3-5 million in initial endowment capital, and finance IBAP's core functions for two years while implementing management plans for at least four parks. Success requires the FBG achieving its full capitalization target (ultimately US$15 million) and generating stable financing from investment returns and other recurrent revenue streams."

"The targeted outcomes of the Shanghai Urban Environment (APL Phase III) activity are to improve Shanghai's resource and environmental sustainability, increase secure provision of good quality water, reduce untreated pollution load, and facilitate sustainable environmental investments in suburban areas. Overall success would be achieved by: an increase in quality water supplied by the Qing Cao Sha raw water system; improved water quality via reduced untreated pollution; Chengtou General's adoption of management decisions for DFV's strategic evolution; better water sector management from IST components; reaching 85% wastewater collection/treatment by 2010; adopting conjunctive use of raw water sources; and ensuring efficient/financially sustainable management of the water sector and DFV's sustainability through institutional reforms."

Targeted outcomes: Inform African research agenda and development decisions with climate data, improve climate model understanding, strengthen DFID climate mainstreaming/ICF integration, and advance African climate research/data uptake. Success requires: policies/investments using climate science for resilience, decision-makers using climate info, institutional processes incorporating climate data, achievement of specific output indicators (new research, collaborations, tools, ICF integration, knowledge exchange, outreach), and stakeholder willingness to use climate products.

The targeted outcomes of the National Urban Water Sector Reform Project 1 are to improve the reliability and financial viability of selected urban water utilities and increase access to piped water networks in specific urban areas. Key specific outcomes include rehabilitating eight existing water supply networks to installed capacity, adding 50,000 new household water connections, initiating two Public-Private Partnership (PPP) contracts and preparing two additional states for PPPs by project closing. Further outcomes involve achieving at least a 20% increase in billing and collection efficiency, at least a 20% increase in water availability to end-users, and 100% Operations and Maintenance (O&M) coverage from revenues in all three State Water Authorities (SWAs). Policy reforms aim for the establishment of three State Water Regulatory Agencies, completion and implementation of a National Water Policy, and establishment of a national strategy to increase low-income household connections. The activity would be considered an overall success if there are demonstrated increases in water delivered through existing and extended networks, improvements in cost recovery, and a substantial increase in the number of household connections to piped water networks, in line with all component-specific targets by the project closing date.

The activity targets improving sanitary conditions in the Panama City metropolitan area and Panama Bay. Key outcomes include: increasing treated wastewater flow from 1.9 m³/sec to 5.1 m³/sec; increasing households served by Juan Díaz WWTP from 104,878 to 235,018 (25.7% to 57.7% of metropolitan households); reducing Panama Bay fecal coliforms from 6,200 CFU/100 ml to 200 CFU/100 ml; and improving Matasnillo and Curundú Rivers' Water Quality Index from <15 and <25, respectively, to >70. Success requires achieving these targets, building MINSA PCU capacity for system sustainability, ensuring operational readiness of the second treatment module, certifying management processes to international standards, establishing a water quality database, and approving a Ramsar site management strategy.

The Post-Chernobyl Recovery Project targets several key outcomes. The primary development objective is to provide the population residing in the Chernobyl-affected area with energy-efficient and reliable heat and hot water services, thereby improving their living environment. Specifically, the project aims to upgrade or replace energy infrastructure to enhance efficiencies, deliver improved services (including comfort and access to better fuel), generate savings on fuel and operational costs, and reduce emissions. This includes ensuring social public institutions in education, health, and social protection receive adequate heat and hot water. It also seeks to connect individual houses currently using wood stoves to gas pipelines for better heating services, which will also reduce the production of potentially contaminated ashes and provide cleaner indoor air. Furthermore, the project intends to improve the population's understanding of the government's energy efficiency and residential gas connection programs, enhance information content and dissemination on radiation safety and mitigation, and contribute to healthier living conditions for households.Overall success of the activity would be measured by: the estimated number of beneficiaries reached with improved energy-efficient services; the estimated volume and value of fuel saved; the number of residential gas connections established; the number of households using gas for heat and hot water services; and improved information content and dissemination. For the Energy Efficiency Component, success hinges on the number of boilers, substations, and kilometers of pipes renovated/replaced, the number of institutions and beneficiaries reached, and estimated reductions in pollutants. For the Residential Gas Connections, success is measured by the number of towns reached and households connected. Additionally, an increase in the number of homes connecting to full gas supply services and enhanced population awareness of radiation impact, coupled with the adoption of mitigation measures, would signify success.

Targeted outcomes include: improved collaborative management of transboundary LVB natural resources; reduced environmental stress in pollution hotspots and degraded sub-catchments; improved livelihoods for dependent communities; increased adoption of cleaner production technologies (at least 30%); and improved sanitation for school-aged children. Success requires: adoption of harmonized policies/legislation and basin-wide management frameworks; percentage increase in land productivity for participating households; and percentage reduction in water hyacinth infestation in the Kagera River.

Targeted outcomes: Commercialize and scale Egypt's wind energy, adding ~2,500 MW via competitive bidding and 400-600 MW annually publicly. Develop infrastructure to evacuate 3,000 MW wind power, ensuring secure grid integration. Reduce GHG by 7.0 MT annually from 2,500 MW new capacity. Create 15,200 jobs, develop 198 MUSD supply chain, benefit 700,000 households. Overall success requires achieving these capacity goals, full infrastructure, secure grid integration, projected GHG reductions, and economic viability with leveraged investments.

The overall targeted outcome is to control desertification and degradation and protect key farmland and infrastructure in strategically selected locations within the Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region. Specific outcomes include stabilizing sand dunes and establishing vegetation cover on approximately 45,000 hectares, protecting and restoring about 25,000 hectares of degraded natural shrub- and grassland, protecting Yellow River banks, and establishing ecological-economic forests to increase local incomes. The project also aims to improve the general ecological environment, provide long-term benefits, safeguard socio-economic development, and involve 300 households in management arrangements.

The Regional Infrastructure Programme for Africa (RIPA) targets speeding up investment in priority regional infrastructure projects, contributing to improved infrastructure to facilitate trade, and strengthening preparation and mobilizing financing for 8 regional infrastructure projects within the Africa Regional Operational Plan framework. It aims to increase the supply of regional project preparation studies, with over 100 new studies commissioned by IPPF and ITF by 2015. The £15 million IPPF funding is expected to lead to an extra £300 million in regional infrastructure investment by 2018, and the ITF investment is projected to leverage at least £180 million of loan financing by 2014. RIPA also aims to improve cross-border and cross-financier coordination to increase the availability of public and private financing for regional infrastructure, and to leverage multilateral financing to reduce trade costs.

The activity aims to provide WASH services to up to 9.8 million people, with an additional 2.5 million people gaining access to sanitation, water, and improved hygiene through the cost extension, contributing to the UK Government's goal of helping 60 million people. Overall, the program targets nearly 7.5 million beneficiaries (Oxfam: 1.68M, Plan: 2.28M, SNV: 3.47M), primarily for improved sanitation, with water supply and hygiene education. Expected impacts include reduced child deaths from diarrhoea, improved health/productivity, enhanced community WASH, reduced anti-microbial resistance, and improved girls' school attendance.

The activity aims to preserve ecosystem services, sustain livelihoods, and improve resilience in Tuvalu via a 'ridge-to-reef' approach. Specific outcomes include increasing LMMAs/MPAs by 15% (approx. 1200 ha/12 km²) across all nine islands, formalizing 9 community management systems with functional plans, establishing a centralized GIS database for biodiversity/natural resources, implementing sustainable land management/agroforestry, remedying Funafuti Lagoon algal blooms, mainstreaming R2R into national policies/budgets, developing a knowledge management SOP, and enhancing awareness/capacities. Success requires achieving these measurable deliverables, ensuring sustained impacts, local ownership, and evidence-based planning.

Targeted outcomes include strengthening SDMA/DDMA capacity in Himachal Pradesh (28 consultants, 20-30% women), 6 state governments adopting Sendai-aligned DRR/climate strategies, 10 gender-integrated DRR/climate action plans, 25 departmental DM plans in Maharashtra, 13 resilient cities in HP, enhanced resilience at 2 airports, and establishment of 1 HRVA system in Pune. Overall success requires meeting these quantitative targets, fostering strong government buy-in, and securing sustained partnerships for continued disaster risk management, resilience building, and emergency planning.

Targeted outcomes include building resilience, advancing gender equality, empowering women and girls, and enabling sustainable livelihoods, economic recovery, and social inclusion in Raqqa. Specific goals involve increased access to comprehensive GBV and RH services (e.g., 35,000 services, 10,000 dignity kits), restoring livelihoods (e.g., 300 farmers with inputs, 600 FHHs with livestock, 100 families with new jobs via workshops), and improving living standards. Success requires meeting these measurable outputs and indicators, such as the number of beneficiaries reached (15,000 individuals via UNFPA, 100 families, 300 farmers, 600 FHHs, 150 youth), operationalizing two WGSS with mobile teams, and demonstrating improved household resilience and women's empowerment, all verified through a robust monitoring and reporting framework that ensures accountability and data-informed decisions.

The targeted outcomes of the activity are to promote the equitable distribution of benefits and conservation of biodiversity genetic resources by strengthening the regulatory framework for the Nagoya Protocol, completing the research phase of Thalassia testudinum for a pharmaceutical product, and initiating clinical trials for cancer treatment. Key outcomes include 631 hectares of marine area under improved management, 475 direct beneficiaries gaining co-benefits from GEF investment, and enhanced institutional and human capacities for bio-product development and non-clinical studies for a natural product from Thalassia testudinum. To be considered an overall success, the activity requires sustained political will, active involvement of national government, local communities, and the pharmaceutical industry, and availability of technical and financial resources. Timely information dissemination, capacity of institutions to implement activities effectively, and targeted beneficiaries' interest in and appropriation of sustainable use methods are crucial. Favorable climate conditions and the receding of the COVID-19 pandemic are also assumed.

The activity targets safeguarding terrestrial biodiversity and ecosystem services by strengthening national capacities, legal frameworks, and environmental law enforcement. Outcomes include enhanced PA management, sustainable charcoal production, reduced forest degradation, and integrated sustainable land management. Success requires: updated legal/institutional frameworks; active environmental funds; effective enforcement via trained guards; improved PA management effectiveness (METT scores); reduced threats to ~41,198 ha of PAs/buffer zones; ~1503 households benefiting from sustainable livelihoods; ~17,767 ha under SLM; 10 communities with strengthened development plans; and robust civil society engagement.

Targeted outcomes include: resilient recovery of 100 indigenous/rural communities in 8 states, addressing economic/productive/labor crisis due to COVID-19. Specifics: (1) food security/local production, (2) water resilience/WASH access, (3) economic reactivation/market access, (4) enhanced coordination. Additionally, 500 families trained in sustainable slope management, 300 hectares reforested (5,000 plants), community brigades trained in pest/fire combat. Success: clear objective, multi-stakeholder collaboration, measurable goals (e.g., 2,500 people trained in sustainable production, 3,000 families reactivate food production), strong financial commitment, and long-term resilience focus.

The targeted outcomes of the "PDI Sembrado Vida y J venes Construyendo Futuro" activity are comprehensive and aim at significant social and economic development in Northern Central America. For the "Sembrando Vida" project, the outcomes include improving productive practices to reduce poverty and elevate the quality of life, fostering and strengthening the rural sector, and increasing productivity in poverty-stricken and migrant rural areas within a sustainability framework. Specifically, this involves incentivizing the cultivation of Agroforestry Productive Systems (combining traditional, high-economic-value crops with the Milpa Intercalada entre  Arboles Frutales (MIAF) system, alongside vegetables, fruit trees, and timber trees) to decrease poverty in high-migration territories, create conditions to reduce irregular migration, provide incentives for family self-consumption, offer knowledge for sustainable agricultural production systems, generate employment in rural communities, and enhance food security and quality of life for people in poverty. For the "J venes Construyendo el Futuro" project, the primary outcomes are linking individuals aged 18 to 29 who are neither studying nor working with public, private, and social workplaces, where they will develop or strengthen work habits and technical skills to improve their future employability. This is supported by monthly economic assistance, medical insurance, and the issuance of a Training Certificate detailing acquired skills to facilitate their integration into productive inclusion mechanisms. Overall, within the broader Integral Development Plan (PDI), the activity aims to reduce poverty, promote social integration, and enhance the well-being of the population in El Salvador, Honduras, and Guatemala.To consider the activity an overall success, several critical elements would need to be achieved. Firstly, the specific, detailed objectives of both "Sembrando Vida" and "J venes Construyendo el Futuro" projects must be met. This means demonstrating a measurable reduction in poverty, an elevated quality of life, increased rural productivity, successful implementation of sustainable agricultural practices, reduced irregular migration, and enhanced food security in targeted communities. For youth, success would involve a high rate of successful placements in workplaces, verifiable development of work habits and technical skills, improved employability leading to subsequent integration into productive employment, and the effective utilization of Training Certificates. Secondly, operational effectiveness is crucial, requiring the successful hiring and deployment of multidisciplinary teams (administrative, agricultural technical personnel, extensionists, coordinators, and monitors) in all participating countries (Mexico, El Salvador, Honduras, and Guatemala), alongside efficient administrative coordination and human resources management. The completion of a diagnostic to profile beneficiaries in Northern Central America is also a key operational step. Thirdly, success hinges on effective coordination across multiple countries and various stakeholders, along with the crucial ability to adapt and tailor implementation strategies to the specific needs of each country and its diverse target populations, including those in poverty, migrant communities, and youth. Fourthly, the strategic use of South-South and Triangular Cooperation must effectively accelerate development and facilitate critical knowledge transfer. Lastly, the activity's objectives must be attained within the extended project duration, efficiently utilizing the substantial budget allocation to achieve these outcomes.

The Chongqing Urban-Rural Integration Project targets reducing inequality in access to basic social services (transport, water, sanitation, health, education) between 16 districts/counties and urban areas, and improving project counties' administrative capacity. Specific outcomes include reliable potable water for over 109,000 people in 3 counties, improved transportation for about 473,000 people in 4 counties, enhanced employment skills for about 507,000 people, and better primary health care access for over 420,000 people in Yubei District. Success requires achieving these outcomes, demonstrating economic viability of subcomponents, local governments' capacity for debt/operations, compliance with evaluation criteria/safeguards, and influencing broader urban-rural integration policies without significant negative impacts.

Targeted outcomes include reducing unmet energy demand by initially 500 million kWh/year (later 1 billion kWh/year with a 335 MW combined cycle unit), saving at least US$20 million annually. It also aims to build institutional capacity in EGCB, GTCL, and PGCB for managing complex power generation technology, implementing best practices in procurement, O&M, and governance, fostering knowledge transfer. Overall success necessitates the project directly fulfilling energy demand, achieving operational efficiency via capacity building, O&M engagement, and Owner's Engineers, integrating vital transmission infrastructure, and crucially, being sustained by broader energy sector reforms and effective tariff policies.

Targeted outcomes: Enhance Pakistan's natural gas supply by reducing physical/commercial losses (UFG) in pipelines. Specifically, reduce UFG by 22.2 bcf/year from 2011's 37 bcf, aiming for 28 bcf by 2018 (9% to 5%). Increase gas for power, reduce oil import dependency, boost energy sector efficiency, and cut GHG emissions. Improve SSGC's financial stability, governance, accountability, customer service, and ensure better consumer access/lower tariffs. Success requires achieving these UFG reductions within 3-5 years, proving its ability to solve the decade-long UFG problem and satisfy consumers.

The activity targets improved irrigation water delivery and management, enhancing system performance and community-based management to build resilience against water-induced hazards. Outcomes include: 50% water users assessing WUA service as satisfactory, 90% O&M resources generated by users, increased crop yields (rice to 3.1, wheat to 1.9, maize to 1.8 Ton/ha) in 40% of command area, improved water delivery for 6,000 female and 19,000 male WUA members, and 33% female WUA executive members. Success requires meeting these targets, effective risk management, and WUA sustainability in O&M and financial responsibility.

Targeted outcomes: improved year-round transitability of San Buenaventura – Ixiamas road and enhanced safety, security, & operational reliability of Rurrenabaque Airport. Success requires completing road civil works (pavement, bridges) and airport infrastructure (taxiway, terminal, control tower, aviation equipment) to reduce flight cancellations. Crucially, NAABOL must hire dedicated project staff within 60 days and update its Operational Manual within 90 days of the second amendment, alongside strengthening ABC/NAABOL capacity via TA, training, and a Good Governance Plan. ABT's monitoring capacity and rigorous safeguard standards are also essential.

["Targeted outcomes include improving wildlife and habitat conservation effectiveness by tackling key regional threats like illegal wildlife trade and habitat fragmentation. This involves harmonized enforcement, best practice sharing, and knowledge exchange to reduce illegal trade and stabilize/increase endangered animal populations in Asia. Enhanced capacity, institutions, and incentives for regional collaboration are crucial, focusing on border areas and flagship species like tigers, snow leopards, and rhinos, while also addressing human-wildlife conflict.", "Overall success requires the development and operationalization of a regional mechanism against illegal wildlife trade and conservation threats. Key indicators include the number of agreed knowledge products shared among SAWEN countries, establishment of wildlife crime control institutions in participating countries, implementation of pilot initiatives addressing trans-boundary wildlife issues, and activities contributing to tiger conservation."]

Outcomes: 600k beneficiaries (new/improved electricity access) by 2014; EDH Cash Recovery Index 51% by 2014; rehabilitate 80k, establish 70k new electricity connections (30k metropolitan, 30k secondary urban/rural, 10k rural); 200-300k households/5-10k enterprises with efficient cooking. Strengthened energy policy/planning, sector sustainability/resilience. Success: Acceptable EDH management structure by Apr 2013 (conditional for 70% budget) and government commitment.

The targeted outcomes are: (i) to increase solar photovoltaic generated electricity supply to the NEA grid, directly supporting Nepal's vision to eliminate energy crises, ensure reliable and affordable electricity, and reduce load shedding, especially during dry seasons when hydropower generation is low. (ii) To reduce NEA's distribution losses in selected distribution centers, enhancing operational efficiency and capacity. This involves generating an additional 25 MWp and recovering 80-120 MW from loss reduction.

The targeted outcomes include reversing fiscal deterioration and protecting the most vulnerable, specifically by: (i) strengthening fiscal and financial management (enhancing economic governance, supporting fiscal consolidation, promoting transparency, reducing wage expenditure by eliminating "ghost staff"); (ii) strengthening power sector management (reducing ENEE financial losses, promoting sector sustainability, cutting electricity subsidies, improving billing/cash flow); and (iii) improving social protection targeting (ensuring resources reach the extreme poor). Overall success would require achieving these objectives, sustaining reform momentum despite macroeconomic shocks, demonstrating strong political commitment, and enhancing governmental implementation capacity through reforms and technical assistance.

The National Electrification Project targets increasing electricity access in Myanmar and providing immediate emergency response. Key outcomes include extending the grid to connect 750,000 households, 11,600 community buildings, and 132,000 street lights via 12,900 miles of lines. Off-grid efforts aim for solar PV for 456,500 households, mini-grids for 35,500 households, and connections for 11,400 community buildings and 19,000 street lights. Technical assistance will build institutional capacity, improve policy, and develop an electrification planning framework. Success entails meeting these specific targets, strengthening the power sector's governance, and effectively responding to crises, contributing to Myanmar's goal of universal electricity access by 2030.

The targeted outcomes include improving stakeholders' capacity to develop and manage irrigation and increasing irrigated areas using a regional 'solutions' approach. This aims to enhance agricultural productivity and incomes, reduce food insecurity and poverty, create jobs, and foster transparent policy and planning. Specific targets include revitalizing/developing approximately 23,000 hectares and conducting feasibility studies for an additional 50,000 hectares, directly benefiting 80,000 households and 480,000 beneficiaries. Overall success depends on achieving these objectives through institutional strengthening, a comprehensive M&E framework, and market-oriented solutions.

["The primary targeted outcomes are to reduce flood risk for people and assets in the greater Paramaribo area, improve the operation of the Saramacca Canal System, and provide immediate and effective response to an Eligible Emergency. This also includes strengthening the operational management of the Saramacca Canal System, improving navigation, enhancing the canal's capacity to discharge water, increasing urban area drainage capacity, and boosting citizen engagement regarding project designs, environmental impacts, and mitigation measures.", "The activity would be considered an overall success when 57,000 people benefit from reduced flood risk for a 10-year return period, with 50% of these beneficiaries being female. Success also requires the introduction of a risk-based maintenance system for the Saramacca Canal System's drainage, an increased flood forecast lead time of 4 hours for the canal system, and the client's capacity to respond to an emergency strengthened within one month. Additionally, achieving 100% improved canal discharge capacity, 12 hours of improved navigation functioning, increased drainage capacity for 70 km2, and endorsement of a management platform is crucial."]

["Targeted outcomes include driving governance/market reforms for legal trade, benefiting forest-dependent people. Aims: shift global norms against illegal timber, bolster regulation/enforcement in producer countries, promote legal timber demand, and apply lessons to wider forest risks (palm oil, soya, beef). FGMC will secure/scale up reforms, extend support, and deliver results in transparency, accountability, land tenure, sustainable economic growth via legal timber, and empowering women/girls (addressing property rights/resource access). Country-specific reforms and consistent global market measures are key.", "Overall success requires new systems established for forest governance, markets, and climate to be well-established and self-sustaining. This includes comprehensive reform of timber sectors in focal countries, such as Ghana, Liberia, and the Republic of Congo following Indonesia's lead in securing licences for legally-verified timber trade. It further implies compliance with national tax, labour, social, environmental, and forest laws and regulations, and achieving far-reaching results in transparency, accountability, tenure, human rights, and economic development."]

["Reduce stunting (40% to 31%), low-birth weight (9.3% to 5%), and anaemia (29% to 19%) by 2021. Reach 1M+ people, including 376K children (<2) and 300K pregnant/lactating mothers, with nutrition interventions. Increase WHO-aligned child feeding to 65% and Zambia's nutrition budget to $17/child by 2021. Enhance children's health/nutrition, promote global prosperity, strengthen crisis resilience, tackle extreme poverty. Increase food security for vulnerable households via cash transfers and treat wasting in drought-affected areas.", "Success requires achieving targeted reductions in stunting (to 31%), low-birth weight (to 5%), and anaemia (to 19%) by 2021. It depends on reaching over one million beneficiaries, successful scale-up of proven interventions across 24+ districts, enhanced Zambian government investment (to $17/child) and ownership, robust assurance systems, effective donor collaboration, and integrated gender equality measures. Timely emergency cash transfers and treatment for wasting are crucial."]

The targeted outcomes of the Fund for Impact Evaluation (i2i Umbrella Facility) are primarily to reduce poverty and increase equity by increasing the use of evidence-based interventions, especially in DFID's priority sectors with scarce evidence. This involves improving the availability and quality of impact evaluation evidence and strengthening capacity and institutions to maximize the use of findings. Outcomes include improved awareness, demand, and use of evidence in decision-making within key sectors, evidenced by policy adjustments (scaling up, scaling down, mid-course corrections), and enhanced effectiveness of development interventions by DFID and the broader development community. Success will be measured by outputs such as 125 strategic impact evaluations improving policy and programme design, increased capacity and knowledge for cross-sectoral/country learning (including a knowledge platform), and fostering communities of practice for stakeholders. Crucially, an independent evaluation of the i2i Umbrella Facility's performance during its third year will inform decisions on potential scaling up and further investment, serving as a key indicator for overall success.

The targeted outcomes of the Private Enterprise Programme Zambia Phase II (PEPZ 2) are to systematically transform the finance and investment environment for SMEs in Zambia, helping them scale business ideas and drive job creation. Specific outcomes include addressing barriers to access sustainable power and international trade. For TradeMark Africa (TMA), targets are over 100 cargo scans per hour and reduced border time from 32 to under 10 hours, enhancing regional integration, investment, trade, and jobs. For Cities and Infrastructure for Growth (CIG), support aims to assist the Ministry of Energy in Integrated Resource Plan (IRP) implementation, mobilizing $11.6 billion in energy investments by 2030, and improving reliable power access. The programme also aims to meet FCDO Zambia targets on capital and climate spend. For the activity to be considered an overall success, it must achieve its core purpose of transforming the SME finance and investment environment, leading to significant job creation and investment mobilization. This includes TMA reaching its border efficiency targets, CIG's support resulting in the targeted energy sector investments by 2030, and both new partners contributing to unified outcome indicators centered on investment mobilized and job creation from April 2025. Additionally, gender equity statements must be coherent within the programme, and FCDO Zambia's capital and climate spend targets met.

["The activity targets include delivering life-saving support to a minimum of 700,000 internally displaced people, refugees, and disaster-affected communities, and building the resilience of 38,000 vulnerable households in conflict-affected areas in Darfur during FY 21/22. Outcomes span three pillars: 1. System strengthening and reform: enable effective humanitarian responses and resilience planning by GoS/UN via TA, supporting emergency capability, conflict sensitivity, and civilian protection. 2. Response to humanitarian needs: provide direct life-saving support (food, healthcare, water/sanitation, education). 3. Building resilience: foster economic recovery, strengthen household resilience to shocks, improve access to essential services, and create livelihood opportunities.", "Overall success is defined by delivering urgently needed, life-saving support to a minimum of 700,000 people and building the resilience of 38,000 vulnerable households in conflict-affected areas in Darfur during FY 21/22. Further success indicators include strengthening systems for earlier/more effective assistance, recovery, and resilience; achieving economic recovery and improved access to essential services for communities; ensuring improved humanitarian preparedness, response, and recovery that reduces the increase in humanitarian need; and implementing partners meeting or exceeding beneficiary targets while demonstrating strong Value for Money through efficient practices."]

Targeted outcomes include delivering demand-led support for governance reforms fostering open societies/economies, improving democratic/economic governance, and helping countries become resilient through inclusive growth. For Azerbaijan, it specifically targets inclusive economic growth, diversification, resilience against shocks, poverty reduction, and economic empowerment of women/excluded groups, while strengthening UK partnerships. Success requires demonstrating sustainable reform appetite, leveraging UK influence, achieving tangible economic diversification and inclusivity, empowering economic actors to demand reforms, and rigorous M&E.

The targeted outcomes of the activity are: 1) Securing livelihoods and strengthening inclusive market systems that support Sustainable Natural Resources Management; 2) Achieving sustainable and restored social-ecological landscapes for enhanced biodiversity and ecosystem services; 3) Strengthening governance systems and structures for conflict-sensitive and sustainable Natural Resource Management and climate change adaptation at community, county, and national levels; and 4) Ensuring effective coordination of Program Monitoring, Evaluation, Accountability, Research, and Learning.

The Integrated Urban Solid Waste Management Program II targets: 1) Proper disposal for 227,500 households, with 100% waste in sanitary landfills by EOP 2028. 2) 25% material recovery, 361 formal jobs (127 women), 100% formal employment for recyclers (including women), and 50% increase in recovered material by EOP 2028. 3) 100% closure of open-air dumps, 60% of centers achieving 25% avoided GHG emissions, and 25% annual micro-dump remediation by EOP 2028. Success requires meeting all these targets, ensuring operational/financial sustainability, socioeconomic viability (EIRR > 12%), and proper infrastructure operation/maintenance.

The activity targets expanding residential sewerage service, reducing nonrevenue water, lessening wastewater discharge impact, and boosting service efficiency. Key outcomes include connecting 3,800 households in La Matanza to sewers by 2027, providing adequate wastewater disposal for 1,333,333 households via the Berazategui system by 2027, and improving wastewater dilution to 1/20 from 1/3.
