The activity targets decarbonization of the electrical sector by reducing CO2 emissions factor to 0.43 tCO2/MWh by EOP 2028 (from 0.46 baseline) and fostering human development via transmission system expansion. Specific outcomes by EOP 2028 include: eliminating 81 MW of forced fossil fuel generation; adding 302 MW transmission capacity for demand; adding 148 MW for renewable energy; reducing energy not supplied by 100,000 MWh/year; avoiding 20% technical electricity losses; and increasing women employed to 15%.

["The activity aims to improve water resources management and ensure sustainable water and wastewater utility services provision by the Barbados Water Authority (BWA), specifically by modernizing the institutional setting and enhancing BWA's operational efficiency. Targeted outcomes by Year 5 include: reducing employees per 1000 connections from 7.8 to 7.2; increasing customer satisfaction to over 75%; improving BWA's financial viability (EBITDA/Total Operating revenues) from -17% to 10%; reducing non-revenue water from 54% to 47%; increasing customers with validated meters from 10% to 70%; reducing non-compliant water turbidity/disinfection samples to 10%/20% respectively; reducing service continuity issues and low pressure instances to 10%/20% respectively; replacing 50,000-52,000 meters and installing district meters covering 90% of the island; upgrading water supply to 20,000 households; and implementing a fully financed wastewater treatment action plan, along with IT system integration and a BWA corporate business plan.", "Overall success requires meeting all ambitious targets, particularly the BWA's financial turnaround (achieving 10% EBITDA/Total Operating revenues from -17%) and the successful implementation of necessary institutional and infrastructure upgrades. This includes reducing non-revenue water to 47%, increasing metered customers to 70%, improving water quality (turbidity 10%, disinfection 20%), service continuity (10% issues), and pressure (20% below standards). A fully financed wastewater treatment plan must be implemented, and a strategic plan for water resource management approved by all stakeholders. The Government's commitment to implementing necessary changes and the support of BWA staff and workers are critical conditions for achieving these outcomes and ensuring sustainable water/wastewater utility services."]

The activity targets include reducing GHG emissions from 25.05 to 67.9 thousand tCO2e by 2020, increasing renewable energy generation from 9.4 GWh to 47.6 GWh by 2020, and leveraging US$8.7 million in private investment by 2020/2021. It also aims to increase average electricity provision to 15.2 hours by 2020 and finance approximately 12 RE projects (8.79 MW). Success hinges on meeting these targets, stimulating private RE investments, enhancing energy services, and demonstrating replicability and sustainability in ZNIs.

The Universal Sanitation Program in Coastal and Tourist Cities aims to increase access and quality of sanitation services, and improve environmental and health conditions in coastal and tourist areas of the Dominican Republic. Specific outcomes include narrowing sanitation infrastructure gaps (wastewater collection and treatment), strengthening institutional frameworks for drinking water management, expanding sanitation coverage, limiting negative environmental and social externalities by eliminating wastewater discharges, fostering tourism, and reducing inequalities.

The activity targets expanding water and sanitation services to communities up to 10,000 inhabitants, ensuring operational and financial sustainability. Key outcomes include: access to piped water for 630,000 beneficiaries (108,000 rural, 18,000 urban households) and sanitation for 450,000 beneficiaries (48,000 rural, 12,000 urban sewerage; 30,000 rural basic sanitation households). It aims to strengthen 25 sub-executors/operating agencies, enable 75 hectares for agricultural reuse of treated wastewater, achieve financial self-sufficiency for 750 rural committees, and execute institutional improvement plans in 15 municipalities. The program focuses on strengthening community management and developing sustainable institutional arrangements.

Targeted outcomes include increasing RMPP potable water coverage to 74.00% by End of Project (EOP). Other outcomes focus on improving the Technical Exploitation Center's (CTE) overall efficiency and achieving financial equilibrium (EOP Operational Ratio: 1.05; water sales revenue: 550,000.00 kHTG/year). The project also aims to enhance RMPP household water supply and service continuity, reducing poor service and increasing consistent access. Overall success requires meeting or exceeding all established EOP targets.

["Targeted outcomes are: 1) Explore 40.00 MW of geothermal potential at feasibility level in the Cosigüina Field and grant one exploitation concession by EOP 2021 (from 0.0 MW/units baseline). 2) Reduce energy not served in program influence zones from 1.18 GWh (2016) to 0.08 GWh by EOP 2021. 3) Increase Nicaragua-Honduras N-S regional transfer capacity from 120.0 MW (2016) to 300.0 MW by EOP 2021.", "Overall success requires achieving all EOP 2021 targets, reversing the negative trend of increased energy not served (2.59 GWh in 2017 vs 1.18 GWh baseline), resolving substantial implementation delays, completing all pending major acquisitions (e.g., drilling commercial wells, feasibility studies), and successfully implementing external reinforcements necessary for SIEPAC capacity optimization."]

The activity targets 17,168 households with piped potable water and sewer connections, 78,000 with wastewater treatment access, and 100% water service continuity. It aims to reduce typical household water costs from 46.30 to 33.00 Soles/month and increase consumption from 3.60 to 19.00 m³/month. An increase of 0.50 m³/second in treated wastewater flow will free up water for the La Atarjea drinking plant, contributing to 20.50 m³/second intake flow. Success requires households to connect to services and be billed, construction of collectors, impulsion lines, networks, the La Atarjea wastewater treatment plant, its Surco Canal interconnection, guaranteed Huachipa supply, replacement of tanker supply, and public health education.

The activity targets include: increasing sanitary sewerage coverage and wastewater treatment in urban/periurban intermediate municipalities, improving potable water service performance in Managua/intermediate municipalities, and strengthening ENACAL's management. Specifically, it aims for 12,090 households with new/improved sanitation access, 7,859 households with treated wastewater, and 65% sewerage system connectivity by EOP 2023. It also targets 95% monitored water production, reduced Non-Revenue Water, improved collection efficiency, fewer network breaks, effective micrometring, and 24/7 water service in Altamira. Success requires achieving these quantitative targets and strengthening ENACAL's operational, technical, and commercial management per contractual obligations.

Targeted outcomes include: increasing effective treated wastewater flow to 1.27 m³/sec; increasing organic matter removed from Uruguay River discharges to 8,010 tons/year; expanding improved sewer service to 97,725 households (97.5% coverage); ensuring sewage effluents are treated for 97,725 households; and achieving 100% financial self-sufficiency for service operators, with two operators incorporating a gender equity approach. Overall success requires achieving these targets, improving service management, reducing greenhouse gas emissions by 4,582 MT CO2e/year, incorporating climate change adaptation, establishing the PCU and Operating Regulations, ensuring MPIyS agreements with municipios, and commencing works within three years, all while maintaining stakeholder coordination and operator MRPs.

["The IDAAN Water and Sanitation Investment Program- Phase I targets include: 1. Improving overall water/sanitation services: enhance collection efficiency from 79.00% to 91.20%, improve labor productivity from 5.40 to 4.50 employees/1000 connections, increase potable water quality control from 56.00% to 90.00%, and raise collections vs. disbursable costs plus debt service ratio from 0.80 to 1.00. 2. Expanding/improving services in Interior provinces: benefit 50,000 households with improved potable water, incorporate 1,250 new households into potable water, 5,250 households into sanitary sewer, and 7,750 new households into sanitary sewer. 3. In La Chorrera/Arraijan: benefit 14,500 households with improved potable water, incorporate 12,500 new households into potable water, and reduce unaccounted-for water from 42.00% to 37.00%. 4. Improving energy efficiency: achieve a total reduction of 800,000.00 kWh/year in energy consumption.", "For the activity to be considered an overall success, the Government of Panama (GOB) must implement sector and IDAAN reforms, public support for IDAAN's transformation must be secured, and complementary investments from the World Bank (BM) and CAF for IDAAN's modernization must be executed. Further success is predicated on critical assumptions that energy prices will remain constant, electromechanical equipment operation and maintenance will improve, trained technicians will be integrated, IDAAN will improve its administrative, technical, and commercial management, and the population will adopt more rational resource use practices."]

The Program to Recover Locations Degraded by Solid Waste in Priority Areas targets the following outcomes by EOP 2023: elimination of degraded areas due to inadequate solid waste disposal, aiming for 0.00 hectares (baseline 178.43 hectares); elimination of solid waste disposed of in dumps, targeting 0.00 tonnes/year (baseline 263,289.00 tonnes/year); and 100% compliance with municipal environmental monitoring for dumps closed by the program (baseline 0.00%). Additionally, the program aims for 70% of recyclers participating in formalization workshops to be added to the MSW management workforce and 50% of recyclers in job retraining workshops to work in activities they were trained for. These outcomes address the recovery of degraded areas, strengthening municipal solid waste management, and improving conditions for informal recyclers. Targeted outcomes also include the completion of engineering studies for dumpsite closure (12 studies) and the actual closure of 12 dumpsites, along with implementing 12 municipal management strategies for maintenance and surveillance of closed dumps, 7 social inclusion plans for recyclers, and 1 social communication plan for environmental best practices. The total program cost is US$35.30 million, with US$30 million from a loan and US$5.30 million as a local contribution.

The WASA Modernization and Wastewater Infrastructure Rehabilitation Program aims to enhance environmental conditions and improve WASA's efficiency. Targeted outcomes include increasing treated wastewater flow to 1.65 m3/sec (from 1.41), ensuring 9,100 households receive treated wastewater, and reducing Faecal Coliforms from 23,000 to 400 cfu/100ml. WASA's operational efficiency targets are decreasing employees per 1,000 connections from 12.5 to 10.8, cutting personnel costs from 50% to 38.2% of operating expenses, and increasing physical assets in good condition from 5% to 90%. Additionally, 90% of wastewater quality samples should comply with effluent standards.

Targeted outcomes: Satisfy electricity demand, sustainably diversify energy matrix, and reduce systemic vulnerability. Specific outcomes and success criteria: GWh/year generated in Uruguay increases to 13,392.00 (from 9,524.00 baseline); non-hydroelectric generation increases to 52.00% (from 32.00%); firm thermal installed capacity reaches 80.00% (from 64.00%); and thermal park's emission factor reduces to 0.51 tCO2/MWh (from 0.87). Overall success requires final acceptance of the 530 MW combined-cycle plant, full implementation of UTE's modernized environmental management program, and achieving all specified quantitative targets.

The activity targets include: connecting 3,080 households to sanitation with treated wastewater by 2028; reducing collector sewer failures from 54 to 41 by 2028; connecting 4,400 households to storm drainage by 2027-2028; protecting 298 households from flood risk by 2028; creating 350 new jobs for formalized sorters (105 for women) by 2027; and implementing two gender plan actions by 2027. Overall success requires achieving all these quantified targets and strengthening the IdM's institutional capacity for integrated sanitation management, verified via semiannual reports and complaint system data. Solid waste management targets are no longer part of this reformulated program.

Targeted outcomes for the activity include: strengthening community organizations for water/sanitation services, ensuring 68 water supply systems function as designed and are financially sustainable, and providing potable water access to 10,880 households and new individual sanitation solutions for 10,880 households in rural communities under 5,000 inhabitants. Overall success requires reaching 81.2% drinking water service coverage and 73.2% sanitation coverage in these communities, along with delivering 68 community-managed, functional, and sustainable water systems, and 10,850 individual sanitation solutions.

["Outcomes target public institutions effectively implementing policies/programs supporting sectors for job/income acceleration. Public policies will be better oriented at central, provincial, and local levels to develop job-creating value chains. Value chains and CSR initiatives will be identified, analyzed, and developed for increased community revenues and jobs. Improved natural resource management and green economy engagement by the Congolese state are also key.", "Success hinges on 7,500 people gaining employment/self-employment, and 35,000 jobs created via developed value chains. Public policies must be better oriented for job-creating value chains at all levels. Value chains and CSR initiatives need successful identification, analysis, and development. Rehabilitation of socio-economic infrastructures with 94,500 modern stalls occupied by vendors. Improved natural resource management and green economy engagement by the Congolese state are vital, along with strengthened SME capacities and successful '3 x 6 plus' approach."]

The activity targets include: 1. Strengthening national/provincial New Deal participatory planning capacities, evidenced by 3 PSGs-based sectoral plans and 8 provincial development plans elaborated/validated. 2. Implementing the 2019-updated COMPACT roadmap for development coordination, by designing 1 effective dialogue and 1 effective financing modality for national/provincial plans. 3. Institutionalizing humanitarian aid to development transition via Nexus operationalization, through 3 provincial task forces, 3 joint analyses, and 3 collective outcomes elaborated/validated.

The activity targets include: operationalizing 87,323 ha of new PAs and strengthening management in 198,776 ha of existing PAs, increasing management effectiveness by an average of 45%. It aims to formally recognize and conserve 40,839 ha of HCVFs, integrate biodiversity principles into 1,218,175 ha of territorial plans, and establish 50,000 ha of buffer zones. SLM in 147,268 ha of pasturelands will restore 65,361 ha of degraded pastures and 4,886 ha of degraded forests. Key outcomes are a 15% reduction in illegal wood cutting and 100% reduction in poaching of globally threatened species like the snow leopard, alongside capacity building for its conservation and wildlife trafficking enforcement. Overall success hinges on achieving these quantitative targets, including PA coverage, restoration, threat reduction, and contributing to global biodiversity goals.

The activity targets gender equality in natural resource management through women's and girls' leadership, reducing climate-induced conflicts, and supporting social cohesion/local governance. Key outcomes include improved women's/girls' access to decision-making in natural resource management (NRM) and conflict resolution, empowerment of women's groups in NRM, generation of capital (Peace-RECs) for economic decisions and NRM governance, strengthened female environmental leadership, and inclusive community NRM mechanisms. Groups will implement environmental protection and tension reduction initiatives, becoming key recovery actors via renewable energy profitability and peace mobilization.

Targeted outcomes include mainstreaming climate change into planning/budgeting via a climate budget tagging system; supporting NDC targets (50% emissions reduction by 2030, 60% renewable energy, 30% EVs); mobilizing resources; developing climate policies for Balochistan, environment policy for Gilgit Baltistan, EV regulatory frameworks, NDC investment plans, MRV guidelines, and emission reduction strategies. It also aims to provide green skill development for 3000 youth and 1000 young women, establish green business centers/career launchpads, improve waste management, and disseminate knowledge. Success requires all specified policies/guidelines (target 9) developed and implemented, 4000 people trained in green jobs, green business centers/launchpads established, and NDC targets on track.

["The targeted outcomes of the URU19002 activity are to enhance Uruguay's environmental quality and sustainability. This involves: 1) Strengthening MVOTMA's strategic planning, evaluation, control, and environmental monitoring functions. Outcomes include improved environmental information for decision-making, enhanced sustainability of environmental management services, and increased control. Specific targets: five territorial ordering programs by 2023, a validated MVOTMA environmental strategic plan by 2021, one redesigned water quality evaluation program by 2021, environmental flows in two priority basins by 2023, and improved DINAMA analytical capacities to monitor 89 pesticides (up from 39), 2 toxins, and 50 new substances. 2) Enhancing integrated management of priority basins to reduce pollutant loads from agricultural activities. Outcomes include reduced pollutant loads in three priority basins and tools for sustainable agricultural practices.", "Overall success would be achieved when: a measurable increase in environmental variables and their frequency; approval and implementation of an executive decree for environmental authorization/permit fees; increased fixed pollution sources under effective control; successful establishment of five territorial ordering programs and validation of MVOTMA's strategic development plan; redesigned water quality evaluation program and environmental flow establishment in two basins; significantly enhanced DINAMA analytical capabilities for 89 pesticides, 2 toxins, and 50 new substances. Additionally, demonstrably reduced pollutant loads in three priority basins; established buffer zones and increased areas under integral control of diffuse sources; successful development and adoption of sustainable agricultural practice tools. Success also relies on effective financial backing, strong local ownership, integration of lessons learned, prioritization of vulnerable populations, gender analysis, and robust monitoring against specific targets."]

The Moldova Economic Governance DPO1 aims to support the Government of Moldova in reducing fiscal risks and leveling the playing field for private sector development.Targeted outcomes include:increasing the average old age pensions replacement rate to 26 (from 24) by 2018;raising excise tax collections from tobacco by 12 percent in real terms by 2018 (from MDL 1.7 billion);achieving 50% of audited financial statements for medium and large MEs and SOEs by June 2019 (from 38%);60,000 asset and interest declarations filed electronically by 2018 (from zero);reducing the number of authorizations (permits and licenses) to 153 by 2018 (from 306);increasing imported plant varieties, types of fertilizers, and pesticides by 10 percent by June 2019 over 2016 baselines;ensuring 5 banks materially comply with governance provisions by June 2019 (from zero);and reaching 50 percent of electricity purchased under published Guidelines by 2018 (from zero).Overall success would entail meeting or exceeding these specific targets, thereby demonstrating reduced fiscal risks, strengthened economic governance, and a more level playing field for private sector development through streamlined bureaucratic processes and improved transparency in key sectors like banking and energy.

Targeted outcomes include improved RMNCH in DRC, increased basic RMNCH service coverage for at least 3.5M people in 30+ health zones, reducing under-5 mortality by 50%, 355K CYP by program end, 75K births attended by skilled personnel annually, 100% facilities offering EmONC, and water/sanitation for 600K. Success requires achieving these sustainably, strengthening government and community empowerment/accountability, and ensuring health access gains are maintained.

The targeted outcomes are to improve Georgetown's sewerage system performance, enhance GWI's operational and financial capacity, improve GWI's energy efficiency, and control/eliminate water-related Neglected Tropical Diseases in Region 4. Success requires: 52,000 people served by an improved sewerage system; GWI staff-hours on preventive maintenance reaching 12,193 annually; energy consumption in 12 pilot facilities reduced to 2,600 MWh/year; 3 STH/LF prevalence assessments completed; LF infection prevalence <=1.00%; STH infection prevalence <=10.00%; 80.00% of surveyed individuals aware of diseases/MDA; reconstruction of critical sewerage components; and improved GWI asset management.

The targeted outcomes are to improve collaborative management of the transboundary natural resources of the Lake Victoria basin for the shared benefits of the EAC Partner States, and to reduce environmental degradation to improve the livelihoods of communities. Success requires: adoption of harmonized policies, legislations, and basin-wide frameworks for water and fisheries management; a percentage reduction in water hyacinth infestation; a percentage increase in fish stock and land productivity for participating households; and a percentage reduction in untreated effluent disposed by targeted municipals and industries into Lake Victoria. Reduced harvesting pressure on Nile perch is also key.

Targeted outcomes include reducing spatial inequalities, enhancing rural living conditions, combating poverty, and fostering inclusive economic growth and agricultural transformation. Expected outcomes are significant poverty reduction, improved access to basic socio-economic infrastructure (schools, health, roads, water, electricity), increased rural producer income, enhanced food security, better income-generating access for youth/women, improved risk management, and strengthened local governance/community resilience. Overall success requires specific achievements: 423 schools, 283 health centers, 28,330km rural roads, 172 solar micro-plants, 1,680km solar streetlights, equitable benefits for all, environmental mitigation, and an irreversible structural transformation reversing poverty and inequalities, leading to resilience, human capital accumulation, national solidarity, and social cohesion, with converged rural/urban incomes/living conditions.

Targeted outcomes: Improve target groups' access to/use of participatory local governance, basic infrastructure, social services, and economic opportunities. Specifically, enhance living conditions, fight social exclusion, and promote social/economic inclusion for poor/vulnerable, including women and youth (education, governance, economy). Success criteria: By 2015, 70% girls in Dar Taliba graduate; 77% rural population has improved water; 40% income-generating activities are viable; 22% participate in participatory diagnostic; 12% in monitoring/evaluation. Additionally, high infrastructure quality, increased women/youth in governance, and effective project implementation/audit follow-up are crucial.

The targeted outcomes of the Gambia Electricity Support Project are to increase the availability and reliability of electricity supply for existing customers. This will be achieved through: 1. Expansion of Available Generation Capacity: Improving NAWEC's generation capacity and efficiency at Kotu and Brikama through rehabilitation, equipment replacement (e.g., two 6.4 MW engines), and urgent maintenance. 2. Reduction of Technical and Commercial Losses: Enhancing the T&D network in the GBA to reduce forced outages, diminish voltage drops, improve supply continuity/quality, and increase prepayment metering coverage to reduce technical and commercial losses. 3. Institutional Strengthening and Project Implementation Support: Strengthening NAWEC's technical, financial, and managerial capacity, including implementing a new integrated IT system, effective project supervision, and providing implementation support, staff training, and strategic studies. Overall success would be considered when these objectives are met, leading to demonstrably increased electricity availability and reliability for existing customers, reduced system losses, and a stronger, more efficient NAWEC.

The targeted outcome is to improve infrastructure services and institutional capacity for increased tourism contribution to the local economy in selected regions of Armenia. Success by 2021 entails: 1.8M international arrivals (from 1.2M), US$1.5B international tourist receipts (from US$749.5M), US$1.108B international receipts (from US$923M), and 147,070 tourism jobs (from 133.5k). 5,000 people with all-season road access, 14 hours/day piped water, US$862M tourism spending, and 3 operational Destination Management Organizations are key. Positive NPV (US$14.5M), FIRR 12.06%, EIRR 23.43% are essential for success.

The targeted outcomes of the Malawi Agricultural Support and Fiscal Management DPO are to improve incentives for private sector participation in agricultural markets and to strengthen fiscal management through more effective expenditure controls and greater transparency. This broader goal aims to enhance Malawi's ability to absorb and recover from exogenous shocks. The program is structured around two pillars of policy and institutional reforms: (a) making agricultural markets work better; and (b) strengthening expenditure management and accountability in public finances. To consider the activity an overall success, the following specific targets must be met by 2017/18:

For agricultural markets:
- The private sector share of retailing under the Farm Input Subsidy Program (FISP) must increase from 0% to 70%.
- The level of public subsidy under FISP must decrease from 97% to 60%.
- The coefficient of price variation in maize markets must decrease from 0.51 to 0.38.
- 30,000 expired estate land leases must be notified.
- 10,000 expired estate land leases must be renewed or cancelled.
- A warehouse receipts financing framework must be in place.
- Commodity trade restrictions consistent with international standards must be implemented.
- The number of districts under the Social Cash Transfer Program must increase from 18 to 28.

For fiscal management and accountability:
- The number of civil servants remaining on the payroll six months after exiting employment must be reduced from 8,004 to 500.
- A 5-year backlog in the publication of public audited financial statements must be eliminated.
- Operational independence of the external audit function with timely reporting must be established.
- Compliance by Controlling Officers with monthly reporting requirements must increase from 0% to over 90%.

The targeted outcomes are to achieve sustainable and inclusive economic development by increasing productivity in selected economic sectors (agriculture, fisheries, energy) and improving transparency and accountability in government decision-making processes (education, procurement, anti-corruption). Specific expected outcomes include increased food production, reduced inflation and food imports, fiscal savings, and an expanded tax base. For the activity to be considered an overall success, the following targets must be met by 2020: average rice yield increased to 2.0 kg/hectare (from 1.5); active industrial fishing licenses decreased to 30 (from 85); revenue recovered increased to 63% (from 49.6%); teacher absenteeism reduced to 15% (from 30%); share of e-procurement transactions increased to 20% (from 0%); and share of public officials submitting asset declarations increased to 90% (from 20%).

["Targeted outcomes aim to increase agricultural commercialization. Key targets include: 15,000 MT/year increase in producer organization sales, US$10M supplemental sales, 5 value chain products with >50% commercialization increase, 40,000 farmers reached with assets/services (incl. 16,000 female farmers), 391,000 emergency response beneficiaries, 50% female farmer satisfaction, 20 agribusiness reforms, and 12,000 smallholder households with secure land tenure. This should lead to farmers in competitive supply chains, diversified commercial agriculture, a conducive agribusiness environment, and resilient farming systems.", "Overall success requires achieving the Project Development Objective to increase commercialization of agriculture value chain products. This means meeting or exceeding all specific end targets for sales, farmer reach, satisfaction, reforms, and emergency response beneficiaries. Furthermore, success involves transforming subsistence agriculture into diversified commercial agriculture, enhancing food security, reducing poverty, and promoting shared prosperity through broad-based agricultural growth, reflecting farmers integrated into competitive supply chains with resilient systems."]

The State Consolidation Development Program targets reestablishing basic fiscal management, transparency, and economic recovery in CAR. Success is defined by achieving specific 2018 targets: domestic revenues >8.1% of GDP, 50% wage-bill reviewed by CAAT, <25% expenditures via exceptional procedures, 7 budget documents published, 2 Loi de règlement submitted, RMF Maintenance Program adopted/implemented with 50% execution, 16,000 hectares of cotton planted, 8 active forestry concessions, 65% 2G network coverage, and 45% mobile telephony penetration. These measurable outcomes are crucial for success in a post-conflict environment.

["The activity aims to: (A) improve public expenditure management (PPM) through strengthened public financial management & admin reform (PAR); (B) improve financial sustainability & efficiency of energy SOEs; (C) improve financial sustainability & efficiency of transport SOEs. Key outcomes include: PPM (90% payments registered, employment limits met, 75% positions mapped); Energy SOEs (80% electricity tariff convergence, 70k energy vulnerable beneficiaries, 5% educated EPS workforce, 87% Srbijagas collection, approved Srbijagas plans); Transport SOEs (RSD 11bn budget support for Railways, 15% improved productivity/asset utilization, 25% Railways wage bill reduction, agreed Roads of Serbia performance levels).", "The activity will be considered an overall success upon achieving all specific targets by their respective deadlines. This includes: A1 (at least 90% executed payments duly registered), A2 (public sector employment within institutional limits), A3 (75% of positions mapped to new grades), B1 (80% electricity tariff convergence), B2 (70,000 Energy Vulnerable Program beneficiaries), B3 (5% EPS workforce with education), B4 (87% Srbijagas collection rate), B5 (approved Srbijagas development plans), C1 (RSD 11 billion budget support to Railways), C2 (15% improvement in Railways labor productivity/asset utilization), C3 (25% reduction in Railways wage bill), and C4 (government agreement on Roads of Serbia performance levels for 2019 budget)."]

The activity targets: (i) fiscal sustainability by reducing central government expenditures to 2.8% of GDP, increasing non-oil tax revenue to 13% of GDP, and using COP3.7 trillion FONPET savings. (ii) Fostering non-extractive sector productivity by reducing customs clearance import times to 20 hours, increasing firms registered online (12,000), and boosting Science, Technology, and Innovation projects (COP410bn). (iii) Supporting migrant regularization, aiming for 280,000 Venezuelan migrants registered on RAMV and 50,000 receiving National Employment Agency services. Overall success hinges on achieving these targets, raising investor confidence, de-risking the economy, reducing private sector costs, enhancing resilience to shocks, and fostering medium-term growth.

The activity primarily targets supporting fiscal measures and compliance with the Fiscal Responsibility Law, and Grenada's transition to a Blue Economy by strengthening marine/coastal management, ecosystem health, and climate resilience. Specific outcomes include: maintaining the wage bill below 9% of GDP, increasing successfully targeted customs audits to 5 annually, achieving 100% SOE compliance with new monitoring frameworks, protecting 20% of marine areas, reducing single-use plastic imports by 20%, screening 60% of PSIP infrastructure projects with CCORAL, and ensuring 25% of government contracts have sustainability requirements by 2020.

Targeted outcomes (by FY17/18) for: 1) Fiscal Consolidation: non-sovereign tax/VAT to ~6.7% of GDP, wage bill to 7.4% of GDP, 4+ MoF audits. 2) Sustainable Energy: subsidies to 3.2% of GDP, 1,000 MW electricity surplus (from 5,540 MW deficit), 1,500 MW private renewable energy. 3) Business Environment: 65% increase in business entry, industrial license compliance to 160 days. Success also hinges on FY2018/19 macro-fiscal targets (5.5-6% GDP growth, 24% poverty, ~7% fiscal deficit, 7-8% inflation, 5.5 months import cover), social sector reallocation of savings, and effective social impact management.

The activity aims for sustainable, inclusive economic development by increasing productivity in key sectors (agriculture, fisheries, land, energy, education) and improving transparency in government decisions (PFM, extractives, anti-corruption). Targeted outcomes include: increasing licensed private seed/fertilizer firms (>10 each by 2021); boosting average rice yield to 1.8 MT/ha by 2021; reducing active industrial fishing licenses to 60 by 2021; decreasing electricity losses to 28% by 2021 and teacher absenteeism to 15% by 2021; achieving 85% public procurement through open competition by 2021; 100% new mining leases compliant with EIRL by 2021; and 90% public officials submitting asset declarations by 2021. Success hinges on meeting these productivity and transparency targets, along with effective use of COVID-19 fiscal space.

The activity targets accelerating reforms for an inclusive and resilient recovery in Kenya. Targeted outcomes include: improved transparency and efficiency in public procurement, with 5 government entities procuring on the E-GP platform by mid-2023; enhanced domestic debt market performance with KES 50-75 billion benchmark bond size; 100% cost-recovery in KPLC retail electricity tariffs, terminating or deferring 400 MW of pipeline capacity, and reducing fossil fuel electricity to 5% by mid-2023. Further, it aims for enhanced environmental and climate change governance, with 25 counties developing Climate Change Action Plans, 15 County Value Maps submitted, 70% of WRA's operating costs financed by abstraction revenues, and 300,000 people gaining improved water/sanitation access. Human capital outcomes include 1,000,000 poor and vulnerable households covered by government-financed health insurance subsidies, 65% capacity to respond to public health emergencies, and 30% performance-based recurrent funding for universities by mid-2023. Overall success would be achieved when these policy and institutional reforms are implemented, leading to strengthened KPLC finances, reduced fiscal risks, enhanced transparency in government spending, a green-oriented economic recovery, and fortified health and education sectors, all while meeting the specific quantitative targets across the fiscal, electricity, natural capital, and human capital pillars.

["The activity targets enhancing Nepal's resilience to natural disasters by supporting the government in disaster risk reduction, developing a national resilience strategy, and strengthening its disaster management system. This involves: improving laws/enforcement for building codes; increasing community access to preparedness info and awareness (targeting 50,000 people, establishing 110 community organizations); and enhancing first responder capacity. Specifics include training 600 government staff, improving construction standards in 5 Kathmandu Valley municipalities, reconstructing 160 schools/health posts, training 200 skilled workers, training 4,000 volunteers (including Red Cross), strengthening urban search/rescue, training 28 hospitals in 10 districts, strengthening 36 Emergency Coordination Centers, and storing emergency supplies at 2 IDP sites.", "Overall success will be determined by DFID's support being effectively delivered through key partners (UNDP, ICRC, MSB, BRC, other INGOs) responsible for implementing operational activities within Nepal's disaster risk reduction framework. Crucial factors contributing to success include: DFID's own monitoring and evaluation based on logframe and milestones; independent operational monitoring and impact evaluations by disaster risk reduction experts; and the presence of a well-defined Theory of Change with a comprehensive approach addressing policy, community, and emergency response, coupled with a robust monitoring and evaluation framework involving key partners and independent oversight."]

["The activity targets improving public sector management by: increasing PIMA ratings for government institutions; selected LGs implementing sustainable development plans and ADPs; implementing three service delivery pilots; ensuring PC-1s meet quality criteria; implementing PFM reforms with improved PEFA ratings; increasing budget execution rates; implementing four post-COVID-19 recovery initiatives for women and girls; strengthening social protection systems; developing climate/disaster budget tagging; delivering PM's Strategic Reforms Roadmap; developing two Provincial Own Source Revenue and two Efficiency saving plans; evaluating 10 innovative pilots; and, in Sindh, enhancing efficient planning, disaster risk financing, and risk governance.", "The activity would be considered an overall success if it achieves: improved public sector management; sustainable government institutions and enhanced public finance management; timely, equitable, and citizen-needs-reflective plans/budgets, especially for women, girls, and the poor; government institutions adapting to post-COVID-19 recovery and climate change; additional public service funds from increased tax revenues and improved resource use; innovations deepening reforms, increasing efficiency/transparency; improved state institutions and basic service delivery; fostered political stability, strengthened democracy, and reduced poverty; and alignment with UK priorities for women/girls, climate action, and effective institutions, with initiatives continuing beyond programme lifetime through strengthened systems."]

Targeted outcomes include increasing legal certainty over rural property; clearing 100% lowland areas by 2017; and clearing/titling 100% of national territory (conflict-free) by 2020. By 2021, Gross Value of Agricultural Production is targeted to reach US$1,098/Ha and household income US$1,224/year. Over five years, 24,585,901 hectares are to be regularized and 1,340,196 titles issued for 496,075 households. Success requires achieving these quantitative targets, strengthening women's participation in titling, enhancing deforestation monitoring, and reducing land conflicts. Initial operational success depends on signing subsidiary agreements, appointing key staff, and implementing operating regulations.

Targeted outcomes include strengthening the state's capacity by 2020 for environmental sustainability policies, achieving a 35% reduction in annual HCFC consumption by 2020, and implementing an updated legal/regulatory framework for ODS control, with 100% advancement in drafting related regulations. Other outcomes involve efficient data review/reporting, strengthened coordination with national entities and stakeholders (ensuring 100% compliance knowledge among companies and 170 customs officials), supervised ODS phase-out plans, sustained awareness, regional cooperation in Montreal Protocol meetings, and overall institutional capacity strengthening for UNO/MINSA to fulfill commitments.

The activity targets minimizing environmental/health risks by enhancing sustainable Plastic Waste Management (PWM) through a socio-technical model. Outcomes include managing ~90,600 MT of plastic waste, improving socio-economic conditions for ~38,500 Safai Mitras, and establishing 50 economically sustainable Material Recovery Centres (Swachhta Kendras) for collection, segregation, recycling. It aims to ensure regulatory compliance, improve resource utilization, develop digital governance/knowledge management, propose policy amendments, and achieve gender equality (GEN 2) with national replication strategies.

Targeted outcomes: Increased community coping capacity via strengthened disaster preparedness (knowledge, authority links, DRR mainstreaming); reduced vulnerability via improved health (clinics, inoculation), education (infrastructure, sanitation, literacy), universal potable water, household lighting, zero open defecation, livelihoods. Strengthened NRM, clean energy, DRR policy frameworks, and AAI replication across 128 airports. Success needs: 6 states adopt climate adaptation/DRR strategies (by 2022); 10 DRR plans implemented; enhanced energy access; visible improvements in health, education, water, sanitation; mitigated operational constraints; and demonstrated AAI replication.

["Outcomes: Reduce local communities' climate vulnerability; enhance capacity (integrate climate/gender in plans, train >130 agents/NGOs); resilient investments (9 water infrastructures, 300ha riverbank stabilization, 300 households adopting resilient practices); diversify livelihoods (~4000 people, 3281 women trained, 300 entrepreneurs/50 SMEs capacity). Success: 30-50% vulnerability reduction, 50% annual income increase for target population, climate/gender integration in all municipal plans, and active community participation with minimal disruptions."]

The activity aims to accelerate Kenya's 100% clean cooking ambition by 2028, support resilient development, and mitigate climate change. Targeted outcomes include transitioning 5 public institutions to clean cooking, distributing 10,000 efficient cookstoves to households, building capacity for 500 women/youth in clean cooking enterprises, and strengthening energy policies in national and 3 county governments. It targets directly benefiting 100,000 and indirectly 2,000,000 individuals, contributing to 8.8 million people with clean cooking by 2026. Success is achieving these specific targets, along with demonstrated CO2 emission reductions and new access to green energy, ultimately advancing Kenya's clean cooking goals.

Targeted Outcomes: Reduce system losses, improve revenue collection, enhance financial sustainability and reliability of distribution companies, modernize operations, increase efficiency, improve service quality, and support energy sector restructuring, including institutional capacity building for the Energy Regulatory Authority. Success Criteria: UBEDO must reduce system losses from 33% to 16-20% and accounts receivable from 70 to 30-45 days. Success depends on sustained government commitment to suitable tariffs, bill collection, and time-bound action plans (June 2002 aimag cost financing; Nov 2001 sector reform).

The targeted outcomes of the IN Madhya Pradesh Water Sector Restructuring Project are to improve water productivity for sustainable growth and poverty reduction in the selected focus river basins (Chambal, Sindh, Betwa, Ken, and Tons) of Madhya Pradesh. Specifically, the project aims for: (i) improved incomes of targeted river basin stakeholders, (ii) improved intersectoral environmentally-sustainable water resources management, (iii) modernized and well-performing assets, (iv) increased crop production per unit of water delivered, and (v) increased fisheries production in targeted water bodies. To consider the activity an overall success, all these targeted outcomes must be achieved. This includes 100% of targeted families having increased incomes, the establishment and operationalization of integrated water resources planning and management institutions (SWaRA, SWaTREC, and two RBDMBs), and the preparation of sustainable basin development plans based on a reliable knowledge-base. Additionally, 620,000 hectares of Gross Command Area (GCA) must be modernized, 100% of the target crop yield per unit of water delivered must be achieved, and 100% of the expected fisheries production (3500 tons) in targeted water bodies must be realized, as outlined by the Year 6 target values for the outcome indicators. Success also encompasses the effective implementation of institutional reforms, improved irrigation service delivery, and the modernization of physical assets across the designated basins.

The RY-Integrated Urban Development Project (IUDP) targets improving living conditions for poor and at-risk populations and reducing environmental degradation in priority urban centers (Sana'a and Taiz). Specific outcomes include: improved access to basic services in selected informal settlements in urban areas of Taiz (Al-Maftash, Madinet Al-Nour, Al-Haraziya, and Al-Arous), encompassing improved sanitation, water supply, and all-season roads; strengthened dialogue on a national framework for urban upgrading; and introduction of a viable institutional model for municipal service delivery. This involves upgrading public infrastructure and enhancing Taiz government capacity through institutional strengthening and capacity building activities such as GIS unit establishment and land inventory.

The targeted outcome of the Plan Belgrano Water Supply and Sanitation Services Development Project (P125151) is to increase sustainable access to sanitation and water supply services in Argentina's Norte Grande region through infrastructure investments and supporting institutional development. To be considered an overall success, subprojects must comply with technical, economic, financial, institutional, environmental, and social eligibility criteria, demonstrate consistency with project objectives (especially poverty reduction potential), and show local ownership and results of stakeholder consultation. Additionally, provincial service providers must commit to providing Operation & Maintenance (O&M) funding and operate according to project rules, with an Action Plan for sustainability required from the provinces.

The Solid Waste Sector DPL3 targets enhancing the economic, environmental, and social performance of the waste sector. Outcomes include: strengthened governance (accountability, participation); enhanced institutional/financial sustainability for local authorities (professionalization, fiscal mobilization, waste charges, private sector); upgraded environmental monitoring/control (frameworks, inspections); and viable/inclusive waste valorization (ecotax, recycling, waste picker integration, job creation). Success requires significant improvements in these areas, resolving challenges in service quality, financial sustainability, and monitoring, and increasing the recycling rate towards the 20% 2020 target, delivering professional, integrated waste services and positive social/environmental impacts, especially for vulnerable groups.

["Targeted outcomes include: By 2018, verifying 15% of high-risk public officials' interest declarations, improving public procurement score to B+, reducing graft index to 10%, increasing power/heat cash collection (0.97 KGS/kWh, 1364.31 KGS/Gcal by 2017), disclosing heating sector KPIs. Also, reducing export sales tax to 0%, increasing HACCP/EEU FBO firms to 20 each, 25% VAT refunds via green channel, 50% firms filing social contributions, 20% annual sanitary inspections, 500 firms providing inspection feedback, and increasing international internet bandwidth to 6500 Mbps.", "The activity targets improving public sector governance (integrity, service quality, energy sector financial viability) and strengthening private sector competitiveness (trade, business environment, connectivity). Overall success requires achieving these specific quantitative targets, realizing long-term positive distributional effects from economic growth, improved institutional capacity to deliver quality public services (especially to bottom quintiles), more client-oriented public services, private sector growth benefiting the bottom 40%, and contributing to shared prosperity and inclusive growth."]

The activity targets increasing electricity access, efficiency, and reliability. Key outcomes: connecting 3.2 million new customers (regional electrification to ~90%) by expanding MV/LV lines (19,487/23,594 circuit-km) and 28,327 transformers; improving service quality (reduced SAIDI/SAIFI, MV feeder interruptions, stable voltage, SCADA/GIS upgrades); decreasing distribution losses via infrastructure upgrades and planning; boosting residential energy sales; and strengthening institutional capacity (planning guidelines, ERP/GIS integration).

The activity targets shielding public accounts from hydropower deficit impacts on electricity generation costs, mitigating electricity tariff volatility, cushioning UTE's financial balances from rainfall shortages, and fostering predictable decision-making. It aims to improve Uruguay's weather-related risk management, reduce unexpected borrowing, enhance FEE's risk-mitigating capability, and increase FEE's contribution to reducing short-term electricity tariff volatility. Success would be achieving: 1. Reduction in central government financing needs for electricity generation costs during droughts from US$500 million to US$200 million. 2. Increase in FEE's coverage of these costs from 85% to 95%.

The activity targets enhancing MPUDC's capacity to improve urban service coverage and increase revenue of participating ULBs. Outcomes include: 60 projects completed by MPUDC; 51 ULBs with >=10% annual own-source revenue growth; 280,000 people with improved water access; 340,000 people with improved sanitation. Overall success requires achieving these targets and crucially mitigating identified 'High' Environment and Social risk, and 'Substantial' risks in Technical Design, Institutional Capacity, and Fiduciary management, ensuring sustainable outcomes.

Targeted outcomes: Strengthen public expenditure management and public administration reform (Pillar A); improve financial sustainability and efficiency of energy sector public enterprises (Pillar B); improve financial sustainability, efficiency, and quality of service delivery of public transport companies (Pillar C). Success: A: 100% invoices in central register; employees within limits; 60% on new grades. B: 80% electricity tariff market parity; 100k energy vulnerable beneficiaries; 10% EPS wage bill reduction; 87% Srbijagas collection rate; Srbijagas development plan approved. C: RSD 11bn railways budget support; 15% railways productivity/asset utilization improvement; 25% railways wage bill reduction; Roads of Serbia funding agreement.

The activity targets supporting the turnaround of Rajasthan's electricity distribution sector by strengthening governance, restructuring finances, and improving operational performance under the 24x7 Power for All program. Overall success requires: incentive for FY19 performance disbursed; audited annual accounts by Sept 30, 2019 (zero delay); annual DISCOM loss taken over by state reduced to 10% (FY19); ACoS-ARR gap reduced to INR 0.70/kWh (FY19); AT&C losses reduced to 17% (FY19, 12.5pp reduction); 100,000 consumers on pre-paid/AMR/AMI meters; 16,000,000 LED lamps distributed; 75% IT cadre positions filled (15% female share); and unelectrified households reduced to 750,000 by Sept 2019.

["Targeted outcomes: Remove investment, trade, entrepreneurship barriers (eliminate 10, simplify 17 authorizations; dematerialize 3 trade procedures; reduce Foreign Trade Cert. to 3 days; TUNEPS use: Ministries 100%, SOEs 60%, Parastatals 50%; 20% public contracts to SMEs; operational e-registry for movable collateral; TND 500M SME loans; TND 1,175M microcredits, TND 600M for women). Efficient energy (subsidies to 1% GDP; losses to 12.5%; 82% bill collection; 300 MW private solar). Economic/social inclusion (apply social assistance targeting; finalize children's cash transfer program).", "Overall success requires achieving all specific 2019 targets: 10 eliminated/17 simplified investment authorizations; 3-day Foreign Trade Certificate; TUNEPS use (Ministries 100%, SOEs 60%, Parastatals 50%); 20% public contracts to SMEs; operational e-registry for movable collateral; TND 500M SME loans; 1% GDP electricity/gas subsidies; 12.5% power losses; 82% bill collection; 300 MW private solar; social assistance targeting applied; children's cash transfer design finalized; TND 1,175M microcredits (TND 600M women). Also, improved transparency, reduced costs, increased competition, private sector activity, job creation, and social acceptance of reforms."]

The activity targets increasing electricity supply reliability in western Georgia, achieving GSE's financial viability, and enabling access to long-term commercial financing. Outcomes include eliminating end-user outages from 500 kV OHL failures (from 4 outages/161 minutes to zero), adapting the transmission sector to climate change, improving GSE's financial viability by reducing FX risk, strengthening capacity, eliminating EnergoTrans' cash deficit (from US$3,064,460 to US$0), and mobilizing US$100 million equivalent in long-term commercial financing. Success requires GSE exiting rehabilitation, eliminating EnergoTrans' deficit, ensuring least economic cost investments, strengthening institutional capacity (project management, technical, fiduciary, safeguards), and de-risking first commercial borrowing via IBRD Guarantee.

The Cabo Verde DPO targets (A) reducing fiscal risks from SOEs and improving infrastructure service delivery, and (B) strengthening fiscal management. Pillar A outcomes (2021 targets): Zero central government support to CVA; increased passenger (537k) and cargo (>528k tons) movement; ELECTRA commercial losses <20%; IFH support <0.5% GDP. Pillar B outcomes (2021 targets): Primary balance >=0.4% GDP; 5 additional SAl audits; "largely compliant" Global Forum peer review; tax expenditures/GDP 3.5%; 100% PIMS-compliant new projects. Overall success requires achieving all 2021 targets, completing SOE reforms (e.g., CVA equity sales, privatization of 22 SOEs), issuing an MTFF consistent with new laws, revising the 2020 Public Investment Program, and maintaining a positive macroeconomic trajectory with reduced fiscal deficit and debt-to-GDP ratio.

The activity targets reducing air and climate emissions from solid waste open burning and vehicle emissions (PM10, PM2.5, GHG, black carbon), reducing solid waste fires, and increasing resilience to air pollution. Success entails achieving measured reductions in GHG from municipal solid waste and public buses, black carbon emissions, solid waste fires, increased self-protective actions, an enhanced air quality decision support system, a Climate and Air Quality Action Plan, and improved waste management and e-bus services.

Targeted outcomes: Strengthen water/sanitation policy framework; improve/expand sanitation/wastewater treatment for 128,000 consumers (18,000 poor) in Puerto Plata; improve CORAAPPLATA's financial/operational performance (0.7 operating ratio, consumer cadastre); enhance other regional utilities for APL II (2 signing performance agreements). Overall success requires a functioning Water Sector Council, National Strategy adoption, expanded sanitation coverage (MDG targets), compliant wastewater disposal, and utilities' financial sustainability. APL II triggers include 50% disbursement, 50% APL II contracts ready, CORAAPPLATA compliance, P&FSA signatures, National Strategy adoption, and SEA methodology.

Targeted outcomes: Improve public health & quality of life through sustainable SWM systems in Argentina; develop new environmentally safe regional landfills; close open dumps; establish legal/regulatory frameworks; improve municipal financial management; ensure positive social impact for waste pickers; facilitate recycling; apply Carbon Finance for GHG reduction. Success: Quantifiable waste to sustainable landfills; specific number of dumps closed; enacted SWM plans/regulations; increased recycling rates; improved waste picker well-being; robust financial management; achieved GHG reductions.

The Urban Water Sector Project targets increasing sustainable access to water and sanitation in targeted urban areas. Key outcomes include providing improved water sources to 527,000 additional people, reaching 95% urban water access in Ouagadougou and 94% in Bobo-Dioulasso. For sanitation, 246,000 additional people will gain improved onsite sanitation, and 120,000 students will access adequate school sanitation. Ouagadougou and Bobo-Dioulasso aim for 50% urban sanitation access. ONEA's financial equilibrium will be maintained, with its employee ratio improving from 4.3 to 3.5 per 1,000 connections and a 97% bill collection ratio.

The activity targets include: improving access to and sustainability of water supply and sanitation (WSS) services for consumers in Lusaka, achieved through LWSC achieving 100% operational cost coverage, 90% collection ratio, 2.5% biological samples failing for drinking water, annual leakage control reduction, annual installation of 10,000 new meters, reduction in water supply interruptions, and human resource strategy completion. It also aims to support a comprehensive institutional structure for WSS investments, requiring a completed coordinated investment program for WSS, and operationalization of the Rural WSS Financing Mechanism by MLGH.

The activity targets improving management and accountability for environmental and social issues in large infrastructure investments, initially focusing on the energy sector. Key outcomes include reducing negative externalities of large infrastructure projects; developing a transparent, stable, and fair framework for managing environmental and social risks to improve the enabling environment for investments; and institutionalizing information flows between affected populations, stakeholders, and political decision-makers. It also aims to strengthen the Ministry of Environment and other ministries like Social Affairs, Health, Culture, and Lands, along with civil society, to manage these issues effectively, harmonize national standards with international best practices, and ensure compliance with environmental laws.

The targeted outcomes of the "VN - Mekong Delta Water Management for Rural Dev" activity are to protect and enhance the utilization of water resources in the project provinces of the Mekong Delta region. This is intended to sustain gains in agricultural productivity, raise living standards, and contribute to climate change adaptation.Specific, measurable outcomes with target values include:  * An increase in agricultural production across 50,000 hectares.  * Agricultural production maintained in 50,000 hectares by preventing future salinity intrusion and flood risks.  * Provision of a sustainable and safe potable water supply to 60,000 rural households.  * An increase in water productivity in pilot areas by 20 percent.  * Transfer of 40,000 hectares to water user organizations for managing tertiary and quaternary irrigation facilities.  * Improved sanitation services for 10,000 rural households.To consider the activity an overall success, it would need to achieve its overarching objective of protecting and enhancing water resource utilization to sustain agricultural productivity, raise living standards, and contribute to climate change adaptation in the Mekong Delta. This would be demonstrably achieved by meeting or exceeding all the specific outcome targets outlined above by the project's conclusion.

The activity aims to improve urban infrastructure in Xifeng District, focusing on roads and environmental services. Targeted outcomes include saving freight journey time by 36% (to 18.2 mins), increasing freight traffic share on key corridors (East to 22%, West to 72%), boosting bus speeds on Nan Bei Street by 50% (to 15.5 km/h), expanding storm water/wastewater service to 92% of built-up areas, and reducing wastewater pollution (BOD by 1168, COD by 2569, SS by 2219 tons/year). Economic benefits like reduced travel costs, increased productivity, fewer accidents, better public service access, energy savings, pollution reduction, and improved land value are also expected. Success hinges on achieving these specific targets, demonstrating economic viability, and strengthening institutional capacity for sustainable infrastructure management.

Targeted outcomes include strengthening management systems for national protected areas conservation and wildlife law enforcement, enhancing national institutional capacity, protecting watersheds, developing livelihoods linked to environmental benefits, preserving economic opportunities from ecosystem services, fostering transfrontier ecosystem management, preserving tiger habitat, diminishing illegal cross-border wildlife trade, and limiting biodiversity degradation. Overall success is measured by increasing area under enhanced biodiversity protection (15% METT improvement), submitting international illegal trade reports (target 5 annually), and improving the functional capacity of PA/wildlife management institutions. Success also requires clarifying access/tenure, establishing well-structured PAs with strong leadership, robust law enforcement, and integrating conservation with livelihood support.

The primary targeted outcome is to increase access to clean water for residents in the Greater Maputo Area, specifically expanding water supply services to unserved peri-urban areas. This includes increasing total connections in the Greater Maputo Area by 55 percent and enhancing the financial sustainability of the Maputo water supply system.

The targeted outcomes are to improve sustainable water resources management and increase access to sanitation in Espírito Santo, achieved by strengthening water sector institutions, expanding wastewater collection/treatment, supporting reforestation, and enhancing disaster risk management for 3.5 million people. Overall success requires: 1,590 tons/year BOD pollution removed; 164,485 urban people with improved sanitation access; 9 integrated plans/studies executed; and 2.63 million direct beneficiaries (50% female) by YR6. Further, 2,000 hectares re/afforested and 1,850 hectares with sustainable land practices.

Targeted outcomes for LACEEP include increasing electricity access for approximately 10,300 new household users and 476 commercial/institutional customers, strengthening institutional capacity in the electricity sector (MLME, LEC), establishing an MLME framework for private sector participation in electricity generation, and shifting to more cost-effective HFO-based power generation. To be considered an overall success, the project must achieve these specific targets, including benefiting 48,410 direct beneficiaries (49% female), constructing 16,200 m3 HFO storage, improving cost-effective and reliable electricity supply, and enhancing MLME/LEC planning capabilities for broader service expansion.

The targeted outcomes of the activity include increasing the resilience of coastal communities in Tamil Nadu and Puducherry to a range of hydro-meteorological and geophysical hazards. It also aims to improve the capacity of project implementing entities, government institutions, civil society, and communities to respond promptly and effectively to eligible crises, emergencies, and for risk mitigation. Further outcomes involve reducing community vulnerability through improved multi-hazard resilient housing, evacuation infrastructure, and resilient electrical networks, alongside developing sustainable fisheries and related livelihoods with enhanced safety at sea. The project also aims to promote a local reform process for improved marine fisheries performance, ensuring sustainable social and economic benefits and a healthy marine environment.

The targeted outcomes of the Liberia Renewable Energy Access Project are to increase access to electricity and foster the use of renewable energy sources. Specifically, the project aims to provide 50,000 people with electricity access through off-grid/mini-grid household connections and 100,000 people nationwide with access to modern energy services through stand-alone solar PV systems. It also aims to benefit 150,000 direct project beneficiaries, with 49.6% targeted to be female, and to enhance the capabilities of RREA and MLME. The project also targets decentralized electrification in Lofa County (benefiting at least 8,000 new users), development of regulations for decentralized electricity services and institutional strengthening of RREA, and fostering a market for solar energy lighting devices nationwide.

The Second Water Sector Institutional Development Project targets strengthening selected water sector agencies (PWSUs, INRH, IRSEA, EPAL, DNA) and increasing water service coverage. Specific outcomes include providing 951,150 people (50% female) in urban peri-urban areas with access to improved piped water services by Year 6. Institutional goals include PWSUs tracking costs/billing, IRSEA defining/implementing regulations, and INRH receiving/recording hydrometric data. Other outcomes involve resolving 60% of grievances and 100% community consultation in expansion contracts. Broader outcomes include poverty reduction and public health.

["The primary outcome is to improve the quality of municipal services and pilot energy efficiency and seismic resilience retrofits of urban infrastructure in participating towns. This includes improving water supply (increased duration and quality for 40,000 residents in Sulukta/Kerben, reducing women's burden), solid waste management (improved collection for 40,600 people), and street lighting (more energy-efficient, enhancing road safety, benefiting 15,000 residents). Additionally, it aims to enhance energy performance and seismic resistance of social infrastructure (e.g., schools) for 5,000 students, improving comfort, safety, and yielding energy savings (16,414 mWh) for towns. The project also targets strengthening the capacity of SAACCS in urban planning and participating towns in delivering local services, along with institutional strengthening for utilities. It aims to benefit 59,327 residents overall, with 29,663 females, by enhancing comfort, safety, and reducing the burden of water management and improving public spaces.", "Overall success will be measured by specific PDO-level results indicators. The activity will be considered a success if it achieves an 8-hour daily water supply to households in project-affected areas, provides 40,600 people in urban areas with access to regular solid waste services, realizes 16,414 mWh of projected lifetime energy savings, improves the seismic resistance of 3 social infrastructure units, and reaches 59,327 total project beneficiaries (including 29,663 females). Furthermore, success requires meeting mandatory citizen engagement requirements, enhancing citizen involvement in municipal functions, providing new engagement models, mainstreaming beneficiary feedback, and ensuring equal participation of women. The project's economic viability with an estimated ERR of 16.1% and NPV of USD 18.6 million also contributes to overall success."]

The North Macedonia Public Sector Energy Efficiency Project targets reducing public sector energy consumption (2,454,434,000 MJ lifetime savings) and establishing a sustainable EE Fund. Outcomes include reduced CO2 (235,896 t lifetime) and PM (69,997 t lifetime) emissions, 
A3,851,705 energy cost savings, increased EE project capacity, and 90% enduser satisfaction. Success requires the EE Fund's financial sustainability and widespread commercial financing for public building renovations.

The targeted outcomes for the Vietnam Coastal Cities Sustainable Environment Project are to increase access to sanitation services and improve the operational performance of sanitation utilities in Project Cities. Key outcomes include enhanced connectivity, reduced flooding risks, improved environmental conditions, and better health, especially for vulnerable households. To be considered an overall success, the project must achieve the following Project Development Objective (PDO) Level Results Indicators: 290,000 direct project beneficiaries (with 147,000 or 50.7% female), 256,000 people gaining access to sanitation via household sewer connections, and a 100% cost recovery level for drainage and wastewater services.

The targeted outcomes of the Ecosystem Conservation and Management activity are to improve the management of ecosystems in selected locations in Sri Lanka for conservation and community benefits. This includes enhancing the capacity of the Department of Wildlife Conservation (DWC) and Forest Department (FD) for effective Protected Area (PA) and landscape management, improving nature-based tourism quality, and fostering better institutional coordination. Locally, the project aims to directly benefit approximately 15,000 people (with at least 30% female), improve access to income-generating activities for 10,000 people, and mitigate human-elephant conflict (HEC), focusing on women and vulnerable groups. It also targets securing ecosystem integrity, maintaining viable species populations, and promoting green infrastructure development in three priority conservation landscapes, contributing to the government's goal of increasing forest cover to 35% by 2020 and harnessing ecosystem benefits like watershed protection. Specifically, the project aims to bring 500,000 hectares of landscapes under recognized standards and 200,000 hectares under enhanced biodiversity protection. Success in human-elephant co-existence (HECOEX) would mean protecting 75 villages and agriculture plots, achieving a 50% reduction in HEC-induced damages to crops, properties, and lives, and a 75% reduction in human lives lost in project sites. Tourism outcomes include a 10% increase in visitor revenue and a 50% increase in visitor satisfaction in selected PAs.Overall success would require achieving these specific targets, effectively addressing weak institutional coordination and outdated capacity, and successfully managing the Substantial risks related to Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability, and Fiduciary aspects. The project's strong alignment with national and international development goals and its detailed component structure contribute to its potential for success if these challenges are overcome.

The activity targets providing short-term employment to 58,700 individuals (30% female, 20% IDPs/returnees, 30% youth 16-25) and access to key services for 387,000 poor people (40% female). A critical outcome is preserving the implementation capacity of SFD and PWP by retaining 70% of their core staff. Success will be measured by achieving these beneficiary numbers and staff retention percentages as per the disaggregated targets.

The Sindh Solar Energy Project targets increasing solar power generation and access to electricity in Sindh Province. Key outcomes include developing 400 MW of utility-scale solar by the private sector and 20 MW of distributed solar on public buildings. Overall, it aims for 420 MW of new renewable energy generation capacity and to provide 1,200,000 people with new or improved electricity service. Other outcomes include reducing GHG emissions, lowering public sector electricity bills to help reduce circular debt, decreasing household energy expenditure, and enhancing educational/livelihood opportunities. Success requires effectively implementing competitive bidding, attracting private sector investment, achieving generation/access targets, competitive tariffs, public institution participation, and consumer demand for Solar Home Systems.

Targeted outcomes: Strengthen integrated pollution and watershed management, increase access to improved water supply, reduce nonpoint pollution (nitrogen, phosphorus) and soil erosion, restore degraded ecosystems, develop long-term management, and enhance coordination. Also, increase landscape resilience and mitigate climate change via Climate Smart Agriculture.Overall success: Measured by nitrogen/phosphorus load reduction, decreased soil erosion, and increased access to improved water sources. Key for success: strong alignment with plans, significant financial commitment, effective innovative approaches (CSA, IPM, PES), and robust institutional capacity building/monitoring.

The activity aims to: (1) support the transition of the electricity social tariff (ST) scheme from federal to provincial level; (2) strengthen institutional capacity for ST delivery in Buenos Aires, including governance, procedures, and data systems; (3) improve identification and monitoring of ST beneficiaries by closing information gaps and reducing users with missing identification/socioeconomic data; (4) enhance transparency and grievance mechanisms; (5) improve ST targeting efficiency by reforming eligibility criteria, reducing inclusion/exclusion errors, and decreasing reliance on ad-hoc assessment mechanisms. Overall success requires the electricity ST scheme's full transition to Buenos Aires provincial control, with approved institutional arrangements and strengthened PBA capacity. This includes creating a bipartite agency and integrating ST monitoring functions. Key success indicators are: a substantial reduction in users with incomplete identification/socioeconomic data (e.g., to 630,000 from baselines over 830,000), establishment of an integrated, transparent database, and a decrease in ST users included via ad hoc mechanisms (from 18.3% to 10%), signifying improved targeting and reduced errors. Achieving medium-term ST reforms is also crucial.

The targeted outcomes include promoting sustainable forest management, improving protected area management, enhancing livelihoods, reducing flood/drought/landslide risks, increasing forest cover, enhancing nature-based tourism, protecting biodiversity, reducing greenhouse gas emissions (over 10.2 million tons CO2eq), creating jobs, reducing poverty, boosting forest productivity, and supporting climate adaptation/mitigation. Overall success requires: 1) maintained/increased landscape forest cover (ha), 2) protected areas scoring >50 on METT, 3) households/groups engaged in improved livelihoods, and 4) beneficiaries (50% women) from job interventions.

The North Macedonia Public Finance and Competitiveness DPL targets strengthening public finances, improving market competition, and reducing regulatory burdens. Success would be achieved by: reducing PAYG pension deficit to 4.45% of GDP by 2019; increasing social assistance coverage of the bottom quintile to 50% overall and 40% for female-led households by 2019; decreasing general government arrears to below 2.7% of GDP by 2019; increasing personal income tax revenues to 2.7% of GDP by 2019; raising fossil fuel excise tax coverage to 92.9% by 2019; enabling 100% electricity consumer choice by 2019; increasing average bidders per procurement above 3.41; and introducing risk-based inspections in at least three inspectorates by mid-2020.

Targeted outcomes: Strengthen NES performance; improve grid capacity/reliability for large-scale renewable energy (RE) integration; enhance operational efficiency; improve electricity supply reliability; advance market/institutional reforms; commercialize NES; enable financing; enhance regional trade; transition to wholesale market; aid NES financial recovery; improve RE forecasting. Success is measured by: increased national transmission capacity (MVA/kVA), reduced average interruption frequency (SAIFI), enabled integration of large-scale RE (MW), and prevented direct GHG emissions (metric tons CO2).

The activity aims to significantly reduce diesel consumption in electricity generation for hydrocarbon activities, projecting annual displacement of ~245.74 million gallons of diesel and ~223,013 barrels of crude by 2018. It targets annual net economic savings exceeding US$785 million from 2018. Key outcomes include reducing imports of petroleum derivatives, enhancing electricity supply through increased renewable energies and natural gas, and improving efficiency and sustainability in electricity service. The program also seeks to curb inefficient LPG consumption, optimize electricity use, and establish a regulatory framework for replacing energy subsidies, adjusting rate schedules to promote an LPG-to-electricity shift. Regional electricity integration is a target, involving SINEA agreement coordination, energy surplus export, and progress on Ecuador-Peru transmission lines and electricity exchange policies. Ultimately, the activity contributes to climate change mitigation via reduced CO2 emissions.

The Montevideo Urban Sanitation Program (PSU V) targets reduced daily organic load to Montevideo Bay in dry weather from 47.00 tonDBO/day (2015) to 16.00 tonDBO/day by EOP 2022, and reduced Manga neighborhood flooding during heavy rains (20mm/30min) from 100% (2015) to 0.00% by EOP 2022. It aims for increased households with enabled sanitation connections in Manga/Casabó from 0.00% (2015) to 43.00% by EOP 2022, and increased sanitation tariff billing revenues from 1,414 million current pesos (2015) to 2,077 million current pesos by EOP 2022. Other goals include expanding sanitation coverage, reducing storm drainage, diminishing pollution, and enhancing municipal sanitation services management, aiming for 20,000 new customers and 70 million cubic meters billed annually by 2020. Overall success requires achieving these specific targets, ensuring financial sustainability (revenues cover operation, maintenance, debt service, and local investment costs), and effective mitigation of environmental/social impacts.
