["The program targets a more sustainable, efficient energy sector in Guyana by: 1) Promoting sustainable electricity loss reduction, including improved GP&L billing, new Customer Information System installation, continuous loss monitoring, educational campaigns, and achieving electricity losses under 20.4% (12-month average). 2) Implementing institutional, legal, and regulatory reforms, such as enacting updated legislation/licenses, strengthening the PUC, establishing new tariff structures, and a new power sector policy. 3) Strengthening utility capabilities through improved corporate governance, new bylaws, and a revised Board.", "Overall success requires: 1) Borrower's macroeconomic framework consistency. 2) Updated legal/regulatory framework (PUCA/related legislation) fully enacted with operating regulations, implemented for 6+ months. 3) PUC capacity enhanced per approved plans. 4) Utility's new corporate bylaws/instruments implemented for 6+ months. 5) New Customer Information System (billing module) fully installed/operating. 6) Utility continuously monitoring electricity losses. 7) Utility Board continuously monitoring loss reduction plan. 8) Electricity losses under 20.4% (12-month avg). 9) Utility continuing loss reduction/demand management educational campaign."]

Targeted outcomes: Strengthen Muanda communities' climate resilience; establish climate risk information system for coastal protection planning; enhance state/stakeholder climate risk management capacity; implement urgent adaptation measures, including Early Warning System (EWS) and meteorological forecasting; promote 200 improved cookstoves; restore ecosystems; create 100 youth jobs through alternative livelihoods; protect lives/livelihoods. Success needs scientific/technical capacity, active community participation in vulnerability assessment/adaptation, and accessible information, while overcoming barriers like lacking operational risk management, low meteorological capacity, and limited finance.

Targeted outcomes include reducing GHG emissions through widespread EC&EE/low-carbon technology adoption in public sector buildings. This involves establishing an ICT-based EMIS for energy monitoring, creating demand via mandatory EE targets and public procurement, and stimulating market supply for energy management services (EPC, ESCO financial mechanisms). Other outcomes are the design and demonstration of 20 EC&EE/LC technology applications, increased participation and awareness in energy monitoring/initiatives, achieving energy/cost savings, institutionalizing supportive policies for sustainability, and enhancing local ESCO/consulting firm capabilities.

["The activity aims to improve quality of life in the Asunción Metropolitan Area by integrating transport, solid waste management, and green infrastructure. Specific outcomes include: establishing a functional green city framework; implementing sustainable mobility reducing 1,227,442 tCO2e (plus 255,100 tCO2 from TOD) and restructuring 84 bus routes; improving waste/chemical management to reduce 13.2 g-TEQ UPOPs and 110 tCO2e, formalizing 50 informal recyclers (50%+ women), and piloting 6 tons/day recycling; enhancing management of protected urban green areas, approving RBSMBA plan, and increasing 5 emblematic bird species by 1%; and disseminating lessons learned.", "Overall success requires achieving all environmental targets (GHG/UPOP reductions, bird population increase). It means fully implementing the green city framework, functional sustainable transport (optimized routes, eco-shelters, emission standards), and a robust waste/chemical management system with formalized recyclers and effective facilities. Success also entails approved green area management plans and enhanced urban biodiversity. Crucially, it demands addressing challenges like non-compliance, technical capacity gaps, low public awareness, and gender inequality, coupled with strong institutional collaboration and long-term financial sustainability."]

Targeted outcomes: Mainstream BD conservation in Mexico's tourism via a new Sustainable Tourism Development model, strengthening capacities, establishing mandatory legal/policy BD safeguards, creating incentives, enhancing local management, enabling sustainable practices, integrating BD into laws/strategies/norms, establishing 10 indicators/monitoring systems, and addressing market failures for BD/ecosystem services survival (including blue carbon sinks) and enduring change. Success requires political will, active stakeholder involvement, resources, effective BD safeguard integration, functional monitoring, market failure resolution, local participation, capacity building, and long-term model viability.

["The activity aims to eliminate or avoid mercury use in the Artisanal and Small-Scale Gold Mining (ASGM) sector, targeting 11 municipalities in Colombia. This is pursued through four pillars: strengthening institutions and regulatory frameworks for mercury-free ASGM, increasing ASGM communities' access to financing for mercury-free technologies, enhancing capacity for mercury-free practices (technical assistance, technology transfer, formalization), and raising awareness. Key quantitative outcomes include eliminating or avoiding at least 20 metric tons of mercury over 5 years (4 tons annually), achieving complete elimination in prioritized communities, and directly benefiting 292,697 individuals (141,924 women, 150,773 men) by reducing mercury exposure risk. Additionally, the project targets the participation of at least 2,000 miners.", "To consider the activity an overall success, it would be needed to fully achieve the elimination or avoidance of 20 metric tons of mercury over the 5-year duration, along with the complete elimination of mercury use in all 11 prioritized communities. Success also entails the direct benefit of 292,697 individuals from reduced mercury exposure, the active participation of at least 2,000 miners, and the establishment of at least two new national alliance mechanisms with financial institutions/private investors and one new international refiner alliance to boost sustainable ASGM financing. Furthermore, sustained capacity building for 22 government entities, improved ASGM regulation, a developed consulting sector, and established market routes leading to increased income for mercury-free gold producers are crucial for overall success."]

["The activity targets the development of a sustainable national and local solid waste management (SWM) strategy in Haiti, providing technical assistance to the Ministry of Environment. It aims to strengthen SWM capacities in Jérémie and Ouanaminthe, establish innovative SWM infrastructure, promote a circular economy, and create SWM-related jobs, particularly for vulnerable groups. Key outcomes include improved public health, reduced environmental pollution (air, soil, water), enhanced cooperation among stakeholders, and ultimately, poverty reduction and better living conditions for the most vulnerable.", "Overall success requires the definition and implementation of a sustainable national and local SWM strategy, with strengthened capacities of local authorities and the Ministry of Environment. It necessitates the operationalization of appropriate SWM infrastructure (e.g., Jérémie ecosite), demonstrable reduction in environmental pollution and improved public health, successful promotion of a circular economy, creation of formal SWM jobs, increased waste collection coverage, and establishment of a functional cost recovery mechanism. Enhanced cooperation and active community participation, addressing gender inequalities, are also critical."]

["The activity aims to increase economic opportunity and biodiversity conservation for local communities and indigenous peoples through fair and equitable sharing of biodiversity benefits. Targeted outcomes include strengthened national ABS regulatory frameworks and institutional mechanisms, enhanced understanding of the ABS regime and traditional knowledge, and the demonstration of benefit-sharing agreements under the Nagoya Protocol. Anticipated impacts are increased wealth creation through safeguarding biological resources, recognition of customary law/indigenous knowledge, and improved management/sustainable use of genetic resources.", "Overall success requires a robust national ABS framework with revised policies, clear procedures, and a functional administrative system for ABS. It also demands enhanced capacity and awareness among all stakeholders (government, IPs, communities, private/research sectors) on ABS implementation and traditional knowledge value. Success includes successfully negotiating at least one ABS agreement with fair and equitable benefit-sharing, developing at least two bio-products, and realizing sustainable wealth creation, customary law recognition, and improved genetic resource management."]

The targeted outcomes of the "Reducing Maritime Trafficking of Wildlife between Africa and Asia" activity are:1. Stakeholders at ports and across the shipping sector working towards and maintaining best practice in combating maritime wildlife trafficking.2. Strengthened South-South cooperation and enhanced UN coordination on combating wildlife trafficking.3. Continuous learning and sharing of lessons across the Global Wildlife Program.The overall objective of the project is to reduce maritime trafficking of wildlife between Africa and Asia by strengthening wildlife law enforcement capacity at ports and improving South-South cooperation. Specifically, this involves breaking down barriers such as a lack of awareness and capacity at African and Asian ports, and insufficient capacity and willingness for cooperation along trade routes.For the activity to be considered an overall success, the following would need to be achieved:1.  **Increased Stakeholder Involvement:** Stakeholders and authorities at four targeted ports must be actively involved in efforts to combat maritime wildlife trafficking.2.  **Increased Beneficiaries:** A total of 200 direct project beneficiaries should be reached, with 40% being female.3.  **Enhanced Enforcement Statistics:**-   A 10% increase in the number of seizures of illegal wildlife products.-   A 10% increase in the number of arrests related to wildlife trafficking.-   A 10% improvement in the percentage of seizures that translate into arrests.Key elements for success also include:4.  **Public and Private Sector Engagement:** Successfully bringing together public and private sector interests in transportation and trade routes to establish best practices for combating wildlife trafficking at ports, responding to commitment #10 in the Buckingham Palace Declaration.5.  **Incentive Mechanisms and Capacity Building:** Establishing sufficient incentive mechanisms and providing capacity building to motivate port staff to prevent trafficking.6.  **Awareness and Diligence:** Increasing awareness among supply chain workers about penalties for wildlife trafficking and the benefits of diligence.7.  **Stronger Cooperation:** Fostering stronger familiarity and cooperation among officials at selected ports for intelligence sharing and joint operations.8.  **Coordinated Support:** Leveraging enhanced coordination and aligned support from UN and international organizations.9.  **Continuous Improvement:** Ensuring ongoing monitoring, evaluation, lessons learning, and sharing throughout the implementation.

Targeted outcomes: Strengthen national capacity for PCBs and uPOPs management, achieving PCB elimination and reducing uPOPs from open burning. Specific targets: sound disposal of at least 75 tonnes of PCB-contaminated equipment/waste, and a 35 grams I-TEQ/year reduction of uPOPs. Key outcomes include drafting/implementing a national Environmental Sound Management (ESM) system for PCBs/waste, upgrading transformer storage facilities, and utilizing a comprehensive national PCB inventory. Success requires control and secure, environmentally safe disposal of PCB equipment, cooperation from owners, and building adequate, sustained national capacity for ongoing PCB and uPOPs management post-project.

The activity targets reducing vulnerability of communities/ecosystems in San Francisco Menéndez to drought, soil erosion, and flooding through forest landscape restoration. Outcomes include increasing forest cover, improving hydrological cycles/water availability, reducing land degradation, and enhancing food security/community resilience. Concrete targets: 65 community restoration plans (3,865 ha), restoration of 284.52 ha riparian forests/141 ha mangroves, 2,708 ha agroforestry for grains, 664 ha silvopastoral, 67 ha coffee/cocoa agroforestry, and strengthened local capacity in water management. Success means achieving these targets, improving over 30,000 livelihoods, and establishing a coordinated adaptation governance.

The activity targets three main outcomes: 1) Strengthening policy, legal, and institutional frameworks for MPA creation and management, including establishing a national strategy and an MPA unit within INBAC. 2) Implementing an integrated management plan for a priority high biodiversity MPA to conserve endangered marine species, reduce threats, and promote sustainable fisheries/tourism with livelihood opportunities for women. 3) Ensuring lessons learned through knowledge management, monitoring, evaluation, and gender mainstreaming support national/international MPA creation/implementation. Overall success hinges on establishing Angola's first MPA, achieving stable/increasing marine species populations and habitat conditions, mitigating unsustainable development impacts, ensuring equitable community benefits, improving national/provincial MPA frameworks, and widely disseminating project learnings to inform policy.

The activity aims to reduce climate change impacts on local communities and coral reef-dependent economic sectors in Mauritius and Seychelles by implementing thermal tolerant coral reef restoration. Key outcomes include enhancing food security, livelihoods, and disaster risk mitigation by restoring essential ecosystem services. It targets generating and disseminating regional knowledge on coral reef restoration to other SIDS and WIO countries, building regional capacity. Specific outcomes cover developing sustainable partnerships and community/business approaches, establishing coral farming/nursery facilities, active restoration of degraded reefs, improved knowledge management, regional/global lesson sharing, and training for long-term sustainable restoration.

["The targeted outcomes of the Tina River Hydropower Project include increased generation of renewable energy on the Honiara grid, aiming for hydropower to generate 68% of Honiara's electricity by 2025 (up from 1% baseline in 2018). It also targets a reduction in CO2 emissions by 49,500 tons per annum by 2025. The project aims to improve economic conditions for Honiara, reduce the cost of power supply generation by replacing diesel power, and expand residential access to the Honiara grid. It aligns with UN SDGs 7 and 13.", "Overall success would require achieving 68% renewable energy generation for Honiara's power and reducing CO2 emissions by 49,500 tCO2e annually, both by 2025. Key outputs like the 15 MW Tina River Hydropower Plant, a 20 km access road, and a 23 km 33 KV transmission line must be constructed and commissioned by December 2024. Additionally, at least 80% of livelihood training participants (30% women) must report improved skills, ensuring improved economic conditions and expanded residential grid access."]

The activity targets developing a Shire River Basin planning framework to improve land/water management for ecosystem/livelihood benefits. This involves strengthening institutions for monitoring/planning/decision support, investing in water infrastructure, reducing erosion/sedimentation/flooding, enhancing environmental services/agricultural productivity/livelihoods, improving Lower Shire flood management, and protecting ecological services infrastructure. Success requires: a Shire Basin Plan; 10% vegetation cover increase; 8% less hydropower downtime; 20,000 households re-classified lower flood risk; 430,000 beneficiaries (50% female); operational multi-sector institution; real-time hydromet data; national catchment guidelines; Kamuzu Barrage (stage one) completed; effective Lower Shire flood warning; and GoM commitment.

["The targeted outcomes of the Pakistan Punjab Jobs and Competitiveness Program for Results include improving the investment climate and promoting investments and jobs in more inclusive and sustainable industrial estates in Punjab. It aims to contribute to the Punjab Growth Strategy (PGS) to increase growth and job creation, particularly for women, and improve financial and environmental sustainability. A key outcome is re-igniting manufacturing growth, targeting 10% annual growth in five years, and ensuring sustainable growth.", "Further targeted outcomes include generating employment and increasing per capita income through policies facilitating private sector investment, firm productivity, and internationally competitive, export-oriented industrial clusters. The program seeks to improve the Ease of Doing Business Indicator across business registration, contract enforcement, construction permits, and property registration; reinforce institutions for business environment improvement (e.g., Public Private Dialogue, Investment Promotion, online regulation registry); develop a spatial economic development plan; improve industrial estates (including Common Effluent Treatment Plants or CETPs); and increase support to SMEs.", "The program also aims to improve Punjab's contribution to Pakistan's compliance with GSP+ Labor Conventions; improve investment promotion, particularly for foreign investors through more announcements; effectively develop industrial infrastructure through Public-Private Partnerships (PPPs); design and implement a new institutional framework to support Industrial Estates and clusters; and ultimately increase the number of additional workers (including women) and new investments (including FDI) in existing industrial estates.", "To be considered an overall success, the activity needs to achieve an aggregate improvement in Punjab's performance in business registration, contract enforcement, construction permits, and property registration, reaching a maximum target of 75 points (with a minimum of 64 points). Success also requires taking specific steps to improve Punjab's compliance with GSP+ Labor Conventions (measured as Pass/Fail for each step) and significant progress in investment promotion, targeting up to 45 investment announcements by foreign investors (with a minimum of 1 investment announcement or restructuring steps).", "Further success criteria include developing and implementing the Punjab Spatial Strategy, marked by recommendations from a pre-feasibility study for a new city (Year 1), Cabinet approval of the Spatial Strategy (Year 2), and 80% consistency in subsequent years. Effective development of industrial infrastructure through PPPs is crucial, measured by up to 6 industrial estate and CETP projects presented to the PPP Steering Committee (minimum 1 project), and in Year 5, one CETP project in an existing Industrial Estate presented to the PPP SC must become operational. Designing and implementing a new institutional framework for industrial estates and clusters, alongside increased employment (especially for women) and new investments by new companies (including FDI) in existing industrial estates, are also key."]

The targeted outcomes of the activity are to: (i) improve the delivery of irrigation and drainage services in selected areas, and (ii) develop improved policies and procedures as a basis for a national program of land registration, ultimately ensuring owners of irrigated or drained land in selected areas have registered land titles. Specifically, it aims to directly benefit about 31,000 farming households cultivating approximately 26,000 ha of agricultural land through increased agricultural productivity, and ~19,000 households with unregistered agricultural land plots in pilot areas will benefit from improved land registration policies and procedures, with a longer-term benefit for ~640,000 rural households. It also targets capacity building for the MOA Department of Amelioration, UASCG, and NAPR. Expected benefits include increased yields (30-50%), cropping intensity (100-115%), ~13,000 ha of unused arable land returned to production, and an incremental annual gross margin of US$690 per farm household. The project aims to create pathways for farm households to escape poverty through higher farm/non-farm sales, wage earnings, or rental incomes via secure land titles.

Targeted outcomes: improved economic/social conditions in Alto Solimoes by raising income, increased potable water/sanitation access, improved health outcomes, enhanced public sector management, enabling institutional environment, and better living conditions for all. Success requires: 2/3 productive subprojects operational, 2 productive chains viable/sustainable; WSS consortium established/operating in >half municipal capitals, water supply optimized in >=4; >=1 new health service model, >=2 municipalities using performance-based health grants for >=2 years with compliance, NGO health TA to >=3 municipalities, new Family Health model approved, and positive WSS environmental/social impacts.

The targeted outcome is to increase access to safely managed sanitation services and strengthen municipal sanitation service delivery capacity in selected cities. Overall success is defined by: 197,193 people gaining access to safely managed sanitation (including 98,597 women and 78,878 from poorest quintiles); increasing cities with an operational sanitation entity compliant with regulatory framework from two to five; and approving and publishing five municipal bylaws. Additional success metrics include reduced water/sanitation-related diseases, child stunting, cholera, and improved climate resilience.

The targeted outcomes of the activity are to improve access to improved water supply and sanitation services in targeted areas in Eswatini and to strengthen the regulatory framework for national rural water supply and sanitation service provision. More specifically, the project aims to increase potable water supply coverage for domestic, institutional, and commercial consumption in the Shiselweni region by constructing a transmission pipeline between Nhlangano and Matsanjeni and a distribution network, thereby providing safely managed or basic water access to populations along the pipeline. Quantifiable targets include providing 18,478 people (including 9,239 female) with access to improved water sources, 8,000 people (including 4,000 female) with access to improved sanitation services, and reaching 38,233 people through hygiene behavior awareness campaigns.What would be needed to consider the activity an overall success includes: achieving the specific quantifiable targets for improved water supply and sanitation access and hygiene awareness campaigns; successful provision of improved water access and sanitation in the target areas through infrastructure investments; a decrease in water-related diseases and an improved quality of life among vulnerable populations; sustainable use of improved water supply and sanitation facilities; increased population resilience to extreme water-related events, including those caused by climate change; an improved ability to plan for future water supply and sanitation investments; increased capacity of the Government of the Kingdom of Eswatini (GoKE) to prepare for, manage, and respond to climate change-related risks, particularly droughts; a reduction in the time women and youth spend fetching water, enabling their participation in other activities (economic or educational); and the strengthening of the regulatory framework for national rural water supply and sanitation service provision.

["The targeted outcomes include increasing Sierra Leone's electricity access from ~15% to 30%, electrifying all district headquarters by 2023, and connecting 20 villages and eight towns per district to the grid or off-grid schemes. Electricity generation aims to rise from 25% to 60%, installed capacity from 100 MW to 350 MW, with 65% from renewables. It also targets providing new or improved electricity to people, health facilities (~200) and schools (~500) via standalone PV, and ~9,100 industrial/commercial entities. Other outcomes include improved energy sector financial performance, enhanced rural electrification with private sector involvement, reduced GHG emissions, strengthened institutional capacity for MoE and EDSA, and promotion of gender equality and economic opportunities for women.", "Overall success requires achieving the primary goal of increasing national electricity access from ~15% to 30% by 2026-12-31, including electrification of all district headquarters by 2023. This involves successfully connecting target populations, health facilities, and schools, and providing ~9,100 businesses with electricity. Quantifiable success also includes boosting generation to 60% and capacity to 350 MW, with 65% from renewables, and meeting GHG reduction targets. Furthermore, sustained financial performance of the energy sector, effective private sector engagement in rural areas, strengthened institutional capacity, and tangible socio-economic benefits (e.g., job creation, improved health/education) and adherence to environmental/social safeguards are crucial."]

The activity aims to strengthen CSOs and enable citizens, especially the poor and vulnerable, to engage with and influence government, fostering increased trust, more equitable public services, and improved human rights. Outcomes include effective CSO engagement, promoting sustainable peace via greater inclusion of women and civic actors in national dialogue and transitional justice, and protecting civic space. It also seeks to increase disadvantaged groups' voice and access to services, support girls' education, women's livelihoods, reintegration of women prisoners, and address mental health, while fostering CSO self-financing.

Outcomes: Soil conservation/water runoff regulation in 15% of priority areas; 8 communities adopt soil/water conservation in 50% productive areas; 10% communities incorporate new activities (solar tents, fishing/fish farming); 1,400 farmers benefit; 35% (358 ha) priority gullies/slopes, 28 ha slopes with sediment retention/stabilization; 115 ha priority degraded areas intervened. Success: Year 3: 42% production areas/30% families in 8 communities use conservation. Year 4: Fishing/fish farming, solar tent pilots underway, showing participation/capacity. Baseline and monitoring system established.

The activity targets enhanced climate-resilient development, reduced water scarcity vulnerability, and increased resilience for health, food/water security, and vulnerable populations. Key outcomes include: 3-3.5% groundwater overdraft reduction in the Dead Sea Basin, 1.83 MCM water savings in 10 years (5.49 MCM lifespan), 10,600 hectares of resilient agricultural land, and benefitting 212,416 people (47% women). Success entails achieving these reductions, savings, and beneficiaries; optimizing reclaimed water reuse for 968 farmers; installing 8250 rooftop harvesting systems; training 6,000 farmers and 400 women; and implementing policy/curricula reforms to overcome adaptation barriers.

The activity targets maintaining Kalimantan's forest areas, biodiversity, and ecosystem functions by reducing conversion threats across at least 418,419 hectares, with flow-on effects on 2.36 million hectares, and saving up to 8.76 million T CO2. Success hinges on at least four incentive payments totaling $5 million leading to 50,000 hectares of avoided deforestation and improved biodiversity health index. Additionally, 7 documented lessons must be applied in other sub-national/national contexts by project end, alongside mainstreaming green growth policies.

The targeted outcomes of the activity are: (1) strengthening multi-level governance for sustainable management and production in multiple use landscapes (MUL) and high value conservation forests (HVCF) in the Ecuadorian Amazon (CTEA); (2) enhancing access to markets, credit, and incentives for sustainable production of main products within these landscapes; (3) implementing sustainable practices at the landscape level aligned with conservation and restoration of HVCF; and (4) effective dissemination of lessons learned, monitoring, and evaluation. Overall success requires catalyzing transformation of land use planning in the CTEA via a governance and sustainable production framework. This includes maintaining ecosystem services in 1,859,600 ha of MUL/HVCF, avoiding 257,566.69 tCO2e (6 years) and 8,726,514.50 tCO2e (20 years), conserving 439,369 ha of HVCFs in indigenous/community lands, managing 172,646 ha sustainably, improving capacities for 5,164 producers, and achieving 35% female beneficiaries with equitable access and leadership.

Targeted outcomes include: bringing over one-third (>3M ha) of natural forest estates under sustainable forest management (SFM), increasing timber asset value from $4.4B to $6.4B over 40 years, and increasing total growing stock to ~135M m³ by 2045. It aims to reduce poverty and improve livelihoods for 80,000 poorest households in 60 communes by providing clear forestland-use rights, generating 23M person-days of wage opportunities, and ensuring an additional $110/household income in year 1. Other outcomes are enhanced biodiversity protection, improved watershed performance, pro-poor/market-friendly sector governance, and strengthened capacity for SFM (6 provinces, >550 professionals, ~1000 technicians, 20 new SMEs, 75 community groups). For overall success, the activity must achieve: over one-third of natural forest estates under SFM, secure >70% of national timber needs (2010-2015), eliminate hunger and halve poverty for 80,000 poorest households, increase timber asset value as projected, meet demand for sustainably harvested resources, and curtail illegal logging.

The targeted outcomes of the Shandong Hai River Basin Pollution Control activity are to improve the urban environment and public health in Shandong province by reducing pollution of the Hai River Basin (HRB) from various sources. This includes providing improved and sustainable wastewater and solid waste management (SWM) services through economically and environmentally sound principles, establishing appropriate utility companies, and implementing an integrated approach for managing solid waste, municipal, and industrial wastewater. Specific outcomes include reducing high levels of untreated wastewater (75% COD removal), adopting cleaner industrial technologies, promoting water reuse, controlling non-point source pollution via effective SWM, and developing sustainable wastewater treatment and SWM companies. For MWM, this means eliminating a source of groundwater pollution and promoting water reuse. For SWM, it aims to improve residents' quality of life and eliminate a major future pollution source. For IWM, the goal is to significantly reduce regulated pollutants (300,000 t/y COD, 200,000 t/y inorganic salts), improve resource recovery, and eliminate groundwater pollution.Overall success depends on addressing project startup and procurement delays, strengthening institutional arrangements for sustainable operations (including establishing financially sustainable, operationally efficient, and managerially autonomous utility companies with direct tariff collection and phased tariff increases), strong commitment from municipal/provincial governments, local government and consumer support for tariff adjustments, synchronization of loan procedures, robust project ownership by all stakeholders, close coordination, commercialization of utility operations, improved regulatory roles, and establishing practical project performance monitoring systems. Financial sustainability also requires appropriate user charges and government subsidies. Compliance of paper mills with stringent discharge standards by 2007 is also key.

["The targeted outcomes of the activity, by 2029, are to improve the efficiency, reliability, and quality of power supply to consumers in Uttarakhand, ensuring continuous reliable power for all and catalyzing a clean energy transition. This will involve strengthening, modernizing, and climate-proofing the power network and enhancing the institutional capacity of implementing agencies.", "To be considered an overall success by 2029, the activity must achieve specific targets: AT&C losses reduced to 12% (from 15.25% baseline in 2021), average duration of outages (SAIDI) for urban feeders decreased to less than 50 hours/consumer/year (from 99 hours/consumer/year baseline in 2022), and greenhouse gas emissions reduced by 178,527 tons per year. Critical assumptions for success include full implementation of smart metering under the RDSS program and power system installations' ability to withstand extreme weather within design projections."]

{"outcomes": [{"description": "Targeted outcomes include increasing electricity access to approximately 16% of households and at least 50% of identified public institutions by 2012, requiring over 200,000 new grid connections (specifically boosting urban/peri-urban consumers from 110,000 to 210,000 households by end-2012). The project also aims to assist the Government of Rwanda (GoR) in mainstreaming the energy Sector-Wide Approach (SWAp) by establishing a functioning donor partnership framework for sustained financing and capacity strengthening, aligned with national priorities and results."}, {"description": "Success would be indicated by achieving these specific connection targets. It also requires demonstrating improved service quality (measured by a reduced rolling average of monthly interruptions per km of MV line) and enhanced cost-effectiveness (measured by the average cost per connection, aiming to reduce MV line costs from $55,000/km to $30,000/km)."}, {"description": "Financial sustainability of RECO is critical for success, requiring it to maintain retail tariffs for a positive return on assets, contribute at least 20% of its investment program costs (including 10% from consumer contributions), limit accounts receivable from public sector and large consumers to less than 90 days, and establish a Distribution Capital Reserve Account. Success also depends on an increased share of sector financing from donors aligned with the government's sector investment prospectus, achieving the estimated 40% Economic Internal Rate of Return (EIRR) for the overall National Electricity Access Program, and effective implementation of \"green connections\" for affordability and efficiency."}]}

The targeted outcomes are: reducing commercial losses in electricity and gas systems (e.g., electricity losses to 10% by 2010; 76 MMCM increase in billed gas sales over four years); establishing a foundation for improving the financial viability of utilities; increasing billed gas sales; improving services, building institutions, and fostering private sector development. Success is defined by: significant reduction in commercial losses; improved financial viability (e.g., 30% self-financing for investments; electricity tariffs reaching 2.1 US cents/kWh by 2010; 20% revenue increase from unified seasonal tariffs; 1.15% CAGR in electricity sales); achieving reasonable operating conditions.

The targeted outcomes of the ELETROBRAS Distribution Rehabilitation activity are primarily to improve the quality of electricity service provided by Eletrobrás-managed distribution companies ("DisCos") companies to the populations in the States of Acre, Alagoas, Amazonas, Piaui, Rondonia, and the City of Boa Vista. This higher-level objective aims to enhance the quality of life and foster economic growth in these areas. More specifically, the project development objective is to improve the financial, operational performance, and commercial management of the six DisCos by reducing electricity losses, increasing bill collection rates, and enhancing service quality. The project also aims to strengthen the management, operations, and corporate governance of these DisCos through two main components: Service Quality Improvement and Loss Reduction, and Institutional Strengthening. Project success will be measured by a decrease in the number and duration of service interruptions, lower technical and non-technical losses, increased revenue collection achieved through a combination of lowered losses and increased bill collection, and institutional improvements. These institutional improvements include the implementation of performance-based management programs, improved environmental management practices, enhanced monitoring and evaluation supported by sound information systems, and effective social action and communication programs. The activity will be considered an overall success when these key performance indicators demonstrate a measurable improvement in the quality of electricity service, financial and operational performance, and commercial management of the DisCos, ultimately contributing to better quality of life and economic growth for approximately 3 million customers across 118 municipalities.

["The Uganda Grid Expansion and Reinforcement Project (GERP) targets increasing the availability and efficiency of bulk electricity supply in Northern and West Nile regions. Key outcomes include constructing 314 km of new transmission lines, increasing bulk supply capacity by 200 MW, reducing technical losses from 28.9% to 25.6%, and reducing power outages. It aims to boost household electricity access, stimulate income-generating activities for approximately 1.6 million beneficiaries, and support Uganda's economic growth and regional integration by interconnecting isolated grids and strengthening UETCL/MEMD capacity.", "Overall success for GERP requires achieving its Project Development Objective of increased bulk electricity supply availability and efficiency. This entails constructing 314 km of transmission lines, increasing bulk supply capacity by 200 MW, reducing technical losses to 25.6%, reducing power outages, and benefiting targeted populations. Crucially, success also hinges on the timely implementation of the Grid Development Plan to meet surging demand and significantly enhancing UETCL's project implementation and operational capacities, ensuring efficient execution and sustainable management."]

["The Energy Sector Development Project targets enhancing Tuvalu's energy security by reducing dependence on imported fuel and improving electricity system efficiency/sustainability. Key outcomes include contributing to Tuvalu's 100% renewable energy generation target by 2020 and a 30% energy efficiency improvement goal. It aims for a 25% increase in overall RE penetration, delivering an RE fraction target of about 40%, 15,693,382 MJ lifetime fuel savings, 1,724 MWh energy savings, and reducing TEC debtor days from 100 to 10. Benefits extend to 1,500 direct beneficiaries (50% female), improved TEC finances, grid stability, remote monitoring, efficient bill collection, reduced government subsidies, and better service/cost savings for customers.", "Overall success requires achieving enhanced energy security, reduced imported fuel reliance, and an efficient, sustainable electricity system. This means meeting targets: 15,693,382 MJ lifetime fuel savings, 1,724 MWh energy savings, 1,500 beneficiaries (50% female), and reducing TEC debtor days from 100 to 10. Success also includes integrating solar PV/wind with battery storage for grid stability, fully operational remote monitoring and communication systems, strengthened TEC capacity (operations, management, data reliability), reduced government subsidies, and tangible improvements in service reliability and cost savings for all electricity customers, contributing to Tuvalu's 100% RE and 30% EE national goals."]

The targeted outcomes of the "Strengthening Institutional Capacity for Government Effectiveness Project" are to improve fiscal management by strengthening Government institutional capacity to produce timely statistics for policy making, mobilize domestic revenue, improve public expenditure controls, and improve the mining regulatory framework. The ultimate beneficiaries are the citizens of Burundi, who are expected to gain from improvements in the quantity and quality of public services, increased availability of funds for basic social services due to better revenue mobilization (including from mining), greater effectiveness across public administration through improved expenditure management and control, and more strategic decision-making and better targeting of vulnerable groups through improved access to statistical data. The project also aims to create economic benefits such as an improved business environment, increased stability and predictability of tax policy, greater efficiency in revenue mobilization, reduced compliance costs for taxpayers, improved efficiency in managing public spending, and decreased leakages through enhanced audit and oversight.

The targeted outcomes are to improve the financial sustainability of the energy sector, diversify energy and enhance its legal/institutional framework, and strengthen tax collection/public procurement processes. Success requires meeting 2018 targets: reducing cross arrears to CFAF 60 billion, improving SONABEL's EBITDA to above CFAF 40 billion, reducing customer outstanding bills to below CFAF 39 billion, achieving 77 MWp of new solar capacity, increasing interconnected network capacity to 62%, signing 2 IPP projects (with 50% under competitive process), increasing VAT revenues to 7.0% of GDP, non-VAT tax revenues to 10.5% of GDP, and reducing public procurement completion time to 100 days.

The targeted outcomes of the Heat Supply Improvement Project are to improve the efficiency and quality of heating in selected Project areas in the Kyrgyz Republic. This includes modernizing district heating systems in Bishkek (individual heat substations, meters, network sections), strengthening BTS's operational and management functions, piloting efficient and clean heating stoves for ~14,000 low-income households, and demonstrating energy efficiency/seismic improvements in public buildings. The project aims to reduce fuel costs, improve indoor comfort, reduce indoor air pollution for beneficiaries (especially women, children, elderly), lower recurrent energy costs for public buildings, strengthen local service providers, and reduce carbon emissions. To be considered an overall success, the project needs to achieve a projected lifetime fuel savings of 122,126,740.05 Mega Joules (MJ) and demonstrate that a significant percentage of Project beneficiaries report an improvement in the quality of heating.

The restructured activity's Phase I targets are to improve: (i) warnings of high impact weather and hydrological hazards, and (ii) Kelani Basin multi-purpose water infrastructure for climate resilience. The overall program's objective (PrDO) is to increase the number of people and assets protected against flood risk in priority river basins. To be considered an overall success, the activity needs to achieve: 3,500,000 people taking action based on early warnings (from a baseline of 15,000); 622,500 people protected from floods in the Kelani and Mundeni basins (from 0); and US$715,000,000 worth of assets protected from reduced flood risk (from 0).

Targeted outcomes include increasing transmission capacity and improving reliability of Moldova's power transmission system, enhancing security of supply by diversifying energy sources, establishing a competitive electricity market, fostering regional integration with Romania, contributing to climate change mitigation, and improving Moldelectrica's institutional capacity. Overall success requires an increase in transmission capacity along the Vulcanesti-Chisinau corridor to 900,000 kVA and a reduction in interruptions between Vulcanesti SS and Chisinau SS to 1.

The activity targets increasing vulnerable people's access to water services and reducing water losses and pollution loads in the Metropolitan Region of São Paulo. Specific outcomes include providing improved water sources for 532,000 people, reducing non-revenue water by 33.30 million cubic meters annually, and increasing treated wastewater from the Guarapiranga basin by 1.90 million cubic meters annually. Over 2 million people will directly benefit. Overall success requires achieving these targets, ensuring sustainable and resilient water services, improving SABESP's asset management for efficiency and cost reduction, successfully implementing innovative solutions and private sector engagement, and fostering civic engagement.

Targeted outcomes: Foster a conducive private sector environment by reducing business costs, enhancing regulatory predictability, and easing finance/market access. Expand economic opportunities and labor market flexibility, simplifying regulations, and broadening social safety nets. Support fiscal consolidation, promoting public investment efficiency, maximizing private investment, and refining debt management. Overall success relies on the Government of Jordan's sustained commitment to reform, improved governance, and effective implementation to overcome structural challenges.

The activity targets improved landscape management, increased environmental and economic benefits for Eastern Province rural communities, and enhanced capacity for crisis response. Key outcomes include: 66,000 ha under sustainable forest management, 59,000 ha under climate-smart agriculture, 30% crop yield increase, and 40,000 people (30% women) receiving increased benefits. Success hinges on strengthening institutional, technical, and implementation capacity at all levels, building local planning and land tenure capacity, ensuring financial incentives for communities via natural resource management, bolstering the private sector, and supporting decentralization. For Climate-Smart Agriculture, success means organized farmer groups, market access, and value-adding activities.

The activity targets sustaining Uzbekistan's economic reforms and transition to a market economy by increasing the role of markets and the private sector, and enhancing social inclusion. Key results anticipated include increased private-sector led trade, competition, investment, and formal job creation; improved efficiency of safety nets for the most vulnerable; and enhanced voice and accountability through fiscal transparency. Specific targets include: horticultural exports to US$960M; 13,000 wholesale trading businesses; increased banking sector compliance with Basel Core Principles; at least 1 RE project to commercial tender; 1 electricity and 1 cold-water tariff amendment; airport ownership separation and concession process initiated; valuations for Uzbekenergo, Uzbekneftegaz, and 5+ other SOEs; 1.95M male and 1.20M female formal private sector employment; new national educational assessments piloted in 2+ regions; unified social registry utilized by 10 administrative units; and publication of 2019/2020 Citizen Budget documents.

The targeted outcomes of the Malawi Watershed Services Improvement Project (SoP-1) are to increase the adoption of sustainable landscape management practices and improve watershed services in targeted watersheds, and to respond promptly to eligible crises. Specifically, the project aims for: 1) 70% of target farmers adopting sustainable landscape management practices; 2) 95,000 hectares of land under sustainable landscape management practices; 3) 50,000 hectares of land showing an increase in Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) and Land Surface Water Index (LSWI); 4) 42,000 people gaining access to water for productive use; and 5) 50% of target farmers benefiting from increased sales of products or income from marketed products. To consider the activity an overall success, all these end targets must be met. Furthermore, as the initial project in a series, its success is also foundational for achieving the broader 11-year program goals, including strengthening key watershed management institutions, restoring at least 50% of degraded landscapes in priority catchments, reducing sediment yield by at least 30%, and contributing to improved water security, a 70% increase in agricultural productivity, and at least a 15% increase in household incomes.

The activity targets: (1) UMAPS fully operational with transferred functions, governance, and gender policy. (2) 180,417 people receiving at least 12 hours of water daily. (3) 3 WTPs at >=95% capacity. (4) 14 hydraulic sectors meeting non-revenue water (NRW) targets. (5) Reduced staff per thousand connections to 5.00. (6) Approved dam safety/Water Resources Management (WRM) plans. (7) 50% households adopting rational water use. Success requires achieving these targets, increasing water service efficiency/reliability, implementing institutional reforms, and effective environmental/social risk management.

The activity targets improving health and increasing access to water and sewerage services for 500,000 inhabitants in periurban areas. Key outcomes include: improved service continuity from 4.00 to 6.00 hours, increased drinking water connections to 582,163 (from 567,759), and sewerage connections to 363,316 (from 265,212). Health outcomes aim to reduce acute diarrheal disease cases in children under 5 to 92,734 (from 97,615) and increase wastewater treatment plants to 25 (from 15). Additionally, it aims to strengthen service providers, targeting 10 entities with operational efficiency less than or equal to one (from 2), and 10 entities capable of measuring water quality compliance with NB 512 standard (from 2).

The activity targets improving water quality in Educandos igarapé (reducing BOD5, fecal coliforms, COD; increasing OD), eliminating flood-affected population (15,500 to 0), decreasing severe diarrhea (57/10,000 to 21/10,000), and reducing squatter dwellings by 5%. Key outputs include 11,699m drainage, 3,779 properties titled/resettled, 17,376m roads, 24.6Ha parks, 3,929 homes with water, and 8,226 homes with sewer. Success requires achieving 100% of outcomes, leveraging PROSAMIM I's successes and lessons, addressing its prior underachievements, and mitigating legal/economic risks.

Targeted outcomes include establishing an efficient national cadastral and rural property registration system to provide legal certainty, support tax policies, and inform land management. Specific goals are: increasing rural household credit access by 35% and boosting municipios' taxable rural property base by 100%. Success entails covering 100% of the country (236,122 sq km) with 1:5,000 orthophoto maps, increasing properties in cadastres to 800,000, regularizing 170,000 properties, enabling 50 cantons with full cadastral/assessment systems, operationalizing the Geomatics Center, and implementing the National Land Information System across 50 cantons, all supported by comprehensive monitoring.

The activity targets increased land tenure security and improved land management services for rural households in pilot areas. Specific outcomes include: 20% increase by 2016 in agricultural productivity, agricultural investments, and natural resources management. By project end, a 10% increase in land transactions and land value is targeted, alongside a reduction in average parcel registration time from 301 to 66 days and cost from $600 to $150. At least 66,000 parcels will be demarcated/clarified. Success requires meeting these targets, effective inter-agency coordination, a continually updated land database, and demonstrated incremental impact from formalization.

The activity targets improving Kingston's quality of life through reliable water supply, enhancing KSA services, and boosting NWC's efficiency/sustainability. Targeted outcomes: 20% point UFW reduction, 22% NWC energy bill cut, 23% staff reduction (saving 27% employee expenses, targeting 30% of total billing). Success criteria: Sustained 26.36% tariff implementation (from Jan 2004) ensuring NWC self-sustainability for O&M/capital works. Operating revenue to US$136.5M and 29,000 new connections in five years. O&M expenses decrease 2.9% annually to US$88.4M. Enactment of Water/Sewerage Service Act by 2004 (NWC independence, OUR autonomy), universal water access by 2010 are crucial.

The activity targets reducing organic load to 16 tons BOD/day, increasing adequately disposed sewage flow to 3.9 m³/s, boosting sanitation coverage to 90%, and raising the cost coverage index to 95%. Success requires achieving these impact and outcome targets, completing infrastructure like the 2 km underwater outfall and a pretreatment plant, delivering midterm/final evaluation reports, submitting annual performance indicator reports, commissioning an engineering firm for advisory services, and meeting the service sustainability target, all while maintaining satisfactory progress and borrower commitment.

The activity targets enhancing climate resilience for 1.7M direct beneficiaries (50% women) and 10M indirect beneficiaries in India's coastal areas by 2027. Outcomes include protecting/restoring 14,945 hectares of coastal ecosystems (e.g., mangroves, coral reefs), sequestering 122,766 tCO2 eq annually, and strengthening coastal governance to integrate ecosystem-based adaptation into policies. Success requires achieving these beneficiary/ecosystem targets, demonstrating economic returns >20% for livelihoods, and transforming coastal management via integrated climate adaptation and resilient livelihoods nationwide.

The Climate Aggregation Platform (CAP) targets increasing access to low-cost financing for small-scale, low-carbon energy in developing countries. Key outcomes include promoting the scale-up of financial aggregation for these assets; increasing awareness, information exchange, and engagement in financial aggregation activities; achieving financial closure for several aggregation transactions; and enhancing the market architecture and environment for replication and scale-up of financial aggregation transactions in three developing countries.

["The activity targets: Long-term (10-15 years): Stabilize/increase populations of threatened wildlife (lions, cheetahs, wild dogs, leopards) from 732 baseline; secure wildlife migratory corridors; enhance communal land productivity; reduce pressure on protected areas. Mid-term (5-10 years): Decrease poaching (from 6 big cats/year baseline), reduce illegal wildlife trade & retaliatory killing; improve rangeland conditions. Quantifiable (5-8 years): Increase prosecuted poachers/traders to 95% (from 85%), convictions to 85% (from 11%); decrease human-wildlife conflicts by 50% (from 404 cases/year); manage 0.5M ha landscape & 50K ha communal lands under integrated plans; increase benefits for 500 local people (50% women) by 25% via non-wildlife consumption ventures.", "Overall success requires: Reduced poaching (elephants <50% PIKE, big cats/rhinos reverse decline). 95% prosecution & 85% conviction of poachers/traders; maximum sentences applied. Strengthened wildlife crime laws/regulations. Increased law enforcement coordination & intelligence operations. 50% reduction in human-wildlife conflicts. Enhanced community benefits from NRM, 3 ecotourism ventures, 4 supply chains, 25% income increase for 500 locals. Improved rangeland conditions, increased protected area management effectiveness. Secured wildlife corridors, enhanced communal land productivity."]

The activity aims to mainstream biodiversity and ecosystem services conservation into public policies and practices to mitigate current and future threats across productive mountain landscapes. Targeted outcomes include: strengthened institutional capacity and coordination for sustainable landscape management, maintained/increased conservation coverage, reduced soil erosion rates, fostered sustainable livelihoods (e.g., agroforestry, beekeeping, agro-ecotourism), and safeguarded globally important biodiversity and ecosystem services, enabling local populations to benefit from environmental services. Success is contingent on sustained political will, active involvement of key stakeholders (local governments, civil society, private sector), availability of sufficient technical/financial resources, the establishment of three credit mechanisms for sustainable productive activities (one per pilot site) with 50% of producers accessing financing options, and functioning ecotourism pilot units.

The targeted outcomes of the GEF Mgmt Mercury in Artisanal Gold Mining GOLD ISMIA activity are comprehensive, focusing on mercury reduction, institutional and policy strengthening, improved access to finance, community capacity building, and awareness raising, while integrating gender-sensitive approaches. Specifically:

1.  **Mercury Reduction:** A primary outcome is to reduce mercury releases by at least 5 metric tonnes/year starting from the third year, culminating in a total reduction of at least 15 metric tonnes over the project's lifespan across 6 Artisanal and Small-scale Gold Mining (ASGM) communities in Indonesia.
2.  **Institutional and Policy Strengthening:**
    *   National and local institutions, government agencies, private sector partners, NGOs, and CBOs will have advanced capacity to assess, plan, support, implement, and monitor sustainable and mercury-free interventions.
    *   Capacity will be increased for 23 government entities in national systems to assess, plan, and implement sustainable and mercury-free interventions in the ASGM sector.
    *   15 policies, regulations, and standards will be revised and/or developed to improve the enabling environment for ASGM and mercury phase-out.
    *   The formalization process (local concessions, environmental licenses) will be simplified, clarified, and made more affordable for well-functioning ASGM cooperatives.
    *   Specific policies and regulations, including a BUMDes business policy on mercury-free ASGM, draft village/district/province regulations on mercury-free ASGM/economic activities, and a Ministerial Agreement for harmonizing relevant laws, will be developed.
    *   Approximately 340 government staff members will receive training, including gender sensitization, to enhance their ability to coach communities, monitor ASGM activities, improve enforcement, support regulatory reform, enhance cross-sectoral coordination, and integrate gender dimensions.
3.  **Access to Finance:**
    *   Mining communities will have increased access to finance for mercury-free processing technologies.
    *   2 new or improved financial products/mechanisms (including women-friendly ones) will be established for the ASGM sector.
    *   Loans for the purchase of mercury-free processing equipment/investments will be accessible to legalized ASGM miners and cooperatives.
    *   10 ASGM groups (with 20% women miners) will be capacitated to apply for loans for mercury-free processing equipment/investments, leading to the development of 10 loan applications with project support, with 50% approved.
    *   120 miners and managers of mining groups (BUMDes and/or Cooperatives), including 20% women, will be trained on record keeping and reporting.
4.  **Community Capacity Building and Technology Transfer:**
    *   The capacity of mining communities for mercury-free ASGM will be increased through technical assistance, technology transfer, and formalization support.
    *   A total of 1,200 miners will be trained on mercury-free mining practices and the sound management of mining tailings at existing and newly established ore processing plants and laboratory installations.
    *   30 trainers (from project partners, mining communities, and training centers) will be trained in the use of training resources and equipment for mercury-free mining.
    *   At least one (1) mercury-free ore processing training plant will be established in a project location.
    *   150 kg of gold will be produced per year without mercury through the introduction of Best Environmental Practices (BEP), Best Available Technologies (BAT), and socially and environmentally sound ASGM practices.
5.  **Awareness Raising and Dissemination:**
    *   Awareness will be raised and best practices and lessons learned on mercury phase-out in the ASGM sector will be disseminated.
    *   The Ministry of Health will successfully facilitate awareness programs about mercury use risks.
    *   The Ministry of Communication and Information Technology will develop and implement effective awareness-raising campaigns leveraging wide-spread media.
6.  **Gender Mainstreaming:** Equitable economic empowerment will be advanced, health and safety risks for women will be reduced, and women's groups will be supported through strategic partnerships and gender-sensitive approaches.

To consider the activity an overall success, all these targeted outcomes must be achieved. Specifically, the project must demonstrate:

*   A verifiable reduction of at least 15 metric tonnes of mercury over its lifespan, with a sustained yearly reduction of 5 metric tonnes from the third year onwards.
*   Tangible improvement in the capacity of national and local institutions, particularly the 23 government entities, to effectively implement mercury-free ASGM interventions.
*   The successful revision and/or development of 15 policies, regulations, and standards that create a simpler, clearer, and more affordable formalization process for ASGM cooperatives.
*   Measurable increase in mining communities' access to and utilization of finance for mercury-free technologies, evidenced by the establishment of new financial products, successful loan applications, and increased participation of women miners.
*   The successful training of 1,200 miners and 30 trainers, leading to widespread adoption of mercury-free mining practices and the establishment of functional mercury-free processing training plants, yielding 150 kg of mercury-free gold annually.
*   Effective awareness programs facilitated by the Ministry of Health and widespread campaigns by the Ministry of Communication and Information Technology.
*   The Ministry of Trade successfully issuing and enforcing regulations for mercury distribution.
*   The Ministry of Cooperative and SMEs gathering comprehensive socio-economic data from pilot sites to confirm strengthened community cooperatives and downstream business development.
*   The Ministry of Villages, Less Developed Regions and Transmigration successfully mainstreaming village development concepts and legalizing miners.
*   Demonstrable progress in advancing equitable economic empowerment, reducing health and safety risks, and effectively supporting women's groups through gender-sensitive approaches throughout the project.

Targeted outcomes include improved and integrated biodiversity management providing sustainable livelihoods, demonstrated across Qinling Mountains, leading to sustainable biodiversity conservation and economic growth. Success entails: by 2020, 15% reduction in land degradation/soil erosion in QBCDA, 15% increase in natural habitat/species recovery in all Qinling nature reserves, 15% increase in rural incomes, and halved rural poverty. By 2015, five globally-threatened species protected, increasing endangered/threatened species numbers, ecotourism visitors reaching 800,000 (1.2M by 2020), 1,200 new jobs, a long-term plan developed by 2013, and 7,000 ha of forest managed.

["The primary targeted outcome is increased adequacy and sustainability of Indonesia's energy supply. By 2027, this means generating an additional 873 gigawatt-hours of geothermal electricity annually and avoiding at least 705,699 tons of CO2 equivalent annually. Specific outputs by 2025 include commissioning two 55 MW geothermal electricity generating plants and their transmission connections at Dieng and Patuha. Additionally, institutional capacity of PT Geo Dipa Energi (GDE) will be strengthened, evidenced by 10 staff (20% women) with increased reservoir knowledge, 400 staff training days (30% women) in project/contract management, and an expanded, accredited EMS (ISO 14001:2015, SMK3 PP 50/2012) covering new facilities with gender, climate, and community safety considerations.", "The activity will be an overall success upon achieving all targeted outcomes and outputs. This includes the full commissioning of the 110 MW geothermal capacity at Dieng and Patuha by 2025. Crucially, the project must meet its 2027 targets of generating an additional 873 GWh of geothermal electricity annually and avoiding 705,699 tons of CO2 equivalent annually. Successful institutional strengthening of GDE, including increased staff knowledge, completion of dedicated training days, and an expanded, accredited EMS integrating gender, climate, and community safety, is also essential. Sustained government commitment to geothermal power generation and overcoming technical drilling/reservoir challenges are also key."]

The activity targets: 1) 33,000 households with continuous potable water (24-hour, norm-compliant pressure); 2) 90% of water samples meeting quality standards; 3) Improved IDAAN management, with 64% metered consumption, 42% non-revenue water, 5% service interruption in San Andrés/San Francisco, and 85% collection efficiency; 4) 2,200 new sewerage connections. Success requires achieving all these EOP targets, overcoming initial disbursement delays, by the planned end date.

["The activity targets: 3,092 ktonnes CO2 lifetime emission reductions; 640,499 MWh annual reduced electricity consumption; 5% of SMEs with firm plans for EE motor procurement; 5,000 inefficient motors recycled; 1 national motor survey completed; 2 harmonized policies/standards; 50 DGSIIP personnel trained; 250 motors tested annually at TSI; 2,408 motor energy efficiency investment plans (MEEIPs); and $47.92 million in cumulative USD investments through the one-stop shop FSM.", "Overall success requires achieving all quantitative targets. It also necessitates improved energy efficiencies of motors in industrial SMEs, sustained government support for EE motors aligned with Turkey's INDC plan, adoption of upcoming EU eco-design measures, increased availability of information on best international practices for energy efficiency, and increased awareness among end-users and policymakers. Verification relies on project reports, surveys of energy savings, and OIZ energy management unit reports."]

["The activity aims to reduce risks of POPs on human health/environment by strengthening institutional capacity, policy, and regulatory framework for environmentally sound chemicals management. This involves enhancing these frameworks, capacitating public/private institutions, and designing systems for sound collection, labeling, storage, transport, and disposal of hazardous chemicals/waste. Specific targets include identifying, labeling, soundly managing, and exporting 24 metric tons of PCB-containing equipment/wastes, and preventing approx. 15 g-TEQ PCDD/F releases.", "Overall success requires: 1) Enhanced policy/regulatory frameworks & capacitated institutions (e.g., 4 regulatory documents, 1 national guideline, 3 regional guidelines; capacity in 10+ government/private entities). 2) PCB elimination by 2025, destruction by 2028, with 24 metric tons of PCBs disposed. 3) UPOPs reduction: preventing 15 g-TEQ PCDD/F release. 4) Awareness: 5,000 people informed. 5) Employment: 224 jobs created (20% female). 6) Functional harmonized CCMS/PRTR established. 7) Gender Action Plan target achieved."]

["The activity targets developing an efficient Southern African regional power market. Core objectives: decelerate SADC electricity price increases via competition, enhance industrial competitiveness, foster accelerated private investment in the power sector, and promote regional integration. Key outcomes: increased low-cost, environmentally friendly DRC electricity exports to SAPP, improved Short-Term Energy Market (STEM) operations/volume, equal market information access, integrated infrastructure services along new transmission lines, and enhanced grid reliability. It also aims to strengthen the Coordination Center, remove transmission bottlenecks, connect non-operating members, and establish a regulatory framework for open access/private participation.", "Overall activity success hinges on achieving clear, well-defined objectives and measurable KPIs. It requires strong strategic alignment, the Bank's substantial financial/advisory role leveraging private investment, and directly addressing critical sector issues like transmission bottlenecks and regulatory gaps. Realization of anticipated benefits, including substantial economic savings, enhanced system reliability, and increased competition, is crucial. Diligent management is vital to overcome challenges (transmission limitations, inadequate communication). Pivotal for long-term sustainability are the successful transition of SAPP from a cooperative to a tighter, competitive pool, and the STEM market's evolution to ensure fixed cost recovery for new generation."]

The targeted outcomes of the CM - Lom Pangar Hydropower Project are to increase hydropower generation capacity, reduce seasonal variability of water flow in the Sanaga River, and increase access to electricity. Specifically, the project aims to add 120 MW to existing downstream hydropower plants (Edea and Song Loulou), generate 30 MW from the Lom Pangar project itself, electrify approximately 2,400 new households, improve electricity services for 22,000 existing consumers in the Eastern Grid, and replace 12 MW of expensive thermal capacity.

The activity aims to increase sustainable, affordable electricity and modern energy access in peri-urban/rural areas, alleviating poverty. Key outcomes: increased grid-based access (25,000 new customers, network rehab); boosted off-grid access via RETs (500 solar PV for schools/clinics, 30 villages electrified, 50,000 improved stoves, 1,250 charcoal kilns, 70 multifunctional platforms); improved institutional performance (ME, EdM, FUNAE, CNELEC); a national Rural Electrification Strategy (RESIP); and mainstreamed Sector Wide Approach (SWAp). Success requires: increased household/community connections, higher customer satisfaction, completion of CNELEC trigger, effective use of IDA ($80M) and parallel financing (>$120M), and project remaining low-risk and on track.

Targeted outcomes include increasing income-generating opportunities (creating 3,300 new jobs from MSEs, 2,205 new jobs from creative industries/tourism facilities), improving access to infrastructure and services (benefiting 4,676,098 people through urban-rural regeneration, improving sanitation for 8,211 households, rehabilitating 66.22 km roads), and strengthening institutional capacity. Success requires achieving these: USD 100M co-financing from PFIs, 3 PFIs completing institutional development plans, 6 culture/tourism strategies adopted/budgeted, and 80% beneficiary satisfaction.

["The targeted outcomes are increased renewable energy penetration and electricity access in Vanuatu's off-grid areas. Specifically, the project aims to give an additional 20% of households electricity access, serve ~37 public institutions and ~8,400 rural households (~42,000 people) via SHS/micro grids, and ~550 rural households (~2,750 people) and public institutions/businesses via mini grids. Overall success requires achieving these specific targets and contributing to Vanuatu's 2030 goals of 100% electricity access and 100% renewable energy.", "The targeted outcomes are increased renewable energy penetration and electricity access in Vanuatu's off-grid areas. Specifically, the project aims to give an additional 20% of households electricity access, serve ~37 public institutions and ~8,400 rural households (~42,000 people) via SHS/micro grids, and ~550 rural households (~2,750 people) and public institutions/businesses via mini grids. Overall success requires achieving these specific targets and contributing to Vanuatu's 2030 goals of 100% electricity access and 100% renewable energy."]

["Targeted outcomes include serving 1.36M people with new/improved electricity, connecting 250k households, 400 enterprises, and 3.8k km of distribution lines. It also targets 4k mini-grid customers and 18k off-grid RBF households. Aims to reduce connection costs and eliminate the MZN 3,500 upfront charge. Supports Mozambique's universal electricity access by 2030, WBG's CPF, SDG7, and Gender Strategy (EDM 40% Gender Index, 30% women in programs). Promotes private sector participation and citizen engagement for safe use and feedback.", "Overall success requires providing new or improved electricity services to 1,360,000 people, specifically connecting 250,000 households and 400 enterprises to the grid. Success also means reducing connection costs and eliminating the MZN 3,500 upfront charge. For off-grid, reaching ~4,000 mini-grid customers and ~18,000 RBF households, while fostering private sector participation, is key. EDM must achieve a 40% Gender Index by 2030 and 30% women in young professional programs. Effective citizen engagement is essential. Ultimately, success aligns with Mozambique's national development goals, WBG's CPF, SDG7, and Gender Strategy."]

The activity targets: efficient government resource mobilization/allocation, reduced private sector development barriers, and protection/inclusion of vulnerable populations. Key outcomes include: - Budget difference: 10% (2021) - Gov spending: 13.8% of GDP (2021) - Energy subsidies: 37.1% of oil revenues (2021) - Tax revenues: 14.9% of GDP (2021) - Int'l arbitration contracts: >=3 (2021) - New companies: 10800 (2021) - Days to import: 4.3 (2021) - Financial sector efficiency: >0.2 co-movement, 17% public bank financing (2021) - Social compensation: 80% bottom 40% (2021) - Debit card transactions: 57M (2021) - Labor reform regulatory framework: Approved (2021) Overall success requires effective, sustained implementation of these reforms by the Government of Ecuador.

The Gazetted Forests Management Project in Benin targets several key outcomes: (1) To improve the integrated management of targeted Gazetted Forests (GFs), specifically involving the adoption and implementation of agroforestry schemes, taungya methods, and agricultural intensification techniques. (2) To increase access for the main consumption cities (Cotonou, Abomey-Calavi, and Porto-Novo) to sustainably produced fuelwood. (3) To strengthen selected non-timber forest product (NTFP) value chains for forest-dependent communities. (4) To contribute to addressing Benin's climate-related threats and vulnerabilities, supporting its Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) by aiming for a 21.4 percent reduction in emissions and an unconditional 3.5 percent reduction, particularly by addressing land use change. This includes a target reduction of emissions by 110 MtCO2e compared to the Business as Usual scenario. (5) To support large-scale plantations, regeneration of degraded lands, protection of conservation areas, and sustainable extraction of forest resources. (6) To enhance the operational capacity of the DGEFC and CENATEL to establish a more robust forest governance framework. (7) To enhance farm productivity, generate income for farmers, reduce forest encroachment, and maintain existing forests through improved conservation management and sustainable resource extraction, ultimately leading to improved livelihoods and increased rural income. Overall, the project aims to reduce GFs deforestation and degradation, ensure sustainable fuelwood supply, reduce climate variability, improve biodiversity protection, improve livelihoods, and increase rural income.To consider the activity an overall success, the project must achieve the following based on its PDO-level indicators: (i) Increase the area of Gazetted Forests under sustainable management based on defined criteria (which include agroforestry, taungya, and agricultural intensification techniques). (ii) Reduce net greenhouse gas emissions (metric tons). (iii) Increase the standing volume of wood energy plantations supported by the project (cubic meters), specifically those supplying the three major energy consumption hubs. (iv) Increase the number of communities adjacent to targeted GFs with increased access to income sources through NTFP value chains, covering both monetary and non-monetary benefits like capacity building or improved market access. (v) Achieve a satisfactory percentage of beneficiaries, disaggregated by gender and age, in terms of their satisfaction with project interventions, focusing on GF co-management associations, farmers benefiting from agroforestry and intensification techniques, and performance-based incentive payments.

Targeted outcomes: Improved management of high Himalayan landscapes for snow leopard conservation, habitat protection, and ecosystem services. Diversified sustainable livelihoods for communities to reduce ecosystem pressure. Enhanced enforcement, monitoring, and trans-boundary cooperation to reduce wildlife crime. Improved knowledge and replication of conservation approaches. Success: Stable/increasing snow leopard populations, stable alpine meadow area/productivity, and stable sub-alpine forest area, ensuring sustainable livelihoods and community socio-economic benefits.

The activity targets strengthening the Ministry of Environment's management for comprehensive environmental policies and empowering municipalities. Specific outcomes include improved urban solid waste management, enhanced environmental education, stronger protected areas, and developed climate change policies. Success requires: 50 municipalities implementing environmental promotion, 40 open-air dumps eradicated, a climate change cabinet implemented, 10 protected areas restored, 100 forest management plans approved, a Jurisdictional Environmental Education Strategy produced, and sustainability improvements in 90 MiPyMEs.

["The activity targets the transformation of China's national protected area system in Gansu through legal and institutional reform for biodiversity conservation. Key outcomes include increasing forest growing stock to over 262 Mm³ and 12.58% cover; improving legal frameworks for PAs, KBAs, and GT species; strengthening the West Qinling-Minshan Mountains PA & Ecological Corridor Network by reducing habitat loss and HWC; enhancing biodiversity monitoring and knowledge management; stabilizing and improving populations of indicator species (e.g., Giant panda); and fostering engaged, resilient communities with diversified livelihoods.", "Overall success requires the expansion of the PA and Ecological Corridor Network by over 25,000 ha, including specific areas like Yuhe NR and Hezheng County. A 30% reduction in the PA system financing gap (at least USD 57,218,000) and a >30% increase in annual PA financing are essential. Success also hinges on approving three biodiversity laws, updating the Gansu Biodiversity Strategy, integrating conservation into the Provincial Five-Year Plan, implementing community co-management agreements, improving PA governance, stabilizing/improving GT species populations, and reducing habitat degradation and HWC."]
