The targeted outcomes of the activity, "Anpassung an den Klimawandel im Wassersektor in der MENA Region," were to improve the capacities of regional and national water sector institutions to develop and implement strategies for adaptation to climate change. This involved supporting policy processes at the regional level and improving strategic resource management capacities at the national level for selected water ministries. The project also aimed to implement pilot adaptation projects that directly involve the broader population affected by reduced water availability, with a specific emphasis on women benefiting from these measures. Four main outputs were targeted: Policy and strategy, Institutional framework, Tools and methodology, and Technologies and resource management systems. The activity also intended to strengthen existing structures of the Arab Ministerial Water Council (AMWC) and the League of Arab States (LAS), including regional organizations like ACSAD, and to support NGOs in capacity building for climate mainstreaming, proofing, finance, awareness raising, and climate change capacity development. Furthermore, the activity aimed to contribute to the implementation of the "Arab Strategy for Water Security," focusing on improving water services, protecting common water rights, fostering cooperation, and enhancing institutional development, capacity building, research, awareness, and participation. Developing regional policy guidelines for the Water-Energy-Food Nexus was another targeted outcome. Success, as defined at the beginning of the activity, would necessitate the achievement of these targeted outcomes. Specifically, it would require demonstrably improved capacities of regional and national water sector institutions to effectively develop and implement climate change adaptation strategies, the tangible delivery of the four main outputs, the successful benefit of pilot adaptation measures for the target population, the strengthening of regional and national institutions, significant progress in implementing the "Arab Strategy for Water Security" and its goals, the development and adoption of WEF-Nexus policy guidelines, and enhanced skills among direct target groups in climate change adaptation.

The activity aimed to contribute to a more energy-efficient operation of Power Plant IV, leading to a more economically sustainable and emission-reduced energy and heat supply, enhanced supply security, and global climate protection. Success was defined by achieving a reduction in specific coal consumption of 9.1 kg/MWh for electricity production and 1.91 kg/GJ for heat production. Additionally, targeted reductions in specific CO2 emissions were 37.9 kg/MWh for electricity and 7.96 kg/GJ for heat production. These efficiency gains were projected to be equivalent to an additional 11.7 MW of power plant capacity.

The targeted outcome of the activity is to improve the coordination and cooperation among relevant parties in the sustainable management of natural resources and value chains in the Trifinio region, guided by the Trinational Commission of Plan Trifinio (CTPT). Success entails strengthening CTPT's institutional capacities, enhancing local stakeholder networks, consolidating and disseminating climate-adapted resource management models, and promoting associated value chains. Specifically, success also requires the CTPT to organize 2 donor coordination rounds annually for evaluating interventions.

Targeted outcomes: Rapid response to disaster events (e.g., supporting ~132,000 people with relief/recovery); 7 months of food aid for 33,000 Rohingya (including targeted nutrition for 10,000 vulnerable women/children and school feeding for 12,000 children); strengthening institutional preparedness, planning, and logistical support (robust assessment capacity); building an evidence base for effective responses. Success requires consistent engagement, proven reach, adaptability, enhanced systemic resilience, preventing WFP food pipeline breaks, addressing malnutrition, foresight, and effective rapid deployment.

The activity aims for sustainable, inclusive economic development, fostering greener economies, generating high-quality jobs, and bridging the infrastructure finance gap while meeting SDGs. It seeks to expand UK infrastructure and cities programming across Asia and Africa by providing technical assistance to FCDO Posts and supporting partner governments in developing 'bankable' infrastructure projects to attract finance from BII, PIDG, and UKEF. Specific outcomes include increased access to infrastructure services, more bankable projects, more people adapting to climate shocks, increased employment from infrastructure construction, and a higher volume of installed renewable energy.

The eco.business Fund aims to promote business and consumption practices that contribute to biodiversity conservation, sustainable resource use, and climate change mitigation/adaptation. Targeted outcomes include enhanced integration of sustainability in financial institution lending, increased application of sustainable business practices (e.g., agroforestry, water-saving irrigation, organic fertilizers), and fostering an enabling environment for a greener economy (awareness, collaboration, capital availability). Success is measured by KPIs like hectares of agroforestry, biodiversity scores, water saved, CO2 stored, and jobs supported, ensuring investments meet both financial return and development impact requirements.

Targeted outcomes include greenhouse gas reductions, enhanced climate resilience, and reduced unemployment disparities between coal provinces and the rest of South Africa. By 2027, key outcome targets are: improving project preparation capacity in at least 4 municipalities; mobilizing $200m in climate finance (plus $500m in private finance); creating at least 2000 direct/indirect jobs (30% for women), with circa 5000 jobs including SME investments; and enhancing infrastructure/services for 20,000 people in coal mining areas. Outputs include identifying at least 10 JETP projects (4 to financial close) and creating UK business opportunities. Overall success is achieved by meeting all these targets.

The targeted outcomes for the Integrated Water and Sanitation Programme for the Placencia Peninsula are twofold: first, the operationalization of a wastewater treatment system in Placencia, and second, the strengthening of Belize Water Services Limited (BWSL). To consider the activity an overall success, it must achieve the following:For the operationalization of the wastewater treatment system, water quality must improve, with BOD5 reduced from 190.00 mg/L to 30.00 mg/L and TSS reduced from 225.00 mg/L to 30.00 mg/L by the end of the project. An annual volume of 600,000.00 m3 of wastewater must be treated, 750 households need to be connected with new or upgraded sanitary connections, and 7,000 visitors must be served via the sewerage network.For the strengthening of Belize Water Services Limited, the activity must achieve a 1.00 score in benchmarking best practices for coastal water and wastewater systems in transition. Additionally, a 90% Placencia Collection Efficiency must be met in line with BWSL standards, 90% of the population must properly use the sanitation infrastructure, and customer satisfaction should reach 75% by the end of the project.

The activity targets sustainably consolidated tourism in Costa Rica's state-protected areas. Outcomes include increasing tourism revenues by 20%, growing MSMEs in the sector by 20 units, and raising annual visitation to 1,222,977. Success requires achieving these targets, strengthening SINAC's financial sustainability and institutions, and augmenting local socioeconomic/environmental benefits, thus contributing to sustainable management and conservation. This ensures comprehensive program achievement.

The activity's targeted outcomes include a sustainable increase in agricultural income for beneficiary rural households, expanded irrigated agricultural area, improved water use and distribution efficiency, and enhanced climate change adaptation. Overall success requires: 1) Gross production margin per hectare reaching US$1,554.00 from US$526.00. 2) Technified irrigated area increasing from 9,440 ha to 15,843 ha; optimal area per irrigator group from 20 ha to 133 ha; traditional area from 294,481 ha to 299,487 ha. 3) Value of production per hectare increasing from US$1,322.00 to US$2,857.00; average beneficiary productivity up 29%; water use efficiency in technified systems from 25% to 75%, and conventional systems to 50%. 4) 7 governorates using SNIR for planning, and a fully functional SNIR website.

Phase I targets constructing two new Wastewater Treatment Plants (WWTPs) in San Fernando and Malabar, providing improved sewerage connections for 16,626 households, ensuring 100% population coverage by WWTPs in these catchments, and treating 90% of wastewater to meet Water Pollution Rules with positive/zero downstream impact. It also aims for WASA's institutional strengthening: 720 hours/month preventative maintenance, operation of 2 new WWTPs to design standards, implementation of new corporate governance policies, and improved commercial management. 50 women will be trained in water quality monitoring. Furthermore, 400,000 households are targeted for new sewerage network connections in pilot areas, and 17,026 households with treated wastewater.

["The Joint Programme on Charcoal (PROSCAL) targets reducing unsustainable charcoal production, trade, and use, promoting energy security, and resilient livelihoods in Somalia. Outcomes include: strengthening government capacity and legal frameworks to enforce the charcoal trade ban; promoting alternative energy sources; providing sustainable alternative livelihoods for charcoal value chain beneficiaries; and initiating country-wide reforestation. Broader targets are revitalizing the Somali economy, enhancing livelihoods, generating employment, and fostering sustainable natural resource management.", "Overall success would be marked by: an agreed and enforced Regional Policy Framework for charcoal regulation; establishment of a Charcoal Reduction Fund; widespread adoption of energy-efficient/renewable technologies and an established LPG market; creation of captive plantations; and diversification of income for charcoal value chain beneficiaries. Further indicators of success include reduced poverty, halted environmental degradation, improved energy security, enhanced resilience, social equity, reduced conflict, and fostered peace."]

The targeted outcomes of the activity are to improve the quality of water and sanitation services in communities with 5,000-500,000 inhabitants. This involves rehabilitating/expending water purification plants (WPPs) and laboratories, and enhancing water service management. Specific targets by project end include: 18 WPPs operating per technical specs; increased household drinking water access in San Fernando de Apure (23,828), San Juan de los Morros (29,651), Valle de la Pascua (24,795), and Puerto Cabello (38,331); 100% service continuity in target cities; 81% of laboratories operating per protocols; and improved per capita water consumption in San Fernando de Apure (241 L/p/d), San Juan de los Morros (189 L/p/d), Valle de La Pascua (189 L/p/d), and Puerto Cabello (126 L/p/d). The activity will be considered an overall success upon achieving all these measurable targets.

The activity targets strengthening local disaster risk reduction (DRR) capacities and their integration into DRR Plans, including fortifying CGRR, PATs, and local hydrometeorological/ANM Early Warning Systems. It aims to enhance coastal settlement resilience via climate change adaptation strategies, rehabilitating wetlands, demonstrating nature-based solutions, establishing CCC-GCA centers, and implementing environmental monitoring. Finally, it seeks to integrate DRR and ACC into local development planning, developing municipal Environmental Management Models and territorial plans with an integrated DRR/ACC approach.

Targeted outcomes: 1) Enhance UNCCD biophysical indicators, data quality, management, and monitoring via stakeholder identification, training, equipment, status reports, and a comprehensive database. 2) Successfully prepare and submit 2018 national reports, including committee formation, workshops, training on PRAIS 3 Portal, report drafting, validation, and submission. 3) Support HCEFLCD in implementing 5th national report recommendations. Success requires achieving strategic objectives: improved living conditions (e.g., poverty line), enhanced ecosystems (e.g., vegetation cover), general UNCCD benefits, and resource mobilization. It also demands effective implementation of institutional, income-generating, knowledge, and direct anti-desertification actions, robust data management, and commitment to recommendations.

{"outcomes": "The activity targets fostering climate and disaster resilient communities and ecosystems, promoting sustainable consumption, ensuring access to clean energy and natural resources to reduce marginalization, poverty, and inequality. By 2022, environmental management will be strengthened, and communities will have increased access to clean energy. By 2027, various actors will take informed actions on climate change and environmental issues. Specific outcomes include implementing gender-responsive disaster risk reduction and climate change plans; restoring 26 million hectares of degraded land by 2030; and 1.5 million people (50% women) directly benefiting from environmental protection and sustainable resource use by 2027. Annual targets for 2023 include developing 8 gender-responsive training modules and training 6000 people (at least 50% women), piloting 20 gender-responsive livelihood plans, and developing 12 A/V products and case studies.", "success_criteria": "The activity would be considered an overall success by achieving its overarching vision of resilient communities and ecosystems. This entails meeting all UNSDF/CPD Outcome and Output targets, including strengthening environmental management, increasing access to clean energy, implementing gender-responsive DRR/climate change plans, restoring degraded land, and ensuring 1.5 million beneficiaries (50% women) by 2027. Success also requires meeting annual targets such as developing 8 gender-responsive training modules and training 6000 people (50% women), piloting 20 gender-responsive livelihood plans, and creating 12 A/V products. Consistent integration of gender equality throughout all initiatives and effective financial management are also crucial."}

The targeted outcome is a complete 100% phase-out of HCFCs by 2030, aligning with Sri Lanka's Montreal Protocol obligations. This will be achieved through strengthened policies and regulations for HCFC consumption and alternative refrigerant safety, comprehensive technician training, improved training facilities, and enhanced monitoring and evaluation. Intermediate outcomes include strengthened policies and enforcement, improved capacity of RAC Training Centers and workshops, enhanced HCFC reclamation, and strengthened project management and coordination. Success will be measured by achieving the 100% HCFC phase-out target by 2030, a 67.5% reduction by 2025, a 100% reduction of ODS, and effectively addressing institutional capacity gaps, policy coordination, and public awareness, while promoting gender equality in the RAC sector.

Targeted outcomes aim for an effective, equitable, human rights-focused COVID-19 response and recovery, safeguarding SDG progress. Output 1: 80% universal health coverage, access to quality health services, essential medicines/vaccines, and waste management. Output 2: Strengthened national/sub-national crisis management (e.g., 6 communities covered by DCS, 30 actors trained); safeguarded human rights (e.g., 5 legal aid clinics, 150 people receiving aid, 40% women/girls); community engagement (e.g., 1,200 reached in border areas, 150 small grants). Output 3: Address socio-economic impacts, support vulnerable workers/SMEs.

["The primary targeted outcome is the complete phase-out of Annex C, Group 1 Hydro-chlorofluorocarbons (HCFCs) in Sri Lanka by 2030, which is ten years ahead of an earlier target. This includes an intermediate target of a 67.5% reduction of baseline HCFC consumption by 2025, building upon a 35% reduction achieved in 2020. Key outputs to achieve this include strengthening policy, regulations, and enforcement; capacity building for the Refrigeration and Air Conditioning (RAC) servicing sector; public awareness and outreach; robust project management, coordination, and monitoring; and integrating a gender action plan to ensure equal opportunities and women's participation. Social and environmental safeguards for the transition to alternative refrigerants are also targeted.", "Overall success will be measured by achieving the 67.5% HCFC reduction target by 2025 and the complete 100% phase-out by 2030. It also entails the successful implementation of all planned project activities, including effective strengthening of policy, regulations, and enforcement; enhanced capacity in the RAC servicing sector; increased public awareness; robust project management; and successful gender integration with women's participation. Key indicators for success include strong political commitment, sustained support from UNDP, full funding, effective inter-agency coordination, and the NOU's demonstrated capacity for execution, along with proper consideration of social and environmental safeguards."]

The targeted outcomes are institutional strengthening for climate disclosure, carbon pricing, and taxation mechanisms in Mauritius, leading to integrated, gender-sensitive systems by 2028 for climate change adaptation/disaster risk reduction, with enhanced public/private capacities for mitigation/adaptation. This includes formulating a framework for climate disclosure (national green taxonomy, reporting guidelines per Climate Change Act) and developing long-term climate strategies with carbon pricing to phase out fossil fuel subsidies. Success hinges on transitioning Mauritius from ad-hoc to sustainable, integrated systems for climate reporting/finance, overcoming Green/Blue Carbon/disclosure challenges, and achieving full implementation of the Paris Agreement ETF/national Climate Change Act.

The activity targets improved access to basic services (water, sanitation, stormwater drainage, solid waste management) and enhanced public safety (reduced crime, improved perceptions) in targeted poor inner-city communities. It also aims to create employment for low-income groups through labor-intensive infrastructure work and strengthen community capacity for infrastructure management. Success requires significantly increased service coverage, reduced crime rates, new employment for low-income groups, community ownership through a 20% contribution and O&M agreements, and enhanced investment sustainability via community capacity building.

The activity targets supporting 10 countries in sustainable, clean irrigation to enable decent livelihoods, improved living standards, and nature-positive impacts. It aims to demonstrate and test innovative, replicable solar water pump (SWP) deployment models, increasing agricultural productivity, incomes, and building climate resilience. Outcomes include promoting efficient water/energy use, fostering knowledge exchange, developing national capacities, addressing poverty/gender inequalities, and extending solar energy to other decentralized applications. Specific targets: enhanced energy access for vulnerable communities, demonstrating 4 new SWP models, and significantly benefiting women.

["The activity aims to establish a sustainable program expanding rural electricity access, supporting economic development and poverty alleviation. Key outcomes: connect ~400 rural towns/villages (~1M inhabitants) to EEPCo grid, upgrade 17 grid substations, extend distribution networks, connect ~250,000 new household/commercial/industrial customers over six years, install efficient public street lighting, and distribute 200,000 CFLs. Capacity building will improve UEAP Office effectiveness, incorporate lessons, maximize benefits for the poor, strengthen planning, and develop income-generating opportunities.", "Overall success is measured by: number/population of electrified rural towns/villages, electricity consumption levels, number of connected households/commercial/industrial customers, percentage of households using electricity beyond lighting, EEPCo revenues from these towns, and number of CFLs distributed. Success also requires credit effectiveness conditions: GoE/EEPCo subsidiary agreement for IDA credit injection to EEPCo for UEAP financing; and appointment of M&E coordinator/accountant plus establishment of an M&E unit within UEAP Office under satisfactory terms."]

The activity targets renewing and enhancing existing infrastructure assets in selected medium-sized cities during economic transition. Specific urban environment outcomes include reducing water pollution in the Liao River Basin and Bohai Sea, increasing water use efficiency, improving solid waste management, and enhancing utility operational/financial performance. Urban transport outcomes aim to relieve bottlenecks, improve network quality, and build capacity for sustainable planning. Additionally, it targets improving municipal financial management, utility regulation, and developing public-private partnership models. Overall success hinges on effective implementation of institutional reforms, capacity building, regulatory regimes, incorporating lessons from previous projects, leveraging the National Road Safety Law, promoting PPPs, and careful management of environmental/resettlement impacts.

["The primary targeted outcomes are to increase the capacity of the Ministry of Environment, Nature Conservation and Tourism (MECNT) and the Congolese Nature Conservation Institute (ICCN), and to foster increased collaboration among government institutions, civil society, and other stakeholders for the sustainable and equitable management of forests for multiple uses in Pilot Provinces. It also aims to enable public institutions and civil society to implement, enforce, and monitor the sector governance reforms and innovations of the new Forest Code.", "Overall success requires MECNT and ICCN to fulfill core regulatory functions like controlling logging, enforcing management plans, and rehabilitating national parks. It also relies on local communities gaining capacity and instruments to engage in decision-making, management, and benefit-sharing mechanisms of the new forest code, ensuring indigenous populations have equal access and avoiding marginalization. Crucially, civil society must effectively engage in dialogue with government and partnerships for monitoring the new code, illegal logging, and forest revenue use."]

The activity targets increasing modern energy access in rural areas, improving sector planning/management, and electricity supply reliability. Key outcomes include setting up a Rural Energy Fund, enhancing legal/regulatory frameworks, strengthening sector institutions (MINEE, ARSEL, AER, EDC) capacity, and improving energy project preparation (including LPHP). Success requires increasing electrified villages (grid/off-grid), adopting a long-term least-cost sector development plan, AES Sonel's full compliance with at least two concession obligations, and LPHP preparatory activities adhering to international technical, environmental, and social standards per World Bank safeguard policies. Sustainable REF operations and demand-driven capacity building are also crucial.

The "BR Federal Integrated Water - Interaguas" activity aims to support the Government of Brazil in improving coordination and strengthening capacity among key federal institutions in the water sector for an integrated approach. Specifically, the targeted outcomes include:

*   **Component 1 (Water Resources Management):** To implement tools and instruments within the National Water Resources Management System (SINGREH), improve institutional development, reduce disparities between federal and state water resource management systems, identify climate change mitigation and adaptation actions, optimize procedures, and ensure compliance with the National Water Resources Policy (Law No. 9.433/1997). This involves modifying federal and state legal structures to improve coordination and reduce institutional conflicts, supporting PNRH plans and state and river basin agencies, implementing and expanding the water resources monitoring network, supporting the implementation of water rights and charges, planning and implementing a National Program for water resources management training, and implementing a Results-Based Management System for PNRH.
*   **Component 2 (Water, Irrigation, and Disaster Risk Management):** To provide institutional strengthening for water infrastructure, irrigation, and SINDEC activities, and to increase overall capacity for evaluating strategic water infrastructures and managing disaster risks, including floods, droughts, and other emergencies, through the development of risk management methodologies. This also entails assessing federal and state water infrastructure plans in the Northeast, developing criteria for selecting water infrastructure projects, preparing state and national irrigation plans with incentives to optimize irrigation systems, creating a risk management information system, developing a risk management plan (including risk identification, monitoring, early warning systems, and emergency response plans), and increasing strategic planning for drought risk reduction.
*   **Component 3 (Water Supply and Sanitation):** To support the implementation of Law 11.445/2007, improve the quality of Water Supply and Sanitation (WSS) service provision, and promote universal access to these services through planning, management, studies, and institutional development, including expanding integrated WSS service technology in slum areas. Key actions include implementing the Federal and National Water Supply and Sanitation Policy, assessing PAC investments in WSS infrastructure, developing national technical cooperation in the WSS area, and developing and expanding a training, research, development, and technology national network in WSS, and developing local/regional WSS plans.
*   **Component 4 (Intersectoral Coordination and Integrated Planning):** To focus on integrated planning; identify areas of mutual interest, overlap, or conflict across sectoral plans that affect and/or depend on water resources; support studies and institutional improvements involving multiple sectors, coordinated by the Project Technical Secretariat within ANA; and support water conservation measures in the São Francisco and Araguaia-Tocantins river basins, integrating planning and regulation across water resources, WSS, energy, and shipping sectors.
*   **Component 5 (Project Management, Monitoring, and Evaluation):** To ensure the coordination, monitoring, and evaluation of all interventions to meet specified targets, timetables, and objectives; handle overall administration including reporting, financial management, and auditing; and facilitate effective knowledge transfer and the use of best practices at the subnational level through a robust communication program, including workshops and seminars.

To consider the activity an overall success, it would need to:

*   Effectively address the historically uneven implementation of Brazil's National Water Resources Policy (Law 9.433/1997), which has been hampered by a lack of solid management instruments and poor enforcement of water use rights.
*   Overcome the challenges impacting the implementation of WSS service guidelines (Law 11.445/2007), such as limited institutional capacity, financial sustainability issues, and the inability of many federal agencies and WSS providers to leverage necessary investments due to inefficiencies, high costs, and low operating margins.
*   Achieve improved institutional coordination to reconcile conflicting objectives and create synergies across various economic, governmental, and societal sectors, recognizing the inherent intersectoral nature of water problems.
*   Demonstrate positive indicators such as the establishment and regular functioning of a Program Steering Committee involving all participating institutions.
*   Successfully include and independently evaluate 25 integrated water sector activities and projects in the Government of Brazil's Multi-Year Plan (PPA 2012-2015).
*   Implement a publicly accessible water resources indicator portal.
*   Ensure that 13 state water resource agencies and 4 basin agencies are functioning correctly, effectively applying legal management instruments, and are considerably well-staffed.
*   Mitigate the identified "substantial" or "high" critical risk associated with the project.

The activity targets increasing equitable access, improving quality and relevance of the public higher education system by focusing resources on priority disciplines and strengthening institutional management. Success requires increased access and retention for disadvantaged students, higher enrollment in science/technology, improved faculty qualifications (Master/PhD), and more accredited programs. Overall success also hinges on achieving specific IIA targets, fostering strategic planning, and enhancing system-wide coordination and SINAES capacity.

The targeted outcomes are to improve institutional capacity of key MDAs in NRE sectors for revenue forecasting, benefit-sharing, monitoring, and district-level service delivery; and to increase the knowledge base and strengthen capacities at government and local levels to support NREG policy reforms. Success requires achieving these capacity improvements, sustaining NREG policy reforms, improving environmental outcomes, ensuring benign forest management changes, and empowering forest communities, all supported by strong stakeholder engagement, safeguard compliance, and continuous vigilance.

["Targeted outcomes are to expand access to water and energy services in rural areas and establish mechanisms for sustainable service provision. Specific targets by 2021 include providing access to electricity (off-grid/mini-grid and renewable sources) to 55,000 people, and access to 'improved water sources' for 55,000 people. The project aims to serve ~220 villages, significantly increasing rural electrification and drinkable water infrastructure access (from 15% and 43% towards 80% by 2025). Other outcomes include increased electricity access for rural communities, businesses, and social services, institutional capacity strengthening, and reduced peak demand through efficient lighting.", "Overall success requires achieving the targeted access for 55,000 people to electricity and improved water sources by 2021, and providing services to ~220 villages. Crucially, it demands establishing sustainable O&M mechanisms, including the successful recruitment of regional operators and Bank-approved O&M Financing Frameworks, ensuring functional services in operated villages. Success also depends on effectively mitigating high risks such as institutional capacity, technical design, political/governance, and macroeconomic factors, necessitating robust monitoring, adaptive management, and strong political commitment for long-term sustainability and sector improvement."]

The primary targeted outcome is to reduce flood risks to people and property and improve flood management in selected flood-prone areas of Metro Manila, specifically those served by targeted pumping stations. This will be achieved through constructing approximately 20 new and modernizing 36 existing pumping stations and their appurtenant infrastructure, switching pumps from diesel to electric power where needed, and increasing short-term water retention capacity in drainage areas. The project also aims to improve associated waterways and drainage channels, and develop new channels.

The activity targets improving Niger's PFM/PIM systems for better project quality, absorption of investment credits, and budgetary transparency (e.g., reducing capital budget deviation to 25%, publishing quarterly reports within 4 weeks). It aims to establish a robust policy/regulatory framework for the public irrigation sector, with ONAHA assessing 100% of schemes, and a functional Kandadji Dam operation model. For electricity, it seeks improved governance, efficient supply expansion, and financial sustainability through a new Electricity Code, independent regulator, and NIGELEC's enhanced operational/financial performance (e.g., 80% collection rate, cost-reflective tariffs). Success requires achieving these quantitative targets and implementing the specified legal and institutional reforms.

Targeted outcomes are economic stabilization and structural reforms across three pillars. 1. Expenditure Rationalization: Improve wage bill efficiency (payroll fraud safeguards), enhance public investment management (feasibility studies for >20% capital projects >ID100,000 billion), refine social targeting (>=50% poor covered, <=20% inclusion error, social insurance actuarial deficit period >28 years), improve public debt management (domestic debt >=35% of total). 2. Energy Efficiency: Reduce gas flaring (gas processing >=1,300 MMscfd, local gas-to-power >=1,200 MMscfd, gas in power mix >=70%), reduce electricity subsidies (revenue collection >50% of generation). 3. SOE Transparency/Governance: Improve non-financial SOE transparency (ISAR Benchmark >15%), reduce state-owned banks' exposure to non-financial SOEs. Overall success is achieving these 2018 targets.

The Ethiopia Urban Institutional and Infrastructure Development Program (UIIDP) targets enhancing the institutional performance of participating urban local governments to develop and sustain urban infrastructure, services, and local economic development. Key outcomes include enhanced citizen participation and engagement in ULG planning and budgeting, increased own-source revenue, improved infrastructure, service delivery, operation and maintenance systems, improved efficiency and effectiveness in fiduciary management, improved environmental and social management and safeguards, and strengthened accountability and oversight systems. New focus areas aim for gender equality, strengthened urban resilience and disaster risk management, and improved local economic development for long-term job creation.

["The activity targets mobilizing private investment through Public-Private Partnerships (PPPs), improving competitiveness to foster private sector growth, and enhancing public transparency and accountability for good governance. Specific outcomes include reforming the power, logistics, and telecom sectors for private participation; improving the investment climate and modernizing the financial sector; ensuring proper functioning of government bond and foreign exchange markets; and promoting citizen information and participation and improving public enterprise management. Macroeconomic reforms aim for fiscal consolidation (deficit <3% GDP by FY21), easing inflation (~9% in FY19), and a more flexible exchange rate. Ultimately, Ethiopia aims to become a lower-middle-income country by 2025.", "The activity would be considered an overall success upon achieving specific targets by 2021. This includes all PPP transactions selected transparently; power sector full cost recovery, institutional restructuring, and losses reduced to 15%; customs clearance for imports in 10 days and exports in 4 days; US$120 million cumulative FDI in logistics; 3 telecom licensees with data costs at 5% of GDP per capita; 5% mobile money usage; US$5 billion overall FDI; 10% tradable market instruments in government borrowing; all CSOs eligible for social accountability; and 9 SOEs publishing audited financials. Fiscal deficit below 3% GDP by FY21 and inflation easing to ~9% in FY19 are also key success indicators."]

Targeted outcomes aim to enhance Kerala's resilience against natural disasters and climate change, institute transformative policies, and improve preparedness. Key results include: resilient recovery from 2018 floods per RKDP guidelines, mobilization of additional public/private financial resources for recovery, improved emergency/disaster/climate risk management capacity, improved river basin planning and water infrastructure operations for climate resilience, and more resilient/sustainable infrastructure operations management. Overall success requires achieving these specific outcomes.

Targeted outcomes: Strengthen Amazonas' fiscal sustainability by reforming social security, controlling wage bills, improving public investment (with climate filters); and integrate development with forest conservation by combating deforestation, promoting sustainable forest concessions, and streamlining environmental regularization. Success requires: reducing state personnel spending to 46.5% (2021), deforested area to 1,130 km², validating 8,054 CARs, granting 60,000 ha in forest concessions, and enrolling 12,000 families in Bolsa Floresta. Overall success also depends on sustained political commitment, effective inter-institutional coordination, robust monitoring, and adaptability.

The targeted outcomes of the activity were the long-term ecological stabilization of the Guinée Forestière region and an improvement of the local population's livelihoods, specifically through preservation and sustainable management of state forests involving local populations. Success additionally required stable wood/agricultural prices/demand, a consistent legal/political timber framework, no significant population growth/immigration, and no negative climate change effects.

["The activity targeted the management of pilot areas in Guizhou according to sustainability principles (SFM) at municipal, user-community, or company levels. Expected outcomes included increased annual tree growth, improved forest stand structure, regulated plant competition, an improved road system, increased forestry knowledge for farmers, and the integration of sustainable management principles into national forest policy. Higher-level objectives aimed for increased added value of forest areas, environmental protection, improved valuable timber stock, climate change resilience, enhanced local income, and influencing national forestry policy.", "To achieve success, as defined at the outset, the activity targeted the adoption of approximately 110 forest management plans meeting international certification, and their implementation on at least 35,000 hectares. Further success metrics included the renewal of 75% of these plans after 10 years, an increase in valuable timber volume in the project area compared to reference areas, and an improvement in forest structure, specifically an increased proportion of mixed forest stands, in the project area."]

The targeted outcomes of AgMIP include an improved understanding of climate change impacts on agricultural development and the effectiveness of adaptation options, leading to long-term improved food security and livelihoods for poor people across Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia. Specific results expected from the extension include assessments of climate change impacts on additional crops and livestock, expanded test locations, integrated climate/crop analyses with economic models in new regions, co-designed applications of the AgMIP toolkit to inform decisions in at least three countries, and evidence on how AgMIP models can inform policy. Other outcomes are 7-10 co-authored policy briefs and fact sheets, a co-designed web-based dynamic data interface (AgMIP Impacts Explorer), risk maps and other evidence-based products, and capacity strengthening for 100-120 developing country researchers. Regional and local workshops, stakeholder needs assessments, an M&E framework, and communications materials are also expected. The activity will be considered an overall success if these specific results are delivered, demonstrating an improved understanding of climate change impacts and adaptation effectiveness, ultimately contributing to enhanced food security and livelihoods.

The targeted outcomes of the "Growth in Rural Economy and Agriculture in Tajikistan" activity include fostering inclusive economic growth in rural Tajikistan and reducing poverty through private sector development and job creation. This involves directly supporting farmers and rural entrepreneurs, improving the business environment, expanding access to microfinance, and boosting revenues from cross-border trade. Specific outcome targets for success include a 95% rise in loans for private investments over the 2012 baseline with a further 20% increase by 2014, and sales value in two key value chains remaining 30% above baseline despite currency depreciation. Output targets by March 2016 include for the Business Enabling Environment: 40% of interviewed business people confirming positive impact from government reforms on business sales, up to 5% more business licenses issued, and an additional 375 Business Association members receiving high-quality services. For Rural Economic Activity: a 30% yield increase in lowland areas and 15% in upland areas for farmers receiving advice, advisory services covering an additional 2,979 hectares in lowlands (7,278 families) and 698 hectares in uplands (7,324 families), 10 new members for SAROB cooperative, 105 new members for 26 agricultural machinery cooperatives, and 100 hectares of alternative crops cultivated. Also, an additional 40 businesses in non-agricultural value chains are to receive sector-specific training and add innovative products. For Access to Finance: assisting 2,100 businesses in accessing tailor-made financial products, disbursing 1,500 start-up loans, engaging 60,000 new credit clients (a 15% increase), and supporting a 15% increase (154,000) in individual saving accounts. For Cross-border Trade: cross-border markets and businesses turnover increasing from £164,000 to £493,000 per month, and revenue from businesses along transport corridors increasing from £15,000 to £27,000 per month. For Economic Policy Decisions: the Ministry of Economic Development and Trade (MOEDT) better equipped to use economic analysis for policy decisions, and five analytical reports produced, with at least one directly influencing new policy. To consider the activity an overall success, it would need to achieve this overarching goal of inclusive economic growth and poverty reduction by meeting all the specified outcome and output targets, including the financial, agricultural, trade, and policy strengthening metrics. This would demonstrate continued strong progress, similar to past “A” ratings, and effectively transition towards a planned second phase, incorporating findings from an independent program evaluation and enhancing the Government of Tajikistan’s capacity to measure and anticipate economic changes, with a continued emphasis on inclusive development, particularly for women.

WRAP targets strengthening water governance and institutions, climate change adaptation via Nature-Based Solutions (NbS), and environmental sustainability. It aims to reduce water wastage, improve economic benefits, and upscale innovative water solutions. The extension expands NbS/governance interventions to more vulnerable communities (250,000 additional people), increases land under sustainable management, improves water distribution in two provinces, and boosts women/minority roles. Success requires achieving these targets, consistent high performance ('A' ratings), government buy-in, mainstreaming into national climate discourse, and demonstrating strong Value for Money.

The SIDAR programme targets: sustainable & inclusive capacity building for SIDS; UK partnership for economic development; improved access to affordable finance; & impacting the policy environment. Specific outcomes include increased GEF funding for adaptation (e.g., early warnings, resilient infrastructure); strengthened SPREP capacity for a resilient Pacific environment; enhanced PFTAC support for macroeconomic stability & climate fund access; improved Fintech access to finance; innovative finance for global system improvement; & PRF bridging mid-size climate finance gaps. Success requires achieving these outcomes to respond to climate, economic, & disaster pressures.

The targeted outcomes of the "Fortalecimiento Institucional Oficina Tecnica Ozono" activity are:

1.  **Ratification of the Kigali Amendment:** The successful publication of the Executive Decree ratifying the Kigali Amendment in the Official Gazette "La Gaceta," followed by the official deposit of Costa Rica's ratification instrument with the Montreal Protocol Secretariat, resulting in Costa Rica being listed as a ratifying party on the Montreal Protocol website.
2.  **Evaluation and Adaptation of National Legislation and Policies:** The development of national technical standards related to the use of natural refrigerants in the Refrigeration and Air Conditioning (RAC) sector, agreed upon with the sector and formalized by an Executive Decree, addressing identified gaps and adapting appropriate international standards. This also includes the national implementation of a CO2 quota system for controlling HFC imports, formalized by an Executive Decree, building on the existing HCFC system for the gradual elimination of HFCs.
3.  **Development of Coordination Mechanisms and Sensitization:** The establishment of a National Committee comprising public and private RAC sector actors to support decision-making for Kigali Amendment implementation. The adoption of new technologies and alternative refrigerants by the RAC Sector, evidenced through participation in promotional workshops. The publication of a control mechanism for import quotas, informed by the exchange of international experiences. Additionally, there will be increased awareness among importers, marketers of RAC equipment, customs officials, public customs function auxiliaries, fiscal control police, and end-users regarding Costa Rica's commitments under the Kigali Amendment, promoting the acquisition and use of energy-efficient, ozone-friendly equipment with low global warming potential.
4.  **HFC Import Control System Optimization:** The optimization of the current HFC license concession system, in coordination with the Foreign Trade Promoter and the General Directorate of Customs, to ensure reliable information for HFC import notification. This includes the design of a new quota concession system for HFC imports to be distributed among importers, with the collaboration of international experts.

To consider the activity an overall success, the following conditions would need to be met:

*   The **Kigali Amendment is officially ratified** by Costa Rica, demonstrated by the publication of the Executive Decree in "La Gaceta" and the deposit of the ratification instrument with the Montreal Protocol Secretariat, with Costa Rica appearing as a ratifying party on the Montreal Protocol website.
*   **National legislation and policies are effectively adapted and implemented**, including the development and formalization of national technical standards for natural refrigerants via Executive Decree, and the national implementation of a CO2 quota system for the gradual elimination of HFCs, also formalized by Executive Decree.
*   **Robust coordination mechanisms are established and operational** between public and private sectors, including a functioning National Committee for decision-making support.
*   The **RAC sector demonstrates significant adoption of new technologies and alternative refrigerants**, evidenced by high attendance and engagement in promotional workshops, building upon the baseline of over 1200 previously trained individuals.
*   **Public and stakeholder awareness** regarding Kigali Amendment commitments and the importance of low global warming potential, energy-efficient technologies is substantially raised, effectively reaching importers, customs officials, and end-users.
*   The **HFC import control system is fully optimized, and a new quota system is designed and implemented**, providing reliable data for import notification and controlled distribution among importers.
*   Progress is consistently monitored against the **clearly defined objectives, specific activities with measurable indicators, baselines, and verifiable results**, within a comprehensive framework that effectively targets diverse stakeholders and leverages both international experience and existing national capacities.

["The activity targets increased representation of women in parliament, local hurals, and Cabinet, aiming for MDG3 targets of 30% seats in Parliament and 15% at Aimag level (from baselines of 14.5% parliament, 0% Aimag governors/hural chairs, 27.3% local hurals). Expected outputs for 2015 include: new/amended legal provisions promoting gender equality in election/political party reforms; enhanced capacities for women in political office; and raised public awareness/positive image of women in politics.", "Overall success requires achieving outcome targets of increased women's representation (30% in Parliament, 15% Aimag level). This includes enacting new/amended gender-sensitive legal provisions by 2015, enhancing women's political capacities, and fostering positive public perception. Critical for success is securing the $500,000 unfunded budget to fully implement planned activities, coupled with effective partnerships, clear objectives, measurable indicators, and timely pre-election intervention."]

The activity targets two outcomes: 1) Expanded and strengthened MPA network with biodiversity mainstreamed into marine spatial planning. Success indicators include 40,000ha MPA expansion, Chinese white dolphin population, established collaborative governance, improved rules/regulations, and financial sustainability. 2) Improved management effectiveness of MPA/ESAs in pilot areas. Success indicators include increased MPA agency capacity, higher METT scores, community engagement, reduced biodiversity threats, and improved public/decision-maker understanding of MPAs. Overall success requires achieving these targets.

The targeted outcomes include: Output 1: Creation and institutionalization of a structural implementation mechanism for reform, aiming for 5 task forces established, 5 action plans developed, 5 consultation methodologies designed, and 1 communication strategy developed. Output 2: Operationalization of the Policy Matrix via technical assistance, targeting 18 technical experts deployed, 146 practical solutions developed, and support for 146 reform actions. Overall success hinges on the achievement of these clearly defined, measurable targets for both outputs, with progress monitored through desk reviews of MoP and progress reports.

Targeted outcomes: strengthen Iraq's national capacities to access and manage GCF climate financing by establishing an effective NDA, implementing a national No Objection procedure, executing GCF bilateral agreements, improving climate finance monitoring/oversight, enhancing stakeholder engagement and women's representation in consultations, developing gender-sensitive country programs with adaptation/mitigation priorities (including a project prioritization tool), and identifying/assessing potential direct access entities. Success: these preparatory activities lay a foundation for Iraq to effectively and coordinately kick-start its climate financing needs.

["Targeted outcomes include a paradigm shift to innovative, deforestation-free, organic cocoa, reducing 5.5 million tCO2eq emissions, and delivering climate adaptation co-benefits for a low-carbon economy. It aims to establish agroforestry and restore land, improving practices on 514,899 ha. Objectives involve enhancing smallholder incomes, food security, reversing deforestation for 7,550 direct (30% women) and 600,000 indirect beneficiaries, and fostering financial independence. It seeks to operationalize REDD+, strengthen multi-stakeholder platforms (3, 40% women), build capacity for Integrated Landscape Management, and test new inclusive business models (1 model adopted by increasing farmers, 35% women).", "Success means achieving a paradigm shift to innovative, deforestation-free, organic cocoa; 4.38 million tCO2e emission reduction; adaptation co-benefits; and reversed deforestation. It requires strengthened multi-stakeholder cooperation, capacity-building, integrated landscape management, climate-resilient practices, innovative value chains, inclusive business models, and sustainable standards. Institutional capacity, effective coordination, farmer/financing adoption, and the low-carbon model's self-sufficiency are vital. Key targets: significant area restored and under improved practices."]

Targeted outcomes: Co-develop innovative waste solutions, empower entrepreneurs, facilitate transparent data access for policy-makers, enhance knowledge on waste valorization, connect solution providers/off-takers, stimulate research, build capacity for sanitation management, create sustainable projects/employment, and contribute to SDGs and national policies. Overall success requires: a collaborative platform, a waste resource map, local sanitation capacity building, a market for innovative waste solutions, waste segregation awareness, academic research promotion, successful support for five innovative projects, and leveraging local knowledge for national ownership and sustainability.

The primary targeted outcome is to further enhance the NOU's programme management capacity to meet Montreal Protocol commitments, especially ODS policy enforcement and supervision. Specific outcomes include sustaining ODS phase-out progress, achieving HCFC control targets, and implementing national HFC phase-down. Key outcomes are improving policy formulation/enforcement, strengthening data reporting, enhancing coordination, improving NOU project management, and promoting public awareness.

["The activity targets include strengthening COVID-19 preparedness and socio-economic recovery in Samoa, particularly for unemployed women and youth in the agriculture and fisheries sectors. It aims to build social cohesion and community resilience, diversify the economic sector, ensure inclusive growth respecting land/ocean resources, and mitigate long-term COVID-19 impacts. Specific goals are reducing import reliance for essential goods, creating local income-generating opportunities, empowering vulnerable women/youth (60%+ beneficiaries), and enhancing community resilience via sustainable income activities and resource safeguarding.", "Overall success hinges on sustained Government commitment and community ownership. Key assumptions include public trust/demand for local sanitizing/PPEs, sufficient agriculture/fisheries labor, high participation of women/youth, and increased household incomes. Specific 12-month targets include supporting 3 government entities, 5-10 CBOs responding (60%+ women/youth led), creating 100-150 jobs (60%+ women/youth), producing/selling 100,000 hand sanitisers, commercializing 2 sanitizing product types, 30 health/retail facilities purchasing local products, trialling 1 biodegradable mask liner, starting 10-20 green/blue businesses, and establishing 10 community seaweed farms (60%+ women/youth led)."]

["The activity targets supporting the Philippines' NDC by accelerating its transition to a circular economy, achieving economic, social, environmental goals, and significantly reducing GHG emissions across all sectors. Specifically, it aims to reduce energy-related GHG emissions from 55.7 mMT CO2e (2010 baseline) to 38.4 mMT CO2e, and transport-related emissions from 25.3 mMT CO2e to 21.3 mMT CO2e. This also includes establishing a circular economy data system, developing a CE framework for NDC, and implementing CE solutions and investments at the city level, contributing to adaptation co-benefits, community resilience, and post-pandemic recovery.", "Overall success is determined by the actual reduction of CO2 emissions (tonnes avoided or reduced), the number of beneficiaries gaining new access to green/sustainable energy, increased climate change resilience among direct beneficiaries, creation of green/sustainable jobs, and the number of people trained, educated, or informed. Success hinges on clear problem identification, a data-driven methodology, a multi-stakeholder collaborative approach, focus on practical solutions in targeted urban areas, emphasis on gender-responsive and inclusive interventions, and leveraging international expertise through partnerships, all within a defined funding and timeframe to achieve low carbon and resilient development."]

["The Lagos Metropolitan Development and Governance Project targets increasing sustainable access to basic urban services via critical infrastructure investments in nine slums. Key outcomes include: increasing safe water access to 90% (from 26%), reducing in-house flooding to 0% of households (from 29%), increasing garbage reaching landfills to 90% (from 50%), and increasing households with tarred roads to 70% (from 34%). Additionally, it aims to strengthen metropolitan policy dialogue, build capacity for Citizens' Mediation Centers and the Public Defender, and enhance solid waste management through private sector participation and infrastructure upgrades.", "To consider the activity an overall success, achieving the ambitious five-year quantitative targets for water access, flood reduction, waste disposal, and tarred roads is crucial. Success also hinges on demonstrating \"unequivocal ownership\" of the project, reinforced by inclusive implementation arrangements overseen by a coalition of public and private sectors. Furthermore, the effective functioning of a robust Monitoring & Evaluation framework, including biennial updates of a city-wide baseline of non-income poverty, is essential for measurable outcomes and transparent reporting to citizens via community report cards. Successful implementation of urban policy, public finance, and service delivery reforms is also key."]

The WMIP aims to enhance irrigation service delivery and water management, sustainably increasing irrigated agricultural productivity (targeting a min. 10% crop yield increase and 7% irrigated area expansion on some schemes), and improving national water resource governance. Success requires achieving >=90% tariff collection within two years of rehabilitation, >=80% of rehabilitated schemes properly operated/maintained, increased WUAs receiving requested water, high water user satisfaction with DWR, a >=10% crop yield increase within four years post-rehab, and water use aligned with approved river basin plans.

The Matanza-Riachuelo Basin Sustainable Development Project (APL-1) aims to improve sewerage services in the M-R River Basin and other parts of Buenos Aires by expanding transport and treatment capacity, reduce industrial discharges to the M-R River through industrial conversion grants for small and medium enterprises, promote improved decision-making for environmentally-sustainable land use and drainage planning, and strengthen ACUMAR's institutional framework for ongoing and sustainable clean-up of the M-R River Basin. The activity will be considered an overall success when: a) the percentage of sewage from AySA's concession area that is appropriately treated and disposed of is achieved; b) regular monitoring for at least the 50 most polluting enterprises is established; c) the percentage of enterprises in the matching grants program have effectively reduced discharge loads; d) both the Integrated Territorial Development Plan and the updated Drainage Master Plan are agreed with key stakeholders and implementation arrangements are piloted; and e) ACUMAR is fully staffed, operates with its own budget, and can fulfill its legal functions.

The activity targets increasing privately owned renewable energy production and enhancing industrial demand-side energy efficiency to reduce greenhouse gas emissions. Overall success requires: increased total renewable electricity/thermal heating capacity and incremental production; greater share of renewable electricity in total generation; significant heat/electricity savings from efficiency; substantial CO2 emission reductions (e.g., 2.25 mtCO2 annually directly); and cost-effective CTF use for CO2 reduction. Quantitative markers per US$100M invested include ~42 MW renewable capacity, ~175 GWh/yr generation, ~219k tons/yr CO2 reduced (RE); and ~482 Tcal/yr saved, ~190k tons/yr CO2 reduced (EE).

The targeted outcomes of PNO4 are to improve socio-economic conditions of the rural population and promote better protection and management of natural resources, employing an integrated participatory approach. To be an overall success, the activity needs: 1) A percentage improvement in a composite socio-economic index at the administrative sector (imadas) level, covering employment, housing, household equipment, access to potable water, accessibility, and basic services (health, schooling, electricity). 2) An increase in sectors where the population is organized into Agricultural Development and Fishing Groups (GDAP) or informal development committees that have prepared and are implementing their Community Development Plans (PDCs) with partners. 3) An increase in land developed with water and soil conservation works, perennial plantations, and improved pastures in the intervention zones. This also includes strengthening agricultural productivity, increasing average agricultural income, diversifying production systems, raising farmer awareness about climate change, and integrating resilience measures into PDCs.

Targeted outcomes aim to preserve key lifeline infrastructure (transport, community, agriculture), reduce household vulnerability through employment and food security, restore all-weather road access, rehabilitate schools/health centers, and enhance disaster risk management (DRM) capacity including a contingency fund and Early Warning Systems. Success requires rapid achievement of these objectives, sustainability through "Build Back Better" and climate-proof construction, effective DRM, mitigation of political capture via transparent targeting and enhanced supervision, synergistic implementation, institutional strengthening (e.g., ARM), and paving the way for fuller government re-engagement.

The targeted outcomes are to enhance Northern Benin Savannah Ecosystems biodiversity protection through conservation and reduced human pressure. Success entails: increased PA-METT scores (Pendjari NP: 85 to 93; W NP: 68 to 76); strengthened CENAGREF management capacity, including effective ecosystem management, feasibility studies for forest reserves, and improved fiduciary practices (communication strategy, study submissions, financial reports); improved PNP wildlife indicators; successful, sustainable Income-Generating Activities for communities, reducing pressure; and a fully operational FSOA with Conservation Trust Fund for long-term park financing post-project closure.

The activity aimed to enhance Midelt and Tata provinces' capacities in renewable energy and energy efficiency. Targeted outcomes for success, known at inception, included: developing energy management capacities in four pilot municipalities; raising local awareness and establishing two energy information centers (one per province); creating specialized information and professional training on RE/EE technologies; demonstrating benefits via pilot projects; and institutionalizing experience evaluation. A key success criterion was for each province to have a strategic RE/EE orientation and evaluation system, targeting two provinces by 2020 (from a 2016 baseline of zero).

The activity aims to provide farmers access to new agriculture technology and innovation to tackle pests and diseases, increase food production, protect the environment, and reduce poverty and hunger. Specific outcomes include: WorldVeg reaching 235,000 households with improved varieties and 30,000 farmers with seed/mungbean transformation. CABI targets 35 million Plantwise reach, 10 million households for invasive species reduction, and sustainable private sector models. icipe aims for 30,000 households with biopesticides, 10,000 with reduced tick infestation, 300,000 with drought-resilient push-pull, and 200,000 dairy farmers. New workstreams target increased vegetable production, reduced chemical use, and improved crop yields/food safety.

The activity targets improving health and survival of Malawi's most vulnerable populations, including women and children. Key outcomes include increased provision of quality, cost-effective life-saving primary health services for up to 4 million rural residents annually, improved priority setting, resourcing, management, and strengthened accountability of district-level health service delivery. It also aims to improve district governance, resilience to public health threats, and significantly advance Sexual and Reproductive Health and Rights (SRHR) by improving FP commodity availability and access (especially for adolescents and people with disabilities), targeting a 60% contraceptive prevalence rate by 2030. Overall success will be measured by a demonstrable reduction in preventable maternal, child, and neonatal mortality, evidenced through demographic health surveys and programme monitoring, along with positive Value for Money impacts and sustained improvements in health systems.

The activity targets strengthening TBI DRC's capacity to influence NRM and governance as an equal partner. Outcomes include improved legal frameworks, sustainable NRM, and enhanced local/indigenous community livelihoods via community forestry, SFEs, and artisanal mining. It aims for communities to advocate against oil exploitation and promote Just Energy Transition, empowering CSOs/marginalized groups into recognized coalitions, and improving landscape governance. Overall success is achieved when TBI DRC acts as an equal partner, communities advocate effectively, a strong SFEs/CSOs coalition is recognized, local well-being improves, and inclusive landscape governance is established.

Targeted outcomes: 1. Equitable/sustainable spatial planning via CSO-led participatory village mapping (RTRW-Desa, RPJM-Desa) & Strategic Environmental Assessments (SEAs). 2. Private/government use High Conservation Value (HCV) tools via EEA Forum & L&A. 3. Sustainable oil palm management/trade through community livelihoods ('quick wins'), BUMDES, and value chain advocacy. 4. Village/community participation in sustainable agro-industry. 5. Increased local community involvement in sustainable forest management (village forests, social forestry). Success requires improved local/govt capacity in natural resource governance, effective participatory planning, sustainable livelihoods preserving forests, and strong multi-stakeholder coordination. Key assumptions include respected village planning, effective BUMDES, and communities as effective forest stewards.

The Small Community Water Program targets improving quality of life in rural areas by increasing sustainable potable water/wastewater service coverage for 200,000 new rural beneficiaries in approximately 500 communities, contributing to the IDB Water Initiative. Overall success requires: 1) Achieving these beneficiary/community targets, monitored via intermediate outputs (e.g., 500 projects inscribed by Year 4, 500 projects operating sustainably for 12 months by Year 6). 2) Fulfillment of conditions precedent to disbursement (e.g., subsidiary agreement, FPS internal arrangements with required professionals, IDB Operating Regulations approval, Environmental License). 3) Community contributions (20% capital costs, user tariffs for O&M). 4) Strengthened institutional capacity (FPS, municipalities, EPSA).

The activity targets accelerating structural transformation towards green, inclusive, and digital transitions. It aims to strengthen leadership for climate ambition in 24 countries, enhance integrated governance by developing 32 new/improved institutional structures for NDC implementation, integrate 19 gender-responsive approaches, and enhance 28 monitoring/transparency systems. Additionally, it targets supporting 17 countries in developing NDC Partnership Plans and 4 with technical assistance, delivering evidence-based design by updating/reflecting NDCs in 51 national/sectoral plans, preparing 16 gender-inclusive action plans, developing 12 long-term LEDS, strengthening 22 NAMAs, and building capacities to prepare 24 de-risking/private sector investment strategies for climate finance.

Targeted outcomes include UNDP's strategic repositioning and comprehensive support to GRZ for 7th & 8th NDP completion/formulation. It will accelerate catalytic initiatives feeding into CRNA, 8th NDP, CCA, and next CPD, leveraging expertise in sustainable mining, youth empowerment, early recovery/resilience, and digitalization. Specific 2022 targets are: 5 mining strategy plans/policies & 10 private sector partnerships; 5 women's entrepreneurship pipelines/concept notes, 1 Joint Programme & 1 Resource Mobilization Strategy; 1 youth empowerment coordination mechanism, 1 Graduate Volunteer Scheme & 2 pilot initiatives. It contributes to UNDAF/CP Outcome 3.1 and CPD Outputs across nine key development areas.

The activity targets transforming the Dominican Republic's production and consumption model by integrating sustainable solutions, protecting natural capital, and reducing vulnerability to climate change and disasters, with active participation from civil society and institutions, particularly for at-risk women. Success will be evident if MSME loan access increases by 10% by 2027, GHG emissions reduce to 32,634.80 GgCO2eq by 2025, and 10 national/municipal sustainable tourism plans are achieved by 2027. Further success relies on sustained public/private sector collaboration, knowledge transfer, government commitment, and no new major emergencies.

Targeted outcomes: Increase LAC countries' capacity to mobilize diverse nature finance (public/private/international cooperation) for NDC implementation. This involves enhanced knowledge management for reporting, robust institutional/regulatory frameworks for climate finance, and catalyzed investments. Success criteria include: an S-S exchange platform with 2 exchanges/year engaging 20 countries; implementing green budgeting in 3 new countries; 3-4 countries developing investment roadmaps for NDCs; piloting open-source carbon footprint software in 2 countries; developing IPLC inclusion methodology; creating bankable investment proposals; and institutionalizing Environmental & Social Risk Analysis in financial systems.

The targeted outcome of the Port Moresby Power Grid Development Project is a better power supply for Port Moresby. This will be achieved by reducing the duration of power outages for customers by 42% by the end of 2016, compared to the January 2013 baseline. To consider the activity an overall success, its impact should result in PPL's residential and business customers undertaking more economic activities, specifically by reducing unserved demand in Port Moresby by 20% (measured in GWh) by the end of 2020, against the January 2013 baseline of 0.4 GWh. Key details to achieve these targets include: 1. Upgrading and rehabilitating hydropower capacity: This involves installing 6 MW capacity at Rouna 1 by end 2016 and rehabilitating the Sirinumu toe-of-dam hydropower plant to 1.6 MW by end 2015, leading to an annual reduction of CO2 emissions by 12,000 tCO2e due to less diesel generation. These upgrades will supply 13% of the renewable energy or 3.6% of the total energy for the Port Moresby grid. 2. Upgrading the Port Moresby distribution grid: This entails installing 30 km of 11 kV distribution grid by end 2016 and connecting 3,000 additional low-income customers (including households headed by women). This will increase the access rate in urban and peri-urban areas of Port Moresby from 69% to 74% and connect 5% more households. 3. Upgrading substation capacity: This involves constructing a new Kilakila substation, a 6.1 km (66 kV) transmission line, and retrofitting existing substations. These improvements are estimated to reduce the average outage duration for customers by 42% (and by 80% within the central business district), significantly contributing to a more reliable power supply.

The activity targets increasing clean, carbon-negative solar energy supply by expanding to 4 MW capacity, generating 5.5 GWh/year for 10 years, and reducing CO2 emissions by 1,644 tons/annum. It also aims to increase PPA offtake to 6.1 million kWh/year, improve energy affordability, and create up to 20 construction and 5 permanent jobs. Success requires commissioning the 4 MW system by end-2017/Q1-2018, consistent generation, carbon reduction achieved, signed PPA addendum, job creation, and stable PPA, while supporting Samoa's renewable energy goals.

["Targeted outcomes include increasing OSE's sustainability by enhancing water system reliability and resilience (e.g., 20,000m³/hour Aguas Corrientes intake capacity increase, new flood-resistant WTPs for Durazno/Treinta y Tres serving 85k inhabitants until 2035), improving efficiency (e.g., reducing non-revenue water by approximately 20 million m³/year, 12% energy savings from replacing 250 pumps, establishing DMCs in Montevideo, Rivera, Salto, Las Piedras), and strengthening management capacity (e.g., strategic planning, risk management, environmental sustainability).", "Overall success requires OSE to achieve increased sustainability by significantly enhancing water system reliability/resilience (reduced outages, climate protection), substantially improving efficiency (20 million m³/year NRW reduction, 12% energy savings, DMCs operational in specified cities), and effectively strengthening management, planning, and risk mitigation capacities. Success also implies meeting water demands for target populations in Durazno/Treinta y Tres until 2035, contributing to cost reduction, lower greenhouse gas emissions, and advancing OSE's strategic plan."]

Targeted outcomes include contributing to sustainable management and utilization of Congo's forest ecosystems and improving local populations' well-being. The project aims to enhance national forest resource knowledge, ensure land security, and improve land allocation to boost agricultural productivity. This facilitates concerted national territory zoning, revision of national forest policy, and enables land registration/allocation based on stakeholder consensus. It also supports strategic planning, quantifies timber/non-timber products for local incomes, provides REDD+ data (monitoring, carbon stock), improves CNIAF staff skills, and establishes a permanent forest monitoring and MRV system.

At the beginning of the activity, the targeted outcomes included contributing to securing and stabilising the electricity supply in southern Togo, improving voltage regulation with lower losses, channelling energy to Lomé, and achieving better electricity quality and availability for households and businesses. The activity also aimed to promote economic development in the greater Lomé area and strengthen regional cooperation within the West African Power Pool (WAPP). Success was defined by achieving a maximum input capacity of 75 MW in the substation in its first full year of operation and a substation availability of 99%.

The targeted outcomes of the activity, as known at the beginning, were to improve ecosystem services to mitigate the negative impacts of climate change on coastal zones in Jamaica, Saint Lucia, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, and Grenada. At the impact level, the aim was to contribute to reducing climate-related risks for coastal populations. Specific expected outcomes from the ecosystem-based adaptation approach included improving the integrity of adaptation-relevant ecosystems, building more resilient coastal ecosystems, reducing the threat of climate change impacts, alleviating the vulnerability of coastal populations, rehabilitating coral reef ecosystems, improving fish biomass, increasing resilience to climate change, reducing coastal erosion, and creating sustainable livelihood opportunities. A second component aimed to strengthen regional knowledge management on ecosystem-based adaptation, including systematization and dissemination of lessons learned, to achieve synergy effects and knowledge sharing. To achieve success as defined at the beginning of the activity, up to 100,000 people in the four Small Island Developing States (Jamaica, Saint Lucia, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, and Grenada) would need to benefit from the measures. Additionally, success was conceptually dependent on ensuring sustainability through close involvement of local authorities to identify tailor-made measures, generate ownership, ensure operating and follow-up costs, and integrate local adaptation measures into national adaptation strategies via institutionally and issue-based project management.

The activity aimed to reduce electricity consumption, associated costs, and CO2 emissions in EcoCasa housing units compared to reference units, and reduce greenhouse gas emissions in the household sector relative to a business-as-usual scenario. Success was defined by financing 8,400 "carbon-neutral" housing units (8,000 in Component 1, 400 in Component 2), meaning units achieving 70% of a reference unit's energy demand. An overall target of 350,000 tCO2e reduction for EcoCasa II was set. Forecasts included average annual electricity savings of 41 kWh/m  and gas savings of 16 kWh/m , translating to projected household cost savings of ~122.62 USD/year per 47.27m  unit, and 1,056 kg CO2e reductions per average unit/year. EcoCasa units were expected to be up to 20% cheaper for low-income buyers.

["Targeted outcomes focus on providing strategic forestry knowledge, tools, and analysis to policymakers for scaled-up investments in deforestation/restoration, aligned with UNFCCC/SDGs. Goals: scale program impact, generate new knowledge for sustainable forest management, inform UK policy, elevate forest restoration globally, mainstream gender in knowledge/policy, advance low-carbon development, and enhance community resilience via improved food security/nutrition through forests. Specific targets: commit additional 40M ha to Bonn Challenge, mobilize $444M for World Bank forest investments, and establish REDD+ benefit-sharing roadmaps in 2-3 countries.", "Overall success requires scaling up early impacts, generating new knowledge for sustainable forest management (UNFCCC, REDD+), informing UK policy, and elevating forest restoration globally with tools for implementation. It demands partner institutions fully integrate gender into knowledge work, mainstreaming it into international processes. Success is indicated by: forests' role in poverty reduction/food security reflected in national planning/dialogues; establishing REDD+ benefit-sharing roadmaps in 2-3 countries; PROFOR developing cross-World Bank workstreams; all partners using new evaluative tools to track uptake/influence; and evidence of gender integration in knowledge planning/measurement. Continued generation of critical knowledge and lessons, plus effective measurement of uptake/influence, are also crucial."]
