["PIDG2 aims to grow yearly commitments to $1bn by 2030, leverage $25bn additional finance ($12.5bn private), increase bankable project pipeline, unlock local currency, and attract commercial investors into climate resilience and just energy transitions. It will rebalance InfraCo incentives towards impact, increase urban investments, and build out themes in energy, transport, sustainable cities, and natural resource management. Objectives include accelerating an equitable transition to net zero, supporting systemic change in infrastructure financing, and creating commercially viable, investment-ready, climate-resilient assets that contribute to SDGs, growth, and poverty reduction.", "Overall success for PIDG2 will be measured by achieving $1bn in yearly commitments by 2030 and leveraging $25bn additional finance (>$12.5bn private). Key performance indicators (KPIs) include a Private Sector Investment to PIDG Commitments ratio > 3.5x, >50% investments in DAC I/II countries, >45% in FCAS countries, and a Development Impact Rating >= 64.5. Success also entails building a strong pipeline of bankable, high-impact projects, maintaining external credit ratings for debt/guarantee arms, and demonstrating systemic change, rising living standards, inclusive job creation, and sustainable, climate-resilient development, all while adhering to the Climate Strategy for net zero transition."]

["Tegma nursery fully operational: installed irrigation, 2 equipped cultivation beds with shade houses (08mX30), 1 culvert (50mx40), 16 solar panels for admin. Waste management unit operational: 1 shredder acquired, 39 personnel trained in waste valorization.", "Overall success needs State recruitment of 1 manager, 2 cultivation managers, 6 guards, 30 workers, 1 driver, and their capacity building. Additionally, addressing original inaccessibility, producing up to 500,000 plants, covering plant needs for Tizi-Ouzou and neighboring wilayas, ensuring improved plant quality, socioeconomic valorization, and sustainable resource management are crucial."]

Targeted outcomes include HCFC consumption reductions: 50% by 2021, 56% by 2022, 60% by 2023. A ban on HCFC 141B import/use by Jan 1, 2020. Specific sustainable reductions target 5,800 MT (141b foam), 1,140 MT (22 AC manufacturing), 1,250 MT (22 RAC servicing), totaling 8,190 MT (769.49 ODP tons) HCFC consumption reduction, yielding 8.5 million MT CO2-eq emission reductions annually from 2023, and capacity building for technicians. Success requires meeting these HCFC phase-out targets, ensuring conversions to non-ODS/low-GWP technologies, and India's compliance with the Montreal Protocol.

["The activity aims to enhance EMASEO EP's solid waste collection services in DMQ, improving contingency management and efficiency. Targeted outcomes include: 1) Acquiring 3 satellite collectors, 745 side-loading containers, and 2 water treatment plants to boost operational capacity. 2) Conducting a study and report to identify alternatives for outsourcing waste collection. 3) Developing a Project Document (ProDoc) to outline an efficiency plan and secure future support. 4) Strengthening technical capacities of EMASEO officials via training.", "Success requires: 1) Acquiring and deploying all 3 satellite collectors, 745 side-loading containers, and 2 water treatment plants, significantly boosting EMASEO EP's contingency capacity. 2) Completing a detailed study and report with actionable outsourcing alternatives. 3) Developing and approving a robust Project Document (ProDoc) outlining a viable plan for future service efficiency and sustained support. 4) Fully implementing technical capacity strengthening for EMASEO officials, leading to improved skills and overall efficiency in DMQ's waste collection services."]

Targeted outcomes: Enhance Jiaozhou Bay environment, water resources, and wetlands in Jiaozhou City/downstream, reducing pollution and flood losses. Success requires: Jiaozhou Bay surface water quality at least Class V by 2014; no flooding/poor drainage hazards; 40% reduction in waterborne disease incidence (from 2008); wastewater treatment capacity expanded by 50,000 m³/day; contribution to MDG 7, target 10 (halving safe water/sanitation access by 2015); and 3,400 construction jobs.

The targeted outcomes of the Coral Reef Rehabilitation and Management Program-Coral Triangle Initiative Project are to enable coastal communities and their supporting institutions to sustainably manage coral reef resources, associated ecosystems, and biodiversity, thereby increasing their economic and social welfare, and to enhance the capacity to manage coral reef ecosystems both inside and outside target Marine Protected Areas (MPAs). The project also aims to support the government's plan for establishing effective MPAs, improving their management, sustainability, and resilience. For the activity to be considered an overall success, by 2024, there should be sustainable management of coral reef ecosystems in the project area, evidenced by live coral cover increasing or remaining stable, and Napoleon wrasse stocks in Anambas reaching a stable population. Furthermore, household incomes of project beneficiaries should increase by 10-15%. By 2018, MPA management effectiveness for 2.75 million hectares must increase by at least one level based on government criteria (from red/yellow to green/blue), and operational management plans with business and financing strategies must be approved and implemented in all 10 target MPAs. Specific outputs contributing to success include deploying 20 community development extension workers, approving and implementing 50 national, district, and village laws/regulations related to sustainable fisheries and MPA management, and strengthening the capacity of 57 community coral reef resource management institutions, integrating them into local administrative structures, with a target of 15% women in official positions and 30% women as members. At least 30% of participants in marine-based livelihood training are also targeted to be women.

["Increased energy security and access to a higher share of electricity generated from renewable energy sources. This fosters inclusive economic growth, job creation, and environmentally sustainable growth by reducing annual CO2 emissions by 2,930 tons.", "Success requires: by end of 2018, four southern group islands fully converting energy systems from diesel to renewables; by 2020, 100% of inhabited islands converting. Key outputs include solar PV systems (780 kW by 2016 on Mangaia, Mauke, Mitiaro; 2,400 kW by 2017 on Atiu, Aitutaki, Rarotonga) and an energy efficiency policy implementation plan by 2017. Enhanced project management and institutional capacity are essential."]

Targeted Outcomes: To improve power sector performance, increase electricity supply capacity (southern/western grid to 14,135 MVA by 2024), reduce transmission losses (to 2.5% by 2024) and CO2 emissions (174,595 tons/year by 2024), enhance power transfer, and strengthen utility governance. Overall success requires: national electricity for all by 2021, uninterrupted industrial power by 2020, combined T&D losses reduced from 13% to 9%, achieving 14,135 MVA capacity, 2.5% transmission loss, 174,595 tons CO2 reduction by 2024, completion of Gopalganj 400/132 kV substation by 2020, and 126 km 230 kV line and 104 km 400 kV line by 2023, alongside a capacity program for 50 students (>=40% women) and 30 staff (>=30% women).

["The targeted outcomes are sustainable tourism and business growth in Yangon, leading to increased tax revenues, improved foreign exchange earnings, creation of opportunities for small and medium-sized businesses, and increased local sourcing of goods and services, all contributing to broader economic growth and poverty alleviation. The project also aims to increase overall business and tourism activities while preserving Myanmar's heritage, generating immediate employment and income for the poor. The district cooling plant intends to reduce cooling consumption, alleviate electrical grid strain, decrease power plant fuel consumption, provide cost savings, and reduce greenhouse gas emissions.", "Overall success requires achieving sustainable tourism and business growth in Yangon, increasing tax revenues, improving foreign exchange earnings, creating opportunities for small and medium-sized businesses, and boosting local sourcing of goods and services. Additionally, 3.9 hectares of integrated urban renewal development must be built and operating, and an energy-efficient district cooling system installed. Success tracking will use annual reports from the Ministry of Hotels and Tourism, multilateral agency reporting, annual industry reports, and development effectiveness monitoring reports. Mitigating risks like an evolving legal framework, worsening political/economic situations, permit delays, and rising costs is also essential."]

The activity targets restoring livelihoods, enhancing agricultural/other production and incomes for conflict-affected communities, and building their capacity for sustainable empowerment. It aims to improve poor communities' access to basic social/economic infrastructural services and support production activities, complementing the government's poverty-reduction strategy. Specific goals include re-establishing sustainable village-level institutional arrangements, improving village infrastructure, creating household-level employment for vulnerable groups, ensuring irrigation structure stability, and fostering a shift to high-value commercial agriculture by building knowledge and capacity.

The targeted outcomes of the Second Water Sector Investment Project (PISEAU II) are structured around three primary development objectives, aiming to improve water management and access across rural Tunisia and strengthen institutional decision-making. These objectives, along with specific success criteria, are detailed as follows:1.  Promote more efficient management and operation of selected public irrigation schemes by participating farmers: The project aims to increase the rate of irrigation intensity in project-supported schemes from a baseline of 0.8 to 1.2 irrigated hectares cultivated per irrigable hectare. It also seeks to ensure that 80% of participating farmer organizations (GDAs) in irrigation are fully functional. By 2013, 2,300 hectares of new irrigation schemes should be created and functional, 25,000 hectares of existing schemes rehabilitated and functional, and 18,000 hectares developed for drainage. Furthermore, 100% of exploitation wells should be replaced or rehabilitated, and 100% of schemes should be managed by GDAs. A functional environmental screening system for irrigation projects is also a key outcome.2.  Enhance access to and consumption of drinking water for rural households in un-served or poorly serviced communities: The goal is to significantly increase the average annual water consumption of beneficiary households, targeting 320% of the baseline (equivalent to 18 m3/capita/year) by 2013. The project also aims for 70% of participating GDAs in water supply to be fully functional. Specific targets include providing new water service to 10,000 households and improving and transferring 8 water supply systems to a water utility.3.  Empower the Ministry of Agriculture and Water Resources (MARH), the Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development (MEDD), and other stakeholders to make more informed decisions regarding integrated water resources management: A central outcome is the full operationalization of the Integrated National Water Information System (SINEAU) and its sub-systems by 2013, alongside the publication of the "Eau 2050" study in the same year. This objective also includes establishing a functional real-time groundwater surveillance system, realizing 176 boreholes and installing 100 piezometers, developing functional aquifer management models for three aquifers, completing a borehole inventory, supporting participatory management of three aquifers, and publishing an artificial aquifer recharge strategy. Additionally, functional COPEAU and soil water quality monitoring systems integrated into SINEAU, increased awareness among users about treated wastewater use in agriculture, and the undertaking of four research-action projects/themes are expected.To consider the activity an overall success, all three primary development objectives must be met by achieving the specific targets outlined in the Project Outcome Indicators and Intermediate Outcome Indicators by the project's closing date of March 31, 2015. This entails the successful creation, rehabilitation, and management of irrigation schemes as per hectare and GDA functionality targets; the expansion and improvement of rural water supply to meet household consumption and GDA functionality goals; and the full operationalization of SINEAU, publication of the "Eau 2050" study, and successful implementation of groundwater and environmental monitoring and institutional strengthening activities. Success also depends on adhering to the common procedures and indicators agreed upon by all lenders and proactively addressing any slow progress through additional supervision and dialogue with the government.

The primary targeted outcome is to support the Government of Vietnam (GoV) in addressing climate change by adopting policies and strengthening institutional capacity to promote climate resilient and lower carbon intensity development. This involves four policy goals: 1) Improving the resilience of water resources, 2) Exploiting energy efficiency potentials to lower carbon intensity, 3) Strengthening capacity to formulate, prioritize, and implement climate change policies, and 4) Strengthening the financing framework for climate change action. Expected results include strengthened legal/organizational arrangements for water management, implemented energy efficiency practices in industrial sectors, improved scientific/monitoring capacity for climate actions, and climate finance planning. The DPO also aims to enhance preparedness/adaptation, improve water/energy efficiency, and reduce carbon emissions.

The activity aims to achieve macro-fiscal stability, improved public expenditure efficiency (via strengthened fiscal policy, reduced deficits, better revenue/expenditure management, and SOE oversight), and poverty reduction through efficient social protection. It also targets improved infrastructure services, a better investment climate, enhanced labor force skills, well-functioning labor markets, and stronger environmental protection to foster a competitive, private-sector-led economy.

["The activity targets two primary outcomes: (i) supporting long-term fiscal sustainability and strengthening fiscal resilience by ensuring adherence to fiscal rules, improving customs efficiency, enhancing SOE transparency and accountability, building fiscal buffers via a contingency fund, ensuring debt sustainability, and strengthening wage bill management; and (ii) supporting Grenada's transition to a Blue Economy by strengthening marine and coastal management, marine ecosystem health, and climate resilience, which includes restoring marine health, improving climate resilience in planning, and addressing non-biodegradable products.", "Overall success would be achieved when: the Contingency Fund aggregates EC$ 10 million in inflows; the real public wage bill increase is less than 9 percent; 5 customs post-clearance audits are successfully targeted; 100% of SOEs follow the new monitoring framework; contingent liabilities are quantified in the Fiscal Risk Statement; Marine Protected Areas cover 20% of Grenada's territory; the Sustainable Development Trust Fund has 2 revenue sources; import volume of Styrofoam food containers, single-use plastic bags, plates, forks, and spoons is zero; 100% of new building applications align with amended codes; and 25% of government contracts for goods adhere to sustainability requirements."]

The targeted outcomes of the activity, known at its outset, aimed to ensure the continuous, technically and financially efficient supply of safe water to the population in selected centers, alongside reducing unaccounted-for water. At the impact level, objectives included contributing to protecting scarce water resources and mitigating health risks from water-related diseases. For success, the following targeted outcomes were necessary: unaccounted-for water reduced to <5% in transport pipelines and <20% in distribution networks (one year post-commissioning); full use of rehabilitated and newly built production, transport, and distribution capacities (peak load, three years post-commissioning); supplied water quality consistently meeting Moroccan standards (three years post-commissioning); maximum investment cost recovery within 14 years; and peak daily demand consistently covered, even in critical dry years. Additionally, selected locations required an expected return on investment <14 years and a minimum internal rate of return of 5% p.a.

The activity targeted securing power supply infrastructure, integrating renewable energies, improving energy efficiency, and contributing to CO2 emission reduction and economic development. Specifically, it aimed to close the 110 kV circular main, eliminate transmission bottlenecks, reduce load shedding, stabilize voltage, and improve electricity supply in Southern Albania, leading to reduced power cuts and voltage fluctuations. This was expected to enhance production and investment conditions, benefit tourism, and foster sustainable economic and social development. Success would be achieved if annual availability of new transmission capacity reached ">"=98%, deviation from 110 kV standard voltage was maximum 5%, and power transmission losses were 2%.

The activity targeted improving structural and institutional framework conditions for implementing environmental reforms, enhancing environmental protection, biodiversity conservation, and climate change adaptation (SDGs 13 & 15). Specific targets included developing/utilizing a climate information system (SNICC, 100% software development), financing/implementing climate adaptation plans, implementing protected area policies, and establishing deforestation-free value chains. Success was contingent on fulfilling 14 specific triggers for loan disbursement, absence of budget constraints, high governmental reform willingness, improved standing of environmental authorities, cohesive national government operations, continuous ownership of macro issues, and mitigation of opposition/risks.

Targeted outcomes: Improved resilience for 5M extreme poor (1.1M households) and ~1.2M newly vulnerable urban individuals (~300K households) via cash transfers, protecting livelihoods, nutrition, and mitigating income loss from COVID-19; Enhanced TASAF capacity for self-sustaining social protection, robust financial management, effective targeting, and future shock response; Promote women's economic empowerment, increase incomes, improve consumption/food security, boost resilience, prevent poverty reversal. Success: Achieving specified resilience for beneficiaries, TASAF's demonstrable self-sufficiency/effective operation, sustained poverty reduction gains, and successful lobbying for increased contributions.

Targeted outcomes include helping India achieve its Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) by fostering tech innovations leading to job creation, improved access to services, and inclusive economic growth. The fund aims to directly support 150-200 tech start-ups (including AI, digital security, quantum computing), creating ~3,000 jobs, mobilising ~£750m in new investments, and benefiting 5 million individuals. It also seeks to leverage UK tech/expertise, foster UK-India partnerships, promote responsible investment, gender/diversity, forge a strong UK-India Tech Partnership, and contribute to ICF indicators, while maintaining high ESG standards.

The Global Fund for Coral Reefs (GFCR) targets four main outcomes: 1. Protecting priority coral reef sites and climate change-affected refugia. 2. Transforming the livelihoods of coral reef-dependent communities. 3. Developing restoration and adaptation technologies. 4. Supporting the recovery of coral reef-dependent communities from major shocks. These objectives aim to safeguard valuable coral reefs, which support 25% of marine life and benefit over 1 billion people, while also addressing a significant finance gap for their protection.

The targeted outcomes include: formulating and submitting a practical, well-informed National Climate Change Capacity Building Project proposal to UNDP, building on existing climate change activities and institutional settings; identifying linkages with UNDAF/CP Outcomes; delivering a detailed, nationally-owned workplan for the future project; establishing frameworks for LEDS, GHG inventory, NAMAs, and MRV, with identified gaps and new work areas; defining appropriate methodologies; establishing synergies; and assessing institutional/technical capacity building needs. Success requires the proposal to be consensus-agreed by stakeholders, submitted, and approved by UNDP, establishing a robust, nationally-owned foundation for the subsequent Programme.

["The project aims to promote and implement integrated low-carbon urban systems in Ashgabat and Awaza, reducing GHG emissions and generating other environmental, social, and economic benefits. Targeted outcomes include improved capacities and enabling conditions in Ashgabat and Awaza for identifying, designing, and implementing low-carbon/climate-resilient solutions in public spaces and for tourism; reduced GHG emissions and other negative environmental impacts (e.g., 20-30% energy savings from smart lighting, curtailed transport emissions); and nationwide replication and scaling-up of results, with enhanced agency capacity and policy adoption (e.g., green urban practices, fuel efficiency norms).", "Overall success will be achieved by effectively addressing the root causes through capacity building in urban sustainability planning and design, developing and adopting national policies and regulations (e.g., for green urban practices, fuel efficiency), investing in necessary infrastructure (e.g., smart grids, optimized waste facilities), fostering public awareness and behavioral change. Crucially, success requires a measurable reduction of urban growth's negative impacts (GHG emissions, pollution, waste) while advancing socio-economic goals, and the nationwide replication and scaling-up of project results and best practices."]

["The activity targets protecting human security and enhancing Moldova's resilience to socio-economic, food, and energy shocks. Specific objectives include enhancing people's energy security through affordable energy access, livelihoods support, and energy-efficient production; strengthening food security by assisting the government with its Food Security Strategy and capacitating agricultural producers; and improving community security by capacitating police and community actors to foster social cohesion and peaceful coexistence. It also aims for transformative change, resilience to external shocks, self-reliance for refugees/host communities, social cohesion, and prioritizing women's vulnerabilities.", "Overall success requires achieving all three specific objectives. This includes: increasing solid biofuel production efficiency (26 companies), enhancing Biofuel Association advocacy/capacities (webpage, platforms, legislative support, articles), 1,439 households with biomass heating, operational remote monitoring and biomass support call center, 715 trained boiler operators, operational online trading platform, 35,000 beneficiaries of energy awareness campaigns (including 1k women), 5k trained public/community members, adoption of energy-efficient agri-technologies; strengthening agricultural resilience for food security and traceability; and supporting evidence-based food policy-making. Furthermore, capacitating police/community actors for social cohesion is crucial."]

The targeted outcomes of the WAPP APL4 (Phase 1) activity are twofold, aligning with its two sub-projects: (1) to reduce the cost of and increase the electricity supply at the utility level in Liberia, Sierra Leone, and Guinea, and concurrently increase the export capability of Côte d'Ivoire, and (2) to increase the technical integration of the WAPP network. Success would be measured by: (a) an annual electricity supply from CLSG imports reaching 300 GWh (100 GWh for each of Liberia, Sierra Leone, and Guinea), (b) a reduction in the annual weighted cost of electricity supply at the power utility level in Liberia and Sierra Leone to 0.30 USD/kWh, (c) Côte d'Ivoire's export capability to CLSG countries increasing to 200 MW, (d) a total of 5.2 million direct project beneficiaries (with 52% female), (e) WAPP Master Plan Priority projects prepared to financial closure, and (f) an improved ratio of annual total hours of synchronization of WAPP Zone A against total hours within the TCN-CEB-GRIDCO interconnection.

["The Cusco Regional Development project aims to improve tourism quality and solid waste management, while increasing the tourism sector's resilience to natural disasters in Calca, Urubamba, and Cusco. Key outcomes include: consolidating tourist attractions through infrastructure upgrades; enhancing commercialization of tourism products; developing small/micro-enterprises; establishing improved solid waste collection, transport, and disposal systems (including three new landfills); implementing early warning systems for natural disasters and risk mitigation strategies; and strengthening institutional capacity for tourism operations, project management, and M&E. It anticipates reducing public health risks, eliminating informal dumpsite damages, boosting tourist arrivals, and increasing private sector profits.", "Overall success will be measured by specific indicators. These include a 30% reduction in visitor dissatisfaction with improved tourist sites, the number of direct project beneficiaries (including women), the number of people (tourists and residents) receiving improved solid waste collection and disposal services via new landfills, and the number of people covered by operational natural disaster early warning systems. Effective implementation of environmental and social safeguards and the operational success of planned infrastructure and enterprise development initiatives are also crucial for project success."]

The activity targets improving water resources information extent, quality, and accessibility, and strengthening management institutions' capacity in India. Outcomes include modernizing monitoring, establishing hydromet data systems, web-enabled WRISs, analytical tools for forecasting/operations, and enhanced human capacity. Specific aims cover better irrigation design/operation, water quality, and groundwater management. Success requires: 12,000 online monitoring stations, 50 information products, 25 institutions reaching benchmark levels, 50% WRIS user satisfaction, 30 functional data centers, 900k WRIS page views, 30 regular water availability reports, 325 improved streamflow forecasting stations, and 2,000 trained professionals, leading to a shift to IWRM, improved water allocation, and better drought/flood risk management with socioeconomic benefits.

The targeted outcomes of the Jharkhand Power System Improvement Project are to increase the transmission capacity of the electricity network in Jharkhand and strengthen the institutional capacity of state-owned power transmission and distribution utilities. These contribute to increasing electricity availability, spurring productive growth, enhancing quality of life, and promoting inclusive growth and sustainable development in Jharkhand. For overall success, the project aims to achieve: 1. An increase in electricity wheeled through the JUSNL network from a 2018 baseline of 9,705.00 GWh to 18,792.00 GWh by 2024. 2. An increase in the transformation capacity of the power transmission network by 2,400,000.00 kVA from a 2018 baseline of 0.00. 3. An increased percentage of consumers serviced through an upgraded commercial system. 4. Strengthened Financial, Procurement, and Contract management framework in JUSNL.

Targeted outcomes include increasing electricity delivery and system capacity, improving operational efficiency and reliability, meeting growing demand, reducing AT&C losses and distribution transformer failure rates, providing 24x7 power to households and access for unconnected consumers, and reducing poverty and GHG emissions. Success criteria are: electricity supply reaching 74,000 GWh; AT&C losses reducing to 9.25% in Kurnool and 15.5% in Anantapur; increased transmission capacity; decreased transformer failure rates; 20.0% economic rate of return; and 0.9 million tons CO2e reduction.

The activity targets two main outcomes. Pillar 1 aims to improve fiscal management by mobilizing tax revenues, enhancing tax collection efficiency, public investment spending, and debt management. Pillar 2 seeks to strengthen the financial viability and service delivery in key infrastructure sectors, specifically energy and Information and Communication Technology (ICT), by fostering competition and regulation.

The project aims to strengthen institutional capacity for climate resilience and market efficiency in Brazil's energy and mining sectors, establishing climate-smart institutions, efficient markets, and effective policies. In the energy sector, it seeks to boost upstream investment, monetize pre-salt resources, achieve affordable domestic energy, support Distributed Energy Resources (DER) adaptation, and establish an open, competitive natural gas market, reducing prices by 40% and attracting investment. It will modernize the electricity sector, focusing on climate resilience, dispatch rules, market design, renewable/gas integration, and price formation to reduce costs. In mining, it aims to improve ANM's regulatory capacity and IT platforms for better management, safety, and introduce climate-smart mining. Overall, it enhances energy security, efficiency, and sustainability, fostering economic growth and job creation.

The activity targets enhancing Ukrainian power grid flexibility through storage investments and market expansion to support timely synchronization with ENTSO-E by end of 2023 and power sector decarbonization. Key outcomes include increasing battery storage capacity and available Frequency Containment Reserve (FCR), reducing variable renewable energy curtailment, ensuring reliable, secure, and clean electricity supply at lower costs, and improving energy security. It also aims to enable more EU electricity trades, introduce green ancillary services, boost renewable generation, expand storage markets, improve resilience, and foster private sector participation, yielding significant economic savings.

Targeted outcomes include reducing pressure on wildlife from illegal trade, alleviating poverty, creating a high-risk/low-reward IWT environment, and empowering law enforcement with innovative measures. Quantitative targets for Round 7 include 600-1000 law enforcement trainees, 80-130 criminal cases, 1.5-2m improved awareness, and 4000-7000 alternative livelihoods. Success hinges on demonstrable reductions in illegally killed animals and poverty, transformative fund impact, effective law enforcement, community empowerment, operational excellence (VfM, scalability, merit-based selection, equity), and leveraging additional funding for sustainable, system-wide change.

Targeted outcomes: Sustainable water production (San Mart
 plant) of 3.2 million m
/day; 2 turbidity non-standard days/year; 97% inspectable underground river length. Serving 280,000 inhabitants (West Tigre Collector) and 26,471 new West zone households with sanitation; 3.15 m
/s secondary treated wastewater. AySA operational performance targets include 19,800 m
/day water savings/recovery; 5 annual network interventions/km/year; 100% cost recovery. Success requires achieving all these targets, ensuring effective financial management/accurate reporting (resolving discrepancies), and implementing institutional strengthening for sustainability.

The activity targets increasing collected and treated sewage flow to 1,540.00 L/s in ETE Alegria and 515.00 L/s in ETE Alcântara. It aims to connect 260,000 households to secondary sewage treatment (188,365 in ETE Alegria, 45,495 in ETE Alcântara, 26,140 in Baixada Fluminense). Other targets include adjusting 8 contracts, updating 1 monitoring plan, and approving 1 water loss reduction plan. Environmental quality goals involve reducing BOD5 and increasing DO at Canal do Mangue, Alcântara, Pavuna, and Sarapuí plants, and removing 45 tons of BOD5/day. Infrastructure targets include specific lengths of collector and network construction. The program also seeks social equity, poverty reduction, and environmental sustainability. Success requires achieving all these objectives within the original timeframe (by June 30, 2019) and budget (US$639.55 million), necessitating a considerable and sustained acceleration in implementation, effective cost monitoring, and maintaining project viability.

Targeted outcomes include enhancing operating efficiency by reducing technical losses, improving power supply continuity/quality (via reduced SAIFI/SAIDI), increasing EDESUR's capacity to meet growing demand, and fostering economic empowerment for women. Overall success requires increasing energy supplied by EDESUR from 5,010.4 GWh (2017) to 6,753 GWh in four years, verified by EDESUR Management Control Reports. Additionally, success depends on increased average service availability, improved demand compliance, and the installation of 105,000 control UBM meters.

Targeted outcomes include strengthening the Dominican Republic's cocoa value chain to improve farmers' livelihoods and sustainable production, transforming cocoa farming into a business of choice, empowering men, women, and youth via entrepreneurship, and protecting/restoring land/forests. It also aims to elevate socio-environmental sustainability, resilience to climate change, and promote sustainable consumption/production. Success requires Cocoa Life partners (male/female) adequately implementing projects with sustainable practices, meeting annual targets (e.g., 3 partners by 2019, 2 by 2023). Effective monitoring, communication, and capacity building ensuring equitable outcomes and national goal alignment are also crucial.

The activity aims to advance poverty eradication, contribute to SDGs 6 & 14, and improve global/local water & ocean governance through policy, capacity, and innovative solutions. Targeted outcomes include strengthening national/sub-national capacities for basic services, promoting sustainable natural resource management (incl. shared water ecosystems), strengthening gender-responsive frameworks for resource use, and applying nature-based, gender-responsive recovery solutions. Overall success is measured by countries reporting progress in multi-stakeholder development effectiveness monitoring frameworks, meeting UNDP Strategic Plan Output Indicators, and achieving specific project outputs on thought leadership and framework strengthening, all adhering to S.M.A.R.T. IATI standards.

The targeted outcomes of the Khulna Sewerage System Development Project include establishing a sustainable and inclusive sewerage system in Khulna, ensuring basic needs are met amid rapid urbanization, strengthening institutional capacity in delivering sewage management services, and increasing public awareness regarding safe sanitation services. To consider the activity an overall success, the following would need to be achieved by 2028: service coverage of sewage collection and treatment through the centralized sewerage system reaching 50% of households (about 120,000 households) in the urban area of Khulna city; service coverage of fecal sludge treatment in fecal sludge treatment plants increasing to 15% of households (about 30,000 households) in the urban area; and 100% of the Khulna Water Supply and Sewerage Authority's (KWASA) operation and maintenance (O&M) costs being financed by user charges.

The activity targets efficient, inclusive, and sustainable Green Agenda implementation in Serbia, improving policy frameworks, and supporting pilot projects. Outcomes include developing local low-carbon development policies, aligning national EE/RE regulatory acts with EU norms, strengthening permitting for IPPC/IED operators, and fostering circular economy practices in waste management. It aims to improve air quality through regulatory recommendations and local action plans, and establish a National Afforestation Body with pilot restoration plans. Furthermore, it provides seed co-financing and technical assistance for innovative decarbonization, renewable energy solutions, green industrial transitions, circular economy projects, and depollution measures, enhancing Serbia's green transition and EU accession.

["Targeted outcomes include increasing Rokhia power plant's generation capacity to 120 MW with 53% efficiency, reducing electricity costs, and reducing AT&C losses through smart metering and distribution strengthening. The project aims to modernize 27 substations, install 1,560 km covered conductors, 140 km underground, and 900 km aerial bunched cables, and establish modern monitoring. It will deploy smart meters in 100,000 households, improve financial viability of utilities, enhance institutional capacity with a gender-sensitive workplace policy, and promote energy-based livelihoods for 150 women from 15 SHGs.", "Overall success requires the Rokhia power plant to achieve 120 MW capacity with 53% operational efficiency, ensuring incremental capacity meets unserved demand and generates 418,652 tons annual GHG reduction. Distribution network strengthening should reduce AT&C losses to 12%-15% by FY2025. Smart meter deployment must improve collection rates and financial viability. Institutional capacity is strengthened by a gender-sensitive workplace policy and increased staff knowledge on gender issues. Livelihood activities must enable 150 women from 15 SHGs to acquire certified skills and receive equipment, enhancing energy security and power supply reliability for consumers."]

The targeted outcomes are rural vitalization in the YREB and an improved living environment in rural areas along the Gan River. Key outputs involve enhanced institutional capacity for environmental management, piloted green development and financing, improved rural waste and sanitation management, and improved water/soil conservation practices. Success requires economic viability (EIRR > 9.0%), financial sustainability where the Ganzhou Municipal Government covers debt/O&M, and meeting output targets like 15 new rural wastewater systems, 100km ecological river embankments, and 5,000 ha afforested forest areas by 2028.

["The Coastal Towns Climate Resilience Project aims to strengthen the climate and disaster resilience of 22 coastal towns by enhancing their ability to anticipate, absorb, accommodate, and recover from climate shocks and stresses. This involves improving priority municipal infrastructure and essential services for climate resilience and local economic development, enhancing livelihood resilience and adaptive capacity of vulnerable households through a graduation program in six high-poverty towns, and strengthening institutional capacity, governance, and climate awareness for integrated urban development.", "The activity will be considered an overall success by 2030 if: the capacity of EWCD-friendly cyclone shelters increases for 10,000 people with enhanced safety features and spaces for 5,000 women; at least 75% of households in project areas report reduced inundation for less than three days; livelihoods are improved for 1,500 women in six project pourashavas; and 85% holding tax collection efficiency is achieved in 20 pourashavas. These metrics indicate the desired strengthening of climate resilience and benefits to vulnerable populations."]

Targeted outcomes: Enhanced livelihood resilience for pastoral/agro-pastoral communities in drought-prone areas, and improved government crisis response. Outcomes include: secure access to sustainably managed trans-boundary natural resources (water, land, conflict resolution); improved market access for livestock/products (infrastructure, info systems, policies); enhanced livelihoods (livestock health/production, diversification); and strengthened drought preparedness/response (EWS). Success requires: reduced livestock death rates; increased livestock traded (number/value); reduced early warning to response time; increased direct beneficiaries (female percentage); positive environmental impacts; and strengthened community/government resilience.

The Tamil Nadu Sustainable Urban Development Project targets improving urban services delivery sustainably and piloting better urban management. Outcomes include benefitting 2 million urban residents (40% female) via enhanced water, wastewater, storm drainage (reducing flooding), and city roads. It aims for strengthened governance, financial sustainability, and improved urban management in 'Model Cities' (ULB empowerment, spatial planning, sustainable finances, e-governance). It also seeks increased economic returns, cost-recovery, and long-term sustainability of capital programs through governance reforms and technical assistance.

["The primary objective is to enhance and diversify household income in select farm and non-farm sectors for targeted beneficiaries. This will be achieved by: (a) mobilizing and aggregating rural producers (SC/ST, smallholders, women/men) into Producer Groups (PGs) and Producer Organizations (POs), focusing on production system diversification/intensification and improved value chain participation; (b) strengthening competitive advantage through climate-resilient production techniques, value addition, and effective market linkages; and (c) improving access to financing. Higher-level outcomes include increasing farmers' net income, conserving natural resources, and reducing gender gaps in agriculture, financial inclusion, and food/nutrition security.", "Overall success requires a 50% increase in the average real annual household income of targeted beneficiaries. Key success indicators include: at least 50% of total production sold through producer collectives, operating at least three private sector partnerships, and a 50% increase in the sale volume of select Non-Timber Forest Products. Additionally, success involves training 11,000 Community Service Providers to earn at least Rs 3,500/month and 3,000 entrepreneurs. Long-term success also depends on achieving quicker income growth, household resilience, and risk management through multi-sectoral diversification, robust M&E, and sustained government commitment."]

The Transmission Efficiency Project (TEP) targets improving capacity, efficiency, and reliability of electricity transmission services. Key outcomes include increasing additional transfer capacity to 80% by 2019, reducing average O&M expenses to $0.83/MWh by 2019, and decreasing average fault duration (e.g., North Red River to 203.88 minutes by 2019). Institutional strengthening aims for 100% annual transmission tariff applications by 2017, 100% in-house IFRS financial statements by 2016, and a fully operational Asset Management System by 2019. Additionally, 60% of affected households should have women participate in compensation consultations by 2017. Overall success requires achieving these specific targets to alleviate congestion, reduce unserved demand, improve operational margins, and enhance the overall transmission network performance and reliability.

The activity aims to strengthen public financial management and improve the environment for inclusive growth. Outcomes include better RERF management, public debt monitoring, procurement, fisheries JV oversight, expanded competitive telecommunications for outer islands, and improved affordable electricity/water services for the poor. Success is achieving: RERF return <=0.25% below benchmark & costs <=0.1%; comprehensive debt database; operational CPU; all fisheries JVs offer value; Tarawa mobile penetration 50% (2+ operators), outer islands 20%; PUB improves (90% billing, <=15% power loss, SAIFI 6, SAIDI 880); and 70% poor Tarawa households benefit from new electricity tariffs.

["The Project Development Objective (PDO) is to enhance market access, water access, and sanitation, adopting climate resilient approaches for targeted beneficiaries in selected areas of Ceará. Specific targets include a 15% increase in gross value of sales for participating organizations, 7,000 farmers adopting improved agricultural technology, 94,500 people with access to improved water sources, and 19,845 people with improved sanitation. Broader outcomes include strengthened family agriculture with increased incomes, skilled producer organizations, and strengthened public institutions.", "Overall success hinges on achieving the PDO targets and intermediate results. This includes 390 subprojects for market access, 40 for vulnerable groups, 13,000 farmers reached, 60% orgs strengthening market access, 2,500 trained beneficiaries, 200 water reuse systems, 165 WSS in operation, 40% improved technologies, 5,250 sanitary kits, EMATERCE restructuring, and 400 staff trained. Critical assumptions must hold: rural producers implementing subprojects, meeting funding, market demand, improved org capacity, effective inter-agency coordination, political commitment, available climate-resilient tech, adequate technical assistance, and a supportive legal framework."]

The project targets increased agricultural productivity, evidenced by higher crop yields and mobilization of 4,497 ha of irrigable land; enhanced market access for small producers and agribusinesses through increased sales volume, improved transport, storage, and market information; strengthened agri-food value chain competitiveness via improved advisory services, producer organizations, value addition, and facilitated access to finance; promotion of sustainable/climate-resilient agriculture (e.g., water-efficient irrigation, climate-smart practices); improved land tenure; and inclusive development for women and youth.

The activity aims to improve quality of life in Belo Horizonte, focusing on the Bonsucesso watershed, by reducing flood risk, restoring waterway quality, and ensuring sustainable urban environmental management. Specific targets include: increasing sewerage service coverage to 90.7%; eliminating 12 flood points; intercepting 4,670 kg DBO/day contaminant load; reducing irregular solid waste disposal to under 100 m³/year; and improving the Environmental Health Index to 0.91-0.96. It also targets constructing 7.75km macrodrainage and 5.26km microdrainage channels, establishing 5.40ha linear parks and 1.00ha social use areas, building 4.41km bank-side roads, and relocating 1,130 families in the Bonsucesso watershed. For overall success, the activity requires meeting all quantitative targets and fulfilling critical conditions. Conditions precedent include: legislative creation and staffing of a program executing unit (PEU), hiring social/environmental consultants and a works management firm, and approval of the Program Operating Manual. Execution conditions involve: signing a legal instrument for sewerage works maintenance between MBH and COPASA; submitting the Environmental and Social Management Plan and Expropriation, Compensation, and Resettlement Plan (PDR) for Bank’s no objection; providing prior evidence of duly expropriated properties, compensated affected parties, and covered families according to the PDR; and hiring a works supervision firm before commencing works. Continued satisfactory implementation of the broader DRENURBS program is also essential.

The activity targets an enabling environment for low carbon urban transport, aiming for 69,013 tCO2e GHG reduction by Dec 2020. Key outcomes include effective policy enforcement, a low carbon vehicle Master Plan, and adoption/implementation of low carbon transport plans in 4 major cities by 2020. It also targets increased private sector participation/investment, capacitating 2 planning institutions, establishing 2 centers of excellence, accrediting 2 technician training institutions, and deploying 100,000 E-trikes to reduce petroleum consumption by 2.8% and achieve 79% CO2 footprint avoidance.

["The activity aims to provide rural water supply and sanitation facilities to 1,000 communities in 20 districts and 500 communities in Aceh/Nias, serving an estimated 1.2 million people with safe drinking water and 0.6 million with improved sanitation. It targets improved health status, quality of life, and sustained access to water/sanitation, aligning with MDG 7 (halving people without access by 2015) and MDG 4 (reducing child mortality due to waterborne diseases by two-thirds by 2015). By 2010, key outcomes include 50% of households with improved water, 30% with improved sanitation, 50% adopting improved hygiene, reduced diarrhea, and less time collecting water. Other targets include reduced poverty, increased community empowerment, strengthened local government capacity, and 100% SHBC program reach by 2010 with 80% recall.", "Overall success requires the establishment of a health sector information system. Communities must be committed to the project, and the project must generate enough momentum to sustain the use of new facilities and changed hygiene behavior. Key conditions also include district governments retaining responsibility for basic social services with adequate funding for decentralized mandates, the central government acting as facilitator/regulator, and strengthened internal controls within the Executing Agency to prevent fraud. Success also depends on identifying and promoting factors that motivate communities to sustain hygiene and sanitation changes."]

The project targets enhancing urban quality of life and economic competitiveness in the Hai River Basin by alleviating water resource degradation. Key outcomes include increasing wastewater collection and treatment to over 70%, reducing reliance on polluted groundwater, improving water quality in canal/river systems and surface/groundwater, and providing adequate public water supplies to 98% of urban residents. It also aims for sustainable, commercially managed water/wastewater utility services, benefiting 1.5 million urban residents, reducing waterborne disease by 40%, and creating employment.

["The activity targets addressing environmental legacy issues from open ash dumping, enabling KEK to free land for community development, facilitating Kosovo A ash dump removal, and building KEK's capacity for ongoing clean-up and environmentally sound mining. It aims to improve environmental conditions, promote sustainability, and attract strategic investors. Additionally, it seeks to strengthen Kosovo's legal and institutional framework for environmental regulation of mining/power projects, build MESP capacity for implementation/monitoring/enforcement, develop a resettlement framework, and implement a pilot reclamation project to free land for resettlement.", "Overall success requires the cessation of ash disposal at Kosovo A and its redirection to Mirash mine (100%), elimination of groundwater pollution from ashes, reduction of dust, successful reclamation of ~6.5 km² land for habitats/agriculture/resettlement, decrease in property loss from geotechnical instabilities (0 houses at risk), and a measurable increase in KEK/institutional capacity for sound environmental mining. Specifics include 100% Mirash mine readiness for ash disposal, complete redirection of ash flow, full removal of Kosovo A ash dumps, and initiation of land reclamation. Training of PMU/KEK/MESP staff and KEK fully assuming clean-up responsibility are also key."]

The project aims to (i) improve public management and regulatory capacity, enhancing transparency; and (ii) strengthen local technical skills in Ghana's oil and gas sector. Overall success requires meeting key targets: training 60 MoE and 80 Regulator staff, 30 EPA staff in sector management; publicly releasing oil/gas contracts by YR2. It also requires 1000 RMU, 500 TTI, 200 KTI students, and 300 KNUST petroleum engineering students to complete training, achieving 1270 direct beneficiaries (10% female). Intermediate success includes upgraded MoE offices, outfitted regulator offices, modern GNPC data repository, and equipped EPA for environmental response. High government commitment to transparency and external support are crucial.

Targeted outcomes include increasing installed capacity (MW) and electricity production (MWh) from the Noor-Ouarzazate Solar Complex, especially during peak hours. Broad goals are enhancing energy security, reducing fossil fuel dependence, and limiting CO2 emissions (506,000 tonnes avoided). The project aims to contribute to Morocco's 2000 MW solar capacity by 2020, develop a local solar industry, create jobs, and foster global CSP technology development, including cost reduction and macroeconomic spillovers.

The primary targeted outcomes of the National Transmission Modernization I Project are to increase the capacity and reliability of selected segments of the national transmission system in Pakistan and to modernize key business processes of the National Transmission and Despatch Company (NTDC). Specifically, the project targets a reduction in the average duration of forced outages per substation from 1689 minutes to 676 minutes, and a decrease in the average frequency of forced outages per project substation from 2.56 times to 1.28 times. For the four substations converted to Gas Insulated Switchgear (GIS), the average duration of forced outages is targeted to reduce from 10,723 minutes to 1,082 minutes, and the average frequency from 34 times to 5 times. Additionally, the project aims to add a maximum of 12,516,000 kilovolt-ampere of substation capacity to the system from a baseline of zero, and reduce the time for submitting audited financial statements from a baseline of 14 months to an end target of 6 months. The project also aims to contribute to Pakistan's higher-level development objectives by improving energy supplies through investments and reforms in generation, transmission, and distribution, connecting lower-cost resources, enabling higher load factors, ensuring improved dispatch efficiency, and reducing losses. It will also contribute to improving Pakistan's Doing Business ranking, achieving structural and cost transformation in the energy sector, reducing load shedding, lowering electricity production costs, improving financial sustainability, supporting power system growth, and Pakistan's transition to a low greenhouse gas emissions path. The beneficiaries will experience improved power supply, reduced unserved demand, and fewer and shorter power outages, while NTDC will benefit from improved financial and accounting systems, management, operations, higher productivity, and upgraded staff skills. For the activity to be considered an overall success, these specific objectives and ambitious targets must be met. This will critically depend on diligent risk management and robust institutional support from NTDC and other stakeholders, given the identified 'Substantial' risks in Political and Governance, Sector Strategies and Policies, Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability, Fiduciary, and Environment and Social categories.

The targeted outcomes of the One WASH—Consolidated Water Supply, Sanitation, and Hygiene Account Project (One WASH—CWA) are to increase access to safe water supply, sanitation, and hygiene services and strengthen capacity for water resources management and service delivery.Specific targets for project success include providing 3,012,000 people with access to safe drinking water services (2,015,000 rural, 997,000 urban, 1,506,000 female beneficiaries) and 1,893,000 people with access to safe sanitation services (1,451,000 in rural areas). Furthermore, the project aims for 80% of kebeles in project woredas to be declared open defecation free, 75% of water schemes to be functional and supply safe drinking water, and 75% of rural and urban water supply scheme designs to use data from the water resources management information system. Success also involves improving hygiene behavior, strengthening service provider capacity, enhancing water resource management data use, and making drought and flood early warning systems enhanced and operational.

The Fostering and Leveraging Opportunities for Water Security Program aims to strengthen national capacity for managing Kosovo's water resources and improve water security in the Morava e Binces basin through integrated land and water resource management. Targeted outcomes include improved capacity to plan and manage water resources, enhanced water availability, increased storage, improved irrigation, greater flood resilience, and increased water supply for residential/industrial use. Success is measured by: approved National Water Resources Investment Plan; strengthened institutional capacity for basin management with stakeholder engagement; updated spatial, hydro-met, and climate data (20 stations); Morava e Binces basin with approved management plan, agreed coordination, increased irrigation/sustainable land area, more people with improved water access (95% of Hidromorava customers with >=18hrs supply); and 25,000 people with dam early warning access.

The Liberia Electricity Sector Strengthening and Access Project (LESSAP) aims to increase electricity access and improve LEC's operational efficiency. Targeted outcomes include providing new/improved electricity services to people, electrifying 200 health facilities via solar, regularizing illegal connections, and implementing an optimal LEC structure. Success requires achieving these PDO targets, connecting 170,000+ households, reducing LEC's commercial losses from 63% to 30%, increasing annual energy consumption to 533 GWh, and ensuring financial sustainability with stakeholder support for affordable electricity.

The China Plastic Waste Reduction Project targets several key outcomes. Overall, the project aims to improve plastic waste management at both national and sub-national levels, thereby significantly reducing plastics pollution originating from municipal solid waste and decreasing plastic waste leakages into the world's oceans.Specific targeted outcomes include:1.  **Policy and Institutional Strengthening**: Developing and implementing forward-looking policy directions, establishing robust policy frameworks, and strengthening institutional setups for plastic pollution control and recycling at the national level. This involves creating concrete measures to ban certain single-use plastic items, promoting biodegradable plastic substitutes, improving standards for waste and plastic management (including monitoring, reporting, and supervision), and fostering eco-design and material recycling to reduce plastic consumption, with an emphasis on recycling and utilization of renewable resources. It also addresses institutional fragmentation in waste management service planning and delivery.2.  **Enhanced Waste Management Systems**: Improving municipal solid waste management (MSWM) systems, particularly in Ningbo and Chongqing. This encompasses improving the top-level design of waste classification using advanced technologies (e.g., blockchain, AI, IoT, 5G), eliminating kitchen waste bags, establishing traceability and incentive systems for accurate domestic waste classification, and promoting intelligent application of waste classification through integrated management platforms to improve collection and transportation efficiency. It also aims to establish grid-based renewable resource sorting centers and enhance Resource Utilization Park functions, including centralized deep processing and utilization bases for plastic waste.3.  **Pollution Reduction**: A direct outcome is the reduction of plastics pollution from municipal solid waste and, consequently, the reduction of marine plastic debris.4.  **Beneficiary Impact**: Providing better SWM services, cleaner living environments, and a lower risk of plastic waste pollution for 10.2 million people in Chongqing and 3 million urban residents in Ningbo.5.  **Global Contributions**: Contributing to global public goods, specifically in terms of plastic marine debris control and climate change mitigation by abating greenhouse gas emissions from improved waste management and recycling.To consider the activity an overall success, the project must demonstrably achieve its primary development objectives: significant improvement in plastic waste management at national and sub-national levels, and a measurable reduction in plastics pollution from municipal solid waste. Success would also be indicated by the full implementation and integration of the proposed solutions, leading to more efficient waste classification, collection, and recycling systems. Furthermore, the project's success hinges on realizing its intended positive impacts for the direct beneficiaries, including improved living conditions and reduced exposure to plastic pollution, and making tangible contributions to reducing marine plastic debris and mitigating GHG emissions on a global scale.

Targeted outcomes: enhanced climate resilience of forests in water supply areas, restoration of 10,664 ha pine forest and 28,400 ha agroforestry systems, 904 ha deforestation avoided, 31% reduction in fire-affected areas, and 8,531 ha optimal density in private forests. Governance aims for 188,836 ha with CES mechanisms, one constituted Water Fund, and 29% increased women's participation in forest associations. Success requires: interagency framework agreement (SEFIN-ICF), PMU team designation, Operating Regulations approval (with ESMP), MOUs for CES sustainability, and signed agreements for CES implementation (Subcomponent II.2). Robust M&E and impact evaluation are crucial.

The activity aims to reduce poverty, foster sustainable forest management, and improve local populations' income and living conditions (targeting 650,000 people). Success requires: human poverty index reduction (45.5% to 22.15% by 2015), increased food crop production (23-43% by 2011) and farming income (28-43%), women's income rise (33-66%), improved basic services in 200 villages, functional forest safeguarding committees, and a 15% economic rate of return. Success is contingent on population adherence, sustained decentralization commitment, and legal text adoption.

The activity targets increased transparency and efficiency in public resource use, fostering private sector and civil society participation, and improving the investment environment. Key outcomes include enhanced fiscal management, efficient budget processes, strengthened financial controls (e.g., reduced discretionary accounts, robust audit/inspection bodies, increased tax revenues), reformed public procurement, an anti-corruption unit, better civil service HR, transparent public enterprise/road maintenance, and stronger public-private partnerships. Success requires maintaining a sound macroeconomic framework, satisfactory budget execution with disseminated data, limited discretionary spending, improved public communication, and sustained civil society engagement.

["The activity aims to strengthen PA system effectiveness to conserve globally significant biodiversity, specifically snow leopard and Przewalski's gazelle, across 2.10445M ha. Outcomes include PA sub-system expansion by 833,950 ha (29,350 ha ecological corridors), improved management effectiveness, habitat protection/restoration (20,000 ha degraded grasslands restored, 60,000 ha improved), reduced biodiversity threats, and stable/improved target species populations. It also targets 4,025 direct beneficiaries and enhanced PA financing.", "Success requires achieving strengthened PA system effectiveness, improved management of 2.10445M ha, PA sub-system expansion by 833,950 ha, increased management effectiveness (40% METT score increase or >67 'sound management'), stable/improved snow leopard and Przewalski's gazelle populations, reduced biodiversity threats, successful restoration of 20,000 ha degraded grasslands and 60,000 ha improved habitat, enhanced local community participation, and integrated sustainable PA financing into provincial plans."]

The Green Climate Fund project targets a 20% reduction in gross GHG emissions from Ecuador's LULUCF sector by 2025 (compared to 2000-2008 FREL), reforesting 210,000 hectares, and maintaining climate regulation services and biodiversity. It aims to reduce deforestation, transition to low-emission development pathways, strengthen local capacity in land-use planning and forest control, establish inter-institutional coordination, implement financial/economic incentives for "deforestation-free" agriculture, and activate UNFCCC Warsaw Framework instruments (NFMS, SIS). It also seeks to strengthen institutional and citizen capacities for rights of nature and sustainable development.

The activity targets: eliminating barriers to climate agenda positioning, consensus, ownership, and financing for adaptation/mitigation. Outcomes include national agreements for NDC review/implementation, strengthened sectoral analysis and public finance management for climate action, developed financing strategies to mobilize diverse funds for NDC actions and green recovery, established strategies for resilient/net-zero pathways, and empowered stakeholder participation. Success requires achieving broad consensus across diverse sectors, successfully mobilizing significant public and private financing, and sustained commitment to data generation, capacity building (including climate budgeting), private sector engagement, and a detailed financing strategy until 2030, alongside supporting NDC updates and transparency reporting.

Targeted outcomes include preparing and submitting Uruguay's 6CN (2023) and BUR4 (2021) to UNFCCC, integrating climate change into national/sectoral objectives, and building capacity. Key results involve updated national circumstances and institutional arrangements (gender/human rights-sensitive) in BUR4/6CN. Participation in SNRCC working groups is targeted to increase from 43 to 50 (35 women, 15 men). Success requires: 100% alignment with country/UNDP plans, timely BUR4/6CN submission, "Satisfactory or above" quality ratings for APR/PIRS, Terminal Evaluation, and overall project results, alongside achieving participation targets and robust stakeholder engagement.

The activity targets improving lives of commodity producers, protecting ecosystems, achieving producers' adoption of sustainable practices at scale. Success entails governments, producers, companies, civil society, and financial actors co-financing/implementing national action plans. Key changes include robust regulatory frameworks, economic incentives for sustainable practices, and strengthened small producer support. Overall success requires 20 additional countries by 2026 with national public-private partnerships for green growth enabling frameworks, a decrease in multidimensional poverty, and 20 countries incorporating multi-stakeholder collaboration for systemic change in sustainable commodity systems.

The activity targets improved rural livelihoods, sustainable small-scale water resources management, and enhanced agricultural productivity for 209,000 ha of land and 324,400 households. Outcomes include 118,000 person-days incremental employment (30% for women), up to 68% increase in household net agricultural income, and 46% of financial benefits to vulnerable groups. Success requires sustainable infrastructure, timely institutional strengthening of LGED/IWRMU, WMCAs capable of O&M, and achieving financial viability (FIRR 28%, B-C ratio 1.4).

The project targets improved environmental and economic conditions in Guilin, focusing on strengthening institutional capacity for environmental preservation, reducing pollution in the Li River, and preserving cultural heritage, aiming for 14,195 tons/annum GHG reduction. Success requires operational smart Li River management platforms; construction of at least 42 sewage treatment facilities; ecological rehabilitation of at least 35 km of river embankments and 100 hectares; 65 km of new bicycle/pedestrian facilities; 100% of 60 cultural facilities integrating gender/social inclusivity; upgrading two transport facilities; and operating at least 20 electric boats.

The project's targeted outcomes are to increase electricity access, improve service reliability, and strengthen KPLC's financial situation. Success requires increased household/non-residential connections, reduced average outage duration (SAIDI), and KPLC achieving financial health (lower commercial losses, improved Current Ratio, Return on Assets to Equity, DSCR >1.2x, Current Ratio >1.0x, Self-Financing Ratio >25%). Key infrastructure upgrades like SCADA/EMS, 90% Nairobi network automation, AMI, live-line maintenance, and strategic initiatives like the National Electrification Strategy are crucial for overall success.

["The targeted outcomes of the Ceara Rural Sustainable Development and Competitiveness activity are to improve the sustainability of rural production and rural income generation, and to contribute to the Borrower's efforts to universalize access to water services. Key results include an increase in the real revenue of beneficiary rural producers' organizations, an increased number of people in rural areas with access to improved water sources and improved sanitation, and an increased number of beneficiary organizations participating in environmental recovery and conservation activities.", "To be considered an overall success, the activity needs to achieve a 20% increase in the real revenue of beneficiary rural producers' organizations, 10,000 new piped household water connections, 39,200 people in rural areas with access to improved sanitation, and 25 beneficiary organizations participating in environmental recovery activities. Success also requires tracking productive and female-led investments, access to formal markets, adoption of sustainable technologies like irrigation, implementation of PSA pilot mechanisms, sustainable basic sanitation investments, and business management training."]

Targeted outcomes include strengthening institutional capacity for enhanced public service delivery in health, education, and environmental management. Success hinges on specific achievements: improving PEFA indicator PI-12 from B+ to an A rating; increasing IDERIO education scores to 5.4 (initial) and 5.2 (final); establishing 20 agreements for services in UPP areas; implementing 4 ongoing impact evaluations; and covering 60% of city planning areas with the GEE emission monitoring system.

The targeted outcomes of the Second South West Indian Ocean Fisheries Governance and Shared Growth Project - Madagascar are primarily to improve the management of selected fisheries at regional, national, and community levels, and to increase access by targeted fishers to alternative livelihood activities. This aligns with the broader Series of Projects Development Objective to increase economic, social, and environmental benefits to SWIO countries from sustainable marine fisheries, aiming for reduced degradation of fish stocks, increased fisheries-related GDP, and increased local fisheries-related added-value for households.Specific targeted outcomes, as measured by Project Development Objective (PDO)-level indicators, include:1.  An increase in the compliance rate of SWIO countries with IOTC Resolutions to an average of 77.00% (with specific targets for Somalia at 76.00% and Comoros at 80.00%).2.  A reduction in the percentage of small-scale fishing units in priority areas observed using prohibited gear during on-shore inspections to 30.00%.3.  An increase in the number of additional villages in priority areas accessing electricity for postharvest and value-addition activities to 20.4.  An increase in the share of targeted community members rating 'Satisfied' or above on project interventions to 25.00%.Overall success for the activity would be achieved by the efficient completion of the project and the attainment of these stated objectives and their corresponding targets. This also means successfully implementing the project's Theory of Change, which involves enhancing governance, realizing economic, social, and environmental benefits from sustainable marine fisheries through effective regulation, fostering new behaviors, restricting access to overexploited fisheries, prohibiting destructive practices, and enforcing management measures. Crucially, success also hinges on ensuring the acceptance and adherence to new measures, facilitated by the transition phase's incentives, including financial transfers, alternative livelihood support, business management training, financial education, and a comprehensive communication campaign.

["The activity targets increased irrigated agriculture productivity, employment, and incomes in Karakalpakstan. It aims to improve Amu Darya river water quality via safe drainage effluent disposal, enhance delta wetlands, and foster institutional development for improved water management, O&M of irrigation/drainage systems, promoting sustainable participatory irrigation. Outcomes include boosting agricultural production/farmers' incomes across nearly 100,000 hectares, improving water use efficiency, reducing drainage effluent volume/salt, and breaking the cycle of waterlogging/secondary soil salinization. Environmental benefits and reduced infrastructure damage are also targeted.", "Overall success requires: increased agriculture production for main crops; reduced areas affected by shallow groundwater levels and soil salinity; and improved water supply reliability. Efficiency gains will be monitored via increased canal conveyance efficiency and reduced drainage water discharges. Sustainability will be indicated by established Water Users Associations (WUAs) and their effective O&M. Success also includes increased non-saline and total irrigated areas. Crucially, meeting Uzbekistan's international obligations to improve Amu Darya water quality for downstream users by safe drainage effluent disposal away from the river is paramount."]

The activity's primary goal is to enhance electricity supply efficiency and reliability in Morocco. Targeted outcomes include reducing transmission losses from 4.7% to 3% by 2012, decreasing un-served energy from 833 MWh to 400 MWh by 2012, and achieving 300 GWh electricity savings with a 200 MW peak reduction from CFLs. Other outcomes involve establishing 1400 MW exchange capacity with EU and 1300 MW with Algeria, developing regional exchanges, reorganizing the electricity sector for partial competition (2010), and increasing transparency in production, transmission, and distribution. Success requires meeting these targets, completing transmission and distribution infrastructure on schedule, implementing DSM and AT activities, maintaining a cumulative debt to equity ratio below 4.5 (after FY2010), and submitting semi-annual project reports.

The targeted outcomes are: (a) Strengthening property rights for the population within its influence area by enhancing regularization, titling, and property registration services; (b) Improving Nicaragua's capacity to respond rapidly and effectively to eligible emergencies. To be considered an overall success, the activity must achieve 750,000 direct beneficiaries (with 51% female beneficiaries) and ensure 90,000 households receive legal documents through its services.

Targeted outcomes aim to improve water and sanitation services in Guilin. This includes meeting future water demand, reducing pollution discharge loads from WWTPs, preventing environmental pollution from sludge disposal, and strengthening Lijiang River water quality monitoring. Success requires providing improved water sources to 248,000 urban residents, removing 1,113 tons/year of BOD pollution, and upgrading 4 WWTPs to achieve Class IA effluent discharge standards per GB18918-2002, completing 100% expansion of Chengbei WTP, rehabilitating/constructing 40 km of sewer, upgrading 3 sludge facilities, and safely treating 150 tons/year of sludge.

The RBNUDP-NM targets enhanced city-level capacity for infrastructure investment (evidenced by approved annual plans & staffed management units), timely delivery of urban infrastructure projects, sustainable infrastructure investments (asset management plans, sub-projects in full service, increased own-source revenues by 12% y-o-y), and an enabling national policy environment with MOC capacity for oversight. Success hinges on addressing past implementation weaknesses, effective resource utilization, achieving these specific outcomes and DLIs, strengthening local capacity, ensuring sustainable financing, and fostering effective national oversight and policy frameworks.

Targeted outcomes: Implement Ecuador's REDD+ Action Plan, reporting national emissions reduction by BUR 2022 (mid-term 2020). Local governments incorporate climate actions into land use plans by May 2020, boosting conservation/restoration. Consolidate 2 biodiversity value chains, securing 2 long-term trade agreements, establishing 2 sustainable management plans, designing/implementing 2 community business models, and generating 2 commercial agreements. Restore at least 2,000 hectares in REDD+ priority areas. Success hinges on comprehensive low-GHG policies, GAD political will, effective performance-based payments, and robust monitoring.

By 2012, Apia's environment/public health enhanced: reduce typhoid/gastroenteritis cases nationwide (<300 each), decrease total coliforms in urban watercourses (<250 MPN/100 ml), ensure all greater Apia premises access SWA sewerage, reduce Fugalei flooding (1:5 year ARI), maintain 8,000 septic tanks, and cut urban flooding duration (<30 mins). Success depends on sustained Government/community commitment, adequate maintenance budget, mandated sewer connections via regulations, public acceptance of tariffs, and effective risk management.

Targeted outcomes include increasing the Gourma elephant population (to 206-259 from 192-242), stabilizing forest/woodland areas, reducing annual poaching (to 0-2 from 9), maintaining zero retaliatory killings, reducing deforestation (to <2 ha/year) avoiding 2.4M tCO2eq, and decreasing uncontrolled bush fires (to <8,500 ha). Other targets involve improved national wildlife crime regulations (e.g., functional WCIU, DNEF capacity from 34% to 50%), increased elephant/habitat protection (METT score from 36 to 56, anti-poaching staff to >60, patrolling to >1050 inspector/days/month), and increased sustainable community-based natural resource management. Overall success requires Malian Government approval/support/funding, effective law enforcement, stable security, local communities perceiving benefits and adopting sustainable practices, and adherence to Result-based Management with clear, measurable indicators and gender equality objectives.
