It aims to bring 2,000 ha (5,000 acres) of new area under high-value crops, mobilize US$75 million in private investment, and reach 150,000 farmers (30% women) with agricultural assets/services. Additionally, 14,000 watercourses will be improved to achieve up to 50% lining, 10,000 acres will receive HEIS, and 5,000 on-farm storage tanks/ponds will be constructed, strengthening resilience to climate shocks.", "The activity will be an overall success if it achieves its objective of improving irrigated agriculture performance for farmers in the project area. This means realizing a 25% reduction in water losses, a 10% increase in water productivity for wheat/maize and a 45% increase for citrus/vegetables using HEIS. Success also requires bringing 2,000 ha of new high-value crops, mobilizing US$75M in private investment, and reaching 150,000 farmers (30% women) with assets/services. Improvement of 14,000 watercourses, 10,000 acres with HEIS, 5,000 tanks/ponds, and enhanced climate resilience are key."]

[{"value": "The project aims to reduce poverty and promote sustainable livelihoods by strengthening institutional capacity for effective environmental protection and natural resource management in Angola. Key outcomes include reviewing/revising environmental policies, laws, and strategies; extensive training (40 post-graduate, 120 short-term, 200 EIA inspectors, 100 EIA technicians, 40 judges); developing sectoral EIA guidelines; establishing/strengthening the CRMU, INABIO, Environmental Training School, and a UN Conventions Unit; and creating four pilot sites for sustainable land-use, climate change adaptation, and biodiversity conservation, benefiting approximately 8,000 people."}, {"value": "Overall success hinges on achieving the goal of poverty reduction and sustainable livelihoods through effective natural resource management and environmental protection. Specifically, it requires deforestation reduction to 0.3% by 2013, implementation of at least 3 sectoral EIA guidelines by 2011, revised environmental policy and law enforcement by 2011, mainstreaming climate proofing in development interventions by 2010, and all new development interventions screened using EIA/climate proofing guidelines by 2010. Additionally, successful training and functional key environmental institutions (INABIO, CRMU) are crucial, alongside sustained government commitment and inter-sectoral collaboration."}]

The TIETÊ RIVER CLEANUP PROGRAM, STAGE IV targets improving environmental and sanitary conditions in the Upper Tietê River watershed by reducing the organic load from households by roughly 70 tons of BOD5 per day. This involves sustainable expansion of sewage collection, transport, and treatment systems, aiming for 876,814 new households with treated wastewater and 406,723 new household connections. Wastewater treatment capacity in the MRSP will increase by 3 m³/s to 29.5 m³/s. The project will construct 420 km of collectors/interceptors and 780 km of collection networks. It also aims to reduce climate change vulnerability and greenhouse gas emissions by capturing biogas. Institutional outcomes include upgrading SABESP's operational capacity, implementing an equipment maintenance facility, installing a pilot plant for new technologies, developing one innovative technology, and a regulatory master plan.

The targeted outcomes of the PRELUB activity are: to reinforce and better secure the electricity network in Lubumbashi, increasing the electricity supply rate in Karavia city, thereby contributing to the reliability of essential services by improving the quality and quantity of electricity. Specifically, the project aims for three main results: (R1) achieving security and increased transformation capacity of the Southern Distributor (RS) substation; (R2) accomplishing the electrification and public lighting of Karavia City; and (R3) ensuring the management and maintenance of the new installations. For the activity to be considered an overall success, the following would need to be met: R1 success requires an increase in the maximum electrical power delivered by the RS substation and an improved availability rate of THT/HT and HT/MT transformers. R2 success requires the installation of new MT/BT substations, new MT and BT lines, and an increased number of users connected to these lines, as well as improved public lighting in Karavia City. R3 success implies an improved number and reduced duration of monthly maintenance interventions by teams and an increase in trained SNEL personnel for management and maintenance. Furthermore, for the specific objective, success is indicated by a reduction in average monthly MT outages, shorter average monthly MT outage durations, an increased rate of access to electricity for Karavia's population, and a higher number of domestic and semi-industrial subscribers in Karavia.

Targeted outcomes include advancing global thinking on inclusive and sustainable development in drylands by producing 4 knowledge products and supporting 5 initiatives (2015-2017). Further outcomes aim to strengthen science-policy-practice for biodiversity/ecosystem services (2020-2028) by establishing 18 national BES platforms, implementing 54 BES actions, having 18 platforms contribute to IPBES, endorsing 8 NEA reports, engaging 200 stakeholders in assessments, integrating NEA findings in 40 orgs, and improving awareness for 430 professionals. Success means achieving all these specific targets.

The activity targets strengthening Egypt's Nature Conservation Sector and promoting community-based income-generating mechanisms for Protected Areas (PAs) management, aligning with UNDAF Outcome 5.3. Key outcomes include enhanced PA management through Community-Based Natural Resource Management (CBNRM), promotion of Responsible and Sustainable Eco-Tourism, and institutionalizing innovative participatory approaches for inclusive growth. Specific targets include a 25% increase in revenue from five supported PAs, ensuring 100% coverage of PA management costs by year 3, activating at least 3 businesses and creating 30 decent employment opportunities (50% for youths, 25% for women) in each project IA by year 3. Overall success requires a developed communication strategy during inception, defining goals, methods, and indicators for stakeholder engagement, awareness, and eco-tourism promotion.

Targeted outcomes aim to strengthen joint management capacity for the Orange-Senqu River basin's IWRM Plan and demonstrate environmental/socioeconomic benefits via SAP priority actions. This addresses four main threats: increasing water demand, changes to the hydrological regime, declining water quality, and land degradation, through measures like improved monitoring, water use efficiency, pollution reduction, and land rehabilitation. Other outcomes include institutional/policy reforms, technical capacity building, improved shared water body governance, and enhanced water/food/energy/ecosystem security. By project end, all four countries are targeted to be fully capacitated and actively participating in cooperative water management.

The activity targets enhancing transboundary cooperation to prevent biodiversity fragmentation, restoring 18,900 ha of forest and leveraging 16,100 ha for climate mitigation by 2022. It aims to achieve sustainably managed biodiversity corridors covering 1.93 million ha by 2018, providing tenurial security and improving livelihoods for 2,600 households. Specific outcomes include increasing household income/assets by 40-55% and generating 5.3 million cash-based labor days, while augmenting ecosystem service flows through carbon sequestration and improved watershed management.

["The activity targets saving 1,435 GWh electricity by 2025-05-21 (from a 0 GWh baseline in 2017 and 517 GWh by 2020), transforming Sudan's markets for energy-efficient (EE) lighting and air-conditioners, providing climate change mitigation benefits, and decreasing energy poverty. It contributes to SDG 7, UNDAF/Country Programme Outcome 2, and UNDP Strategic Plan Output 1.5. Outcomes include developing a national EE strategy and capacitating the ERA as a focal point.", "Further outcomes involve adopting and localizing internationally recognized regulatory mechanisms (MEPS, labeling, testing, importing) and developing a financial mechanism to promote EE. An internationally recognized MVE program will be established and adopted by trained personnel, with market data collection mechanisms in place. Capacities of local supply chain stakeholders and end-users will increase, and hazardous material leakage from lighting/AC products will be reduced/minimized. By project end, 75% of lighting and AC products in market should comply with regulations.", "Overall success requires achieving the 1,435 GWh electricity savings target by project end. Specific indicators include 75% of market lighting and air conditioning products complying with adopted regulations, 3,000,000 lighting lamps and 50,000 air conditioning units distributed via financial incentives, and 40 staff from relevant entities trained in the MVE system. Additionally, 75% of the annual sales volume for lighting and AC products should be generated from EE products.", "Success also hinges on market players complying and benefiting from standards, and the project's actions aligning with Sudan's energy objectives. Consumers must adopt new technologies after appropriate demonstration and initial cost reduction. The resulting electricity savings are expected to free up funds for rural electrification, contributing to an 80% electrification rate by 2031, and ensure greater grid stability as demand increases, representing a transformational change in electricity consumption."]

["The project targets sustainable WWTP sludge management with energy recovery and biosolid reuse, improved urban lake/channel water quality (Class IV, biological diversity by 2020), reestablished water circulation, enhanced flood resilience (50% reduction in storm water flooding by 2015), better environment, living conditions, public health (30% reduction in waterborne diseases by 2020), and increased public awareness. Expected outcomes include over 50% of urban WWTP sludge treated sustainably by 2015, extending landfill life by 16 years, improving WWTP performance (25% less inorganic sludge matter by 2015), full wastewater service cost recovery by 2018, and 15% annual GDP growth from 2015 in the project area.", "Success requires effective implementation of Wuhan's master/sector plans, strong enforcement of environmental laws/standards, proper infrastructure maintenance, adequate non-residential connection/pretreatment enforcement, and pollution control. Government support for tariff increases and customers' willingness to pay are crucial. Effective stakeholder participation, strong local government support, synchronized WWTP sludge treatment upgrades, timely land acquisition/resettlement, and suitable dredged material disposal arrangements are also essential. Setting a national policy framework for sludge management is a key factor."]

The activity targets increased electricity availability in Assam by June 2025, specifically 469 GWh/year of hydroelectricity from Lower Kopili HEP and a 360,000 tCO2e/year GHG reduction. Overall success requires: 120 MW Lower Kopili hydropower plant constructed by Dec 2023; APGCL's institutional capacity strengthened by Dec 2023 (at least 30 staff, ERP system, asset verification); and resource management/community resilience initiatives implemented by June 2023 (dashboard for state/5 local govts, 500 people trained in disaster management).

The targeted outcomes for Tranche 2 are improved agricultural production and sustained economic growth in the North Central, Central, North Western, and Eastern Provinces of Sri Lanka, achieved through new and improved water conveyance and storage infrastructure. Overall success by 2024 requires commissioning the Kalu Ganga-Moragahakanda Transfer Canal (9 km), Upper Elahera Canal (92 km), North Western Province canal, two new reservoirs, and associated infrastructure. By 2021, specific successes include constructing an additional 21% of 101 km conveyance channel linking Kalu Ganga and Mahakandara, and an additional 45% of Mahakithula and Mahakirula Reservoirs and Mahakithula Inlet Tunnel. By 2019, multidisciplinary investment program management must be operational, evidenced by Tranche 3 PFR submission to ADB and on-time, within-budget delivery of Tranche 2 outputs.

The targeted outcomes of the Hunan Xiangjiang River Watershed Existing Solid Waste Comprehensive Treatment Project include an improved environment in the Xiangjiang River watershed in Hunan Province, specifically through the reduction of long-term pollutants discharged to the watershed. The project is expected to directly benefit an estimated population of 6.9 million people and more indirectly in the wider watershed. The activity would be considered an overall success if, by 2024, landfill sites are closed and restored and/or remediated at 11 localities, covering 32.85 ha of MSW landfill area. Additionally, integrated MSW management systems must be established in seven localities with a capacity of 4,340 tons per day. Success also requires the replication of the project's best practices at three landfills for closure, restoration, and/or upgrading, and at two localities for the establishment of MSW systems in up to five further locations.

Targeted outcomes: Add 25MW renewable energy capacity (3 small HPPs with 24MW, mini HPPs with 1MW, 22km transmission line). Develop climate-resilient hydropower master plan. Strengthen Uzbekhydroenergo's financial management and corporate governance, including gender integration. Achieve 97 GWh increased hydropower generation and 52,000 tCO2 annual reduction by 2024. Success is achieved when: 25MW HPP capacity is commissioned, 22km transmission line built, 97 GWh generation increase & 52,000 tCO2 reduction by 2024; master plan adopted; Uzbekhydroenergo staff trained in planning, finance, gender awareness, and corporate policy adopted.

The Greater Male Environmental Improvement And Waste Management Project primarily targets the creation of a healthy living environment in the Greater Malé capital region and its outer islands. The core outcome is improved climate- and disaster-resilient solid waste management (SWM) services. To be considered an overall success, the project must achieve the following specific targets by 2024 for its outcome and by 2023 for its outputs:1. Outcome Targets (by 2024): At least 90% of all solid waste in the project area collected and transferred to Thilafushi or Island Waste Management Centers (IWMC), a significant increase from the 2018 baselines of 86% for household, commercial, and resort waste, and 62% for construction and demolition waste. The number of daily complaints for non-collection received by WAMCO must be reduced by 80% from 150 (2017 baseline). Controlled dumping at the Thilafushi dumpsite must be implemented without waste burning (from a baseline of unmanaged dumping and smoke in 2018). A Disaster Risk Management (DRM) operational plan must be approved and adopted by WAMCO management.2. Output 1 Targets (Waste collection, transfer, and disposal systems improved and made climate and disaster resilient by 2023): A new waste collection and transfer strategy developed; 10,000 bins distributed in Greater Malé; 21 additional refuse collection vehicles commissioned in Malé and Hulhumalé; 3 vessels commissioned for waste transport from islands to Thilafushi; new climate- and disaster-resilient transfer stations constructed in Malé and Villimalé; 11 heavy machinery and 20 containers commissioned for Thilafushi dumpsite management; and construction and demolition waste and End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV) processing plants established in Thilafushi.3. Output 2 Targets (Community-based outer island waste management systems targeting poor and women enhanced by 2023): At least 22 IWMCs developed or upgraded in the outer islands; 18,000 bins distributed for waste collection in outer islands; 4 refuse compaction vehicles and 25 dump trucks commissioned for outer islands; at least 150 outer island inhabitants (at least 30% women) reporting enhanced SWM skills, including IWMC operations, DRM, and participatory action planning; and at least 500 people in outer islands (at least 50% women) reporting improved awareness on sustainable SWM and 3R benefits.4. Output 3 Targets (Institutional capacity and public awareness in sustainable waste management strengthened by 2023): 100 eligible WAMCO staff (at least 70% women) reporting improved skills in sustainable SWM service delivery and DRM; 100% of WAMCO workers in Thilafushi reporting knowledge on public health, human-induced, and natural hazards, including climate change impacts; WAMCO's gender-inclusive strategy developed and adopted; WAMCO's DRM plan developed; and at least 1,000 people in Greater Malé (at least 50% women) reporting improved awareness.

The activity targets improving PPUC's corporate governance and financial management, and enabling private sector participation in electricity and water subsectors. It also aims to strengthen the capacity of the Ministry of Finance, PPUC, and Palau Energy Administration, supporting long-term energy, water, and sanitation reforms. Overall success hinges on: 1. By 2023, the operationalization of a cost-recovery tariff for electricity and water/wastewater. 2. By 2023, an increase in private sector participation in power generation capacity to at least 20%. Additionally, PPUC's board must approve key policies like an accounting policy, an annual revenue model, a revenue enhancement plan, and a corporate governance code.

The activity targeted improving conditions for a decentralized, environmentally friendly energy supply in Togo. Success was defined by: strengthened capacities of the Togolese Agency for Rural Electrification and Renewable Energies (AT2ER), including organizational development, a financing plan, a functional cartographic unit, and technical/material support; an improved Togolese legal and regulatory framework for the energy sector; and the introduction and testing of pilot financing/entrepreneurial models in rural regions. A key specific target was for 550 rural households (135 women-headed) to gain access to high-quality basic energy technologies, encompassing village grid connections, pico PV/solar home systems, and improved cookstoves.

The activity aimed for stronger utilization of regenerative energy sources and improved energy efficiency in Peru. Success required an annual CO2 saving of 186,000 tCO2, RE/EE measures proving profitable (positive IRR), and COFIDE's RE/EE financing portfolio increasing to EUR 112 million. Critical were no outstanding loan arrears, basic PV power in remote areas, and promoting energy efficiency. Phase II aimed to supply urban populations with basic services.

The activity targeted a cumulative saving of 5,250 GWh and a 360 MW peak load reduction by 2030. Its overarching goal was to contribute to efficient energy use and climate action by avoiding 3.675 million tonnes of CO2 by 2030. Additionally, it aimed to improve living conditions for disadvantaged groups by increasing disposable income through reduced electricity bills. Success depended on achieving these targets, primarily through the installation of 10 million energy-saving lamps.

The activity targeted supplying selected institutions (Ministry of Health, UNHCR, UNICEF) with cost-effective, sustainable electricity to enable financial flexibility for maintaining/expanding services, improving living conditions and coexistence for Syrian refugees and host communities, and contributing to global climate protection. To achieve success, the project aimed to inject 45 GWh of electricity annually in its first full year, ensure electricity cost savings were available for improved target group supply (50% of MoH savings for service improvements), and achieve an annual avoidance of 26,000 tons of CO2 emissions.

Targeted outcomes for the activity include ensuring all relevant governmental, civil society, and private actors contribute to improved water resources management in Central Tunisia, aiming to enhance living conditions in disadvantaged areas through a sound water balance and sustainable environment. Specific targets involved protecting and sustainably using water, fostering cooperation, establishing functional water forums for dialogue, initiating a decentralized IWRM committee, improving irrigation and drinking water services, raising water user awareness, and developing a legal framework for participatory water management. Success was defined by active stakeholder participation in forums for improved water use decisions and water associations experiencing reduced conflicts and better services, alongside achieving the main objective.

{"targeted_outcomes": "The targeted outcomes of this Project Preparatory Grant (PPG) activity are the comprehensive development and finalization of all necessary project documents and plans to enable the main \"Integrated Management for Sustainable Reduction (IMSRed) of POPs and other Highly Hazardous Pesticides and industrial chemicals in Argentina\" project to proceed to the CEO endorsement stage. These include: 1. Comprehensive Technical Studies and Reviews (Component A): Detailed baseline and situational analysis; assessed development challenges, strategies, and policy/legislative frameworks; developed problem and solution trees; identified lessons learned from past projects; conducted a dedicated gender analysis to understand gender-differentiated needs and identify measures for gender equality; performed Social and Environmental Standards (SES) screenings and assessments to identify and plan avoidance/mitigation of risks; identified project pilot/demonstration sites; confirmed co-financing and mobilized investments; detailed stakeholder analysis; formulated project implementation and execution modality, including capacity assessments of potential Implementing Partners; and developed an Occupational Health and Safety Plan. 2. Formulation of the UNDP-GEF Project Document, CEO Endorsement Request, and Mandatory Annexes (Component B): A complete UNDP-GEF Project Document and CEO Endorsement Request; a comprehensive and socially inclusive Stakeholder Engagement Plan, including a project-level Grievance Redress Mechanism; a Gender Action Plan and Budget with gender mainstreaming indicators for the Results Framework; an updated Social and Environmental Screening Procedure (SESP) and outlined necessary management plans (e.g., environmental/social management plans for identified risks); prepared GEF Core Indicators data; secured official co-financing guarantee letters and a GEF OFP endorsement letter (if applicable); prepared mandatory annexes such as project maps, a Multi-Year Work Plan, a Monitoring Plan, a UNDP Risk Register, and Terms of Reference for key project staff; developed a well-articulated project's theory of change; and a detailed Monitoring and Evaluation Plan and Budget. 3. Validation and Finalization (Component C): Validation of project activities and the final draft of the Project Document through a stakeholder workshop; and a mandatory Validation Workshop Report. Assessment of indigenous peoples/ethnic minorities and determination of FPIC applicability.", "success_criteria": "The activity would be considered an overall success if the following conditions are met: 1. Meeting the CEO Endorsement deadline of December 5th, 2025, as non-endorsement will lead to automatic cancellation by the GEF Secretariat. 2. Securing high co-financing ratios for the project. 3. Ensuring comprehensive stakeholder engagement throughout all preparatory phases. 4. Achieving robust gender mainstreaming throughout the preparatory phases, effectively reflected in all plans and documents. 5. Robust adherence to social and environmental standards throughout the preparatory phases, including the identification, avoidance, or mitigation of negative impacts as identified in SES assessments. 6. The successful production and validation of all required documents and plans (Project Document, CEO Endorsement Request, Stakeholder Engagement Plan, Gender Action Plan, SESP, etc.) in line with UNDP and GEF requirements."}

{"targeted_outcomes": "The targeted outcomes of the North Macedonia: Local Roads Connectivity Project are to improve government capacity to manage local roads and enhance access to markets and services. This includes improving the quality, safety, and resilience of municipal roads and streets, strengthening both local and central government capacity for road management, and establishing a sound planning and governance framework. The project aims to reduce transport costs, improve connectivity, and enhance local economic competitiveness, ultimately leading to gains in productivity, welfare, and quality of life.", "success_criteria": "For the activity to be considered an overall success, the following specific indicators must be met: a significant number of markets and services must be connected by improved, safe, and resilient local roads; there should be high beneficiary satisfaction (gender-disaggregated) with the project roads; a specified number of municipalities must be utilizing the simple asset management methods developed under the project; and a national policy for local roads must be adopted. Additionally, success involves the sustained improvement in the condition of local roads, proper utilization of maintenance expenditures by municipalities, and effective central government oversight and support for local road management. The project also aims for community-driven infrastructure pilots focused on vulnerable groups and the inclusion of clauses requiring contractors to employ women (Roma and non-Roma) and Roma men in project works."}

Targeted outcomes (known at activity start): 1. Impact-level: Improve environmental situation, promote sustainable water resource management, reduce negative health impacts. 2. Module-level: Ensure continuous, consumption-oriented water supply, hygienic wastewater discharge, and environmentally compatible disposal/treatment in Ganja and Sheki. Success requires: 24h/day, 325d/year continuous water supply; max 32% non-revenue water in rehabilitated zones; at least 90% water supply/wastewater connection in rehabilitated areas; treated wastewater meeting BSB5 < 25 mg/l, CSB < 125 mg/l, TSS < 35 mg/l; minimum 75% water utility operational efficiency; and 100% operating cost recovery within one year after commissioning.

The targeted outcome is to improve conditions for implementing sustainable energy services aimed at climate protection in Senegal. This includes achieving five outputs: improved policy frameworks and implementation capacities for RE/EE; enhanced planning and implementation capabilities for RE/EE training; better information/services to leverage EE's economic potential; demonstrated technical/economic feasibility of productive solar energy in rural areas; and developed process elements to remove market barriers for isolated grids.

The ADHOC II activity targeted improving municipal solid waste management (SWM) in five refugee-hosting municipalities and at the Ministry of Local Administration (MoLA). This included enhancing municipal workshop operations, improving waste collector truck maintenance, and strengthening MoLA's SWM monitoring capacity. Overarching goals were effective solid waste collection, fostered social cohesion, and environmental protection. Success was defined by achieving a 40% cost coverage ratio in Irbid and 39% in Mafraq, implementing 10 measures from municipal SWM plans in 4/5 municipalities, and utilizing a digital SWM improvement process based on customer feedback in 4/5 municipalities.

["The activity aimed to integrate sustainable agriculture approaches, considering climate change, into policy processes and programs of national/international development cooperation (EZ) actors. This sought to enhance BMZ's visibility and positioning, strengthen EZ actors' competencies, contribute to public discussion on innovations, and boost implementation of demand-driven products. Higher-level impacts included strengthening advisory services for sustainable agro-food systems, anchoring concepts in policies, expanding resources, and contributing to resilient, low-emission systems, Agenda 2030, and relevant SDGs. Outputs included providing BMZ with demand-driven information/services, offering updated products/multipliers for EZ actor competence development, and making technical/conceptual products available to national/international EZ actors for policy integration.", "To achieve success, the following targets were necessary: BMZ was to shape 4 national/international policy processes on sustainable agriculture using the activity's advisory products. 3 regional actors were to apply the activity's competence development products with national expert institutions in 15 countries. Additionally, 4 bi- or multilateral EZ actors were to integrate one updated advisory product from the activity into their projects, with 2 of these products having gender-relevant content."]

The activity aimed for the Bangladesh Forest Department (BFD), with co-management structures, to implement co-management, SMART, and ecological monitoring for sustainable Sundarbans Mangrove Forest (SMF) management. Key targeted outcomes for success, known at the beginning, were: 1) 10 co-management executive committees (CMECs) in Chandpai Range having funding modalities and one VCF application; 2) A two-point improvement in female Protection Forces' (PF) decision-making participation (from 3 to 5); 3) 10 BFD master trainers independently conducting SMART patrol trainings; 4) An updated Integrated Resources Management Plan (IRMP) agreed with the BFD.

The Naturaleza para la Paz activity targets several outcomes: 1. Contribute to sustained peace and prevent new socio-environmental conflicts by increasing "green investments" in PDET municipalities. 2. Strengthen knowledge, technical, organizational, and operational capacities of rural communities, including women's organizations, in high biological and cultural diversity areas. 3. Facilitate community participation in green businesses, sustainable production, REDD+ initiatives, and Greenhouse Gas (GHG) removal activities. 4. Generate economic resources for communities, promote forest conservation, and improve living conditions. 5. Develop business models and community-private alliances that economically recognize and reward conservation. 6. Implement landscape management tools for sustainable rural production, contributing to GHG removal/reduction and creating carbon sinks. 7. Vitalize and increase the competitiveness of associated small rural producers by generating tradable environmental services in carbon markets. 8. Address challenges for small producers in remote, conflict-affected areas to facilitate their participation in environmental markets. 9. Strengthen local governance and decision-making for deforestation reduction and REDD+ implementation. 10. Promote women's active participation and leadership in conservation and climate action, ensuring increased benefits and improved quality of life for women, their families, and communities.

The targeted outcome was to improve framework conditions for sustainable energy supply to enterprises and households in Uganda. Success required: 25% of MEMD's planning/M&E processes compliant with ISO 9001 by 2019; application of 2 QMS products in SPPAD/DERD; 50% of DERD project reports derived from QMS M&E; 1 major energy policy with gender/HIV/AIDS goals; a roll-out strategy for district energy structures in the master plan; increased RE/EE business association participation in quality standard development; a 10% increase in energy consumers with energy management systems; energy audits in companies; and auditors fulfilling international certification requirements.

Targeted outcomes aimed to enable residents, administrations, and ICF to implement sustainable natural resource management and protection in RPBR, improving its conservation, development, and logistics functions and local livelihoods. This involved supporting land registry, granting land titles, equipping institutions, improving sustainable local management (forestry, coffee, cocoa), promoting local economy, enhancing land registry capacities, strengthening indigenous groups, diversifying incomes, increasing tax revenues, professionalizing land registry, decentralizing, building trust, and empowering indigenous groups. Success required an annual deforestation rate <1% and SIMONI system updates at least once yearly.

Targeted outcomes: protect Mesoamerican Reef marine resources/biodiversity, maintain ecological functions, ensure MAR Fund financial/operational stability (Fund A), fund reef conservation/restoration (Fund C), consolidate funding for nine priority MPAs, improve MPA management/regional cooperation. Area targets for mangrove/seagrass: Phase I (B) 

28,000 ha; Phase II (D) Mangroves 47,000 ha; Seagrass 21,000 ha. Success required favorable market/investment conditions, effective fund targeting, sustained government budgets, MAR Fund's additional fund mobilization, reduce financial gaps for MPA management.

["The overarching targeted outcome is to strengthen the drought resilience of the (agro-) pastoral population in the Afar and Somali regions of the Ethiopian lowlands.", "CDSDR-II aims to enable governmental and non-governmental actors, alongside pastoral and agro-pastoral communities, to create the conceptual basis for the rehabilitation and use of dry valleys.", "CDSDR-II Output A targets developing and testing sustainable, conflict-sensitive management models for operating rehabilitated dry valleys, including concepts for maintenance, financial flows, user planning, and state authority supervision.", "Output A also aims to document and test income and employment options for local populations, focusing on women and young people, on rehabilitated land, and to develop criteria and processes for legal recognition of community user rights.", "CDSDR-II Output B targets disseminating a comprehensive, coherent, and gender-sensitive concept for the rehabilitation of dry valleys.", "CDSDR-II Output C aims to strengthen the professional skills and organisational capacities of districts and municipalities for planning and executing dry valley rehabilitation measures.", "CDSDR-II Output D aims to expand the range of relevant service providers for dry valley rehabilitation.", "IFTAR aims to increase the capacities of the population and institutions to secure productive livelihoods and food for the long term, even during climate-induced weather extremes.", "IFTAR Output A targets improving conditions for access to clean water.", "IFTAR Output B aims to increase the population's capacities for securing their food.", "IFTAR Output C aims to improve capacities for disaster risk management (DRM)."]

["The activity aims to advance a sustainable, participatory, people-centered development model integrating economic, environmental, social, gender equity, social justice, resilience, territories, innovation, and sustainable production. Outcomes include strengthened subnational capacities (Product 1), models for a just, sustainable, and resilient transition (Product 2), a robust democracy and citizenship strategy (Product 3), and knowledge/inputs for a shared development vision (Product 4). These contribute to rights access, poverty/inequality reduction, strengthened democracy/trust, inclusive territorial development, and sustainable resource management.", "Overall success requires achieving specific targets by 2026: strengthening subnational capacities with gender-focused strategies (Product 1), generating knowledge and implementing initiatives for a just socio-ecological transition (Product 2), disseminating diagnostics and testing initiatives to strengthen democracy, participation, and combat disinformation (Product 3), and producing knowledge and recommendations for a common development vision, including publishing a Human Development Report (Product 4). This implies fostering a shared vision, reducing inequalities, and building trust."]

Targeted outcomes: Improved energy security and transition to sustainable energy in Lebanon, enhancing public services and industrial competitiveness via renewables. Key targets include significant reduction in fossil fuel imports (4,900 T/year) and GHG emissions (12,000 tCO2eq/year), 30% fuel reduction in public services, 4,250 MWh/year renewable energy generation in both sectors, 5,000 green jobs, 30 empowered women engineers, and increased turnover for 20 MSMEs. Success criteria: Achieving all stated targets, active participation from industries, SMEs, public entities, start-ups, sufficient funding, stable political/economic environment, and consistent local partner collaboration.

The activity targeted the following outcomes to improve framework conditions for increasing energy efficiency (EE) and renewable energy (RE): 1) At least 18 new or amended legal frameworks in place. 2) At least 10 beneficial legal framework conditions applied in practice. 3) At least 14 new or improved support/dissemination programmes/mechanisms with private sector involvement. 4) An increase to 4 million sqm of solar collectors and 70 MW of photovoltaics by 2016. 5) At least 3,000 people (including 750 women) qualified in RE/EE subjects. 6) At least 3 institutionally anchored offers for RE/EE information/awareness, with gender aspects in at least one. Achieving these targets would define success.

The targeted outcomes of the activity were to better protect the rural population in the identified project areas from human-wildlife conflict (HWC) and to reduce human-wildlife conflicts through the application of agreed guidelines and instruments. Success, as defined at the beginning, would be achieved when: 1. A National Strategy and Action Plan (NAP) for HWC reduction is operationalized in selected states. 2. Newly developed HWC mitigation instruments are piloted in selected states. 3. Institutional and individual capacities for HWC reduction are strengthened.

The activity targeted enhancing economic utilization of bush biomass, aiming for improved livelihoods and environmental conditions. Key outcomes included restoring 496,000 ha of bush-encroached rangeland, creating 1,715 additional jobs (totaling 8,915 by 2021) in biomass production/use, and increasing traded biomass products to 5 by 2021. Success was defined by achieving these specific numerical targets, developing a sustainable bush biomass certification, establishing sustainable supply schemes, introducing adapted technologies, and implementing 3 approaches to promote women's employment.

The program aims for heightened coral reef protection, integrating reefs into Aqaba's sustainable economy through CSBEI-led investments and regulations, coral reef regeneration via new technologies, and knowledge management for scaling solutions. Success requires leveraging USD 30M private funding, reducing degradation, fostering regional action, advancing scientific understanding/restoration, establishing a self-sustaining CSBEI, strengthening MPA governance, and filling critical monitoring/valuation gaps with community participation.

The targeted outcomes of the Integriertes Wasserressourcenmanagement (AGIRE) activity include: improving Integrated Water Resources Management (IWRM); reducing the overuse of water resources; actively involving women and user groups in water management; strengthening institutional, legal, and organizational frameworks for IWRM adapted to climate change; supporting participatory planning and management of water resources; and generalizing good IWRM practices and instruments, including rainwater and ecological wastewater management. Specific quantitative targets include implementing contracts for 3.2 million inhabitants; ABHs using satellite data to monitor 28,000 km2 for 6.4 million inhabitants regarding irrigated areas and agricultural water consumption; 10 national multipliers launching initiatives for good rainwater management; and recovering wastewater/sanitation products for 300,000 inhabitants.

The 'Green Markets and Sustainable Consumption' activity aimed to expand market access for socio-biodiversity and organic farming products. Success was defined by achieving: 1. A doubled value of Amazonian socio-biodiversity and organic products in National School Dining and Food Buying Programmes in two SEAD areas, targeting BRL 2,715,552. 2. A 50% increase (to 23) in companies communicating their products' ecological/social value. 3. Two stakeholder groups using gender tools in policy/program planning to improve women's income/conditions in two socio-biodiversity value chains. 4. A 20% sales increase (after inflation) for cooperatives/smallholder farmers in Acre, Amapá, Amazonas, Pará for socio-biodiversity/organic products, targeting BRL 826,560.

Targeted outcomes for success included public and private sector actors developing approaches to synergize economic, ecological, and social sustainability in Pakistan's textile industry. Specific targets for 2023 were: 1) Number of employees registered through PESSI in Punjab to reach 1,108,204. 2) Implementation of 3 out of 4 measures from the jointly elaborated Action Plan for Sustainable Production in the Textile and Fashion Industry. 3) 15 out of 20 entrepreneurs using sustainable training/consultancy services confirming new value-added activities or markets.

The activity targets strengthening transboundary marine governance and building robust ecosystem-based management for PACA. Key outcomes include: developing a participatory TDA and a formally adopted SAP; building human/social capital through training (e.g., Blue Economy MOOCs); implementing pilot interventions for learning (billfish, MSP, whale watching) to generate regional plans/guidelines; establishing a collaborative information system for long-term monitoring; and documenting/disseminating lessons. Success hinges on effective implementation and ministerial adoption of regional plans/guidelines, overcoming data deficiencies, strong national commitment, and empowerment of stakeholders.

The activity targeted contributing to Agenda 2030 (SDGs 7 & 13), increasing electricity access to 70% of households by 2030, with over 75% from renewables. It aimed to support renewable energies, reform the regulatory framework for a better investment climate, and empower the private sector. Further targets included supporting ADER's calls for tenders, establishing the National Electricity Fund, and reducing 1.18 million tons of CO2. Success required achieving these access and renewable energy targets, establishing a favorable investment climate and regulatory framework, and actualizing planned renewable energy projects.

Targeted outcomes included establishing foundations for sustainable development, income generation, and a more equitable society through sustainable protection and management. The activity aimed for territorial planning to integrate natural resource protection and the needs of conflict-affected groups. For success, by 2020/21, targets were: 4 representatives from conflict-affected groups contributing to planning; 4 multiplier organizations disseminating sustainable business models; and 2 financing models for sustainable business models available locally.

Targeted outcomes aimed to: consolidate 5 PDCs, implement 55 GIRH/MIC projects; develop 14 PDCs; establish early warning systems in 49 municipalities; afforest 7,500 hectares; monitor water quality in 20 bodies. Other targets included 9 agreements, 18 research topics, 12 educational materials, capacity building in 50 municipalities, raising awareness, soil protection. Success would be achieving these, plus an average monthly agricultural income >1,128 BOB by 2018 and reducing chronically undernourished children to <23.7% by 2016, and a GIRH/MIC sustainability index of 0.49.

The activity targets enhancing Cook Islands' capacity to manage its protected area estate and landscapes, promoting biodiversity conservation, sustainable resource use, food security, and livelihoods. Key outcomes include operationalizing the Cook Islands Marine Park and strengthening diverse protected/locally managed areas. It aims for a paradigm shift to holistic 'ridge to reef' management, integrating traditional systems, and tailoring policies. Outcomes also involve mainstreaming biodiversity into production sectors, improving PA management effectiveness, and establishing sustainable financing.

Targeted outcomes: Develop and apply integrated spatial and land use policies and instruments in Tamil Nadu/Odisha; demonstrate land use policy and guideline application for district/regional plans; improve state administration capacity through training and institutional coordination; introduce integrated state land use planning (political backing, standards, plans, cooperation, capable training institutions); contribute to SDGs. Success depended on: capable national/state administrations (from capacity development); increased acceptance of integrated planning (via model land use plans).

The activity aimed to contribute to Pakistan's energy transition, leading to improved RE supply, enhanced financing, climate protection, economic growth, efficient energy use, broader electricity access, improved energy intensity, and a conducive energy policy, increasing RE share. Targeted success: Output 1: 2 utilities introduce 2 new grid management tools/processes each by 12/2023. Output 2: Grid-connected PV capacity up to 1 MW increases to IESCO 52 MWp and LESCO 58 MWp by 12/2023. Output 3: 3 Pakistani institutions disseminate rural electrification business model results at 2 international conferences and 5 publications by 12/2023. Output 4: 3 economic, technology, or academic cooperation arrangements between Pakistani and German institutions by 12/2023.

["The activity targeted the recording, transport, treatment, and disposal of a large majority of the dangerous hazardous waste in the country's specialist industrial regions, in accordance with applicable environmental standards and legislation. This was intended to protect Tunisia's environment and natural resources and reduce the population's health risks. To achieve this, the plan involved creating three regional plants (initially Sfax, Gabès, and Bizerte, though Bizerte was later separately financed by Tunisia). These plants would pre-treat and dispose of some waste locally, and transport the most dangerous waste to the central disposal center in Jradou for further treatment.", "Success for the activity was to be measured by the following targeted outcomes: a 90% capacity utilization rate of the decentralized plants, at least 70% of waste quantities produced in heavily industrialized zones treated in these plants, compliance with Tunisian standards for emissions and wastewater release, and dangerous hazardous waste transport revealing breaches in less than 10% of regular checks."]

The targeted outcomes were to increase renewable energy (RE) prevalence, ensure an environmentally friendly, need-based, and reliable power supply, diversify the electricity mix in Central America, and contribute to global environmental protection and climate change mitigation. This was to be achieved by partially financing seven individual private sector RE projects (wind, hydropower, geothermal) through BCIE development loans. Success required this financing to bridge the financial gap, expand sustainable RE technologies, ensure reliable energy supply, and reduce harmful emissions.

The targeted outcomes of the activity, as defined at the beginning, were to ensure environmentally friendly and hygienically safe collection and treatment of wastewater in Bihać. This was intended to improve living conditions for the population and protect natural resources, including the Una river and groundwater. Specific quantitative targets for success included: an increase in the population's connection rate to the urban wastewater network from a baseline of 31% to 53%; achievement of specific wastewater treatment quality standards for the sewage treatment plant (BOD 
A
25 mg/l, COD 
A
125 mg/l, SS 
A
30 mg/l); improvement of the Una River's surface water quality (BOD < 5 mg/l, COD < 69 mg/l, E.coli < 10^2 MPN/100ml); and achieving 100% coverage of operating costs from tariff revenues upon commissioning. The wastewater treatment concept was designed to meet future EU standards.

The targeted outcomes for 'Umweltkreditlinie SIDBI (ZV)' included: reducing industrial pollution impacts and health risks; increasing MSME investments in eco-friendly production and sustainable disposal solutions; boosting MSMEs' contribution to ecologically sustainable growth; and expanding SIDBI's financing options through new financial products. Specific targets at the outset were: at least EUR 48 million in replacement investments by ultimate borrowers within two years post-disbursement, and SIDBI maintaining a gross Non-Performing Assets ratio of maximum 2%. For success, it was recognized that sub-loans would likely require substantial subsidization to adequately incentivize the targeted replacement investments.

["Targeted outcomes known at the beginning: 1. Overall Objective (Outcome Level): Supported communities in Northeast Namibia possess rights, capabilities, and resource information to sustainably utilize community forests. 2. Impact Level: Supported community forests are preserved as a livelihood basis. 3. Specific Results: 15 CFs established, effectively/sustainably managed with management plans; CFs integrated into Conservancies; income generation promoted; DoF enabled to monitor/support CFs; strategic steering by an established PSK.", "Success was defined as achieving the following outcomes: Supported communities in Northeast Namibia possess rights, capabilities, and resource information to sustainably utilize community forests; supported community forests are preserved as a livelihood basis; 15 CFs established, effectively/sustainably managed with management plans; CFs integrated into Conservancies; income generation promoted; DoF enabled to monitor/support CFs; and strategic steering by an established PSK."]

The targeted outcomes known at the beginning of the activity, and necessary for success as then defined, included: the submission of at least three additional legal regulations or contractual frameworks for promoting renewable energy and/or energy efficiency to the decision-making body for adoption; organizational changes in two out of four key authorities' organizational units in accordance with the new regulatory framework; improved technical and management capacities among at least 70% of 400 surveyed experts and managers who participated in training activities; and the submission of at least one dissemination programme to promote renewable energy and energy efficiency technologies to the decision-making body for approval.

["The overarching goal of the activity was for relevant actors in Mauritania to utilize improved capacities and instruments for climate change adaptation in coastal cities.", "Targeted outcomes focused on enhancing capacities and improving planning frameworks and instruments for climate-resilient urban development at national, municipal, and local levels through three intervention areas.", "These included: 1) Mainstreaming climate change adaptation by developing methods and instruments, strengthening technical and organizational capacities of decision-makers and staff, training public officials, organizing sensitization workshops, and fostering international networking.", "2) Improving the knowledge base for climate-resilient coastal management and urban planning through off- and on-the-job trainings for staff managing Information Management Systems (IMS).", "3) Developing and implementing model pilot projects (e.g., closing breaches in the dune belt and replanting dunes) to strengthen the planning and implementing capacities of national and municipal stakeholders.", "Specific targeted outcomes (Objective Indicators) included: 1) Three institutions in charge of coastal city administration proving enhanced management capacities in coordinating and planning climate change adaptation, with two concrete examples for each institution.", "2) Three project-initiated pilot measures for climate change adaptation (for Nouakchott and the coastal zone) being integrated into three urban or township development plans for continuation or scaling up, with 50% of beneficiaries from two measures being women.", "3) Enhancement of management capacities, largely referring to the development, planning, coordination, and implementation of pilot projects.", "4) Pilot project development and implementation with specific consideration for at least 50% women in the target group.", "Success was defined as stakeholders' ability to use their acquired capacities by applying strategic and legislative documents, utilizing the information system for knowledge management, and enabling large-scale replication of model solutions."]

The activity primarily aimed to secure the electricity supply for refugees at Zaatari Camp and stabilize the grid for host communities in northern Jordan. It sought to contribute to reducing the economic and social consequences of the Syrian war by providing a more ecologically sustainable electricity supply, exploiting Jordan's solar energy potential. Success criteria included covering 75% of the refugee camp's electricity demand with the PV system (targeting 15 GWh), ensuring a contractually guaranteed energy quantity of 24.4 GWh per year, and reducing congestion in the electricity grid of northern Jordan.

The activity aimed to enhance the capacity of local and national actors in South-Eastern Europe's solid waste and circular economy sectors to reduce waste discharges into the Mediterranean Sea and its tributary rivers. Targeted outcomes included: 1) Fostering improved regional cooperation in reducing waste discharge into the Adriatic Sea, involving agreement on common monitoring approaches and signed agreements for plastic leakage reduction. 2) Developing enhanced national instruments to curb plastic waste leakage into water bodies, specifically by drafting national legislation on single-use plastics and implementing EPR guidelines. 3) Implementing local measures to diminish waste discharge into water bodies and the Adriatic Sea, including pilot projects engaging approximately 300,000 inhabitants to reduce plastic waste entry. The project also intended to contribute to EU standards and SDGs related to reducing marine pollution, mitigating city environmental impacts, reducing waste generation, improving water quality, and reducing pollution-related illnesses. Success was defined by achieving the overarching objective and these three key results.

The Green Economic Growth Programme (GEGP) aims to: 1) provide technical support for bottom-up SME development; 2) increase local authorities' confidence in economic management and attract investors; 3) identify and support SMEs to inspire a green investment strategy, catalyzing further green investment via public and private finance. It directly targets overcoming five critical barriers: weak capacity of SHFs/SMEs, market access difficulties for remote SHFs/SMEs, limited credit access, lack of coordination among supporting agencies, and high costs/rent seeking for permits. Overall success hinges on successful SHFs/SMEs catalyzing green investment and the effective overcoming of these critical barriers for sustainable long-term impact.

Targeted Outcomes: By 2018, 15% urban poverty reduction (<1 in 6 below poverty line); 400K urban poor supported (planning/management); local service quality enhanced for 300K people; 40% proposed projects completed; 80K additional projects managed (resilience); >10% local government projects audited. Immediate outcomes: improved living conditions for 8% urban poor/vulnerable (5 yrs); vocational training for rural youth; livelihoods for 300K in disadvantaged districts; 6 earthquake-resistant schools (>800K students); 100 local health posts established. Success requires: effective central/local government coordination, financial/natural disaster risk management, strong focus on underserved communities, leveraging past experience.

The activity targets improving fiscal and tax policies for revenue mobilisation and certainty, enhancing public spending in economic and social sectors, improving budget execution, and strengthening financial accountability. It also aims to improve government spending efficiency and enable results in sectors like education and water. Overall success would involve achieving these targets, effectively resolving weaknesses in fiscal/tax policies, low budget credibility, unpredictable fund flows, and the weakening business environment. Crucially, success requires Tanzania to continue reducing aid dependency towards eventual graduation from aid, sustained improvement in government spending efficiency, and effective adaptation of activities based on performance and context.

The activity targets developing and testing a scalable model of climate-resilient health and education facilities to maintain and improve access to services, strengthen health and education outcomes, improve service delivery environments, reduce disruption from climate events, and limit environmental impacts while ensuring energy reliability. Success requires the model to be demonstrably scalable and adoptable by State Governments, proving the effectiveness and value for money of low-cost interventions through rigorous research, and achieving strengthened technical capacity at facility and community levels for climate resilience and WASH infrastructure.

["The Evidence for Health (E4H) activity targets contributing to a more inclusive, prosperous, and stable Pakistan, less reliant on aid, and more resilient to shocks. It aims to strengthen the health sector, focusing on COVID-19, vaccination, disease surveillance, long-term health security, and climate-resilient health systems. Key outcomes include providing technical assistance for flood relief and climate resilience to benefit 386,000 children and 43,000 pregnant women in KP, and 940,000 children and 110,000 pregnant women in Punjab. It seeks to influence Pakistan's National Health Support Programme (NHSP) results by increasing the Universal Health Coverage (UHC) index from 49.9% to 60%, improving antenatal coverage from 51% to 60%, and increasing modern contraceptive use from 25% to 30% nationally, particularly in flood-affected regions. Furthermore, it aims to strengthen equitable primary care delivery and ensure the effectiveness of World Bank investments through robust MERL.", "Overall success for the E4H activity hinges on achieving its specific numerical targets: an increase in the Universal Health Coverage (UHC) index from 49.9% to 60%, improved antenatal coverage from 51% to 60%, and increased use of modern contraceptives from 25% to 30% nationally, especially in flood-affected regions. Success also requires effectively delivering technical assistance and health services to the targeted 386,000 children and 43,000 pregnant women in KP, and 940,000 children and 110,000 pregnant women in Punjab. Additionally, it must ensure effective short-term flood relief, progress in building climate-resilient health systems, strengthened equitable primary care delivery, and demonstrated effectiveness of World Bank investments through robust monitoring, evaluation, research, and learning (MERL). Adherence to gender equality benchmarks and mitigation of related risks are also crucial for overall success."]

The targeted outcomes of the Tanzania - Backbone Transmission Investment Project are to improve the sufficiency, security, and reliability of grid-based power for business enterprises, households, and large industrial customers in Northwest Tanzania. This is to be achieved by increasing the capacity of the transmission system to supply power to this region, which is experiencing a fast-paced electricity demand increase of 8-10% annually. Specific objectives include the construction of an approximately 650 km HVAC 400 kV backbone transmission line from Iringa to Shinyanga via Mtera, Dodoma, and Singida, the development of associated facilities for rural electrification along this new line, and the installation of Optical Fiber composite overhead ground wires (OPGW) on the proposed line, including an optical terminal and access equipment at Shinyanga. Additionally, the project aims to enhance TANESCO's capacity in designing, developing, and executing investment projects in both the public and private sectors.To consider the activity an overall success, several conditions would need to be met. Primarily, the project must successfully deliver improved sufficiency, security, and reliability of grid-based power to business enterprises, households, and large industrial customers in Northwest Tanzania, effectively increasing the transmission system's capacity to meet the region's rapidly growing electricity demand and alleviating the overloading of the existing 220kV transmission line. This includes the successful construction and operationalization of the 650 km HVAC 400 kV backbone transmission line, associated rural electrification facilities, and the installation of OPGW. Furthermore, the technical assistance component must successfully strengthen TANESCO's capacity in project design, development, and execution, thereby mitigating implementation risks and enhancing the long-term sustainability of power infrastructure development. Overall success also hinges on effective environmental and social management, including the successful implementation of the Environmental and Social Impact Management Plan (ESMP) and a comprehensive Resettlement Action Plan (RAP) to address land acquisition challenges and mitigate impacts on affected houses and structures, ensuring that any necessary resettlements are handled justly and effectively. The project's contribution to supporting the anticipated national long-term economic growth rate of 6.8% per annum by providing robust power infrastructure would also be a key indicator of success.

The targeted outcomes are to expand domestic power generation capacity to support sustained economic growth in Botswana and build institutional capacity for sustainable development of the energy sector, encompassing enhanced power systems, improved Morupule A operations, and strengthened regulatory capacity. For overall success, effectively expanding 600 MW generation capacity and building institutional capacity are essential. Crucially, environmental and social risks must be effectively addressed through planned mitigation measures and robust capacity building in Botswana institutions, alongside achieving the lowest possible cost of energy.

The targeted outcomes are to improve mobility and accessibility within and to Xiangcheng District of Xiangyang in an integrated, efficient, and safe manner. A key focus is demonstrating integrated improvements in public transport services, traffic safety for pedestrians, and traffic management on selected corridors. Success requires successful implementation of all components, adherence to Environmental Assessment and Involuntary Resettlement policies, authorization of the Subsidiary Agreement, maintenance of a well-resourced PMO, provision of loan proceeds to XCIC, strong inter-agency coordination, and capacity building, all built upon a solid project foundation that met readiness criteria and incorporated best practices.

The activity targets increasing market production of agriculture and forest products by rural communities and reducing net deforestation in project areas. It aims for rural development through integrating economic activity and natural resource conservation. Key outcomes include climate-smart agricultural value chain development, improved management of natural and planted forests, nature-based tourism, and community-based sports hunting. Security of community land rights, improved land administration, and stronger community rights to natural resources are crucial. The project seeks to maximize resource value for rural communities via private sector engagement, strengthening processor-trader linkages, and enabling climate-resilient development to capture international climate finance. It also focuses on improving community land rights through delimitation, supporting land use planning, and building capacity for land administration and local-level institutional management of resources.

Targeted outcomes include enhanced municipal infrastructure & services (water, public transport, solid waste, energy, social infra, firefighting, resilience, pollution reduction) & increased Sub-borrower capacity in design, construction, & safeguards. Success requires Sub-projects to be technically feasible, economically/financially viable, environmentally beneficial/sustainable, comply with ESMF/LARPF/environmental laws, and Sub-borrowers maintaining satisfactory resources. Effective project management, monitoring, & reporting by the Borrower are also crucial for overall success.

The targeted outcomes of the Wasserkraftwerk Middle Marsyangdi (Inv.) activity, known at the beginning, were to contribute to an efficient, reliable, long-term secure, environmentally friendly, and climate-friendly power supply. Specific targets included achieving a peak load of at least 70 MW, generating a minimum of 398 GWh of electrical energy, and producing at least 100 GWh of electrical energy during peak load times. Additionally, a sufficient mandatory water quantity for ecological reasons was to be discharged, although its specific volume was not defined at appraisal. The project aimed to reduce the gap between electricity demand and supply, an indispensable prerequisite for economic growth, job creation, and improved living conditions through increased grid access.

["The activity targeted the proper collection and treatment of domestic and partly pre-treated commercial wastewater in Mateur, El Alia, Ras Jebel, Raf Raf, and smaller neighboring municipalities to improve sanitation.", "It aimed to contribute to preventive water protection for Tunisia's Ichkeul and Bizerte lakes, and the Mediterranean Sea, as well as protect groundwater resources.", "The activity intended to reduce health risks and improve living conditions for the population in the Bizerte region, also fostering a positive impact on tourism.", "Success was defined by serving a target population of 120,000 inhabitants.", "A 90% connection rate for households and an 80% connection rate for commercial entities to the sewage network were targeted.", "Success was defined by treating 10,200 m³/d of wastewater (originally with three treatment plants) with effluent BSB5 values below 30 mg/l.", "Achieving 95% recycling or controlled disposal of sewage sludge was also a defined target for success."]

The targeted outcomes of the activity included contributing to water and environmental protection, reducing health risks from contaminated ground and surface water, and treating municipal wastewater according to valid Chinese standards to ensure adequate water protection. To achieve success, the following targeted outcomes were necessary, to be measured three years after commissioning: 70% average capacity utilization for financed wastewater treatment plants; 100% compliance of individual projects with legally prescribed discharge values (at least GB 8978-88); 100% of individual projects meeting requirements for eco-friendly disposal or recycling of sewage sludge; 70% of private households and 60% of businesses connected to the sewerage system. The overarching goal would be considered achieved upon fulfilling the module-level objective.

The primary overall objective was to contribute to increasing forest cover, promoting positive effects from forests, and actively involving private forest owners in supporting Costa Rican forest policy. The specific program goal was to ensure the agreed management and preservation of contracted areas in the program region and to realize the positive environmental effects stemming from private forest areas. For success, the activity targeted contracting 75,500 hectares of land, with planned sub-targets of 60,000 ha for natural forest protection, 7,900 ha for reforestation, 6,000 ha for sustainable natural forest management, 100,000 trees for agroforestry, and achieving 80% adherence to agreed management guidelines.

The primary objective at appraisal was to contribute to an economically efficient supply of environmentally friendly electrical energy for the national grid, with economic efficiency tied to achieving low or acceptable CO2 avoidance costs. The intended impact was to contribute to global climate protection. Key targets for success, from the second complete operating year, included: an annual average supply of 320 GWh of electrical energy; annual avoidance of 180,000 tons of CO2 emissions (or 171,500 tons as per CDM PDD); a CO2 avoidance cost of 10 USD per ton of CO2; and technical availability greater than 95%.

The activity targeted instituting effective protected area management, generating additional, environmentally friendly income sources for local populations in and around protected areas, and increasing agricultural yields, aiming to conserve forests while boosting residents' income. Success would require: proper implementation of protected area specifications, consolidated and improved management, reduced fires, effective monitoring and control, controlled land use, utilization of infrastructure, offsetting operating costs, demarcation of protected areas, institutionalized conflict resolution, and income growth for target groups.

The targeted outcomes of the "Drinking Water Supply" activity were to ensure a reliable, clean water supply for citizens in eight selected Serbian medium-sized cities (projected to benefit approximately 500,000 people for Programme I and 400,000 for Programme II). This was intended to be achieved at cost-covering and socially acceptable prices, thereby improving living conditions for the target group. Immediate measures included supplying and installing water meters, procuring leak detection equipment, and replacing transport and distribution pipelines to reduce leakage losses. Complementary measures aimed to enhance the economic sustainability of individual water utilities through analyses, optimization strategies, and establishing a benchmark system. A performance-based approach was designed to enable municipalities and public utility companies to prioritize urgent investments, considering sustainable operation and financial capacities. Success was defined by achieving these outcomes: a continuous, 24-hour per day water supply, and tariff income covering operating costs, debt service, and at least EUR 20 per invoiced house connection for maintenance, all while maintaining socially acceptable prices.

The National solid waste management programme (NSWMP) targeted a powerful, sustainably effective waste management system. Success required establishing: 1. An independent supervisory authority (WMRA) with budget/technical authority, a national sector strategy, and municipal waste framework legislation. 2. SWMUs in four governorates with mandates/personnel for planning/controlling sustainable waste management. 3. Exemplary models for labor-intensive, resource-efficient, cost-effective municipal waste collection/recycling in one municipality per each of four governorates. Five SMART success indicators with defined target values were formulated for achievement assessment.

The activity targeted an increase in energy efficiency in the DPDC power supply system and an improvement in system reliability, quality, and transmission capacity, aiming to reduce electricity losses. Success would be achieved by increasing transmission power within the DPDC network by 570 MVA to the 132 kV level, increasing voltage in the 132 kV distribution system by at least 5%, transmitting an additional 1,600 GWh of electricity three years after commissioning, connecting 72,000 new end customers three years after commissioning, and achieving an average utilization of 50% for installed 132/33 kV transformers at peak load.

At the beginning of the Windparkprogramm BNDES activity, the targeted outcomes were an installed capacity of 80 MW, electric power sold of 200,000 GWh/year, and CO2 avoidance of 54,000 t/year. To achieve success as defined at the outset, these specific targets for installed capacity, electricity sales, and CO2 avoidance would need to be met or exceeded.

The targeted outcomes were to significantly reduce Transmission & Distribution (T&D) losses and provide a reliable, eco-friendly, and affordable power supply in rural areas, improving living conditions. It was also anticipated to increase rural productivity, contribute to economic growth, and increase women's labor market participation. Overarching goals included contributing to sustainable development and reducing greenhouse gas emissions. Success would be achieved if technical T&D losses were 10% or less, grid availability was at least 95%, high-quality electricity complied with Vietnam's Distribution Code/Grid Code (or EN 51160 criteria), and 185,000 tonnes of CO2 emissions were annually avoided.

Targeted outcomes for the 'Programm Wasserversorgung und Abwassermanagement' activity included strengthening institutional preconditions for water and wastewater companies, enabling Affiliated Companies (ACs) to provide more efficient, effective, and sustainable services, and enhancing service reach in underserviced areas. Specific targets necessary for success were the introduction of one performance index for AC services by end of 2014, a 10% reduction in operating cost subsidies for six regional water and wastewater companies (to 40% of income from subventions), and 600 water rangers (50% women) active in informal area schools.

The activity targeted ASEAN developing and implementing regionally coordinated climate protection policies/strategies in agriculture and forestry. Success required: 1) Adoption of 2 regional climate protection strategies/policies by AMAF. 2) Presentation of a common ASEAN position at 3 international conferences/negotiations on climate change. 3) 70% of agreed priority areas and value chains being relevant for improving women's situation.

The activity targets enhancing capacities of the Ministry of Water Resources and Irrigation (MWRI), the Ministry of Agriculture and Land Reclamation (MALR), and water users for implementing integrated water management (IWRM) to achieve efficient and sustainable water use. Success depends on: 1. Four units at MALR/MWRI having IWRM-compliant mandates. 2. Implementing a joint action plan with water user groups in three districts (Mahmoudia, Sidi Ghazi, Qena). 3. Adopting two measures for three polluted drainage ditches in the Western Delta. 4. 70% of 200 female farmers finding gender-specific measures useful.

Targeted outcomes of the "Masterplan im Wassersektor" activity, known at the beginning of the activity, were: to provide improved services in the water sector, and to achieve an efficient and transparent allocation of funds within the water sector. This was to be accomplished by developing a national water master plan for short, medium, and long-term planning. The master plan would include a report with an investment plan derived from a national needs analysis for the water sector, integrate national development strategies, and provide a water sector analysis, all based on a Geographic Information System (GIS). All future investments in the country's water supply and sewerage sectors were to be collated in this master plan and prioritized according to agreed criteria.Intensive consultation with other donors at a very early stage of implementation was planned to cover aspects like specifications for the consultant and prioritization criteria, aiming to ensure broad stakeholder buy-in.To achieve success as defined at the beginning of the activity, the necessary targeted outcomes would be: the successful creation of an up-to-date and transparent water master plan that serves as the basis for the Albanian government's investment decisions and is supported by international donors. This master plan must enable the efficient and transparent allocation of funds within the water sector by effectively collating and prioritizing all future investments in water supply and sewerage sectors according to agreed criteria.

The activity aimed to strengthen the role of municipalities as a driving force for implementing energy efficiency (EE) measures. Targeted outcomes included improved legal frameworks for EE, enhanced service provider offerings, and municipalities developing operational energy management (MEM) systems to increase EE measures, energy conservation, and climate change mitigation. Success entailed 6 municipalities operating an MEM system (defined by dedicated unit, budget, plan, regular tasks, and initial energy inventory), 4 legal document proposals submitted to decision-makers, and 8 supported networks conducting 8 gender-sensitive activities.

The activity targeted improving Zambian water sector legal, organizational, and institutional frameworks to boost poor populations' access to clean drinking water and sanitation, and foster integrated water resource management, considering climate change. Specific targets included establishing a transparent financial mechanism for urban/peri-urban water/sanitation, implementing urban sanitation measures in four cities, enacting Gender/HIV/AIDS/Hygiene policies in at least five Commercial Utilities, completing 2013/2014 Joint Water Sector Review reports verifying access, and achieving 33% WRMA cost coverage from water fees. Success was defined by achieving these improvements and targets.

Targeted outcomes of the activity included improving wastewater and waste management services in selected municipalities and regions to align with EU environmental legislation, and stimulating further investment in pollution control. Success required developing capacity for new waste and wastewater management structures in five pilot municipalities, strengthening institutional capacity, introducing circular economy practices, fostering inter-municipal cooperation for regional solutions, and promoting innovative approaches to raise environmental awareness and facilitate new services in line with EU standards.

["The primary targeted outcome was for public and private actors at provincial and district levels to increasingly apply experiences from pilot measures towards sustainable biodiversity management and conservation. This included generating good practices in biodiversity management and climate change adaptation, leading to increased income (especially for women), greater community awareness, and higher likelihood of biodiversity protection. It also aimed for changed attitudes among farmers and staff, resulting in future proposals emphasizing biodiversity, and strengthening the legal framework.", "Key targeted outcomes necessary for success included the adoption of one Biodiversity Action Plan for the province, incorporating measures for sustainable land, forest, and water resource management.
