The overall impact is rural vitalization in the Yangtze River Economic Belt, with improved ecosystem services, livelihood opportunities, and climate resilience in Dabie Mountain. Success requires achieving these benefits, including an estimated GHG reduction of 34,636.940 tons per annum and eventually net-zero emissions. Measurable indicators include training 30,000 farmers/fisherfolk and 300 government staff, improving rural infrastructure (wastewater treatment plants, pipelines, river/forest rehabilitation), developing 2,200 ha of climate-smart agriculture, and supporting 30 MSMEs. Sustained economic viability and financial sustainability, ensuring willingness to pay for ecosystem services, are crucial.

Targeted outcomes aimed for Ethiopia to have a consolidated system to preserve its biodiversity and forests, improving local livelihoods. The specific project outcome was for institutions managing protected areas and forests to be enabled to implement strategies for biodiversity protection and sustainable use broadly. Success required enhanced organizational, conceptual, and technical capacities of institutions at all levels, broad adoption of improved park management and community engagement approaches, regional governments adopting biosphere reserve management plans, and key agencies translating knowledge into guidelines. Visible economic effects on populations, reduced anthropogenic impact, sufficient institutional capacity, and increased forest cover were also targeted.

The targeted outcomes are to modernize ANAM and local governments' capacity for environmental management, enhance environmental management instruments for competitiveness and sustainability, strengthen ANAM's regional and local governments' capacities, and consolidate a water information system for resource administration and participatory management in key watersheds. Overall success requires ANAM's commitment, submission of monitoring reports, midterm and final evaluations, agreements on economic/financial instruments and new regulations, ANAM's continued watershed management policy, and effective inter-institutional collaboration.

Targeted outcomes include improving connectivity and conserving biodiversity, strengthening institutions for low-carbon management and peace-building, preventing deforestation, improving quality of life through sustainable production and forest management, and increasing sustainable landscapes. Overall success will be achieved when 80,000 hectares of sustainable production landscapes reduce deforestation/emissions and promote peace, 1,655 hectares of new sustainable production systems are established, key species are protected in consolidated connectivity areas, 300 conflict-victim families implement sustainable production, 400 producers benefit from economic mechanisms, and knowledge on low-carbon rural development/environmental management/peace-building is improved.

The targeted outcomes of Subprogram 2 are to achieve a reliable, sustainable, and affordable energy system, which includes increasing gas supply to 5 billion scfd by June 2017, raising private investment in the power sector to 23% by June 2018, reducing average power distribution and transmission system losses to 17.86% by June 2017, decreasing unaccounted for gas to 8% by June 2018, and lowering power sector subsidies to 0.3%-0.4% of GDP by June 2016. Additionally, outputs aim to manage tariffs and subsidies by reducing tariff determination time to within 4 months by June 2017, improve sector performance, and enhance accountability and transparency.

The activity targets improving practices and the enabling environment for forest and land management. Key outcomes include reducing deforestation by 40% and restoring 1 million hectares of forests by 2030. It aims to increase commercial forest plantation areas from 60,000 to over 1 million hectares by 2030. Benefits for 163,000 households and 3,300 small/medium landholders include land titles, economic opportunities, improved inputs, technical assistance, training, and market access. Government institutions will receive strategic planning and governance support. Indirect benefits include improved governance, reduced deforestation, and GHG emissions. Quantifiable results include increased land area and users adopting sustainable land management practices, improved forest governance scores, and a high share of satisfied beneficiaries regarding information and participation in forest and land-use decisions.

The activity targeted enhancing soil productivity of agroforestry plots (Wadis) to improve indigenous Adivasi living conditions. Outcomes included higher agricultural yields and incomes, strengthened advocacy, sustainable management, improved ecosystem services (erosion control, water balance, microclimate, CO2 sequestration), and stronger self-help capacity. A secondary goal was reducing poverty-induced seasonal migration and Adivasi uprooting. Success required at least a 50% increase in participating families' real income and a decrease in seasonal migration for survival reasons.

The targeted outcomes included: natural resources in and around forest areas managed, protected, and utilized based on sustainability criteria by rural populations, businesses, municipal administrations, and state institutions. Success would require: expanding forest area under protection/sustainable management to 50,000 ha; expanding agroforestry areas under sustainable management to 2,000 ha; 75% of indigenous population believing their land tenure and food production capacities improved; and 75% of advised men/women believing women participate more in natural resource decisions. Additionally, improved political/institutional frameworks, enhanced development planning and land rights, increased local actor competitiveness and income from sustainable resource use, and strengthened capacity of partner institutions were targeted.

The activity targeted supporting Algerian actors in acquiring improved capacities and effective instruments for the National Climate Plan (PNC) implementation, aiming to reduce negative climate change effects, contributing to SDG 13. Success required creating an institutional framework for PNC implementation, testing climate change integration instruments in sectoral policies and local planning, strengthening capacities within relevant ministerial departments and institutions, and improving civil society's awareness of climate change.

The Integrated Urban Water Management Project aims to improve access to and efficiency of water supply and wastewater management for urban households and other customers in the People's Republic of China. The primary targeted outcome is the improved delivery and management of water and wastewater services in second- and third-tier cities. Key outputs include the construction of water supply and wastewater treatment plants and associated engineering works, and the generation of local employment.  To be considered an overall success, the project needs to achieve the following by the specified deadlines: by 2025, the amount of water treated and distributed must increase to 6.20 million m

3

per day (from a 2016 baseline of 2.31 million m

3

per day), and the amount of treated wastewater must increase to 2.50 million m

3

per day (from a 2016 baseline of 0.73 million m

3

per day). Additionally, at least CNY2.00 billion must be paid to the government during construction and operation from 2018 to 2025, and by 2022, at least 1.5 million additional households must have new or improved water supply. By 2021, urban water subprojects with a total value of CNY9.00 billion must be constructed, contributing to an installed water treatment capacity of 10.00 million m

3

per day and an installed wastewater treatment capacity of 1.80 million m

3

per day. The project also needs to manage 5,100 km of sewage pipeline networks and provide at least 2,000 jobs during the construction phase by 2021.

The targeted outcomes include supporting Mauritius' transition to a competitive economy by addressing infrastructure bottlenecks, improving national infrastructure via a high-quality Public Sector Investment Program (PSIP), enhancing road network conditions through upgrading and performance-based maintenance, alleviating urban transport congestion, establishing a framework for enhanced energy security (including increased renewable energy), and increasing water/wastewater access and network coverage to 50% by 2012. Institutional reforms for water/energy regulation and the Land Transport Authority are also targeted. Success will be measured by the PSIP's capital budget disbursement ratio, the number/value of implemented infrastructure projects, reduced travel times and accidents on Quartier Militaire Road, and increased sewerage network connections to 50% of households by 2012.

The activity targets strengthening local government financing, improving rural service delivery, and enabling immediate emergency response. It aims for long-term sustainability, Guinea's decentralization consolidation, and institutionalization, supporting governance, participation, anti-corruption, and human capital development. Success requires 50% of FNDL funds executed by CRs, 179 CRs using participatory budgeting/M&E, 85% beneficiary satisfaction, and 3.326M direct beneficiaries. Also crucial are an operational FNDL, 100% trained mayors/staff, 100% updated participatory Local Development Plans, 15% mining revenue transfers to local communities, 65% CRs increasing annual revenue by 10%, and 50% women/youth in committees.

The targeted outcomes of the Integrated Water Management and Development Project are to improve access to water supply and sanitation services, enhance integrated water resources management, and boost the operational performance of water and sanitation service providers within the Project areas. Specific outcomes include increasing the availability of safe drinking water, reducing travel distances to safe water supply and sanitation (WSS) systems, securing water sources for sustainable supply, improving sanitary conditions at household and community levels, strengthening the resilience of WSS systems to water-related shocks, building capacity for modern land use management and improved agroforestry farming practices through catchment management activities, and empowering local communities and organizations to identify, prioritize, and support water-related infrastructure and services investments. The project also aims to enhance the quality of life by reducing the time and effort women and children spend collecting water, reducing the incidence of waterborne diseases, and diminishing absenteeism from work and school. To be considered an overall success, the activity must achieve the following measurable targets: it must benefit 1,403,000 direct project beneficiaries, with 51 percent being female and 7 percent refugees. It needs to bring 47,209 hectares under integrated water resources management in selected catchments. Furthermore, the project must significantly improve the operational performance of water and sanitation service providers by increasing the percentage of service areas achieving a cost recovery ratio of 1.1 from a baseline of 38 percent to 80 percent. Ultimately, the project needs to provide 1,151,862 people with access to improved water sources, including 587,450 females.

Improve Georgia State Electrosystem (GSE)'s financial sustainability, autonomy, and performance by enhancing operational structure, efficiency, corporate governance, financial management, transparency, and debt sustainability. Key outcomes include improved EnergoTrans revenues for transmission investments (target RAB 17.5%), increased women's participation in energy sector training to 30%, and creating GEL460 million (1% of GDP) budgetary headroom for critical investments to foster economic growth and reliable electricity.

The activity targets improving urban management and access to infrastructure in selected urban areas, particularly for poor neighborhoods, and increasing resilience to natural hazards. Success requires: improved urban living conditions for 651,400 people (313,310 female); protecting 280.00 hectares from a 10-year flooding event; and a target number of cities achieving at least 80% of their City Contract performance indicators for livability, sustainability, and management. Other factors include updated urban databases, functional asset management, satisfactory maintenance by CTDs, and high beneficiary satisfaction.

The activity targets improving technical efficiency and reliability of Uzbekistan's power transmission networks by upgrading substations in various regions, benefiting 31.8 million people through enhanced electricity services. It aims to boost UE's financial and operational efficiency via reduced system losses, O&M expenses, and GHG emissions. Further outcomes include building system resilience with emergency outage management systems and strengthening UE's financial management, corporate governance, and transition to IFRS, as mandated by a Presidential Decree.

The targeted outcomes were to strengthen the capacities of pastoralists and agro-pastoralists for sustainable natural resource management, improving their livelihoods and drought resilience. Success required: 1. Optimized access to water, pasture, and arable land with sustainable use knowledge. 2. Coordinated usage agreements and land-use planning. 3. Strengthened local service provider capacities for sustainable resource management. 4. Improved political framework for conflict-sensitive rural development in Afar. This would ultimately lead to increased drought resilience, climate change adaptation, and optimized living conditions, income, and food and nutrition security.

The activity targets improving the operational framework to reduce CO2 emission intensities in Egypt's power supply and consumption. Outcomes include sustainable power grids, long-term GHG emission reduction via renewable energy, and improved national energy efficiency. This aims to provide a reliable, cost-efficient energy supply with reduced energy intensity and integrated renewable energy, contributing to economic growth, social equity, and climate protection. Success involves NREA establishing 1 standard land demarcation methodology for renewable energy areas (e.g., West Nile) and EgyptERA holding 3 biannual meetings to analyze energy audit impacts and reduce CO2 emissions.

The targeted outcomes are: improved environmental conditions and quality of life for Georgetown and 15 NDCs by providing a sustainable solid waste solution. Specific outputs include completing 2.5 km of access road construction, rehabilitating 1.5 km of access road, and finishing Cell one of the Haags Bosch Sanitary Landfill (HBSLF) to receive 273 tonnes/day of waste. Overall success requires achieving these outputs, ensuring HBSLF Cell one is operational and receiving 273 tonnes/day of waste, and compliance with contractual and environmental safeguards (EIA/ESMR recommendations, General Norms Article 4.01(a), (b), (d), (e)).

Achieve Viet Nam's net-zero by 2050 commitment. Intermediate outcomes include: just, inclusive, and sustainable JETP implementation; improved NDC implementation; and scaled-up successful models in climate change adaptation, disaster risk reduction, preparedness, and resilience-building for enhanced human security.

Targeted outcomes include reducing open defecation, achieving "Swachh Bharat" by Oct 2, 2019, accelerating rural sanitation coverage, improving rural quality of life, motivating communities to adopt sustainable sanitation, encouraging cost-effective technologies, developing community-managed SLWM, and strengthening MDWS program management capacity. Success requires significant reduction in open defecation, sustained ODF status, increased rural population with SLWM, and MDWS operationalizing the Performance Incentive Grant Scheme. It also hinges on a time-bound, results-oriented framework, adequate financial resources, state flexibility, independent verification, and a robust M&E system.

The OECS Regional Health Project aims to improve preparedness capacities of health systems for public health emergencies in the OECS region. Targeted outcomes include improvements in laboratory services, harmonization of public health policies and procedures, enhanced regional collaboration on public health surveillance and emergency response, and resource sharing of high-cost specialized assets. It also involves providing immediate surge funding for public health emergencies via the Contingent Emergency Response Component (CERC) and strengthening health systems to withstand extreme weather events, while supporting training in the Minimum Initial Service Package for Sexual and Reproductive Health in disasters. The activity will be considered an overall success when 4.00 countries achieve a health system capacity to withstand extreme weather events based on an A-70 rating on Smart Health Facility standards. Country-specific targets for this capacity are: Dominica (3.00), Grenada (5.00), Saint Lucia (14.00), and Saint Vincent and the Grenadines (3.00).

The activity targets consolidating Mexico's institutional and operational framework for its climate change agenda, mainstreaming climate change in public policy, and implementing mitigation and adaptation tools. For this third operation, targeted outcomes include: maintaining macroeconomic stability; progress in executing the 2009-2012 PECC (105 objectives, 294 targets), adopting recommendations from "The Economics of Climate Change in Mexico" study adoption for PECC design, developing/evaluating additional PEACC guidelines, utilizing guidelines in five additional states, and training PEACC research groups. Mitigation outcomes aim for continuous CDM promotion, expanded energy-saving agreements, authorization of sustainable electricity permits, financing at least one climate project (CTF/CIF), enforcing Sustainable Energy Use Act and Renewable Energy/Energy Transition Financing Act regulations, preparing federal GHG inventories, approving GHG inventory certification procedures, and strengthening mitigation action evaluation skills. Adaptation outcomes involve INE systemizing regional climate change data, implementing/advising on five additional adaptation actions, monitoring early warning programs by SEGOB, and progressing the National Civil Protection Program for risk prevention.

Targeted outcomes: Submission of Montenegro's 4NC/1BTR to UNFCCC by Dec 2024, engaging at least 100 direct beneficiaries (min. 50% female). Bolstering UNFCCC reporting capacity, integrating climate change into national/sectoral plans, and strengthening M&R institutional capacity. Overall success requires: Timely 4NC/1BTR submission by Dec 2024, achieving beneficiary targets, building national capacity for long-term reporting, formalizing data processes, establishing Enhanced Transparency Framework (ETF) compliance mechanisms, and addressing lack of coordinated climate finance expertise.

The targeted outcomes for the Emergency Multi-Sector Rohingya Crisis Response Project are to strengthen the Government of Bangladesh systems to improve access to basic services and build disaster and social resilience of the displaced Rohingya population. This involves enhanced access to water, sanitation, roads, street lighting, lightning protection systems, climate-resilient multipurpose disaster shelters, and increased participation in gender-sensitive community services, workfare, and GBV prevention. Success will be measured by: (1) the number of people (disaggregated by gender) with access to improved public infrastructure and climate resilient multi-purpose disaster shelters; (2) the number of households (disaggregated by gender) participating in community workfare and services; and (3) the development of a Displaced Rohingya Population (DRP) Camp Management System.

Achieve a more sustainable energy supply in selected regions of Kyrgyzstan, contributing to improved production opportunities and better living conditions, fostering economic and social development. Targeted outcomes for success include: reduction of commercial (non-technical) losses to 

≤ 20% in the meter component area, billing system, and illegal connection task force; reduction of technical losses by 30 GWh/year in the selective network rehabilitation area (Phase 1), with an overall target of 35 GWh loss reduction across both phases; reduction of power outages (SAIFI) to 600; approximately 80% collection efficiency among private customers; reduction of CO2 emissions by 3,700 tons per year (considering the financing share); and implementation of a new customer and billing system with smart meters, intended as a pilot for national rollout. Success also requires continued functionality of the rest of the electricity distribution grid, availability of sustainable new and replacement investments, and implementation of sustainable tariff increases.

Targeted outcomes included maintaining/rehabilitating forest/mangrove areas on Panay/Negros, increasing indigenous income, safeguarding/restoring biodiversity/ecosystem services, and improving household living conditions. Success required: establishing 9,000 hectares; 30% average income increase from livelihood measures (from PHP 12,562 baseline) and forest management (from PHP 17,085/family); average timber growth >7 m³/ha; volume/value increase of plantings matching 75% of estimates; 70% infrastructure maintained for 6 years; at least 67% positive perception by local population; biodiversity/water quality improvement; and soil erosion reduction from 25 to 10 t/ha.

The targeted outcomes include increasing MoEnv's capacity for policy-making and management. Specifically, establishing a monitoring system for environmental metadata, including a MoEnv web portal accessible to the public and capable staff for data updates. Additionally, setting up an e-monitoring system for hazardous waste to collect, monitor, and exchange data, enhancing MoEnv's governance capacities for hazardous waste management, reducing environmental risks, and contributing to a healthier environment. Improved capacity to prioritize environmental legislation and strengthened stakeholder cooperation are also key. Success necessitates that a MoEnv web portal is available, staff can update environmental data, an IT-based hazardous waste system is established and used, and capacities for environmental legislation and stakeholder cooperation are improved.

The activity targets the development and timely submission of BiH's BTR1 (Dec 2024) and combined NC5/BTR2 (Dec 2026) to the UNFCCC, following MPGs. Outcomes include updated National Inventory Reports (NIRs), comprehensive reporting on NDC implementation, adaptation, and support, and the integration of gender considerations (Rating 3) into NDC analysis and operational MRV systems, including a climate finance module. Overall success hinges on BiH's ability to develop MPG-compliant NIRs, operationalize the MRV system, effectively integrate gender data, and ensure timely government adoption and submission of both reports.

["The primary targeted outcome is to improve the sustainable management of coastal fisheries in the Project Provinces of Vietnam. This involves: improving sustainable and responsible marine/coastal aquaculture practices, leading to increased sustainability, quality, and risk management; improving resource management for near-shore capture fisheries including strengthening enforcement and monitoring; and rehabilitating fishing ports/landing sites for improved hygiene and reduced post-harvest losses. The project also aims to strengthen institutional capacity for sustainable fisheries management and improve inter-sectoral planning at provincial and local levels.", "To consider the activity an overall success by Year 5: 50% of farms must meet national water effluent standards via Good Aquaculture Practices (GAP), and shrimp disease losses in GAP-applying areas must be reduced by 20%. Also, 50% of areas need to apply sustainable Near-Shore fisheries resource management systems. Crucially, 100% of project provinces, districts, and communes must receive inter-sectoral planning training, and provincial planning teams must be established in all 8 project provinces. Additionally, 20,000 farmers should be trained in GAP, 16 districts should adopt near-shore co-management, and 16 fishing ports/landing sites should operate with improved hygiene."]

Targeted outcomes: Improved capacities of water sector institutions for planning, steering, and regulating water/wastewater management. This aimed for improved services, financially viable providers, and a secure water supply, even in emergencies. Specific outcomes included: strengthened PWA/Gaza regulating capacities; gender-sensitive strategies/bylaws, PWA Gender Unit; house connection bylaw plans; Gaza private water supplier baseline; WSRC regulatory tasks; Area C strategies; UPWSP WSP training; improved WSP performance, bylaw implementation (tariffs); PWA/Regulator emergency response. Success required achieving these specific, quantified targets, introducing tariffs, WSP regulation.

The targeted outcome for IP4Biodiv was to improve conditions for peaceful collaboration between indigenous peoples and other groups concerning biodiversity conservation in Agusan Marsh Wildlife Sanctuary and other biodiverse indigenous areas. Success for the module required: 1. Integrating conflict/gender-sensitive indigenous management approaches in PA management. 2. Establishing inclusive biodiversity monitoring in the PA. 3. Improving livelihoods for marginalized groups. 4. Incorporating relevant recommendations into national policies. The module contributed to the broader programme's success: reducing violent conflicts (targeting 33 conflicts addressed peacefully) and implementing gender/conflict-sensitive biodiversity management in three biodiverse areas with indigenous populations.

The activity targeted more efficient energy use in selected sectors (public buildings, facilities, agriculture, households, industry) in India, aiming to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and foster a sustainable energy supply. It also aimed to build EESL into a viable ESCO operation, capable of efficiently implementing projects and integrating environmental and social standards. Success required an average 20% reduction in final energy consumption for EESL investments, an internal rate of return of at least 10% for individual investments, and a positive net return from EESL's ESCO operations. Initial CO2e reduction targets were 25,000 t/year (Phase I) and 1,000,000 t/year (Phase II). A 50% price reduction for LED lamps was targeted for Phase II.

["At the outset of the 'Abwasserentsorgung Klein- und Mittelstädte II' activity, the primary outcome-level objective was the efficient, environmentally compatible, and proper collection, treatment, and disposal of household, commercial, and industrial wastewater in the programme sites. At the impact level, the goal was to contribute to improving the hygiene situation in the towns and to protecting regional above- and underground water resources.", "Success for the activity, as defined at the beginning, would require achieving the following targeted outcomes: a household connection rate of 70-95% and industrial connection rate of 70-100% to the wastewater network; treating 4,000-5,800 m³/day of wastewater; effluent quality with BSB 30 mg/l, CSB 90 mg/l, and SS 30 mg/l; a treatment plant utilization rate exceeding 70% at peak load; and complete/proper disposal of sewage sludge. No specific quantitative impact-level indicators for hygiene improvement or resource protection were defined at the outset."]

The activity targeted efficient protection of the four national parks (Bwabwata, Mudumu, Mamili, Khaudum) against usage pressure via improved management, ensuring their wildlife migration corridor function, and stabilizing/increasing wildlife populations. It also aimed for park residents and local populations to benefit economically, leading to fair access and sustainable management of natural resources, an improved environmental situation, stabilized production base, and increased rural incomes. Success meant achieving specific NAMEFTT index scores (e.g., Bwabwata: 75 by 2020), management plans existing and implemented in all four parks, weekly staff meetings, and stable or growing wildlife populations by 2020.

The activity's core outcome was to improve biodiversity conservation quality by federal and state protected area authorities in the Eje Neovolcánico. Success was defined by: 30% of projects including gender-differentiated measures; four civil society participation processes established for planning; 60% of protected area managers using effective planning/monitoring instruments; 90% federal and 50% state protected area data accessible in an information system; and one package of gender-differentiated recommendations/guidelines for natural resource management available. This involved enhanced cooperation, regional platforms, capacity building, and support for sustainable farming/tourism.

["The PEDECEL project aims for socio-economic development through universal electricity access in Ouagadougou (districts 8 & 3) and Saaba, correcting urban-rural disparities. Specific outcomes include extending/densifying the electrical distribution network, providing 8,125 connections for households and socio-economic points, and strengthening electricity sector actors' capacities. Physical targets for Lot 1 are 32km MV lines, 169km LV lines, 26 new MT/BT cabins, 14 new MT/BT substations, and 125 public lighting points. Socially, it targets job creation, commercial activity growth, and improved living conditions.", "Overall success hinges on diligent execution of environmental and social mitigation/monitoring strategies, including full ESMP implementation (budgeted at 53.65M FCFA), ensuring regulatory compliance, and strengthening ESMP management capacities. Achieving all physical outputs for Lot 1 (e.g., 8,125 connections, 32km MV, 169km LV lines, 40 substations, 125 public lights) and strengthening operational capacities of electricity sector actors are crucial. Continued strong government and AfDB support and alignment with national development plans are also vital."]

The activity targets reducing greenhouse gas emissions through urban green solutions and promoting resource-efficient growth. Key outcomes include implementing 2 energy-efficient demonstration projects and achieving a 50% energy consumption reduction by the end of 2019. It also aims to upgrade municipal lighting systems at two Yerevan demo sites to contemporary norms and identify projects for ESCO/PPP financing, providing support for negotiations with investors. Overall success requires the full implementation of these 2 demonstration projects, realizing the targeted 50% energy reduction, successful lighting system upgrades, and identification/support for ESCO/PPP financing.

The activity aims to enhance the Philippine Government's capacity to manage natural disaster impacts by strengthening institutional capacity for ex-ante and ex-post disaster risk management efforts, mainstreaming risk reduction into development planning, and better managing fiscal exposure. Specific outcomes include increased local government capacity to manage natural disasters through preparedness, risk reduction, and mitigation, as well as more resilient provincial and key sectoral investments.

The activity targets addressing immediate needs of drought-affected people and supporting resilient recovery by providing livelihood opportunities and restoring agricultural/pastoral production. Specific outcomes include direct support (food/cash) for up to 523,000 people, FAO Cash-for-Work for 109,800 rural people, improved safe water access for up to 656,000 people, and livestock support for 225,000 households (8.5M livestock). Overall success requires rapid, efficient implementation to achieve immediate impact and transition to medium-term recovery, effective use of 'Agility' and partners (ICRC/FAO), Cash-for-Work for food/assets, and meeting all specific beneficiary targets.

The targeted outcomes of the activity, known at its commencement, included enabling public and private stakeholders to apply economic approaches for biodiversity conservation, sustainable use, and fair benefit sharing. Specifically, success was defined by achieving the implementation of 5 out of 20 recommendations for integrating economic assessments of ecosystem services into national programs by Dec 2020; 15 out of 20 IBBI member companies using IBBI content for biodiversity management by Dec 2016; 150 (50%) women in Himachal Pradesh benefiting from ecotourism training by Dec 2020; two additional Western Himalayan states engaging in exchange on biodiversity experiences by Dec 2020; and six good ABS practices implemented by biodiversity management committees by Dec 2020.

Targeted outcomes include enhanced adaptive capacity for Syrians, green sustainable recovery, improved living conditions, and increased resilience, emphasizing gender equality and natural resource protection. Success is defined by achieving enhanced stakeholder capacity in sustainable resource management, improved community access to eco-friendly solutions, decreased vulnerability, increased adaptive capacity, improved resilience, and better social/economic conditions, with specific metrics like training 200 engineers and 1870 in sustainable agriculture.

["Targeted outcomes are China's preparation and submission of 4NC, 3BUR, 4BUR to UNFCCC, fulfilling Paris Agreement obligations. This involves enhancing national GHG inventory quality (transparency, accuracy) by preparing/recalculating for 2017, 2018, 2020, and historic 2005, 2010, 2012, 2014 across energy, industrial, agriculture, LULUCF, waste sectors (IPCC 1996/2006 guidelines). Also: improving GHG database, establishing QA/QC, strengthening governmental capacity for CCM/CCA, raising public awareness. HK/Macao SARs: improved emission understanding and NC/BUR capacities.", "Overall success requires: Direct benefit to 100 males, 120 females. 100% completion, government-approval, and submission of 4NC, 3BUR, 4BUR reports to UNFCCC. Key targets: 8 completed GHG inventories & 8 updated yearly data sets used in NC/BURs. 10 governmental entities capable in CCM/CCA areas. 1000 people with improved climate change understanding. For HK/Macao SARs, 3 SAR agencies capable of improved GHG inventories, V&A, and CCM/CCA measures. Also, 8 required reports prepared, translated, and submitted to UNFCCC."]

["Targeted outcomes include: (1) Inclusion & Opportunities: 3 indigenous projects prepared by 2017; culturally differentiated health/education programs for Red de Oportunidades by 2017; 2 semiannual Beca Universal M&E reports by 2017; reduced youth drop-out rates; increased formal youth employment; significant reduction in poverty among elderly and general population. (2) Service Delivery: ~US$100M annual public expenditure reduction; reduced average electricity outages; 100% potable water/zero latrines; improved health outcomes & job access via WSS. (3) Fiscal Management: ISTMO operational; increased transparency & improved cash management; reduced financing costs; increased fiscal space; strengthened budget/treasury management; enhanced disaster risk management through a comprehensive policy.", "Overall success requires: strong Government commitment (legislative/institutional); stable macroeconomic environment (robust growth, fiscal deficit <1% GDP by 2018); clearly defined objectives with measurable indicators met; effective culturally appropriate development for Indigenous Peoples; robust fiscal management including disaster risk; sustained engagement via participatory approaches (e.g., Indigenous Peoples' National Development Plan); and structured implementation (phased system roll-outs, DPL triggers). This should lead to poverty reduction, improved living conditions, reduced public expenditures, and an enhanced business environment."]

Targeted outcomes include preparing and submitting Serbia's BTR1 (by Dec 2024) and combined NC4/BTR2 (by Dec 2026) to UNFCCC, meeting reporting obligations. This involves strengthening technical/institutional capacity for climate change mainstreaming, updating GHG inventories (BTR1 to 2022, NC4/BTR2 to 2024) with higher tiers, providing NDC implementation progress up to 2022, operationalizing the MRV platform, developing bylaws, and integrating gender perspectives. Success is achieved by submitting both reports on time and compliantly, 10 government agencies regularly using the MRV platform, 300 direct beneficiaries (">=50% women"), and 4 knowledge products produced, indicating enhanced capacity and climate policy monitoring.

The targeted outcomes are to strengthen the resilience of targeted communities and areas in coastal Western Africa by enhancing absorptive, adaptive, and transformative capacities and reducing shared natural and man-made risks, specifically decreasing households' exposure to erosion, flooding, and pollution. Overall success hinges on achieving measurable reductions in household exposure to erosion, flooding, and pollution; a high share of 'Satisfied' beneficiaries; and an improved regional integration score. Additionally, success requires the adoption of Project Implementation and Monitoring and Evaluation Manuals and the establishment of Project Implementation Units by recipient countries and WAEMU.

The activity targets generating global environmental benefits across Biodiversity, Land Degradation, and Sustainable Forest Management. Key outcomes include the development and implementation of a Territorial Management Plan (PGTC) based on indigenous criteria, ensuring sustainable management of forest resources in indigenous territories. It aims to develop capacities and support to generate ecological, economic, and social benefits from the multiple elements within Indigenous Peasant Original Community Lands (TIOCs) life systems, maintaining biodiversity and environmental functions. The project seeks to manage TIOCs with a holistic "system of life" perspective to effectively confront external threats like fires and livestock expansion, and achieve complementarity between sustainable use and biodiversity conservation. Specific objectives involve orienting resource utilization to ecologically sustainable levels, prioritizing forest species diversity, strengthening indigenous peoples' capacities for balanced management, stabilizing land-use changes around TIOCs, promoting sustainable non-forest productive activities to prevent invasion, and revaluing indigenous knowledge.To be considered an overall success, the activity must achieve these outcomes by ensuring that exploitation activities do not exceed ecological limits, forest species diversity is maintained, indigenous communities' collective land rights are consolidated, and their motivations for forest conservation are sustained. It also requires the effective implementation of integral and sustainable forest management, strengthened local governance, and improved coordination among government entities, preventing forest degradation, overexploitation, and socio-economic decline, thereby establishing a robust foundation for forest conservation and ecological health.

["Targeted outcomes included improving the institutional and energy policy environment for promoting renewable energy and energy efficiency in the Caribbean, preparing regional and national stakeholders for a growing energy market, increasing RE/EE experts, enhancing energy supply security and environmental sustainability, improving economic conditions, reducing poverty and pollution, and decreasing energy service costs. The project also aimed to create preconditions for implementing the Caribbean Sustainable Energy Roadmap and Strategy (C-SERMS) and improve the political, regulatory, and institutional framework for RE/EE investment.", "Success, as defined at the activity's start, required the implementation of 40% of 20 central C-SERMS activities; regional educational institutions to include four additional RE/EE modules (totaling 6); three regional/national companies to offer new RE/EE technologies/services; three model projects with regional relevance in RE/EE to be implemented; and the Caribbean Development Bank and other financing institutions to approve five additional RE/EE projects totaling USD 20 million."]

Targeted outcomes: Successfully applying "Conceptual foundations for the spread of renewable energies and energy efficiency in Bangladesh." This included developing practical RE/EE dissemination approaches via pilots for households, SMEs, businesses, and industry; strengthening SREDA's capacity for RE/EE measure design, policy, and regulation; improving energy access and health for women; encouraging women's business roles; and reducing wood use, deforestation, conventional energy use, and pollution. Success required achieving this objective and 80 service providers offering RE/EE technologies, with 15% female staff.

["Targeted outcomes include: (i) reducing business costs, improving market accessibility, and encouraging new investment (e.g., 2.5% FDI increase, 20% business compliance cost reduction by 2021); (ii) creating more flexible and integrated labor markets and providing more efficient social assistance (e.g., expanding women's job options, reducing informal sector); and (iii) improving fiscal sustainability, energy sector sustainability, efficiency, and risk (e.g., raising domestic revenues, revised PPP Law, improving energy transparency).", "Overall success requires: strong alignment with Jordan's Vision 2025 and economic plans; sustained reform commitment; and concrete institutional and policy measures. These include operationalizing an investor grievance mechanism (JIC), streamlining online business registration, abolishing unnecessary licenses, operationalizing public procurement oversight and independent complaints units with e-GP rollout, strengthening the PPP process and revising its law, leveraging the digital economy, and capitalizing on Jordan's competitive advantages. Continued and timely implementation of structural reforms is essential."]

The targeted outcomes of the Geothermie in Zentralamerika activity included: increasing demand for geothermal energy and improving the investment climate in Central America. Success, as defined at the beginning, required: 1) Approval of 3 new/adapted strategies/laws/norms in at least 2 countries. 2) 4 funding applications for new geothermal plants in at least 2 countries. 3) 100 specialists/executives confirming improved activity quality due to new knowledge. 4) 6 activities promoting geothermal use in at least 3 countries via dialogue forums.

The long-term impact objective of the activity was to contribute to effective conservation and sustainable use of natural resources, fostering rural development in selected districts and safeguarding globally important ecosystems. The immediate objective was for central government, autonomous protected area authorities, and local governments to implement mechanisms that improve the conservation of globally significant wildlife resources and create incentives for local populations for sustainable resource management.Targeted outcomes necessary for success, as defined at the beginning of the activity, included:1.  **For the Wildlife Division of the Ministry (WD/MNRT) to effectively perform its role in sector coordination and policy guidance:** This entailed developing a new vision, mission, and corporate identity for the WD; advising on monitoring, codes of conduct, and evaluation of sector governance; and building capacity for WD managers to develop an identity and cooperative relations with stakeholders. Specifically, two additional instruments to coordinate wildlife resource management were to be developed by the WD, and the WD was to hold annual consultant and coordination meetings with key stakeholders on governance and regulations in the wildlife sector.2.  **For the newly established Tanzania Wildlife Management Authority (TAWA) to implement processes for managing wildlife:** This involved institutional and human resources capacity development, including planning, finance management, leadership, establishing a Planning Unit, operationalizing a GIS and remote sensing laboratory, and reviewing public-private partnerships. TAWA was targeted to increase its share of photo tourism revenue by 20% in three years (from USD 6,451,967 to USD 7,742,360) and to develop two instruments for benefit sharing and community participation. Success also required TAWA to implement its core duties in an increasingly efficient manner across five protected areas (Kijereshi, Swaga Swaga, Mkungunero, Selous, Ikorongo), with management plans implemented in these areas, and TAWA disposing of adequate resources and management capacities within a favorable legal framework.3.  **For units of district administrations (Ngorongoro and Serengeti) to improve service provision for natural resources management:** This included ensuring at least 10% of the annual natural resources and tourism-related revenues from the two supported districts were reinvested in the same areas. Each district was expected to implement two additional services in natural resources and tourism, with one tailored to women's needs. The time taken by district response teams to respond to human-wildlife conflict was to be reduced by an average of 50% (from 24 hours to 12 hours). Ten approaches (5 to improve existing income sources and 5 new business ideas, 3 targeting women) were to be developed, ready for execution and financing. The supported districts were also expected to carry out five defined core tasks (reducing human-wildlife conflicts, supporting tourism, strengthening sustainable livestock practices, promoting sustainable forest management and beekeeping, improving agricultural productivity, and enhancing environmental education) 20% more efficiently.Overall success depended on consistent actions by central and local governments and autonomous protected area authorities, coupled with sector coordination, to increase the effectiveness of government support for conservation and sustainable use of wildlife resources. Benefit sharing mechanisms institutionalizing the transfer of funds to communities were considered crucial for promoting sustainable livelihoods and ecosystems. Furthermore, growing political support to contain poaching and disrupt illegal trade, and the economic potential of natural resource utilization not being negatively affected by security incidents, were necessary assumptions for the project's contribution to the programme objective. The project also aimed to contribute to Sustainable Development Goals, particularly SDG 15 (sustainable management of natural resources, protection of wildlife, and containing poaching) and SDG 1 (livelihood improvement).

The activity targeted improving access to safe sanitation and hygiene for disadvantaged groups (women, children, persons with disabilities, IDPs, migrants, ethnic minorities), enhancing sanitation professionals' capacities and employability, and institutionalizing steering instruments for donor engagement. Success, as defined at the outset, required achieving these outcomes, contingent on strong partner ownership, household financial strength, continued donor funding, municipal cooperation, relevant law enforcement, respected public-private contracts, leveraging SDG 6.2 for funding, and identifying sufficient sanitation infrastructure projects.

Targeted outcomes and success criteria as defined at the activity's start (by 2024-12-31) include: 10 funded innovators and 5 female innovators increasing revenue by at least 20% through WEF innovations; 180,000 smallholder farms (30% women-run) introducing WEF innovations; 100 processing companies introducing WEF innovations; 5 organizations referring to the WEF nexus approach in their projects/strategies; and the International Initiative cooperating with 5 strategic partners who continuously promote the WEF nexus.

The targeted outcomes of the Emergency Power Rehabilitation Project are to establish a reliable electricity supply in Kabul and one or more of the major cities (Kandahar, Mazar, Herat, and Jalalabad), corporatize the power utility, and reduce electricity subsidies. This involves rehabilitating and expanding power facilities, initiating improved commercial operations, implementing a cost-recovering tariff scheme as part of power sector reform, and improving capacity within the Ministry of Water and Power and the power utility. Overall success would require achieving these objectives, which includes increased electricity access and availability, a commercially viable and efficient power utility (DABM) with improved billing and collection, reduced technical and non-technical losses, a functional cost-recovering tariff system, and enhanced institutional capacity, all contributing to a reliable power sector in Afghanistan.

The targeted outcomes of the Zambia: Livestock Development and Animal Health Project are to improve the productivity of key livestock production systems for targeted smallholder producers in identified areas and to enhance the safety of meat and milk products in slaughterhouses, markets, and milk collection centers supported by the project. For the activity to be considered an overall success, it would need to achieve the following specific objectives: (i) an increase in the yield of targeted production systems among participating smallholder producers, (ii) a decrease in the incidence of key animal diseases in the project areas, and (iii) the building of capacity within the Animal Health system and Veterinary Services.

The activity targets: costed options for post-2015 development compact; enhanced evidence for global climate action; improved methods for development actors in LMIC/LIC governance transitions; practical approaches for social transformation of hard-to-reach girls/young women; practical approaches for LICs/LMICs competitiveness in a carbon-constrained world; timely knowledge for systemic shocks/crises policy responses; and identifying post-2015 risk/resilience challenges/gaps. Success requires ODI to deliver high-quality, evidence-based policy support/advice, aligned with DFID priorities, informing DFID's plans, influencing the wider development community, and achieving all these specified results.

Targeted outcomes for the Solar Nigeria Programme include strengthening solar PV markets, improving poor Nigerians' lives, and reducing carbon emissions. Success metrics: 1M households with electricity, over 11 MWp installed PV capacity, mobilizing significant finance (£19M+ consumer, £40M+ private, £25M+ trade), and avoiding 300,000+ tonnes CO2e. The Borno State extension targets improved health outcomes in three general hospitals via 24-hour solar power for critical loads, freeing up diesel funds for staffing, and increasing energy resilience. Overall success requires achieving these outcomes and mitigating risks.

["Targeted outcomes include the avoidance of U-POPs, PBDEs, and CO2 releases through integrated, sound management of e-waste, hazardous, healthcare, and municipal solid waste, fostering a waste circular economy based on 3R principles. This encompasses developing an ESM system for e-waste (improved regulations, capacity for POPs-contaminated waste), achieving sound healthcare waste management (enhanced practices, training, technology), and enhancing waste diversion/resource recovery to reduce U-POPs/GHG emissions via recycling, RDF, and improved hazardous waste management. Social protection benefits via gender mainstreaming and community involvement are also key.", "Overall success means achieving the avoidance of U-POPs, PBDEs, and CO2 releases, and significantly contributing to a waste circular economy based on 3R principles. Key indicators of success include operationalizing an effective E-waste ESM system (with improved regulations and POPs-contaminated waste capacity), establishing environmentally sound HCW management (enhanced practices, training, technology), and demonstrating reduced U-POPs/GHG emissions through waste diversion/resource recovery. Generating substantial social protection benefits via gender mainstreaming and active local community involvement, and ensuring the approach is highly sustainable and replicable, also define success."]

["The activity 'Aufforstung IV (Inv.)' primarily targeted protecting natural resources from erosion and degradation and improving living conditions. Its specific objective was the rehabilitation, reforestation, and sustainable management of 19,000 hectares of permanent mixed forest to protect endangered natural resources (soil and water) from degradation and erosion. The direct target group comprised 14,218 households of smallholders. At inception, 15% forest coverage was noted, aiming to address severe environmental degradation.", "To achieve success as defined at the beginning, the activity needed to: successfully reforest/regenerate 19,000 hectares of forest; ensure a survival rate of at least 80% for new plants 3 years post-planting; effectively protect soil and water resources from degradation and erosion; improve living conditions for the 14,218 beneficiary households by mitigating environmental degradation impacts; and address root causes such as unsustainable land/forest management, lack of environmental awareness, insufficient knowledge, and absence of local population involvement in resource management."]

The activity targeted collecting, purifying, and reusing wastewater in agriculture to ensure safe and hygienic disposal, reduce health risks, and protect scarce water resources. Success required achieving a >70% connection rate to the Wadi Shallalah treatment plant, treating approximately 7,400 m³/day, and meeting specific discharge parameters: BOD5 <30 mg/l, TSS <30 mg/l, NO3-N <25 mg/l, T-N < 50 mg/l, Koliforme < 1000/100 ml, Nematoden < 1/1000 ml. Also, a disinfection plant was envisioned. Treated water was planned for agricultural reuse after mixing with other plant effluents in the Jordan Valley. Sustainability targets included YWC's operating cost recovery rate reaching >=125% from 2001 and engagement of a private operator for the plant, along with YWC organizational improvements.

The targeted outcomes were to reduce utilization pressure and improve park management, with the ultimate goal of preserving the biodiversity of the expanded Phong Nha - Ke Bang National Park. Success was defined by strengthening law enforcement, increasing park financial resources through tourism, and fostering alternative income opportunities for local populations (e.g., forest rehabilitation of 4,250+ ha, orchid/honey/mushroom/fruit production, micro-loans for ~56,000 inhabitants). Achieving success necessitated effectively reducing pressure, engaging target groups in anti-poaching, making long-term timber investments attractive, addressing park management structural factors, and ensuring provincial government capacity and clear institutional setup.

Targeted outcomes aimed to: provide min. 120 l/c/d year-round drinking water (from 8h/day); reduce specific consumption to max. 150 l/c/d (from >400); decrease unaccounted for water to max. 30% (from 70%); achieve min. 90% wastewater collection (from 0%); treat wastewater to standards (COD 125, BOD5 25, TSS 35 mg/l); strengthen Batumi Tskali, implement GIS, and a sludge concept. Success required meeting these, ensuring sustainable municipal development, improved hygiene, and enhanced living conditions for >150,000 residents.

The activity aimed to provide a secure basic energy supply of up to 200 Wh/d via Solar Home Systems (SHS) for households, hospitals, and schools in remote rural areas, with an overarching goal to improve living conditions, health, education, income, and quality of life for the poorest populations. Success was defined by: at least 90% of installed systems operational after one year; average annual power failures due to technical issues below 10 days; PV system theft rate under 2%; payment rate over 90% from all customers; less than 10% of systems removed within the first year; and average energy consumption of at least 4 kWh per household per month.

The activity's targeted outcomes included formulating climate protection guidelines (e.g., GHG limits), devising methods to safeguard IDB projects from climate change, integrating sustainable economics, and establishing Sustainable Infrastructure Strategy foundations. Success, as defined at the beginning, meant IDB integrated its climate protection strategy instruments into its performance processes. This required, as targets, applying 5 climate protection guidelines in 2+ sectors, 2 climate change safeguarding methods in 2+ sectors, implementing 2 international climate instrument concepts, considering 6 bilateral cooperation experiences, engaging 4 IDB units in Sustainable Inclusive Growth, and including 3 contributions in a knowledge platform.

The targeted outcomes of the activity, as known at the beginning, were primarily to strengthen the capacities of regional organizations and Pacific ACP states, including East Timor, for climate change adaptation and mitigation, and to reduce the causes of climate change. The program aimed to achieve this by enhancing resilience through awareness-raising, mainstreaming climate change considerations, education, strengthening regional organizations, implementing pilot measures at community level, and promoting measures such as resilient agriculture, improved water supply, fishing practices, and, where necessary, the relocation of communities to higher ground. The activity was structured around six focus areas, each with targeted outcomes: strengthening regional advisory and management competencies; mainstreaming climate-related considerations and adaptation strategies; implementing adaptation and mitigation measures; promoting sustainable tourism and climate protection; sustainable energy management; and education on climate change.To achieve success as defined at the beginning of the activity, the following targeted outcomes were deemed necessary:1.  **Advisory Services Capacity**: Regional organizations (Secretariat of the Pacific Community (SPC), Secretariat of the Pacific Regional Environment Programme (SPREP), and the Melanesian Spearhead Group (MSG)) would double the number of experts dedicated to climate change advisory and climate protection promotion compared to 2010, and maintain this staffing level. These services, including technical expertise, policy advice, training, information, and networking, would be increasingly provided and demanded by partner countries.2.  **National Policies and Strategies Implementation**: Twelve national adaptation strategies or sectoral policies (in agriculture, forestry, land-use planning, fisheries, tourism, and education) that explicitly consider climate change would be in implementation in nine selected Pacific ACP island states, including East Timor.3.  **Community-level Resilience**: In 10 rural communities across 5 member states and in 50% of the 3 particularly vulnerable sub-districts of East Timor where adaptation measures are implemented, 50% of residents (with at least 40% being women) would report that their livelihoods are better secured against climate change.4.  **Energy Projects**: Five energy projects, based on concrete requests from partner countries, that integrate climate change considerations and improve sustainability, reliability, and cost-effectiveness in the energy sector, would reach the investment phase.5.  **REDD+ Financing**: One contract for financing climate-relevant forest protection measures (REDD+) would be signed.

The activity targeted a 20% income increase for 75% of families involved in sustainable natural resource management models. At least 120 women were targeted to generate an additional annual income of USD 200. Nine regional authorities were aimed to gain experience and develop plans for disaster preparedness. It was expected that six out of eleven competent national authorities would confirm improved cooperation and efficiency in transnational water and forest resource protection. Additionally, at least six lessons learned in sustainable natural resource management were to be processed, used, and made available for regional strategies. To achieve success, as defined by the project's evaluation criteria, the activity would need to secure an overall rating of 12-13 points across relevance, effectiveness, impact, efficiency, and sustainability.

The targeted outcomes of the activity, known at the beginning, included an increased demand from member countries for MRC's consultancy and training services on sustainable hydropower sustainability principles. Success required: MRC technical capacity and communication skills strengthened; MRC developing communication products, strategies, assessment instruments, and guidelines/benefit-sharing mechanisms; member countries utilizing these services; establishment of NSHD-M; adoption of at least three new MRC guidelines on sustainable hydropower/benefit-sharing; MRC providing comprehensive information; and national specialists applying MRC guidelines in assessments.

The targeted outcome was the broad-based international implementation and utilization of sanitation systems, adhering to SuSanA's five sustainability criteria for positive health impacts. Success required: 1) Publications documenting initial results from advising five countries on country-specific, broad-based sanitation approaches in GIZ/KfW projects. 2) SuSanA securing at least seven strategic partners OR establishing a membership-fee financing model for long-term independence. 3) Anchoring broad-based, poverty-oriented, sustainable sanitation approaches in two relevant political processes with documented usable results for German development cooperation.

The activity's overarching objective is for relevant actors in agriculture at national, state, and district levels to increasingly use information from the climate change knowledge network. This aims to improve natural resources, reduce climate risks, enhance agricultural productivity/incomes, and make innovative adaptation technologies available to reduce small-scale farmers' vulnerability. Success requires: 1. Network members in content development higher in 70% of months in year 2 vs. year 1. 2. NICE logins higher in 70% of months in year 2 vs. year 1. 3. 70% of district stakeholders, FEAs, farmers confirm info quality. 4. 12 community-level adaptation plans based on network info by project end. 5. 25% of communication channels utilized in 6 districts.

The activity targeted the urban/rural population utilizing sustained, affordable drinking water and basic sanitation meeting health standards. The direct project outcome was for water/sanitation/hygiene stakeholders to establish conditions ensuring equal access. Key outcomes included: developed sector strategies; improved Ministry of Health/DNSP services; enhanced SONEB-commune cooperation and secured water quality; strengthened municipal capacities and SONEB training quality. Success was defined by achieving the direct project outcome, measured by SMART indicators tracking improved sector conditions and sustainable service provision.

The activity targeted increased sustainable access to safe drinking water and basic sanitation for the urban poor, and improved water resources management. Specific outcomes aimed for 1,600,000 poor people gaining drinking water access and 400,000 gaining basic sanitation via WSTF upscaling. It also targeted improved water supply/sanitation for 11 million urban residents, fairer resource management with better raw water quality, and reduced burdens for women/girls. Success was defined by achieving the module objective, measurable through specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound (SMART) indicators.

At the start, the activity aimed to improve preconditions for pro-poor, sustainable urban water and sanitation. Key success targets (SMART criteria) were: 85% of urban population with piped water have pro-poor tariffs; private businesses handle faecal sludge for 40,000 additional people (60% poor) for one year; 80% of female NWSC trainees apply new skills; wastewater discharge compliance to Inner Murchison Bay improves by 25% (quantity/quality); 10% of climate change adaptation measures in NWSC water source protection plans implemented. Broader goals included: 600,000 additional people (400,000 urban poor) gain access to hygienically acceptable drinking water; 250,000 urban poor gain access to basic sanitation (100,000 to sewerage); 75% of water users in selected basins confirm reduced water resource conflict potential.

The activity aimed to improve intersectoral management of biodiversity and ecosystem services, based on robust data. Targeted outcomes included testing local management instruments and coordination, improving capacity of ministries and training institutions, fostering more positive public perception, and enhancing regional exchange on sustainable management. Success required successful intersectoral coordination leading to win-win decisions, local experiences informing national decisions, decisions based on robust data, and improved public knowledge/attitudes/practices regarding biodiversity.
NO RESPONSE

The activity targets improved sustainable and cost-effective indigenous renewable energy utilization, developing RE/DSM/EE policies/frameworks, adopting RE technologies for electricity generation, and financing RE/DSM/EE investments. Key outcomes include enhancing resilience and capacity in environmental/climate change management, achieving inclusive/sustainable energy solutions, enforcing clear RE policies, recommending smart grid control systems for reliability, developing RE grid connection codes, and a 25,267 tonnes GHG emission reduction. It also aims for 100% renewable electricity by 2025, increased biomass energy application, a biomass-gasification project, enhanced community capacity, SCADA system utilization for reliability, and effective power plant dispatching.

Targeted outcomes include a 99.5% HCFC phase-out by 2020, sustainably reducing the servicing tail by 2030, and meeting Montreal Protocol commitments. This involves upgrading national legislation, strengthening customs capacity (training ~100 personnel), improving the servicing sector (training ~520 technicians on zero-ODS/low-GWP technologies), establishing new safety standards for natural refrigerants, strengthening the HCFC re-use system, demonstrating zero-ODS/low-GWP cooling technologies, and enhancing public awareness with gender mainstreaming, including women's involvement in RAC.

The targeted outcomes of the Song Bung 4 Hydropower Project are to contribute to Viet Nam's increasing power demand in an environmentally sustainable and socially inclusive manner. Specifically, the project aims for increased power generation capacity in Quang Nam Province, central Viet Nam, through the sustainable use of hydropower, mitigation of adverse environmental impacts, and restoration and improvement of the livelihoods of project-affected persons. To be considered an overall success, the activity would need to achieve no power shortages in Viet Nam by 2015, a 10% reduction in greenhouse gas emissions per unit of economic output and electricity output over 2008-2015, and the addition of 1,000 MW of hydropower generation capacity in Quang Nam Province without significant outstanding environmental and social issues. Furthermore, the project's success hinges on the completion of the 156 MW Song Bung 4 Hydropower project and associated facilities in accordance with the technical design by 2013, the relocation of affected persons by 2012, and the improvement of their livelihoods by December 2013 as outlined in the Resettlement and Ethnic Minority Development Plan (REMDP). Implementation of capacity building programs for affected persons as outlined in the REMDP by 2013 and no legitimate claims by project-affected persons in the Vu Gia river basin during 2008-2013 are also crucial for success. Key to overall activity success is the effective implementation of the resettlement and ethnic minority development plan, particularly the livelihood improvement activities funded by the Japan Fund for Poverty Reduction grant, which goes beyond current government practice.

Targeted outcomes: Improve productive capacity, access to finance, community resilience, and food system responsiveness for 1000 women smallholder farmers in Mullaitivu and Kilinochchi. This includes piloting an innovative agro-pastoral model, sustaining 700 people through climate-smart agriculture, and certifying 700 farmers for organic practices. Three inclusive financial products will be developed and piloted. Overall success requires creating sustainable pathways by building investor confidence and mobilizing private capital for food systems development beyond the project's duration.

Targeted outcomes: Universal, equitable access to safe drinking water and sanitation, ending open defecation; enhanced reliability and efficiency of resilient WSS in Dehradun, Haldwani, Nainital, and Tanakpur. Key outputs include expanded water supply networks (24-hr, reduced NRW, metered connections), advanced sanitation (STP, sewers, drainage, FSSM), computerized maintenance systems, and strengthened institutional capacity (governance, community awareness, gender focus). Significant GHG reductions are targeted. Success requires maintaining "on track" project performance (physical progress, WSS connections, contract awards, safeguards, risk management) and the additional financing's technical, economic, financial viability, consistent with objectives.
NO RESPONSE

The activity aims for improved quality of life and a sustainable aquatic ecosystem in Chao Lake, restoring water quality from Class V to Class III by 2025, reducing harmful algal blooms, improving hygiene for 0.8 million people, and creating 7,000 jobs. By 2018, it targets reduced discharges of chemical oxygen demand (24,000 t/yr), ammonia nitrogen (1,740 t/yr), and phosphorus (280 t/yr), connecting 1.4 million more people to wastewater collection. Success requires effective implementation of the Chao Lake environmental protection management plan, county development and pollution control plans, adequate government budget, effective environmental regulation enforcement, and proper wastewater collection/WWTP O&M. Community participation, skills training (especially for women/poor), and robust project risk management (including gender mainstreaming) are also crucial.

The National Grid Improvement Project (formerly Powergrid) targets several key outcomes. The project's ultimate impact is the accelerated development of the interregional grid system to deliver increased power supply to sustain India's economic growth. Specifically, the project aims to achieve increased reliable power supply from private Independent Power Producers (IPPs) and public utilities within the interconnected grid network. This will be facilitated by strengthening interregional transmission capacity and achieving more efficient operations. The project also intends to integrate electricity markets, create power trading opportunities through open access and competition, and expand the market for potential power sellers and buyers beyond their own regional grids, thereby promoting private investment in generation and distribution. A critical technical feature is the implementation of an 800 kV High Voltage Direct Current (HVDC) transmission system, which is expected to enhance energy efficiency by reducing transmission losses by approximately 3% compared to conventional levels, stabilize power supply frequencies and voltages, and support smart grid operations. Furthermore, the innovative financing structure, combining sovereign and nonsovereign loans, aims to strengthen POWERGRID's self-financing capacity, diversify its funding sources, establish a benchmark for foreign commercial borrowing, and enhance its corporate credit capabilities. To consider the activity an overall success, the following would need to be achieved: completion of the high-capacity power transmission corridor program to supply an additional 40,000 MW (from 2006) of power from 55 IPPs by 2018; private sector participation in the bidding of an additional 14 interstate transmission projects by 2018; an increase in interregional transmission capacity to 75,000 MW by FY2017 (from a baseline of 23,800 MW in 2011) to enhance power trading; the delivery of additional reliable power supply and trading of 3,000 MW through interregional transmission from 14 IPPs in Chhattisgarh to the northern region by 2017; maintaining transmission system availability at no less than 98% at the 2010 level; reducing interregional transmission loss by 3% of power supplied by 2017; and POWERGRID creating a benchmark for its funding source diversification from international capital and/or commercial bank markets, along with the functionality of an enterprise risk management system for POWERGRID. Additionally, the project's alignment with ADB's Strategy 2020 and India country partnership strategy for leveraging financing capacity of central public sector undertakings (CPSUs), and the successful adoption of the state-of-the-art 800 kV HVDC transmission system are crucial for overall success.

The activity targets: improved electricity distribution quality and services, reduced electricity losses and operational expenses, enhanced maintenance/safety, modernized metering, network rehabilitation/augmentation, new customer connections, and international management/automated control systems. It also seeks to boost ENA profitability, maintain stable tariffs, and reduce CO₂ emissions by 11,400 tons/annum, supporting SDG7/SDG8. Success is defined by: distribution losses lowered to 8.0% by 2021 (from 9.7% in 2016), 400 km of lines, 900 transformers, 550 substations, and 250,000 automatic meters upgraded/installed by 2020, alongside ENA's profitability and tariff stability.

The targeted outcomes of the Hubei Enshi Qing River Upstream Environment Rehabilitation Project are the improved river health and water resource management in the upper Qing River basin. To consider the activity an overall success, the following specific targets must be achieved by 2022: households affected by 1-in-20-year frequency floods in the project area should be reduced by 61% from the 2014 baseline of 82,670; households connected to newly installed sanitary sewers should increase by 42% from the 2014 baseline of 96,000; pollution loads in Enshi and Lichuan need to be significantly reduced, specifically COD to 3,725 tons (from 5,460 tons), Total nitrogen to 685 tons (from 844 tons), and Total phosphorus to 37 tons (from 64 tons); and wastewater treatment coverage (m



³ per day) must increase to 90% from the 2014 baseline of 57%.

["The targeted outcomes of the Integrated Wastewater Management Project are to mitigate water pollution in rivers and lakes in the People's Republic of China, and to expand the provision of wastewater management services under integrated concessions in selected central and western provinces. Specifically, this entails increasing treated wastewater to 755 million m 3 per year by 2028, providing at least 400 jobs for local workers during operation, spending at least CNY6,189 million in domestic purchases, and paying at least CNY2,356 million to governments from 2017 to 2028.","For overall success, the project must achieve these impact and outcome targets, including the construction by 2024 of integrated river or lake rehabilitation subprojects valued at CNY9.0 billion, a total installed wastewater treatment capacity of 2.3 million m 3 per day, sludge treatment subprojects with 2,070 m 3 (2,900 tons) per day capacity, and 190 km of sewage pipeline networks. Additionally, CNY7,388 million in domestic purchases and 1,200 construction jobs must be provided, with all facilities complying with ADB safeguard and eligibility criteria."]

["The XSDP's targeted outcomes include achieving Xiangtan's carbon emission peaking by 2028 and increasing the use of low-carbon enabling systems. It aims for annual GHG reductions of 2,400,000 tons. The program addresses critical operational priorities such as accelerating gender equality, tackling climate change, building climate and disaster resilience, enhancing environmental sustainability, making cities more livable, and strengthening governance and institutional capacity.", "Overall success requires achieving Xiangtan's carbon emission peaking by 2028 and annual GHG reductions of 2,400,000 tons. By 2026, specific success indicators include: at least 337 ktCO2e reduced per year; 10% increase in public and nonmotorized transport users; over 20% savings in energy, embedded energy, and water in at least two additional public buildings; and at least 100,000 residents benefiting from low-carbon, resilient community improvements."]

Targeted outcomes are improved electricity supply reliability, efficiency, and financial sustainability in Nepal. Key targets include achieving 700 kWh per capita electricity consumption by 2026, reducing system losses to 13% by 2027 (from 15.3%), and 100% recovery of NEA's operating costs by 2027 (from 90%). Success requires meeting these targets, alongside modernizing the distribution system, enhancing supply security, fostering NEA's institutional development, and reducing greenhouse gas emissions by 6,736,000 tons per annum through the planned infrastructure and system improvements.

The targeted outcomes of the Kenya - Natural Resource Management Project are primarily to improve the management of water and forest resources in selected districts. This revised Project Development Objective (PDO) streamlines the original goal by focusing on management improvements rather than directly financing activities to reduce water shocks.Specific outcomes include:1.  **Enhanced institutional capacity for water resource management:** This involves strengthening the Water Resource Management Authority (WRMA), direct investments in the Upper Tana Catchment for erosion control, terracing, and small to medium water storage, and improving on-farm agronomic practices. It also includes the consolidation of irrigation reforms, development of new irrigation infrastructure, and rehabilitation of existing schemes.2.  **Improved management of forest resources:** This entails a shift towards greater joint management with local communities and the private sector, improving forest governance and participation, and fostering investment.
