Favorable political, administrative, economic, and environmental conditions are also essential, alongside increased stakeholder awareness and collaborative action."]

Targeted outcomes: environmentally sustainable, socially inclusive urbanization in Huanggang, improved urban environment (enhanced water quality of Baitan and Chiye lakes from Class V to Class IV, increased flood-retention capacity from 5- to 20-year return period, enhanced aesthetic value). It aims to foster economic prosperity, livability, public amenity, investment, and job creation (2,450 construction, 133 operation). Success: achieving water quality/flood improvements, delivering health benefits, benefiting 100,000 residents/industries, meeting job targets, effective gender mainstreaming, pollutant removal, and climate resilience.

The targeted outcome is improved power transmission service coverage, reliability, and quality in Pakistan. Overall success by 2026 requires: supplying 5.0 GW of additional peak power through the grid; reducing transmission losses to under 2.5%; and achieving full compliance with NEPRA Transmission Rules, License, and Grid Code. Key outputs for success include: commissioning 350 km of new climate-resilient 220 kV transmission lines; adding 2,680 MVA substation capacity across six substations (including six augmented for climate-resilience and one new climate-resilient substation in Peshawar); adopting NTDC's Gender Mainstreaming Policy; and ensuring at least 80% of NTDC staff report improved knowledge on harassment policies.

The activity targets reduced air pollution (PM2.5 to 49  	
µg/m 	
³ by 2020, sharp reductions in SO2, NOx) and improved public health in the greater BTH region. It aims for a CO2 reduction of 3,990,000 tons/annum, improved public health, and enhanced access to clean heating for 2,091 poor women-headed households. Success requires achieving these environmental targets, at least 40% women staff with improved knowledge, creating 700 new jobs for women, reducing women's time on heating chores, and serving as a scalable pilot.

The Shanxi Low-Carbon and Inclusive Rural Development Project targets improved low-carbon, climate-resilient, environmentally sustainable, and socially inclusive public services in Shanxi Province. By 2030, key outcomes include: at least 30,000 tons of CO2 emissions avoided annually; an additional 1,200 rural households receiving climate-resilient wastewater treatment; an additional 13,000 rural households receiving low-carbon and uninterrupted water supply; and at least 500 new skilled jobs created in urban-rural facilities (at least 50% for women). By 2029, Output 1 aims for: submission of at least one low-carbon/climate adaptation guideline; augmentation of a digital information management system; establishment of a smart wetland and carbon asset management platform; and publication of a project best practices study. The activity will be an overall success if it achieves high-quality and green development and CO2 emissions peaking in Shanxi Province and the Yellow River Ecological Corridor by 2030, alongside all specified outcomes and outputs.

["The activity primarily targeted improved institutional and technical capacities of stakeholders in the Nile Delta for efficient water use in agricultural production, including climate change adaptation. This focused on water-saving cultivation methods in irrigated agriculture. Expected outcomes included enhanced technical and advisory competence of field advisors, institutional innovation within the state advisory system, leading to increased effectiveness of knowledge transfer and relevance of advice, ultimately resulting in smallholder producers permanently adopting water-saving practices and improved water efficiency at individual farm, Nile Delta system, and field levels. Contributions to SDGs 6.4, 6.5, and 12.2 were also intended.", "To achieve success as defined at the beginning of the activity, the following targeted outcomes would be necessary: 1. Adoption of a description of roles and responsibilities within the Ministry of Agriculture as a prerequisite for establishing Marwa committees. 2. Establishment of one unit to strengthen the Farmer-Field-School approach, including a gender-sensitive mandate, job description, and personnel resources, in two governorates (Beheira and Kafr El-Sheikh). 3. 350 smallholder farmers (50% women) organized in Marwa committees implementing water-saving and climate-adapted agricultural practices in two consecutive planting seasons on 50% of their cultivated area."]

The targeted outcomes of the 'Energieeffizienzberatung' activity, known at its beginning, included: 1. Publishing responsibilities and procedures for 70% of NEEAP measures for residential buildings. 2. Adopting Local Energy Efficiency Action Plans (LEEAPs) in five municipalities. 3. Submitting a first monitoring report from a national data management system. 4. Qualifying 100 professionals (architects, civil engineers, energy managers) through new training programs for NEEAP implementation. Success was defined as implementing the National Energy Efficiency Action Plan for the buildings sector, aiming to save 16% of energy consumption, achieved through these specific outcomes.

The activity targeted preserving the area size of eight primal forests in eastern Côte d'Ivoire, substantially reducing uncontrolled logging and clearing of new agricultural land, establishing sustainable forest management plans, and developing participatory concepts to stabilize local farmers. Success was defined by achieving these objectives, alongside strengthening SODEFOR's institutional capabilities, promoting alternative income, and ultimately preserving the forests' functions for climate, water balance, and agriculture.

The targeted outcomes known at the beginning of the activity included: Overall: Improve population's access to drinking water & sanitation services long-term; enhance efficiency, social/environmental compatibility, sustainability of services. PNS 2015 goals: Drinking water supply to 83% nationwide (89% urban, 70% rural); sanitation to 77% (84% urban, 60% rural); urban wastewater treatment to 100%. 2012 Targets: Nationwide drinking water supply 78%; nationwide wastewater disposal 69%; average operating costs per m³ drinking water 1.34 PEN; SEDEPAL return on sales 20%; other utility return on sales 12%; SEDEPAL wastewater treated 56%; other utility wastewater treated 8%. Impact indicators: Increased access to water/sanitation; supply continuity >18 hrs/day; increased treated wastewater; increased population satisfaction; water suppliers' operating income >10% of costs. Success required achieving these targets, strengthening sector financing & management, and ensuring transparency.

["The activity's targeted outcomes include promoting the adoption of CO2 technology in commercial refrigeration through training, identifying adequate refrigeration systems for conversion, and undertaking a pilot project. A key outcome is the design of a Project Document with a Roadmap for implementing a comprehensive strategy for the gradual reduction of HFCs (KIP Stage I). These efforts collectively aim to enable the Government of Peru to meet its commitments under the Kigali Amendment to the Montreal Protocol regarding HFC consumption reduction.", "To consider the activity an overall success, it would require the successful implementation and completion of the pilot project demonstrating CO2-based technology in commercial refrigeration, alongside effective promotion through training and identification of suitable systems. Crucially, the successful design and implementation of the Project Document with the Roadmap for an HFC reduction strategy (KIP Stage I) must occur. Ultimately, Peru's ability to make significant progress towards or fully meet its Kigali Amendment commitments for HFC consumption reduction would signify overall success."]

The targeted outcomes of the Indonesia Infrastructure Guarantee Fund Project are to strengthen the Indonesia Infrastructure Guarantee Fund (IIGF) as a single window institution for appraising infrastructure Public Private Partnership (PPP) projects that require government guarantees. This involves assisting IIGF in establishing robust, WB-validated appraisal standards and operational procedures, codified in a single Operations Manual (including an Environmental and Social Management Framework), and advising Contracting Agencies (CAs) on project preparation to improve the overall quality of PPP projects. The project also aims to signal to the market the high quality of IIGF's appraisal standards, governance, internal controls, and financial viability through a loan component enabling IIGF to collaborate with the World Bank on appraising complex projects. Component 2 specifically targets enhancing IIGF's institutional capacity to screen, appraise, and supervise projects, manage operations, build capacity within CAs, Sponsors, and other parties, and develop standardized PPP project preparation documents. It also aims to raise project preparation standards upstream and mitigate private sector exposure to risks downstream through early IIGF guidance, ensuring only well-prepared, bankable projects receive guarantees. For the project to be considered an overall success, IIGF must swiftly demonstrate its capacity for technically and financially sound project appraisal and its independence. This includes the consistent implementation of robust appraisal standards and operational procedures, including the single Operations Manual (which encompasses the Environmental and Social Management Framework), and compliance with WB-reviewed appraisal procedures and standards, which will be a condition of loan agreements. The project's success also relies on enhanced institutional capacity within IIGF for project screening, appraisal, and supervision, effective management of its operations, and successful attraction of third-party capital and partners. Crucially, IIGF's environmental and social development specialists and consultants must be fully conversant with the Operations Manual, with World Bank supervision ensuring adequate safeguard capacity is established within IIGF.

["The activity targeted contributing to an efficient, reliable, and long-term secure electricity supply from non-conventional renewable energy sources and to environmental and climate protection through CO2 emission avoidance. Initially, four small hydropower plants were planned with a combined capacity of 53 MW, aiming for 252.5 GWh/a production and preventing 66,000 tonnes of CO2/year.", "After adjustments, the focus shifted to two plants (João Borges, Barra do Rio Chapéu) with a total installed capacity target of 34.15 MW, 164 GWh/a secured annual gross power generation, and greater than 95% system availability, preventing 38,193 tonnes of CO2/year. Success would be achieving these capacity, generation, availability, and emission reduction targets."]

Targeted outcomes and success criteria: Higher-level goal: Preserve natural resources' economic, ecological, and climate-sensitive roles under climate change. Direct objective: Enable sustainable natural resource management by residents and administrative bodies. Success entails: (1) Sustainable natural resource management coordinated in 70% of supported water catchment areas. (2) 70% of agricultural producers implement climate change adaptation measures. (3) Infiltration rates increase by 10% (from 0.3 cm/h). (4) Endemic/adapted tree species increase by 5% (from 33). (5) Max water outflow reduces by 10% (from 68L). (6) Soil erosion reduces by 10% (from 235 t/ha). (7) Curculionidae remain stable (15/m²). (8) Natural forest proportion remains stable (100%). (9) Reduced fires. (10) Positive income effects.

The targeted outcomes of the Amazonienfonds activity included supporting national forestry policy, encompassing forest protection, sustainable forest management, and the expansion of carbon reservoirs. The developmental objective was to achieve positive effects on climate protection and economic development. Project objectives aimed to combat deforestation, protect forests, and support their sustainable use, with intervention areas including sustainable production, environmental monitoring/control, regional planning/protected areas, and science/research. Success was defined by a targeted 80% decrease in gross deforestation in Amazonia by 2020, compared to the 1996-2005 average.

Targeted outcomes were to ensure a continuous and sustainable water supply (24 hours/day) with WHO-compliant water quality, and create conditions for supply expansion. Success required 95% domestic water meter installation, maintenance costs of EUR 1.5 per resident supplied, and non-discriminatory supply to all ethnic groups. Additionally, targets included an operating cost ratio >100%, reducing water losses to <30%, household consumption at 
M120 l/c/d, adequate maintenance budget (EUR 10/connection/year), systematic leak detection, timely payments, and socio-economically acceptable tariffs (<5% of household income).

The targeted outcomes of the activity include: the consolidation and fixation of 75 hectares of dune cordon through the planting of 45,000 endemic and local plant species, leading to enhanced protection against floods, improved erosion control, better water quality, increased carbon sequestration, and the preservation of crucial wetland habitats. Additionally, the project aims to return 19 million liters of water to the wetland complex. It seeks to foster economic autonomy for 70 rural women through the delivery of one production unit for an agricultural micro-project focused on responsible water use in the prickly pear value chain. The activity also targets the establishment of one ecotourism circuit, the development and distribution of 1,000 communication flyers, and the promotion of organized visits within the complex. Education and awareness are key, with 12 annual environmental animation programs for classes and 12 school outing programs to the CESP to be validated and implemented, targeting civil society, students, and local farmers. Control of invasive species and rational use of resources are also expected outcomes, contributing to the broader integrated management plan for the Guerbes-Sanhadja wetland plain.To consider the activity an overall success, the following must be achieved: 75 hectares of dunes fixed with 45,000 plants planted; 19 million liters of water returned to the wetland complex; one production unit delivered and a group of 70 rural women trained, achieving economic autonomy; one ecotourism circuit established and 1,000 communication flyers developed and distributed; 12 annual environmental class animation programs and 12 school outing programs to CESP validated and implemented. Success also hinges on demonstrable improvements in flood protection, erosion control, water quality, carbon sequestration, habitat preservation, effective invasive species control, and an increase in local population's appropriation of environmental issues and sustainable resource management, contributing to the long-term durability of the wetland complex. These outcomes collectively demonstrate the activity's success in environmental rehabilitation, community empowerment, and sustainable resource utilization.

["The activity targets modernizing 12 power transmission lines and 4 substations, improving their reliability (lines by 85%, substations by 100%). It aims to digitize the grid with SCADA and digital relays to accommodate increased renewable energy generation, enabling real-time control and emergency management. Outcomes include strengthened financial sustainability of transmission, significantly improved gender inclusiveness and increased job/leadership opportunities for women (e.g., 30% female board representation), enhanced NEGU corporate governance, improved project management capacity, and integration of climate mitigation/adaptation measures.", "Overall success requires adherence to all ADB requirements (anticorruption, safeguards, gender, etc.), effectiveness of the subsidiary loan agreement, and rehabilitation of all 12 transmission lines and 4 substations by Q4 2025. NEGU's financial sustainability must be addressed by establishing an independent regulator for cost-reflective tariffs and creating JSC Uzpowertrade by 2024. Corporate governance must be enhanced by 2027, including 30% female board leadership, a gender-responsive HR unit (30% female staff, GBV/sexual harassment policies by 2024), and improved PMU capacity with a monitoring dashboard and training."]

The activity's targeted outcomes include improving the systematic design of energy sector transformation within bilateral and multilateral development cooperation. It aims to systematically process and integrate German energy transition and partner country energy policy experiences into BMZ's work. The primary focus is on achieving a needs-based energy supply and systematic transformation of energy sectors in BMZ partner countries.

Targeted outcomes include removing barriers to deploying high technologies for air pollution reduction across industries, urban infrastructure, and agriculture in the Greater Beijing–Tianjin–Hebei (BTH) region. It aims for systemic microeconomic transformations via cleaner production, supporting inclusive economic growth, and environmentally sustainable growth by addressing concerns, conserving resources, and improving the urban environment. Specific outcomes involve institutional development, knowledge sharing, fostering partnerships for private sector investment, and an annual reduction of 5,000,000 tons of carbon dioxide (CO2). The project targets demonstrating the commercial viability of high technologies, financing, and business models to stimulate further demand, and the deployment of technologies such as deep-well geothermal, industrial by-product gas use (LNG), hydrogen-based transport, advanced biogas, smart industrial zone development, and super ESCOs for energy efficiency and emission reduction, leading to abatement of local pollutants and avoided use of fossil fuels.

["Ensure environmentally and health-friendly wastewater disposal in the western part of Nablus city and the upper course of the Wadi Zeimar.", "Contribute to the protection of regional water resources and indirectly to public health.", "Address the critical issue of scarce water resources endangered by untreated wastewater discharge into the Wadi Zeimar.", "Protect the western groundwater aquifer from urban wastewater contamination to aid regional conflict reduction.", "The first phase of the wastewater treatment plant serves 152,000 inhabitants of Nablus and five villages in the upper reaches of the Wadi Zeimar.", "The treatment plant operates with a hydraulic capacity of 14,860 cubic meters per day.", "Sewage sludge is treated, thickened, dried, and stabilized in a digester.", "The plant produces up to half of its required energy by utilizing methane gas.", "Wastewater discharge values from the treatment plant are less than 20 mg BOD5/I.", "Wastewater discharge values from the treatment plant are less than 30 mg SS/I.", "Success requires the establishment of an environmental monitoring center.", "Success requires comprehensive training for operating staff, support for technical and commercial management, and qualification of wastewater treatment plant personnel.", "Success requires the construction consortium to instruct the executing agency on wastewater treatment plant operation.", "Success requires two years of operational assistance from a consultant to ensure proper operation and maintenance.", "Success requires financially motivating businesses along the Wadi Zeimar to pre-treat their wastewater before discharge, with the project covering 40% of costs and businesses 60%.", "Success requires the City of Nablus to insure project-financed assets with a local insurance company, budgeting approximately 100,000 Euro annually to extend their lifespan.", "Success requires co-financing of operational costs over three years to address concerns about financial sustainability and unlikely full cost recovery through wastewater fees."]

The activity aimed to improve the provision of hygienically safe drinking water to poor households in rural and urban areas. Success was defined by the following targeted outcomes: a 15% reduction in households consuming bacteriologically contaminated water (from 42,827 to 36,400); 80% of 800 urban standpipes managed by private companies adhering to contractual rules; an increase of 100,000 people benefiting from potable water from a protected source (from 97,000 to 197,000); and 50,000 people in rural areas benefiting from potable water through recommissioned points.

["The activity targeted an improvement in the management of the Taï National Park, while simultaneously addressing the needs of the surrounding communities.", "A key outcome at the impact level was enhancing biodiversity protection in selected areas of Côte d'Ivoire.", "A primary goal was the sustainable management of the Taï National Park and its protection from destructive human impact.", "Success in stabilizing forest cover in the Taï National Park was defined by maintaining at least 523,152 hectares, the baseline established at the beginning of the activity in 2011.", "The project aimed for the continued development of an Ivorian nature conservation foundation (FPRCI) to ensure the long-term financial sustainability of the park.", "Targeted outcomes for local communities included benefiting from the economic utility of biodiversity preservation and developing potential income sources through participatory park management, micro-projects, and eco-tourism.", "Educational and awareness-raising measures were intended to foster greater acceptance of the park among local populations and involve them in protection and monitoring efforts.", "Micro-projects were planned to enhance food safety and security for farming families through animal protein production, such as pig, poultry, and fish farming, and by supporting a tailoring and sewing training center and school farms.", "Infrastructure improvements targeted the rehabilitation of water pumps for drinking water supply, construction of enclosures for hospitals and health centers, building primary schools, and furnishing a birth center, with an aim to improve sanitary security.", "The activity intended to promote participatory co-management through the establishment of Village Associations for Conservation and Development (AVCDs) in peripheral zones, involving them in park patrols, data collection, research work, boundary marking, and clearing illegal fields.", "Eco-tourism development was a significant aspect, with plans for establishing an eco-hotel and a camp, training local villagers as tourist guides, and recruiting staff for the eco-hotel, thereby creating jobs.", "Public relations and awareness campaigns aimed to increase public awareness and environmental education through information events, theatre performances, school competitions, guided park tours, and promoting the eco-museum in Taï."]

The targeted outcomes of the activity, as known at its beginning, are: The overall objective is to improve the conditions for the development of scientific research-based innovations in the areas of biodiversity and climate change. This will be achieved through three main outputs: 1. Practical application of selected scientific results is demonstrated. 2. Academic networks are strengthened, including contacts with other actors. 3. New demand-oriented scientific cooperations between Ecuador and Germany are initiated.For success as defined at the beginning of the activity, the following targeted outcomes would be necessary: Scientific project results within the framework of the National Biodiversity Research Agenda (ANIB) are used by the public or private sectors. Representatives of Ecuadorian academic institutions confirm improved research positioning for their institutions. At least three new demand-driven scientific cooperation projects between Ecuador and Germany within the ANIB framework are selected for promotion under a joint call. These new scientific cooperations should possess elements of demand-orientation and applicability of their future results. Nine (9) Ecuadorian students and/or researchers begin training or postgraduate processes within joint activities/projects. The scientific cooperation projects promoted by CoCiBio describe the possible gender impacts of their research in their proposals and reports. The participation of any one gender (male or female) in the projects/activities funded does not exceed 70%. Additionally, INABIO (National Institute of Biodiversity) is strengthened as an articulating institution, leveraging virtual platforms for greater cooperation and using the ANIB as a guide for biodiversity research. Research funds are mobilized from both Ecuadorian and German partners, and the demand-orientation of scientific research is strengthened. Measures with a gender focus are implemented in applied biodiversity and climate change research.

The activity targeted improving skills and capacities of national and local actors in climate finance for 2030 Agenda implementation, benefiting vulnerable populations. Key outcomes included ERD officials promoting SDG-relevant climate finance, improved private sector access to climate finance, and local actor involvement in SDG adaptation projects. Success required significant financial resource mobilization, enhanced policy coherence for sustainable development, and contributions to SDGs (e.g., poverty eradication, climate action, gender equality, sustainable cities), with local actors and vulnerable groups actively engaged and stakeholders utilizing resources.

The National Emergency Rural Access Project aims to enable the rural population in Afghanistan to benefit from year-round access to basic services and facilities. This will be achieved by rehabilitating and maintaining rural access infrastructure through private sector and community contracts, thereby enhancing well-being and promoting equitable economic growth. For the activity to be considered an overall success, the following targets must be met: beneficiaries within a 2 km or 30 minutes walking distance to an all-weather road; a 30% reduction in travel time for beneficiaries to district centers; a 50% increase in trips taken by beneficiaries to district centers; and key consumption and production commodity prices in beneficiary villages within 15% of those in the nearest town. These indicators align with Afghanistan's National Development Strategy of providing access to 40% of all villages by 2009.

The activity targeted improving energy efficiency in Brazilian social housing (FSHP) and municipal water supply. Outcomes included a paradigm shift in FSHP towards quality, thermal comfort, and reduced energy use via life cycle assessments; developing and embedding technical proposals for FSHP energy efficiency adopted by decision-makers; training 50 social housing and 50 water supply specialists; developing three water supply energy efficiency regulatory measures; piloting 450 energy-efficient housing units; and contributing to GHG reduction. Success depended on key actors understanding/adopting proposals, active participation, and achieving these specific targets.

The targeted outcomes included: achieving sustainable erosion control and management of water catchment areas, stabilizing/increasing local production potential, and large-scale control/prevention of sedimentation in irrigated perimeters. Specific targets for success included: organizing 320 erosion control groups, immediately intervening in 129 catchment areas, 600 ha of afforestation, and protecting at least 12,000 ha of directly protected irrigation area. Success also required sustainable stabilization of erosion processes and formalization of land rights over up-scaled areas.

The targeted outcomes for the activity, Project B: "Reservat Ankarafantsika/ Umweltaktionsplan IIIa", are the effective protection and sustainable use of the Ankarafantsika Protected Area and its peripheral zones, thereby contributing to the preservation of the natural ecosystem and the socio-economic development of the catchment area. To achieve success as defined at the beginning, the following would be necessary: marked park boundaries, expanded park and administrative infrastructure, developed tourist infrastructure (bungalows, hiking trails), constructed or rehabilitated social infrastructure in the peripheral zone, support for alternative income opportunities for local residents, and consulting services for park administration.

The targeted outcomes of the Wasserverlustreduzierungsprogramm Karak, as known at the beginning of the activity, were to reduce technical and commercial unaccounted-for water (UfW) in the Karak Water Administration (KWA) supply area. This was aimed at ensuring a cost-effective supply of clean drinking water to users in Karak Governorate and contributing to the efficient and sustainable management of scarce water resources. The target group encompassed all inhabitants of Karak Governorate, estimated at around 316,000 in 2015. To achieve success as defined at the beginning of the activity, the following targeted outcomes were necessary: a reduction in total Non-Revenue Water (NRW) from an initial 53% (in 2006) to less than 25%; achieving a 95% regular and comprehensive water supply to the population (from an irregular but nearly comprehensive state); an operating cost recovery ratio of over 50% (from an unknown status); and the processing of 80% of customer queries within four days (from a very low status). Additionally, for enhanced sustainability, the WAJ administration in Karak was intended to be strengthened and restructured into a semi-autonomous entity (Karak Water Administration/KWA), along with measures to establish an electronic customer register, update customer data in the Geographic Information System (GIS), analyze human resources, and identify and implement training programs.

The targeted outcomes of the activity, Ressourcenschutz Fogo, included the ultimate objective of conserving partly endemic biodiversity through promoting resource-saving and income-generating practices for the population. The project aimed to reclaim natural resources via improved agro-sylvo-pastoral land-use systems and a functioning natural park, aligning activities with conservation zones and improving population living conditions. A key quantitative target for success was a 20% increase in agricultural and livestock production by participating families compared to 2005. Success also required increasing population awareness, establishing effective land rights and land-use planning, and implementing monitoring and penalty mechanisms for compliance.

The targeted outcomes aimed to reduce poverty and health risks for approx. 100,000 impoverished inhabitants in Eastern Province and urban peripheral areas, by ensuring sustainable access to and active utilization of drinking water. Success criteria, to be met by the third year of operation, included: 95% water quality compliance; >=10 liters/person/day consumption; 80% target group usage; >=50% female membership in Area Pump Minders and most well committees; and a shift in hygiene practices (with baseline). Wells intended to serve >=200 residents, within 500m.

The targeted outcomes, known at the activity's outset, aimed to strengthen Maghreb municipalities for sustainable development, fostering qualified regional dialogue. Success required: Moroccan & Tunisian municipal associations introducing 6 recommendations (3 each) into national politics; 80% of surveyed municipal representatives rating learning network usefulness 
3 (1-5 scale); 5 civil society organizations per country confirming municipal services became more citizen-friendly, processes more transparent, and participatory, each increasing 2 scale intervals from baseline (1-5 scale); 20 tested learning experiences on municipal solutions shared via networks; and regional Advisory Board testifying to intensified/improved regional dialogue (2 scale interval increase from baseline, 1-5 scale).

The targeted outcomes of the activity were to establish the conditions necessary for the development and dissemination of concentrated solar power (CSP) in Brazil, contribute to a reliable and sustainable energy supply for all Brazilian end energy consumers, and provide non-electrical energy for productive uses in local industries in the poor north-east region. The project also aimed to mitigate climate change by promoting CSP to replace fossil fuel-based power plants, enable CSP plants with thermal storage for dispatchable energy, and explore distributed CSP for process heat. Success was defined by the creation of these foundational conditions for CSP development and dissemination in Brazil, coupled with deepened partner knowledge and experience of CSP technology from German expertise.

The targeted outcome was the coherent implementation of biodiversity and climate-related policies, strategies, and practices by the MET and partners, contributing to diversified and secured livelihoods for local resource users. Success would involve developing three new gender-sensitive income sources, implementing ecosystem-based climate adaptation in five additional areas, and two innovative policy approaches for mainstreaming biodiversity and climate adaptation. Additionally, an external audit rating of at least 2 (on a 1-6 scale) for MET's performance in EMA implementation and CBNRM coordination was a key target.

The activity targeted the introduction of instruments for sustainable ecosystem service valorization by state institutions, considering climate risks. Success was defined by implementing four formal, gender-sensitive, climate-risk-aware agreements in two regions for four distinct ecosystem services, involving user groups/institutions (target: 4, baseline: 0). These outcomes were necessary to create jobs, improve economic resilience, and enhance competitiveness for the rural population. Also vital were appropriate legal/institutional frameworks, effective governance, information/monitoring systems, and strengthened community competencies for sustainable resource use and Access and Benefit Sharing (ABS).

The activity targeted better integration of practical concepts and instruments for sustainable agriculture into international cooperation. Success, as defined at the outset, required achieving several specific outcomes: ensuring the BMZ's position on sustainable agriculture is reflected in 10 guidelines, strategy papers, and international coordination processes; 3 international cooperation projects pilot recommended sustainable production and post-harvest methods; 3 international cooperation projects integrate recommended agricultural advisory approaches and implement qualification measures; and establishing agreed recommendations for 9 best practices and instruments in sustainable agriculture with German and international actors for international cooperation.

The overarching goal is for relevant actors in the ASEAN region to utilize regional technical and political exchange to improve framework conditions for renewable energies. Success, as defined at the beginning, requires: 1) Political ASEAN energy committees adopting one Renewable Energy guideline by June 2016. 2) Decision-makers in individual ASEAN Member States discussing three project-supported approaches for promoting renewable energies. 3) The ASEAN Sub-sector Network for Renewable Energies (RE-SSN) initiating six new thematic focus groups.

Targeted outcomes at the beginning of the activity included: concluding the decision-making process for one additional biogas project; achieving an additional savings potential of 230 GWh (without SECO cofinancing) from Municipal Energy Efficiency Programme (MEEP) investment projects, which were planned to enable annual savings of 100 GWh; and increasing the forecast energy savings from MEEP subsidies by 25% to 0.205 GWh per one million rand (from a baseline of 0.164 GWh). Success would require achieving these specific targets. Broader anticipated outcomes included higher electricity generation, more efficient electricity distribution, and stronger institutions, all aimed at reducing greenhouse gas emissions.

The targeted outcomes aim to improve the electricity sector's commercial and financial performance. This includes reinstating sound utility management, achieving commercial recovery for state-owned enterprises, enhancing billing, accounting, and customer management systems, and strengthening sector governance. Overall success requires a demonstrable improvement in the sector's commercial and financial performance. This hinges on effective component implementation and adherence to legal covenants: establishing a fiduciary team, recruiting an internal auditor within six months, and installing acceptable accounting software within four months.

Targeted outcomes: improved market access for businesses, farmers, poor, with lower logistics costs by alleviating transport bottlenecks. This includes upgrading 94km national highways (Class II-IV); improving 301km national trunk waterways (Class III) for accessibility, reduced congestion, lower costs; upgrading 205km secondary roads to all-weather standard; improving 58km feeder canals; enhancing provincial ports/landing stages. Institutional outcomes involve strengthening MOT's multi-modal transport planning and VIWA's waterway management. Success means achieving these improvements for efficient, accessible, lower-cost transport, with effective safeguard and timely project execution.

The activity aimed to establish sustainable forest management in selected regions of Anhui, enhancing production and regulatory ecosystem services, contributing to local livelihoods, and promoting environmental sustainability. It targeted increased income from timber harvesting (forecasted EUR 222-1,564/ha) to ensure economic sustainability and finance forest management. Success required: provincial administration providing sustainable forestry training/information and managing plans, increased potential for sustainable timber harvesting, local welfare contribution from forestry, increased mixed forests, and decreased erosion-prone areas.

The activity aimed to develop the Seulawah Agam geothermal site (40-55 MW capacity) for grid-connected power, demonstrate private-sector investment in Indonesian geothermal resources, and indirectly reduce CO2 emissions (targeting >230,000 t/year avoidance from a follow-up power plant). Key targets included financing a transaction advisor for a PPP agreement, exploratory drilling, a feasibility study, and training. Success hinged on: successful pre-negotiation of a PPA, sufficient qualified bids for the concession tender, concluding a PPP agreement, positive exploration results leading to a feasibility study, a decision for power plant construction, and pre-negotiated tariffs ensuring cost recovery (targeting 12% return with max 9.7 US cents/kWh).

The targeted outcomes of the activity were to strengthen the capacities of the Serbian administration to prepare and implement EU negotiations. This involved building capacities in organizational structures, processes, and human resources for civil servants; establishing negotiation structures; building an effective coordination system; strengthening existing capacities; and preparing essential documents (strategies, action plans, master plans). To achieve success, 70% of relevant partners would need to confirm effective coordination by SEIO. Additionally, 3 screening reports and strategies for the negotiation process should be available, and 75% of participants and external experts would need to confirm the relevance of training measures by SEIO.

["The activity targeted strengthening municipalities as a driving force for energy efficiency, enabling them to operate systematic, cross-sectoral energy management systems, and continuously identify and implement energy efficiency measures. Expected benefits included environmental protection, climate change mitigation, enhanced energy supply security, and job creation. Lessons from pilot projects were intended to inform city associations and contribute to national energy policy reform. It also aimed to introduce energy management strategies in six cities and disseminate procedures and promotion measures to additional municipalities.", "Success at the beginning of the activity required the 'operation' of a municipal energy management system in four municipalities, including the use of project-devised instruments and procedures, as per initial Module Objective Indicator 1. The use of project procedures and instruments (Indicator 2) was also necessary. Achieving positive impacts related to poverty reduction, climate change mitigation, environmental protection, resource conservation, and participatory development/good governance were integral to the project's success criteria."]

The overarching goal of the activity was to alleviate obstacles and support the emergence of a modern mining sector as a driver of sustainable development in the Central Asian region. The specific objective of the technical cooperation module was to improve the conditions for the development of a modern mining sector as an engine of sustainable development in Kyrgyzstan, Kazakhstan, and Tajikistan. For the activity to be considered successful, specific targets were set at its inception, including: 1. Two suggestions from the regional Public-Private Dialogue (PPD) resulting in governmental reform proposals for improving state framework conditions in each of Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, and Tajikistan, totaling six proposals. 2. Two local development strategies in potential mining areas in Kyrgyzstan and Kazakhstan meeting five predefined criteria: having implementation plans for national economic strategies, being developed participatively, outlining how local employment would be created, containing plans for mitigating environmental impacts, and considering the differing interests of women and men. 3. Managers from at least ten companies in the raw materials or related sectors in Kazakhstan and Tajikistan confirming an increased commitment to qualifying their staff at project-supported further education institutions.

The activity targeted: German DC approaches in 5 intl. programmes/action plans; 10 bilateral DC programmes/projects using shared strategies; 10 examples of German strategy influence on partner country policy-making; and a 3-country increase in active EUEI PDF contributors. Success required meeting these targets and achieving 12-13 points ("successful") or 14-16 points ("very successful") based on OECD-DAC criteria.

The targeted outcomes of the 'Klimawandel in der pazifischen Inselregion II' activity were to enhance climate resilience and climate protection within Pacific African, Caribbean, and Pacific (ACP) island states. This overarching goal was planned to be achieved through six key results: 1) strengthening climate change policy and management at the regional level, 2) mainstreaming climate change nationally, 3) implementing adaptation and mitigation measures, 4) promoting sustainable energy, 5) integrating climate change into education, and 6) improving countries' readiness for climate finance. Success was defined by the achievement of these outcomes, further necessitating extensive participatory consultation, robust partnerships, and strong coordination with regional and governmental partners.

The activity aimed to increase Kindaruma HPP's installed capacity from ~40 MW to ~72 MW (existing machines to ~48 MW, new unit ~24 MW). Targeted outcomes included producing reliable, climate-friendly electricity for the national grid, contributing to "Vision 2030" and climate action, reducing power cuts, ensuring stable supply, decreasing thermal/diesel generator reliance, and facilitating grid expansion. Success required feeding 240 GWh/a electricity into the grid and achieving a minimum 80% availability for the three 25* MW turbines.

Targeted outcomes (at appraisal) for the Programme for the Promotion of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy included: 20% average increase in energy efficiency from individual measures, energy intensity below 0.16 toe/USD thousand GDP (from 0.16). Renewable energy share to exceed 40% (from 38%). Annual CO2 savings of 30,000 tonnes. Macroeconomic profitability (IRR) of 8% per project. 50% SME investor participation. Mobilized investments in RE/EE projects of 24.5 million Euro. Enhancing supply security and strengthening HBOR/investor capacities. Success was defined by achieving these specific targets and establishing the credit line for private capital mobilization for RE/EE.

The activity aimed to improve political, regulatory, and institutional framework conditions and build technical-economic expertise to facilitate RE/EE investments in the Caribbean. Success required: adoption of 8 legally binding RE/EE instruments; realization of 18 RE/EE investment projects; 5 financial institutions granting RE/EE loans; 6 inquiries for CARICOM Secretariat energy consulting; integration of 3 RE/EE modules in curricula; and submission of EE standards for selected electrical appliances to decision-makers in 3 OECS countries.

Targeted outcomes aimed to improve the implementation of sustainable biodiversity management concepts in the South Caucasus. By 2017, success entailed: 100% completion, adoption, and poverty/gender-equitable application of NBSAP II in two countries; 100% incorporation of NBMS recommendations into NBSAP II implementation in two countries (Georgia/Azerbaijan); 100% integration of biodiversity-focused environmental education into secondary curricula in two countries; 100% agreement on national standards for climate-adapted sustainable management of forests/pastures/agricultural lands in two countries; and 100% integration of regional/cross-border biodiversity conservation challenges into NEAPs/NBSAPs in two countries.

The activity targeted improving urban water and sanitation services in Eastern Province, specifically Katete and Chipata, to mitigate health risks and enhance living conditions. Outcomes aimed to ensure reliable drinking water for most small-town residents and entities. Success required: increasing water availability to at least 18 hours/day; reducing unaccounted water to <30%; achieving 100% water meter coverage; 80% population access to public network drinking water; 5 liters/person/day consumption from kiosks; water quality compliance; 85% billed water collection rate; and 100% cost recovery for running costs.

["The UK Nigeria Infrastructure Advisory Facility (UKNIAF), particularly its £10m CDEL extension, targets enhancing climate-smart infrastructure management and planning in Nigeria. Key outcomes include supporting Nigeria's energy transition, decarbonizing its energy sector, and increasing energy/transport access. Specific targets for the £10m CDEL over five years are: catalyzing five principal commercial investments by end of 2022; providing 200,000 people with improved energy access by end of 2024; reducing/avoiding 1396 CO2te tonnes of GHG emissions annually (approx. 10,000 tonnes over asset lifetimes); mobilizing £35m in public and private finance; and installing 6 MW of energy capacity by end of 2024. The facility also aims to de-risk investments in private sector off-grid energy solutions (e.g., minigrids, solar home systems, clean cooking products) and gather evidence on consumer affordability and technology viability.", "Overall success requires achieving the quantitative targets for investments, energy access, emissions, finance mobilization, and energy capacity. It also necessitates successfully de-risking investments in high-risk, developmental areas to catalyze private and public sector engagement. Success relies on addressing critical market gaps, such as the lack of working capital and upfront debt finance for off-grid energy and clean cooking businesses, by effectively deploying the blended finance model. Leveraging strong policy alignment (Nigeria's NDC), supportive institutions (REA), and a conducive regulatory environment (minigrid regulations) is crucial. Furthermore, the activity must overcome past fund disbursement delays, demonstrate systemic market impact by fostering new technologies and firms, and ensure inclusive benefits, particularly for women and disabled individuals."]

The targeted outcomes of the activity are to improve access to basic social services, expand economic opportunities, and enhance environmental management for communities hosting refugees in the target areas. Overall success hinges on establishing and maintaining County Steering Committees, recruiting three auditors for oversight, and establishing County Integrated Project Implementation Units (CIPIUs) within six months of the effective date, all with satisfactory composition and resources. It also requires robust risk mitigation and management for substantial risks (Political and Governance, Technical Design, Institutional Capacity, Fiduciary, Environment and Social), adherence to safeguard policies, integration of gender considerations, and achieving projected cumulative disbursements of US$103.00 million by 2022.

["Targeted outcomes are to develop a robust policy, fiscal, legal, regulatory, and institutional framework for Mongolia's mining and extractive sector, meeting the needs of government, industry, and civil society. This includes Erdenes MGL LLC operating per good international commercial practices. Specific outcomes cover strengthening capacity for revenue management, regulatory oversight, state equity management, policy/strategy development, institutional reviews, legal/regulatory regimes, macroeconomic impact management, improved tax collection/audit, benefit distribution, and environmental/social impact management with human resources development. A Strategic Environmental and Social Assessment (SESA) is also targeted.", "Success requires: establishing a Project Steering Committee, Director, PIU, and adopting manuals; submitting annual implementation plans; engaging an independent auditor by Mar 2009; MRPAM developing and finalizing model contracts by Mar 2010; MoF completing and implementing an institutional assessment by Jun 2010. Erdenes MGL LLC must be established with OECD-consistent corporate systems, publicly disclose reports, and prepare a strategy for stock exchange listing by Jun 2010. Ultimate success is achieving these robust frameworks and Erdenes MGL LLC's adherence to international commercial practices."]
NO RESPONSE

The targeted outcomes of the Inclusive Growth through Improved Connectivity Program, Subprogram 2, are to achieve more developed domestic and international connectivity through rigorous coordination and regulation, ultimately generating more and better jobs and reducing poverty. The overarching impact of the program is to reduce transport and logistics costs, contributing to higher and more inclusive economic growth that is sustained over the medium term. For the activity to be considered an overall success, it would require successful implementation of the new regulatory frameworks and pilot mechanisms, addressing deep-seated issues of inequality and high informal sector employment (particularly in eastern Indonesia) through sustained efforts beyond infrastructure, and the effectiveness of the revitalized Policy Committee for Acceleration of Priority Infrastructure Delivery (KKPPI) and the Viability Gap Fund (VGF) in resolving bottlenecks and attracting private sector investment. Factors indicating potential success include strong government commitment, creation of fiscal space through fuel subsidy reduction, collaborative cofinancing from multiple international partners, and a focus on high-impact policy reforms.

The targeted outcomes of the activity are to improve the efficiency and management of Cape Verde's national road assets and to lay the groundwork for transport sector State Owned Enterprise reform. The activity would be considered an overall success upon the achievement of these objectives. While "PDO Level Results Indicators" are referenced in the project document as a means to measure success, however, these specific details are not present in the provided pages.

The Climate Smart Jobs Programme aims to create jobs, establish UK partnerships supporting inclusive economic growth, create large-scale youth jobs, position the UK as a trade/investment partner, and drive ambitious climate action. Targeted outcomes include growing climate-smart agribusinesses to create jobs, increase incomes for over 300,000 households, and build climate resilience; supporting climate-smart land management to protect biodiversity by storing up to 1.8 million tons of CO2 and delivering 
£80m in benefits; and removing business barriers to yield a 
£60m return on investment. Success requires achieving these specific targets.

Targeted outcomes: Achieve inclusive economic growth and reduce key barriers in OPTs. The addendum aims to stabilize the West Bank economy and support early recovery in Gaza. Specifics include:*Tasdeer: 1,000 jobs (min. 390 in Gaza, 200 women); £5m increased annual sales (£1.53m Gaza SMEs); £609k annual savings for 50 solar-equipped companies; preserved West Bank jobs/businesses; reduced export losses; increased energy efficiency; PA support for Gaza recovery.*World Bank: Resumed basic water/energy services in Gaza; improved PA institutional capacity; developed West Bank water/energy infrastructure.*OQ: Analytical work/lobbying for Gaza basic services; potential re-engagement in Israel-PA dialogue.Overall success requires: effective implementation of robust risk mitigation (aid diversion, fraud); adaptability to dynamic political/security environment; strong partner capacity in high-risk context; conflict allowing delivery (especially Gaza); PA Government's commitment to reform; overcoming fiscal crisis/revenue issues.

["Enhanced provision and long-term sustainability of national and global environmental services through a scaled-up Payment for Environmental Services (PSA) system. Enhanced conservation and long-term sustainability of globally significant biodiversity in productive landscapes, buffer zones, and corridors. New sustainable financing mechanisms for PSA. Strengthened FONAFIFO and expanded PSA program. Increased medium/small-holder participation via technical assistance, capacity building, secure land rights. Improved payment efficiency and positive impacts on biodiversity, carbon sequestration, and socio-economic conditions, especially in three pilot watershed areas.", "Overall success requires achieving the Project Development Objective (enhanced provision and long-term sustainability of national/global environmental services via scaled-up PSA) and the Global Environmental Objective (enhanced, sustainable biodiversity conservation in productive landscapes/corridors). This includes effective establishment and operation of new sustainable financing mechanisms for PSA, and successful institutional strengthening of FONAFIFO for expanded PSA implementation. Increased medium/small-holder participation, improved payment efficiency, and positive, measurable impacts on biodiversity, carbon sequestration, and socio-economic conditions (especially in pilot areas) must be verified by M&E. Fulfillment of all dated covenants and agreements, like establishing Fundacion Banco Ambiental and Fondo para la Biodiversidad Sostenible, is also critical."]

The targeted outcome of the Meghalaya Community-led Landscapes Management Project is to strengthen community-led landscapes management in selected landscapes in the state of Meghalaya. However, the provided documents (pages 1-10 of the Project Appraisal Document) do not detail specific measurable criteria or indicators that would be needed to consider the activity an overall success. While 'PDO Level Results Indicators' are referenced, their content is not included in these pages.

Targeted outcomes include: By 2025, achieve 90% access to safe drinking water (25L/person/day within 500m) and 90% access to improved sanitation, reducing Under 5 mortality to 50 per 1000 live births. By 2015, increase rural water supply coverage from 55% to 70%, and sanitation coverage from 40% to 50%, with 85% water facility functionality. The program aims to provide 3.8 million rural inhabitants with improved water/sanitation. Specifically, AfDB's intervention targets an additional 300,000 rural inhabitants for improved water and 500,000 for improved sanitation/health/hygiene facilities (50% women for both). Expected benefits include reduced water-borne diseases, improved girls' school attendance, and increased production time due to saved water collection time.

Targeted outcomes: improved integrated water resource management and catalyzed investments for diverse water needs. Specific targets include mobilizing 90% of downstream investment financing by 2020 and establishing strong water institutions with 90% of planned staffing/budget by 2020, managing resources per the IWRM Plan. Overall success requires achieving these, plus by 2030: 50% population with adequate water, 10,000 ha new irrigated areas, and 100% IDPs recovering livelihoods. Key outputs, like approved inception (12 months) and interim (16 months) reports, validated IWRM plan (20 months), investment plan (26 months), and 4 rehabilitated reservoirs (29 months), along with strengthened MoWR/community capacity, are also essential.

The targeted outcomes of the activity are to improve the management of target conservation area landscapes and enhance the living conditions of communities in and around these conservation areas. To consider the activity an overall success, it would need to demonstrate strong strategic alignment, resilience to climate change and disaster risks, and effective gender integration with specific actions and monitored indicators. Vital for success is diligent management of environmental and social impacts through triggered safeguard policies. Furthermore, effective implementation requires the creation and maintenance of dedicated, well-resourced units in each Conservation Area Landscape, led by a coordinator with safeguards and value chain specialists, and the satisfactory update and execution of a Subsidiary Agreement with BIOFUND and the Project Implementation Manual.

The activity targets enhancing logistics connectivity and efficiency of Xiaogan Logistics Park. This involves constructing external roads, establishing a Logistics Management Building, a Public Information Platform for logistics, and an Intelligent Transportation System (ITS). Other outcomes are reducing logistics costs, improving reliability, mitigating environmental externalities (green logistics), enhancing road safety, and strengthening institutional capacity. Success requires full operationalization and effective utilization of all components, achieving measurable improvements in connectivity, efficiency, cost reduction, reliability, environmental impact, and road safety, and successful land resettlement.

The primary targeted outcome of the Forest Sector Modernization and Coastal Resilience Enhancement Project is to improve coastal forest management in the Project Provinces. The provided documents do not detail specific metrics or additional criteria beyond this overarching objective that would be needed to consider the activity an overall success.

The targeted outcomes of the Nigeria Electricity and Gas Improvement Project (NEGIP) are to improve the availability and reliability of gas supply to increase power generation in existing public sector power plants, and to improve the power network's capacity and efficiency to transmit and distribute quality electricity to consumers.

["The targeted outcomes of the Jiaozuo Green Transport and Safety Improvement Project are to improve transport safety and efficiency along selected transport corridors and to promote non-motorized trips within a pilot green corridor in Jiaozuo City.", "To be considered an overall success, the project would need to achieve improved transport safety and efficiency along the selected corridors, and successfully promote non-motorized trips within the pilot green corridor. Specific measurable targets for these achievements are not detailed in the provided documents."]

The activity targets are three-fold: to consolidate key incentive measures, increase sustainable irrigation schemes, and intensify agricultural diversification for targeted actors engaged in irrigated agriculture. These objectives build upon achievements and lessons learned during Phase I to further the broader goals of the three-phase Adaptable Program Loan (APL). Objectives are supported by components for Rural Incentives and Capacity Building; Infrastructure for Irrigated Agriculture; Support to Agricultural Supply Chains; and Project Administration.

The targeted outcome of the BF-Transport Sector Modernization and Corridor Trade Facilitation Project is to improve the efficiency and safety of transport services on the Burkinabé section of the Ouagadougou (Burkina Faso) - Abidjan (Côte d'Ivoire) road transport corridor. The activity would be considered an overall success upon the realization of these improvements in both the efficiency and safety of the transport services along the specified corridor section.

The VN-Trung Son Hydropower Project aims to provide least-cost electric power for Vietnam's economic development and improved living standards, through environmentally sustainable and socially responsible hydropower. It is a multipurpose project for power generation and flood control, intended as a 'good practice' case. Success requires introducing improved planning, design, and implementation practices to enhance economic, social, and environmental outcomes; building capacity for sustainable development; supporting new financing models; and effectively implementing safeguard policies (EA, RAP, IPP) with meaningful public participation to minimize impacts.

The activity targets five key outcomes: clear governance for transparency activities with formal roles; an integrated Monitoring, Reporting, and Verification (MRV) system for continuous data collection and reporting under Paris Agreement Article 13; effective data management for GHG sources/sinks; effective data management for the transparency framework of action and support; and the ability to track Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC) progress and share results internationally. It also commits to gender-disaggregated data provision. Success requires Armenia to shift to a continuous MRV process, build institutional and technical capacities to meet Article 13 requirements, precisely measure mitigation, identify effective adaptation/mitigation investments, link support to national policy, and integrate climate change into national policies through formal data arrangements.

The activity aims to improve forest fire prevention and suppression in select ecosystems and enhance forest management in pilot regions. Key outcomes include: a decrease in human-origin fires; better controlled fires via earlier detection, reduced response times, improved communication, enhanced firefighting capacity, and strengthened interagency coordination; strengthened institutional/policy frameworks for forestry governance; increased public awareness and education for long-term behavioral change; enhanced forestry and protected area management capacity; improved forest regeneration, afforestation, and carbon sequestration; and development of a centralized geospatial database and a national forest fire management strategy for Protected Areas.

Targeted outcomes include improving livelihoods, income, and food security for 1 million extremely poor people. Specifics: protecting 270,000 from floods via raised homesteads, providing 450,000+ with sanitary latrines, and increasing income/assets for 225,000+. It also aims to ensure all main homesteads are raised, water/sanitation systems are in place, foster community support, empower women/girls, integrate health/education, ensure product marketing, and influence national policies. Success is achieved when participants independently maintain/improve livelihoods, foundational goals (flood protection, sanitation, assets, health, education) are met, women/girls are empowered, community groups are engaged, national policies are influenced, and all specific measurable targets (flood protection, sanitation, income/asset increases) are fully achieved.

The targeted outcome of the Niger Solar Electricity Access Project (NESAP) preparatory activities is to facilitate the development of a proposed project designed to increase access to electricity through solar energy within Niger. This involves defining financing mechanisms, developing a rural electrification master plan and identifying the first ten rural concessions, preparing project safeguards instruments (EIA, resettlement plan), supporting tax exemption regulations for solar products, devising communication strategies, and preparing project manuals.

The activity targets increasing electricity access for underserved populations through priority investments, enhancing reliability of electricity services by developing alternative production sources and providing targeted technical assistance, and improving the efficiency of the electric utility through technical assistance.

The targeted outcomes of the CASA activity include increasing economic opportunities for smallholder farmers, demonstrating the commercial viability of businesses with smallholder supply chains, and attracting more investment into the sector. It aims to support early-stage agribusinesses in securing investment and integrating poorer smallholders into supply chains, while globally strengthening larger agribusinesses' smallholder supply chains and fostering community engagement. Learning activities will influence discussions and approaches of investors, donors, and governments on smallholder agriculture. Overall success requires a shift from subsistence farming to a productive, commercially vibrant agriculture sector in Africa and South Asia, enhancing economic prosperity, stability, reducing migration incentives, and increasing global food supply.

The targeted outcomes are to improve the climate resilience of Tonga's transport sector and provide an immediate response in the event of an Eligible Crisis or Emergency. Additionally, the project aims to analyze and address gender-relevant gaps through specific actions to improve women's and men's empowerment. Overall success would be considered upon achieving improved climate resilience in the transport sector, demonstrating effective immediate response capabilities during crises, and successfully implementing and monitoring actions that address gender disparities with positive outcomes, as measured by dedicated indicators in the results framework.

["The activity aims to improve the lives of around 12 million people by 2025. Specific outcomes include developing 148 and deploying 22 new technologies, attracting £35 million private investment, and 50% new prize entrants. The addendum targets 24 FTL pilot projects, 1:1 matching of DFID funding, 10 proven frontier technologies, and 5 picked up for private sector investment. It also aims to deliver assistive technology access for at least 3 million people, catalyse 10 disruptive ATs, develop 6 innovative AT service models, spark 30-50 new AT start-ups, establish an East Africa AT Innovation Hub, and double DFID's initial AT investment.", "Overall success requires achieving an 'A' rating in annual reviews and meeting/exceeding logical framework targets. It involves building on the successful 'portfolio' approach, further catalysing innovation, and delivering on key DFID policy objectives, including becoming a world leader in harnessing technological innovation and advancing Assistive Technologies. Success also hinges on identifying and scaling effective approaches, leveraging private and partner funding (e.g., £5 for £1), and ensuring innovations and lessons feed into broader DFID programming and strategic thinking, leading to significant improvements in impact and efficiency across various development areas."]

Targeted outcomes include improving efficiency and effectiveness of water/wastewater services, reducing pollution discharges to local rivers, expanding town water supply in 78 towns, and improving wastewater service delivery in Danyang, Nanjing, and Taixing. Success requires water supply companies to maintain revenues covering operating/capital expenses and a >=1.3 debt service coverage ratio (Danyang from 2011). Wastewater companies (except Nanjing) must cover operating/debt service and maintain a >=1.1 debt service coverage ratio (from FY 2013). Further, a non-revenue water (NRW) reduction strategy must be designed by Sep 30, 2010, piloted in 3 towns, and implemented in 6 more by Jan 1, 2013. A comprehensive provincial tariff structure review and revision is crucial, alongside addressing high investment costs, lower connection rates, high leakage, weak capacity, and minimizing resettlement impact.

The activity targets improving lives through impact evaluation by enhancing policies and programs. This involves addressing critical evaluation gaps in development interventions, particularly in social protection and agricultural innovation (including micro-insurance). Key outcomes include increasing partners' capacity for evidence-based decisions, ensuring access to impact evaluation evidence, producing high-quality, policy-relevant impact evaluations and evidence syntheses, strengthening the enabling environment for evidence production, and building a sustainable institution. For micro-insurance, this means funding high-quality, policy-sensitive studies with wide dissemination and increased evaluation capacity in study countries.

The activity targets improved urban living conditions regarding environmental health, sanitation, drainage, vehicular access, and solid waste management sustainably, emphasizing the poor. It also aims for significant improvements in sanitation access for slum dwellers, aligning with MDGs 6 and 7. Success requires achieving these sustainable improvements, Metropolitan/Municipal Assemblies implementing all subcomponents and meeting performance criteria for further funding, and an increased proportion of people with improved sanitation access.

The activity targets providing clean, affordable, and reliable energy to 340,000 people by 2021, improving income, assets, health, education, and reducing CO2 emissions, transitioning Sierra Leone to renewables. It aims for tariff stability, an average tariff of $0.65/kWh, and a ~30% increase in electricity demand for productive use, job creation, and improved health/education outcomes. Success entails completing 44 WP2 mini-grids by 2021, maintaining the $0.65/kWh average tariff, a positive benefit-cost ratio (1.74), and ensuring 50% women's participation in training and grants.

The targeted outcomes are: doubling Dar es Salaam port's cargo capacity, increasing Tanzania's total trade volume by two-thirds, preparing up to six major public infrastructure projects catalyzing £600m IFI financing, and progressing 30-40 small/medium PPP pilots with 5-10 reaching financial close. It also aims to implement sector reforms, reduce trade time/cost and business expenses, boost growth, create jobs, and alleviate poverty. Overall success is achieving these outcomes, especially the substantial increase in trade volume and successful infrastructure project progression/financing.

The targeted outcomes of the Darwin Plus activity are: sustained improvement in policy and practice by local communities and stakeholders resulting in biodiversity gains; future projects benefiting from gathered knowledge regarding implementation and policy; and, where possible, projects scaling at the landscape level or in other geographies through policy reform. Overall success would be achieved when the rates of biodiversity loss and degradation in UK Overseas Territories are slowed, halted, or reversed, and the implementation of international agreements and conventions, such as the Convention on Biological Diversity, is supported. This success is contingent upon key assumptions including stakeholders' willingness to implement change based on project findings, manageability of external factors, availability of sustained finance, and receipt of high-quality, scalable applications.

The targeted outcomes of the P4: Pacific Regional Connectivity Program: Phase 3B, FJ Connectivity Project are to reduce the cost and increase the availability of internet services in the Northern Division of Fiji's territory.To consider the activity an overall success, several key conditions and requirements must be met:1.  **Project Management Unit (PMU):** A Project Management Unit must be established and maintained in the Ministry of Communications within three months of the Effective Date, staffed with adequate and qualified personnel and consultants. This PMU is responsible for comprehensive technical and administrative project coordination, supervision, planning, reporting, monitoring, evaluation, communication, and outreach, with competencies in technical project management, project coordination, procurement, financial management/accounting, and safeguard support.2.  **Submarine Cable and IRU Procurement:** The Borrower must successfully procure the submarine cable and ancillary equipment for the "Savusavu Spur" and an Indefeasible Right of Use (IRU) for at least 25 years on the SSCC main submarine cable connecting Suva to Apia and Upolu, Samoa.3.  **Contractual Framework with Open Access:** Both the "Savusavu Spur Supply Agreement" and the IRU must ensure:    *   **Cross-dependency:** Cross conditions and cross defaults must be established to guarantee that IRU capacity is available when the Savusavu Spur is commissioned, and vice versa, making them interdependent for system functionality.    *   **Open Access:** Open access to the infrastructure and services on transparent, cost-based, and non-discriminatory terms.    *   **Assignability:** The ability to assign all rights and obligations of the Borrower under related Legal Agreements, Regulatory Instruments, and Permits concerning the ownership, operation, and maintenance of the Savusavu Spur and the IRU.    *   **Anti-Corruption Compliance:** The Savusavu Spur must be installed, financed, owned, and operated in accordance with Anti-Corruption Guidelines.4.  **Savusavu Landing Party Agreement:** A "Savusavu Landing Party Agreement" must be established with FINTEL for the landing station three months prior to the estimated Ready for Commercial Service Date. This agreement must also guarantee open access and comply with Anti-Corruption Guidelines.5.  **Protection of User Interests and Compliance:** The Borrower must comply with all its obligations and exercise its rights under these agreements (Savusavu Spur Supply Agreement, IRU, and Savusavu Landing Party Agreement) to best protect the interests of ICT service users in the Northern Division and effectively achieve the project's objectives. Any assignment, amendment, abrogation, or waiver of these key contractual provisions requires the Bank's written agreement.

Outcomes: Sustainably improve poor incomes/livelihoods, enhance business environment, create jobs, diversify economy. MOST: 120,825 beneficiaries (+42%), £7.53M income (+38%, £45.50/beneficiary), 163,350 improving business practices (50% women), 7k sesame, 22.5k access to finance. Output increases in market actors, reforms, understanding. MICF: 326,100 poor with positive income/livelihood (+30%), 14k households with additional income (+40%), 25% grantees leveraging new finance. Outputs: 250 concept notes, 35 proposals, 16 companies, $8.5M private investment, 50% commercial viability, 35k smallholders, 30k consumers. Success: Achieve quantified objectives, adaptive management, strong gender focus, strategic pivots (sesame/finance), robust M&E.

The activity targets developing a main project to strengthen solid waste management governance and improve services in selected Senegalese cities. Outcomes include completed feasibility/design studies, environmental/social impact assessments/frameworks, ecotax/fiscal reform support, and recruitment of key project staff. Success requires completing these preparatory activities, maintaining a qualified UCG, diligent monitoring, robust financial management, adherence to World Bank procurement/anti-fraud guidelines, and executing the main project's Refinancing Agreement by June 30, 2019.

The targeted outcomes of the Sarajevo Waste Water Project are to improve the living conditions of people in the Sarajevo Kanton and downstream riverside communities. This will be achieved by: (a) reducing the people's exposure to, and reliance on, highly polluted water from the Miljacka and Bosna rivers; and (b) improving the efficiency of the waste water collection network in the Sarajevo Kanton. These objectives are sought through financing investments in the rehabilitation of the Sarajevo waste water treatment plant (WWTP) and sewerage network. Additionally, the project aims to enhance the financial management and operational efficiency of the Sarajevo Water and Sanitation Utility (ViK) through institutional development support, including capacity building and strengthening for long-term financial viability, and preparing for future investments in water and waste rehabilitation.The activity would be considered an overall success by: (1) successfully providing significant investment funding for the rehabilitation of waste water infrastructure and basic local services in Sarajevo; (2) strengthening the management of infrastructure and services to increase the long-term financial viability of ViK and the municipalities; (3) effectively leveraging the European Bank for Reconstruction and Development's (EBRD) support for institutional capacity building at ViK Sarajevo; and (4) ensuring project readiness at appraisal to minimize implementation delays. Key to long-term success will be the implementation of crucial financial and organizational reforms within ViK to ensure the sustained viability and operational capacity of the project's investments.

["The activity aims for a transformational shift to a climate-resilient and productive agricultural economy in Ghana, addressing barriers to private sector investment. Key outcomes include building markets, creating rural jobs, increasing incomes for 20,000 households, and improving climate resilience. It targets supporting 5-10 climate-resilient agribusinesses with ", "10m direct investment, enhancing adaptation, improving climate considerations in Ghana's agricultural interventions, boosting government capacity for climate adaptation policymaking, and enhancing regulatory predictability to attract private investment. A critical goal is to contribute to Ghana's NDCs on agriculture and land use, aligning with UK COP26 objectives on Adaptation, Resilience, and Nature.", "Overall success hinges on the Theory of Change: addressing poverty and adaptation via private investment and supportive rules, with a new emphasis on policy influence.
