Critical factors include: identifying 5-10 commercially attractive investments with development and sustainability impacts; sustained political will and increased Government of Ghana commitment to climate adaptation; adequate resources from investors and government for climate-smart investments and policy reforms; no impediments from the Covid-19 pandemic; the smaller ATG scale attracting partners (especially GoG); effective FCDO policy influencing; and successful leveraging of Centrally Managed Programmes despite resource constraints. Achieving specified targets like ", "10m direct investment and reaching 20,000 households would also denote success."]

The activity aims to support the Government of India's (GoI) initiative to bridge India's infrastructure deficit by catalyzing private investment. Specifically, its objective is to increase the availability of long-term financing for infrastructure Public Private Partnership (PPP) projects in India and stimulate the development of a long-term local currency debt financing market.Key performance indicators include an increase in the number of PPPs achieving financial closure through IIFCL's long-term debt financing (targeting at least 200 new PPPs) and a four-fold increase in private capital (including long-term debt and equity) available for infrastructure projects over the project life.To be considered an overall success, the activity needs to:1. Demonstrate successful PPP sub-projects to stimulate greater private investor interest and reduce risk perceptions. This relies on well-designed, procured, and supervised sub-projects, along with GoI and state governments strengthening PPP frameworks.2. Ensure IIFCL's effective performance, supported by GoI's initial capitalization and annual sovereign guarantees, and IIFCL's commitment to its business plan and human resources strategy, complemented by the project's capacity building component.3. Address market failures by successfully providing longer-term local currency debt for PPPs.4. Implement robust risk management policies, phased adoption of a Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR), strengthened credit appraisal capacity, and appropriate regulatory oversight for IIFCL.5. Couple technical assistance with Lines of Credit (LOCs) to build IIFCL's capabilities.6. Enhance transparency through web publication of procurement details, bidding, and contract awards.7. Ensure implementation oversight by beneficiaries, citizens, and civil society using tools like Community Score Cards.8. Emphasize appropriate risk allocation in PPP design and ensure the integrity and transparency of PPP contracting through annual procurement audits.9. Accelerate complaints processing and action, and enhance project supervision.10. Effectively manage potential environmental and social impacts by adhering to all triggered safeguard policies, including Environmental Assessment, Natural Habitats, Physical Cultural Resources, Involuntary Resettlement, Indigenous Peoples, Safety of Dams, and Projects in Disputed Areas.11. Successfully build IIFCL's organizational capacity in areas such as ISO 9000 quality standards, human resources, Corporate Environmental and Social Safeguards Framework (ESSF) development and mainstreaming, Corporate Social Responsibility, project appraisal skills, risk management, new product development, staff training, and project monitoring.

The targeted outcomes include increased governmental accountability and responsiveness from the Government of Tanzania to its citizens, contributing to improved access to entitlements, public goods, and services for poor Tanzanian citizens. Indicative outcome-level results include strengthened democracy via improved information access for 6 million people annually, approximately 400,000 cases of citizen engagement, 500 national and 900 local level legislative and policy improvements, and strengthened rule of law. Expected impacts include more responsive public sector budgets, 2 million people annually benefiting from improved basic services, 0.5 million benefiting from improved livelihoods, and almost 6 million citizens reached annually with climate change resilience information.
NO RESPONSE

The activity aims to increase national electrification from 22% to 30% by 2011, enhancing modern energy access (electricity, LPG, biofuels) in urban, peri-urban, and rural areas, improving quality of life. Key objectives: power sector reforms, private sector involvement in SBEE, and reliable, cost-effective electricity. Secondary objectives: rationalizing biomass energy, promoting inter-fuel substitution, sustainable forest management. It supports four MDGs (health, education) and environmental sustainability, displacing 690,000-1,460,000 tons of CO2 from grid electricity and abating 1,147,500 tons carbon/year via woodfuels. Development goals: accelerating electricity for growth, improving governance/financial sustainability, reducing deforestation, regional cooperation.

The activity's targeted outcomes are to improve accountability and reporting of the central government's Specific Purpose Grants (DAK) for infrastructure sub-sectors within pilot local governments (LGs). This will be achieved through improved financial and technical reporting, and verification of outputs delivered by LGs using DAK. Success would be indicated by these improvements, enhanced LG capacity leading to better DAK utilization in other sectors, reinforced framework for DAK expenditure quality, and strengthened monitoring and verification of DAK implementation.

Targeted outcomes: Increase livelihoods' resilience and nutrition for 2.5M people via improved income, food availability, utilization, and stability, with a focus on conflict areas, climate adaptation, mother/child nutrition, and landless. 2014 targets: 450K people with 5% agricultural productivity gains; 800K with 1 month improved food security. Success requires achieving these goals, demonstrating transformative change (knowledge platform, addressing systemic obstacles, building government capacity, enhancing economic potential via nutrition/skills), and advancing gender equality.

Targeted outcomes: Improved drainage and flood prevention in peri-urban Dakar for residents. Integrate flood risks into urban planning, strengthen institutional capacity, and develop future stormwater management programs. Establish primary/secondary drainage systems with effective O&M in Pikine/Guédiawaye. Increase local/national awareness and community engagement in flood prevention/adaptation measures. Ensure efficient project management, monitoring, and evaluation.Success needs: Reduced flood vulnerability and improved livelihoods. Effective environmental/social safeguard implementation with mitigated adverse impacts (e.g., resettlement for ~224 households in phase 2). Strong stakeholder participation, sustainable O&M, and local ownership of initiatives.

The targeted outcomes of the HEROS activity are: the UK's ability to provide an effective and timely response to at least six new crises annually (including one large response and ongoing support to protracted crises); increased FCDO's ability for bespoke and flexible responses to rapid-onset/protracted/chronic crises; improved FCDO's capacity for preparing and responding to the full range of humanitarian crises through robust humanitarian supply chain and operational capability; and FCDO having proportionate and scalable operational capacity to contribute effectively to National Security Objectives for addressing conflict and instability overseas. Overall success would involve consistently achieving these outcomes, maintaining an 'A' score at annual reviews, delivering effective and timely responses with the number of different responses exceeding set targets, generating secondary benefits, and successfully implementing recommendations from evaluations and reviews. For the extension, success also means ensuring no break in service and a smooth transition to the successor programme, averting capability loss.

MECS aims to accelerate clean cooking uptake, mitigating 3.2M annual deaths from polluting fuels, and contributing to SDG 7.1.2, SDG 13, and Paris Agreement goals. The cost extension targets improved clean energy access for >= 1.7M people and leveraging >= £200M. Key country outcomes include rolling out 10,000 EPCs in Tanzania (supporting institutional transition by 2024/2025, TANESCO partnership) and Uganda (incl. 100 schools, carbon payment pilot for 2,000 households). Overall success requires achieving these targets, scaling innovations, informing policy, and ensuring Country Demonstrators meet eligibility criteria.

["The targeted outcomes include co-development of the initiative with strong Southern-Hemisphere and Small Island Developing States participation, alongside Northern Hemisphere partners. It aims for the articulation of a clear agenda for solution-focused global adaptation and resilience research, coordinated investment mobilization towards a shared agenda for developing and monitoring solutions, and a profiled youth agenda energizing student/young scholar engagement. Additionally, it targets agreements on international collaboration for transferring tailored knowledge, tools, and technologies to developing nations at risk. The broader purpose for CLARE is to progress, pilot, and scale-up successful climate science, technology, and resilience interventions to better inform DFID/HMG policy and support global/national actions against climate change.", "Overall success would mean achieving more effective adaptation and resilience actions that reduce climate risks for vulnerable populations in both developed and developing countries, ensuring their representation in the research agenda. It also entails facilitating access to climate finance through a stronger, more tailored, evidence base, and significantly increasing the capacity of developing countries to conduct and utilize research for local adaptation and resilience solutions. This requires securing international commitments, increased funding, knowledge sharing, and capacity strengthening."]

The activity aims to reduce climate change impacts, build resilience, and promote low-carbon development in Nepal. Key outcomes: improve resilience for 700,000+ vulnerable people, 400+ efficient brick kilns, 95,000+ households with clean energy (16 MW capacity), scale LAPAs for 1.2 million people, support Nepal's engagement in GGI, LEAF, REAP, AAC, Methane Pledge, launch 'Mountains of Opportunities' framework, increase climate ambition, and develop the Green Resilient and Inclusive Development Action Plan. Success requires delivering UK's committed climate outcomes, meeting existing adaptation/renewable energy targets, protecting 753 local governments by 2030, achieving Nepal's 15 GW clean energy targets, national program scaling, and attracting other climate finance.

["Targeted outcomes include enabling new provincial/local governments in Nepal to play a constructive role in the federal structure through capacity building, promoting political coherence, integrating consultative decision-making for accountability and empowerment, aligning development partner programs with federal structures, and strengthening World Bank support for federalism through technical assistance and coordinated partner efforts.", "Overall success requires a clear purpose, an integrated approach with multiple partners (MOFAGA, DFID, International IDEA, World Bank), a significant budget, detailed implementation plans with specific provincial focus and flexible funding mechanisms, and a strong emphasis on Gender Equality and Social Inclusion (GESI)."]

The activity targets improved health and time saved for women and girls in rural areas to invest in opportunities, resulting in a sustained increase in demand, supply, and use of WASH services for the rural poor. Specific outcomes include 1,120,000 people benefiting from improved water, sanitation, and hygiene; increased community demand and participation in 1,100 communities; responsive provincial/district governments; and central government supporting effective decentralization of WASH service delivery, enhancing sector performance and accountability. Overall success requires achieving these outcomes and effectively mitigating risks like lack of political will, weak capacity, fiduciary risk, and sustainability issues through technical support, financial incentives, monitoring, and lesson-learning for sustainable improvements.

Targeted outcomes include better understanding of firm development constraints in LICs, leading to high-quality evidence-based policymaking, poverty reduction, and firm growth. The program aims to generate new research on market forces, trade, high-growth entrepreneurship, and social compliance, directly influencing 8-15 policies/programs in LICs, and strengthening research uptake/dissemination. Success is achieved by producing high-quality research, convincing policymakers to act on findings, directly influencing target policies, and effectively disseminating research to key audiences (policymakers, wider development community, DFID) to unlock private sector potential and job creation in LICs.

Targeted outcomes include restoring and increasing Bissau's access to safe water, improving water service quality, enhancing electricity supply reliability, improving water quality and quantity via emergency investments, securing lower-cost heavy fuel oil (HFO) for power generation, and re-establishing revenue collection for EAGB's sustainability. Overall success hinges on sustained political stability, effective institutional reforms within EAGB, and successful donor coordination.

Targeted outcomes include comprehensive impact evaluations for the FMD vaccination project and two pipeline projects (on-farm storage in West Africa, rural incomes in Eastern Indonesia), assessing AgResults' effect on private sector involvement, farmer uptake, scale, and cost-effectiveness. The activity also aims to generate learning on gender, prize structure, market development, and push/pull mechanisms, and provide technical assistance to partners for pull mechanism adoption. Success requires completing these evaluations, generating and disseminating robust learning products, and effectively enabling partner adoption of pull mechanisms, all while demonstrating value for money and gender equality.

Targeted outcomes: Strengthen Ethiopia's wheat rust early warning system for timely rust-resistant variety dissemination; test cassava viral disease early warning in 10 West African countries for disease control; achieve agricultural tech adoption 'step change'; build climate/disease/pest resilience; increase smallholder farm productivity in SSA/South Asia. Design APHI for common tech platform, improved surveillance/advisory, and coordination. Success requires: early warning systems effectively controlling epidemics/providing advance warnings; APHI operational and expanding; demonstrable increases in smallholder farm resilience and productivity.

The activity aims to help participating governments build and enhance shared capacity, institutions, knowledge, and incentives to jointly tackle illegal wildlife trade and regional conservation threats. Overall success will be achieved through: developing regional protocols for research, knowledge sharing, collaboration, and division of labor; gaining agreement among enforcement agencies on regional protocols; establishing regional training/skills enhancement programs; implementing regional pilot initiatives; and increasing stakeholder awareness via a comprehensive communications strategy.
NO RESPONSE

The UK Caribbean Infrastructure Fund (UKCIF) aims to provide critical infrastructure to establish foundations for growth and prosperity, reduce poverty, promote gender equality and inclusion, and increase climate change resilience across the Caribbean. It seeks to deliver major economic infrastructure projects (roads, bridges, renewable energy, water, sea defences, ports, sanitation/wastewater management) that generally achieve at least a 12% economic return, are climate-resilient, and socially inclusive. The fund also targets job creation, improved livelihoods, incentivized positive policy action, and strengthened regional/governmental institutions. Specifically, the Montserrat Little Bay Port project aims for more reliable regional connectivity, substantially improved economic/trade/tourism opportunities, greater financial self-sufficiency, reduced ODA reliance, climate change resilience, increased regional/international trade, boosted cruise tourism, improved food security, enhanced access to critical services, opened regional transport opportunities, and improved port operational efficiency.

The targeted outcomes of the Freetown Emergency Recovery Project are to rehabilitate selected critical infrastructure and to strengthen the government's capacity for managing disaster risk. For the activity to be considered an overall success, it would need to achieve these objectives. Specifically, success would entail the successful rehabilitation of public infrastructure and slope stabilization as outlined in Component 1, and the effective strengthening of institutional capacity as per Component 2, which is designed to address the substantial risk related to institutional capacity for implementation and sustainability. Furthermore, the project's success is contingent upon the effective management and mitigation of substantial risks identified in Political and Governance, Macroeconomic, Fiduciary, Environment and Social, and Stakeholders. Adherence to legal covenants is also crucial for success, including the timely preparation and adoption of a Project Implementation Manual, and the preparation, adoption, and disclosure of environmental and social safeguards instruments (ESIA, ESMP, RAP/ARAP) with their recommendations incorporated into bidding documents before civil works commence. Finally, the project's success also includes implementing specific actions to address identified gender gaps and monitoring their outcomes through indicators in the results framework.

["The targeted outcomes of the Norte Grande Water Infrastructure Project are to expand sustainable access to urban water supply and drainage services in Argentina's Norte Grande region. This will be achieved by providing infrastructure investments and supporting institutional development for service providers and participating provinces.", "To consider the Norte Grande Water Infrastructure Project an overall success, its development objective of expanding sustainable access to urban water supply and drainage services in the Norte Grande region must be achieved. The project's strong alignment with national development priorities and a broader, well-resourced regional strategy are noted as factors that bode well for its overall success."]

The targeted outcomes are to support recovery in priority areas affected by the 2015 floods and landslides in Myanmar, and to provide immediate and effective response in the event of another eligible crisis or emergency. Success would require careful attention to institutional capacity, governance, and financial management, continuous adherence to environmental and social safeguard provisions, maintenance of project implementation support teams, adherence to the Project Operational Manual, and timely submission of annual work plans and budgets.

The activity aims to integrate climate change actions into sub-national planning, benefiting local Indian communities. Targeted outcomes: enhancing state authorities' capacities in Madhya Pradesh, Sikkim, and Uttarakhand to plan/implement SAPCCs; operationalizing these SAPCCs for community benefit; gaining national/international project visibility. Success hinges on creating a replicable/scalable SAPCC model with documented tools, strengthening state systems, facilitating sectoral strategy implementation, fostering inter-state cooperation, showcasing implementation, and leveraging international expertise, all underpinned by effective PMU management and PSC oversight.

The SHINE activity aims to improve health among under-2s in Zimbabwe by assessing WASH and infant feeding impact on stunting/anemia. Targeted outcomes include generating knowledge to inform child health programs, strengthening MoHCC, increasing sanitation, and intervention uptake. Success requires publishing 7 peer-reviewed articles and influencing 2 programs/policies by 2017/18. The extension focuses on research uptake, aiming for successful dissemination: 3 high-quality policy briefs, 8 presentations to policymakers, 1 press interview, and 2 forums on costing/VfM by 2018. Sustained VHW engagement and demonstrating positive impact on child length/haemoglobin are crucial.

The targeted outcomes of the Engagement Facility Resilience and Environment (ICRP) activity are multifaceted, aiming to build an inclusive, sustainable, climate-resilient, and green society in Nepal with reduced disaster impacts by 2027. This involves enabling national and sub-national governments and stakeholders to implement enhanced climate mitigation and adaptation actions through integrated environment and climate resilience planning, thereby contributing to the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). Specific outcomes include the planning, financing, and implementation of green, resilient, and inclusive development at federal, provincial, and local levels, the introduction of innovative resilience-building solutions to reduce disaster and climate change risks, the strengthening of sustainable watershed and ecosystem management, biodiversity, and environmental governance, and the promotion of reliable, affordable, and efficient clean energy solutions. Strategically, the project aims to provide evidence-based policy and implementation support for Nepal to achieve zero carbon by 2045 and become a risk-resilient society by 2030, through the implementation of its revised Nationally Determined Contributions (NDC) and National Adaptation Plan (NAP). This includes supporting data analysis, capacity building for SDGs and Multilateral Environmental Agreements (MEAs), and integrated planning across climate, environment, biodiversity, water, gender equality, and inclusion. The project also targets conserving critical landscapes, identifying sustainable biodiversity financing, scaling up climate actions, documenting lessons, and developing knowledge products on green financing, urban resilience, green business/jobs, sustainable infrastructure, waste management, and corporate sustainability. The facility also seeks to enhance internal capacities for integrated programming, internalize gender and social environmental safeguards, and develop a robust knowledge database. 

Overall success would be achieved when: 
1. By 2027, more people, especially women, youth, and the most marginalized and poor, are increasingly benefiting from and contributing to an inclusive, sustainable, climate-resilient, and green society with reduced disaster impacts at federal, provincial, and local levels. 
2. National and sub-national governments and stakeholders are demonstrably capable of implementing enhanced climate mitigation and adaptation actions through integrated environment and climate resilience planning, thereby contributing to the achievements of the SDGs. 
3. Internal (UNDP and UN Agencies) and external (national and sub-national governments) capacities for integrated climate and environment programming, linked to gender and green recovery outcomes, are built and strengthened, evidenced by an increased number of communities supported, GHG inventories, integrated planning, mobilized volunteers, trained UN staff, and UNDP approved projects with integrated gender analysis. 
4. The agriculture sector's resilience to climate change risks is enhanced through scaled-up prioritized climate actions via increased public and private finance, as indicated by prioritized climate mitigation and adaptation actions with gender considerations, organized trainings, and developed business plans. 
5. National and sub-national government and non-government agencies benefit from new knowledge about resilient infrastructure, biodiversity, urban resilience, green business and jobs, waste management, green investments, and corporate sustainability for further scaling up, demonstrated by implemented green financing models, developed and disseminated knowledge products, enhanced communication, and explored new programmatic opportunities. 
6. National and sub-national governments exhibit strengthened climate-resilient and gender-responsive planning, enhanced data and transparency systems, and evidence-based learning, advocacy, and knowledge exchange to promote a long-term climate resilient and net-zero vision, reflected in prepared gender equality and social inclusion frameworks/plans, strengthened sub-national governments, prepared country-specific emission factors, government delegates supported in climate policy dialogues, organized dialogues/events/campaigns, and youths engaged in climate advocacy. 
7. Enhanced green, local livelihood opportunities for women and excluded groups in remote and vulnerable communities are created through green investment and climate finance, evidenced by integrated development plans/budgets, applied system enhancement tools, developed knowledge products/policy briefs, trained officials/stakeholders, initiatives on sustainable finance, and interventions enhancing accountability for climate finance. 
8. National capacities and stakeholders for the phase-out of Hydrochlorofluorocarbons (HCFC) are enhanced by 2030, with equipped training centers and RAC servicing shops.

The targeted outcome is improved energy efficiency across industrial and commercial companies, municipalities, municipal sector enterprises, and Energy Service Companies by facilitating sustainable financial intermediation for energy efficiency investments. Success hinges on addressing the lending standstill by facilitating credit, overcoming structural energy efficiency challenges, sustained government commitment (e.g., achieving 20% energy intensity decrease by 2015, depoliticizing heating prices), and prudent project execution through strict covenants (e.g., sub-borrower Debt-Service-Coverage-Ratio of at least 1.3:1, environmental compliance) and careful risk mitigation.

The targeted outcomes of the activity are threefold: (i) to increase access to water supply and sanitation in selected small towns and urban areas within the Recipient's territory; (ii) to strengthen the service delivery capacity of water supply and sanitation sector institutions in these selected areas; and (iii) to enable an immediate and effective response in the event of an Eligible Crisis or Emergency. These are the project's development objectives as outlined in the Project Appraisal Document (PAD1834). Following these objectives for water supply, sanitation, and emergency preparedness will fulfill the project's core purpose for the Republic of Benin. This comprehensive approach addresses critical needs in infrastructure and institutional strengthening, ensuring a sustainable impact on public health and resilience within the designated regions. The inclusion of a gender tag further ensures that specific actions are taken to address gender gaps, with indicators to monitor the outcomes, adding another layer of targeted success criteria for the project's development objectives and broader societal impact. Ultimately, the project aims to improve living conditions through better essential services.

The targeted outcomes are to expand access to and improve the quality of basic services (education, health, agriculture, water, rural roads) by strengthening local government capacity, transparency, accountability, and financial management. This involves financing recurrent expenditures through block grants, deepening citizen engagement, fortifying woreda-level public financial management for reliable resource flow, and enhancing results management via data and monitoring. Success entails sustained performance against Core PBS Principles (Sustainability, Accountability, Fiduciary standards, Effectiveness) through JRIS monitoring and achieving DLI-linked results, signifying effective, accountable, and transparent service delivery.

["The V4D activity targets poverty reduction for the 'bottom billion' by improving basic services and livelihoods for 5.5 million vulnerable and poor people, building their resilience. It aims to empower the poorest in decisions, hold development actors accountable, and reduce gender inequality. Outcomes include improved health, education, and livelihoods; enhanced partner effectiveness (350+ organizations); deployment of 4,000+ expert volunteers; mobilization of 5,000+ alumni; and widespread adoption of global minimum volunteer standards. The extension focuses on resilient health, education, and livelihood services, mitigating COVID-19 effects, and strengthening social inclusion and accountability.", "Overall success requires achieving improved health, education, and livelihood outcomes for 5.5 million marginalized people, enhancing 350+ partner organizations' effectiveness, deploying 4,000+ expert volunteers, and mobilizing 5,000+ volunteer alumni to champion SDGs/UK Aid. It also includes widespread adoption of global volunteer standards, successful COVID-19 response ensuring service access, and demonstrable progress against Foreign Secretary's priorities. Success will be seen in building resilient, inclusive, and accountable communities where citizens can exact accountability, and in reducing gender inequality, monitored by disaggregated data."]

The targeted outcomes for the Mozambique - Niassa Provincial Towns Water Supply and Sanitation Project are to significantly improve health, quality of life, and reduce poverty through sustainable water and sanitation services. By 2015, key outcomes include: increasing water supply coverage from an average of 12% to 70%; reducing water fetching time from 1.5 hours/day to 0.5 hours/day; decreasing unaccounted-for-water (UFW) from over 50% to 25%; and increasing average daily water supply hours from 1.5 to 24 hours. The project also aims to raise the percentage of the population practicing good health and hygiene/sanitation from 25% to 50% by 2015, leading to at least a 30% reduction in diarrhea, dysentery, and cholera incidence, and a decrease in maternal mortality from 408/1000 births to 310/1000 births. Physical outputs targeted by 2013 include laying 36.4 km of pipe in Lichinga and 26.6 km in Cuamba, rehabilitating and expanding two water treatment plants and pumping stations, creating 5,000 new connections in Cuamba and 7,000 in Lichinga, constructing 100 on-site facilities for schools and health centers, and rehabilitating or constructing 6,000 household latrines in Cuamba and 10,000 in Lichinga. Institutional development support also targets enhanced commercial, financial, and technical capacities for water services, and strengthening municipal sanitation departments. Overall success hinges on sustained water sector reforms, continuous Government of Mozambique (GOM) commitment, and timely resource allocation. Mitigation measures, such as twinning arrangements and in-project technical assistance, are crucial for addressing potential challenges related to counterpart funding and ensuring capacity development activities are sustained.

The targeted outcomes of the RERED II Project are to increase access to modern, reliable energy for rural households and enterprises through renewable energy, and to improve Bangladesh's capacity for emergency response. To be considered an overall success, the activity needs to achieve: 1) increased electricity access for households and enterprises from renewable sources (e.g., another 550,000 Solar Home Systems); 2) adoption of higher performing cook stoves by households; 3) distribution of Compact Fluorescent Lamps (CFLs) in exchange for incandescent lamps; and 4) establishment of a functional Sustainable and Renewable Energy Development Agency (SREDA) for promoting renewable energy and energy efficiency.

Targeted outcomes: Eliminate 5,000 tons of PCBs, reducing human/environmental exposure. Benefit 1,000 workers and up to 500,000 people. Establish permanent national PCB elimination platform by 2028, improving economic impact via enhanced management/certification of destruction facilities (2 existing, 2 new) and environmentally sound elimination, with cost reductions over 25%. Strengthen private sector in PCB management, enforce NOM-133, deploy national SISG, certify 100 maintenance companies. Success requires: Achieving 5,000 tons PCB elimination, certifying 100 companies, fully deploying SISG, strengthening PROFEPA enforcement, generating market demand for facilities, effective public-private partnerships, and ensuring sustainable PCB management post-project.
NO RESPONSE

["The Productive Safety Net Programme II targets several key outcomes. These include an average improvement of 2.5 months in the food gap for over six million transfer beneficiaries, a reduction in the incidence of distress asset sales among beneficiary households from 30% in 2010 to 20% in 2014, and an increased access to financial and extension services for cash transfer beneficiaries from 10% to 40%. Additionally, an improved natural environment, characterized by reduced soil loss and enhanced productivity of land and the ecosystem, is a targeted outcome. The activity would be considered an overall success upon the achievement of these specific, measurable outcomes.", "The Productive Safety Net Programme II targets several key outcomes. These include an average improvement of 2.5 months in the food gap for over six million transfer beneficiaries, a reduction in the incidence of distress asset sales among beneficiary households from 30% in 2010 to 20% in 2014, and an increased access to financial and extension services for cash transfer beneficiaries from 10% to 40%. Additionally, an improved natural environment, characterized by reduced soil loss and enhanced productivity of land and the ecosystem, is a targeted outcome. The activity would be considered an overall success upon the achievement of these specific, measurable outcomes."]

The targeted outcomes of the activity were to ensure the utilization of HPP Rama at its 2006 performance level for at least 20 additional years and to guarantee the safe transmission of the electrical energy generated. The overarching impact-level objective was to contribute to sustainable macroeconomic development in Bosnia and Herzegovina. To achieve success, the following targeted outcomes were necessary: an average annual energy generation of 550 GWh, unplanned downtime for HPP Rama of less than 90 hours per year, and unplanned downtime of the 220 kV switchgear of 0 hours per year.

The activity aimed to fundamentally improve living conditions for 13,500 tribal farming families (70-80k individuals) by enabling them to become 'full-time farmers,' increasing incomes above the poverty line, and improving standard of living. Objectives: produce/sell mangos/cashews/other products; reduce soil erosion; increase soil productivity, water conservation, and vegetation coverage. Success targets: 70% of participants' incomes above poverty line within 5 years; >65% Wadis better than 'poor'/'drop out'; average yields of 1000kg mangos/acre (10yrs) and 600kg cashews/acre (8yrs); sales at market prices; demonstrated water conservation/erosion reduction; increased vegetation.

The targeted outcomes for the 'Erweiterung der Kläranlage IBB' activity, known at the beginning, included: expanding the Ibb Wastewater Treatment Plant capacity to serve an anticipated 190,000 inhabitants by 2010; ensuring environmentally friendly and healthy treatment of wastewater from the sewer network; and properly treating wastewater collected within the sewage system. Success was defined by preventing groundwater contamination, protecting the downstream well field, and reducing health risks for the population by eliminating risks from inadequately treated wastewater and minimizing water-induced diseases.

Initially (2003), the activity targeted reducing health risks from water rationing and unregulated disposal, and making a punctual contribution to demand-oriented, continuous, and hygienically sound water supply. Adapted objectives (2010) additionally aimed to build capacities for water administration management and maintenance, procure leak detection equipment and water meters, install 5 km of pipelines, two deep wells, and 380 new connections, and foster public perception of the state water provider's increased capacity for basic services. Success would mean achieving these contributions, capacities, and public perception improvements.

The activity targeted implementing a pilot promotional scheme for energy-efficient residential buildings, contributing to CO2 emission reduction and sustainable economic development. It aimed to facilitate access to finance for buyers of energy-efficient apartments, create a new housing finance niche, and establish an enabling regulatory environment. Success required: energy needs of refinanced buildings to be at least 30% lower than benchmark; "EnEff:ResBuild Tool" applied to calculate energy needs for 
10 larger buildings; 
10 larger buildings certified with an Energy Efficiency Label showing 
30% lower energy needs; and 
half of PLIs confirming interest in continued energy-efficient apartment promotion and loans post-project. The credit line was planned to be fully disbursed within three years.

The targeted outcomes of the Programm Windenergie activity, as known at project appraisal, included the efficient and environmentally friendly provision of energy, contributing to climate protection and economic development in the region. Specific quantitative targets were an annual 128 GWh of electrical energy fed into the main grid, maximum dynamic production costs of 0.433 CNY/kWh (0.056 EUR/kWh), and prevention of 119,500t CO2 emissions per annum. Success was defined by achieving these targets, implementing investments with very good quality, operating efficiently and profitably with low costs, covering costs even with up to 50% curtailment, ensuring no negative social or environmental impacts, and aligning overall results with appraisal expectations through a professional and financially sound executing agency.

Targeted outcomes (known at activity start): 1) Contribute to Sri Lanka's economic development via efficient, cost-covering electricity. 2) Meet increasing electricity demand in Greater Colombo by 2001/2002. 3) Construct 4 substations (132kV/33kV & 132kV/11kV, 2x31.5MVA each); install 15km 132kV underground cable; complete 132kV ring. 4) Establish n-1 criterion for supply security. 5) Achieve projected total peak loads (164MW@5yr, 194MW@9yr, 205MW@10yr) and electrical work (839GWh@5yr, 976GWh@9yr, 1031GWh@10yr) at substations. 6) Increase national electrification (>48%). 7) Productive power use in Colombo >60% (from 51%). 8) Economic cost recovery >100%.

Targeted outcomes aimed to improve political/institutional frameworks/capacity for biodiversity conservation, adapting key forest ecosystems to climate change, and enhancing rural living conditions. Success required: an annual national "Information, monitoring and MRV System" from 2013; by Feb 2015, adoption of REDD+ Readiness Roadmap, a National action plan on forest resource monitoring, and an MRV System for climate reporting; by 2021, 80% of timber demand met by legal/certified producers; from 2018, annual forest cover loss below 0.2%; sustained improvement in protected area management effectiveness; stabilized/increased Ministry of Environment budget, with a 20% increase for protected area management (2012-2015).

The activity targeted improved transboundary natural resources management of Lakes Prespa, Ohrid, and Shkodra/Skadar in accordance with EU environmental and biodiversity protection objectives. Key outcomes included: 1. Coordination of resource protection management measures across borders by regional institutions at technical and political levels. 2. Establishment of EU-compliant local action plans for five endangered species per lake. 3. Implementation of one transboundary fishing regulation per lake aligned with EU biodiversity objectives. 4. Introduction of coordinated monitoring programmes for all three lakes, per the EU Water Framework Directive.

The targeted outcomes of the Xining Flood and Watershed Management activity are to improve the protection of property and safety of people from flood events and ensure the sustainable utilization of land and water resources within Xining Municipality. This will be achieved through a greater level of flood protection, enhanced flood management, improved wastewater collection, improved soil and water conservation in catchments, and institutional strengthening and capacity building. Key intermediate outcomes include improvement of flood control capacity and protection of human life and property in Xining city and three counties, reduction of sewage discharge into rivers, strengthening the capacity for sustainable management and development of watersheds, and capacity building for project management and implementation. The activity would be considered an overall success if, by the end of project completion, the following key performance indicators are met: 414,300 people are protected from flooding, an annual reduction of 2 million tons of urban wastewater directly discharged into rivers is achieved, and an annual reduction of 898,000 tons of soil erosion is accomplished.

["The activity aims to enhance Russia's national capacity to deliver reliable and timely weather, hydrological, and climate information to the public and economy. It also seeks to strengthen Russia's integration into the global meteorological services system, improve information accuracy for hazard protection, and enable public institutions to make informed economic decisions based on comprehensive climate models and assessment capacity. This involves further modernizing Roshydromet's services.", "Overall success requires achieving the following targets by 2018: increasing lead times of basic weather forecasts for major administrative centers with reliability over 70% to 168 hours (from less than 120h); increasing reliability of seasonal water inflow forecasts for Volga River basin to 85-90% (from 75-80%); achieving an annual increase of 5% in RHM sectoral data users from the 2011 baseline of 30,000, resulting in 37,500 users; and increasing user satisfaction with RHM services to 75% (from 65%)."]

The targeted outcomes of the "Medium-Size Cities Integrated Urban Development Project" are to support the Borrower in: (1) improving selected urban infrastructure, public spaces and assets, and access to services in Participating Cities; and (2) strengthening the institutional capacity of the Borrower's relevant agencies to deliver and manage local infrastructure. To consider the activity an overall success, the following Project Development Objectives (PDO) level indicators must be met: 100,000 people (cumulative, with 50% female) provided with access to improved urban services; 12 square kilometers of new or rehabilitated urban public spaces, which are accessible to persons with disabilities; and 3 municipalities with an asset management system in place and trained municipal officials to use them. Additionally, several intermediate outcomes contribute to the overall success: Under Component 1 (Improvement of Urban Services and Enhancement of Public Urban Spaces), the project aims to rehabilitate 10 kilometers of urban roads, improve street lighting on 26 kilometers of streets, create or rehabilitate 10 kilometers of pedestrian walkways, restore or adaptively use 4 public buildings (including cultural heritage buildings), and replace or lay 140 kilometers of water transmission and distribution pipes. Under Component 2 (Institutional Strengthening and Capacity Building), success will involve training staff in 3 municipalities to use asset management systems, providing training to 100 people (cumulative, with 50% female) in selected municipalities, and designing the National program of medium-size cities. Component 3 (Implementation Support) contributes to success by ensuring 100% of registered grievances related to project benefits are addressed, maintaining a project website with up-to-date information, and carrying out 3 beneficiary satisfaction surveys.

The Hunan Integrated Management of Agricultural Land Pollution Project aims to demonstrate a risk-based integrated approach to managing heavy metal pollution in agricultural land, ensuring safety of agricultural production areas. Key outcomes include establishing an AEM database, developing a risk-based land management tool, and implementing local regulations/standards for pollution management and prevention. Success requires: all agricultural production areas in participating counties having safety risks classified; 80% of crop sampling points in demonstration areas meeting food safety standards; and 8,000 hectares of agricultural land managed under the project.

Targeted outcomes: Increase electricity access in Niger for 326,400 people and 9,000 non-household entities via 51,000 new household connections across seven urban centers. Improve power system performance by reducing losses, blackouts, and voltage fluctuations. Strengthen MEP's capacity in policy/regulation and Nigelec's performance in distribution system planning/operation, aiming for 60,000 new clients. Success requires achieving connection targets, effectively mitigating substantial risks (institutional, fiduciary, sector strategies), and robust oversight via monitoring framework.

Targeted outcomes: 2.76M people with safely managed excreta and 623K with improved water access. Achieve operational cost coverage ratios of 1.29 (Addis Ababa) and 1.34 (secondary cities). Annual savings of 10.84M m³ from Non-Revenue Water. Overall success requires achieving these targets, increasing access to enhanced WSS in an operationally efficient manner, improving health and productivity, strengthening institutions, and making utilities business-oriented and financially viable, capable of self-financing 25% for replacement/expansion.

The targeted outcomes of the Water Supply and Sanitation Improvement Project are to increase access to piped water supply and improved sanitation services, and to strengthen the operational performance of service providers in selected towns/communes. The project will be considered an overall success when: 105,000 people gain access to improved piped water supply connections; 27,000 people in Siem Reap City gain access to improved sanitation services; 70% of selected water supply service providers achieve operational cost recovery; and 75% of the operational costs of the Siem Reap Sewerage and Wastewater Treatment Plant Unit are covered by customer revenue. This includes specific targets for female beneficiaries, poor households, and households with young children for water access, and female beneficiaries for sanitation.

["Targeted outcomes include contributing to food security, sustainably increasing natural resource productivity (soil fertility, water valorization), leading to intensified and diversified agricultural production. This involves boosting yields for sorghum, rice, cotton, maize, millet, and groundnut, securing production, improving water mastery, and ensuring long-term profitability. The project also aims to reduce poverty, improve living conditions, raise family income, strengthen rural finance and farmer capacities, establish a robust seed system, and combat health issues like HIV/AIDS and malaria.", "Overall success by the sixth year requires achieving the overarching goal of food security and sustainably increased natural resource productivity, with a substantial reduction in poverty and improved living conditions. Specific verifiable indicators include: 100 functional biogas units; 10,000 improved stoves; 1,000 hectares protected by Soil and Water Conservation; 7,500 hectares fertilized using biological techniques; 33,000 meters of dykes protected by sodding; 2,350 hectares of lowlands rehabilitated with average rice yields of 3 tons/ha; and agricultural production covering 85-97% of demand."]

The AR Basic Municipal Services Project aims to improve the quality and provision of water supply and sanitation, urban drainage, and roads infrastructure services at the municipal level, ensuring transparency, equity, and fiscal sustainability. Specifically, outcomes include improved coverage, quality, and efficiency of water and sanitation services; enhanced access to urban drainage and paved roads to reduce logistics costs; and improved operational/commercial performance of municipal basic services through technical assistance. Higher-level objectives involve poverty reduction, increased access to basic services, improved quality of sector investments and planning capacity, and positive health impacts (reducing water-borne diseases) by focusing on under-serviced populations.

["The activity targets reducing biodiversity pressures from tourism and mainstreaming conservation into tourism development in ecologically sensitive areas. Outcomes include strengthening legal/policy frameworks, avoiding/reducing/compensating adverse impacts from infrastructure in 10,000 km² sensitive areas, reducing impacts from inappropriate tourist practices, expanding/strengthening management in 7 Protected Areas (including one new PA and two expanded), establishing Community-Based Natural Resource Management (CBNRM) systems for local benefits, promoting Egypt as an ecotourism destination, and fostering behavioral change among stakeholders via scenario planning.", "Overall success requires reduced biodiversity degradation and effective mainstreaming of conservation into tourism. This means robust legal/policy frameworks are implemented, adverse impacts are avoided/reduced/compensated, and inappropriate practices are minimized. Protected Areas demonstrate strengthened management, sustainable financing, and prevention of harmful activities. Local communities actively benefit from and sustainably manage resources through CBNRM. Crucially, diverse stakeholders achieve collective action and consensus on sustainable tourism through adaptive approaches like scenario planning, demonstrating a shift in behavior and thought, leading to achievement of CBD Aichi Targets."]

Targeted outcomes: improved end-use energy efficiency in heating buildings, affordability, enhanced user health/functional environment, reduced local/global environmental impact from dirty fuels. Success criteria: strong GOS commitment to energy reform, public officials using energy efficiency in capital decisions, continued energy pricing reforms. KPIs: reduced CCS pollutants and greenhouse gases, quantified energy savings/cost per square meter in retrofitted public buildings, and increased end-user satisfaction. Effective implementation relies on competent consultants, gas supply to CCS, and proper facility operation.

The primary targeted outcome is to lower marketing costs for small farmers in two micro-regions (Dondon-St. Raphael and Thiotte-Anse a) and to rebuild selected infrastructure in natural disaster-affected areas. This involves reducing transport costs for goods and inputs, minimizing other marketing costs (e.g., transit losses), and ensuring the long-term sustainability of transport and other basic infrastructure investments. Specific outcomes expected are decreased transport costs, reduced travel time (halved from 3-5 hours), decreased transit losses, and increased traffic.

["The project aims to improve the provision of efficient and sustainable wastewater services in participating coastal municipalities and reduce the nutrient load entering Croatia's coastal waters, ultimately enhancing coastal water quality to EU standards sustainably. It also seeks to pilot innovative wastewater treatment solutions, support Croatia's EU membership by aligning with government programs, and enhance public spending effectiveness through increased cost recovery.", "Phase 2 will scale up efforts to 30 municipalities, benefiting 326,000 people (664,000 population equivalent), strengthening institutional capacity, supporting the Water Management Strategy, enhancing MWSC monitoring, and advancing Croatia's EU accession. The GEF component will specifically pilot a medium-term strategy for marine nutrient and organic pollution, and seawater quality monitoring will be expanded to all participating municipalities.", "The broader Adaptable Program Loan (APL) program ultimately targets improved wastewater services for 476,000 people (1.22 million population equivalent including tourists), an increase in wastewater service access from 45% to 78%, and an increase in wastewater treatment to EU/national standards from 15% to 80% in participating municipalities, while ensuring MWSCs meet minimum operational and financial performance criteria.", "To be considered an overall success, the project requires 76% of households in participating cities to be connected to wastewater services and 71% of collected wastewater to be treated to applicable legislation. Furthermore, participating Municipal Water and Sewerage Companies (MWSCs) must meet performance criteria: their operating ratio should be below 1, collection rate above 80%, and debt service ratio above 1.5.", "Overall success also hinges on achieving a 50% reduction in pollution and nutrient load in municipalities with enhanced nutrient reduction facilities. Additionally, increased knowledge and dissemination of alternative nutrient reduction wastewater treatment technologies are vital.", "Finally, the project's overall success in maintaining coastal water quality and enhancing tourism potential is dependent on 98% of bathing area samples in participating towns complying with applicable seawater quality standards, as assessed through program outcome indicators."]

The activity's targeted outcomes are to strengthen national water resources development and management and increase the Corumana Dam's yield to augment water supply for the Greater Maputo Metropolitan Area (GMMA). Expected results include an increased percentage of river basins covered by water resources management plans, more operational hydro-climatic stations, and the achievement of the planned incremental reservoir yield for GMMA. Success requires meeting these objectives: effectively augmenting GMMA water supply, increasing potable water access, reducing unaccounted-for water, completing the Corumana Dam safely, implementing environmental and social plans, and developing future water resource investment pipelines.

The targeted outcomes of the Second Kerala Rural Water Supply and Sanitation Project (Jalanidhi II) are to increase rural communities' access to improved and sustainable water supply and sanitation services in Kerala, using a decentralized, demand-responsive approach. Specifically, by year 6, the project aims to reach 1,841,848 direct beneficiaries, with 1,150,848 people gaining access to improved water sources and 691,000 people gaining access to improved facilities for safe disposal of solid and liquid waste. Additionally, 200 Gram Panchayats (GPs) in the state are targeted to be implementing decentralized, demand-responsive projects in rural water supply and sanitation. The activity would be considered an overall success upon achieving these cumulative targets.

["The targeted outcomes of the activity include improving agricultural productivity for small and medium-size farmers in Sindh, specifically by enhancing irrigation water management at tertiary and field levels. The project aims to increase irrigation water conveyance efficiency by at least 15% in 5,500 water courses, reducing seepage, spillage, and operational losses, and decreasing waterlogging. It promotes the adoption of High Efficiency Irrigation Systems (HEIS) like drip and sprinkler irrigation to achieve up to 90% efficiency and save up to 68% of water, alongside encouraging crop diversification and improved agronomic practices.", "Substantial water savings, equivalent to more than two sizable dams, are expected from a 10 percentage point reduction in water course command losses. The project intends to benefit approximately 198,000 farm households through improved water courses, 2,600 through high-efficiency irrigation, 60,000 farmers from land improvements (laser leveling, deep plowing), and 33,750 from training in improved agronomic practices. Additionally, 10,000 female-headed households and landless farmers will receive high-efficiency irrigation kits for kitchen gardening.", "Overall, the project expects at least 250,000 farm households, benefiting over 2 million people, to increase their agricultural production. Flood mitigation is another key outcome, with 106,650 poor and vulnerable people and 114,000 farm animals gaining access to 380 permanent flood shelters. Indirect benefits include employment generation for landless farm laborers and temporary/permanent workers in construction and manufacturing related to HEIS installation, along with increased business for private suppliers. The project also targets a 20% increase in cropping intensity in water course areas and a 50% increase in cropping intensity through HEIS.", "To be considered an overall success, the activity must achieve a minimum 15% increase in water course conveyance efficiency in 5,500 water courses. It must also reach 2,000,000 direct project beneficiaries, with a target of 25% female. Success is also tied to 210,000 water users receiving new or improved irrigation and drainage services, with a target of 25% female. Furthermore, the project aims for a 20% increase in cropping intensity in water course areas and a 50% increase in cropping intensity through HEIS."]

Targeted outcomes include reducing industrial pollution in targeted Industrial Enterprises, strengthening the monitoring and enforcement capabilities of the Ministry of Environment (MOE), and operationalizing the BDL financial mechanism for environmental pollution control. Overall success requires establishing a robust compliance and enforcement system, providing appealing financial incentives for industries to adopt compliant practices, and significantly enhancing MOE's monitoring capabilities. Key performance indicators for success include an increase in borrowing institutions complying with environmental standards (target: 15) and over 50% reduction in Chemical Oxygen Demand (BOD) and PM10/other hazardous airborne pollutants discharged by targeted institutions (target: 10 institutions with >50% BOD reduction).

["The activity aims to promote efficient energy use in Mexico's municipalities by undertaking energy efficiency investments in selected municipal sectors and strengthening the enabling environment.", "Targeted outcomes include lower energy use, reduced energy costs, improved quality of targeted municipal services, and the creation of fiscal space for other investments.", "The project seeks to bolster the enabling environment for energy efficiency at the municipal level, including capacity building, sector-wide policy support, and project monitoring and management.", "It aims to demonstrate the operational, economic, financial, and environmental value of energy efficiency investments, and to develop and test revolving financing schemes and implementation models for a sustainable, replicable framework.", "To be considered an overall success, the project must achieve projected lifetime energy savings of 1,020,714 MWh.", "Success requires the signing of 23 Energy Services Agreements (ESAs) and the acceptance of a framework by SENER to scale up municipal energy efficiency in the country.", "Further success indicators include achieving projected lifetime GHG emission reductions of 463,405 tCO2.", "Maintaining a municipal default rate (average non-payment over total outstanding loan balance) of 10% or less is also a success factor.", "Overall success is measured by designing 28 sub-projects and implementing interventions in 9 street lights, 8 water and wastewater systems, and 6 municipal buildings.", "Implementing 25 capacity-building and outreach activities and designing 3 energy management systems (EnMS) for various municipal sectors are key to success.", "Engaging 1,000 participants in consultation activities, with 40% being female, also contributes to overall project success."]

Targeted outcomes: Increasing rural electricity access (7,925,000 household, 80,000 non-residential connections), scaling renewable energy (13.00 MW generation capacity by SPPs), and strengthening sector institutional capacity. Success requires achieving these PDO targets, including 1,585,000 grid connections, 1,350,000 off-grid connections, 60,000 km of lines, 200,000 clean cooking solutions, and mobilizing private investment ($16.5M for SPPs, $3.25M for clean cooking). Robust institutional capacity, efficiency, and accountability are also key.

The activity targets improving access to, and strengthening the framework for, longer-term finance for MSMEs. Key outcomes include providing long-term local currency finance to banks to extend MSME loan maturities, enhancing BICE's second-tier operations capacity, implementing financial infrastructure reforms, establishing a credit guarantee fund, and developing a SEPYME-operated business development service platform for MSMEs. Success requires: 1) MSME sub-loan average portfolio maturity under the project reaching a 1.00 ratio compared to PFIs' other MSME lending; 2) BICE having a dedicated, improved risk management unit for second-tier operations; 3) A functioning SEPYME business development platform; 4) USD 53,000,000 in Bank support for SME lines of credit; and 5) 106 cumulative sub-loans provided by PFIs to MSMEs with project funds.

The Community-Based Recovery and Stabilization Project for the Sahel targets several key outcomes. Its overall objective is to contribute to the recovery and resilience of communities in target areas through a regional approach that supports (i) integrated socio-economic services and infrastructure, (ii) livelihoods and territorial development, and (iii) regional data and coordination. The long-term vision is to strengthen the recovery-stabilization-climate resilient development nexus in the Liptako-Gourma region.Specific targeted outcomes include the short-term restoration of basic livelihoods in conflict-affected areas and addressing communities' needs for economic resources, particularly through climate-smart agricultural practices, livelihood diversification, and income-generating public works. The project also aims to expand and improve local service delivery, build integrated infrastructure for local development, and support regional integration. This includes improving access to basic services critical for coping with climate shocks, ensuring climate-resilient design in infrastructure, and addressing water scarcity and flood risk. A further outcome is the development of an integrated, publicly available multi-layered database for the Liptako-Gourma region, including climate data, and supporting regional dialogue and policy recommendations.The activity would be considered an overall success if:1. A significant number of beneficiaries achieve improved access to socio-economic services and infrastructure (disaggregated by females, youth, and forcibly displaced).2. A substantial number of people gain access to improved water sources.3. Beneficiaries report an increase in annual income (in %) as a result of project activities (disaggregated).4. Hectares of land area adopt sustainable agricultural, land, and water management practices suited to local and regional agro-ecological conditions.5. Joint statements and action plans are agreed upon during annual coordination meetings involving Burkina Faso, Mali, and Niger.Additionally, success is tied to ensuring that 65% of Component 2 financing, and 65% of activities across Subcomponents 1a and 1b, directly contribute to climate mitigation or adaptation and/or improved natural resource management. Subprojects under Component 2a must be sustainable, with operating costs covered and capable of being maintained and staffed.

The Potable Water and Sanitation Investment Program aims to improve living conditions by enhancing ENACAL's water, sewer, and sanitation services in terms of efficiency, quality, financial sustainability, and management, contributing to Millennium Development Goals. Targeted outcomes include: increasing cost coverage from 0.86 to 1.15; providing water to 39,000 and sewer to 12,000 additional low-income inhabitants; and increasing water supply continuity by 27.0 Mm³/year by year two. Overall success hinges on ENACAL achieving financial sustainability, providing adequate service, fulfilling regulator commitments, and establishing INAA-authorized rates ensuring system sustainability, alongside strong stakeholder commitment and effective oversight.

The activity targets contributing to rural economic and social development by increasing sustainable energy access. Specifically, it aims to enhance energy access in rural areas through renewable energy investments and robust support for sustainability. Expected outcomes include reliable and adequate mini-grid energy services, improved technical and financial sustainability of existing systems, and enhanced FUNAE capacity in planning and project management. This involves constructing off-grid systems for productive use, increasing rural energy access (targeting 400 villages, schools, health centers etc.), strengthening operator/FUNAE O&M skills, reinforcing community involvement, and fostering private sector participation.

The activity targets a 65% reduction in HCFC consumption from baseline by 2021, meeting 2020 Montreal Protocol targets, and eliminating HCFC-141b in the foam sector, alongside HCFC-22 in R&AA services. Key outcomes include the conversion of 41 foam manufacturing companies (28 Austral group, 8 Ixom group, 5 individual) to non-HCFC alternatives (HFOs, cyclopentane), resulting in 0 metric tons of HCFC-141b consumption from these entities by 2021, and strengthening public bodies' capacity for social policies. Success requires achieving the 65% HCFC reduction target (48.12 ODP tons by 2020, 30.62 ODP tons by 2021), verifying all company conversions and zero HCFC consumption, securing INEMA S.A.'s counterpart funding, and full implementation of the monitoring program.

["The \"MUNICIPIO MARTI ENERGIA SOSTENIBLE\" activity aims to develop a sustainable development model in Martí, focusing on local food/energy needs, climate change mitigation/adaptation, and resilience. Outcomes include minimizing fossil fuel dependence in transport, strengthening food security via agroecology, promoting citizen participation, and improving municipal governance. Specifics: improved biogas/biomethane production/use in public transport, enhanced farmers' agroecology/biogas use capacity, and increased municipal capacity for sustainable, gender-inclusive development. Targets cover decentralization, jobs, renewable energy, and climate knowledge.", "The activity is successful upon achieving significant increases in decentralization initiatives (from 20 to 70), citizen participation/gender equality mechanisms (10/15 to 15/35 territories/institutions), trained farmers (0 to 13), new jobs (800 to 2,100), renewable energy in public transport (76 to 146 initiatives/communities), mobilized financial resources ($110M to $210M), territories with agroecology (213 to 237), and people with climate change adaptation knowledge (608,200 to 3,316,959). Also, reduced GHG emissions (2 to 5 initiatives) and enhanced local authorities' sustainable public transport capacities, ultimately creating a replicable carbon-neutral model for other municipalities."]

The activity targets climate change mitigation by stabilizing deforestation and forest degradation, reducing poverty, and improving life quality for local communities (women, youth, indigenous peoples) in Haut-Uele. Key outcomes include improved natural resource governance via capacity building, land/resource use planning, and clarifying land rights. This entails operational multi-actor platforms (1 province, 3 territories, 12 chiefdoms/sectors, 72 village terroirs), administrative capacity for forest monitoring, concerted land use plans, and land rights clarification. Forest protection is strengthened by promoting productive, diversified, forest-friendly agricultural models and alternative livelihoods. Success would mean achieving stabilized deforestation/degradation, improved community incomes/life quality, operational governance platforms with specified representation (F30%, Y25%, IP5%), validated 72 Simple Land Use Planning (PSAT) plans, 300,000 people sensitized on REDD+, and 75% of grievances treated, laying groundwork for future REDD+ initiatives.

["The project targets improved access to good quality, affordable, reliable electricity for rural communities, supporting socio-economic development. This involves upgrading networks in ~1,200 communes, converting ad-hoc management systems to legal LDUs for better financial sustainability, and building capacity. Outcomes include connecting ~2 million new households (85% rural household electricity access by 2011), increased electricity for productive uses, and reduced greenhouse gas emissions (259,000 tonnes by 2011). Key performance indicators are a 20% poverty reduction, 10% distribution loss, 700 VND/kWh cost-recovery price, and a commercial efficiency index of 100 by 2011.", "Overall success requires achieving targets: 85% rural household electricity access, 20% poverty reduction, 10% distribution loss, 700 VND/kWh cost-recovery price, 100 commercial efficiency index, and 259,000 tonnes carbon emission reduction by 2011. It also depends on sustained Government commitment, equitable electricity access, and PPC capacity to implement changes. Financial success needs EVN/PCs to meet covenants, generate 25% internal funds for capital expenditures, adhere to debt ratios, and ensure LDU financial sustainability."]

The Kali Gandaki A Hydropower Plant Rehabilitation Project targets improved power supply reliability, plant safety, and Nepal's emergency response capacity. Key outcomes include reducing non-scheduled outages to 270 hours/year (from 312), rehabilitating 144 MW generation capacity, and operationalizing a Dam Safety Plan by 2017. Improved desander efficiency, operational dam safety instrumentation, and repaired key plant components. Overall success requires addressing systemic issues, building NEA's capacity, and consistently following new optimal operating rules agreed with the Load Dispatch Center. The Dam Safety Plan must be operationalized with trained staff, data analysis, and effective reporting.

The Clean and Efficient Energy Project aims to improve ONEE's capacity to supply and dispatch clean electricity and more efficiently meet targeted customer demand. Key outcomes include constructing 75 MW of solar renewable energy capacity, establishing an operational renewable energy dispatch center, benefiting 412,056 direct beneficiaries (50% women), and avoiding 78,018 tons of GHG emissions. It also seeks to provide reliable green energy to specific regions, fostering economic potential, health, and quality of life, contributing to climate change mitigation and private sector development.

The targeted outcomes are to improve rural road connectivity in project districts and enhance rural road management in Bihar, providing all-weather access for 1.2 million people and benefiting 30 million with improved networks. Success entails: road connectivity to habitations reaching 62,000; 50% of roads in good/fair condition; 50% improved expenditure effectiveness via cost-effective designs; asset management systems used in 10 districts; 2,500 km of rural roads constructed with 85% quality; 10,000 km under planned maintenance; 85% effective citizen feedback; and 2,000 road safety audits.

Targeted outcomes are to improve (1) expenditure management (public sector wages, capital investments, debt, pensions); (2) energy supply sustainability (gas flaring, gas-fired electricity, electricity subsidies/tariffs); and (3) state-owned enterprise transparency (financial/employment metrics, state deposits, FATF action plan). Overall success requires achieving these specific targets, maintaining economic stability, mitigating shocks, stabilizing the macroeconomy, improving public services (electricity focus), and critically depends on strong public support and substantial external financing.

The activity targets: improved basic drinking water for 400,000 people (200,000 females, 300,000 rural); basic sanitation for 70,000 pupils in schools and 7,500 in rural healthcare facilities; and strengthening water sector institutions. Success requires meeting these targets: 70% collection rate for utilities, 70% beneficiary satisfaction, 40,000 people (40% women) trained in WASH, 16,000 female students benefiting from MHM, National Water Supply and Sanitation Program (2021-2030) implemented, 5 standard designs/manuals, 50 trained in project management, 10 stakeholder meetings, 80% grievance response, 100% of targeted utilities adopting improved financial systems, and reducing water collection time for women/girls to 30 minutes.

The targeted outcomes of the Hezhou Urban Water Infrastructure and Environment Improvement Project are to improve flood risk management and reduce the discharge of water pollutants within Hezhou Municipality. Specifically, the project aims to achieve the following: 1. Protect 105,600 people through improved flood mitigation infrastructure. 2. Develop and implement a comprehensive flood risk management system. 3. Complete an integrated flood risk and urban drainage masterplan for Hezhou Municipality. 4. Provide 100,000 people with access to an improved sewer system. 5. Remove a total of 1,073.10 metric tons of Biological Oxygen Demand (BOD) pollution loads annually upon the completion of the sewer system, with 766.50 metric tons/year removed by the sewer system south of the He River and 306.60 metric tons/year by the system north. Beyond these direct outcomes, the project also targets the development of a smart internet-based information system for flood and water quality monitoring, early warning, and integrated system control, fostering economic growth through pollution reduction, creating a "green ring" urban parks and waterfronts, increasing real-estate value, and attracting investments. It also aims to influence future water management projects across China, deliver global public goods by promoting urban climate resilience, and achieve an annual net GHG emission reduction of 1,697 tCO2-eq from wastewater infrastructure investments, with lessons learned to be shared globally. The project also targets strengthened institutional capacity for water management within the Hezhou Municipal Government. To be considered an overall success, the activity must meet the specific end targets: protecting 105,600 people with improved flood mitigation infrastructure, fully developing and implementing the flood risk management system, completing the integrated flood risk and urban drainage masterplan, providing 100,000 people with access to an improved sewer system, and achieving the targeted annual BOD pollution load removal of 1,073.10 metric tons.

The Punjab Rural Sustainable Water Supply and Sanitation Project targets several key outcomes. The primary objective is to provide equitable and sustainable access to safely managed water and sanitation and reduce child stunting. Specific targets include reducing child stunting (0-3 years) from 37% to 22%, providing safe drinking water to 6 million beneficiaries in heat-stressed and water-scarce areas (up from zero), and equipping 2,000 villages with wastewater treatment facilities to generate safe effluent (up from zero). The project also aims to increase the percentage of households with WHO quality drinking water at point-of-use from 35% to 75%. Financial sustainability is targeted, with 75% of Operations and Maintenance (O&M) costs covered by tariff collection. Overall success hinges on establishing and maintaining financially viable Punjab Rural Sustainable Water Supply and Sanitation Project - Technical Operations (PRMSC-TOs) through capacity building and tariff collection, ensuring community ownership and participation, and implementing resilient and environmentally sustainable infrastructure designs that protect water resources and meet environmental quality standards.

["The activity targets include: strengthened \"blue economy\" via integrated marine ecosystem management; durable achievement of management objectives; enhanced local community well-being through participatory approaches; reduced threats to coastal/marine ecosystems; upscaling/replication across ASEAN through knowledge sharing. Global environmental benefits include improved MPA/habitat management, cooperative LME management, sustainable marine fisheries, and direct benefits for 1,750 people (50% women). Specific outcomes involve conceptualized MPA networks, improved management of 11 target MPAs addressing threats and ensuring financial sustainability, and initiated management/governance of marine corridors.", "Overall success requires sustained government and cross-sectoral commitment, institutional partners adopting designs, sufficient resource allocation, verified connectivities, MPA entities adopting integrated approaches, supportive governance, equitable participation by women/indigenous peoples, private sector's long-term vision, adequate MPA networks, multi-stakeholder collaboration, financial sustainability, and stakeholders' willingness to adopt best practices. Success also entails producing 30 knowledge products (10 gender mainstreaming), 10 experience notes, 1,000 knowledge sharing visits, and improved institutional capacities for integrated management."]

The activity targets strengthening Maharashtra's capacity for multi-sectoral water resource management and improving irrigation service delivery sustainably to boost agricultural productivity and reduce rural poverty. Specific outcomes include: 100% institutional restructuring/capacity building (e.g., MWRRA operational, WRD/WALMI strengthened, ICIS established, WUAs formed in ~286 schemes); 30% increase in water use efficiency; 20% increase in crop production value per unit of irrigation water supply; and 40% increase in farm household incomes.

The activity targets increasing access to improved, sustainable rural water supply and sanitation; achieving 100% water supply coverage for all villages (including Not Covered/Partially Covered); improving village-level environmental sanitation; and enhancing operational/financial performance of existing water schemes. Success requires 100% habitations fully covered for drinking water, 90% rural households with year-round safe water, and 90% satisfactorily performing community sanitation. It also necessitates high operational sustainability and 100% O&M cost recovery by GPWSCs, timely/cost-effective scheme completion (95%), and the successful transfer of O&M to GPs/GPWSCs, leading to reduced DWSS O&M allocations.
