The project aims to enhance efficiency and sustainability of water, wastewater, and solid waste services in LMC-2 cities, reducing land-based pollution to the Bohai Sea. Key outcomes: 200,000 m3/day new wastewater treatment capacity; 145.8 km drainage pipelines; 246 km water pipelines, 185,200 meters; 2,170 tons/day new landfill capacity; 500 training man-days; master plans; provincial utility benchmarking. Success requires: municipalities/utilities achieving financial sustainability from user revenues (via FIPs), improved utility regulation/management (benchmarking, asset management), comprehensive solid waste management, and significant Bohai Sea pollution reduction.

Targeted outcomes include: increasing access to clean water, sanitation, and basic healthcare in 10 priority municipalities to reduce child IDD morbidity/mortality; improving neonatal healthcare in 25 reference hospitals to reduce early neonatal mortality (5% total reduction); and building capacity in relevant public institutions. Success requires: achieving Barema indicator targets (e.g., Mother Kangaroo Program, deliveries, neonatal death investigation, Neonatology Service, Milk Banks, ITU/ICU beds, management contracts); an overall effectiveness rate of 80-100%; successful enrollment of pregnant adolescent women in PRO-NASCER; and effective risk management given "substantial" critical risks.

["The Ningbo New Countryside Development Project aims to enhance sustainable development and improve living standards in Ningbo's small towns and rural areas. Specifically, it targets improving rural wastewater management in selected villages and enhancing infrastructure and township management in Chunhu Town, through basic infrastructure investments, technical assistance in rural development strategy, spatial planning, institutional strengthening, and demonstration projects, supporting Ningbo Municipality's broader New Countryside Development Program.", "Overall success is determined by key performance indicators: the number of households receiving appropriate rural wastewater management services; traffic volume on the Fenghua-Chunhu access road; the proportion of Chunhu's population served by reliable and higher-quality water supply; the wastewater treatment rate in Chunhu Town; the adoption of energy efficiency guidelines by the NMG; and the completion of the NCD plan proposal for the 12th Five Year Plan and Chunhu Town planning. Financial sustainability, achieved through user charges and tariffs meeting O&M expenses and full cost recovery, is also essential."]

Targeted outcomes include increased economic opportunities and access to enabling infrastructure, attracting private investment, creating jobs, poverty reduction, and shared prosperity. Success is measured by an increase in jobs and formal firms in Targeted Regions. Specific metrics include 4,800 new tourism jobs by 2024, 19,000 additional tourists by 2024 (totaling over 400,000), increasing dried cocoa production to 8,000 tons and ginned cotton to 17,000 tons by 2019. It also entails benefiting over 400,000 individuals, creating a conducive business environment, improving local governance, rehabilitating roads, and enhancing basic services and agricultural output.

["The activity aims to improve piped water supply and sanitation services for selected rural communities in Assam, Bihar, Jharkhand, and Uttar Pradesh via decentralized systems, increasing state capacity for emergency response. It targets contributing to 'Nirmal Status' by 2022 and India's 'transformation'. Key outcomes include benefiting approx. 7.8 million rural people (3.8 million women) with increased access to and usage of improved water and sanitation facilities, promoting latrine use, and empowering rural women. It also seeks to build institutional capacity across MoDWS, state, district, and village levels for managing and sustaining RWSS, improve sector operations, finance (O&M cost recovery), and governance. Promotion of green technologies, 24/7 water supply, 'clean villages' through integrated sanitation, effective ICT, and enhanced accountability are also targeted outcomes.", "Overall success hinges on achieving the Project Development Objective: improving piped water supply and sanitation services through decentralized delivery and increasing state emergency response capacity. Success will be measured by key PDO level results indicators, including 7.8 million people gaining increased access to piped water and improved sanitation/latrines, and demonstrable improvements in O&M cost recovery. The project must also successfully pilot new management and PPP models, strengthen local PRI institutions, integrate water/sanitation with health/environmental programs, institutionalize proven policies, and ensure enhanced sustainability (financial, water source, service delivery). Effective sanitation programs leading to 'open defecation free' villages and proper waste management are crucial, alongside robust implementation of capacity building and governance action plans."]

The activity targets post-Ebola recovery, sustained growth, and poverty reduction. Specific outcomes by Dec 2017 include: health sector budget allocation at 4.0%; 2000 new healthcare workers/teachers; 30 MT private sector fertilizer imports; VAT revenue/GDP at 4.0%; 68 audited procurement contracts; and single-source public investment contracts reduced to <40%. Success entails meeting these targets and enhancing mining sector transparency and energy sector financial health, signaling overall economic recovery and improved public financial management.

[{"value": "The Belarus Health System Modernization Project primarily targets improving selected aspects of health care quality in Belarus. This involves establishing a comprehensive e-Health system for electronic medical record exchange, improving accountability, supporting clinical decision-making, reducing errors, and informing patients. It also aims to optimize PHC network processes, manage NCDs, and reduce hospital burden. Further outcomes include enhancing clinical competencies of healthcare providers in NCD management and modernizing neonatal care at RCMC. The project anticipates averting approximately 177,000 DALYs by 2030, benefiting the overall Belarussian population, especially rural communities, men (cardiovascular disease), women (hypertension), and newborns (Chernobyl consequences), contributing to poverty reduction and shared prosperity by addressing regional disparities."}, {"value": "Overall success will be measured by achieving specific Project Development Objective Indicators. These include: 100% of selected health facilities electronically exchanging patient summaries (from 0%); 7 oblasts cumulatively using e-prescription (from 1); 50% of PHC doctors trained in clinical skills (from 0%); 86% survival rate for newborns at RCMC's Intensive Neonatal Department (from 78%); and 80% of diabetes patients receiving an annual A1C blood sugar test (from 30%)."}]

["The activity aims to strengthen the Recipient's disaster risk management (DRM) capacities, enhance critical infrastructure resilience against natural hazards, and improve overall disaster response. This involves fostering a deeper understanding of disaster risks, promoting risk-informed planning, and guiding resilient design/reconstruction of critical infrastructure (bridges, flood protection, riverbank erosion-prevention). It will also modernize crisis management centers/systems for improved hazard monitoring, interagency coordination, timely early warnings, and reduced emergency response times. Capacity building for CoESCD, MoF, MoT, ALRI, and IoSEE is key. It seeks to benefit approximately 650,000 people, directly impacting 122,000 in Khatlon/GBAO, with 51% women/girls.", "Overall success requires crisis management centers and systems to be 100% operational, including the national CMC and mobile command vehicles, functioning for hazard monitoring and emergency response. It also requires updated seismic hazard maps and improved understanding of seismic risks (via national assessment and Dushanbe micro-zoning). Success is further measured by the number of bridges reconstructed/strengthened based on multi-hazard/climate risk designs, and the number of people whose disaster risks are reduced by resilient flood protection/erosion-prevention infrastructure. A strengthened capacity to coordinate and respond to emergencies, effective institutional capacity building for government entities, and mainstreamed gender considerations are crucial. Full financing and adherence to 'building back better' are foundational."]

The Benin Electricity Access Scale-up (BEAS) Project aims to connect approximately 150,000 households (25% female-headed), 1,000 businesses (20% female-headed), and 500 public infrastructures to the grid, electrifying around 1,100 sites. It targets constructing/rehabilitating at least 2,000 km of Medium Voltage lines, 4,000 km of Low Voltage lines, and adding 100,000 kVA of transformer capacity, along with 20,000 LED streetlights. Outcomes include crucial policy reforms for sustainability, capacity building for implementing agencies, reduced energy poverty, and promoting women's economic involvement, while effectively managing environmental and social risks. The project will be an overall success if these infrastructure development targets are met, policy reforms and institutional capacity are strengthened, financial accessibility and social inclusion are ensured for beneficiaries, and environmental/social risks are effectively managed, all aligning with Benin's 2030 universal energy access vision.
NO RESPONSE

["Targeted outcomes include: stable or increasing populations of flagship species (elephants, lions, buffalo) and stable woodland cover. Mid-term impacts aim for decreased poaching/IWT, reduced retaliatory killing, a 30% decrease in deforestation rate, and at least a 30% decrease in annual area under uncontrolled veld fires. Specific outcomes also include increased national capacity for IWT control and integrated wildlife/woodland management, improved capacity of PA network and CAMPFIRE Wildlife Conservancies to protect biodiversity (more arrests, fewer HWC cases, effective NRM), increased area under sustainable management, greater community ownership, and enhanced economic/social benefits from conservation-linked businesses (reducing firewood consumption, veld fires, agricultural expansion, promoting sustainable grazing).", "Overall success requires: strong stakeholder participation and ownership, flexible project design, alignment with existing needs, and integrated capacity building. Essential factors include official and sustained Zimbabwe Government support for policy amendments; strong economic interest from local communities in Conservancies and natural resources; effective use of new skills/equipment by law enforcement; sufficient staffing/funding for Multi-Agency Units, CWC/PA management plans, and the small grant facility; signed international TFCA agreements; interest from stakeholders in learning from the project; accurate identification of all key conservation threats; and leveraging Zimbabwe’s conservation leadership and proven multi-agency unit effectiveness."]

Targeted outcomes: Strengthen local systems and address gaps in environment, economy, health, education in Akyem Abuakwa by 2023. This means: 1) localized regeneration/reforestation via enhanced livelihoods; 2) increased access/quality social services for community resilience/wellbeing/productivity. Key outcomes: strengthened community-led environmental governance/ecotourism; increased productivity, nutrition, quality basic education/TVET; and increased access to quality/affordable public health. Success hinges on: sustained community support for environmental by-laws, enabling political environment, continuous private sector involvement, social cohesion, adequate school/health infrastructure, and full national policy implementation.

The Ethiopia Geothermal Sector Development Project aims to develop geothermal resources for electricity generation. Targeted outcomes include: drilling 22 production wells and building a steam gathering system at Aluto to establish economic viability for ~70MW generation; drilling 4 exploration wells at Alalobad to identify resource potential, establish economic viability, and finalize a feasibility study; procuring two modern drilling rigs for national capacity building; and developing a legal, institutional, and regulatory framework with associated stakeholder capacity building. Overall success requires 26 geothermal wells drilled/tested, 13 wells demonstrating electricity generation potential, and 2 geothermal sites confirmed.

The project targets restoring critical urban services to 1.4 million Yemenis (49.5% women) to improve health, living conditions, and local economy. Outcomes include 90 tertiary municipal services, 600,000 with rehabilitated water/sanitation, 400 km roads, and 60,000 kW energy. Success requires meeting these targets, ensuring beneficiary consultation in 10 cities, 30% women's participation, and achieving the 94.3% overall economic internal rate of return, while building local capacity and adhering to 'build back better' principles.

["The activity targets building a resilient economy. Outcomes for Pillar 1 (Fiscal Policy & PFM): public debt 72.0% of GDP by 2020 (60% by 2030); contingency fund EC$30M by 2020; published government procurement and commercial SOE spending analyses; income/profits tax revenue EC$165M by 2020. Outcomes for Pillar 2 (Climate Resilience & Adaptation): 100% new building code compliance; 50% coastal sand mining reduction (17,685m ); 2/3 reduction in imported single-use plastic bags (151 tons); 3 Priority Pollution Watch Sites monitored; 2 SVGCF sustainable revenue sources; 1 sustainable aquaculture PPP implemented, all by 2020 where applicable.","Overall success requires meeting all outlined 2020 targets (and the 2030 public debt convergence target). This includes achieving public debt reduction to 72.0% of GDP by 2020, a contingency fund of EC$30M, published analyses of procurement and SOE performance, EC$165M in income/profits tax revenue, 100% new building code compliance, 50% reduction in coastal sand mining, 2/3 reduction in single-use plastic bags, 3 PPWS regularly monitored, 2 SVGCF sustainable revenue sources, and 1 sustainable aquaculture PPP."]

The Albania Competitiveness Development Policy Loan (DPL) targets enhancing Albania's competitiveness by improving its investment regime, making it easier to do business, and facilitating trade.The activity will be considered an overall success if the following specific outcomes are achieved by 2018:Under Pillar I: Attracting and Retaining Investment, the targeted outcomes include developing a comprehensive investment regime, creating a mechanism for investor grievances, establishing a strong and transparent investment policy framework to spur local business and attract foreign investors, consolidating investment laws into a single, clear framework, and reducing international investment disputes.Specific success criteria for this pillar are: (1) an increase in the number of investment leads generated annually by AIDA by 20 percent (from a baseline of 15 to a target of 18), and (2) US$60 million in investment retained through the Investor Grievance Mechanism.Under Pillar II: Making it easier to do Business, the activity aims to upgrade systems and processes in construction permitting, electricity, business licensing, and national quality infrastructure to reduce administrative burdens.Specific success criteria for this pillar are: (1) an improved business environment evidenced by an increase in the Doing Business Distance to Frontier (DB DTF) for Overall Doing Business to 70, (2) a streamlined construction process evidenced by an increase in the DB DTF for Construction permits to 66, (3) reduced time and procedures for new electricity connections evidenced by an increase in the DB DTF for Electricity to 48, and (4) the commencement of inspections for at least two groups of non-food consumer products.Under Pillar III: Facilitating Trade, the DPL aims to streamline border clearance processes, introduce risk management for food products, and reform the port of Durres to increase trade volumes.Specific success criteria for this pillar are: (1) a 15 percent reduction in imported cargo physically inspected by NFA inspectors at border inspection points, (2) the annual value of imports under local clearance procedures reaching $70 million, and (3) a 20 percent reduction in cargo dwell time at the port of Durres.Overall success of the DPL also depends on broader economic growth, with projections of Albania's growth rising to 3.2 percent in 2016 and 3.5 percent in 2017. The DPL's reforms are expected to contribute significantly to improving fiscal balances and sustaining high revenue collections as part of ongoing fiscal consolidation. Crucially, the medium-term economic recovery and the potential success of the DPL are heavily dependent on the pace and depth of additional structural reforms in the energy and financial sectors, as well as improvements in public investment management.

The Haiti Sustainable Rural and Small Towns Water and Sanitation Project targets several key outcomes. First, it aims to increase access to improved water supply and sanitation in targeted rural areas and small towns, particularly in cholera-affected zones. This involves providing 150,000 people with access to improved water sources through household connections and kiosks, 50,000 with improved sanitation via community-led campaigns and latrine construction, and 100,000 through small repairs and expansions, totaling an estimated 300,000 direct beneficiaries (nearly 50% women and girls). Second, the project intends to strengthen the Recipient's water and sanitation service delivery mechanism at the deconcentrated level, including enhancing DINEPA's capacity to develop and manage a national programming mechanism and operationalize its strategies. This also involves defining a long-term sustainable financing plan for the WSS sector. Third, it seeks to improve the Recipient's capacity to respond promptly and effectively to an Eligible Emergency.The activity would be considered an overall success if the following are achieved:1.  **Increased Access to WSS:** A measurable increase in the number of people in rural areas with access to improved water sources and sanitation facilities (disaggregated by sex, following UNICEF-WHO JMP definitions), and the targeting of priority cholera communes.2.  **Strengthened Service Delivery:** Operationalization of a national programming mechanism for deconcentrated WSS service delivery, evidenced by tools such as a WSS baseline (disaggregated by gender), a 2030 roadmap for universal coverage, a Medium Term Expenditure Framework (MTEF), and a 3-year rolling consolidated Program Budget. Success also includes the deconcentration of required functions to targeted OREPAs (e.g., procurement, financial management, payroll) and an increased proportion of project contracts managed at the OREPA level. Furthermore, sustainable management of piped water systems by professional operators would be achieved, indicated by signed contracts, dedicated bank accounts, 100% O&M cost recovery, consistent water service (over 6 hours/day for household connections), and over 80% of water samples meeting DINEPA's chlorine residual quality requirements. DINEPA's conduct of annual joint Government/partners sector reviews, addressing a high proportion of grievances, and at least one OREPA utilizing the project-developed baseline for prioritizing interventions and informing DINEPA's Program Budget are also crucial.3.  **Improved Emergency Response:** Effective use of the Contingent Emergency Response component for rapid response to eligible emergencies.

The Integrated Public Sector Reform Project aims to improve public resources management and accountability in the Republic of Congo, leading to enhanced public service delivery, increased funds for social services (from revenue mobilization including forestry/land use), and greater effectiveness in public administration (via strengthened expenditure management). Overall success requires: non-oil tax revenue cumulatively increasing by +50% (from US$875M baseline); capital expenditure execution rate reaching 70% (from 40%); 50% of civil servants managed via SIGASPE (from 0%); coverage of internal audit reaching 70% of total expenditures (from 50%); and full implementation of a tax administration modernization strategy.

Targeted outcomes include increasing access to improved water for 40,000 people (20,000 female) and electricity for 16,000 people (8,000 female). Water production capacity will reach 300 m³/hr and energy generation 1.00 MW. Non-Revenue Water will decrease to 62%, and Energy Bill Collection Ratio will increase to 70%. Success requires achieving these targets, enhancing SODECA's (75%+ cost recovery) and ENERCA's (stabilized finances, $700k+ annual retainable income) operational performance, and reducing losses.

The Sustainable Energy Development Project targets increasing renewable energy generation share from 2.00% to 9.00%, enhancing electricity supply reliability by reducing SAIDI from 3300.00 to 2400.00 minutes, and improving energy efficiency by reducing distribution losses from 0.00% to 2.00%. It also aims to provide more efficient, reliable, affordable electricity to consumers, reduce RMI's imported fuel dependence and energy sector subsidies, strengthen utility technical capacity, and reduce GHG emissions. Overall success requires achieving the 9.00% renewable energy share, 2400.00 minutes SAIDI, and 2.00% distribution loss reduction.

["The MG - Inclusive and Resilient Growth DPO targets tackling the micro-foundations of inclusive and resilient growth by strengthening individuals' resilience against shocks and creating an enabling environment for economic opportunities in rural communities. Pillar 1 outcomes by 2019 include: 1. Civil Registration: Establishment of a centralized system for monitoring new birth registrations (Target: Yes from Baseline: No). 2. Social Protection: 200,000 households registered in the national social registry (from Baseline: 0). 3. E-money: MGA 1,945 billion volume of transactions on e-money accounts (from Baseline: MGA 1,125 billion). 4. Credit Access: Licensing of private credit bureaus. Pillar 2 outcomes by 2019 aim to: 1. Land Tenure: Increase the number of land certificates issued. 2. Local Gov. Resources: Ensure Equalization Fund executes planned transfers to local governments. 3. Rural Roads: Improve percentage of Road Maintenance Fund expenditure on communal roads. 4. Rural Electrification: Increase number of electricity production concessions and authorizations issued per year for rural areas.", "Overall success requires achieving all specified result indicator targets by 2019. This includes demonstrating a pro-poor impact by improving conditions for Madagascar's poor through increased visibility and state accountability via birth registration, enhanced resilience and opportunities via social safety nets, strengthened financial inclusion via e-money, increased resilience to shocks via credit access, positive welfare and productivity from formal land tenure recognition, potential poverty reduction from decentralization, and positive effects on poverty and resilience from improved rural roads and electricity access. It also necessitates effectively navigating and mitigating climate and disaster risks by addressing technical and financial capacity constraints."]

The Angola Growth and Inclusion DPF aims for sustainable, inclusive growth via a conducive macro-financial/institutional environment and financial/social inclusion. Key targets (by 2021) include: 4 quarterly fiscal accounts; RGI score 60; 90% insured deposits; SOE transfers at 2% GDP; cost-recovery fuel/utility pricing; 3 ARC enforcement decisions; 1% non-oil FDI; 800k poor households with cash transfers; 70% adults with transaction accounts. Success hinges on achieving these targets, reforms enhancing resilience/confidence/fiscal sustainability, and sustained government commitment/effective implementation.

["The activity targets strengthening fiscal reporting and transparency to improve public investment decision-making and oversight. Key outcomes include fiscally sustainable scaling-up of investments, considering all liabilities. Success requires 100% of government loan guarantees and on-lending agreements to be subject to credit risk assessment, published, and comply with guarantee limits by 2020. Additionally, a consolidated SOE contingent liabilities register must be established and updated annually by 2020, with a moderate risk assessment, freeing up fiscal space for social and infrastructure spending.", "The activity aims to improve electricity sector governance. Outcomes include strengthening JIRAMA's financial position and sector expansion planning to improve energy access. Success hinges on improving JIRAMA's liquidity and solvency to reduce its financial drain on the state budget, lower risks for private investors, and support its Business Plan. This is crucial for the sustainability of the Bank's broader energy portfolio, ultimately leading to increased access to reliable and affordable energy, and reducing fiscal transfers to increase capacity for social and infrastructure spending."]

["The Northern Mozambique Rural Resilience Project targets improving access to livelihood opportunities for vulnerable communities and enhancing sustainable natural resource management. Key outcomes include 370,000 total beneficiaries, with 55,500 IDPs and 111,000 women, accessing livelihood improvement activities. It aims for 15,000 Ha under sustainable agriculture, improved management in 2 conservation areas, and 6,076 licensed artisanal fishing gears. Further outcomes involve strengthening institutional capacity and generating global environmental benefits by protecting forested areas and mitigating 7,000,000 tons/year of CO2 emissions.", "Overall success for the project hinges on meeting its Project Development Objectives and end targets. This includes achieving 370,000 beneficiaries accessing livelihood improvement activities (including 55,500 IDPs and 111,000 women). Success also requires 15,000 Ha under sustainable agriculture, 2 conservation areas with improved management, and 6,076 licensed artisanal fishing gears. Furthermore, it necessitates 100% inspected forest concessions, 2 consolidated community co-management partnerships, 7,000,000 tons/year of avoided CO2 emissions, 90% of grievances effectively addressed, and 3 provincial multi-stakeholder platforms in operation."]

["The activity targets enhancing resilience in 13 villages by providing electrical solutions for income generation through solar mini-grid powered multifunctional platforms (for agricultural product transformation, crafts, services), electrifying community buildings, and offering household energy services (solar kits, improved cookstoves). Outcomes also include strengthening the regulatory framework for private rural electrification, establishing micro-credit solutions and flexible payments, and training villagers in electricity-leveraged trades to boost productivity, diversify incomes, and create modern economic hubs.", "Success requires impacting 150,375 people in total, with 30,075 households directly benefiting. Key indicators include achieving a 20% Rate of Access to Modern Energy (TAEM), a 25% Rate of Access to Modern Electrical Energy (TAEME) benefiting 37,575 households (2,500 from solar kits, 5,000 from mini-grids), and a 17% Rate of Access to Modern Non-Electrical Energy (TAEMNE) for 20,000 households (wood energy) and 7,500 (income-generating activities). Additionally, success entails supporting 300 economic units with training/subsidies, and achieving financial sustainability for mini-grids with investors recovering initial investment within 3-4 years, supported by 10+ year usufruct."]

The activity targets: 314,900 beneficiaries (157,260 women); improved management for 113,451 ha of terrestrial PAs; 120 ha wetlands restored; 15% METT score increase for PAs; 9 PA management plans incorporating climate threats; non-deterioration of Serbian spruce, Alpine newt, and Bosnian pine populations; 15% reduction in forest fires; 2 functional community fire-fighting units; 20% funding gap reduction; 1 operational public-private concession agreement; 4 PAs in government tourism programs; 20% increase in visitors. Success requires meeting these targets, fostering slow tourism, linking PAs to government recovery funds, and ensuring long-term economic sustainability via local businesses & community partnerships.

The activity targets strengthening urban municipalities' capacity, improving resource mobilization and municipal/urban management, and increasing access to and quality of public infrastructure and services. It aims for an upgraded operational setup for urban local authorities, a fully equipped MDA, and enhanced urban/metropolitan governance, integrating municipalities into decentralization and increasing fiscal transfers. Overall success requires a 30% increase in current municipal revenues, 7% of the local authorities' budget annually for infrastructure maintenance, and at least 1,550,000 direct beneficiaries from a subproject. Other success indicators include timely budget processes, effective non-tax revenue mobilization, timely municipal repayments, and efficient implementation and maintenance of facilities through city contracts, ensuring MDA/CMA operational efficiency.

Outcomes: Enhanced water productivity via reforms/investments for a better water management system, including enforceable water rights, devolved O&M to WUAs/FOs, improved water distribution (reliability, efficiency, equity), modernized irrigation/drainage, and strengthened Sindh institutions. SIDA will become a sustainable Provincial Water Management Organization; Irrigation Dept. a Provincial Water Authority. Success criteria: Increased channel conveyance efficiency, improved water supply reliability/equity, reduced canal breaches, enhanced user satisfaction. Overall success hinges on SIDA/Irrigation Dept transformation and meeting APL triggers (SIDA water allocation, O&M devolution to WUAs/FOs) with political commitment.

The activity aims to enhance environmental management capacity in Brazilian Federal, State, and Municipal institutions, improving natural resource management, pollution control, and environmental quality. This includes strengthening policies, regulations, licensing, monitoring, and economic instruments via subprojects. Overall success requires demonstrated progress towards the Development Objective, ensuring sustainability, and Phase 3 readiness. Success is measured by states fulfilling eligibility criteria in a policy reform matrix and demonstrating achievement across environmental management areas. Specific targets include state qualification, environmental asset partnerships, and states/IBAMA having functional licensing systems with monitoring.

["The activity targets strengthening Madagascar's fiscal framework and enhancing the investment environment. By 2019, key outcomes include: increasing revenue effort to 12% of GDP (from 10.4%), reducing transfers in total expenditure to 15% (from 22.5%), raising priority spending to 1.3% of GDP (from 0.7%), appraising 30% of large investment projects (from 0%), awarding 70% of power generation contracts competitively and publicly (from 0%), randomly allocating 100% of cases (from 0%), resolving commercial disputes in under 365 days (from 871 days), and implementing risk-based supervision.", "Overall success requires achieving the stated targets by 2019, alongside sustained government reform momentum, effective governance, transparency, and balanced ambition with capacity. It also depends on successful coordination, addressing poverty and weak private sector environment, and leveraging support from the World Bank and IMF. Future reforms in the second operation, particularly strengthening the PPP framework and public investment appraisal mechanisms, are crucial. Additionally, improved financial soundness of utilities, better electricity provision, a stable financial sector, and enhanced commercial justice are vital for broader success."]

The targeted outcomes of the Maritime Investment in Climate Resilient Operations (MICRO) activity are to improve the climate resilience of Nanumaga harbor and Funafuti port, and to provide an immediate response in the event of an Eligible Crisis or Emergency. Additional outcomes include: use of planning tools for resilient infrastructure and disaster preparedness, enhanced inter-island and intra-island connectivity, enhanced port operations and efficiency (including reduced cargo damage and improved energy efficiency), identified enabling environment solutions, and improved ability to respond to disasters. The project also aims to reduce GBV/VAC impact and enhance women's economic opportunities. Success will be measured by: 100% of climate-resilient investments constructed and operational on Nanumaga and Funafuti, 2% reduction in cargo damage at Nanumaga, and $50,000.00 improved operations at Funafuti port. Strategic success also involves supporting outer island livability.

The activity targets several key outcomes. The overarching objective is to operationalize an integrated landscape approach by strengthening biological corridors, sustainable forest and agricultural systems, and building climate resilience within community livelihoods. Specific outcomes include enhanced institutional capacity for integrated landscape management (ILM) and climate change resilience; established and demonstrated biological corridor governance and management; the development of climate-resilient livelihood options for communities through diversification, sustainable land management (SLM), climate-smart agriculture, and enhanced climate-resilient infrastructure; and the establishment of a knowledge management system to support sustainable management of landscapes and communities. Specifically for enhancing sustainable resilient livelihoods (Component 3), targeted outcomes involve introducing enhanced options for climate resilience in rural livelihoods through investments in agricultural production, post-production value addition, and market analysis. This includes implementing climate-resilient irrigation and road design, crop diversification, creating biodiversity conservation-oriented livelihoods and jobs, and enhancing the capacity of agriculture extension officers in SLM and climate-resilient practices. Institutional capacity at dzongkhag and gewog levels will be enhanced for climate risk transfer mechanisms, including piloting crop and livestock insurance. Community livelihoods and income sources for vulnerable populations will be strengthened through post-production value-addition, diversification, and innovative income-generating opportunities, alongside promoting organic produce commercialization via cooperatives with focus on certification, branding, packaging, and marketing.

The targeted outcomes are to secure wild cat populations and their habitats by establishing an integrated, replicable landscape-scale conservation model in priority landscapes of India. This involves developing and institutionalizing landscape-level master plans, national Species Recovery Action Plans (Caracal, Fishing Cat, Clouded Leopard), and integrating small cat guidelines into big cat conservation strategies. Success hinges on strengthening policy and institutional capacity, improving government management of habitats, fostering community stewardship (e.g., via participatory monitoring, livelihood diversification, HWC solutions), and enhancing private sector partnerships, regional collaboration, and knowledge transfer. The activity will be successful if wild cat populations and habitats are secured, threats reduced, and the integrated model is effectively implemented and replicated, contributing to SDG 15 and Aichi Targets C11/C12.

["The activity aims to improve SIEA's operational efficiency, system reliability, and financial sustainability through enhanced financial and operational management, loss reduction, and increased revenue collection. Specific outcomes include SIEA operating profitably, reducing electricity costs, improving supply reliability, and increasing access in rural areas. Quantitatively, SIEA should achieve a net profit of at least SB$25 million by 2011, reduce SAIDI by 50% to 3.24, cut network losses from 21% to 16%, improve collection ratio to 90%, and reduce debtor days to 30.", "Overall success requires SIEA to operate profitably (break-even by 2010, NPBT of SB$25 million by 2011), significantly improve system reliability (SAIDI to 3.24), and reduce system losses to 16% by 2011. Key indicators of success also include improving generator efficiency, increasing revenue per kWh to SB$3.00, reducing overdue payments to 15 days, boosting the collection ratio to 90%, installing 8000 prepaid meters, and reducing debtor days to 30, all supported by improved management and financial systems."]

The targeted outcomes of the Aceh Infrastructure Reconstruction Financing Facility (IRFF) are: to assist the province of Aceh and 12 local governments in Aceh and Nias in reconstructing/rehabilitating strategic infrastructure and extending key infrastructure services of adequate quality standards that meet community needs, where infrastructure was damaged by the 2004 tsunami and subsequent earthquake. The overarching goal is sustainable post-tsunami infrastructure reconstruction and development. Specifically, MDF involvement aims to provide an additional US$100 million to partially fill infrastructure funding gaps (due to misallocation of resources and inflation), leverage US$191 million of BRR's funds for high-quality, IREP-prepared investments, and strengthen sustainability by financing subproject implementation until IRFF's scheduled closing in June 2010. It also aims to facilitate the continuation of infrastructure development beyond BRR's mandate by extending IREP TA to local and provincial governments for implementation and construction supervision, and supporting TA activities for the post-BRR transition. The facility also targets the financing of individual infrastructure reconstruction subprojects, ensuring they meet stringent eligibility criteria based on sector, geographic location, technical, economic, financial, and environmental safeguards, with sound technical designs and adequate supervision. A key outcome is the development of Local Government Annual Investment Plans (LGIPs) and Provincial Government Annual Investment Plans (PGIPs) that reflect local needs and strategic priorities. Overall success would require: 1. Strategic infrastructure in Aceh and Nias being successfully reconstructed/rehabilitated to provide adequate quality services meeting community needs. 2. The US$100 million from MDF effectively filling funding gaps and leveraging BRR's funds for high-quality investments, with the total US$273 million allocated by April 30, 2008, and all IRFF-financed subprojects completed by January 2010. 3. Ensured sustainability of infrastructure reconstruction and development beyond BRR's mandate through completed subproject financing and extended technical assistance for local/provincial governments. 4. All subprojects adhering to stringent eligibility criteria, including sector, geographic, technical, economic, financial, and environmental safeguards, with sound designs and adequate supervision, and specifically avoiding significant negative environmental or social impacts (e.g., on sensitive sites, inter-basin water transfer, coastal zone alterations, large-scale land clearance, toxic waste, or resettlement). 5. Achievement of key performance indicators for infrastructure subprojects related to their use, quality, user satisfaction, cost, and timeliness. 6. Effective consultative planning processes through LGIPs and PGIPs that identify and prioritize needs-based subprojects, with urgent needs addressed rapidly. 7. Robust monitoring and evaluation through IPM reports, annual audits, surveys, and BRR's MIS system, confirming design and implementation quality.

The State Employment and Expenditure for Results Project targets enhancing employment opportunities and access to socio-economic services for youth and communities in Bayelsa, Edo, Delta, and Rivers states, while simultaneously improving public expenditure management systems in these states. Specifically, Component A aims to generate youth employment through small public works, provide technical, vocational, and agricultural training, and execute community development subprojects. Component B focuses on reforming state public finance management systems, including reviewing legislation, strengthening budget execution and multi-year frameworks, modernizing accounting, restructuring auditor-general offices, supporting IFMIS, strengthening public procurement with civil society engagement, and enhancing tax administration. This component also seeks to develop social accountability mechanisms and provide public access to fiscal information. Success will be measured by an increase in the number of people with access to services in targeted communities, a rise in employment in selected communities, and an increase in the income of rural farmers supported by the project. For PFM reforms, success will be indicated by a reduction in the percentage deviation of actual aggregate public expenditure from budgeted expenditure, an increase in the percentage of public contracts awarded through competitive processes above a defined threshold, and an increase in internally generated revenue in selected participating states. An overall success requires achieving these improvements, acknowledging that PFM reforms need sustained effort to translate into improved service access over time.

Targeted outcomes for Programmatic Development Policy Operation 1 include: semi-annual publication of an anti-corruption action plan report (from a base of no report); an improvement in the Global Competitiveness Index (Institutions Pillar Rank) from 137 to 120 out of 144; finalization of the judicial appointment process, targeting all 401 local court judges appointed; improvement in PEFA PI-2 from 'C' to 'B'; timely publication of annual public procurement reports by the Ministry of Finance; and a reduction in business registration days. Overall success requires meeting these targets, alongside sustained government commitment to structural reforms, overcoming political, social, and implementation risks, and addressing capacity limitations.

Targeted outcomes include enhanced availability, efficiency, and accountability of Haryana's electricity supply by strengthening transmission and distribution systems, alleviating power supply bottlenecks, ensuring reliable, affordable, and sustainable power, increasing transmission capacity, reducing overall system losses, reducing Aggregate Technical and Commercial (AT&C) losses in urban areas, and strengthening T&D companies' management and governance. Overall success requires: increased total energy transmitted through the transmission system (in million units); reduction in urban AT&C losses (%); and the development and implementation of an institutional strengthening action plan and a corporate governance and financial accountability (CGFA) action plan.

["The targeted outcomes of the Turkey - Resilience, Inclusion and Growth DPF are to strengthen foundations for inclusive and sustainable growth. This involves three main pillars: i) increasing domestic savings and enhancing fiscal transparency by boosting private and public savings, partly through auto-enrollment in private pensions and improved fiscal discipline via audited financial statements for general budget institutions; ii) supporting the economic inclusion of women, youth, long-term unemployed, and Syrians under temporary protection by improving labor market conditions, promoting flexible employment, increasing access to childcare, and integrating foreigners; and iii) addressing structural bottlenecks to sustainable growth by enacting industrial property protection, improving credit access for SMEs, liberalizing railways, and supporting renewable energy zones.", "To consider the activity an overall success, specific targets must be met by 2018: 2,000,000 employees covered in the auto-enrollment private pension system (from a 2016 baseline of 0); availability of 45 TCA financial audit reports of General Budget Institutions with financial statements (from a 2016 baseline of 0); a female labor force participation rate of 34.9 percent (from a 2016 baseline of 32.5 percent); and a 40 percent net enrollment rate for childcare provision capacity (from a 2016 baseline of 35.5 percent)."]

["The AREAN project aims to enable low carbon energy access, sustainable energy, and green growth, supporting Niue's goal to generate 80% of its electricity from renewable sources by 2025. Key outcomes include reducing national energy utilization intensity from 0.109 to 0.089, achieving a cumulative GHG emission reduction of 9,242 tons CO2 (with 99,633 tCO2 over equipment lifetime), saving 3,281 tons of oil equivalent, and an overall reduction of ~112.2 kilotons CO2. It also targets improved energy policies, enhanced institutional capacity, better financing for low carbon initiatives, and increased adoption of climate-resilient technologies.", "Overall success for AREAN requires continuous commitment from the Government of Niue, irrespective of political changes, and the realization of committed co-financing from the national government. Crucially, the project must effectively resolve critical technical challenges, particularly grid instability, which has historically hindered solar PV initiatives. Sustained support from the national government, financial sector, consumers, and the private sector is also essential for implementing policies, institutional arrangements, financing schemes, and replicating successful demonstration projects."]

The activity targets improving access and quality of water supply/sanitation services in target rural communities and strengthening the capacity of WSS institutions. Success would be achieved by: providing 100,000 people with improved water access, 16,000 people with improved sanitation facilities, achieving an Operating Cost Coverage Ratio greater than 1.0 in project areas, ensuring an average water supply of over 18 hours per day, and developing and approving an Institutional Support Plan for DDWSWD. These outcomes collectively indicate enhanced service delivery, financial sustainability, and robust institutional capacity.

["The activity primarily targets improving solid waste management services for urban populations in selected Indonesian cities. Key outcomes include benefitting approximately 28 million residents (12M in Citarum, 16M in Component 3.2 cities, including 2.8M poor/near-poor and 1.4M women) through improved waste collection, decreased burning, reduced pollution, and sanitary disposal. It also aims to re-skill and train informal waste workers (including women and vulnerable groups) for formal employment or alternative livelihoods, addressing gender inequalities. Globally/regionally, outcomes include decreased waste entering oceans and reduced greenhouse gas emissions.", "Overall success requires: 85% of urban populations in selected areas to have regular household waste collection; 20,000,000 m³ of operational landfill disposal capacity; 16% of solid waste recycled, composted, or treated to reduce volumes; and 70% of beneficiaries (disaggregated by gender) rating interventions as 'Satisfactory' or above. Additionally, leveraging significant public, private, and international funding is crucial. Success also depends on increased households receiving improved solid waste management, implementing gender-informed measures to address inequalities, and informing national policies with analytical work on social and gender impacts."]

The activity aims to strengthen early warning systems, climate resilient shoreline protection, and immediate disaster response in RMI. It targets enhancing early warning/preparedness for communities, NDMO restructuring, financial support for response, and integrating DRM/climate change planning. Success requires: for RMI (P160096), reducing coastal hazard risks for 10,000 people (50% female), 13,000 people receiving timely warnings, $700,000 reduced annual damage, and community involvement. For SPC (P163699), 50% of trained staff applying early warning/preparedness knowledge during disaster response/drills, focusing on the new NDMO Operations Manual.

Targeted outcomes: Expand supply, reduce costs, and improve equitable access to energy and ICT services. Specifically, strengthen energy sector governance, management, reliability, and SENELEC's financial sustainability. Enhance the ICT legal and regulatory framework for competition, investment, and equitable access. Success requires: SENELEC debt cover ratio reaching 0.90 by 2019; un-served energy decreasing to 20 GWh/year by 2019; overall electricity losses reducing to 18% by 2019; non-HFO based generation mix reaching 43%; rural electricity access increasing to 35% of the population; and an improved ICT Network Readiness Index ranking (A.1.02 sub-index).

Targeted outcomes: Strengthened policies and capacities for sustainable natural resource management, biodiversity conservation, and reduced vulnerabilities (UNDAF). A development model incorporating environmental care and risk reduction (Country Program). Strategies for sustainable natural resource/ecosystem management and integrated territorial management (CP Product). Technical inputs for conservation policies (UNDAP). Increased PA management effectiveness by integrating a landscape approach. Success requires: 10% insufficient PA personnel competencies across categories; 20-24% SNAP financial gap; 3,321 ha (Lunarejo), 707 ha (Laguna de Rocha), 4,074 ha (Laureles-Cañas/Queguay) tax exemptions; $100,000 for SNAP fund; improved METT scores across 7 PAs. Overall success hinges on overcoming collaborative challenges, scaling models, sustained political commitment, and active stakeholder participation for long-term PA effectiveness.

Targeted outcomes are to improve access to infrastructure and basic services in selected unplanned settlements (Brazzaville/Pointe Noire) and strengthen government and municipal capacity for urban upgrading, including preventing new unplanned settlements. Specifics include upgrading roads, anti-erosion works, expanding water/electricity/sanitation networks, and communal facilities across 750 hectares. Success requires implementing four upgrading plans, 70% of key municipal assets managed as planned, 75% beneficiary satisfaction (target: 74,531 female), access to all-season roads within 500m, increased improved water access/piped connections, rehabilitated non-rural roads, and creation of temporary employment.

DPL1 targets addressing Ukraine's economic crisis roots and laying foundations for inclusive, sustainable growth. It aims to promote good governance, transparency, and accountability in the public sector; strengthen the regulatory framework and reduce business costs; and reform inefficient, inequitable utility subsidies while protecting the poor. Expected results include curbing corruption via improved public finance transparency, increasing official accountability, closing tax loopholes, improving the investment climate by reducing business cost/risk, and reducing fiscal/quasi-fiscal subsidies in utility/energy sectors while strengthening social safety nets. By 2015, targets include increasing competitive procurement to 55%, 100% of new public investment projects appraised/selected via a public investment management system, and 100% of financial disclosures externally verified.

The targeted outcomes are to increase renewable energy generation and improve electricity supply reliability, reducing imported fossil fuel consumption and network losses, enhancing EMAE's financial health, and benefiting 90,000 direct consumers. Success is measured by reaching 90,000 direct beneficiaries (51% female), rehabilitating 2.2 MW of hydropower generation capacity, and reducing electricity losses to 33% per year in the project area.

["The Regional Off-Grid Electricity Access Project targets increasing electricity access for households, businesses, and public institutions in West Africa and the Sahel using modern stand-alone solar systems. For SOP1, specific targets include providing new or improved electricity service to 1,755,000 people (including 877,500 female), providing access to 585,000 households (58,500 female-headed) and 65,000 SMEs (6,500 women-led), reducing GHG CO2 emissions by 385,000 metric tons, and engaging 57 commercial financial institutions. Other key outcomes are establishing a regional quality assurance framework, a market observatory for product information, and annual publication of beneficiary feedback from consumer engagement programs.", "Overall success for the activity will be measured by achieving the specific SOP1 targets, which include providing new or improved electricity service to 1,755,000 people, 585,000 households, and 65,000 SMEs, alongside 385,000 metric tons of GHG CO2 emission reduction, engaging 57 financial institutions, and establishing regional quality assurance and market observatory. Additionally, success requires the establishment of a sustainable regional market where people can access affordable, quality-verified solar products, solar companies can access finance and expand, and governments can achieve their electrification targets for various sectors."]

The targeted outcomes are to improve SBEE's operational performance, expand electricity access in targeted areas, and promote community-based forest resource management. Specifically, it aims to reduce distribution losses from 24% to 21%, increase revenues from high-consuming customers (specific target not provided), and provide new or improved electricity service to 125,000 people. Operational performance enhancements include smart meters, management information systems, a theft detection crew, and a gender-sensitive call center. Success requires achieving these targets: 21% loss reduction, increased high-customer revenues, and 125,000 people with new/improved service, alongside effective sustainable biomass management and enhanced SBEE operations and customer relations.

The activity aims to establish cross-border transmission capacity between SAPP and EAPP for regional power trade, increase power transmission capacity to southern Tanzania, and strengthen institutional capacity in Tanzania and EAPP for regional power trade. Success hinges on: increased power transmission capacity along the Iringa-Kisada-Mbeya-Tunduma-Sumbawanga corridor; completion of TANESCO's SAPP interconnection infrastructure; compliance with regional power trade operational requirements; TANESCO's ERP software being installed and operational; and EAPP market rules adopted by the EAPP Steering Committee.

The activity targets promoting innovation and community engagement for climate-smart urban development (CSUD), fostering new ideas from civil society, public, and business communities, and jointly developing, financing, and implementing them. Key outcomes include improved access to and availability of data via an open data approach for CSUD monitoring in Serbian municipalities, developing municipal capacities for integrated, real-time data management, and creating public web/mobile platforms. It also aims for new innovative technical/systemic solutions, knowledge management, M&E for replication, and establishing enabling legal/regulatory frameworks for CSUD.

Targets: Strengthen intersectoral IAS coordination and technical capacity to prevent, reduce, and eradicate IAS threats to agrobiodiversity. Establish functional coordinating groups, develop IAS management plans (NSAP, PSAP), guidelines, and methodologies. Build capacity for 12,000 personnel and 6,000 farmers. Implement operational IAS management plans in 35,000 ha. Success: Achieved with long-term threat reduction in 35,000 ha, capacitated personnel/farmers, adopted effective IAS plans, strong political support, investments, cross-sector coordination, and robust knowledge management systems.

The targeted outcomes of the "Resiliencia Corredor Seco" activity include improving food and nutritional security and resilience for families (especially children under 5 and women) in Guatemala's Dry Corridor, enabling them to adapt to climate change. This involves enhancing water security and developing drought-adapted food systems, strengthening knowledge on nutrition and health, and establishing sustainable water governance. The activity also aims to bolster capacities of central and local government entities in these areas. To be considered an overall success, by the fourth year, key achievements must include: at least 30% of assisted families increasing sustainable climate-adapted agricultural production and food access by 15%; 100% of assisted communities developing at least one organized group for agricultural/non-agricultural ventures for product sales; at least 40% of families practicing four or more good health, food, nutrition, and hygiene practices; at least 50% of homes showing at least one improvement in sanitary and habitability conditions; 100% of assisted communities developing a women's health program; 100% of assisted communities having at least 15 designed/improved/constructed grey and green water infrastructure works; 100% of assisted municipalities having micro-basin councils approved by COMUDE; 100% of municipalities having an elaborated and implemented municipal water security strategy; and at least 90% of municipal health districts having solid bioinfectious waste and wastewater management plans and having developed a diagnostic for bioinfectious waste management for health services.

The targeted outcome is to increase the supply of clean and low-cost hydropower electricity to Burundi's national grid. Success is measured by: 1) Cumulative generation of hydroelectricity (e.g., 213.5 GWh by Year 6, full 236 GWh annually). 2) Annual avoidance of CO2 emissions (cumulative target over 232,978 Kg by Year 6). 3) A levelized cost of production from Jiji and Mulembwe that is less than oil-based generation. 4) Transforming REGIDESO into a creditworthy enterprise, facilitating an electricity access program targeting 35% household access by 2030, and improving REGIDESO's efficiency and governance through sector reforms.

The targeted outcomes of the Mali Drylands Development Project include improving agricultural productivity and strengthening rural household resilience, encompassing enhanced food security, sustainable resource use, access to safety nets, diversified livelihoods, and better crisis response. Success requires: a 20.00% overall yield increase for major crops (sorghum to 1.44 mt, maize to 3.00 mt, millet to 1.20 mt); 90.00% of cash transfer beneficiary households achieving a food consumption score above 35; a 65.00% beneficiary satisfaction rate; and reaching 20,000 direct beneficiary households, including 2,200 female-headed households.

The targeted outcomes for the Second National Water Development Project include increasing access to sustainable water supply and sanitation services for people in cities, towns, market centers, and villages, and improving water resources management at the national level. Specifically, this involves improved water supply for 250,000 people and new connections for an additional 250,000. Other outcomes include establishing a national sector investment program, preventing water supply disruptions in Blantyre, planning new water sources for major cities and towns, improving operational and financial performance of water boards, and enhancing capacity for water resource management. Success would be achieved when: the number of people with improved water supply and sanitation significantly increases; the Government of Malawi demonstrates enhanced capacity to manage its water resources, evidenced by the establishment of the National Water Resources Authority and progress on the water resources investment strategy; urban water utilities show improved financial viability and service delivery; and institutional reforms for sector management and regulation are satisfactorily implemented, along with effective sanitation and hygiene promotion programs. Sustained commitment from MWD Directors and the effective implementation of a district-based, community-driven model are also crucial for overall success.

["The activity aims for four main outcomes: 1) Establish a financing and dissemination mechanism through installing 28 pilot pumps and 1,440 additional pumps, creating a National PV Fund, loan criteria, monitoring, and technical standards. 2) De-risk solar PV pumping via quality standards, testing, certification, training, and increasing water use efficiency. 3) Develop a UNFCCC standardized baseline for solar PV water pumping and implement it within a NAMA, including additionality criteria and MRV protocol. 4) Establish a supportive enabling environment and scale up the project by advocating for regulations, tax exemptions, and developing a structured replication program across Sudan's 17 states, embedding PV pumping into national strategies and securing funding.", "Overall success hinges on addressing critical economic/environmental needs by replacing diesel pumps, successful pilot demonstrations, establishing a robust financing mechanism (National PV Fund, customized lending), de-risking PV technology via standards/training, and securing strong government commitment (strategic plans, financial allocations, fiscal concessions). Key indicators include installing 1,468 pumps, achieving 15,659 tCO2/year direct emission reductions, an additional 1,252,694-2,160,005 tCO2 indirect reductions, farmers saving US$56 million over 15 years, and embedding PV irrigation into national strategies for long-term sustainability and replication across all 18 states."]

The targeted outcomes of the "Ethiopia/Nile Basin Initiative: Ethiopia-Sudan Interconnector" activity include fostering regional power trade through coordinated planning and development of power generation and transmission interconnections in the Eastern Nile region, leveraging the hydro-thermal complementarity between the Ethiopian and Sudanese systems to enhance power supply availability and reliability in both countries, and promoting Ethiopia's power export revenue generation capacity and improving the security of its power system by monetizing its hydropower export potential. This project aims to displace and reduce the use of diesel and other thermal units in Sudan for global environmental benefits, reduce overall generation costs, improve supply availability and reliability in Ethiopia, and integrate reserve capacity to lower total reserve margin requirements and associated capital and operating costs across the interconnected system.To consider the activity an overall success, the following would need to be achieved: a significant quantity of electricity must be traded (exports/imports) between Ethiopia and Sudan, and an increase in foreign exchange must be generated from power sales to Sudan. Specific project success indicators include the construction of the 230 kV transmission line from Bahir Dar to Metema (or Gonder to Metema, as mentioned in project components), completion of substation expansion and rehabilitation, and establishment of telecommunication systems. Success will also be measured by the volume of power exports from Ethiopia to Sudan and the revenues for EEPCo from these sales, timely erection of the transmission line, the number of substations upgraded, the full operation of telecommunication and telecontrol systems, satisfactory performance of the supervision consultant, and the compliant implementation of Environmental Management Plans (EMP) and Resettlement Action Plans (RAP).

Targeted outcomes include: developing a multi-year public expenditure program with sound fiscal policies for a long-term sustainable fiscal framework; improving high-priority paved road segments to reactivate the provincial economy and strengthen regional competitiveness; and enhancing water, sewerage, urban drainage access for the poor in vulnerable areas.Success requires: The fiscal framework is regularly updated, showing convergence towards fiscal balance. High-traffic productive road assets are preserved with acceptable average roughness (IRI). Waterborne diseases are reduced.

The targeted outcomes of the Herat Electrification Project are to provide access to electricity to households, institutions, and businesses in selected areas of Herat Province. Specifically, the project aims to provide new or improved electricity service to 230,800 people and 1,600 non-residential consumers (including businesses and community institutions). The activity would be considered an overall success if these targets are met, meaning 230,800 people and 1,600 non-residential consumers are successfully provided with new or improved electricity service.

The targeted outcomes of the Property Jurisdiction Consolidation activity include: efficiently, transparently, and sustainably consolidating the modernization of the Real Estate Jurisdiction (JI) to guarantee legal security of real estate property; completing and extending nationwide the reforms initiated by PMJT, aligning the JI with full LRI implementation, ensuring uniform processes; expanding IT system coverage in high-volume registries and land courts, and extending modernization to seven additional registries (20% of national operations); establishing the Registered Real Estate Guarantee Fund (FGMR) to strengthen system guarantees and JI sustainability; and controlling and reducing Annotated Certificates. Overall success would be marked by: an efficient and transparent JI significantly improving legal security for property rights; successful IT system expansion and modernization; reversing the growing trend of Annotated Certificates, with 75% of transactions using Title Certificates by 2018 and 90% by 2028; reducing properties with Annotated Certificates from an estimated 54% to 30% by 2028; effective control and suspension of new Annotated Certificates issuance; and the full extension of reforms nationwide, ensuring uniformity in processes and standards.

The targeted outcomes of the Vietnam Water Supply Development Project are to enhance the health and economic potential of households by improving financially and environmentally sustainable water and household sanitation services in district towns and large urban centers. Specifically, the project aims to construct water treatment plants to provide drinking water meeting the Clean Water Standard of the Ministry of Health, and to pipe this water to households. 

Under Component 1 ("Competition Route Provinces"), the project targets the construction of treatment plants and distribution systems for towns not currently served by the network. Phase 1 aims to reach an estimated 145,000 people in 15 towns across three provinces, while Phase 2 intends to serve an additional 850,000 people in up to 120 towns across 24 provinces. This component also includes the establishment of a micro-credit scheme to help households finance very small-scale on-site sanitation.

Under Component 2 ("Performance Route Provinces"), the objective is to improve performance levels, coverage, or increase production capacity in Water Supply Companies (WSCs). Phase 1 specifically targets the rehabilitation of the Kien An system in Haiphong WSC. Phase 2 extends this work to the WSCs of Ho Chi Minh City (HCMC), Ha Tinh, and Binh Thuan, focusing on rehabilitation and expansion of existing distribution systems and, later, expansion of production capacity, subject to achieving performance criteria. The related "Targeted Service Expansion through Water Loss Reduction Ho Chi Minh City" project (P097670) also aims to increase access of low-income households in HCMC to sustainable water supplies by reimbursing SAWACO for new, low-income household connections that have resulted in at least three months of water availability, and a corresponding 1m 3/day saving through audited real water loss reduction.

To be considered an overall success, the activity would need to achieve its primary objective of enhancing household health and economic potential through improved water and sanitation services that are both financially and environmentally sustainable. This implies meeting the targeted population coverage (145,000 people in Phase 1 and 850,000 in Phase 2 for Component 1) with drinking water conforming to the Ministry of Health's Clean Water Standard. Success would also entail the effective functioning and sustainability of the micro-credit scheme for sanitation, and the improvements in performance, coverage, or production capacity within the targeted WSCs. Furthermore, the willingness of consumers to connect to the systems at established tariffs, as confirmed in Phase 1 sub-projects, would need to be sustained, and local authorities would need to maintain their readiness to adopt these systems. Financial viability, as indicated by robust Financial Rates of Return (FRRs) and Economic Internal Rates of Return (EIRRs), even under various sensitivity scenarios, is crucial. Finally, effective mitigation of environmental and social impacts, including successful land acquisition management and the implementation of environmental and resettlement plans, along with the borrower's improved capacity in environmental management, would be essential for overall success.

The Evidence Fund aims to increase non-ODA research for broader FCDO evidence needs and HMG's diplomatic decision-making. It seeks to enable project-funded roles to deliver policy engagement, co-design, commission, and monitor research for uptake, and to contribute to UK's G2G science partnerships. The scope for science research partnerships will expand to commission research directly addressing country-specific health, climate, and development challenges, aligning with International Development Strategy. Expected outcomes include better foreign policy and more evidence-based development/diplomatic policies. The activity also aims to strengthen evidence-based decision-making and support UK's international development investments and diplomacy.

The activity targets stabilizing the deforestation frontier in Colombia's most threatened areas by improving land systems and usage rights, building capacity to tackle environmental crime, and promoting sustainable forest livelihoods. Expected results include delivering an updated multipurpose cadastre with land rights data across approximately 16 million hectares to enhance land security, increasing the capacity of at least 180 institutional staff for investigating and prosecuting environmental crimes, and incentivizing sustainable land use through alternative livelihoods. Pillar 3 specifically aims to increase community skills in sustainable practices to protect forests, promote commercialization and market access for sustainable commodities via public-private partnerships, and promote conservation incentives in restricted areas.

["The activity targets an improved environment, sustainable natural resource use, reduced climate impact, and strengthened resilience to environmental impacts, climate change, and natural disasters in Sub-Saharan Africa. Key outcomes involve enhancing knowledge of forest ecosystems and their dynamics, developing sub-regional forest and land-related laws, strategies, and policies, and demonstrating/disseminating sustainable forest and land use practices. It aims to strengthen regional actors' capacity for sustainable management of shared ecosystem services and natural resources, and to boost their resilience to climate change and disasters, including securing food supply. Long-term goals also include harmonizing ECOWAS legal instruments for cross-border natural resource management and improving access to reliable forest and land data for informed decision-making.", "Overall success depends on several critical factors. The activity would be considered successful with improved access to data on forests and land use status, effective demonstration of sustainable forest management practices, and widespread dissemination of lessons learned. Success also requires the harmonization of legal instruments (policies, strategies, and plans) at the ECOWAS level for the sustainable management of cross-border natural resources. Achieving progress in a positive transformational change across the ECOWAS region, driven by the mentioned factors, is crucial. Full participation of ECOWAS in the management and implementation of activities is essential to achieve the intervention's objectives and ensure sustainability of results. Effectively collecting and making accessible reliable forest and land data for decision-making at all levels, serving as a baseline for monitoring, is also key."]

The activity targets bolstering Mozambique's and World Bank's climate change efforts by aiding DPO design for climate resilience and low-carbon development, supporting PPCR initiation and implementation, sustaining government forest management and REDD+ initiatives, and exploring low-carbon development pathways. It also aims to increase DFID/World Bank assistance, develop practical tools for climate impacts, foster robust policy frameworks, empower the World Bank for effective PPCR, and prepare readiness plans for the forest sector.

The activity targets the enhanced adoption of circular economy practices by Local Government Units (LGUs) in collaboration with the private sector and civil society, with a specific focus on youth and gender equality. Key outcomes include improved capacities of LGUs and stakeholders to design, implement, and monitor circular economy strategies; development and application of GEDSI responsive local policies to enable green growth; effective mobilization of public and private financing for gender-responsive and inclusive circular economy strategies; enhanced community involvement in innovation and demonstration projects; improved circular economy behavior among LGUs, private sector, communities, and local stakeholders; capacitation and integration of the informal sector, grassroots, civil society organizations, women, youth, and marginalized groups into the circular economy value chain; and the establishment of circular economy-related knowledge management, MERL, GEDSI, and safeguards to strengthen and replicate uptake across the Philippines. Additionally, it aims to reduce resource consumption, greenhouse gas emissions, and biodiversity loss, enhancing urban sustainability and resilience, while ensuring meaningful participation and equitable benefits for all stakeholders, especially youth, women, and marginalized groups.

The activity targeted financing 5-7 biomass plants with a collective production capacity of approximately 49 MW and an annual electricity output of roughly 272,000 MWh, aiming for 217,000 tons of CO2 savings annually one year post-completion. Success was defined by these plants operating as successful commercial enterprises, where revenues per sold unit of energy surpassed total production costs. Additionally, outcomes included enhancing IREDA's credit processes and establishing a management system adhering to international environmental and social standards.

Targeted outcomes: government adopts/implements new forest laws; companies/farmers apply best practices (CSA, agroforestry, long rotation plantations) for net positive ecosystem/livelihood impacts; degraded forests restored/managed; government reforms NR policies (Forest Law, Land Law, Planning Law, ICCA, PES, FLA, FLEGT-VPA) for inclusivity/sustainability; integrated spatial development plan adopted/implemented; Region IV Forest Development/Restoration Plan implemented; Viet Nam Mekong Council adopts landscape approach. Success requires full achievement: active stakeholder participation, demonstrable deforestation/degradation reduction, measurable positive impacts, and effective landscape governance (e.g., via World Bank project if Srepok Board stalled).

The activity aimed to improve climate change adaptation in the Western Balkans, specifically regarding flood and drought risks. Success was defined by achieving the following targeted outcomes: flood warnings provided to and used by relevant ministries and institutions in the Drin River Basin; at least three countries adopting a national climate change adaptation strategy; 40 municipalities implementing 80% of flood and drought protection measures as per local plans; two concepts and one structure for comprehensive regional water resource management in the Western Balkans developed; and recommendations from climate change adaptation analyses integrated into urban planning and development in three major cities.

The activity aims to enable informed climate action by 2027, reducing national emissions to 50 million tCO2e (from 15 million in 2022) and increasing renewable energy installations to 1,500 (from 200 in 2022). It targets enhancing energy access & livelihoods via DRE systems, improving working conditions & net-incomes, deploying 100 EVs for waste collection, and incentivizing 125 steel units to avoid 92,500 tons CO2 annually. Other outcomes include developing a Just Transition Framework in Jharkhand, skilling women/youth for a low-emission economy, enhancing urban/rural climate resilience, and strengthening climate information & early warning systems.

The activity targeted the reduction of climate-damaging emissions from Indonesia's forestry sector and an improvement in the living conditions of the poor rural population. Success required enabling public and private actors to implement improved policy and institutional frameworks, methods, and services for sustainable forest management, biodiversity conservation, and emission reduction, while enhancing rural livelihoods. Specific success targets included: establishing institutional frameworks at all administrative levels for FMU-led forest management; three pilot districts actively participating in IPCC-compliant REDD+ activities; 150,000 hectares of forest from at least three Kalimantan enterprises managed to national (PHPL) and FSC standards; pilot activity results based on Green Economy principles integrated into two district development plans; and new training instruments (3 modules, 2 competence standards, 2 curricula, including conflict mapping) incorporated into forestry education.

Targeted outcomes included: sustainable and equitable improvement of rural living conditions, natural resource rehabilitation; stabilizing/increasing agricultural/forestry production; 70% functional water/erosion structures; 60% tree survival; 80% controlled grazing in WCA land; min 25% increase in key crop yields (from 2000 baselines); min 10% increase in utilized agricultural land; prevention of large gullies; stable/increased incomes for households; year-round water supply; reduced migration.
