Success required achieving at least a 'satisfactory' rating (Level 3) for effectiveness, developmental impact, and sustainability, meaning positive results dominate despite possible shortfalls.

The targeted outcomes include Lebanon's compliance with national HCFC consumption targets, full phase-out of HCFCs, and a freeze and 80% phase-down of HFCs by 2045. This also involves preparing a comprehensive HPMP (stage III) and a Kigali HFC Implementation Plan (KIP), promoting ozone-friendly technologies, and raising stakeholder awareness. Success hinges on adopting/implementing legislation to control substances, accurate data reporting, efficient national coordination, diligent supervision of phase-out/down activities, effective awareness, regional cooperation, and integrating gender policy, all leading to full compliance with agreed targets.

Targeted outcomes included contributing to socially and environmentally sustainable economic growth via expanded efficient economic infrastructure, enhancing long-term financing for infrastructure, and supporting efficient, sustainable energy provision. Specifically, the activity aimed to provide sub-loans for two run-of-river and four solar power plants, targeting a total installed capacity of 240.25 MW with a 20% financing share per project. To achieve success, the executing agency's average infrastructure loan term needed to be at least 10 years and its annual loan commitments and disbursements for energy and environmental projects needed to increase by at least 40%.

The targeted outcomes include improved compliance with Budget and Procurement Laws for local capital investments (Module 1). For Module 2, improved energy efficiency in public buildings in Khovd and Zavkhan aimags was to be demonstrated, with 8 investment measures (2 in 2014, 3 in 2015, 3 in 2016) and 50% of the local population recognizing benefits. An estimated 7,500 children and 100 teachers/employees were to directly benefit. Module 3 aimed for intensified efforts and investments in energy efficiency by public institutions, companies, and households, with targets including 10 companies reporting 5% energy savings, improved ERC tariff calculation methods, and an increased share of the Ministry of Energy's budget for efficiency interventions.

The Global Innovation Fund aims to identify, rigorously test, implement, and scale innovations delivering greater development impacts with fewer resources, creating a rigorously tested portfolio of cost-effective, transformational solutions at scale. It seeks to promote a global focus on innovation, evidence, cost-effectiveness, and lesson learning, stimulate co-creation and co-investment, and provide a pipeline of investor-ready, commercially viable innovations. Success requires achieving scale with proven results, delivering significant development impact, and driving more effective use of public and private investment by unlocking commercial capital.

The FCFA activity targets reducing climate change disruption and damage, safeguarding economic development, supporting poverty eradication, and enhancing climate resilience in African infrastructure, urban, and rural plans. The extension aims for: 1) Targeted communication of FCFA research, tools, and learning exchanges, reaching many people. 2) Enhanced scientific knowledge of African climate through a 'Model Evaluation Hub,' automated tools, and strengthened international collaboration. 3) Increased integration of International Climate Fund (ICF) funding and improved capacity in DFID Africa Country Offices, integrating climate risks into project designs. Success relies on achieving these outcomes, building on the CCKE's strong track record, high programme scores, strategic importance, and collaborative approach, ultimately making African societies more resilient to climate change, benefiting all, including vulnerable groups.

["Targeted outcomes: promote economic development & climate action investments; improve public investment management, planning climate-smart infrastructure; facilitate KP investment; boost renewable energy access; enhance international climate finance access; build credible post-disaster M&E system; create demand for open data; foster higher public/private investment with better returns; improve provincial economic development plans, debt sustainability, & energy sector viability via TA (reduce losses, improve governance); align with IMF-supported program priorities: stronger public finances, energy sector viability, private-led activity.", "Overall success hinges on: sustained economic stability, growth, & private sector participation; achieving higher Value for Money; ME3F activities yielding returns via data/capacity for better development; strengthened public investment management, transparency, resilience, social contract; effective feedback/data-driven decisions boosting public spending returns; improved intervention/reconstruction quality via audits; enhanced public spending equity/transparency; deep government/private sector engagement for ownership; guiding government through economic crises to sustainable growth; locally designed/delivered development; and avoiding SNG-II/REMIT duplication."]

The targeted outcomes of the activity, known at the beginning, were to sustainably reduce the risk to the nationwide electricity supply from renewable energies by enhancing the operational safety of the plants, thereby contributing to Tajikistan's economic and social development and fostering climate protection. Success was defined by achieving a fault-free discharge of 2,100 GWh per year via the 220 kV switchgear and maintaining its availability above 98%. Additionally, the activity aimed to avoid at least 1.4 million tons of CO2 emissions annually.

Targeted outcomes focused on: biodiversity protection, sustainable tropical forest management, and improving the economic situation of the poor local population. Phase I specifically targeted sustainable management of Kahuzi-Biega National Park and Okapi Wildlife Reserve. Success required strengthening ICCN's capacities, active local population involvement, improving protected area tourism to cover costs and sensitize locals, and supporting forest concession certification to protect forests and reduce overexploitation. A 50% improvement in socio-economic indicators for the local population was a key target.

["Targeted outcomes: Achieve South Africa's Integrated Resource Plan (IRP) target of 3,320 MW wind power generation by 2018/19. This includes reducing CO2 emissions, fostering a green economy, increasing socio-economic development, and operationalizing climate change strategies. Specific targets involve implementing a localisation Monitoring & Verification (M&V) system, installing 4 WASA II masts by 2017, defining preliminary/final Renewable Energy Development Zones (REDZs) by end-2017/2019, developing a 1.8 MW small-scale wind farm demonstration project, and involving a maximum of 5 TVET colleges in vocational training.", "Overall success requires: overcoming strategic barriers to the 3,320 MW target, clearly defining local content targets with adequate manufacturing capacity, establishing a suitable framework for testing and certifying locally manufactured wind energy components (initially small-scale), accurately estimating maximum wind energy resources, and enhancing prospects for a viable small-scale wind energy sector. It also depends on effective policy de-risking, addressing critical barriers (skills, permitting), integration with national strategies, and providing evidence-based data with regular evaluations."]

Targeted outcomes: Catalyse increased private sector investment in low-carbon climate resilient (LCCR) projects in developing countries, supporting sustainable development and tackling climate change. Also, demonstrate commercial viability, cultivate investible projects/innovative funds (SCAF), and promote knowledge sharing. Success requires: significant private investment leveraging, tangible environmental and socioeconomic benefits, achieved commercial viability, SCAF objectives met, effective knowledge sharing, and maintenance of the program's Theory of Change, Value for Money, and impact.

["The targeted outcome of the activity was the introduction of sustainable payment mechanisms for reducing CO2 emissions from deforestation and forest degradation in the Member States of the Central American Integration System (SICA). For success, these mechanisms would need to be effectively developed, adopted, and implemented across the SICA member states, proving capable of reducing CO2 emissions from deforestation and forest degradation. Additionally, strengthening the human resource capacity of the Central American Commission for the Environment and Development (CCAD) was necessary for it to effectively pursue its mandate and support the sustainability of these mechanisms.", "The targeted outcome of the activity was the introduction of sustainable payment mechanisms for reducing CO2 emissions from deforestation and forest degradation in the Member States of the Central American Integration System (SICA). For success, these mechanisms would need to be effectively developed, adopted, and implemented across the SICA member states, proving capable of reducing CO2 emissions from deforestation and forest degradation. Additionally, strengthening the human resource capacity of the Central American Commission for the Environment and Development (CCAD) was necessary for it to effectively pursue its mandate and support the sustainability of these mechanisms."]

Targeted outcomes of the NAM NGIEP 1 HYDROPOWER PROJECT include increased regional cooperation between the Lao PDR and Thailand through the trade of clean energy, aiming to raise power exports from 2,105 MW in 2014 to 7,000 MW by 2024. The project also targets an increased household electrification rate in the Lao PDR from 82% in 2012 to 92% by 2024 by providing 18 MW for domestic supply. It seeks to promote environmentally and socially sustainable private sector investment in hydropower, specifically by ensuring at least three new hydropower projects constructed in the Lao PDR during 2014–2024 fully meet government environmental and social protection requirements. The project aims for the increased private sector provision of reliable and clean power, delivering about 1,400 GWh of electricity annually to Thailand and 100 GWh annually to the Lao PDR starting in 2020. A significant outcome is the avoidance of approximately 500,000 tons of carbon dioxide emissions annually in Thailand from 2020. Financially, it targets about $600 million in royalty, tax, and dividend revenues for the Government of the Lao PDR over the 27-year concession period and the employment of approximately 80 Lao workers during operations.To consider the activity an overall success, several conditions must be met. The 290 MW hydropower plant must be commissioned by July 2018, with its main components (272 MW main power station, 18 MW reregulation power station, and a 125 km transmission line) fully operational and delivering the specified electricity amounts to Thailand and Lao PDR. Crucially, all appropriate environmental and social mitigation measures must be implemented according to international best practices, including lessons from previous projects. This involves bringing the standards of living of 100% of poor and vulnerable households affected by involuntary resettlement to at least national minimum standards within 10 years post-start-up, issuing 100% of replacement land titles in joint spousal names, and reserving 40% of postsecondary scholarships for ethnic Hmong girls. Effective stakeholder consultation, grievance mechanisms, and livelihood restoration programs are essential. Furthermore, the project's financial targets, including generating $600 million in government revenues, must be achieved, and the anticipated 500,000 tons per annum CO2 reduction must be realized. The plant must operate according to specifications, the Nabong substation must be completed on time, and EGAT must honor its power purchase agreement obligations. Ultimately, success hinges on demonstrating that socially responsible investment is compatible with financially sound business and contributing to inclusive economic growth, environmental sustainability, and regional integration.

The activity aimed to achieve specific environmental policy goals by supporting institutional development and mainstreaming ecological sustainability. Key targeted outcomes for 2013 were: Min. 10 EIAs reviewed; Protected Area revenue increase by min. 2M USD/year; Emergency air pollution plans in 5 cities; Lima air data published real-time by 2 authorities; Min. 10 mining brownfield projects financed; Min. 60 mining sites with participatory monitoring; Low-sulphur diesel in min. 30% of fuel stations; 80,000 vehicles converted to natural gas with 90 NG stations; 600,000 vehicles tested in Lima & 80,000 in 3 other cities via TÜV; 100% fishing fleet under quota system; 5,000 (later 3,000) fishermen using incentive system. For success, effectiveness, higher-level impacts, and sustainability needed at least a 'satisfactory' rating.

The targeted outcomes of the activity, known at the beginning, were to ensure the efficient and long-term management of the Lobéké, Dzanga-Sangha, and Nouabalé-Ndoki protected areas, including cross-border measures (outcome-level), and to contribute to the protection of biodiversity and forest resources within the Tri-National de la Sangha forest complex (impact-level). The mechanism aimed for investment income from an endowment fund to finance a portion of protected area operating costs. The target group was local populations. A total capital of EUR 100 million for the FTNS endowment fund was targeted. To achieve success, it was deemed necessary to: ensure sustainable, long-term financing for operating costs; effectively manage human rights, prevent abuses, and ensure robust conflict resolution via functioning complaints mechanisms; maintain transparency and accountability through annual independent audits and standardized, high-quality financial reporting; and establish a clear ex-ante estimation of operating costs for CTFs.

Targeted outcomes include: ensuring sustainable and inclusive management of forested landscapes; securing community land rights and tenure; reducing deforestation from agrocommodities (especially palm oil); improving forest/land governance (e.g., community forest management, new laws); advancing gender equity in resource management; and facilitating a just energy transition. Overall success requires: CSO coalitions achieving greater impact; increased women's land rights; the EU stopping palm oil in biofuels; Liberia passing the Land Rights Act; communities effectively influencing sustainable resource plans and the private sector; and adaptive strategies informed by mid-term reviews and outcome harvesting.

The activity targeted: 1) electricity users and environment benefiting from just energy supply/use. 2) Selected public, private, civil society actors increasing contribution to promoting renewable energy (RE) and energy efficiency (EE). 3) Strengthened institutional capacities of SREDA. 4) Coalition actors able to sensitise society for sustainable energy use. 5) Improved capacities for national SRTS dissemination, leading to more SRTS installations. Success requires these actors to be capable of sensitising society, strengthening SRTS dissemination, sustaining campaigns (reducing primary energy consumption/GDP), and continuing NSHD operation (increasing RE share).

The activity targets shifting The Gambia's electricity generation from heavy fuel oil to solar power, establishing two 10.5 MW grid-connected solar PV facilities in Basse and Farafenni. It aims to increase renewable energy's share in total electricity generation from 2% to 40% and boost installed capacity from 102 MW to 250 MW, aligning with the National Development Plan. It also targets an annual emission reduction of 14,107 tons CO2eq (12,896 tons considering degradation) and a total of 330,873 tons (or 322,000 tons) over 25 years, contributing to SDG 13. Further outcomes include providing sustainable renewable energy access to approximately 144,500 people, enhancing quality of life, and building the capacity of at least 10 NAWEC staff to manage future PV projects and tenders, positioning NAWEC as a competent IPP partner. The project also intends to demonstrate a practical, successful financial mechanism for PV installation and operation, encouraging replication of similar Public-Private Partnerships and catalyzing up to 30 MW of additional PV capacity post-project. Deploying state-of-the-art solar PV technology supports SDG 9, and finalizing a robust PPA guarantee scheme is a key deliverable.

The Montreal Protocol-IS XII targets enhancing Georgia's capacity to implement its obligations. Outcomes include: adopting/implementing ODS legislation; efficient data collection/reporting; supervising HCFC phase-out; strengthening consultations; raising public awareness; and enhancing regional cooperation. Overall success requires: improved legal-regulatory basis, enforcement, illegal ODS trade monitoring, reporting, managerial, coordination, cooperation, and public awareness on ODS matters. This entails updated regulations, training for inspectors and customs, improved ERMS, timely report submission, NOU/NOO infrastructure upgrade, effective stakeholder consultations/Project Board, extensive private sector/inspector trainings, and robust awareness campaigns including gender-oriented materials.

The activity targeted improving conditions for integrating renewable energy and developing energy efficiency in Bolivia by enhancing legal, economic, technical, and institutional frameworks. Key outputs included: elaborating legal framework decision bases; empowering ENDE/CNDC for renewable energy implementation and system management; establishing institutional/technical bases for energy efficiency promotion; and recommending professional education improvements. Success hinged on a persisting political will for renewable energy expansion and energy efficiency plans, resource availability, continuous political investment commitment, and political stability.

Targeted outcomes at inception: reduced land conflicts, transparent local revenue, inclusive forest governance, decreased CO2, increased employment/sustainable communal forest exploitation, improved indigenous participation, local environmental protection capacity. Success required: sustainable RF management/transparent municipal revenues accelerating social development/environmental adoption; value chain actors gaining economic/socially via volume/value/sustainability; target groups protecting/using resources sustainably via enhanced biodiversity/ecosystem awareness.

The activity targets transforming Cambodia's subsistence rice subsector into a commercially oriented one, improving household/national food security and expanding rice export. Outcomes include removing legal/regulatory constraints, improving paddy productivity and milled rice quality, enhancing value chain support services, and addressing climate change risks. Specific targets: paddy surplus exceeding 4 million tons by 2015; paddy drying capacity by 2,000 tons/day and storage by 40,000 tons in target provinces by 2018; 25% crop yield increase by 2019; paddy production from 8.0M tons (2012) to 9.5M tons by 2018; and milled rice exports from 200k tons (2011) to 1.2M tons by 2017. Overall success requires achieving the targeted transformation, improving food security and exports, with annual rice export revenue reaching $600 million ($120M national gain) by 2017. Other criteria: minimizing domestic price fluctuations, reducing budget deficit, replenishing foreign exchange reserves, enhancing external debt servicing, promoting inclusive economic growth for 1 million farming households (avg. $300 additional annual income by 2020), ensuring good governance/corruption reduction, and achieving economic internal rates of return for subprojects ranging from 29%-31.2%, alongside effective gender mainstreaming including women farmers' access to decision-making and resources.

The activity aimed to institutionalize integrative, sustainable, climate-sensitive, and economically viable land-use approaches in government policy across Central Asia. Targeted outcomes included contributing to SDG 15 (ecosystem protection, combating degradation/loss), increasing green sector budgets, integrating natural resource economic value into planning, harmonizing land-use laws, and benefiting land-users. Success required: key actors in two countries implementing 5 nationwide sectoral strategies; lessons learned incorporated into 5 regional processes; managers implementing 14 strategic change processes towards integrative land use and climate protection.

Targeted outcomes: improved integration of transformative approaches for emission-neutral/climate-safe dev in int'l cooperation per Paris Agreement. Outputs: BMZ climate policy advisory/support, improved climate mainstreaming for NDC impl, and informing decision-makers on Paris Ag. impl. Indirectly: better living conditions for climate-affected poor. Success required achieving these, contributing to 2030 Agenda & cross-cutting issues: gender/environment, and meeting evaluation criteria: relevance, coherence, efficiency, effectiveness, impact, sustainability.

["The activity targeted implementing measures to sustain water supply and sanitation services in selected refugee settlements and host communities in northern Uganda, aligning with national and international strategies. This aimed to strengthen MWE's WASH coordination role, enhance NUWS capacities for operating rehabilitated schemes, improve local district government performance in WASH, utilize local actors for O&M, and facilitate local actors in hygiene, sanitation, and groundwater protection. The project also aimed to contribute to SDGs 3, 5, and 6, and ultimately improve access to drinking water for refugees and host communities in West Nile.", "Success was defined by specific targets: 100% of 20 planned water supply systems (serving 150,000 people, including 75,000 refugees) and 1 faecal sludge management system operated by NUWS under an agreed model; 100% of relevant authorities and DPs having online access to WSS data in Arua and Yumbe; 100% reduction of handpump repair time from six to three months in Arua and Yumbe; and 100% of an additional 5,000 vulnerable households (min 30% host communities) gaining access to individual toilets with hand wash facilities."]

The Baikonyr Solar Power Project targets contributing to Kazakhstan's sustainable electricity supply by increasing renewable energy and meeting demand. Its outcomes include generating additional solar power, avoiding 40,800 tons of CO2 equivalent emissions annually to help meet Paris Agreement targets (15% below 1990 levels by 2030), and aligning with Strategy Kazakhstan 2050 for increased renewable supply. It also aims to generate local employment and stimulate the economy through the installation and operation of a 50 MW peak solar power park.

The core targeted outcome was to establish the necessary prerequisites for implementing climate-friendly sanitation services and faecal sludge management in peri-urban Lusaka, expected to reduce greenhouse gas emissions, increase access to climate-friendly sanitation, improve population health, and enhance national GHG reporting. To achieve success, these prerequisites must be established, alongside four key output-level outcomes: adapted OSS/FSM procedures, coordination measures, improved monitoring compliance, and enhanced qualifications for public/private sector actors.

Targeted outcomes: PC increasingly uses climate risk info in planning/appraisal for resilient public investments, incorporating geo-info/climate risks into national/local planning, enhancing urban resilience, reducing vulnerability. Specific outcomes: 8th FYP/BDP proposals climate-resilient, enhanced PC cooperation, line ministry integration in appraisals, systematic climate risk info use, 184 officials trained, NAPD e-platform, six model Project Proformas. Success targets: 1. 8th FYP adopted with climate risk info in 2/14 chapters. 2. 6/80 BDP2100 proposals with climate risk info. 3. 40% ~400 DPPs/TAPPs appraisals apply climate risk assessment. 4. 172/215 trainees apply resilience knowledge.

Targeted outcomes for Sustainable Watershed Management in the Lower Mekong Basin, known at activity's start, aimed to strengthen institutional capacities by qualifying decision-makers/mid-level professionals, and improve national/regional planning/coordination of sustainable resource management. Success was defined by: 1) sustainable watershed management concepts integrated into planning in at least two riparian states; 2) key IWSM elements replicated in at least three watersheds in two countries; and 3) 8 national plus 2 regional coordination forums established, contributing to improved planning/coordination.

The activity targeted improving the general enabling conditions for scaling up sustainable sanitation in German development cooperation partner countries and in German/international development cooperation. Success was defined by achieving several key outcomes, including: innovative approaches for sustainable sanitation being considered in 6 German DC projects; 10 implementing partners in 8 countries testing innovative approaches for integrating sustainable sanitation into urban planning; and knowledge about sustainable sanitation being prepared and fed into 5 international and national political processes.

The targeted outcomes of the activity, as known at the beginning, included: contributing to MDG 6 (combat waterborne diseases) and MDG 7 (access to safe water); improving access for southern Angola's population to safe, readily available, and affordable drinking water; managing scarce water resources sustainably; and establishing a capable water utility in Kunene. Success was defined by providing clean water access to 110,000 beneficiaries with >20 liters per capita/day, service hours >22 hours, water quality meeting WHO standards, and >75% of the population fetching water in <1 hour. The water abstraction/conveyance system aimed for 7.2 m/s capacity, and the distribution system for 2,000 m/d.

Targeted outcomes included establishing/supporting Promovilidad (manuals, capacity), enhancing municipal SUM planning/implementation capacities (including PMUS), familiarizing planners with innovative SUM technologies/financing, improving national/subnational coordination for SUM investment, and launching selected SUM measures. Social goals included combating harassment, integrating functional diversity measures, and prioritizing non-motorized transport. Success, defined at the outset, required reducing daily transport time by 30%, traffic-related deaths to 5/100,000, and GHG emissions by 20%, alongside achieving safe, reliable, inclusive, high-standard transport systems, municipal application of SUM capacities, and planner awareness of innovative SUM technologies.

The targeted outcomes of the activity were for climate-relevant ministries and subnational authorities of the Indonesian government to coordinate key policy instruments to achieve Indonesia's climate goals. This involved strengthening capacities for implementing climate-change mitigation action plans, harmonising Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) into national planning, developing climate finance instruments, and integrating these into low-carbon development planning. Success was defined by achieving this coordination, alongside the inclusion of gender mainstreaming strategies in three climate action plans and gender equality principles in three climate-relevant budget plans.

Targeted outcomes included: 120 LCD and 24-hour daily supply of continuous, needs-based, hygienically safe drinking water for Batumi, Chakvi, and southern villages. For wastewater treatment in Batumi, targets (one year post-commissioning) were COD 125 mg/l, BOD5 25 ml/l, and TSS 35 mg/l. Operating cost recovery was targeted upon commissioning, with full cost recovery two years later. Success required ensuring hygienic drinking water (even if no explicit target was set), adequate funding for maintenance, proper infrastructure operation (including sludge removal), financially feasible tariffs, alignment with urban development, institutional strengthening, and appropriate O&M planning by the operator, while addressing cost recovery ambiguities.

The targeted outcomes of the Windpark Mesihovina activity, as known at the beginning, included contributing to an efficient, reliable, and environmentally friendly electricity supply through renewable energy diversification, environmental and climate protection, reduced electricity import dependence, and achieving Nationally Determined Contributions. Specifically, constructing a 44 MW wind farm to generate 115 GWh of electricity annually, achieving a plant availability of >97%, and wind power constituting 5% of EP HZHB's total generation capacity. Success would be defined by achieving these targets, offering competitive Levelized Cost of Electricity, contributing to the executing agency's cost coverage, and generating local tax/lease income.

Targeted outcomes: Industrial forest use in the Congo Basin following sustainable management principles, with focus on biodiversity preservation. Specific targets: 10 million hectares of forest area certified by end of 2014 (FSC or comparable system); 90% of companies able to secure their certificates long-term. Initial plans also included reduced-impact logging training, ecological research, wildlife management initiatives (anti-poaching, manager training, monitoring), and digital tool development. Success required achieving these certification targets and the overarching goal of sustainable industrial forest use considering biodiversity.

["The activity's overarching targeted outcome was to improve conditions for renewable energy (RE) and energy efficiency (EnEff) solutions for public institutions and private investors. For Output 1, success required establishing a decision-making basis for the Ministry of Energy (MoE) to operationalize the amended RE Act, specifically by drafting and adopting implementing provisions for at least two instruments. For Output 2, success meant providing the Energy Commission (EC) with a decision-making basis for improved EnEff regulations for buildings, achieved by developing and testing a method for calculating and certifying buildings' specific energy consumption based on existing building examples.", "For Output 3, success involved defining the objectives and content of the \"Government Goes Solar\" initiative, promoting RE and EnEff in public institutions, by developing and adopting a Public Facility Sustainable Energy Action Plan (PF-SEAP) and a list of priority investments via an interministerial working group. Output 4 aimed to formulate concrete measures to improve EnEff and RE use in public facilities, with success defined as demonstrating a 30% reduction in electricity bills in retrofitted buildings. Output 5 sought to enhance the skills of specialists in RE and EnEff, with success requiring technicians and specialists to receive training."]

The activity aims to enhance water resource management for water security and climate adaptation in rural areas at national, state, and local levels. Targeted outcomes include: public entities improving planning/financing using the CWRM framework; local actors adopting CWRM for decision-making and pilot measures; private sector (FPOs) involvement in implementing pilot measures through promoted business models; and NABARD/BIRD increasing farmer capacities in water security. Success requires local actors' interest/capacity to integrate water strategies, support actors contributing to implementation, private sector recognizing business models for water security, and favorable enabling conditions.

["The main targeted outcome is the integration of evidence-based concepts for resource-efficient, climate-friendly, and scalable solid waste management and circular economy (SWM/CE) practices into national and international political and policy processes, initiatives, and development cooperation programs. Specific outcome targets by December 31, 2022, include: BMZ using project inputs in 30 processes/events; 5 DC projects/institutions using project-co-developed SWM/CE working aids; 25 pilot measures on marine litter/waste prevention implemented by partners; actors in 3 partner countries/regional initiatives incorporating SWM/CE learning into activities; and 8 demonstration projects of an alliance promoting CE implemented based on 2 common guidelines/papers.", "Achieving success depends on several factors known at the outset. Public and private actors, along with international organizations, must understand the necessity of SWM/CE concepts, gain access, and possess the interest, buy-in, willingness, and capacity to adopt these sustainable practices, potentially compelled by international commitments. BMZ must have the knowledge and capacity to act effectively and access GIZ experts. Partners need interest, incentive, tenacity to develop SWM/CE concepts. Pilot/innovation projects must be viable and universally applicable, and various actors must have the capacity to participate and innovate. Political buy-in from BMZ and partners, consensus, and partners' flexibility to adopt new concepts are critical."]

The activity aims to reduce emissions from land use, deforestation, and forest degradation, achieve sustainable forest management, conserve/enhance forest carbon stocks, and improve livelihoods. Targeted outcomes include strengthening institutional/regulatory systems for REDD+, developing market solutions for sustainable agriculture to reduce slash-and-burn, and implementing forestry activities for climate mitigation. Success would entail 5.6 million tCO2eq of avoided emissions, 120,000 direct rural beneficiaries, 21,600 households switching to sustainable farming practices, and 200 villages meeting results-based payment criteria.

The targeted outcomes of the Amazonien Fonds für Wald- und Klimaschutz activity, known at the beginning, were to significantly improve the implementation of national and international commitments to deforestation reduction and sustainable development by reducing greenhouse gas emissions, conserving and increasing forest carbon stocks, and promoting sustainable forest management. Success would be achieved by: Disbursing a total value of resources of BRL 1.86 billion (USD 646 million); Approving projects with an aggregated value of BRL 2.8 billion (USD 915 million); Increasing the accumulated volume of project funds disbursed and approved; Increasing the share of project applications meeting technical approval criteria; Introducing aggregated impact indicators in the M&E system; Improving project proposal quality, implementing organization capacities, results monitoring, and knowledge management; Achieving enhanced disbursements in ongoing projects, leading to positive changes in areas like regulation, sustainable production, and forest protection.

["The targeted outcomes of the activity were to contribute to improved living conditions for the urban populations of Imishli (54,000 inhabitants, with 34% refugees and internally displaced persons) and Agzabedi (40,000 inhabitants) by focusing on their basic needs and fostering social stabilization in these project sites. Specifically, the project intended to eliminate serious constraints in residential water supply for these small and medium-sized cities.", "To achieve success as defined at the beginning of the activity, the targeted outcomes would include the successful implementation of construction and rehabilitation measures for drinking water infrastructure in Agzabedi and Imishli to eliminate serious residential water supply constraints. It would also necessitate that the chosen private operator model under a concession prove suitable and sustainable despite known challenges related to prevailing unfavorable economic and legal conditions, the small, refugee-dominated target group, and the sufficiency of the supply area's minimum size for such an approach."]

The targeted outcomes of the activity, known at the beginning, were to protect water resources and reduce health risks for the population by ensuring sustainable, ecological, and safe central collection and treatment of sewage in Korça. To achieve success as defined at the beginning, the following targeted outcomes were necessary: at least 85% population connection to and use of the central wastewater system; discharge values from the pond treatment plant below 25 mg/l BSB5 in a 24-hour mixed sample; no organic contaminants in the raw water of the Turani well field; and elimination of regular wastewater discharge into the Turani ditch after project completion.

["The overall objective is to expand access to modern energy services in Tanzania for sustainable socio-economic development and poverty reduction in an environmentally sound manner. Specific goals include guaranteeing electricity supply from Hale HPP for an additional 30-40 years, enhancing national transmission network reliability and performance, improving plant operational safety, improving generation reliability, extending Hale HPP's remaining lifetime, and avoiding power generation constraints in Tanzania.", "Overall success requires the intervention's relevance (alignment with Swedish/Sida strategies), effectiveness (clear objectives, robust monitoring, reasonable budget), strong organizational capacity (efficient output, internal control, results management), long-term sustainability of objectives, alignment with partner systems, harmonized financing, adherence to detailed project documentation (rehabilitation, environmental plans), effective risk management (internal control, procurement delays, climate impacts), and confirmed feasibility and cost-effectiveness."]
NO RESPONSE

Targeted outcomes: Enhance ecological water conservation for Miyun Reservoir's water quality/quantity, integrate close-to-nature forest management (CNF) across 10,000 hectares into China's desertification program, and ecologically restore small watercourses. Outcomes also included increasing infiltration, water availability, biodiversity, curbing sedimentation, promoting sustainable funding for environmental services, improving inter-authority cooperation, training, and local socio-economic situations. Success criteria: 7.95% additional water yield; no reduction in forested land; water quality at least Chinese standard II or III for ">=300" days/year; 10,000 ha managed under CNF plans; national funding for second thinning; ">=75%" of managed areas rated Excellent/Good.

The activity's overall goal was to improve the Mekong River Commission's (MRC) capacity to provide competent advice for climate change adaptation. For the first phase (ending December 2015), the objective was to improve MRCS services as a knowledge and information hub. Targeted outcomes for success included: 1) Member states using MRC-prepared climate change projections and adaptation analyses in the water sector and vulnerable regions; 2) Member states confirming MRC's added value as a knowledge/information hub and provider of gender instruments for adaptation; 3) MRC using an internationally accepted monitoring and reporting system for its triennial adaptation report (1st by 2013, 2nd by 2016); and 4) MRC processing and publishing a database of knowledge/experience on climate change adaptation, incorporating gender.

The targeted outcomes included improving institutional, regulatory, and management capacities in the Albanian water sector concerning drinking water supply and wastewater management. Success was defined by achieving specific targets: standardized semi-annual progress reports on national water sector strategy implementation by DPRWSS, 70% of utility providers (with >4,000 connections) implementing regulator-approved tariff rates per new guidelines, and cost-benefit analyses on energy efficiency and performance improvement plans drawn up for 20 enterprises.

The targeted outcomes of the activity are: Relevant national and regional organizations, institutions, and development cooperation programs contribute to the implementation of the Strategic Plan 2011-2020, the Nagoya Protocol, and the ongoing development of the Biodiversity Convention. This is expected to improve conditions for achieving CBD goals at local, regional, and international levels, integrate biodiversity's significance into national development goals and programs, and foster better policy coherence across industrial and developing countries. The broader development policy impact anticipated is a significant contribution to poverty reduction through biodiversity conservation, sustainable use, and equitable benefit-sharing from genetic resources. To achieve success as defined at the beginning of the activity, the following targeted outcomes would be necessary: selected questions on biodiversity-climate interactions are incorporated into policy/planning processes in at least three countries; concepts and methods for integrating ecosystem services are adopted into development planning in two pilot countries; effective protected area governance concepts are utilized by at least two international organizations and in at least three partner countries (reflected in at least two German development cooperation project proposals); Nagoya Protocol implementation or ABS agreement drafting is initiated in up to six countries; and a BMZ assessment rates the relevance and effectiveness of the project's contributions at an average of at least 3 (on a scale of 1 to 6).

The activity aimed to improve capacities of Moroccan water sector institutions for integrated, sustainable water resources management. Targeted outcomes included: strengthening legal, administrative, technical, and organizational capacities of MDCEau and ABHs; improving water resource monitoring and control systems; improving rainwater management (e.g., preparing three framework plans with innovative approaches); increasing wastewater recycling (e.g., one PNAR draft with innovative approaches); and developing regional/local water management plans (e.g., two agreed local plans for Tensift sub-catchments). Success was defined by achieving positive changes across five SMART indicators: positive change in ABH services (governance/transparency), increased use of water management tools and effectiveness of measures, change in rainwater management strategies, change in wastewater treatment/reuse, and positive changes in local IWRM cooperation.

Targeted outcomes included: local people sustainably increasing income via product value chains; implementing participatory ecosystem management concepts at local, national, and transboundary levels; and strengthening regional cooperation for conservation and development. Success required for income: 10% increase (vegetables) and 3% (tourism) in Pulan, China; 5% (honey), 10% (vegetables), 3% (tourism) in Pithoragarh, India; 4% (honey), 5% (plant fibre), 3% (tourism) in ANCA/Humla, Nepal for at least 300 households in 5 villages/pilot area. Also, country-specific datasets (springs, cultural sites, human-wildlife hotspots, ecosystem services) made public on the KSL, and a solid foundation for transboundary cooperation established.

Targeted outcomes included: AUC & AMCOW to steer, coordinate, promote, and monitor the African Water Agenda to realize the African Water Vision 2025 & Sharm el-Sheikh commitments. Success would be achieved when: 1. AMCOW's General Assembly endorses PAPs for two focal areas & a compact approach for AWA/Sharm el-Sheikh Declaration. 2. Implementation begins for one thematic PAP on transboundary water resource management with clear responsibilities & initial topics. 3. National Compacts for water & sanitation access are agreed in 10 countries. 4. An additional result-based financing mechanism for national compacts is operational.

The Policy Development Fund extension targets operationalizing GoT's Municipal PPP (8 project assessments/approvals, 20 concept notes finalized, 10 projects reviewed, model documents, staff training) and designing a renewed GoT-donor development framework (strong dialogue, improved Budget Support, capacity building recommendations). Success relies on continued PPP pilot expansion, GoT/DP commitment to renewing the relationship, PPP bridge funding's link to the 'Corridors for Growth' programme, and high-level dialogue facilitation yielding innovative, consensual partnership reforms.

["The activity aimed to reduce the vulnerability of the Jordanian population to climate change impacts by ensuring permanent availability of reliable hydrological and meteorological information, serving as a planning and investment basis for MWI. It sought to contribute to Jordan's Water Strategy 2008-2022 goal of eliminating water deficits by 2022 and strengthen MWI's capacity in water policy by addressing its lack of reliable data from measuring stations. Targets included installing/rehabilitating over 140 measuring stations nationwide, equipping them with telemetry, establishing data transmission paths, developing a suitable database, and strengthening MWI personnel for technical use and maintenance.", "Achieving success necessitated the permanent availability of reliable hydrological and meteorological information at the central MWI level, effectively used for planning and investment. It required substantial progress in eliminating water deficits by 2022, as per the Water Strategy, and significantly strengthened MWI capacities in water policy and technical management. Over 140 telemetry-equipped measuring stations needed to be fully operational, reliably collecting and transmitting data to a functional central database. Active data exchange with stakeholders was crucial, ultimately reducing the Jordanian population's vulnerability to climate change impacts through improved water resource management."]

Targeted outcomes: Enhance international climate spending effectiveness, ensure informed decision-making, improve climate policy/delivery, boost public accountability, strengthen climate finance support, and contribute to poverty reduction, UK climate goals, Paris Agreement, and SDGs. Also, understand gender/social impacts and establish disability reporting. Success requires: Achieving effectiveness/accountability via robust M&E, seamless transition to AMP (with comprehensive data capture, cross-government access), full disability reporting, consistent high platform performance, sustained value for money, and active gender/social inclusion integration.

The targeted outcome is the demonstrated viability of green project bonds for renewable energy in the Philippines, supporting debt recapitalization and deepening capital market development. Success hinges on: (i) successful introduction of Tiwi–MakBan green project bonds for geothermal energy, (ii) achievement of green bond status, and (iii) submission of application for climate bond certification. If successful and scalable, these bonds can mobilize long-term financing from institutional investors for public-private partnerships and infrastructure.

["The activity targets Indonesia's carbon-neutrality by 2060 and low-carbon strategy by 2050. I-JETP outcomes: power sector emissions peak at 290 MT CO2 by 2030, net-zero by 2050 (conditional coal retirement), and 34% renewable energy by 2030. A central outcome is the CIPP finalized by mid-August 2023. UNDP, as JT-WG lead, will develop a Just Energy Transition Framework, conduct comprehensive impact assessments, and formulate a Just Transition Programme. This includes integrating \"Just\" principles and costing into the CIPP. UNDP will also propose its support for the I-JETP Programme implementation.", "Success entails meeting I-JETP targets: power sector emissions peak at 290 MT CO2 by 2030, net-zero by 2050 (conditional coal retirement), and 34% renewable energy by 2030. The CIPP must be finalized and released by mid-August 2023. UNDP, as JT-WG lead, must deliver the Just Energy Transition Framework, impact assessments, and a Just Transition Programme, integrating its principles and costing into the CIPP. Effective mobilization of USD 20 billion funding, overcoming past climate goal struggles, and successful multi-stakeholder coordination are crucial."]

The Southeast Asia Energy Efficiency Project targets addressing the adverse effects of climate change by removing financial constraints and information barriers hindering energy efficiency market development in Southeast Asia. Its primary outcome is to demonstrate the viability of energy efficiency investments across several ADB developing member countries (DMCs) through realized energy savings, emission reduction, and net savings. The project also aims to encourage further investment in clean energy and energy efficiency services, promote private sector development, and foster environmentally sustainable models that reduce greenhouse gas emissions. The included research and development technical assistance (TA) will further promote industry growth by raising awareness among senior managers and identifying specific high-potential opportunities.To consider the activity an overall success, it must demonstrate the viability of smaller-scale clean energy projects, thereby encouraging further investment in clean energy and energy efficiency services across Southeast Asia. Specifically, success would be measured by: 1. An increase in clean energy financing in the region from $949.9 million (2011) to at least $1,187 million by 2022. 2. The registration of at least 10 new Energy Service Companies (ESCOs) in Southeast Asia between 2013 and 2022. 3. Achieving annual energy savings of at least 150,000 MWhe from energy efficiency projects by 2019. 4. An average annual avoidance of 90,000 tons of carbon emissions by 2019. 5. Average annual net savings of $10 million from energy efficiency projects by 2019. Additionally, key outputs must be met, including the successful implementation and operation of energy efficiency projects representing at least $100 million in capital expenditure in at least 3 target ADB DMCs by 2017, with 100% of these projects issuing detailed energy audit reports for baseline comparison within six months of completion. Greater awareness and market information on energy efficiency investment benefits must also be fostered through five industry workshops and the completion and dissemination of at least five case studies by the fourth quarter of 2013, alongside research and development TA workshops and knowledge product dissemination by the same timeframe.

Targeted outcomes: Strengthen LCDS governance/policy, enhance regulatory/institutional/monitoring structures, promote macroeconomic stability, and develop efficient monitoring/verification systems. Specific targets include: sustaining 0.056% annual deforestation; LCDS 2013 update; reviewed/approved extractive industry regulations; an approved/implemented MNRE strategic plan; operative MRVS issuing carbon assessments; and improved forestry/mining management with 100% Code of Practice application. Overall success requires achieving these targets and fulfilling all specific first-operation conditions and second-operation triggers.

The targeted outcomes of the "Promesa Climtica" activity are to support Paraguay in improving its Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) within the framework of the Paris Agreement, ultimately aiming to raise ambitions and limit the global temperature increase to 1.5 0C. This involves four expected results closely linked to Paraguay's National Development Plan 2030, the country's NDCs, the UN Development Assistance Framework, the UNDP Country Program, and UNFCCC commitments.Specifically, the outcomes include:
1.  **Creating political will and social ownership at national and subnational levels:** Achieved through the design of participatory and gender-sensitive consultations and dialogues on adaptation to define NDC objectives and priorities.
2.  **Reviewing, aligning, and updating existing objectives, policies, and measures:** This encompasses estimating the potential for GHG emission reduction and absorption, as well as climate change adaptation actions within the NAMA Livestock, validated with stakeholders and consistent with MRV system methodologies. It also includes an inventory, institutional and policy review, data analysis on vulnerabilities and risks, synergy analysis, and gap analysis between SDGs, NDCs, PND2030, and the National Adaptation Plan (NAP), followed by a medium-term analysis and update of the NAP. Opportunities for the energy and IPPU sectors to contribute to increasing mitigation ambition, considering women's needs, will also be identified.
3.  **Evaluating costs and investment opportunities:** This involves estimating the cost of implementing the NAMA Livestock by 2030 and conducting a differentiated cost-benefit analysis for its inclusion in the updated NDCs.
4.  **Communications and learning:** Focused on developing communication and knowledge products about lessons learned and results in Paraguay, with a potential emphasis on women who have contributed to the NDC process. This could include a mapping of relevant actors to inform the implementation plan of the National Gender Strategy for Climate Change.

Additional targeted outcomes include strengthening data and evidence, creating stronger links with existing sectoral or development plans (including the SDGs), incorporating gender considerations (ensuring women's participation and incorporating a gender perspective into GHG emission data in at least three of the five NDC sectors), and establishing a monitoring plan for NDC implementation. The activity also aims to integrate a human rights-based approach by generating GHG emission reduction data, which contributes to the fundamental human right to information.

To consider the activity an overall success, the following would be needed:
*   Paraguay's NDCs must be improved and updated, demonstrating raised ambition consistent with limiting the global temperature increase to 1.5 0C.
*   Robust data and evidence for GHG emission reduction, absorption potential, and climate change adaptation actions must be successfully strengthened and integrated into national planning.
*   A comprehensive monitoring plan for NDC implementation must be established and operational.
*   Political will and social ownership for climate action must be evident, reflected through successful participatory and gender-sensitive consultations that define clear NDC objectives and priorities at national and subnational levels.
*   The NAMA Livestock's potential for GHG emission reduction/absorption and adaptation actions must be estimated, validated with stakeholders, and aligned with MRV system methodologies.
*   The National Adaptation Plan (NAP) must be updated based on thorough analyses, incorporating priority elements from the National Disaster Risk Management and Climate Change Adaptation Plan for the Agricultural Sector.
*   Opportunities for increased mitigation ambition in the energy and IPPU sectors, addressing women's needs, must be identified and integrated into the NDCs.
*   Costs of NAMA Livestock implementation and differentiated cost-benefit analyses must be estimated and included in the updated NDCs.
*   Communication and knowledge products detailing lessons learned and results must be developed and disseminated, potentially highlighting women's contributions.
*   Women's participation is ensured, and a gender perspective is adequately incorporated into GHG emission data in at least three of the five NDC sectors, contributing to gender equality and women's empowerment.
*   Capacities are developed across the whole of government to integrate the 2030 Agenda, the Paris Agreement, and other international agreements into development plans and budgets.

The activity targets bolstering the energy sector's sustainability by improving energy security through diversification (renewables, natural gas), energy efficiency, and regional integration. It also aims to enhance financial/social sustainability by reducing and targeting subsidies, and strengthening energy planning institutions. For water and sanitation (W&S), outcomes include enhanced coverage and management, improved interagency coordination via strategic planning (e.g., PNSH 2015-2050), and institutional strengthening (e.g., CONAGUA, public sanitation company). Success requires fulfilling policy reform commitments in the Policy Matrix, including drafting a natural gas law, approving a National Plan of Action for energy efficiency, implementing subsidy reforms, and creating key W&S institutions.

The targeted outcomes of the IRAN - Alborz Integrated Land and Water Management Project are to enhance government efforts towards sustainable integrated land and water management at the river basin level. This includes: (i) increasing agricultural productivity through an improved irrigation and drainage system alongside a participatory management mechanism; (ii) reducing soil erosion and sediment yields into the Alborz Dam through enhanced upper watershed management; and (iii) protecting the water environment downstream of the Babol River and other water bodies via improved hydrological/water quality monitoring, reservoir operation, and pest management. The project aims to enable sustainable management of water resources within the Alborz River basin by mitigating siltation risks from watershed degradation, with benefits extending to farmers, livestock herders, urban residents, and forest dwellers.To consider the activity an overall success, the following would be needed: decision-making on water resources management must be fostered at the lowest appropriate levels, involving users from farm-level Water Users Associations (WUAs) and micro-catchment level watershed management/forest cooperatives. A comprehensive river basin management approach should facilitate solutions to basin-level water allocation and water quality problems. Specifically, success would entail: establishing effective soil and water protection measures; improving sustainable and participatory community-based forest and rangeland management (including cooperatives) in the upper watershed; enhancing irrigation water use efficiency and agricultural production/income through rehabilitation and modernization of irrigation/drainage systems and improved Operation & Maintenance (O&M) via participatory water management, training, and extension/research support; establishing a Basin Water Committee (BWC) and Basin Water Fund (BWF) to support integrated land/water resources management and community-based initiatives; enhancing inter-sectoral coordination through a GIS-based system and an optimized water distribution system for efficient water delivery; and the successful implementation of the Environmental Management Plan (EMP), including water quality monitoring, integrated pest management, and other mitigation measures. Furthermore, the project's success is linked to replicating WUAs up to the secondary level across wider irrigated areas (e.g., 3,000 ha) and the operationalization of the BWC concept with adequate civil society participation.

The project aims to assist Jordan in promoting regionally balanced local development by: (a) improving central-local resource transfer equity and adequacy; (b) restructuring CVDB for enhanced efficiency and diversified financial products for municipalities; (c) strengthening municipal financial management, revenue generation, and service delivery capacity; and (d) improving regional planning and inter-municipal coordination for economies of scale and strategic development. Key to this is increased participation, accountability, and enhanced coverage/quality of municipal services, especially in underserved areas.
NO RESPONSE

The targeted outcomes are to increase access to reliable, affordable, and sustainable water supply and sanitation services and improve water and wastewater services in areas served by AWSB, LVNWSB, and CWSB, reaching about 9.3 million persons. Success requires achieving these objectives, contributing to Kenya's MDGs (70% safe water, 93% improved sanitation by 2015), ensuring quality, reliability, affordability, and sustainability of service provision, and fostering financial sustainability of WSBs/WSPs to cover O&M costs and service debts. Successful infrastructure rehabilitation, institutional strengthening, and safeguard implementation are also critical.

The activity aims to enhance municipal technical capacity (fiscal, urban, environmental management), scale-up urban upgrading, and foster socio-economic equality and urban development for 200,000+ poor beneficiaries in the Capibaribe Basin. Key outcomes include improved quality of urban spaces, adequate services (water, sanitation, drainage, housing, tenure regularization), reduced flooding, and better accessibility. Success requires: Net Consolidated Debt to Current Revenue <40%; Personnel Expenditures to Current Revenue <45%; 20% increase in poorest housing property values; population satisfaction/improvement increases (Water Supply +10%, Sanitation +15%, Access/Mobility +8%, Drainage +10%, Parks/Open Space +25%, Environment +15%); and 40% of municipal informal settlement areas classified as ZEIS by Year 5.

The activity targets piloting effective poverty reduction methods for the poorest communities, ensuring more local funding, reducing administrative constraints, and empowering villagers. It aims to improve access to housing, vocational/social services, and off-farm employment; increase small town absorptive capacity; and alleviate dangerous conditions for rural migrants. Additionally, it seeks to pilot sustainable land management and climate change adaptation measures, supporting improved techniques and integrating them into national poverty programs for environmental sustainability.

The activity targets re-establishing lasting, sustainable access between provincial capitals, districts, and territories in Sud-Kivu, Katanga, Province Orientale, and Equateur. Specific outcomes include reopening and maintaining high-priority road networks (initially 1,800km, plus 376km reopening and 741km maintenance under AF), strengthening road sector capacity in strategy/policy, and integrating post-conflict infrastructure recovery with environmental protection and support for Pygmy communities. It also aims to replace worn-out panel bridges on RN4 sections. Key indicators cover road passability, freight traffic, local environmental plans, reduced illegal timber/bushmeat, improved health/education access, and new protected areas.

The targeted outcomes of the Second Rural Electrification activity are to: 1. Increase access to electricity in rural areas of Peru on an efficient and sustainable basis, building upon the achievements of a preceding Rural Electrification Project. 2. Provide new electricity connections through investment in rural electrification subprojects by electric distribution companies, utilizing both conventional grid extension and individual household renewable energy systems (primarily photovoltaic systems) for dispersed or remote populations. 3. Build the capacity of various stakeholders for rural electrification, especially electricity distribution companies and consumers. 4. Promote productive uses of electricity and renewable energy. For the activity to be considered an overall success: 1. It must achieve the development objective of increasing efficient and sustainable electricity access in rural Peru. 2. Investment in subprojects must successfully provide new electricity connections, with subprojects being financially viable at current tariff levels after partial capital cost subsidies. 3. Electricity distribution companies must effectively propose, construct, own, and operate subprojects as part of their long-term regulated service under OSINERGMIN supervision. 4. The model for sustainable PV system service provision must be effectively implemented and maintained, including the engagement of local SMEs for operation and maintenance to address long-term risks. 5. Technical assistance efforts must successfully build the capacity of electricity distribution companies and consumers, and lead to a demonstrable increase in productive uses of electricity and renewable energy. 6. The project must effectively incorporate flexibility in subproject eligibility criteria to adapt to dynamic situations. 7. Distribution companies must comply with social and environmental safeguards. 8. The project must successfully institutionalize and expand upon the achievements and lessons learned from the First Rural Electrification Project.

["The activity targets improved effectiveness of road transport and enhanced efficiency of selected public services. Specific outcomes include: 100% of CREMA State road network in good condition (IRI<3.5) by Year 6, increasing vehicle-km on good paved roads to 2.32 million Veh x km by Year 6, 92% of rural population in western Tocantins having access to all-season roads by Year 6, and substantial achievement of 3 out of 4 intermediate indicators for Component 2 (modernizing administration, strengthening local economy, sustainable resource use, improving education).", "Overall success requires achieving the Project Development Objective by meeting all PDO-level targets: 100% of the 1500km CREMA State road network in good condition (IRI<3.5); 2.32 million vehicle-km travelled on good paved roads; 92% of western Tocantins rural population with all-season road access; and substantial achievement (>=75% of target) for 3 out of 4 intermediate public service delivery indicators (modernized administration, local economy, environmental management, education)."]

Targeted outcomes include advancing preparation of priority regional energy projects in SAPP through: establishing a Projects Acceleration Team (PAT) for technical/financial/legal/E&S guidance; funding comprehensive studies (feasibility, E&S, legal documentation) to improve project packages and enable commercial equity/debt commitments; and providing analytical support to SAPP, including updating the SAPP Pool Plan and conducting studies for a solid investment knowledge base and sustainability. Success entails projects reaching a mature preparation stage, attracting private sector funds for preparation, and high-quality documentation enabling commercial financing commitments.

Targeted Outcomes: Improve key public services in Jiangxi's small towns via upgraded infrastructure: roads, bridges, bus stations, flood protection (dikes, drainage), water supply, wastewater collection, and wetland restoration to improve water quality and biodiversity. Also, strengthen institutional capacity for sustainable infrastructure management. Success Criteria: The project is successful if it leads to increased population with modern road access, more people protected by enhanced flood protection/drainage, and more customers served by piped water supply. Effective institutional capacity and project management are crucial.

["The activity aims to support Shanghai's low-carbon city development, focusing on Changning district, by piloting green energy schemes and scaling up low-carbon investments in buildings. Specific outcomes include supporting Changning in achieving its carbon intensity reduction target through technical assistance and capacity building on green energy buildings, clean energy supply, and green transport. This involves removing market barriers, increasing uptake of IBRD loans, building stakeholder capacity, facilitating energy savings measurement and verification, and ensuring sustainability and widespread replication of low-carbon investments. The project will also develop performance-based building energy efficiency benchmarks, recommend financing mechanisms, support energy audits, and create an on-line energy monitoring platform. It intends to design a pilot distributed generation center and a carbon emission cap and trade scheme, improve public transport routes, and fund low-carbon investments in building retrofits and new low-emission buildings, including energy efficiency improvements and renewable energy applications.", "Overall success depends on strong commitment from all government levels (China, Shanghai, Changning) to carbon reduction and green growth, with Changning demonstrating leadership in piloting bold policies. The activity must effectively overcome identified market barriers for building retrofits, such as small project size, high transaction costs, and perceived risks, through comprehensive commercial bank financing, targeted technical assistance, and capacity building. Success is also tied to robust institutional arrangements, including established committees and a functional PMO, and a sound financial structure that leverages IBRD/GEF funds with significant co-financing and PFIs bearing default risks. A strong technical foundation, including an on-line energy monitoring platform for impact measurement, and mechanisms for capacity building and policy development to ensure sustainability and widespread replication across Shanghai, are crucial for success."]

Targeted outcomes include fostering resilient communities, promoting low-carbon development, and enhancing climate resilience. Quantifiable targets: 1,570,400 beneficiaries; 1,462.8 CO2e tonnes emissions avoided/reduced; 1.325 MW renewable/low-emission energy installed; 2 entrepreneurial models developed; deployment strategies finalized for 3 low-carbon technologies (urban mobility, health, agriculture); 85 EV charging stations; 30 solar cold storages; solarization of 150 PHCs; 1,000 youths and 1,000 former coal-mine workers trained for green jobs/livelihoods; 10 gender-inclusive climate/DRR plans (from 2). Success requires achieving these targets within the one-year timeframe, laying groundwork for India's NDCs and Net Zero Emissions by 2070, and strengthening climate resilience and low-carbon transitions.

["The activity targets poverty reduction and MDG achievement, delivering positive changes through service delivery, empowering individuals, improving accountability, addressing conflict/security/justice issues, ensuring value for money and gender equality. The extension specifically aims for 'Ending Aid Dependency Through Jobs' by improving policies for labour-intensive growth, strengthening labor force/enterprise capacity, and addressing employment barriers for vulnerable groups. Ultimate outcome: 5.5 million poor across 58 countries experiencing poverty reduction via improved health, education, water, sanitation, accountability, resilience, security, and justice.", "Overall success requires projects to meet their objectives, with an estimated 31 out of 34 projects for the extension round delivering benefits. It also means achieving the ultimate outcome of 5.5 million people experiencing poverty reduction through proxy outcomes like improved health, education, water, sanitation, opportunities to hold power to account, resilience to shocks, and conflict reduction with increased security and justice. For 'Jobs' projects, success is indicated by increased income and reduced cash transfer dependency. All projects must demonstrate good value for money, mainstream gender equality, and be supported by effective fund and due diligence management."]

Targeted outcomes include establishing a well-functioning WAPP cooperative power pooling mechanism, promoting risk-free cross-border electricity exchange, mutual assistance, reducing drought vulnerability, and increasing access to stable, reliable, lower-cost regional power. A unified regional electricity market by 2012-2020 is a key aim. Specific infrastructure outcomes are the completion and operation of the entire 330kV Coastal Transmission Backbone by 2009, with the Aboadze to Volta line operational by 2007, and institutional upgrades to the WAPP Secretariat for M&E functions. Measurable outcomes include increased power traded, reduced transmission losses, fewer power outages, faster problem detection, and improved generation station availability.

The GEF Romania Integrated Nutrient Pollution Control Project targets the following outcomes: building institutional and regulatory capacity within the Government of Romania to implement the EU Environment Acquis, reducing nutrient discharges to water bodies, promoting behavioral change at the communal level, and mainstreaming global environmental issues. Success will be achieved when 65% of targeted NVZs show a 10% reduction in nutrient load discharge, 50% of the population adopts preventative measures, inter-governmental coordination is acknowledged as improved, and the EU provides a favorable assessment of Nitrates Directive progress. Additionally, 30% awareness of local actions' impact on Danube/Black Sea water quality, 45% households adopting improved livestock waste management, 75% communes implementing afforestation/pasture rehabilitation, 30% cropped area under nutrient reduction measures, and 30% households with sewage access in targeted villages are also key indicators.

["The Third Tamil Nadu Urban Development Project (TNUDP III) aims to continuously improve urban infrastructure services in Tamil Nadu sustainably. Outcomes include strengthening Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) via decentralization and expanding capacity building. It targets mobilizing sustainable resources for urban infrastructure investments through private financing via TNUDF and capital grants for low-income areas. Institutional Development will advance urban sector reforms and improve ULB managerial and financial performance through benchmarking. Urban Investment will develop sustainable infrastructure in water supply, wastewater, solid waste, storm drains, roads, and common facilities.", "Overall success for TNUDP III requires achieving continuous and sustainable improvement in urban infrastructure services. This means successfully strengthening ULB empowerment via decentralization and expanded capacity building, and effectively mobilizing sustainable resources, including private financing through TNUDF and targeted capital grants for low-income areas. Success also entails advancing urban sector reforms, improving ULB managerial/financial performance, developing sustainable infrastructure investments, demonstrating improvements in urban service/performance indicators (building on TNUDP II), maintaining systems appropriately, and ensuring satisfactory service levels with positive community feedback."]

The Inland Waters Project targets several key outcomes:First, it aims to enhance water supply services, wastewater services, and flood protection measures across selected municipalities in Croatia's inland region.Specific outcomes under Component 1, Technical Assistance, include: strengthening institutional capabilities within Hrvatske Vode (HV) and the Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry, and Water Management (MOAFWM) to implement the Water Management Strategy and prepare projects with necessary documentation (feasibility studies, designs, environmental assessments, bidding documents) for securing location and construction permits and potential EU funding; financing consulting services for Project Reports and annual audit reports, and supporting HV's compliance efforts; and, specifically for utilities, improving operating efficiency, customer service, construction supervision, and sub-project progress reporting.Component 2, Investments, targets improving water supply coverage, increasing sewerage coverage, and constructing wastewater treatment plants in various municipalities such as Virovitica, Northern Baranja (Beli Manastir), Ogulin, Davor-Nova Gradiska, Slavonska-Podravina, Nasice, Southern Baranja, Vukovar, Ilok, Ivankovo, Otok and Komletinci, and Cerna. Additionally, this component will support the implementation of flood protection measures in the central Sava River basin, including financing for the rehabilitation and expansion of dikes, canals, and channels to increase flood management coverage in the central Posavina area.To consider the activity an overall success, the project would need to achieve: the enhancement of water supply and wastewater services in the targeted municipalities; increased sewerage coverage and functional wastewater treatment infrastructure; successful implementation of flood protection measures, including rehabilitation and expansion of flood management infrastructure; strengthened institutional capabilities of HV and MOAFWM, enabling them to effectively manage water resources and prepare projects for future funding, particularly from the EU; demonstrated adherence to financial and safeguard requirements through timely and accurate reporting and audits; improved operating efficiency and customer service among participating utilities; and compliance with regulatory standards and best practices in water management and environmental protection.

["Targeted outcomes: tackling binding constraints to employment-generating growth in high-potential value chains. Key quantifiable targets are: 10,000 jobs in Tourism; 15,000 ICT jobs over 5 years; and up to 50,000 Entertainment jobs over 5 years. Other objectives include: improving wholesale/retail efficiency and income; enhancing Tourism land access, skills, and visitor attractions; strengthening ICT infrastructure; boosting Entertainment's IPR, marketing, finance, and training; and streamlining investment climate procedures (land, permits, tax administration) across target states.", "Overall success will be measured via the enterprise survey, tracking progress against quantified targets for growth, incomes, and jobs. Key: establishing detailed baseline assessments and achieving agreed output, outcome, and impact indicators. A clear logic model showing how activities deliver outcomes/impacts, with critical process milestones, must be evident. Specific success hinges on meeting job creation targets: Tourism (10,000), ICT (15,000 over 5 years), Entertainment (up to 50,000 over 5 years), plus demonstrable improvements in wholesale/retail efficiency, land access, and tax administration."]

Targeted outcomes: (i) strengthen policy development and strategic framework for renewable energy and rural electrification, and (ii) attract investors for sustainable development of new hydropower generation to supply the Port Moresby electricity grid.To be an overall success:1. Rural and renewable energy policies must be completed and submitted to Cabinet, and a participatory rural electrification strategy finalized.2. Financing for the Naoro Brown Hydropower Project must be at an advanced stage, with financiers' due diligence completed, leading to project construction and commercial operation supplying the Port Moresby grid.3. A planning study for future hydropower projects must be completed. Addressing the USD 0.90 million financing gap is critical for project implementation.

The targeted outcomes of the HN Water and Sanitation Sector Modernization Project are: to improve the sustainability, efficiency, and reliability of Honduras's water and sanitation sector institutions; and to enhance the performance of these institutions in fulfilling their roles in accordance with the WSS Sector Framework Law.
