More specifically, the project aims to establish and strengthen municipal service providers, fostering good governance in WSS services through increased transparency and accountability; reinforce national sector actors (ERSAPS, CONASA, SANAA) to effectively carry out their new roles for successful decentralization of services; and reduce non-revenue water in selected areas of Tegucigalpa to provide immediate improvements in service quality.To consider the activity an overall success, the following outcomes would need to be achieved:1.  **Cost Recovery:** 5 of the Water Supply and Sanitation (WSS) utilities reach cost recovery, meaning the ratio of total revenue to total operative cost equals one.2.  **Revenue per Volumetric Unit:** 5 of the WSS utilities reach a rate of revenues per volumetric unit of water produced of at least 80%.3.  **Service Continuity:** 5 of the WSS utilities increase service continuity by 6 hours or more per day.4.  **Decentralization:** At least 90% of the SANAA systems are transferred to the municipalities.5.  **Autonomous Service Providers:** At least 6 design proposals for autonomous service providers are approved by ERSAPS.6.  **Sector Financial Policy:** CONASA issues the new financial policy of the sector.7.  **Customer Satisfaction:** An improved customer rating of water supply and sanitation, expressed as a TBD percent increase in population rating service as satisfactory in participating towns.8.  **Non-Revenue Water Reduction (Participating Municipalities):** At least 4 utilities in participating municipalities reduce levels of Non-Revenue Water (including apparent losses, real losses, and unbilled authorized consumption) by 10 percentage points.9.  **Non-Revenue Water Reduction (Service Area):** Non-Revenue Water (including apparent losses, real losses, and unbilled authorized consumption) is reduced by 15% points in the service area.10. **Income/Produced Water Ratio:** The ratio of total income per cubic meter produced in the targeted area increases by 20%.11. **Transparency Compliance:** The three sector agencies (SANAA, CONASA, ERSAPS) substantially comply with applying the Ley de Transparencia y Acceso a la Información.

The targeted outcomes of the BO PICAR Community Investment in Rural Areas activity are primarily to improve access to sustainable basic infrastructure and services for the most disadvantaged rural communities in some of the poorest municipalities of Bolivia, and to improve food security activities for these selected communities. This will be achieved through two main components: 1) Community Capacity Building, which will finance technical assistance, training, and general capacity building to empower rural communities and community-based organizations to effectively identify, prioritize, implement, and manage community investments; and 2) Community-Driven Development Investment, which will provide matching-grant financing for small-scale investment subprojects that address communities' self-identified priorities, encompassing rural infrastructure, basic services, vulnerability reduction, food security, nutrition, and the development of community-based organizations. To consider the activity an overall success, the following would be needed: 1) The most disadvantaged rural communities must experience improved access to sustainable basic socio-economic infrastructure and services, and enhanced food security. 2) Empowered rural communities must actively identify, prioritize, implement, and maintain their own development subprojects through a participatory process. 3) The capacities of municipal administrations and community members must be strengthened, enabling effective identification and implementation of community investments. 4) The demand-driven small-scale investment subprojects must be successfully implemented on a matching-grant basis and prove their long-term sustainability. 5) Activities must be well integrated into municipal development plans, managed by municipalities for their multi-sectoral nature, and demonstrate synergy and complementarity with other initiatives. 6) Investments must be designed to be easily maintained in poor and remote communities. 7) The project must effectively address the priority concerns of the poorest communities, deepening local poverty-targeting. 8) Impact monitoring must be an essential and effective feature of the project's implementation.

The targeted outcomes are to strengthen governance, urban services, and infrastructure in the Nairobi Metropolitan Area. This includes investing in local infrastructure (roads, markets, street lighting, drainage, paths), improving integrated solid waste management and sewerage collection/disposal, and strengthening service delivery entities (ministries, authorities, utilities). The aim is better planning, reduced chaos, and increased access to urban services for residents. Overall success is when: 1. A specified number of direct beneficiaries (including females) are reached. 2. Target numbers of residents gain access to improved sanitation/sewerage and regular solid waste collection. 3. Urban areas achieve target access to all-season roads (within 500m). 4. Residents near commuter rail stations benefit from improved access. 5. Institutional capacity building strengthens governance and service delivery.

The activity targets improving Oaxaca's WSS institutional framework and enhancing service quality/sustainability in selected urban areas. Outcomes include modernizing WSS laws/regulations, improving service quality/efficiency for 50,000 people in San Juan Chapultepec via infrastructure upgrades (boreholes, networks, metering), and improving service/financial sustainability for 600,000 people in 18 secondary towns. Success requires prepared WSS legal/regulatory tools, 7 decentralized water utilities, WSS monitoring tools in use, and 650,000 people receiving sustainable/improved WSS (continuous service + ">=90% commercial efficiency" in San Juan Chapultepec OR "improved continuity" + "revenues > operating expenses" in provincial towns), supporting 19 utilities.

The Kandadji Project targets poverty alleviation by managing Niger River flow, restoring ecosystems, ensuring reliable municipal water for Niamey, boosting agriculture (46,000 ha irrigated land), and improving energy supply (130 MW, 600 GWh/yr). Success requires full resettlement of 100% of displaced persons by Dec 2015, a legally established Biodiversity Offset Area by Dec 2014, a power purchase agreement by Sept 2015, enhanced NBA capacity, completion of energy infrastructure, and effective local development to prevent social conflicts.

The targeted outcomes of the activity are to improve the quality, reliability, and accountability of Niger's PFM and PIM systems, establish a policy and regulatory framework for a well-functioning public irrigation sector, and facilitate electricity sector reform. To be considered an overall success, by March 2018: 1) PFM/PIM: Deviation between voted capital budget and outturn reduced to 35%; loan signature to 'Avis Juridique' in 45 days; quarterly budget reports published in 4 weeks. 2) Irrigation: 15,000 ha under ONAHA's jurisdiction; 10 operational IWUAs. 3) Electricity: Tariffs set by clear procedures. Success also requires an adequate macroeconomic framework, positive growth outlook, government commitment, and effective reform implementation despite challenges.

Targeted outcomes known at the beginning for Bac Ninh and Hai Duong included: a 30% reduction in BOD5 contamination in open channels and ponds; wastewater treatment plant discharge values of 50 mg/l for BSB5, 150 mg/l for CSB, and 100 mg/l for TSS. Economic success for Bac Ninh meant covering 80% of wastewater operating costs by 2017 and 100% by 2018, and for Hai Duong, 70% from 2019. Overall, success would entail secured and environmentally sound rain and wastewater disposal, reducing health risks, mitigating flood damage, and lessening environmental impacts, all while achieving economic efficiency through low operating costs.

The activity aimed to increase energy efficiency in electricity distribution in rural Haryana to promote economic growth and climate protection. This involved modernizing low-voltage lines to 11kV to reduce technical losses and illegal connections. Anticipated outcomes included improved energy efficiency in the agricultural sector, reduced electricity losses, more reliable supply, better equipment utilization, increased agricultural yields and household income, overall economic growth, and CO2 emission reduction. Success was defined by reducing technical and non-technical distribution losses from a baseline of 31% to a maximum of 15%, and achieving an annual CO2 avoidance of 150,000 tonnes.

The activity targets increasing renewable energy and energy efficiency, improving energy security by shifting from fossil fuels to clean sources, reducing high energy costs, and boosting resilience. Key outcomes include creating sustainable energy systems, a diversified energy mix, and substantial fuel cost savings. Success requires UK support effectively addressing financial and enabling environment barriers, grant funding de-risking projects, and developing robust regulatory frameworks. Specific targets include achieving net benefits of ~£33m for SEEC, a cost-benefit ratio >3.5 for geothermal, Grenada displacing 55% of electricity from diesel via a 15MW plant, and developing a regional roadmap for offshore wind with a potential demonstration turbine and investment plan for CARICOM by 2050, alongside full utilization of M&E funds.

The targeted outcomes for the Abwasserentsorgung Kafr El Sheikh activity, known at the beginning, included contributing to reducing health hazards and improving the environmental situation in Kafr El Sheikh Governorate, and achieving proper wastewater disposal with reduced pollution in canals. To achieve success, the activity targeted: 1) Wastewater collection from at least 66% of the connectable population in supplied settlements, conveyed to centralized treatment plants. 2) 24-hour composite samples to exceed a BOD5 discharge value of 60mg/l only in exceptional cases (maximum twice per month).

The activity targeted rehabilitating 4-7 small hydropower plants (<10 MW each) to generate approximately 40-50 GWh/a total electricity (e.g., Khadori 2 0-3 GWh, Pshavela <1 GWh, Ritseula 20-30 GWh baseline). It also aimed to establish a EUR 5.11 million revolving Renewable Energy Fund (REF) to finance projects, offer consulting to banks, and demonstrate financial sector effects. Success required meeting electricity production targets, rehabilitating planned HPPs, establishing the REF, ensuring ">=30%" owner contribution, and a "
<=10" MW capacity cap per plant.

The targeted outcomes of the activity, known at the beginning, were to ensure a reliable, cost-effective, and environmentally sustainable supply of electrical energy to Montenegro's interconnected grid. Specific targets for success included: maintaining a long-term available peak output of 285 MW from the hydropower plant, achieving an average annual net electricity input to the grid of 845 GWh, and ensuring a minimum power plant availability of 95%. Achieving all these specific targets would constitute success as defined at the beginning of the activity.

Targeted outcomes of the activity, known at the beginning, were: The development objective was to contribute to improved living conditions for the population and to environmental and climate protection. The programme's objective was to ensure a sustainably efficient and more environmentally friendly supply of district heating to consumers connected to the network. Success as defined at the beginning would be achieved by: 1. Ensuring a room temperature of at least 18-20°C without additional electric heaters. 2. Reducing heating consumption by 10%. 3. Reducing water losses in the rehabilitated networks by 10%. 4. Improving the efficiency of rehabilitated boilers by at least 2 percentage points. 5. Improving the cost coverage of the participating District Heating Companies.

The activity targeted the improvement of wetland and floodplain management. Success would be achieved by: 1. Establishing 25 new protected areas based on IFM standards (baseline: 1 existing). 2. Increasing fish catch by an average of 25% in areas with 3-year resource use agreements (baseline: 7.93 kg/person/month CPUE). 3. Achieving 20% female occupancy in key resource management committee positions (baseline: 0%). 4. Increasing stock density of wetland-dependent flora and fauna by 20% in existing and 10% in new regions (baselines: 50 fish, 94 bird, 1.02 dolphin/km, 31 herpetology species). 5. Submitting two national guidelines on sustainable wetland management aspects (baseline: 0).

The activity targeted improving service offerings in waste management, water supply/sanitation, and energy efficiency in selected rural municipalities. Success, as defined by initial outcome-level targets, required: Outcome A: 25 pilot measures implemented; 70 formal agreements signed by LPAs for joint service delivery; 50% of implemented measures in 35 communities improving women's access to local public services. Outcome B: Three RDAs facilitating project concepts with total value of "170 Mio; 70% satisfaction from 30 LPAs with RDA facilitation; 75% of RDA-facilitated project concepts scrutinized for environmental impact.

The primary objective of the activity was the improvement of participatory sustainable forest management in Chunati Wildlife Sanctuary (CWS). This was to be achieved by reducing local people's dependency on forest resources, fostering collaboration between the Forest Department and Co-Management Organizations for reforestation, encouraging local people to protect their forests, and promoting their active participation in reforestation. For success, the project targeted an increase of at least 10% in the average density of indigenous tree species in CWS, a 50% reduction in non-sustainable forest resource use, and at least 80% of female participants in alternative income training confirming increased income to avoid unsustainable forest resource use. Additionally, developing a financing concept based on CO2 certificates/REDD was an initial target.

Targeted outcomes at activity start: cost-effective use of small hydropower plants (SHP) for electricity via long-term financing; improved regenerative power supply; sustainable economic development and climate protection; and professionalized SHP investment financing. Success required: for Phase 1, >=50% increase in electricity from 13 plants, specific investment costs <=1,500 EUR/KW (new) and <=700 EUR/KW (rehab), and >=95% regular debt servicing (volume); for Phase 2, >=30% nominal capacity utilization (electricity production / (available capacity x annual hours)) and >=95% regular/complete debt servicing (total volume).

The activity's targeted outcome was to protect and sustainably manage agrobiodiversity in Timor-Leste. Success would require: 1) Developing one implementation guideline for ABD protection by March 2016; 2) Integrating ABD approaches into at least 20 village development plans by March 2016; 3) Establishing at least two indigenous plant-based value chains in all 4 project districts by March 2016; 4) 750 resource-poor farm households (with 30% women) improving income by at least 10%.

At the beginning, the activity targeted contributing to global climate change mitigation and economic growth in Morocco. Specific goals for success included an average annual electricity production exceeding 370 GWh after the first two full years of operation, an annual reduction of 230,000 tonnes of CO2 emissions two years after commissioning, and an economic return of 0.9 percent. Additionally, success required that three years after completion, at least two more solar power plants would be under construction or in preparation, fostering a Moroccan solar industry.

At the beginning of the activity, success was defined by three targeted outcomes. First, biodiversity-compatible and economically viable production systems were to be implemented across 2,500 hectares in each program region (Amazon, Andes, Coastal), where previously none existed. Second, income for 600 families in each program region was targeted to increase by 10% through sustainable valorization of biodiversity, raising their average monthly income from 200 USD to 220 USD. Third, one strategic measure derived from the national climate strategy was to be implemented within the development plans of local authorities in each program region, where no such measures were previously in place.

["Targeted outcomes of the Solar PV Plant Sakri activity, known at its beginning, included: contributing to solar power expansion and sustainable energy in India; supporting Maharashtra's energy restructuring via the first major grid-connected PV project; achieving 180 GWh annual electrical energy; creating ~100 direct jobs; meeting >=50% of Maharashtra's 2010/11 259 GWh PV requirement; no other even larger solar plants starting construction in India within 3 years of commissioning; increasing renewable energy share to >=10% in Maharashtra/India within 5 years; and preventing 155,000 t p.a. of CO2 emissions.", "Success, as defined at the beginning, would necessitate: annual electrical energy production of 180 GWh from Sakri; creating ~100 direct jobs; meeting >=50% of Maharashtra's 2010/11 259 GWh photovoltaic requirement (>=129.5 GWh); ensuring no other even larger solar plants enter construction in India within 3 years of commissioning (May 2013); increasing renewable energy share to >=10% in Maharashtra/India within 5 years of commissioning; and preventing 155,000 t p.a. of CO2 emissions."]

The activity aimed to promote biogas use in Brazil by improving access to state-of-the-art technology, enhancing the biogas value chain efficiency via training and business models, and laying foundations for widespread use within four years. It sought to integrate energetic biogas use into 4 technical norms/programs; for 6 sector actors to disseminate knowledge; establish 16 German-Brazilian partnerships; 3 water utilities to integrate energy efficiency/biogas use; and establish 4 model plants from wastewater, agriculture, and waste sectors as reference facilities. It also aimed to involve public-private partnerships and contribute to Brazil's energy/climate goals and SDGs.

Targeted outcomes for ProAmbiente included: reducing annual deforestation by 10% in two regions from 2016; increasing legal timber market volume (exports by 10%, domestic use by 20%); improving timber processing company productivity by 10%; confirming improved environmental approval/monitoring in three regions; three regions investing 30% more financial resources in forest/biodiversity projects; increasing SINANPE self-financing by 20%; increasing public/private funding for biodiversity R&I by 20% by 2016; and ensuring at least 30% women and appropriate indigenous representation in environmental policy bodies in three regions. Achieving these targets defines success.

The targeted outcomes of the activity were to ensure RCREEE possessed the prerequisites to provide its services in the Arab region in the long term, and to enhance its performance capacity as a regional sectoral organization for renewable energies and energy efficiency by specifying its service portfolio, establishing partnerships, shaping processes, and refining competencies. The activity also aimed to contribute to regional energy policy and strategy development, aligning with BMZ's sector concept and international SDGs 7, 13, and 17, UNFCCC, and Sustainable Energy for All.

The targeted outcome was to ensure efficient implementation of agreed sector policies and strategies for conservation and sustainable use of natural resources by key actors within SINA. To achieve this, the project aimed to adapt eight policies, implement six examples or 20 measures, and integrate 10 cases into policies/strategies. Specific target values included: implementing six pilot land-use plans; 20 ecosystem-based climate change adaptation measures (five per program region); taking into account ten measures for economic assessment of ecosystem services in policy development; and the Colombian Ministry of Environment having one integrated Environmental Information System (SIAC). Success was defined by meeting these targets, assessed via five SMART (specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, time-bound) module-level indicators, which were designed to reflect project-attributable change processes and contribute to efficient policy implementation. A noted initial challenge was the critical quantitative focus of some indicators.

Targeted outcomes aimed to establish crucial national prerequisites for forest biodiversity conservation and sustainable use, supporting Vietnam's Green Growth Strategy, poverty reduction, climate change mitigation, sustainable forest use, and reduced illegal trade. Success required: 1) Regulations positively impacting the sector. 2) Knowledge transfer strengthening forest stability/competitiveness. 3) Promoting FLEGT, REDD+, and SFM certification. Specific targets included: 70% of MARD/MONRE legal documents harmonized, two gender-sensitive national plans for biodiversity capacity with budgets, and REDD+/FLEGT plans using project experiences.

At the activity's outset, targeted outcomes included the rehabilitation of degraded forests by relevant agencies and users, considering climate change and biodiversity. Success was defined by: 1) an increased admixture of indigenous, climate-resilient species in project forests, enhancing adaptability, and 2) increased average revenues for forest users and local forest enterprises from sustainable forest management. Other targets included strengthening institutions, forming 80 forest user groups, concluding 850 JFM agreements, resolving land-use conflicts, and advancing forestry policy.

The targeted outcome of the activity, known at its beginning, was to sharpen the economic policy profile of German development cooperation concerning the three dimensions of sustainability: social, ecological, and economic. To achieve success as defined at the outset, the following targeted outcomes were necessary: (1) the mainstreaming of innovative economic policy approaches within the German development cooperation portfolio, (2) the development of implementation-relevant positions on economic policy, ensuring consideration of inclusiveness and gender, and (3) the incorporation of economic policy aspects related to environmental and inclusive sustainability into national and international concepts, strategies, and negotiations.

The activity aimed to achieve two module objectives: (1) firmly establish German development cooperation's water security positions in international and national policy, and (2) mainstream the water, energy, and food security nexus approach into development strategies globally. Success targets included: supporting BMZ for 10 high-profile appearances and 400 inquiries; international organizations adopting 2 additional German water security positions; BMZ's positions on women and girls' water/sanitation needs integrated into 6 international conference segments; integration of BMZ Gender Action Plan 2016-2020 water/sanitation objectives; BMZ commissioning 22 nexus-specific projects; and 5 donors implementing Bonn 2011 Nexus Conference strategies.

Targeted outcomes necessary for success included: annual reports from 20 communal conservancies available in the national CBNRM compliance database; an additional source of revenue listed in annual reports for 10 communal conservancies or integrated community forests; a 20% increase in female members' satisfaction (from 50% to 70%) regarding their involvement in decision-making processes and accommodation of their interests; 10 additional communal area conservancies implementing one climate change adaptation measure; and wildlife management and utilisation plans applied in five additional communal area conservancies within the project regions.

["The activity targets preventing large-scale deforestation and fostering a low-carbon green economy in Papua, aiming for a 25% deforestation reduction (1.87M hectares, 224M tonnes CO2 by 2030). By March 2015, outcomes include improved government land-use planning, alignment of all land-use licenses with spatial plans, supporting at least 38 low-carbon enterprises (18 financed/operational), establishing a green investment fund, and 1.25M hectares towards sustainability certification. It also bolsters planning expertise, green investment strategy, partnerships, and environmental awareness.", "Success hinges on achieving quantifiable targets for deforestation reduction, carbon emissions, enterprise support, and land certification. Crucial factors include strong political will, UK's established relationships, national approval of Papua's Spatial Plan, sustained collaboration among government, communities, and the private sector, and a conflict-sensitive approach that strengthens community/government relationships, preventing Papua from following unsustainable development paths."]

The targeted outcomes include: delivering high-quality research in sustainable energy (bioenergy, solar, decentralised generation, urban/transport, energy efficiency) to address development challenges; enabling policy-makers and practitioners to scale up sustainable modern energy services; increasing energy access, resilience, and livelihood opportunities for poor populations in Sub-Saharan Africa and Asia; strengthened coordination and technical monitoring of the USES program; and enhanced networking, lesson learning, and effective external dissemination of research findings, including improved reporting against the program's primary outcome.

The targeted outcomes of the "Building Urban Resilience to Climate Change in Tanzania" activity are to build capacity within Tanzanian cities and villages to adapt to current and future climate change impacts. This will be achieved through improved data and evidence, enhanced urban planning, and upgraded infrastructure, ultimately fostering sustainable economic growth and development.Specific outcomes include:1.  Establishing an open-source climate information system to strengthen urban planning resilience, encompassing data dissemination and training for urban councils.2.  Assisting urban authorities in integrating climate change resilience techniques into urban planning, governance, design, operations, and investments. This also involves influencing urban residents' behaviors through community engagement, incorporating lessons from the water and sanitation sectors, and particularly focusing on increasing preparedness for urban flood risks.3.  Facilitating investment in land management and infrastructure in selected cities, with the benefits informing future urban development plans and complementing existing World Bank urban investments in Tanzania.Overall success for the activity would be achieved when areas and municipal authorities threatened by climate change impacts benefit from increased capacity, resilience, and readiness to cope with disasters. Tanzanian urban areas are expected to demonstrate the ability to use better data and evidence to understand and educate themselves about climate change threats and difficult situations. This will involve improving urban planning and informing important investment decisions in infrastructure and land use to build and strengthen resilience.To consider the activity an overall success, several critical factors must be met:1.  Establishment of robust and sustainable systems for managing project funds.2.  Strong coordination among all stakeholders, including the Government, DFID, and the World Bank.3.  Securing community and government acceptance, especially for Component 3 (land management and infrastructure investment), to ensure effective implementation and sustained investments.4.  Effective implementation of the World Bank's strong plans in procurement and construction to combat corruption, including electronic procurement systems, the use of expert construction consultants for quality oversight, and strict procurement management.5.  Vital joint oversight and monitoring efforts by DFID and the World Bank to control the threat of corruption.

The activity targets delivering positive development impacts with local partners; fostering personal development for UK and in-country volunteers; encouraging increased engagement in social action post-placement; empowering youth to contribute to poverty reduction and sustainable development; building skills and leadership; raising awareness of development issues; and enhancing understanding of global citizenship. Key outcomes include poverty reduction, personal and social development of volunteers, and creating a global network of socially active young people.

["The activity targets increased economic stability and shared economic prosperity in Jordan, aiming for macroeconomic stability, sustainable and inclusive economic growth, and mutual trade and prosperity. This includes fostering private sector-led growth, attracting FDI, and job creation. Specific outcomes involve accelerating reform delivery, addressing NEPCO's operating deficit and structural energy sector problems, assisting MoF with central debt management and PPP-related fiscal analysis, implementing P4R solutions, improving water sector financial sustainability, enhancing MoIN's effectiveness in attracting FDI/PPPs, providing evidence-based policy advice for GoJ on economic management and governance, and identifying UK market access for Jordanian exporters.", "Overall success would be achieved by realizing increased economic stability and shared prosperity in Jordan, demonstrating macroeconomic stability, sustainable and inclusive economic growth, and mutual trade and prosperity. This requires successful implementation of structural reforms, resolving NEPCO's deficit, effective GoJ debt and energy/water sector financial management, and MoIN effectively attracting FDI and promoting PPPs. Success also means GoJ officials show enhanced capability in economic management, apply evidence-based decisions for inclusive growth, and Jordanian exporters gain UK market access. Compliance with UK Gender Equality Act and environmental/counter-terrorism financing policies is also crucial."]

The targeted outcomes are: developing and implementing a fully coherent, overarching evaluative learning framework; translating the P4F programme’s investment and deforestation-reduction objectives into a validated planning/reporting structure with evaluation questions and indicators; developing a baseline for future outcome/impact evaluation; and supporting adaptive management/knowledge management through theory-based evaluation. Overall success requires the framework to effectively achieve these translations, baselines, and learning cycles, thus enabling robust assessment and adaptive management for increased private investment in sustainable forestry and zero-deforestation agriculture.

The Maritime Governance and Environment Programme aims for several key outcomes: improving OT economies via effective marine resource management and cost-effective monitoring; enhancing maritime safety and environmental legislation; increasing OT capability against invasive non-native species; strengthening the environmental evidence base for economic policy and planning; boosting biosecurity capacity, including eradicating mice from Gough Island; improving port access, reducing environmental impact, and enhancing disaster modeling/recovery to lessen UK liability; and establishing effective legislative structures with clear understanding of maritime convention protections, thereby enhancing the UK's and Territories' reputations as shipping registries. The activity would be considered a success upon the realization of these outcomes, demonstrating improved marine protection, reduced illegal fishing, effective management of invasive species, enhanced environmental monitoring, better maritime policy decisions, and full compliance with international maritime obligations through strengthened local capacity and legislative frameworks.

Targeted outcomes: producers implement sustainable natural resource management; consumers demand sustainable products; CSOs, indigenous/peasant communities, and young leaders strengthen integrated biodiversity management, using traditional/technological tools. National agencies will update legislation to enable 4 million hectares under forest management by 2020, per national plans. A forest research network and CSOs leading policy development are also targeted. Success requires achieving these, especially effective legislation, the 4 million hectares target, and active, empowered participation from communities, CSOs, women, and youth.

The activity targets land degradation neutrality, restoration of 60,000 ha of degraded land, and improved land/water resource use on 100,000 ha in the Amudarya watershed, enhancing livelihood sustainability and ecosystem resilience. Specific outcomes include stabilized ecosystem services on 746,000 ha, crops resilient to salinization on 10,000 ha, and strengthened PA effectiveness for 1,077,554 ha (including 60,000 ha new PAs) to secure biodiversity, leading to stable populations of rare bird species. It also aims to expand PA systems, strengthen national capacity, and foster regional cooperation in the Aral Sea Basin.

["The activity targets the Nigerian Government showing willingness to review policies like the Land Use Act by 2017, and increasing engagement between local communities and relevant government agencies to protect communal land rights. By 2020, the aim is for the government to change policies/laws to protect community rights, hold companies accountable for their impacts, and ensure policy implementation. It also targets companies/investors ceasing harmful practices, local communities defending their rights, and increased public awareness on crude/palm oil issues at all levels.", "Overall success would mean the Nigerian government changing policies and laws to protect community rights, holding companies accountable, and ensuring policy implementation. Companies and investors would no longer apply large-scale plantations and oil extraction projects causing deforestation/land degradation, and would abide by Nigerian law. Local communities would successfully defend their rights and promote sustainable livelihoods. There would be increased public awareness on crude/palm oil problems. Specifics include instigating policy changes/revocation of illegally acquired communal lands, a court case initiated in 2017, corporations adhering to international standards, and government agencies enforcing laws and accountability."]

The targeted outcomes of the activity are: the development of a comprehensive project concept for an HFC phase-down management plan (KIP Stage I) in Sri Lanka, aiming for a 10% reduction in HFC consumption during 2024-2029. This involves capacity enhancement for safe handling of flammable/toxic refrigerants, introduction and updating of relevant standards, safety guidelines, and regulations, and developing an overarching strategy that incorporates lessons from HPMP Stage I and a Gender Management Plan.Overall success would be achieved with the timely preparation and finalization of the full-sized KIP Stage I project document. This includes achieving the 10% HFC consumption reduction target for 2024-2029, successfully integrating capacity building for safe refrigerant handling, establishing robust safety standards and regulations, effectively leveraging HPMP Stage I lessons, implementing gender mainstreaming actions, applying social and environmental safeguards, ensuring rigorous quarterly monitoring during the Project Preparation Grant implementation, and maintaining a clear commitment from the Ministry of Environment to achieve phase-out targets.

["Strengthen APN's technical, operational, & administrative capacities; improve forest fire/emergency management (equipment, prevention, rehabilitation) & high-mountain rescue; develop socio-environmental projects for community quality of life & sustainable development; install cisterns for potable water access in communities; fortify co-management for Lanín & Nahuel Huapi National Parks; strengthen National Parks Foundation's operational capacities; manage natural/cultural resources; integrate gender equality (GEN3, GEND).", "APN demonstrates strengthened capabilities & effective fire/emergency response; rescue protocols operational; documented improvement in community quality of life via socio-environmental projects; widespread access to potable water through functioning cisterns & trained beneficiaries; robust co-management in Lanín/Nahuel Huapi benefiting communities; self-sustaining National Parks Foundation with independent project/fund management; positive environmental/social impact; efficient budget use aligned with SDGs; generation of replicable best practices; achieved GEN3/GEND targets; strong stakeholder engagement."]

At the outset, success targets included: 51% of national/sub-national stakeholders confirming improved natural resource policy/management; 30% of natural resource conflicts constructively managed; 20% income increase for 40% of supported households; 40 municipalities/cities in Visayas adopting EnRD approaches funded publicly; 30% women's participation in planning/implementation; protection/rehabilitation of ~15,000 ha forests/mangroves & 4,000 ha agroforestry, reducing ~80,000 tons of CO2e annually; significant biodiversity increase in 50% of marine protected areas & 10% increase in tree species in rehabilitated forests; and over 70% of stakeholders confirming improved disaster protection and stabilized public/economic/social life.

The activity aimed to improve the planning, technical, and institutional capacities of relevant institutions for successful implementation of the Renewable Energy Act. Targeted outcomes included strengthening partner institutions and formulating an implementation strategy, support instruments, and a market study. Success was defined by having an implementation strategy in place, securing agreement on two legislative instruments (e.g., specimen agreements, technical norms, standards, workflows) with the Ministry of Power, EC, and PURC, and publishing one study on renewable energy market development.

The targeted outcome was improved management of national protected area systems in Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru regarding climate change adaptation. Success required: (1) Integrating five common climate change adaptation approaches, developed by the three countries, into national guidelines/management plans, piloted in one protected area per country. (2) Designing three regional/national multisectional concertation processes for adaptation with active participation from non-conservation decision-makers. (3) Using systematized experiences from participatively developed local adaptation strategies in three nature reserves per country for national guideline formulation. (4) Including gender aspects and conclusions from joint analyses in implementation guidelines for protected area management plans and local adaptation strategies.

The activity targeted improving district heating for 10.54 million m² and 100,000 households, with 4.4 million GJ energy output, by acquiring 6.3 km steam pipelines, 13 steam-to-hot-water exchangers, 35.5 km hot water pipelines, 29 heat transfer stations, control systems, and decommissioning 66 boilers. Annual savings targeted: 190,000 t coal, 300,000 t CO2. Success required 18°C room temperature, service centers, medium international facility quality, competent staff, emission standard adherence, high coal/CO2 efficiency per Euro, and production efficiency incentives.

The activity targeted contributing to Mexico's CO2 emission reduction and energy system sustainability. Specific outcomes included financing ~26,000 housing units by 2020, each meeting an 'Energy Efficiency Standard 80' (20% less primary energy than reference buildings), and achieving a 20% or 12 kg/m²/year reduction in CO2 emissions (or 17,784 tCO2 annually). Also targeted were a 64% reduction in household electricity demand and costs, and improved indoor comfort (20-25°C/20-27.5°C with fan for 40-80% of time depending on climate). Success depended on establishing energy-efficient technologies as standard, fostering a market with high demand and affordable offerings, and ensuring bridging loans offset costs for comparable pricing to standard homes.

The targeted outcomes are to support Uruguay's Second Energy Transition (2ET) by 2025, achieving sustainable production and consumption systems. This includes decarbonizing key economic sectors, improving power system efficiency, enhancing vulnerable groups' access to clean and affordable energy (aiming for nearly 100% access by 2024), promoting gender equality and women's empowerment in 2ET sub-sectors, and mainstreaming impact-informed financing in the local financial sector. Overall success requires REIF-financed projects to demonstrate robust technical and financial performance, driving market uptake, along with enhanced regulatory frameworks, developed technology knowledge and capacity, and increased local capacities for SDG-aligned financing.

The activity targets developing a sustainable production and consumption model for Cambodia, aiming for a green, resilient, environmentally sound society by 2028. Outcomes include finalizing comprehensive portfolio documents for natural resource protection, circular economy, and climate action. Specific expected outputs are: strengthened REDD+ elements for higher carbon credit integrity, enhanced roles of Indigenous People and Local Communities in NDC implementation, and establishment of a National Plastic Action Partnership. Overall success requires these successful pilot outputs to lead to full portfolio documents (with theory of change, results, resource framework, partnerships, and resource mobilization plans) and generate new pipeline opportunities for scaling up these initiatives.

The targeted outcome is increased generation and utilization of reliable, clean renewable energy. Success means achieving by 2022: Tuvalu's renewable electricity generation mix at 35% (from 16%); 1,280 MWh/year solar PV power; 317,000 liters/year diesel fuel savings; 844 tons/year CO2e emissions avoided. Also, installing specified solar PV on outer islands (over 90% renewable contribution) and Funafuti (500 kW PV, 1 MW/2 MWh BESS, reaching 32% renewable penetration), enhanced institutional capacity, and gender-inclusive employment (25% women) and training (50% women).

The BR Sao Paulo Water Recovery Project - REAGUA targets several key outcomes. Its primary objective is to increase water availability in São Paulo's critical watersheds by improving water demand management and consumption efficiency, controlling and reducing water losses, and reusing wastewater. The project also aims to enhance water quality by building new and optimizing existing sewerage systems and cleaning selected water streams. Furthermore, it seeks to improve the Water and Sanitation Services (WSS) sector's legal, institutional, and regulatory framework, while providing support to beneficiaries (municipalities and WSS providers) in designing, operating, and maintaining programs and facilities. Specific benefits include expanding access to WSS services, reducing water pollution, fostering the sustainable use of watersheds, lowering long-run WSS provision costs through increased service provider efficiency, improving human and environmental health by reducing water-borne diseases, and enhancing the quality of life for urban populations. The project also serves as a testing ground for the broader application of Output Based Disbursement (OBD) mechanisms across Brazil, with payments based on effective improvements in environmental quality and water supply. Interventions will prioritize areas of extreme water scarcity and populations with high social vulnerability.To consider the activity an overall success, several conditions must be met. There needs to be a rigorous implementation of eligibility and selection criteria for WSS providers, linked to measurable results indicators on water quantity and quality, and methodologies developed to connect inputs, outputs, relative costs, and recovered water volume. The effectiveness of the OBD mechanisms in a monopolistic and often inefficient sector is paramount, as they are designed to incentivize cost-effective solutions and service expansion, especially for vulnerable populations. Moreover, the successful development and approval of environmental management mechanisms are essential to prevent or mitigate potential adverse impacts, ensuring that the project's small-scale infrastructure facilities deliver their intended benefits. Success is also predicated on the clear prioritization of interventions based on objective criteria like water scarcity and social vulnerability, coupled with anticipated positive environmental impacts and a strong commitment from the Borrower.

Targeted outcomes: Improve operational efficiency of electricity distribution, increase population access to electricity, facilitate Ghana's transition to a low-carbon economy by reducing greenhouse gas emissions, enhance reliability/efficiency of power networks, improve efficiency/security of fuels. Success: Measured by improved ECG technical/commercial performance (PVI), increased national household electrification rate, tons of CO2 emissions avoided, and quantitative/qualitative monitoring indicators for power availability, network efficiency, new consumers, and sectoral financial viability.

The targeted outcome of the Argentina Renewable Energy for Rural Areas Project is to increase and improve access to modern energy services in rural areas of Argentina. The project builds upon the success of its predecessor, PERMER I, which delivered reliable electric supply to over 30,000 customers (150,000 people) in 15 provinces using renewable off-grid technologies and a solid operation and maintenance (O&M) scheme, including residential solar home systems, electrified schools and public buildings, and solar thermal systems.To consider the activity an overall success, it would need to achieve this primary objective of increasing and improving access to modern energy services for a significant number of rural beneficiaries, likely at a scale comparable to or exceeding PERMER I. Success also requires the sustainable provision of these energy services through robust O&M mechanisms, leveraging established relationships with stakeholders while incorporating new institutions. Furthermore, success hinges on continued coordinated efforts between the Federal Government and Provinces, effective implementation of its four components (renewable electricity service provision, solar thermal energy service provision, project deployment support, and project management), and strengthening the Project Coordinating Unit (PCU) to build capacity in poorer provinces and ensure safeguards compliance and monitoring.

The targeted outcomes of the activity, Sofortmaßnahmen Wasserversorgung Galle I (TS), known at the beginning, were: to provide immediate materials for reconstruction and support NWSDB in restoring water supply for displaced persons and providing transport capacity. Overarching objectives included contributing to the sustainable reconstruction of the water supply system, supporting regional development, and improving local living conditions. Specific project objectives involved the complete rehabilitation and extension of the centralized water supply system to provide drinking water to about 60% of the inhabitants in the project area, and extending the system's capacity to Ambalangoda and Galle City. Success would be achieved by meeting these targets.

The activity aimed for a reliable, efficient, sustainable, and cost-effective electricity supply, displacing diesel generators. Key outcomes included poverty reduction (MDG 1), prevention of rural-urban migration, and social/ecological sustainability (MDG 7). Success required: transmitting at least 11 GWh via the line, Kampot peak load exceeding 3.6 MW (4 years post-completion), rural tariff reduction by 25% (4 years post-commissioning), Kampot city tariff reduction by 20% (1 year post-commissioning), annually transmitting 20 GWh via 22 kV rural lines, no load shedding, system availability 	at least 95%, doubling rural electrification from 2%, and connecting 	at least 5,000 additional rural households.

Targeted outcomes for success included: Phases I & II: 22 hrs/day secure water supply; reduce technical water losses to 30% (Phase I) / 25% (Phase II); 125 l/c/day household consumption; 95% population centrally supplied; 100% running cost coverage. Phase I: Reduce Batlava-reservoir abstraction to 18,920,578 m

³/year. Phase II: Reduce flood events to 5; Batlava raw water quality meeting EU standards. Phase III: Water quality meeting WHO standards post-treatment; 22 hrs/day supply continuity; 75% treatment plant capacity utilization (3rd year); remove wastewater discharges into Iber Canal. The overall aim was to enhance living conditions and sustainably improve water supply/sewage.

Targeted outcomes included improved management of Selva Maya protected areas and strengthened regional cooperation for natural resource conservation, aimed at maintaining ecosystem functions and cultural values, with direct benefits for the poor population from small-scale productive projects. Success was defined by: 80% of supported protected areas applying updated, community-involved management plans; proper use and maintenance of improved operational infrastructure, equipment, and integrated monitoring tools; improved early detection and fighting of forest fires; and a reduction in the deforestation rate to less than 0.4% (2017-2020) in the project area.

The targeted outcomes of the activity, 'Program for Renewable Energies and Energy Efficiency in the SAPP,' were: To expand energy production capacities from renewable sources, develop requisite national and regional transmission capacities, and increase energy efficiency across the member states of the SAPP. At an impact level, the program aimed to contribute to climate protection, foster economic development in the region, and strengthen regional integration within the SAPP and SADC. Initial targets included financing at least two projects under both the SAPP I and SAPP II credit lines, with a minimum of one project from each aligning with the SADC Regional Infrastructure Development Master Plan. Specific hydropower generation capacity targets included 6 MW and 28 GWh/year for ILA 2 (Lunsemfwa), 360 MW and 421 GWh/year for ILA 3 (Kariba North Bank Extension - KNBE), and 120 MW and 611 GWh/year for ILA 4 (Itezhi-Tezhi HPP). The program intended to implement energy efficiency measures to achieve at least 20% energy savings. Targeted CO2 emission reductions were 120,000 t p.a. for ILA 1, 11,200 t p.a. for ILA 2, 178,000 t p.a. for ILA 3, and 245,000 t p.a. for ILA 4. For ILA 1, success also required attainment of full performance capacity no later than the end of 2014, replacement of at least 70% of coal for Ohorongo Cement with wood chips, and at least 90% availability of wood chip production facilities. For the hydropower projects (ILA 2, 3, 4), the turbines were targeted to be available at least 90% of the time. To achieve success as defined at the beginning of the activity, it would be necessary to meet all these stated targets.

Targeted outcomes: Local population in Sundarbans pilot regions participates exemplarily in safeguarding biodiversity and managing ecologically sensitive dike zones. Strategy included institutional strengthening, participatory dike rehabilitation, and (initially) organic shrimp farming. Success required: 3 agreements for dike rehabilitation/management in one coastal district; biodiversity maintained/increased (5 indicator species) in co-managed dike areas; 1 rehabilitated dike section in each of three districts, managed by 3 committees (>=25% women); and a new project in the region by other donors.

The targeted outcomes of the activity, known at the beginning, were to introduce three regulations, labels, or sets of rules for bioenergy technologies and fuels. Success also required ensuring the availability of biomass with a heating value of 300,000 MWh/year for energy generation in three municipal district heating companies. Additionally, it was targeted to increase the number of households using efficient wood-based heating technologies and dried firewood in model regions by 10,000, aiming for 15% of all households. Achieving these outcomes would be necessary to fulfill the activity's objective of improving capacities and framework conditions for sustainable bioenergy use in Serbia.

The central objective of the activity was to enable key decision-makers and planning authorities to effectively plan and design both existing and new industrial areas to be more climate-resilient. To achieve success as defined at the beginning, the following targeted outcomes were deemed necessary: two subnational state institutions were expected to utilize risk analysis and methodology for climate-resilient industrial development in their planning processes and regulations; two state governments or private sector organizations were to integrate industrial sector climate adaptation into their strategies; one selected industrial development agency was targeted to include climate risks in at least 50% of its planned investments for the financial year 2017; and three industrial areas were to implement prioritized climate-resilience measures in their adaptation and development plans.

["The targeted outcomes of the activity are: to collect and analyze comprehensive nationwide data on HFCs and ODS alternatives (import/export, equipment inventory, servicing information) to enable accurate forecasting and identification of actions to curb HFC growth; to develop a comprehensive HFC Phase-down Management Plan document, including evaluation of needs, strategies, components, and action plans for investment and non-investment sectors, incorporating lessons learned from HPMP for smooth, cost-effective implementation.", "To consider the activity an overall success, the following would be needed: the finalization and approval of the robust HFC Phase-down Management Plan document; the plan providing a clear roadmap for Eswatini to meet its Montreal Protocol compliance targets (HFC freeze by 2024 and 10% reduction by 2029 in the servicing sector); the plan leveraging strengthened regulatory frameworks (amended ODS Regulations, revised HS codes); the design ensuring smooth, cost-effective implementation; and the preparation adhering to gender policies, ensuring inclusive participation and data collection."]

["The project targets two primary impacts: (i) a revitalized and diversified economy with developed non-coal industries, and (ii) an improved living environment, safety, and public health in the project cities (Hegang, Jixi, Qitaihe, and Shuangyashan). The expected outcome is an increase in non-coal economic activities and urban livability, aiming to halt and reverse out-migration and attract residents and companies. This will directly benefit 2.73 million urban residents through enhanced SME competitiveness, job creation, mining remediation for environmental improvement, better water supply, sewerage, district heating, flood protection, roads, road safety, public transport, greater environmental and traffic safety awareness, and increased cooperation in tourism, logistics, trade, and SME support using smart city applications.", "The activity will be considered an overall success if it effectively implements its diverse components, successfully manages the high environmental and resettlement risks, and overcomes the entrenched challenges of economic diversification and private sector development in the coal-mining and state-owned enterprise-dominated region. A comprehensive approach, strong financing, focus on capacity building, private sector engagement, climate change integration, and gender mainstreaming are crucial indicators for achieving its ambitious objectives."]

The targeted outcomes known at the beginning of the activity were: ensuring a safe supply of environmentally-friendly produced electrical energy from the hydropower plant; increasing each generator's net capacity to 200 MW and extending their lifespan by 35 years. Success would be achieved by: an average annual feed-in of approximately 8,000 GWh; technical availability of the 12 generators of at least 98%; and preventing approximately 4.5 million tons of CO2 emissions per annum.

The targeted outcomes were to ensure a minimum cost-effective supply of drinking water within the programme area and to contribute to efficient, sustainable management of the country's scarce water resources. Success, as defined at the beginning, required: reducing the total share of unaccounted for water from approximately 50% (in Irbid/Jerash in 2000) to less than or equal to 25%; an average water pressure not exceeding 6 bar (at 19 of 39 measurement points); regular water supply at least one day per week; and tariff revenues greater than 100% of operating costs. The activity also targeted preventing illegal water abstraction and contracting a private operator for water management.

Targeted outcomes: Improve living conditions by enhancing water/wastewater supply, reducing health risks, fostering economic development, mitigating environmental pollution. Ensure continuous, safe, affordable drinking water; hygienically/ecologically safe wastewater collection/treatment; reduce water losses. Success requires: 24h/day, 120 l/cd water supply (WHO quality); 165k (77%) water connections; 90% private/100% public metered connections; 127k (59%) wastewater connections; water losses to 25%; cost-covering/socially acceptable tariffs proven; smooth private operator takeover.

["The targeted developmental outcome was to improve the health of the Nablus population by reducing unaccounted-for water and ensuring safer drinking water. The outcome-level objective was to enable Nablus to provide drinking water more efficiently through restructuring and rehabilitation. Key planned measures included introducing pressure zones, process automation via a control center, and operational training. Financial targets included full operating cost coverage from tariff income and a 70% collection rate.", "Specific targets for success included reducing non-revenue water from 31% to 25%, decreasing electricity consumption of pumps from 0.93 kWh/m³ to 0.79 kWh/m³, improving supply intervals from 2 days/week to 3 days/week, and increasing water consumption by 10% from 69 l/p/d. Additionally, water quality compliant with WHO standards (positive free chlorine, negative total coliform, negative fecal coliform, nitrate below 50 mg/l) was targeted. Achieving these specific targets would define success."]

["The activity aimed to contribute to the protection of remaining forest areas and biodiversity, and regeneration of degraded areas within the Mata Atlântica in Minas Gerais. Primary outcomes included the sustainable and integrated strengthening of protected areas (UCs) and contributing to a policy for restoration and sustainable use of natural resources in UCs and their buffer zones. Further anticipated outcomes included effectively protected UCs, declining deforestation rates, decreasing human-caused forest fires, and implemented policies for sustainable resource use and regeneration incentives.", "To achieve success, the activity targeted: a positive quality development in UC management (an increase of at least 20% in the 'quality factor' from a 4.0 baseline); over 70% of project UCs having functioning advisory boards; identification and promotion of at least six alternative forms of sustainable use; forest cover in project UCs maintained at least constant; and the restoration/recovery of native vegetation by at least 20,000 hectares. Success also depended on macroeconomic stability, political will, access to project areas, and the absence of land conflicts."]

The activity aimed to improve conditions for planning and implementing efficient energy use in Nepal. Targeted outcomes included creating energy efficiency market opportunities, defining national minimum quality requirements for improved biomass stoves and building related technical capacities, and developing Nepal's energy efficiency policy framework, including adopting key strategies and supporting a central institution. Success was defined by: 1) increase in energy efficiency services from 40 to 150 by mid-2017; 2) doubling of energy efficiency investments from 600M NPR; 3) 60% of biomass stoves meeting national quality requirements; 4) adoption of two energy efficiency strategies by the Cabinet.

["The activity's targeted outcomes, known at the beginning, were to improve living conditions in Batumi and strengthen resilience to climate change. Specifically, the aim was to provide improved protection against flooding for the population and improve bathing water quality at Batumi beach. Secondary potential outcomes included reducing extraneous water inflow to the wastewater treatment plant, enhancing coastal water quality and settlement hygiene, and promoting tourism development.", "To achieve success as defined at the beginning, specific targeted outcomes were necessary: 1) Reduce flooding frequency in park areas to once every two years, residential areas to once every five years, and government buildings to once every ten years. 2) Limit extraneous water inflow to the wastewater treatment plant to 55% of dry weather flow. 3) Improve bathing water quality at Batumi beach to E-coli 500 cfu/100ml and Enterococci 185 cfu/100ml."]

["The program aims to support Guatemala in developing and implementing its national climate change agenda, creating an institutional framework for mitigation, adaptation, and disaster risk management. Its general objective is to reduce GHG emissions, enhance adaptive capacity, and reduce vulnerability long-term. Specific outcomes include: ensuring macroeconomic stability; mainstreaming climate change into public policy across key sectors and national planning; strengthening MARN and SE-CONRED for monitoring and implementation; promoting mitigation activities (e.g., knowledge of emission reduction, GHG offsetting tools, energy efficiency); and supporting adaptation (e.g., expanding knowledge, integrating risk management into info platforms).", "Overall success hinges on the completion of agreed policy reforms and institutional changes tied to the disbursement of all four tranches. Key indicators include: establishing the National Climate Change Commission and Policy; approving guidelines for climate change integration into planning; tabling a Climate Change Law; approving a National Policy for Disaster Risk Reduction; incorporating climate change into annual planning exercises and agency AWPs; establishing climate change units/departments in key ministries (MINFIN, SEGEPLAN, MARN); developing GHG offset methodologies; implementing pilot mitigation/adaptation projects; securing a climate change finance fund; and achieving key results indicators from the program's matrix. Effective leveraging of the PBL mechanism, sustained ministerial support, and coordination with other lenders are also crucial."]
NO RESPONSE

Targeted outcomes: To protect public health, improve Palestinian well-being by controlling pollution from improper health care waste, save the environment, and strengthen resilience. Success requires: 1. Enhancing JSC capacity via a supplied, commissioned, and operated 75kg/h medical waste microwave treatment device. 2. Improving MoH capacity by training 12,000 personnel in health care management and designing/printing awareness materials for 390,000 people. 3. Robust coordination with partners, leveraging existing projects, and strong governance.
