The project aims to expand sustainable electricity access/quality for economic development/poverty alleviation, reduce environmental degradation (GHG), improve energy efficiency, foster renewable energy adoption, and build sector capacity. Success requires: providing reliable electricity to many new people; significantly faster small business growth in electrified areas; establishing private sector-led rural electrification; 100% new customer backlog reduction and improved voltage; enhanced biomass energy management; increased viable renewable energy businesses; GoE adoption of a renewable energy action plan; a Proclamation to Parliament for effectiveness; and functional RES/REF/POM for disbursement.

The Third Environment Program Support Project targets: improved protection and sustainable management of critical biodiversity resources at field level; mainstreaming conservation into macroeconomic management/sector programs; establishing sustainable financing; conserving biodiversity/renewable natural resources in eco-regions with multi-stakeholder participation; strengthening environmental management framework in public policy/investments; increasing area under effective conservation (to 7.5M ha); reducing deforestation; enhancing NEAP financial sustainability; and improving governance. Success requires: achieving these targets, demonstrating sustained efforts, improved coordination, strong local participation, result-based mechanisms, robust law enforcement, and an enabling environment with community empowerment and effective M&E.

["The activity targets increased access to sustainable water supply and sanitation services for rural/urban users, improving stakeholder capacity to plan, construct, and maintain facilities, and enhancing water resource management. It aims to improve 5,500-7,000 rural water schemes (serving ~2-3 million people in ~3,700 communities) and 40-50 town water supplies. Annually, ~5,000 community-managed systems are targeted for rural areas. Other outcomes include underpinning economic development, improving health, and supporting government policies on decentralization, cost recovery, and water as an economic good.", "Overall success is measured by increased population access to improved water supply in rural/urban areas, the number of effective woreda water/sanitation programs, and effective, autonomous water boards established in towns. Success also depends on: good water resource management, demand-responsive community selection, community involvement (cost understanding), effective monitoring, adequately trained staff, autonomous service providers with business plans/cost recovery, performance agreements, private sector involvement, developed supply chains, and integrated sanitation/hygiene promotion with water supply."]

Targeted outcomes: To increase productivity and market access for targeted farmers and community groups through market-driven agricultural intensification and diversification, stimulating Assam's agricultural economy, and empowering farmers. Success is measured by increased yields of crops, fish, livestock, and marketed surplus. What would be needed for overall success: Key indicators: 70% of farmers/groups increase enterprise yields/marketed surplus; cropping intensity up to 195%; 60% reduction in water-logging crop damage. Crucial conditions: Adequate GOA funding for rural road maintenance; 100% AI services cost recovery by Sept 2008; ALDA business plan agreed by Dec 2005, phasing out GOA support by Dec 2008.

The activity, "WAPP: The First Phase of the Inter-Zonal Transmission Hub Project of the WAPP (APL3) Program," targets several key outcomes: to reduce the cost of and improve the security of electricity supply to Burkina Faso, to increase Ghana's electricity export capacity, and to enhance the overall reliability and stability of the WAPP zone A transmission network. To be considered an overall success, the project must achieve the following by 2015: a reduction in the average weighted annual cost of electricity supply in Burkina Faso from a 2010 baseline of 0.2634 US dollars/KWh to 0.2540 US dollars/KWh; a decrease in the annual total duration of outages in Burkina Faso due to generation capacity deficit/load shedding from 130 hours in 2010 to 8 hours; an increase in the annual total hours of operation of the interconnection line from 0 hours in 2010 to 7884 hours; and an increase in the annual quantity of energy exported from Ghana to Burkina Faso from 0 GWh in 2010 to 158 GWh.

["The Turkey Resilient Landscape Integration Project (TULIP) aims to strengthen integrated landscape management and increase access to improved livelihood opportunities and resilient infrastructure services for rural communities in targeted areas. Key targeted outcomes include: the adoption of a National Strategy for Landscape Resilience and Sustainable Recovery; bringing 150,000 hectares of land under sustainable landscape management practices; and 13,000 poor households benefiting from improved livelihood opportunities. Furthermore, 90,000 people are targeted to access resilient infrastructure services, addressing poverty, climate/disaster risks, inadequate infrastructure, and human capital loss.", "For the Turkey Resilient Landscape Integration Project (TULIP) to be considered an overall success, the following must be achieved: the National Strategy for Landscape Resilience and Sustainable Recovery must be adopted; 150,000 hectares of land must be under sustainable landscape management practices; 13,000 poor households must benefit from improved livelihood opportunities; and 90,000 people must access resilient infrastructure services, specifically including protection for 23,000 against flooding/landslides, improved water for 18,000, improved irrigation for 4,000, and rehabilitated rural roads for 45,000."]

The activity targets improving the resilience of pastoralists and agro-pastoralists and strengthening countries' capacity to respond promptly and effectively to pastoral crises or emergencies. Targeted outcomes include more sustained animal assets, increased income generation, improved animal health (e.g., 80% PPR herd immunity, controlled veterinary drugs), sustainable landscape management, enhanced livestock value chains, and better social and economic inclusion for women and youth. Success is measured by achieving conditions for official recognition as a Peste des Petits Ruminants (PPR)-free country, hectares under sustainable landscape management, increased household revenues, and the number of farmers reached with services, disaggregated by women and youth.

Targeted outcomes include reducing deforestation and poverty, enhancing local authorities' capacity in management, planning, land use, tenure security, and law enforcement, and improving local people's human, technical, financial, and organizational management for sustainable environmental exploitation and socio-economic conditions. Environmental outcomes aim for 20% forest regeneration (90 hectares) and a 20% reduction in deforestation/degradation. Economic outcomes target a 15% increase in household budgets via income-generating agriculture, intensifying cultivation on 80 hectares of crops and 5 hectares of rice/fish farms, and distributing 600 sires. Governance includes a forest land register, improved community radio stations, establishing five carbon stock monitoring plots, five farmers' committees, five MRV mechanisms, micro-zoning 115,307 hectares, and developing a management and development policy.

["The activity targets strengthening African climate institutions to generate and widely disseminate climate-related information, fostering climate-resilient development by integrating climate aspects into policies. Outcomes include a pool of qualified African climate scientists, enhanced climate tools (early warning systems, information systems, downscaled models), improved end-user skills to integrate climate data into development planning, increased policy-making efficiency to reduce climate vulnerability, greater access to financial resources for climate impacts, and the production/dissemination of relevant climate data at regional, national, and sub-national levels for policy makers and end-users.", "Success would be achieved if adaptation to climate change is mainstreamed into all national development policies by 2020, reflected in 75% of national budgets, and leads to a 75% reduction in climate change vulnerability by 2020. Other criteria include regional downscaled climate scenarios and decision-support systems developed for all African regions and available in at least 25 countries. Key indicators include at least three African climate centers meeting international standards by 2012, a 50% increase in end-user access to climate observation networks, implementation of sub-regional early warning systems in each region, and a 50% increase in policy makers and women using climate information for agriculture."]

["The activity targets enabling 100 million people to adopt behaviors and access products/services improving health, livelihoods, environment, or well-being by 2025. This includes 40-55 million impacted by behavior change and 20-40 million by transformative business models. Outcomes also include identifying and testing 50 business models, developing a robust evidence base for research insights, and providing funding and support. TRANSFORM aims to unlock a minimum of  20m in match funding and help businesses leverage  20m in private sector investment. High-quality research outputs (e.g., 20 public materials, 10 peer-reviewed papers) and testing social business solutions by 2020 are also targeted outcomes.","Overall success for TRANSFORM hinges on achieving the targeted impact of 100 million people adopting improved behaviors and accessing products/services by 2025, including specific numbers for behavior change and business model impacts. Success is contingent on the initial phase demonstrating momentum, and is measured by meeting pre-agreed milestones for grant payments. A strong M&E system, capable of building long-term capacity within partner organizations, is crucial. Attracting additional investment from other partners, capitalising on established mechanisms for value for money, and successfully testing and refining social business solutions by 2020 are also key indicators of success."]

The Sustainable Development Program for the Central Provinces targets several key outcomes to contribute to sustainable development and improve the inhabitants' quality of life in the central provinces. These include increasing the productivity and income of small agricultural producers, improving access to efficient and safe water services, contributing to regional environmental health, providing environmental protection for critical watersheds and coastal areas, and strengthening institutional capacities at the local level.  To be considered an overall success, the activity would need to achieve the following specific targets:

For **Project 1: Support for technological innovation and market access for small agricultural producers**,
*   The net income of beneficiary producers must increase by at least 15% for up to five evaluated technologies, measured 12 months after adoption, from a baseline of 0% increase.
*   At least 8,000 eligible producers must purchase items from the program's technology menu.
*   A total of 600 demonstration plots must be installed and visited by at least 6,000 poor producers.
*   100 km of local roads used to transport production must be repaired and maintained according to agreed standards.

For **Project 2: Management efficiency and expanded coverage for water, sanitation, and solid waste management services**,
*   Safe drinking water coverage must expand to 200,000 inhabitants and adequate sanitation to 130,000 inhabitants.
*   The total percentage of consumption billed to users based on effective micrometering must increase from an estimated baseline of 40% to 95%.
*   The volume of water losses per user billed must decline from 6,000 gallons to 3,000 gallons per month.
*   The average volume billed per user based on actual metering must increase from 6,000 gallons to 8,000 gallons per month.
*   Water consumption fees collected from users at the provincial level must increase from 70% in 2009 to 90% in 2012, with an uncollectible portfolio of no more than 5%.
*   Effective water service coverage must increase from 70% to 90% in towns over 1,500 inhabitants, improving continuity and quality for 90,000 current users and incorporating 20,000 new users.
*   Effective sewer service coverage must increase from 33% to 50% in towns over 1,500 inhabitants, expanding coverage to 50,000 new users.
*   Three provincial districts must maintain technically, environmentally, and financially sustainable solid waste management systems, improving from a baseline of currently substandard and environmentally harmful disposal.

For **Project 3: Environmental management for the conservation of critical watersheds and coastal areas**,
*   Forest cover in the La Villa and Parita River watersheds must be maintained at or increased from the 2009 baseline, with environmental conditions improving from "critical degradation" to "good."
*   Mangrove cover in Parita Bay and Montijo Gulf must remain at or increase from the 2009 baseline, with environmental conditions improving from "critical degradation" to "good."
*   The beneficiary municipios' revenue from the issuance of permits pursuant to their environmental land use and development plans must represent at least 5% of total revenue by the fourth year of execution.

For **Project 4: Provincial governance and management capacity**,
*   There must be an increased efficiency and effectiveness of institutions in the central provinces, aiming for 85% financial execution of planned investments by the end of the fourth year, from a baseline of 60-80% execution rates in 2006 across different provinces.

The Sanitation Program for the Districts of Arraijan and La Chorrera - Stage 1 targets several key outcomes to improve sanitary conditions and reduce pollution. Specific objectives include: (i) increasing wastewater treatment coverage, (ii) building capacity for works management and sector sustainability, and (iii) promoting the proper operation of the sewer system through comprehensive environmental interventions and health education.The targeted outcomes are:1.  Improved Water Quality in the Caimito River: The water quality in the Caimito River, measured in mg/l BOD5, is targeted to improve from a baseline of 18 in 2016 to 12 by the end of the project.2.  Increased Sewage Treatment Capacity in the Arraiján and La Chorrera Sanitation Area:    *   The number of households whose wastewater is treated is projected to increase from a baseline of 0 in 2016 to 65,800 by the end of the project.    *   The coverage of households whose wastewater is treated is aimed to reach 57% from a baseline of 0% in 2016.    *   The flow of treated sewage is expected to rise from a baseline of 0 m³/sec in 2016 to 0.6 m³/sec by the end of the project.3.  Increased Sewage Collection Capacity in the Arraiján and La Chorrera Sanitation Area:    *   Sanitary sewer coverage is targeted to increase from a baseline of 50% in 2016 to 60% by the end of the project.    *   The number of households with sewer system connections is projected to increase from a baseline of 51,600 in 2016 to 68,400 by the end of the project.4.  Integrated Management System of the Project Coordination Unit (UCPSP) Internationally Accredited: The UCPSP's integrated management system (SIG) is targeted to achieve 5 certifications (from a baseline of 0 in 2016) meeting international quality standards, specifically ISO 9001, ISO 14001, and OHSAS 18001.5.  Strengthened Capacity of Sector Agencies: Seven sector agencies (MINSA, UCPSP, DISAPA, IDAAN, ASEP, MiAmbiente, and MEF) are targeted to be coordinated (from a baseline of 0 in 2016).To consider the activity an overall success, all the aforementioned quantitative targets must be met. Additionally, the institutional strengthening efforts will be deemed successful when the sector sustainability committee is operating under its regulatory instrument. The program's success will also be verified through an ex-post economic evaluation and a before-and-after analysis to confirm the realization of private benefits such as improved home environments and public health, and public benefits including environmental preservation and increased land values, as identified in the ex-ante cost-benefit analysis.

Targeted outcomes: Improve living standards, reduce pollution in Montevideo Bay/beaches (from 47 t DBO/day to 16 t DBO/day), expand sanitary sewer/storm drainage, increase adequately disposed sewage (from 2.3 m³/s to 3.9 m³/s dry season), enhance service management, clean streams/coast, make western beaches safe for swimming, and resettle at least 516 families. Success requires achieving over 90% connection rates, meeting pollution targets for Montevideo Bay and Cerro beach, preventing reoccupation of resettled areas, meeting a service sustainability target, and fulfilling all contractual conditions.

The activity targets improved quality of life in Ciudad de la Costa via increased sanitary sewer service, enhanced environmental quality (Arroyo Pando water safe for swimming, reduced fecal coliforms <2000 FCU/100ml, BOD5.20 max 10 mg/O2/L, ammoniac nitrogen max 10 mg N/L, total phosphorous <0.025 mg P/L), stormwater drainage, and street paving. Success requires 100% completion of WWTP/pumping stations, 5.7 km discharge outlets, 4.4 km pump line, 64.2 km sanitation networks by Year 4, 4,000 household connections, 0.875 m³/sec wastewater treated, plus established maintenance units, approved plans, evaluations, and legal/environmental compliance.

Targeted outcomes include reducing municipal solid waste to uncontrolled dumps from 1,100 to 0 tons/day, building 5 sanitary landfills, increasing separated waste to 4 tons/day, preparing 5 cost recovery studies, signing 3 league of municipios agreements, and 5 sustainability pledges. Success requires achieving these measurable indicators, demonstrating strong government commitment, implementing a comprehensive strategy (infrastructure and institutional strengthening), ensuring operational and economic sustainability through cost-recovery, and effectively managing environmental/social risks.

The targeted outcomes include protecting public health, improving Palestinian wellbeing, and achieving sustainable water management through treated wastewater reuse for irrigation in Rafah. It aims for efficient, solar-powered operation of the Rafah WWTP and post-treatment facilities, enhanced community awareness on wastewater reuse, and improvement of water/agriculture sectors. Economic bolstering (e.g., 13,000 jobs, US$ 84M annually from agriculture) and reduced public electricity load are also targeted. Overall, it seeks sustainable infrastructure/resource management and enhanced, secure access to natural resources with better environmental protection, alongside increasing Palestinians' access to basic services and strengthening climate change adaptation/mitigation capacities.

Targeted outcomes: strengthened rural economy, increased forest cover, improved rural women's living standards, sustainable industries/SMEs, growth in clean/modern biomass technology suppliers. Goals include converting 100,000 SMEs to modern biomass, 500,000 homes using cookstoves, increasing biomass power plants, establishing 15,000 ha fuelwood plantations (80-150k tons/year), 1,000 new entrepreneurs, 50 satellite centers, 10 biomass terminals, and achieving SLS 1551 certification. A 217 ktCO2e annual GHG reduction is targeted. Success requires increased investments in modern biomass technologies and a higher renewable energy percentage in the energy mix, alongside gender equality and environmental sustainability.

["Targeted outcomes include strengthening governance for biological corridors across 582.29M ha productive landscapes and 389,223 ha PAs, with a 10% METT improvement for 15 PAs and reducing their financial gap by 10%. It seeks legal recognition for at least one corridor, increasing decision-making participation to 177 structures. Environmental benefits include installing 2,500 ecological stoves to cut fuelwood/GHG emissions by 70%, capturing 470,601 tCO2-eq over 6,000 ha of coffee/cocoa agroforestry, establishing a national carbon market, protecting 6 key species, implementing sustainable practices on 8,000 ha of farms, declaring 30 water micro-basins, and reducing forest fires by 20%.", "Overall success means the primary objective of enhancing connectivity between PAs and productive landscapes in the arid-humid biological corridor is met. Key indicators include consolidating 971,752 ha of biological corridors, improving conservation in 14 PAs, capturing 470,601 tCO2-eq over 6,000 ha in agroforestry/reforestation, reducing forest fires by 20%, and cutting fuelwood/GHG emissions by 70%. Directly benefiting 16,103 individuals, along with sustained political will and stakeholder interest, is vital."]

The activity targets minimizing negative IAS impacts, conserving Turkey's native biodiversity, and ensuring marine/coastal ecosystem resilience. Specific outcomes include improved IAS management and enhanced ecosystem resilience across 94,800+ hectares (Igneada 34,200 ha, Marmara Islands 46,600 ha, Ayvalik 13,969 ha). Success requires an operational national IAS coordination mechanism, a partially implemented national marine IAS strategy, and implemented shipping sector regulations. Overall success hinges on private sector compliance with the Ballast Water Convention, government financial commitment, increased public awareness, improved data, fully implemented site-based management, and adequate project time/resources, with BWC requirements being manageable for regulation enforcement.

The activity targets by 2030: 75% electricity access (10.57M households); 75% modern cooking solutions (10.57M households); -2.6% energy intensity improvement (7.18 MTOE annual savings); 5% RE share in electricity (591 MW); 10% RE share in thermal uses (11.36M tons sustainable wood). It also aims for significant capacity building (e.g., 1000 trainers, 30k skilled laborers), job creation (over 500k), and an enabling investment environment (100 quality products, 50 RE industries). Success requires effective implementation, securing funding, and overcoming high RE costs & limited grid capacity.

Targeted outcomes: Strengthen Côte d'Ivoire's transparency and MRV capacities aligned with Paris Agreement, establish effective institutional arrangements, improve GHG inventories (2006 IPCC), design a national MRV system, and create an NDC progress monitoring tool. Enhance access to climate finance. Success hinges on overcoming limited institutional/technical capacity, external expertise reliance, poor data/methodology, lack of a centralized MRV system, and absence of systematic NDC review. Requires robust data management, online MRV platform, training, and MRV integration for long-term ownership.

["The activity targets improving Montenegro's institutional capacity for long-term adaptation planning, leading to increased resilience and lower climate vulnerability. Key outcomes include: strengthening adaptation planning governance, institutional coordination, and technical capacity through assessments and training for government, private sector, civil society, and IHSM staff; establishing an enhanced evidence base for gender-sensitive adaptation solutions via climate risk/vulnerability reviews in priority sectors (water, public health, agriculture, tourism) and drafting a gender-sensitive National Adaptation Plan; and developing an adaptation finance mobilization strategy.", "Overall success requires Montenegro to build an enabling institutional, planning, and programming environment for adaptation, enabling identification and addressing of climate-induced risks through adaptation investments. This depends on overcoming barriers like underperforming coordination and insufficient institutional capacity/information. Crucial assumptions for success include the GoM's active engagement, adequate core staffing, sustained support for adaptation planning, timely GCF fund disbursement, and availability/appropriate utilization of qualified consultants."]

The AIS Forum aims to establish a collaborative framework among archipelagic/island states for sustainable ocean use, climate change, marine plastic debris, and maritime governance. Outcomes include strengthening commitment, promoting integrated ocean governance, and facilitating R&D/human capital development, aligning with SDGs 5, 13, 14, 17; Indonesia's RPJMN. Success requires: AIS Secretariat operationalization, robust partner facilitation, 4 startup/women entrepreneur programs, Blue Economy Index for 4 countries, 5 joint research/student exchanges, 5 technical assistances, 5 partnership activities, and 3 global meeting participations.

Overall: Carbon-neutral, climate-resilient local development benefiting Protected Areas via Ecovillages. C1: Functional local governance; 80% conflict reduction; rural land cadaster, local land-use plan for 5 Ecovillages; multipurpose hall with internet, 310 waste bins, 72 latrines. C2: 15-20KW solar mini-central for clean energy, lighting, internet for all; water mastery; agro-silvo-pastoral production. C3: Community forest management; 1562 improved mud/metal stoves; 10 demonstration biodigesters; 2 artisans/Ecovillage trained for stoves/biodigesters.

Targeted outcomes: leveraging results-based payments for Costa Rica's REDD+ Strategy & Carbon Neutrality; channeling cash to indigenous peoples & women via PES for COVID-19 recovery & poverty reduction (SDG 1 & 5); protecting & managing 200,000+ forest hectares for carbon stock enhancement & fire prevention; fully operationalizing a Safeguards Information System; completing UNFCCC reporting to secure Green Climate Fund payments (for 14.9M tons CO2e reductions); and building international confidence in REDD+ results. Success requires: achieving hectare & emission targets, securing & distributing GCF payments without displacing local funds, robust independent verification of results and safeguards, sustained political will for carbon markets, and demonstrating quality & sustainability.

The activity targets strengthening Armenia's resilience to climate change. Outcomes include: installing 11 automated weather stations in Tavush, Gegharkunik, Syunik, integrated into a national network; enhancing hydrometeorological observation and early warning systems with one software solution; introducing one toolkit for strategic climate vulnerability and mudflow hazard risk assessment, including a GIS-based platform; updating two regional development plans with climate adaptation; and piloting adaptation/mitigation solutions (e.g., riverbank reinforcement) preventing 560 tons of carbon loss on 20 hectares. Success requires achieving these specific targets, ensuring all tools are transferred to HMC/WMC for sustained operation, and providing early warning to 505,445 local people.

The TuvaluTIPS Project targets enhancing trade and private sector capacities, improving the business environment, and promoting private investment in Tuvalu. Key outcomes include increased exports and employment/income in agriculture, fisheries, and tourism, creating decent jobs, and empowering youth, women, and vulnerable groups via green/blue economic policies and a business incubator. Success entails achieving revised policies, increased investments/exports, new green/blue businesses/jobs, poverty reduction in PICTs, and a functioning business incubator, aligning with Tuvalu's 'Te Kakeega III' goals.

The activity targets harmonized national/local legal frameworks, strengthened public/productive sector capacities in eco-transition, and implementation of eco-efficiency, circular economy, and decarbonization in value chains. It aims to strengthen sustainable value chains, improve market positioning, and create sustainable project portfolios with export potential via green financing. Further outcomes include designing viable green financial products/instruments, increasing access to sustainability-based financing, improving financial institution capabilities, and enabling small producers to access green credits.

Overarching Goal: Establish sustainable financing for coral conservation & reef resilience, facilitating coral-positive projects/businesses. Key Programmatic Outcomes: 1. Sustainable financing for Maldives MPA network. 2. Catalyzing coral-positive economy & gender-responsive livelihoods. 3. Increased coral R&D/monitoring capacity. Success requires: sustainably financed coral/ecosystem management, active community engagement, strengthened enforcement, increased coral health/fish abundance, growth in coral-positive businesses (1025 new jobs, USD16-30 income increase), and 40% women representation in SDFC loans/activities.

The activity targets restoring climate- and disaster-resilient electricity and education services to pre-Cyclone Ian levels in Ha'apai. Success requires: by end 2018, main electricity grid reconstructed, upgraded (6.6kV to 11.0kV), and supply resumed (100% network functioning by 2020), including 15.2km HV lines, 32km LV lines, 1000 households/30 commercial buildings reconnected, 161 streetlights, 100 solar lanterns, 2km underground cables; education services resumed in all rebuilt and climate-proofed schools (100% students in safe classes by 2020), including improved W&S, furniture, and rebuilt teacher quarters; and by Dec 2014, asbestos removed from damaged school, government, and residential buildings.

The targeted outcomes include developing a commercially viable low-carbon transport fuel system, generating new jobs, and reducing greenhouse gas emissions (approx. 2 million tons per annum by 2020). It also aims to diversify the PRC's energy mix, increasing natural gas use to 15.0% by 2025, and ensuring all major highways and inland waterways have access to natural gas stations by 2025. The project acts as a catalyst for private sector participation in the transport fuel sector.

["Targeted outcomes include: promoting environment-friendly transport; reducing environmental, health, and economic impacts from Bishkek's public transport. Outputs involve: upgrading to 120 zero-emission BEBs & charging infrastructure; enhancing bus depot infrastructure; establishing a pilot green mobility corridor (increasing bus speeds from 15 to 20 km/h); improving bus operations sustainability (10% op-ex reduction, 20% female drivers, 40% female technicians, 20% female training participants, 80% skilled in handling violence).", "Overall success requires: 10,800.000 tons/annum GHG reduction; $1.08M annual net energy import savings; $538M economic benefits ($86K health benefits); EIRR of 10.4% (above 9.0% benchmark); BTD FIRR 3.9% (above 3.3% WACC) with sustained subsidies; 120 BEBs deployed; bus speeds increase to 20 km/h on corridor; 10% op-ex reduction per vehicle-km; enhanced passenger demand; 20% female BEB drivers, 40% female technicians; 80% staff skilled in handling violence against women."]

The project targets several key outcomes: a 15% reduction in deforestation in Virunga Park by 2013; an increase in forest cover with 1,000 hectares of new micro-forests created by 2013; improved living conditions for forest dwellers, specifically a 10% increase in household income by 2013 and reduced poverty; and the establishment of foundational conditions for DRC to qualify for carbon market benefits. This includes making a forest biomass map available by 2012 and establishing a carbon stock determination and monitoring system by 2012. Additionally, it aims to reduce pressure on natural forests, conserve biodiversity, reduce greenhouse gas emissions, increase carbon stock, and create fulfilling jobs.

The targeted outcomes of the "Gabon - Support to Sustainable Forest Resources Management in Gabon" activity are broadly aimed at reducing deforestation and poverty in Gabon and the Congo Basin, while promoting sustainable forest management.Specifically, the project intends to achieve the following outcomes:Formulation of a Land Allocation Plan and inventory of national forest resources.Regeneration of protected forest tree species through plantation.Reforestation of degraded savannah areas located on forest edges.Development of community agroforestry in degraded areas to curb slash-and-burn farming.Overall, the project seeks to manage national forest resources sustainably and contribute to poverty reduction in Gabon.To consider the activity an overall success, the following specific targets would need to be met:At the impact level:The rural poverty rate in the Congo Basin declines from 75% in 2009 to 50% by 2025.The average rate of deforestation and forest degradation drops from 0.19% to 0.10% by 2020.The number of jobs in the forestry sector grows by 50% by 2017.At the outcome level:Income of target communities increases by 30% by 2013.A total of 6,000 ha of forest and savannah plantations are restored by 2013.A Land Allocation Plan and a comprehensive forest inventory report are produced by 2013.1,000 ha of forest plantations are restored by 2013.2,000 ha of savannah reforestation is achieved by 2013.3,000 ha of agroforestry plantations are created by 2013.Additionally, success would entail the establishment and operation of a geomatics laboratory (by 2012), mapping 22 million ha of forest territory and producing a zoning map (by 2013), training 500 planters (by 2013), producing 2 million seedlings (by 2012) for reforestation, and training 1,000 farmers (by 2012) while creating 2,400 ha of agroforestry plots (by end of 2013).

The activity targets expanding catalytic green finance in Africa, doubling its investment portfolio to 54 by 2030, mobilizing over £2bn capital for climate/development/inclusion, improving basic service access for 23M+ people/MSMEs, achieving 100% Paris-aligned investments, and reducing 4.6 MtCO2e. Success requires meeting 2025 targets: £244.25m additional finance, 50,000 jobs, 12.5M financial access, 1.94M basic services, £504m SME finance, 3.7 tCO2e reduction, 15 innovative intermediaries, and achieving 2030 goals.

["The activity targets the ultimate elimination of 100% of national HCFC consumption between 2022 and 2030, with an interim 75% reduction by 2025, eliminating 7.59 ODP tonnes of HCFC-22 and preventing 249,780 tonnes of CO2e emissions. Specific outcomes include improved control of ODS trade via training 200 customs officials and new import prohibitions; reduced HCFC consumption through training 780 RAC technicians in good practices, recovery, and recycling; installation of a national recycling center and R&R network; development of a national technician certification program; and increased public awareness.", "Overall success requires achieving 100% national HCFC consumption elimination by 2030 and the 75% reduction target by 2025 (7.59 ODP tonnes). This includes establishing fully functional import control regulations by January 1, 2025, an operational national R&R network and recycling center, and a robust national certification program for RAC technicians. Widespread public and industry adoption of good refrigeration practices and HCFC alternatives is crucial, along with meeting the HCFC consumption compliance indicator for broader climate change mitigation goals and demonstrating contributions to social development and gender equality."]

Targeted outcomes: Emergency maintenance to not cause customer outages. Total system shutdowns due to line trips to reduce from six (2017) to one per year. Line losses to decrease by 50%. SAIDI and SAIFI values to drop to 75 by project end, regularly achieving monthly minimums of 5.4 (SAIDI) and 6.12 (SAIFI). Overall success requires: No customer outages from emergency maintenance. System shutdowns reduced to one per year. 50% decrease in line losses. SAIDI and SAIFI dropping to 75, with monthly minimums of 5.4 (SAIDI) and 6.12 (SAIFI) achieved by reducing system shutdowns to one per year.

Targeted outcomes: improved DENR service delivery efficiency/effectiveness, enhanced ecosystem services globally/locally via integrated ecosystem management, strengthened DENR institutional reform (Rationalization Plan, EMB restructuring, M&E, MIS), increased client satisfaction, and effective management of protected areas in GEF sites. Success requires: full Reform Agenda implementation, 30% client satisfaction improvement by YR5, 80% investment targets met by YR5, effective management of 106,000 hectares in GEF sites by YR5, EMB restructured functionally by Dec 2009, MIS strategic plan approved/implemented by Dec 2007, and consistent covenant compliance.

The targeted outcomes of the Second Rwanda Urban Development Project are to improve access to basic services, enhance resilience, and strengthen integrated urban planning and management in the City of Kigali and six secondary cities of Rwanda. To be considered an overall success, the project aims to achieve the following: provide improved urban living conditions for 152,288 people (including 78,885 women); ensure 397,273 people in urban areas have access to all-season roads within a 500-meter range; see people benefiting from flood risk reduction and wetland rehabilitation interventions in Kigali; and have cities with detailed area plans that incorporate sustainability principles prepared and adopted. Success also hinges on effective institutional capacity development, gender mainstreaming with women's active participation in planning, and the successful implementation of comprehensive urban upgrading and flood risk management strategies.

The activity targets lasting food security and agriculture sector transformation in the poorest countries. By 2025, outcomes include creating 1.1M jobs, enabling 30+ countries to build sustainable/resilient food systems, increasing income by 20% for 10M farmers, achieving 25% yield increase for 10M farmers via climate-resilient practices, and ensuring all new projects integrate climate mitigation/adaptation. Success requires achieving these targets, demonstrating successful local currency investments for agri-food SMEs, and projects delivering climate co-benefits, nutrition, and women's benefits, ultimately improving incomes and food security for rural communities, aligning with SDGs 1 & 2.

Targeted outcomes include improving/expanding water/sanitation, reaching 1.5M more with drinking water, 1.4M more with sewerage by 2011, achieving 100% drinking water and 80% sewer coverage, plus improving AySA efficiency & controlling Rio Reconquista pollution. Success requires saving 127,000 m
³/day water, 3.1M m
³/day San Martín plant production, benefiting 414,000 for sewer and 185,000 for water system renovation, 100% AySA cost recovery, 85% collection efficiency, 18,000 new connections, PMOEM implementation.

The targeted outcomes include: improved government performance in disaster risk governance (measured by the number of government agencies benefiting from strengthened technological and managerial tools, and improvements in iGOPP Financial Protection and Risk Identification components); enhanced tourism (assessed by tourist satisfaction in Palapa Beach (Caye Caulker) and Goff's Caye); and mitigation of risk for residents (measured by the reduction in people affected by recurrent floods in the Orange Street area and Caye Caulker).

The program targets reducing Jamaica's debt by cutting fuel imports, with a primary goal of a 7.50 million barrels reduction in crude oil imports by EOP 2023. It aims for an annual electricity consumption reduction of 23.6 GWh in 80 HEPA government facilities, avoiding 34,675 tons of CO2. Additionally, it targets avoiding approximately 104 million litres of transport fuel annually and reducing travel time by 55 million hours, thereby avoiding 203,160 tons of CO2. Building capacity in the Ministry of Science, Energy and Technology to update its Integrated Resource Plan is also a key outcome.

Targeted outcomes: Ensure Salto Grande Hydropower Complex (CHSG) availability, enhance Argentina-Uruguay interconnection reliability/efficiency, extend CHSG useful life via modernization. Specific targets: Argentina renewable energy 45% by 2023, Uruguay 98% by 2023, avoid 199,416 TonCO2eq emissions by 2023. Other outcomes: improved operational performance, efficiency, flexibility, increased generation, reduced O&M, enhanced CTM institutional capacities (managerial, gender, digitalization). Success requires achieving all objectives and targets, successful infrastructure modernization, improved operational metrics, strengthened CTM capacities, and robust project management including satisfying pre-disbursement conditions, maintenance, and evaluations.

Targeted outcomes: enhanced community/ecosystem resilience to climate change, increased food/water security, reduced losses from extreme events. Success entails 75,000 households (50% female) adopting diverse livelihoods; 12,500 ha resilient agricultural land; 70% population accessing climate/early warning info via 40 systems. Also, restoring 64,370 ha wetlands, 11,630 ha catchment; 100% agricultural income increase for 50,500 households; 11,200 households in alternative livelihoods. Success hinges on government/community ownership, capacity, financial sustainability, and scaling.

The targeted outcomes include eliminating 5,000 TM of PCBs, 100 TM of obsolete pesticides, and 370 TM of mercury-contaminated waste, benefiting 1,793,700 people. Other outcomes are developing eight policies/standards, strengthening capacity for chemical management and monitoring, implementing a National Strategy for Hazardous Chemical Substances, updating national plans/inventories, sensitizing 4,800 people, and implementing a Gender Action Plan. Success would entail achieving these elimination targets, minimizing risks to human health/environment, fostering full compliance with Stockholm/Minamata Conventions, strengthening institutional/technical capacity, and ensuring sustainability.

Targeted outcomes: Enhance socio-ecological resilience in 7 landscapes via community actions, participatory planning, and sustainable production (agroecology, restoration). Contribute to Mexico's green economy, climate mitigation, energy efficiency, and biodiversity. Success criteria: Restore 2,500 ha land, 100,000 ha terrestrial/6,000 ha marine with best practices. Mitigate 296,878 tCO₂e, install 3.25 MW clean energy. Benefit 4,000 direct (50% women) and 16,000 indirect beneficiaries. Maintain alignment with national/international sustainable development.

The targeted outcomes of the "Plan eliminacion HCFC Fase III" activity are to: (1) achieve a 97.5% reduction in HCFC consumption by 2028 and eliminate 100% of HCFC consumption by 2030, aligning with the Montreal Protocol schedule. (2) Strengthen the legal and policy framework by developing a comprehensive legal framework for HCFC and HFC management, establishing safety standards for refrigerants and equipment, updating regulatory frameworks, and enhancing national systems for monitoring ODS imports/exports through an updated database. (3) Implement an HCFC elimination program in the refrigeration and air conditioning (RAC) sector by training technicians in good refrigeration practices and safety for flammable refrigerants (targeting 1,440 technicians in alternative refrigerant handling), equipping Recovery, Recycling, and Reuse (RRR) centers and professional technical training institutes, increasing refrigerant recovery and reuse rates, and providing equipment to technicians and RRR centers. This also includes establishing a mandatory technician certification system by 2025, promoting low-GWP technologies, and implementing pilot projects for alternative refrigerants like CO2 and ammonia. (4) Conduct awareness campaigns for end-users, technicians, and the general public to reduce HCFC use and promote low-GWP alternatives, supported by specialized public awareness materials. (5) Ensure effective project monitoring and implementation, with annual reporting of ODS consumption, and continuous updates to the registration of certified/licensed technicians, end-users, and HCFC-containing equipment.The activity would be considered an overall success if: (1) The Dominican Republic achieves the complete elimination of HCFC consumption by 2030, with national HCFC consumption (ODP tons) remaining the sole indicator of compliance. (2) All planned activities are executed effectively and synergistically. (3) The legal and policy framework is robust and actively enforced, including a functioning mandatory technician certification/licensing system by 2025 and updated regulatory standards. (4) The RAC sector demonstrates a significant transition to alternative refrigerants, evidenced by trained technicians, equipped RRR centers and training institutes, increased recovery/reuse rates, and successful adoption of low-GWP technologies through pilot projects. (5) There is widespread public and professional awareness of HCFC reduction and alternative refrigerants. (6) A sustainable reduction in HCFC demand is achieved through effective import/export control and the widespread adoption of alternatives, ensuring sustained compliance with Montreal Protocol obligations. (7) The allocated project funding of $2,689,920.00 is effectively utilized to achieve these objectives.

["The activity targets the strengthening of MINERD's institutional capacities in management, communication, indicator management, and document management, aiming to optimize ministerial processes.", "It seeks to enhance MINERD's logistical management for equipment delivery and sustainability, leveraging digitalization and fostering social cohesion within the educational community.", "A key educational outcome is improving the proportion of third-grade students achieving acceptable competence in reading (from 38% to 50%) and mathematics (from 28% to 40%).", "The project aims to implement the 'inglés para vivir mejor' platform to impact 85,000 public school students, providing tools for English language learning and aligning with the National Bilingualism Strategy.", "Outcomes include improving English linguistic competencies for secondary students and pre-university teachers, ensuring a digital English learning platform, and strengthening institutional data systems for language competency.", "It also targets developing English program implementation skills in 7,710 educational personnel by 2023 and achieving 80% completion of B2 level English training by 2025.", "The activity intends to expand the delivery of electronic devices to public education system users, with a goal of cloning and delivering 290,000 devices by 2023.", "It contributes to MCNUDS Effect 1 by strengthening the state's comprehensive response to poverty and inequality reduction through an inclusive, equitable, and sustainable public policy approach.", "Success would be measured by achieving the targeted 50% reading and 40% mathematics competence among third-grade students.", "Overall success also requires the implementation of 20 priority programs for timely decision-making by 2023, and a fully implemented national strategy for equipment repair support.", "The activity is successful if 290,000 electronic devices are delivered and cloned, and 7,710 educational personnel have developed skills for English program implementation by their respective target dates.", "Achieving 80% of participants completing B2 level English training by 2025 and demonstrating improved employment opportunities for pre-university graduates due to enhanced English skills are critical success factors.", "Success is further indicated by a clear pathway to outcome-level change, strong governmental commitment, and adaptive project management that effectively responds to new priorities like bilingualism."]

["Targeted outcomes: improved incomes/livelihoods, increased forest land productivity, reduced land degradation. Specifics include: 10% reduction in degraded forest land (to 3.15M ha by 2016); increased forest cover/density (Gansu 3%, Shaanxi 2%, Xinjiang 1% by 2016); protecting 32M tons carbon & sequestering 3.3 tons by 2016. Beneficiary household net income to increase by 30% (to CNY 2,080 by 2020); protected ecologically sensitive areas up by 130,000 ha (to 310,000 ha by 2020); rural employment up by 48,000 jobs by 2020. Economic tree crops on 38,000 ha to benefit 207,000 households, create 12,800 jobs/year, with 26 profitable enterprises & 368,600 tons carbon sequestration by 2016. Ecological forestry on 300,000 ha.", "Overall success requires: project implemented as designed; effective monitoring of all entities to sustain benefits. Assumptions for success include continued high demand for fruits/nuts and farmers' access to local government support services. Risks that must be effectively managed are: rapid economic growth dominating priorities or polluting the landscape; natural disasters (droughts/erosion); inadequate quality control standards affecting market access; unsecured forest land tenure contracts; and natural disasters disrupting input supply."]

Targeted outcomes include enhanced economic development and livability in aimag centers, improved urban governance, and expanded access to sustainable urban infrastructure and services. Success will be achieved through constructing and operationalizing modern 3,000 m³ capacity wastewater treatment plants in five aimag centers (Baruun-Urt, Bulgan City, Chinggis City, Mandalgovi, Murun) to meet Mongolian effluent standards, providing vacuum evacuation trucks for ger area sanitation, and strengthening strategic planning, management, and cost recovery for wastewater services. Key indicators for success also include 95-100% water/sewerage coverage, 95% ger area water kiosk access, 95-100% service operator revenue recovery, 20% reduction in women's water access time, 12,000 m³/day treated wastewater discharge, and positive PUSO finances.

Improved livelihoods and increased supply of clean, renewable electricity for households in Espiritu Santo and Malekula, enhancing energy access and generation by replacing diesel with hydropower and extending grids. Overall success requires: Construction of a 400kW Brenwe Hydropower Plant supplying >90% of Malekula's grid energy by 2040; extending 79km of distribution lines to connect ~1,050 new households (raising access to 29% in Espiritu Santo, 14% in Malekula); achieving 600 tons/annum CO2 reduction; ensuring economic viability (EIRR 14.9%, ENPV $5.9M); and implementing effective poverty reduction and gender mainstreaming measures.

The Navoi Solar Power Project targets several outcomes, primarily aiming to increase the share of renewable energy sources in Uzbekistan's energy mix and improve electricity access through new generation capacities. Specifically, it intends to deliver an increased amount of renewable power to the domestic grid, generating at least 258.2 gigawatt-hours of clean energy per annum and contributing to the avoidance of 157,502 tons of carbon dioxide emissions annually. The project also seeks to boost local employment and contribute to the local economy through domestic purchases. Furthermore, it aims to enhance basic rural infrastructure for women in nearby communities and promote gender equality within "Nur Navoi Solar" Foreign Enterprise Limited Liability Company (NNS) operations, serving as a landmark transaction to catalyze broader private sector participation in Uzbekistan's renewable energy sector and mitigate climate change. For the activity to be considered an overall success, the following specific targets must be met: By 2023, the project must deliver at least 258.2 gigawatt-hours of renewable electricity per year to the off-taker, and achieve an annual avoidance of at least 157,502 tons of carbon dioxide emissions. The total installed electricity generation capacity of the solar power plant must increase to 100 megawatts by 2022. During its operation, at least 22 jobs must be provided by 2023, with 3 designated for women. During the construction phase, at least 900 jobs should be provided by 2022, with 15 for women. Annual domestic purchases during operation should amount to at least $150,000. Regarding gender equality in NNS operations, dedicated facilities for women employees (separate toilets, rest rooms, and/or water points) must be provided at the project site by 2022. By 2023, an anti-sexual harassment policy with a complaints and response mechanism must be developed and implemented. By 2025, annual training on a gender-sensitive and respectful work environment, including zero tolerance for sexual harassment and gender-based violence, must be provided to all staff and contractors during both construction and operation. Lastly, a corporate social responsibility program that includes specific initiatives to support women and girls in renewable energy must be developed and implemented by 2025. Meeting these quantifiable targets will signify the project's overall success in energy, economic, and social development.

Targeted outcomes by 2026: expanded climate-resilient, smart urban water management in PRC; $40M flood mitigation investment; 2M more people with improved urban water services; 100M m³/year increased water production; 40M m³/year increased wastewater treatment; 10 female employees promoted to mid/high-level management at SZWG. Success by 2025: one flood-risk-reduced sponge city subproject; 400,000 m³/day added/improved smart water supply; 200,000 m³/day added/improved smart wastewater treatment. By 2023: one knowledge product on women's climate resilience; 50 participants (30% women) in knowledge-sharing on smart water/sponge city tech; improved SZWG HR policy for women's recruitment/promotion; 35% female staff in professional training.

The targeted outcomes of the Bahia Poor Urban Areas Integrated Development activity are centered on sustainably reducing urban poverty for the poorest and most vulnerable populations in Salvador and other strategic cities in Bahia. This is to be achieved by providing access to basic services, improved housing, and social support services. More specifically, the activity aims to: 1. Enhance the quality of life and asset base for the poor through the provision of basic infrastructure services, improved housing conditions, access to urban land, and the regularization of land tenure and buildings in informal settlements. This includes improvements in urban drainage, roads, street paving, water supply, sewage, solid waste management, and the reduction of risks from flooding, landslides, and living in risk-prone areas, along with increased access to recreational activities and formality. 2. Improve access to social programs such as income and employment generation, direct support for family survival strategies (like daycare), healthcare, education, job training, and initiatives to enhance community security at the local settlement level, fostering increased social capital. 3. Strengthen the capacity of state and local governments to plan, implement, monitor, and evaluate integrated urban poverty reduction strategies, manage urban upgrading and land delivery, coordinate social policy through participatory planning, and track government programs using poverty and quality of life indicators, ensuring sustainability through improved cost recovery.To consider the activity an overall success, the following specific indicators must be attained: improvement in access to basic infrastructure, services, and housing conditions for 30,000 households in informal urban settlements across 8 municipalities. This includes improvements to 3,400 houses in Salvador and 2,600 in the Interior, and a reduction of 600 housing units in risk areas in Salvador and 1,300 in the Interior. Additionally, the quality of living conditions (IVSU) for target communities should improve to 47.37 in Salvador (from 43.44) and 44.29 in the Interior (from 42.10). There should be a 30% reduction in water-borne or sanitation-related illness among pre-school age children in Salvador and a 35% reduction in the Interior. Finally, 50% of properties in project areas in both Salvador and the Interior should have regularized land tenure.

Successful project implementation requires all 6 water utilities and 878 other service providers to be supported; 100% SVS construction with GP participation; 100% MVS construction/management with ZP participation; 50% of participating GPs meeting full O&M costs via user charges, and 90% contributing towards O&M costs.", "Overall success also includes ensuring 90% of constructed SVS/MVS yield required water quality/quantity, and 100% of commissioned schemes embed source sustainability. Furthermore, at least 2.1 million rural populations and 400 tribal habitations must be directly benefited, and capacity building activities undertaken as per strategy (100% compliance) with annual fund utilization within 10% shortfall of estimates."]}

Targeted outcomes: Rural households gain broader access to sustainable agricultural, forestry, and natural resource management services/innovations, stimulating higher agricultural productivity (15% average increase in indices); INTA's technology portfolio grows by 42 validated technologies (export, food safety, environment); 50% of 50,400 farmers adopt at least two new technologies; agricultural public sector institutions (INTA, INAFOR, FUNICA, MAGFOR) are strengthened for better technology generation/transfer/policy. Success entails achieving these targets, 80% stakeholder satisfaction, MAGFOR's M&E unit fully operational, and 100% of project-related salaries/operating costs domestically financed by 2010, demonstrating sustainable institutional capacity and service delivery.

Targeted outcomes aim to improve reliability, efficiency, and access to modern energy services in Benin, and reduce global greenhouse gas emissions. Overall success requires achieving: a reduction of power losses in transmission/distribution networks to 24 GWh (from 51 GWh baseline); 400,000 additional people with improved access to modern energy and electricity services; and Benin's transmission network responding to (N-1) criteria. Key infrastructure achievements include constructing 310 km of transmission lines, ensuring distribution-level voltage fluctuations below 7%, and reducing peak load/energy savings by 9.7 MW/14 GWh.

The targeted outcomes of the PE Optimization of Lima Water and Sewerage Systems activity are to improve the efficiency, continuity, and reliability of water supply and sanitation services in the Northern Service Area of Lima. To consider the activity an overall success, there would need to be an increase in the number of daily hours water services are provided, a decrease in the annual incidence of water pipe breakages and sewerage blockages per km of their respective networks, an increase in the volume of water saved, and an increased number of learning events on strategic planning and management.

The targeted outcomes of the Jiangxi Wuxikou Integrated Flood Management Project are to reduce the flood risk in the central urban area of Jingdezhen City through the implementation of priority structural and non-structural measures, and to contribute to the establishment of an integrated flood risk management system for the City. Specific beneficiaries include 480,000 people in the central urban areas of Jingdezhen City, who will see reduced flood risk. The entire city will benefit from clean energy generated by the Wuxikou hydropower plant and a more reliable water supply. Additionally, the ecosystem and communities downstream of the Wuxikou dam will benefit from an assured environmental flow. The project also aims to provide necessary flood protection to Jingdezhen City from a 50-year flood event, complementing the existing City Dike System to upgrade flood protection from a 20-year to a 50-year flood event. This includes non-structural measures such as flood forecasting, early warning systems, flood risk zoning, management information dissemination, coordinated operations, emergency preparedness, disaster assessment, public awareness, education, and capacity building.

To consider the activity an overall success, the following specific outcomes would need to be achieved:

1.  A significant reduction in the percentage of the urban population vulnerable to one-in-50-year floods, with a target of 80% protection by 2019.
2.  An increase in the flood protection level for Jingdezhen City from a 20-year flood standard to a 50-year flood standard by 2019.
3.  A substantial reduction in the total area subject to inundation by one-in-50-year floods, from a baseline of 35.58 km 2  to a target of 3.56 km 2  by 2019.
4.  Successful construction and operation of the Wuxikou Flood Control Scheme, including the dam, hydropower plant (32 MW total installed capacity, 81,210 MWh annual generation), and associated electro-mechanical equipment.
5.  Establishment and effective functioning of an integrated flood risk management system for Jingdezhen City, which includes a master plan, a real-time flood forecasting and early warning decision support system, and a municipal flood control and dispatching center.
6.  Successful implementation of awareness-raising, community engagement activities, and specialized technical training for integrated flood risk management.
7.  Provision of a minimum environmental flow of 15 m 3 /s and water supply.
8.  Approval of the Operation & Maintenance Plan for Wuxikou Flood Control Scheme by the Jingdezhen municipal government.

["The activity targets improving the quality of life for Educandos-Quarenta watershed residents, aiming for a 5% increase in the Human Development Index (HDI) for the watershed and a 2% increase for Manaus city three years post-program. Immediate outcomes by program end are: a 50% reduction in fecal coliform, a 100% increase in dissolved oxygen, and a 60% decrease in biochemical oxygen demand in the igarapés; a 36,000-person decrease in the population affected by floods; and 5,000 monthly visitors to new parks. Additionally, it aims to improve environmental and health conditions (drainage, water supply, waste management, remediation), enhance housing conditions (land use planning, titling, housing, recreational areas, education), and build operational/management capacity with community participation.", "Overall success hinges on critical assumptions: the government maintains credibility with the community, and the city retains control over land use and settlement. It is also crucial for success that there is ongoing monitoring of financial management, procurement, and the timely implementation of the new information system. Integrating lessons learned and strengthened institutional capacity, as identified from past challenges, are positive indicators and essential for achieving objectives."]

["The activity aims for conservation, sustainable use, and socioenvironmental recovery of the Serra do Mar State Park, Juréia-Itatins territory, and marine conservation units (CUs). Key outcomes include improved protection and management capacity of CUs, establishment of a botanical garden, reduced human impact through resettlement of 6,750 families with improved living standards, consolidation of urban hubs for 2,410 families, and improved monitoring/inspection systems. It also targets restoration/enhancement of 340 ha of degraded areas, including 80 ha recovered from residential use and 100 ha cleared of exotic species, alongside defined management plans for marine CUs.", "Overall success requires CUs (PESM, Juréia-Itatins) to have signed boundaries, standardized land tenure regularization. 80 ha of residential-degraded land must be recovered, with 120 ha allocated for the botanical garden. 100 ha cleared of exotic species and 350 ha enhanced from 3,000 ha total. Marine CUs need defined management plans, boards, and programs. 6,750 families must be resettled with improved living conditions; 2,410 families' urban hubs consolidated, and a plan for 12,000 families in phase two developed. The PMA must possess a georeferenced analysis system and operational inspection capacity, alongside improved CUs infrastructure and proven voluntary resettlement methodologies."]

Targeted outcomes include increasing investment in renewable energy generation projects (with targets of $7,068 million US$ total from REFF and $1,215 million US$ from third-party co-financing), familiarizing the Mexican banking system with such investments, increasing power generation from renewable sources, and reducing greenhouse gas emissions. Specific output targets are 8 renewable energy generating plants built with REFF financing and an installed capacity of 3,394 MW from REFF-financed plants. The total generation capacity from RE, accounting for REFF investments and demonstration effects, targets 5,282 MW. Overall success requires achieving these investment and capacity targets, demonstrating increased annual power generation from renewable sources and a reduction in CO2 equivalent emissions, enhancing the financial sector's capacity for renewable energy investments, and meeting the specified minimum leveraging levels (CTF matched by IDB, and NAFIN at least equal to CTF).

The Comprehensive Development Project For Water and Sanitation Utilities (PRODI) aims to support Water and Sanitation Utilities (OOs) serving populations between 50,000 and 900,000 inhabitants in their transformation to improve service quality and ensure operational and financial sustainability. The project targets addressing critical deficiencies, including high non-revenue water (ANC) rates (30% to 50%), improving customer service, achieving environmental compliance, ensuring continuous high-quality water supply, and reducing operating costs.Specific targeted outcomes by 2020 include: 1) Increasing households with upgraded access to drinking water (service more than 10 hours/day) from a baseline of 4,000,000 in 2015 to 4,238,000. 2) Raising the percentage of households with upgraded access to drinking water (service more than 10 hours/day) from 40% in 2015 to 42%. 3) Increasing the number of utilities whose staff participate in skills development events from 0 in 2015 to 60. 4) Improving utilities' annual commercial efficiency (water paid/water billed) from 50% in 2013 to over 70%. 5) Reducing the percentage of non-revenue water (Water produced - water billed)/water produced) from 45% in 2013 to below 40%, with an explicit goal to achieve a 50% reduction in physical losses and an 80% reduction in commercial losses for non-revenue water as part of the plan.To consider the activity an overall success, the following would be needed: 1) The transformation of OOs must lead to improved quality of drinking water provision and demonstrated operational and financial sustainability. 2) The specific quantitative targets for household water access, staff training, commercial efficiency, and non-revenue water reduction must be met as verified by final evaluation reports. 3) OOs must successfully develop and have their Integral Development Plans (PDIs) validated by CONAGUA, structuring investments for financial profitability and sustainability, with annual and stage-gate reviews confirming progress. 4) The data definitions and indicators must be refined through pilot projects, and OOs should achieve certification in the AquaRating tool. 5) A robust monitoring and follow-up system for projects must be established and effectively track technical, operational, and normative performance. 6) Consultants must be successfully trained and certified in areas such as corporate governance, tariffs, and transparency. 7) Eligible activities funded by PRODI must align with strategic objectives of increasing revenue, reducing costs, reducing physical losses, and achieving structural improvements within the OOs. 8) The project must align with and contribute to national priorities, such as the National Water Plan 2013-2018, regarding water security and improved service quality. 9) All eligible OOs must have a validated PDI, a signed PRODI Agreement with CONAGUA, and annually submit documented management indicators.

The targeted outcomes of the Shanghai Urban Environment APL Phase 2 are to expand Shanghai's environmental management agenda in the metropolitan area through priority investments, enhanced financial architecture for urban environmental infrastructure, and policy/institutional reforms. This includes: improving water safety/quality for 6.4 million residents (200k in Xujing); managing NW Shanghai wastewater (80% treated to standard, 80% sludge treated); improving sanitary standards by safely closing 2 solid waste sites; supporting environmental infrastructure in suburban districts via DFV (8 agreements, 6 projects); and building capacity (100% IST activities completed). Success requires: evidence of improved effectiveness in operations, management, and finances of urban environmental utilities, increased self-financing of capital, district participation in metropolitan measures, and effective resettlement management according to RAPs.

Targeted outcomes include reducing water pollution in the Pearl River Delta from Foshan and Jiangmen. This involves expanding wastewater treatment (Foshan Zhen'an by 50,000m3/d; Jiangmen Wen Cheng Sha to 200,000m3/d), constructing sludge facilities (Foshan 400t/day), improving river quality (dredging/restoration), and enhancing flood protection. Success requires achieving specific industrial compliance rates (Foshan: 95% by 2010; Jiangmen: >95%), effective operational capacity increases, and demonstrable reduction in water pollution as a result of implemented initiatives and institutional strengthening.

The specific objectives for the Municipality of Uberaba are to promote better living conditions for all residents through integrated urban sanitation, environmental preservation, and expanded parks, recreational, and cultural opportunities. To be considered an overall success, the activity needs to: 1. Guarantee adequate water supply while maintaining Uberaba River flow at minimum environmental levels. 2. Improve the Water Quality Index of the Uberaba River. 3. Achieve citizen understanding of environmental problems and satisfaction with environmental services (Water Supply & Sanitation, drainage, parks) as measured by a survey.

The targeted outcome of the RY-Rural Energy Access Project is to improve electricity access of rural populations in the selected Project Areas. Initially, it also aimed to demonstrate the feasibility of increasing electricity access for rural households in off-grid areas through Solar Home Systems (SHS), but this was later streamlined. For the activity to be considered an overall success, it would need to achieve approximately 107,000 new on-grid connections (with IDA contributing to 24,000) and about 18,000 new off-grid connections via SHS. Success also depends on strengthening institutional capacity for rural electricity delivery, including Rural Electricity Service Providers (RESPs) and micro-finance institutions, building capacity within the Rural Electrification Authority (REA), designing and implementing a financially sustainable tariff framework for RESPs, covering operational costs for the Project Management Unit (PMU) and REA/RESPs, conducting rural energy studies, and developing an LPG supply strategy for rural populations. The establishment and maintenance of functional RESPs by September 30, 2014, and the appointment of an independent consultant for an Environmental and Social Management Plan (ESMP) implementation review report by the same date are also critical requirements. Ultimately, it contributes to the broader National Rural Electrification Strategy goal of increasing rural access from 20% to 46% (520,000 new RHH) over a ten-year, three-phase period, benefiting over 3.5 million people.

The Power Transmission Project in Support of the Energy Sector Reform & Development Program targets several outcomes. The primary objective is to enhance the security, reliability, efficiency, and quality of power supply through the rehabilitation of transmission substations and the strengthening of the power transmission network. A secondary aim is to bolster the institutional capacity and technical capabilities of Ukrenergo (UE), the transmission system operator, to ensure the secure and reliable operation of the high voltage power grid, thereby facilitating the unimpeded functioning and expansion of the electricity market. This also includes supporting market reforms in the energy sector through legal, institutional, regulatory, and technical harmonization with the EU Internal Energy Market, and supporting sustainable economic development.Key targeted outcomes include: progress in implementing the Action Plan for the Energy Sector Reform and Development, including adopting the Electricity Grid Code and gradually opening the power market; reduction of Energy Not Served to consumers by at least 35 GWh per year; reduction of transmission losses by 14 MW; and improved voltage quality in transmission substations included in the project.To consider the activity an overall success by the project's conclusion, in comparison to 2005 parameters, the following key performance indicators must be met: reduction of maintenance and repair costs by US$ 0.8 million annually, reduction of Energy Not Served by 35 GWh per year, and minimization of transmission losses and peak load capacity requirements by 14 MW. Additionally, the average voltage drop at 330 kV and 220 kV substations included in the project should be reduced to less than 5% of the nominal voltage. Broader success indicators include a 10% reduction in Ukraine's energy intensity (use of energy per unit of GDP) compared to 2005, an increasing number of eligible electricity consumers free to choose their electricity supplier, Ukraine's improved preparedness to meet UCTE requirements, and UE's organization and operating practices being compatible with TSOs in the EU Internal Electricity Market.

The targeted outcomes are: a minimum 10% improvement in local tourism services and cultural heritage protection over the 2007 baseline; and sustainable provision of basic water, wastewater, and solid waste services to 150,000 people (100,000 improved, 50,000 new). Broader goals include protecting Lake Titicaca's water quality and ecological integrity, increasing social inclusion, improving quality of life, and boosting tourist arrivals. Overall success requires achieving these targets, complementary investments from other donors/Peru/private sector, a shared regional vision, sustainable service provision (user fees covering O&M), and annual 'State of the Lake' reports by Bolivia/Peru to monitor long-term trends.

["The targeted outcomes for the Electricity Expansion activity are twofold: (a) to increase access to electricity in urban, peri-urban, and rural areas, and (b) to improve the efficiency, reliability, and quality of service to consumers. An additional objective is to sustain the policy, institutional, and regulatory environment needed for these results to materialize. Alternatively, objectives include increasing the capacity, efficiency, and quality of electricity supply and expanding electricity access.", "To consider the activity an overall success, the following key outcome indicators must be met: the number of people and enterprises (including agri-business projects) provided with access to electricity, the number of rural public institutions with access, electricity generation from renewable geothermal capacity (GWh), average power system interruption frequency per year, electricity transmission and distribution losses per year (%), and the number of direct project beneficiaries (including percentage of females)."]

The primary targeted outcomes are to improve the coverage, quality, efficiency, and financial sustainability of water and sanitation services in selected urban and rural communities. This includes creating a National Water Facility (NWF) to channel national and external financing programmatically, and supporting high-priority investments. Additionally, the project aims to enhance the capacity of the Ministry for Construction and Territorial Development (MCTD) to prepare and supervise investment programs, provide technical assistance to operating Apa Canals, and increase energy efficiency in urban water and wastewater systems.

The Syrdarya Water Supply Project targets improved availability, quality, and sustainability of public water supply services in selected rayons of the Syrdarya Region. Success is defined by: 280,099 people gaining 24hr/day water access, 98% potable water samples, 46,683 metered households, an improved financial working ratio (0.9) for SVK, reduced pipe breaks, improved energy efficiency (0.69 kWh/m³/yr), and established institutional capacity for system maintenance and user fee collection sufficient to cover cash O&M costs.

The activity aims to increase energy efficiency and security, reduce end-user energy costs, decrease reliance on imported petroleum products by diversifying the energy matrix with renewables and LNG, strengthen regulatory frameworks, and reduce GHG emissions. Success requires measurable reductions in energy costs (electricity, SMEs), a significant increase in renewable energy and LNG share, decreased imported oil dependence, new private sector investments in generation and EE, reduction in GHG emissions, and a coherent regulatory environment from the Government of Jamaica.

The Tarbela Fourth Extension Hydropower Project aims to facilitate a sustainable expansion in Pakistan's electricity generation capacity, strengthen WAPDA's capacity, and provide approximately 3,840 GWh annually of least-cost, low-carbon renewable energy by installing a 1,410 MW hydropower plant. This will reduce electricity generation costs by at least 2.3%, alleviate financial deficit, save foreign exchange, and abate greenhouse gas emissions. Overall success requires: supplying ~3,840 GWh renewable energy annually; ensuring 1,410 MW additional capacity during summer peak; reducing overall energy production cost by 2.3%; preparing a large Indus River hydropower project; and successfully completing WAPDA's capacity building program.

The Moldova Agriculture Competitiveness Project aims to enhance the competitiveness of the agro-food sector by modernizing the food safety management system, facilitating market access for farmers, and mainstreaming agro-environmental and sustainable land management practices. Key outcomes include the completion of targeted food safety actions for approximation to EU SPS requirements, increased sales of high-value crops by targeted partnerships, increased on-farm area benefiting from sustainable land management practices, and increased area protected by robust anti-erosion shelterbelts. The activity will be considered an overall success if 100% of targeted food safety actions are completed, sales of high-value crops increase by 50% for targeted partnerships, 10,000 hectares benefit from sustainable land management, and 50,000 hectares are protected by rehabilitated anti-erosion shelterbelts.

["The activity targets the rapid restoration and improvement of electricity and water supply services in Bissau.
