This involves rehabilitating/improving water supply networks, converting to groundwater, installing iron removal stations, and water meters. For wastewater, outcomes include rehabilitation/reconstruction of treatment plants, pumping stations, sludge dewatering, collection networks, and modern monitoring equipment. The project also aims to strengthen the efficiency, governance, and sustainability of service provision, reduce water demand by eliminating iron, reducing leakage, and replacing deteriorating networks, and improve operational efficiency, especially energy consumption. Broader outcomes include addressing pollution, inefficient water use, natural groundwater contamination, and Chernobyl's effects. For overall success, the project must achieve its objective of improved service quality, efficiency, and sustainability, as measured by key performance indicators: improved water quality, energy conservation, and improved wastewater discharge quality. Successful implementation of all components, mitigation of critical risks, and adherence to safeguard policies are essential.

The targeted outcomes of the Solid Waste Management Program Project are to improve the quality and coverage of integrated solid waste management (SWM) services across Colombia. Specifically, a key outcome is ensuring that waste generated by approximately 300,000 consumers is adequately disposed of in sanitary landfills constructed under the Project. Other outcomes include the development and investment in SWM disposal systems (e.g., construction/expansion of landfills, transfer stations, closing non-sanitary dumps), institutional strengthening (e.g., enhancing regional SWM planning, capacity building for regulatory/tariff application, developing a national recycling strategy), and financing technical advisory services for SWM investments, social inclusion programs for waste pickers, and resettlement/compensation costs.The activity would be considered an overall success if the waste generated by approximately 300,000 consumers is adequately disposed of in project-built sanitary landfills. Success also hinges on effectively implementing project components, which includes developing robust SWM infrastructure, strengthening institutional capacities, and integrating social safeguards for affected populations, all while adhering to established technical, financial, economic, social, and environmental guidelines and eligibility criteria for subprojects.

The targeted outcomes of the Peru First Programmatic Environmental Development Policy Loan (ENVDPL 1) are primarily focused on two overarching objectives: (i) improving the efficiency and effectiveness of environmental governance and institutions in Peru, and (ii) mainstreaming sustainability principles in the development agenda of key sectors, specifically improving the management of the mining sector, air quality, and the fisheries sector.Key actions include strengthening the legal and institutional framework for environmental management through the Ministry of Environment (MINAM), supporting biodiversity conservation by establishing functions for the National Service of Natural Protected Areas (SERNANP), and strengthening environmental quality standards and monitoring. In the mining sector, actions aim to improve information and capacity for remediating environmental liabilities and strengthening environmental/social governance. For urban air quality, the program supports improving fuel quality (e.g., vehicle conversion to natural gas, reduced sulfur in diesel) and enhancing vehicle inspection/maintenance systems. In the fisheries sector, it aims to reduce overcapacity in the anchoveta fleet and mitigate social impacts.For the activity to be considered an overall success, the following specific outcomes would need to be achieved: (i) MINAM reviews Environmental Impact Assessments (EIAs) for at least 10 large projects annually; (ii) SERNANP applies a financial strategy to increase funding for protected areas by at least US$2 million per year; (iii) air quality data for Lima-Callao is widely published in real-time, and contingency plans are developed/implemented in the 5 most polluted cities; (iv) at least ten priority mining environmental legacies (MELs) are identified and resources confirmed for remediation, with environmental participatory monitoring in at least 60 mining sites; (v) at least 80,000 vehicles converted to Natural Gas, 90 NG service stations operating in Lima, 30% of gas stations supplying clean diesel by 2010, and vehicle inspection/maintenance systems operating in Lima and the three largest cities; (vi) 100% of the anchoveta fleet operates under a quota system; and (vii) at least 5,000 workers benefit from economic incentives for leaving commercial fishing.

The activity targets increasing sustainable access to infrastructure and urban services in Bangui's most deprived districts by rapidly rehabilitating, restoring, improving, and expanding water supply, drainage, solid waste management, and urban roads. It aims to reduce poverty via short-term employment and improved living conditions, and to foster social and political stability by demonstrating visible improvements in citizens' lives. Furthermore, it seeks to strengthen the institutional capacity of GoCAR and service providers for sustainable urban service delivery and program management.

The activity aims to improve agricultural production in water-logged areas and mitigate damage to housing and infrastructure from rising groundwater and salinity. Overall success will be achieved through a reduced groundwater table, increased crop yields for major crops (e.g., cotton, wheat, grapes, fruit crops, vegetables), and a decrease in flooded settlement areas. Specific targets include a 2.00m decrease in groundwater level and a 40%+ increase in crop yields (e.g., cotton baseline 2.4 to 40+, wheat 3.5 to 40+, grapes 12.5 to 40+, private fruit 3.0 to 40+, dehkhan fruit 6.0 to 40+, vegetables 20.0 to 40+) by year 7 and beyond. Additionally, the project aims for 3,400 km of rehabilitated irrigation canals and drains, an increase in drainage water discharge to 13 m³/sec, and 1,000 farmers adopting new practices in sustainable agriculture and water resource management.

The activity targets increasing access to basic socioeconomic services and sustainable urban infrastructures, generating short-term employment, strengthening municipal management and fiscal performance, and enhancing target cities as economic growth poles. It also aims to strengthen institutional partners and the local construction sector. Success hinges on increased access to critical infrastructure (roads, water, sanitation, schools, health centers, income-generating facilities), improved municipal infrastructure maintenance (budget allocation/execution), and increased population access to all-season roads, markets, schools, and health facilities. Effective risk mitigation and beneficiary contributions are also crucial.

The targeted outcomes of the MINIS project are to facilitate investments in infrastructure supporting mining and downstream processing, and to build local capacity to prepare and transact infrastructure projects. Overall success requires: at least two infrastructure assets ready for tender; the establishment and adoption of good practices and procedures for preparing projects for public and private financing; and the effective transfer of knowledge leading to the uptake and implementation of prepared infrastructure projects by various funding sources.

Targeted outcomes include institutionalizing sustainable coral reef co-management via improved local governance, livelihoods development, and knowledge sharing. Specific targets: 20% coral reefs as no-take zones in 8 districts by 2018; increased live coral cover (optimal in 70% sites); significant biomass growth of groupers/wrasses; improved management effectiveness in 8 national MCAs (13 MCAs reaching Yellow/Green/Blue status); 15% net income increase for beneficiaries; successful eco-business in 4 districts with 50% commercially viable enterprises. Success requires achieving these biophysical and socio-economic targets, alongside a declining trend in destructive fishing and operational zoning in all 8 project districts.

The activity targets: bridging the power sector's financial gap sustainably, developing gas supplies and new power generation (at least 700 MW) via private investment for efficient gas-fired plants, and establishing robust policy/institutional frameworks for natural gas management to maximize national benefits. Success entails: improved power sector financial sustainability and transparency, enhanced TANESCO efficiency (reduced losses, increased revenue), strengthened sector governance, implementation of cost-effective projects, a developed legal/institutional framework for gas, a Natural Gas Utilization Master Plan, conducive investment environment, and a significant decline in power production costs due to gas supply. Ultimately, this leads to poverty reduction, improved affordable energy access (boosting the 18% connection rate), and diversified, less vulnerable power sources.

The Piaui Productive and Social Inclusion DPL aims to support the Government of Piaui (GoPi) in promoting sustainable growth and social inclusion. The targeted outcomes are structured around three pillars: Pillar 1 focuses on promoting sustainable growth in rural areas through improved land tenure security, employment and income growth for small-scale producers, sustainable agriculture, water resources management, and rural fire prevention. Specific targets include land-tenure regularization for small-scale producers and 300,000 acres for medium and large producers, establishing an integrated system for regularization, increasing rural employment and income by benefiting an additional 2,000 families through the PROGERE program, creating a State Plan of Low Carbon Agriculture (ABC-PI) to reduce GHG emissions (targeting CO2 emissions reduction for 2014 compared to a baseline), establishing a "State Registry of Water Users" (CEARH) for improved water management, and implementing institutional reforms to combat rural fires. Pillar 2 aims to promote social inclusion by enhancing education and employment opportunities for youth and improving health conditions for vulnerable populations. Specific targets include reducing school drop-out and repetition in secondary education by increasing municipality enrollment in the "More Life Scholarship" (PBMV) program from 44 to 88 by 2017, benefiting an additional 10,500 students, and establishing a "State Unit for the Combat of Neglected Diseases" (NECDN) to address prevalent poverty-linked diseases. Pillar 3 seeks to enhance efficiency in public management by consolidating the Strategic Actions Monitoring System (SIMO) as State policy and extending its project monitoring coverage to all activities in the State's Multi-Annual Plan 2012–2015 (PPA). The activity would be considered an overall success if these specific targets are met, leading to boosted shared prosperity, alleviated poverty (especially in rural Piaui and among vulnerable groups like quilombola communities through land tenure titles and participatory processes), reduced gender inequalities (by issuing land titles in the name of both spouses), supported sustainable practices, efficient natural resource use, and an improved capacity of GoPi to manage water resources and promote social inclusion through health, education, employment, and income generation.

Targeted outcomes: 1.5M natural gas household connections; US$406M annual gov savings from reduced LPG imports; gas leaks below 5 per 1000 consumers; 6.75M direct beneficiaries (50% female, incl. financial support for poor). Success is achieving these, establishing a Gas Regulator, reducing LPG subsidy fiscal deficit, & ensuring gas sector sustainability through reforms.

The targeted outcomes are to strengthen domestic resource mobilization and efficiency, enhance allocative efficiency of public spending in the education sector, and improve fiscal risk management from the electricity sector and PPPs. Success would be indicated by: taxpaying firms/individuals increasing to 105,000 (from 86,945); VAT revenue rising to 2.0% of GDP (from 1.7%); public procurement time reducing to 120 days (from 159); primary school repetition rate falling to <10% (from 15.6%); lower secondary completion rates for poorest families increasing (boys to 33%, girls to 22%); CI-Energies’ net income reaching CFAF 70 billion surplus (from CFAF 63 billion deficit); electricity losses reducing to 20% (from 22%); and signing at least two power purchase agreements with Independent Power Producers, including one for renewable energy.

["The project aims to improve access to water supply and sanitation services for 250,000 people in select peri-urban/rural areas of Jingyang District, targeting 94% household water coverage and connecting 50,850 households to sanitation (or individual septic tanks for 6,712). Key outcomes include creating an integrated WSS utility to reduce Non-Revenue Water, energy consumption, and GHG emissions; enhancing WSS service quality and efficiency; improving wastewater treatment efficiency from 20% to 80%; establishing customer service mechanisms; and serving as a replicable PPP model for China.", "To be considered an overall success, the project must achieve improved access and quality of WSS services for the target population, specifically 94% household water coverage and 50,850 sanitation connections. This entails successfully establishing and operating the integrated WSS utility, significantly reducing NRW, energy consumption, and GHG emissions, and increasing wastewater treatment efficiency from 20% to 80%. Effective customer service, robust local government capacity in PPP oversight, and the successful replication of this PPP approach across Sichuan province and China are also crucial. Full adherence to environmental and social safeguards and effective management of risks are essential for success."]

The targeted outcome is to increase Solomon Islands' renewable energy share via hydropower. Success requires developing, constructing, and operating the 15 MW Tina River Hydropower Facility (dam, waterway, powerhouse); completing access roads (13.2 km upgraded, 5.5 km new) and their planned handover; establishing 21.6 km of 66 kV transmission lines and upgrading Lungga Power Station; and providing technical assistance for project oversight, safeguards (including biodiversity net gain), and community benefits.

["Contribution to reducing the population's health risk from waterborne diseases in the programme locations.", "Efficiently ensuring a sufficient, year-round supply of safe drinking water to the population in the programme locations.", "Eliminating potential risks of sewage contaminating the water supply systems.", "Increasing the efficiency of water provision.", "An average consumption of 40 L/person/day for service connections.", "An average consumption of 10 L/person/day for standpipes.", "A connection rate of 65% in the first year after completion.", "A connection rate of 75% in the third year after completion.", "Technical and non-technical unaccounted for water (UfW) not exceeding 30% for production and distribution.", "The quality of water delivered meets Moroccan standards.", "A collection rate above 90%.", "24 hours/day availability of drinking water.", "An absence of immediate sources of danger for water supply resources.", "For success, the population's utilization of the created capacities was essential for achieving the specific goals.", "For success, investments in water supply would improve the population's health and development by providing quality drinking water, thereby reducing waterborne diseases and enhancing quality of life, addressing a key development bottleneck in the water sector."]

The targeted outcome of the activity was to assist environmental ministries in implementing and advancing an eco-regional conservation strategy as a guiding framework for biodiversity preservation in the Southern Caucasus. To achieve success, the following targeted outcomes were necessary: 1) Introduction of two conservation concepts aligned with international standards and a regional conservation mission statement. 2) An increase in the budget for protected area management compared to 2009 baselines (Armenia: 
1.012M, Azerbaijan: 
1.721M, Georgia: 
3.166M). 3) Participation of representatives from all three countries in 50% of conflict-sensitive regional and inter-sectoral communication initiatives.

"The targeted outcomes of the OMVG Interconnection Project are to enable electricity trade between The Gambia, Guinea, Guinea-Bissau, and Senegal, specifically aiming for an installed export/import electricity trade capacity of 222,000 KVA for Senegal, 340,000 KVA for Guinea, 170,000 KVA for Guinea-Bissau, and 180,000 KVA for The Gambia. The project also aims to establish an institutional structure facilitating electricity trade, indicated by a composite index of 3, implying timely tariff payments, existence of an AMA, and staffed Dispatch Centers. It targets providing benefits to 6,684,250 direct project beneficiaries, of which 3,342,125 are female. The project aims to construct 1677 km of transmission lines, 15 substations, and 1677 km of fiber optic network, and ensure the ATMO (structures for electricity trade facilitation) is performing adequately with a qualified operator for the AMA. Success would also mean increasing overall electricity supply, developing critical interconnection infrastructure capable of handling 800 MW, enabling competitively priced power through economies of scale, and achieving an Economic Internal Rate of Return (EIRR) of 17.03 percent. Additionally, it would involve sustainable performance of generation and transmission facilities through effective Operations & Maintenance, completion of feasibility studies for at least three hydropower developments, and empowering the OMVG to coordinate resource use and future generation development for long-term member country benefits. Addressing the weak capacity and financial viability of national power utilities, strengthening OMVG's corporate governance and operational performance, and adhering to environmental and social safeguards are also crucial for overall success."

"The activity targets improving artisanal-fishing facilities and management systems, specifically enhancing the quality and sustainability of services for catching, unloading, and selling artisanal fish products, and ensuring sustainable management and conservation of fishing resources in coastal marine ecosystems. Overall success requires: 1. Increasing sanitary handling of artisanal-fishing catches to 10,000 tons (approx. 75%). 2. Increasing artisanal fishermen purchasing safe inputs/services to 8,000 (40%). 3. Ensuring 10 project facilities cover operating/maintenance costs via user fees. 4. Expanding sustainably managed coastal area from 75,000 to 1,000,000 hectares."

"The Emergency Water Security and Efficiency Project aims to improve water supply availability in drought-vulnerable areas, increase Water Utilities Corporation (WUC) efficiency, and strengthen wastewater management. Specific outcomes include benefiting 580,000 direct project beneficiaries (50% female), providing an additional 14,500 m\b3/day of water, and improving water supply for 460,000 people in drought-affected areas. WUC's revenue to operating cost ratio is targeted at 0.90, and wastewater treatment plants aim for 85% average annual COD removal efficiency. Capacity in MLWS, WUC, and DWA will also be strengthened for improved water security, efficiency, and resource management. Overall success will be measured by achieving these specific targets."

"Targeted outcomes: Improve irrigation service delivery, strengthen flood risk management, enhance climate change resilience. Success requires achieving these via Components A (irrigation management), B (infrastructure modernization), and C (flood management). It also demands robust mitigation of substantial risks (e.g., technical, institutional, fiduciary, E&S), gender mainstreaming, adherence to Co-financing Agreement, Project Implementation Manual, DVC flood forecasting study, DSRP appointment/actions for dams, and satisfactory Emergency Preparedness & O&M Plans for dams, ensuring long-term sustainability."
